title: Republic of Colombia
text:
## Summary

Colombia deposited its instrument of accession to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] on **16 March 2020**, becoming the 65th Party [1][2]. Its substantive cybercrime law is **Ley 1273 de 2009 "De la protección de la información y de los datos"**, which amended the Criminal Code to create a new protected legal good ("protection of information and data") and established comprehensive substantive offences [3][4].

Operationally, the **Policía Nacional de Colombia** (through DIJIN) and the **Fiscalía General de la Nación** jointly operate the **CAI Virtual** online reporting platform and the "A Denunciar" national virtual reporting system [3][4]. Colombia has been a key Council of Europe GLACY+ and C-PROC capacity-building beneficiary, and its accession in 2020 was the culmination of a multi-year process.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law
Law 1273/2009 created new offences in the Criminal Code:
- Abusive access to computer systems (Acceso abusivo a un sistema informático)
- Unlawful obstruction of computer systems or telecommunications networks
- Interception of computer data
- Computer damage (Daño informático)
- Use of malicious software
- Computer-related theft (Hurto por medios informáticos)
- Violation of personal data
- Phishing / capture of personal data
- Unauthorized transfer of assets

Maximum penalty: 120 months' imprisonment and fines up to 1,500 minimum legal monthly wages [3][4].

### Procedural Powers
The Code of Criminal Procedure provides for search and seizure, production orders, and interception under judicial authorization. Specific procedural tools for electronic evidence have been developed through prosecutorial practice and Fiscalía guidance [5].

### Mutual Legal Assistance
The **Fiscalía General de la Nación** serves as central authority for MLAT requests. Since 2020, Colombia can invoke Budapest Convention Chapter III (Articles 23-35) with other parties. Colombia has bilateral MLATs with the United States and Spain (inter alia).

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — Party, acceded 2020-03-16 [1][2]
- Bilateral MLATs with US, Spain, and other partners

## Key Agencies

### Policía Nacional de Colombia — DIJIN
The **Dirección de Investigación Criminal e INTERPOL (DIJIN)** houses the Centro Cibernético Policial, which investigates cybercrime and operates the CAI Virtual public-facing reporting platform [3].

### Fiscalía General de la Nación
National public prosecutor; central authority for MLA; houses specialized cybercrime prosecutors and operates the "A Denunciar" platform jointly with DIJIN [5].

### ColCERT
National CERT under the Ministry of ICT; coordinates incident response.

## Cooperation Track Record

Colombia is *likely* an active participant in Interpol's cybercrime operations in Latin America. Its accession to the Budapest Convention in 2020 has *likely* increased its formal MLA cooperation with European parties. Colombia is a long-standing US partner with extensive MLAT traffic.

### Verified Operational Participation (primary-source confirmed)

| Operation | Coordinator | Primary source explicitly naming Colombia |
|---|---|---|
| [[operation-avalanche]] | German prosecutors / Europol | Europol press release, 2016-11-30 — inline "Countries involved:" roster lists Colombia (confirmed 2026-04-21 via `curl_cffi` TLS impersonation) |
| [[operation-orion-international]] | INTERPOL | INTERPOL press release, 2024-10-15 (full country roster) |
| [[operation-haechi-ii]] | INTERPOL | INTERPOL press release, 2021-11-30 (full country roster incl. USD 8M BEC case in Colombia) |
| [[imminent-monitor-rat-takedown]] | AFP / Europol | Europol press release, 2019-11-29 ("arrests in Europe, Colombia and Australia") |

**Source-verification policy.** Additions to `operations_participated` on this page are only made when a tier-1 primary source (Europol / INTERPOL / DOJ / national agency press release or court filin

title: Republic of Côte d'Ivoire
text:
## Summary

Côte d'Ivoire adopted a comprehensive cybercrime law in 2013 — **Loi n° 2013-451 du 19 juin 2013 relative à la lutte contre la cybercriminalité** — which criminalises offences specific to ICT, addresses intellectual property infringement, and sets out criminal procedure for digital evidence [1]. The law's Articles 4-13 cover the core Budapest Convention substantive offences (illegal access, illegal interception, data/system interference, misuse of devices), and Articles 15-18 address child sexual abuse material [1].

On **30 June 2022**, Côte d'Ivoire was **invited to accede** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] by the Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe [2]. As of the April 2026 cut-off of this research, Côte d'Ivoire has not yet deposited an instrument of accession, and the invitation remains valid for five years from the date of issuance. Côte d'Ivoire has ratified the African Union Convention on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection (Malabo Convention), which entered into force on 8 June 2023 [3].

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law
Law 2013-451 of 19 June 2013 criminalises:
- Illegal access, obstruction, and illegal introduction into an information system (Articles 4-13)
- Illegal interception of data
- Data alteration, deletion, or production
- Child sexual abuse material offences (Articles 15-18)
- IP-related offences on electronic communications networks [1]

### Procedural Powers
The 2013 law specifies criminal procedure for cybercrime matters and digital evidence. Coverage of the full Budapest Convention procedural suite (expedited preservation, production orders, real-time traffic data collection) *likely* requires further amendment to meet accession conditions.

### Mutual Legal Assistance
The Ministry of Justice is central authority. For ECOWAS/francophone cooperation, regional agreements apply. Accession to the Budapest Convention would open formal MLA channels with 75+ parties.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — **Invited to accede** 2022-06-30 [2]
- **African Union [[malabo-convention|Malabo Convention]]** — Party; Convention in force since 2023-06-08 [3]

## Key Agencies

### PLCC — Plateforme de Lutte Contre la Cybercriminalité
The PLCC is the operational cybercrime unit resulting from a 2011 partnership between the **Direction Générale de la Police Nationale (DGPN)** and **ARTCI** (the telecommunications regulator), updated in January 2014 and July 2017 [1][4]. The PLCC is *likely* one of the better-resourced cybercrime units in francophone West Africa.

### ARTCI (Autorité de Régulation des Télécommunications/TIC)
Telecommunications regulator; co-founder of PLCC; provides technical support and incident response coordination.

## Cooperation Track Record

Côte d'Ivoire is *likely* a regional leader in West African cybercrime cooperation, and PLCC has been highlighted in Council of Europe GLACY+ programming as a model. Cross-border cooperation has *likely* been significant on advance-fee fraud and online scam cases targeting European victims.

## Capacity Assessment

Capacity is *likely* medium by regional standards. PLCC benefits from the joint police-regulator model. French is the working language; English proficiency is *likely* low in most units. Accession process suggests ongoing capacity-building to meet Budapest Convention procedural requirements.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current status of Côte d'Ivoire's accession process (remains "invited to accede" as of research date; deadline five years from 2022-06-30).
- Whether the 2013 law requires amendment for full Budapest Convention alignment.
- PLCC current headcount and caseload statistics.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Loi n° 2013-451 relative à la lutte contre la cybercriminalité (Journal Officiel) | Republic of Côte d'Ivoire | 2013-08-12 | https://www.artci.ci/imag

title: Czech Republic
text:
## Summary

The Czech Republic signed the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] on 9 February 2005 and deposited its instrument of ratification on **22 August 2013**, becoming the 40th Party [1][2]. Ratification entered into force on 1 December 2013 [2]. Czech cybercrime legislation is contained in two codes: **Act No. 40/2009 Coll., the Criminal Code (Trestní zákoník)** and **Act No. 141/1961 Coll., the Code of Criminal Procedure**, both as amended [3].

The operational point of contact is the **Cyber Crime Division** of the Criminal Police and Investigation Service within the **Národní centrála proti organizovanému zločinu (NCOZ — National Organized Crime Agency)**. This division is the designated Article 35 (Budapest Convention) 24/7 point of contact for the Czech Republic [3]. NCOZ was established in 2016 as the successor to UOOZ and has since been the principal Czech unit for cybercrime, organized crime, money laundering, and complex economic crime [4].

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law
Criminal Code (Act No. 40/2009 Coll.) provisions criminalise unauthorised access to computer systems and information carriers, computer-related fraud and forgery, and data/system interference, in line with Budapest Convention Articles 2-10. Specific sections address child pornography and offences against confidentiality, integrity and availability of computer data.

### Procedural Powers
Code of Criminal Procedure (Act No. 141/1961 Coll.) provides for search and seizure of electronic evidence, production orders, preservation, and lawful interception. As an EU member state, the Czech Republic implements the European Investigation Order.

### Mutual Legal Assistance
The **Ministry of Justice** is the central authority for formal MLA requests. Within the EU, the European Investigation Order is the primary mechanism. NCOZ serves as the operational cooperation hub for cybercrime-specific matters.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — Party, signed 2005-02-09, ratified 2013-08-22 [1][2]
- **EU European Investigation Order** — transposed
- **EU Attacks on Information Systems Directive** (2013/40/EU) — implemented

## Key Agencies

### NCOZ — Cyber Crime Division
The Cyber Crime Division of the Criminal Police and Investigation Service of the National Organized Crime Agency is the primary operational body. It is the designated Article 35 24/7 point of contact [3]. NCOZ has noted sharp increases in Russia-linked cyberattacks and a near-doubling of cybercrime reports (18,554 cases in 2022) [4].

### National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB)
National cybersecurity authority; handles incident response and critical infrastructure protection; coordinates with NCOZ on criminal cases.

### Národní protidrogová centrála — predecessor/parallel unit
For specialized operations; coordinates with NCOZ where overlap exists.

## Cooperation Track Record

The Czech Republic *likely* participates in Europol-coordinated operations through EC3 and J-CAT. Specific public attribution to Czech authorities in major multi-national takedowns is less frequent than for Western European states, but EU-wide participation is *likely* steady.

## Capacity Assessment

Capacity is *likely* medium-high. The EU membership, established NCOZ Cyber Crime Division, and designated 24/7 point of contact all indicate operational maturity. English proficiency in specialized units is *likely* medium; Czech legal translation requirements may add friction for non-EU MLAT.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current Czech status re: Second Additional Protocol to the Budapest Convention.
- Staffing levels at NCOZ Cyber Crime Division.
- Whether the separate "National Center for Fighting Terrorism, Extremism, and Cybercrime" (referenced in one source) is a distinct body from NCOZ Cyber Crime Division or a reorganization.

title: Republic of Finland
text:
## Summary

Finland is a Party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]], having signed on 23 November 2001 and ratified in **May 2007** [1]. Finland signed the [[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]] on 12 May 2022 as part of the initial group of 22 signatories [2]. Finland's substantive cybercrime framework is located in **Chapter 38 of the Criminal Code (Rikoslaki)**, titled "Data and communications offences" [3][4].

Finland's National Bureau of Investigation — **Keskusrikospoliisi (KRP)** — is the principal law enforcement body for cybercrime, organized crime, and high-impact criminal investigations, and also serves as the central contact point for mutual legal assistance requests from EU member states [5]. Finland joined NATO in 2023, deepening its integration into Western security cooperation.

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The exact Finnish ratification date (2007-05-24) should be verified against the Council of Europe Treaty Office chart. Search results confirmed Finland as a Party but did not show the day-precise date.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law
**Rikoslaki (Criminal Code), Chapter 38** — Data and communications offences [3][4]:
- Sections 3-4 — Message interception / aggravated message interception
- Sections 5-7 — Interference with communications
- Sections 7a, 7b — Interference in an information system / aggravated interference
- Sections 8, 8a — Computer break-in / aggravated computer break-in (penalty: fine to 2 years imprisonment)
- Section 8b — Offences involving systems for accessing protected services
- Section 9a — Identity theft

### Procedural Powers
The Finnish Coercive Measures Act and Criminal Investigation Act provide for search and seizure, production orders, preservation, and lawful interception. As an EU member state, Finland implements the European Investigation Order.

### Mutual Legal Assistance
The **Ministry of Justice** is the formal central authority for MLA requests outside the EU. The **Keskusrikospoliisi (KRP)** serves as the central contact point for EU member state requests [5]. Finnish and English are both practically workable for many cooperation channels.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — Party, signed 2001-11-23, ratified May 2007 [1]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]]** — Signatory, 2022-05-12 [2]
- **Additional Protocol on Xenophobia and Racism (CETS 189)** — Party [6]
- **EU European Investigation Order** — transposed
- **EU Attacks on Information Systems Directive** (2013/40/EU) — implemented
- **NATO** — Member since 2023

## Key Agencies

### Keskusrikospoliisi (KRP) — National Bureau of Investigation
National-level criminal investigation and intelligence organisation of the Finnish Police. Handles cases of major importance including cybercrime, corruption, securities fraud, and environmental crime [5]. Serves as the EU central contact point for MLA and *likely* as the Budapest Convention Article 35 24/7 point of contact.

### Finnish Transport and Communications Agency (Traficom) / NCSC-FI
National CERT and cybersecurity coordination body; handles incident response.

### Supo (Finnish Security and Intelligence Service)
Handles state-linked cyber threat assessment and counterintelligence; *likely* coordinates with KRP on nation-state-linked cybercrime.

## Cooperation Track Record

Finland is *almost certainly* an active participant in Europol EC3, J-CAT, and Nordic cooperation frameworks. Finnish authorities have *likely* participated in multiple major multi-national cybercrime takedowns, though specific attribution in public reporting is less frequent than for larger EU states.

## Capacity Assessment

Capacity is *almost certainly* high. Finland has strong technical and legal infrastructure, high English proficiency, mature EU and Nordic cooperation channels, and NATO membership adds intelligence-sharing

title: French Republic
text:
## Summary

France is a **foundational partner** in European cybercrime cooperation. It was one of the early ratifying parties to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] (ratified 10 January 2006; entry into force 1 May 2006) and operates a mature 24/7 Point of Contact at the **OCLCTIC** since 2000.

The 2023 **LOPMI** law (Loi d'orientation et de programmation du ministère de l'Intérieur) significantly modernised France's cybercrime procedural arsenal and has already been used in high-profile cases such as the 2024 investigation of Telegram CEO Pavel Durov.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

- **Godfrain Law (1988)**: Loi n° 88-19 du 5 janvier 1988 on computer fraud — pioneering legislation, codified at **Articles 323-1 to 323-7** of the French Penal Code. Covers unauthorised access, hindering system operation, and data alteration.
- **LOPMI (2023)**: Loi n° 2023-22 du 24 janvier 2023 — Orientation and Programming Law of the Ministry of the Interior. Expanded procedural powers, introduced obligations for cyberattack victims to file complaints as a condition for insurance coverage.
- Budapest Convention offences implemented via the Godfrain framework and subsequent amendments.

### Procedural Powers

All procedural powers under Articles 14-21 of the Budapest Convention are implemented through the Code de procédure pénale (CPP).

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2006-01-10 | Entry into force 2006-05-01 |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | Signed at Strasbourg opening ceremony |
| First Additional Protocol (Xenophobia/Racism) | Party | 2006-01-10 | Authorised by Law 2005-493 |

## Key Agencies

### OCLCTIC — Office central de lutte contre la criminalité liée aux TIC
- Created in 2000 within the Direction Centrale de la Police Judiciaire (DCPJ)
- Now part of the **Sous-direction de lutte contre la cybercriminalité (SDLC)**
- Operates France's **24/7 Point of Contact** for Budapest Convention, G7, Europol, and INTERPOL networks
- See [[france-national-police]]

### JUNALCO — Juridiction nationale de lutte contre la criminalité organisée
- National Jurisdiction for Combating Organised Crime, Paris Public Prosecutor's Office
- Specialised cybercrime prosecution section
- See [[france-junalco]]

### Gendarmerie Nationale — C3N
- Centre de lutte contre les criminalités numériques (C3N)
- See [[france-gendarmerie]]

### ANSSI — Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information
- Created 2009; national cybersecurity authority (not law enforcement)
- Technical support to investigations

## Cooperation Track Record

France is deeply embedded in EU cooperation via **European Investigation Order (EIO)**, Europol EC3, and Eurojust. As a Budapest Convention party and G7 24/7 Network participant, France maintains continuous cybercrime cooperation channels.

## Capacity Assessment

*Almost certainly* high digital forensics capacity and mature 24/7 availability. English proficiency in formal MLA correspondence is *likely* medium — French remains the primary working language for formal requests.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the average MLAT response time for French central authorities post-LOPMI?
- How has LOPMI's expanded procedural arsenal affected outbound cooperation requests?

## References

| 1 | Budapest Convention — Chart of Signatures and Ratifications (CETS 185) | Council of Europe | Current | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| 2 | France — Octopus Cybercrime Community country profile | Council of Europe | Current | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/france |
| 3 | Cybersecurity Laws and Regulations Report 2026 — France | ICLG | 2026 | https://iclg.com/practice-areas/cybersecurity-laws-and-regu

title: Federal Republic of Germany
text:
## Summary

Germany is a **key pillar** of European cybercrime cooperation. The **Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)** ([[germany-bka]]) and the specialized cybercrime prosecutor's office in Frankfurt (**Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität, ZIT**) have led or co-led some of the most significant cybercrime operations in history, including [[operation-avalanche|Operation Avalanche]] (2016) and [[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame]] (2024-2025).

Germany's central position in European internet infrastructure makes it both a frequent target and a key partner in infrastructure takedowns. German authorities *almost certainly* host more seized criminal infrastructure during takedown operations than any other EU member state.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Strafgesetzbuch (StGB) — Criminal Code:**
- § 202a: Data espionage (Ausspähen von Daten)
- § 202b: Phishing / interception of data (Abfangen von Daten)
- § 202c: Acts preparatory to data espionage and phishing (Vorbereiten des Ausspähens und Abfangens von Daten)
- § 202d: Handling stolen data (Datenhehlerei)
- § 263a: Computer fraud (Computerbetrug)
- § 303a: Data manipulation (Datenveränderung)
- § 303b: Computer sabotage (Computersabotage)

**BSI-Gesetz (Federal Office for Information Security Act):**
- Framework for critical infrastructure protection
- Reporting obligations for operators of critical infrastructure

**Netzwerkdurchsetzungsgesetz (NetzDG):**
- Social media compliance obligations for content moderation

### Procedural Powers

- **Search and seizure:** Strafprozessordnung (StPO) §§ 94-98, 102-110; includes electronic evidence
- **Interception:** StPO § 100a; telecommunications surveillance requires court order
- **Online search (Onlinedurchsuchung):** StPO § 100b; remote access to devices; highly restricted, requires judicial authorization for specific serious offences
- **Preservation:** Available under MLA framework; informal police-to-police preservation requests through INTERPOL/Europol channels
- **Data retention:** Contested — Federal Constitutional Court and CJEU rulings have limited mandatory data retention; current framework under TKG provides limited retention capabilities

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Central authority:** Bundesamt für Justiz (Federal Office of Justice) for formal MLAT requests
- **Execution:** BKA and state criminal police (Landeskriminalämter) execute operational requests
- **EU MLA instruments:** European Investigation Order (EIO) for EU requests — significantly faster than traditional MLAT
- **Federal structure challenge:** Germany's 16 Länder each have their own criminal justice systems; some MLA requests require coordination with state authorities

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> Verify current status of German data retention legislation following CJEU and BVerfG rulings (as of 2024-2025).

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2009 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022 | |
| UNTOC (Palermo Convention) | Party | 2006 | |
| UNCAC | Party | 2014 | |
| EU European Investigation Order | Participating | 2017 | Transposed into national law |
| US-Germany MLAT | In force | 2010 | |

Germany participates in the Budapest Convention **[[24-7-network]]** and Europol's operational networks.

## Key Agencies

### Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)
See [[germany-bka]] for full profile.
- Federal criminal police; primary IC operational agency
- Houses cybercrime investigation units
- INTERPOL NCB Germany
- Key Europol partner; BKA officers co-located at Europol

### Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität (ZIT)
- Central Office for Combating Internet Crime, based in Frankfurt am Main
- Part of the Generalstaatsanwaltschaft Frankfurt (General Prosecutor's Office)
- *Highly likely* the most prolific cybercrime prosecution office in continental Europe
- Led prosecutorial

title: Italian Republic
text:
## Summary

Italy ratified the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] in 2008 via **Law No. 48 of 18 March 2008**, which simultaneously introduced significant reforms to the Italian Criminal Code, Code of Criminal Procedure, and corporate liability framework under **Legislative Decree 231/2001**.

The primary operational cybercrime agency is the **Polizia Postale e delle Comunicazioni** (Postal and Communications Police), which hosts **CNAIPIC** — the National Anti-Crime Computer Centre for the Protection of Critical Infrastructure. CNAIPIC operates Italy's **24/7 Point of Contact** under the Budapest Convention framework.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law — Italian Criminal Code

Key articles introduced or amended by Law 48/2008 to implement Budapest Convention Articles 2–10:
- **Art. 615-ter**: Unauthorised access to a computer or telematic system
- **Art. 615-quater**: Unlawful possession and dissemination of access codes
- **Art. 615-quinquies**: Distribution of malware
- **Art. 617-quater**: Unlawful interception of computer communications
- **Art. 617-quinquies / sexies**: Installation of interception equipment; falsification of computer communications
- **Art. 635-bis to 635-quinquies**: Damage to computer data, systems, and public utilities
- **Art. 640-ter**: Computer fraud

### Corporate Liability

- **Legislative Decree 231/2001** extended to include cybercrimes via Law 48/2008
- Italian legal persons can be held liable for cybercrime offences committed by employees for their benefit

### Procedural Powers

- **Code of Criminal Procedure Art. 51(3-quinquies)**: Public Prosecutor's Office attached to the Court of the main city of each Court of Appeal District holds exclusive jurisdiction for cybercrime investigations
- Full Budapest Convention procedural arsenal (preservation, production, search/seizure, interception) available

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2008-06-05 | Via Law 48/2008 |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | Among 22 opening signatories at Strasbourg |
| EU European Investigation Order | Participating | 2017 | Transposed into national law |

## Key Agencies

### Polizia Postale e delle Comunicazioni (Postal and Communications Police)
- Unit of the Polizia di Stato (State Police)
- Established in its modern form under Law No. 269/1998
- Primary operational cybercrime agency

### CNAIPIC (Centro Nazionale Anticrimine Informatico per la Protezione delle Infrastrutture Critiche)
- National Anti-Crime Computer Centre for the Protection of Critical Infrastructure
- Specialised unit within the Polizia Postale
- **National 24/7 Point of Contact** for the Budapest Convention
- Protects critical infrastructure from cyber threats

### Other Italian Police Forces with Cybercrime Competence
- **Arma dei Carabinieri** — Raggruppamento Carabinieri Investigazioni Scientifiche (RIS)
- **Guardia di Finanza** — financial cybercrime

### DNA — Direzione Nazionale Antimafia e Antiterrorismo
- Coordinating prosecutorial office for organised cybercrime and cyber-terrorism

### ACN — Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale
- National Cybersecurity Agency (established 2021)
- Technical and strategic (not law enforcement)

## Cooperation Track Record

- Full participation in **Europol EC3** and **Eurojust**
- EU **European Investigation Order (EIO)** for intra-EU requests
- Budapest Convention **[[24-7-network]]** via CNAIPIC
- Active participant in major European takedown operations

## Capacity Assessment

- Rating: *almost certainly* high
- Digital forensics: *almost certainly* high (CNAIPIC, RIS)
- 24/7 availability: confirmed via CNAIPIC
- English proficiency: *likely* medium for formal MLA correspondence

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Has Italy ratified the Second Additional Protocol (moving from signatory to party)?
- How does Italy's centralised cyb

title: Kingdom of the Netherlands
text:
## Summary

The Netherlands is *almost certainly* the most operationally active European country for cybercrime takedowns relative to its size. The **Dutch National Police** ([[netherlands-politie]]), through its **National High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU)**, has been instrumental in major international operations including [[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame]], [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos]], [[hive-ransomware-takedown|Hive Ransomware Takedown]], and [[operation-talent|Operation Talent]].

The Netherlands hosts both **Europol** and **[[eurojust]]** in The Hague, providing unique institutional proximity that *highly likely* contributes to the country's outsized role in cybercrime cooperation. The 2019 **Wet computercriminaliteit III** gave Dutch law enforcement pioneering powers for remote computer access (hackbevoegdheid), making the Netherlands a leader in innovative investigation techniques.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Wetboek van Strafrecht (Criminal Code):**
- Art. 138ab: Unauthorized access to computer systems (computervredebreuk)
- Art. 138b: Unauthorized access to automated work via public telecommunications network
- Art. 138c: Interception of data
- Art. 350a: Data manipulation (wijzigen gegevens)
- Art. 350b: Computer sabotage
- Art. 350c-d: Additional computer crime provisions

**Wet computercriminaliteit III (Computer Crime Act III, 2019):**
- Significantly expanded law enforcement powers for cybercrime investigation
- Introduced "hackbevoegdheid" — legal authority for police to remotely access computer systems
- Controversial but *likely* the most advanced police hacking authority in Europe

### Procedural Powers

- **Search and seizure:** Wetboek van Strafvordering (Code of Criminal Procedure); includes digital evidence
- **Remote access (hackbevoegdheid):** Wet computercriminaliteit III (2019); allows police to remotely access suspects' devices, install monitoring software, and copy data — requires judicial authorization
- **Interception:** Requires judicial authorization; applied to telecommunications and internet communications
- **Preservation orders:** Available under domestic and international cooperation frameworks
- **Production orders:** Can compel providers to produce subscriber and traffic data

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Central authority:** Ministry of Justice and Security
- **EU instruments:** European Investigation Order (EIO) for intra-EU requests; European Arrest Warrant (EAW)
- **Proximity advantage:** Physical co-location with Europol and Eurojust in The Hague facilitates rapid informal coordination before formal MLA processes
- **FIOD** ([[netherlands-fiod]]): Fiscal Intelligence and Investigation Service handles financial cybercrime MLA

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2006 | Among the first ratifiers |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022 | |
| UNTOC (Palermo Convention) | Party | 2004 | |
| UNCAC | Party | 2006 | |
| EU European Investigation Order | Participating | 2017 | |
| US-Netherlands MLAT | In force | 1983 | |

The Netherlands participates in the Budapest Convention **[[24-7-network]]** and Europol's operational networks.

## Key Agencies

### Dutch National Police (Politie) — National High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU)
See [[netherlands-politie]] for full profile.
- Specialized cybercrime unit within the National Police
- *Almost certainly* among the top 3 national cybercrime units in Europe
- Pioneered innovative takedown techniques (e.g., "police malware" deployed to infected systems during Emotet takedown)
- Key infrastructure seizure partner in Europol operations

### FIOD (Fiscale Inlichtingen- en Opsporingsdienst)
- Fiscal Intelligence and Investigation Service
- Handles financial cybercrime, cryptocurrency tracing, money laundering investigations
- Participated in multiple international financial cyb

title: Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea)
text:
> [!info] Stub
> This page was auto-created during ingestion of the 2026-05-07 DOJ announcement of the Knoot/Prince DPRK IT worker laptop-farm sentencings, in which DPRK is named as the threat-source state for an IT-worker revenue-generation scheme targeting nearly 70 US companies. It will be expanded as additional DPRK-side or US-DPRK-related sources are ingested.

## Summary

The Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK; commonly North Korea) is a non-cooperative jurisdiction in the public IC corpus on cybercrime. The state is the named threat-source for multiple US-jurisdictional public-record prosecutions, including DPRK-led ransomware activity attributed to entities such as the Reconnaissance General Bureau and Lazarus Group, and the fraudulent remote IT worker revenue-generation schemes documented in the [[us-v-knoot-prince-dprk-laptop-farms-2026|Knoot/Prince case]] (May 2026) and the broader DPRK RevGen: Domestic Enabler Initiative.

## IC Posture (Public-Record Summary)

Public US enforcement against DPRK cyber and IT-worker revenue-generation operations proceeds through US-jurisdictional charges against US-based enablers (US-based "laptop farmers" hosting victim-company-issued laptops in their residences while DPRK IT workers operate the laptops remotely from China and elsewhere) and through OFAC sanctions actions. The cited 2026-05-07 DOJ release attributes DPRK IT worker collective revenue at "hundreds of millions of dollars annually," with individual workers earning up to USD 300,000 per year, on behalf of DPRK Ministry of Defense and other entities directly involved in the DPRK's weapons programs.

There is no public-record DPRK-side cooperation in the cybercrime IC corpus. Korean (Republic of Korea / South Korea) cooperation against DPRK threats is documented separately at the bilateral US-ROK level, including the **October 2023 joint US-ROK guidance** on DPRK IT worker threats referenced in the cited 2026-05-07 DOJ release.

## Notable Cases

- [[us-v-knoot-prince-dprk-laptop-farms-2026]] (May 2026 — 7th and 8th US-based laptop-farmer sentencings; combined USD 1.2M+ revenue to DPRK; ~70 US victim companies).

## Open Questions

- The full DPRK threat-actor entity attribution (Lazarus Group, APT38, Reconnaissance General Bureau, Ministry of Defense, etc.) is not enumerated in this stub. Future ingest of additional DPRK-relevant sources should expand the threat-actor map.
- The full set of DPRK-tied sanctions actions (OFAC, UN, ROK, EU, UK) is not enumerated in this stub.

title: Republic of Poland
text:
## Summary

Poland is a European Union member and **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**. Its cybercrime law enforcement underwent a major institutional reform in 2022 with the creation of the **Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime** (*Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości*, **CBZC**), a dedicated national cybercrime investigation service within the Polish Police.

Poland participates in EU cybercrime cooperation frameworks through Europol's EC3 and Eurojust.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Poland's primary cybercrime provisions are contained in the **Criminal Code** (*Kodeks karny*, 1997, amended):
- Art. 267: Unauthorized access / interception
- Art. 268-268a: Data interference
- Art. 269-269b: System interference, computer sabotage

Poland's Criminal Code implements the substantive law requirements of the Budapest Convention following accession.

### Procedural Powers

Under the Code of Criminal Procedure, Polish law enforcement has access to search and seizure, production orders, data preservation, and lawful interception. *Likely* compliant with Budapest Convention procedural requirements.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for MLAT requests is the **Ministry of Justice** (*Ministerstwo Sprawiedliwości*).

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratification 2015-02-20) [1]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [1]

## Key Agencies

- **[[poland-police|Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości (CBZC)]]** — Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime, established 12 January 2022 [2][3]

## Cooperation Track Record

Poland *likely* has a strong track record in EU joint operations, particularly through Europol EC3. Specific operation participation records require further research.

## Capacity Assessment

With CBZC's establishment in 2022, Poland has *highly likely* strengthened its institutional cybercrime capacity. EU membership and Europol integration support medium-to-high IC responsiveness.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact count of CBZC staff and operational footprint not confirmed
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status pending
- Specific operation participation records need source backing

| [2] | About CBZC (Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości) | Polish Police / CBZC | 2022 | https://cbzc.policja.gov.pl/ |
| [3] | Poland establishes Central Bureau to fight cybercrime | CoE Cybercrime | 2022 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/-/poland-central-bureau |
| [4] | [[2025-12-16_cbzc-policja-pl_international-money-laundering-jit-poland-spain-czechia-hungary\|CBZC Kraków money-laundering JIT (PL+ES+CZ+HU JIT, RO suspects, GR/IT/ES/BE/NL cash-out; ~EUR 2.23M + PLN 717K laundered)]] | Polish CBZC (Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości) | 2025-12-16 | https://cbzc.policja.gov.pl/bzc/aktualnosci/768,Rozbita-miedzynarodowa-grupa-przestepcza-zajmujaca-sie-praniem-pieniedzy-z-oszus.html |

| [6] | [[2026-02-17_cbzc-policja-pl_47-latek-phobos-zatrzymany-cbzc\|Poland CBZC Phobos affiliate arrest (Operation Aether) — 47-y-o male in Małopolskie, Penal Code Art 269b §1; PL+US named]] | Polish CBZC | 2026-02-17 | https://cbzc.policja.gov.pl/bzc/aktualnosci/823,47-latek-zwiazany-z

title: Portuguese Republic
text:
## Summary

Portugal is an EU member state and **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**. Its 2009 Cybercrime Law (*Lei do Cibercrime*, Law 109/2009) is widely cited as a close implementation of the Budapest Convention. The **Polícia Judiciária** (PJ) — Portugal's criminal investigation police — hosts the **Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e Criminalidade Tecnológica (UNC3T)**, which handles most cybercrime investigations including international cooperation.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Lei 109/2009 — Lei do Cibercrime** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access (Art. 6)
- System interference (Art. 5)
- Data interference
- Computer-related fraud and forgery

The law integrates with the Portuguese Penal Code.

### Procedural Powers

Law 109/2009 also contains procedural provisions including expedited preservation of data, production orders, and interception of content data. *Likely* compliant with Budapest Convention Chapter II procedural requirements.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for MLAT requests is the **Procuradoria-Geral da República** (Attorney General's Office). Portugal is a party to EU mutual assistance instruments as well.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratification 2010-03-24) [2]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [2]

## Key Agencies

- **[[portugal-police|Polícia Judiciária — UNC3T]]** — Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e Criminalidade Tecnológica [3]

## Cooperation Track Record

Portugal cooperates through Europol EC3, Eurojust, and the Budapest Convention 24/7 Network. *Likely* participates in major EU joint operations.

## Capacity Assessment

Portugal's PJ and UNC3T represent consolidated institutional cybercrime capacity. The 2009 Cybercrime Law provides a solid legal framework. EU integration supports strong IC.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records need source backing
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status pending as of 2026

title: Qatar

title: Romania
text:
## Summary

Romania is a major European cybercrime cooperation partner. It is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2004 (one of the earliest) and hosts the **Council of Europe Cybercrime Programme Office ([[c-proc|C-PROC]])** in Bucharest, which runs global capacity-building programmes.

Romania has a dedicated specialized prosecutor service, **DIICOT** (*Direcția de Investigare a Infracțiunilor de Criminalitate Organizată și Terorism*, [[romania-diicot]]), and a police cybercrime service within the Romanian Police's Directorate for Combating Organized Crime ([[romania-police]]).

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law 161/2003** (*Legea 161/2003*), **Title III** — on Preventing and Combating Cybercrime — was enacted to align Romanian law with the Budapest Convention prior to accession. The Criminal Code Arts. 360-366 later consolidated cybercrime offences [1][2].

### Procedural Powers

Romanian criminal procedure provides for preservation orders, production orders, search and seizure of computer data, and interception. DIICOT has specialized investigative tools for organized cybercrime.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice** (*Ministerul Justiției*). Romania operates as both sending and receiving state in substantial MLAT volume.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2004-09-12) [3]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [3]

## Key Agencies

- **[[romania-diicot|DIICOT]]** — Directorate for Investigating Organized Crime and Terrorism (specialized cybercrime prosecutors)
- **[[romania-police|Poliția Română DCCO]]** — Directorate for Combating Organized Crime, cybercrime service
- **[[c-proc|C-PROC]]** — Council of Europe Cybercrime Programme Office (international org, hosted in Bucharest)

## Cooperation Track Record

Romania is *almost certainly* a high-volume cybercrime IC partner. Prior to 2010s it was a significant source country for financial cybercrime targeting Western Europe and North America; Romanian authorities have cooperated extensively with US and EU counterparts in takedowns.

## Capacity Assessment

High institutional capacity (DIICOT, specialized cybercrime judges), EU integration, C-PROC hosting, and experienced prosecutorial practice place Romania in the "high capacity" tier. English proficiency in prosecutorial ranks reported as medium.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records and MLAT statistics need source backing
- Constitutional court decisions on data retention have affected IC scenarios

## References

| [1] | Law No. 161/2003 (Title III) | Romanian Government | 2003-04-19 | https://legislatie.just.ro/Public/DetaliiDocument/43323 |
| [2] | Romania — Octopus Cybercrime Community | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/country-wiki-ap/-/asset_publisher/hFPA5fbKjyCJ/content/romania |
| [3] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| [4] | Cybercrime Programme Office (C-PROC) | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/cybercrime-office-c-proc- |
| [5] | [[2026-01-26_police-hu_swatting-doxing-hungary-romania-joint-investigation\|Hungary-Romania Discord swatting/doxing joint investigation — 4 suspects (HU+RO Bihor County), KR NNI cyber investigators participated in Romanian search]] | Hungarian National Police (KR NNI) | 2026-01-26 | https://www.police.hu/hu/hirek-es-informaciok/legfrissebb-hireink/bunugyek/swatting-es-doxing-miatt-indult-nyomozas |

title: Republic of Rwanda
text:
## Summary

Rwanda is a recent **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party**, having deposited its instrument of accession on **10 January 2025** [1]. Rwanda has been an active partner in Council of Europe capacity-building programmes, particularly the **GLACY+** programme run by [[c-proc|C-PROC]].

Rwanda hosts African Union cybersecurity initiatives and is seen as a model East African cybercrime cooperation partner.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law No. 60/2018 of 22/08/2018 on Prevention and Punishment of Cybercrimes** [2] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access and interception
- System and data interference
- Computer-related fraud and forgery
- Content-related offences including online grooming

The 2018 Law was drafted with Budapest Convention compatibility in mind.

### Procedural Powers

Search and seizure of electronic evidence, production orders, and preservation orders available under the 2018 Law and general criminal procedure.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (acceded 2025-01-10) [1]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — party

## Key Agencies

- **Rwanda National Police Cybercrime Unit**
- **Rwanda Investigation Bureau (RIB)**

## Cooperation Track Record

Rwanda *likely* has a growing cooperation track record through GLACY+, Interpol, and regional East African partnerships.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium institutional capacity with active participation in capacity-building programmes. High English proficiency. Rwanda's recent Budapest Convention accession is expected to improve MLAT workflows.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Specific operation participation records not yet established
- Integration with 24/7 Network pending or in progress following accession

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Rwanda acceding to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/-/rwanda-acceding-budapest-convention |
| [2] | Law No. 60/2018 on Prevention and Punishment of Cybercrimes | Government of Rwanda | 2018-08-22 | https://www.rura.rw/ |
| [3] | GLACY+ — C-PROC | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/glacyplus |

title: Republic of Serbia
text:
## Summary

Serbia is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2009 and is *almost certainly* one of the first states to ratify the **Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)** [1]. Serbia has institutionalized cybercrime enforcement through a **Special Prosecutor's Office for High-Tech Crime** and a specialized department within the Belgrade Higher Court.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Serbia's 2005 **Law on Organization and Competence of Government Authorities for Combating High-Tech Crime** [2] established the institutional framework. Cybercrime offences are defined in the Serbian **Criminal Code** (Arts. 298-304a covering unauthorized access, data damage, and related conduct).

### Procedural Powers

Standard procedural tools including search, seizure, preservation, and production orders.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2009-04-14) [1]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol (CETS 224)]]** — ratified 2023-11-02 (among the earliest ratifiers) [1]

## Key Agencies

- **Special Prosecutor's Office for High-Tech Crime** (Tužilaštvo za visokotehnološki kriminal)
- **Ministry of Interior Cybercrime Department**

## Cooperation Track Record

Serbia cooperates with neighboring Balkans states, EU Member States, and via Europol and Interpol channels. *Likely* strong regional cooperation role.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium capacity; institutional specialization provides focused cybercrime prosecution capability. EU accession process has driven alignment with EU instruments.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Operation participation records need verification
- Second Additional Protocol ratification status confirmed per T-CY news (Serbia was among early ratifiers with Japan and Hungary)

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 & 224 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| [2] | Law on Organization and Competence for High-Tech Crime (2005) | Republic of Serbia | 2005 | https://www.paragraf.rs/propisi/zakon_o_organizaciji_i_nadleznosti_drzavnih_organa_za_borbu_protiv_visokotehnoloskog_kriminala.html |
| [3] | Serbia — Octopus Cybercrime Community | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/country-wiki-ap/-/asset_publisher/hFPA5fbKjyCJ/content/serbia |

title: Republic of Korea (South Korea)
text:
> [!warning] Status — Major reorganization of prosecution system pending (2026-10-02)
> On **2026-10-02** the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청 (Supreme Prosecutors' Office)]] will be **abolished** after 78 years and replaced by two new bodies: **공소청 (Prosecution Service, under 법무부)** for prosecution only, and **중대범죄수사청 / 중수청 (Serious Crimes Investigation Agency, under 행정안전부)** for investigation of six serious-crime categories including **cybercrime**. This is a fundamental restructuring of Korea's criminal justice architecture and will reshape how foreign partners engage with Korea on cybercrime MLAT, extradition, and investigative cooperation. See [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea]] and [[spo-international-cooperation]] for details. Until 2026-09-30 the existing SPO structure remains in effect.
>
> **Budapest Convention status correction:** The prior version of this page stated Korea "acceded in 2024." This is **incorrect**. Korea was *invited* to accede in **February 2023** but accession was stalled because domestic implementing legislation — specifically a 형사소송법 amendment making preservation requests mandatory — had not passed. The amendment **passed the National Assembly on 2025-12-12** and takes effect **2026-07-01**. The Council of Europe Treaty Office chart status dated **2026-04-25** still lists Korea without an accession or entry-into-force date, so Korea is **not yet a party** to the Budapest Convention [15][16][18].

## Summary

South Korea is a **highly capable** partner for international cooperation on cybercrime, with advanced technological infrastructure, specialized cybercrime enforcement units at both national and metropolitan levels, and comprehensive cybercrime legislation. Korea was **invited to accede** to the [[budapest-convention]] in **February 2023** and has been finalizing its accession since: the domestic implementing legislation (a 형사소송법 amendment introducing mandatory electronic-evidence preservation requests) passed the National Assembly on 2025-12-12 and takes effect 2026-07-01. Formal accession may follow, but the Council of Europe Treaty Office chart still recorded no Korean accession or entry-into-force date in its **2026-04-25** status snapshot [15][16][18].

Korea's cybercrime enforcement architecture is undergoing **two major concurrent reorganizations** that will substantially reshape international cooperation by late 2026:
1. **Prosecution system**: [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청 abolished]] 2026-10-02, replaced by 공소청 (prosecution) and 중수청 (investigation of six serious-crime categories including cybercrime)
2. **Police cybercrime system**: [[knpa-cyber-bureau|경찰청 사이버수사국 dissolved]] in December 2023; functions currently exercised at 사이버수사심의관 (deputy-director) level within 수사국; a re-establishment of 사이버안전국 has been proposed for 2027

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**정보통신망 이용촉진 및 정보보호 등에 관한 법률 (Information and Communications Network Act):**
Primary cybercrime statute. Criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access to information and communications networks (Art. 48)
- Distribution of malicious programs (Art. 48-2)
- DDoS attacks / interference with service (Art. 48-3)
- Data breach and personal information violations
- Spam and unsolicited communications

**형법 (Criminal Act):**
- Art. 314: Obstruction of business by interference with computer (컴퓨터등 장애 업무방해)
- Art. 316(2): Violation of secrecy of electronic records
- Art. 347-2: Computer fraud (컴퓨터등 사용사기)

**기타 관련 법률:**
- 전기통신사업법 (Telecommunications Business Act)
- 개인정보 보호법 (Personal Information Protection Act)
- 전자금융거래법 (Electronic Financial Transactions Act)

### Procedural Powers

- **압수·수색 (Search and seizure):** 형사소송법 Art. 106, 107 — includes digital evidence; requires warrant
- **통신제한조치 (Interception):** 통신비밀보호법 — requires court order for real-time interception
- **통신사실확인자료 (Traffic data):** 통신비밀보호법 Art. 13 — court order for subscriber/traffic data
- **보전요청 (Preservation):** Available under 국제형사사

title: Kingdom of Spain
text:
## Summary

Spain is a **key EU cybercrime cooperation partner**, with two specialised national cyber investigation bodies operating in parallel: the **Guardia Civil's Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos (GDT)** — founded 1996 as one of Europe's earliest cybercrime units — and the **Policía Nacional's Brigada Central de Investigación Tecnológica (BIT)**. Spain ratified the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] on **3 June 2010** and modified its Criminal Code and Criminal Procedure Act in 2015 to implement Convention requirements.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Código Penal (Criminal Code):**
- Art. 197 — unlawful access to personal data / computer systems
- Art. 197 bis — illegal access to information systems (post-2015)
- Art. 197 ter — preparatory acts
- Art. 248 — computer fraud (estafa informática)
- Art. 264 — data/system interference and damage
- Art. 264 bis — system interference
- Art. 264 ter — preparatory tools

2015 reform (LO 1/2015) brought the Criminal Code into line with the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] and EU Directive 2013/40/EU on attacks against information systems.

### Procedural Powers

**Ley de Enjuiciamiento Criminal (LECrim)** provides:
- Search and seizure of electronic evidence
- Remote search of computer systems (registro remoto)
- Communications interception (intervención de las comunicaciones)
- Data preservation and production orders

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2010-06-03 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | |
| EU EIO | Participating | 2017 | |
| UNTOC | Party | | |
| UNCAC | Party | | |

## Key Agencies

### [[spain-guardia-civil|Guardia Civil — Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos (GDT)]]
- Founded 1996 as Grupo de Delitos Informáticos
- Renamed Departamento de Delitos de Alta Tecnología, then Grupo de Delitos Telemáticos
- Investigation, forensics, R&D, intelligence

### [[spain-national-police|Cuerpo Nacional de Policía — Unidad Central de Ciberdelincuencia (UCC / BIT)]]
- Brigada Central de Investigación Tecnológica
- Parallel national cyber investigative jurisdiction

### Central Authority
- **Subdirección General de Cooperación Jurídica Internacional**, Ministerio de Justicia — MLA central authority under Budapest arts. 24 and 27
- Spain maintains **two 24/7 contact points** — one for each national force

## Cooperation Track Record

Spain is *almost certainly* a major participant in EU-coordinated cybercrime operations via Europol EC3 and Eurojust. *Likely* frequently involved in operations against Spanish-speaking cybercrime networks operating across Latin America.

## Capacity Assessment

- **Rating:** high
- **24/7 availability:** yes (two contact points)
- **Digital forensics:** high
- **English proficiency:** medium

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the current division of labour between Guardia Civil GDT and CNP BIT on cybercrime cases?
- Has Spain ratified the Second Additional Protocol as of 2026?

title: Swiss Confederation
text:
## Summary

Switzerland is a **high-capability cybercrime cooperation partner**, particularly important for **financial and cryptocurrency investigations** given its role as a global financial hub and the presence of the "Crypto Valley" ecosystem in Zug. Switzerland ratified the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] on **21 September 2011**.

Swiss cybercrime law is embedded within the existing **Swiss Criminal Code (StGB/Code pénal)** and **Swiss Criminal Procedure Code (StPO/CPP)**; no standalone cybercrime statute exists, as the existing codes were deemed sufficient with minor amendments.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Criminal Law

**Swiss Criminal Code (StGB):**
- Art. 143 — unauthorised obtaining of data (unbefugte Datenbeschaffung)
- Art. 143bis — unauthorised access to a data processing system (unbefugtes Eindringen in ein Datenverarbeitungssystem)
  - Art. 143bis para. 2 added to implement the Budapest Convention — criminalising making available passwords, programs, or data intended for committing offences
- Art. 144bis — damage to data (Datenbeschädigung)
- Art. 147 — computer fraud (betrügerischer Missbrauch einer Datenverarbeitungsanlage)
- Art. 150 — obtaining services fraudulently

### Procedural Powers

**Swiss Criminal Procedure Code (StPO)** provides search, seizure, preservation, production orders, and telecommunications surveillance.

**BÜPF** (Bundesgesetz betreffend die Überwachung des Post- und Fernmeldeverkehrs) — Federal Act on the Surveillance of Post and Telecommunications governs interception and data retention obligations on providers.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

- **Federal Act on International Mutual Assistance in Criminal Matters (IMAC/IRSG)** — primary MLA statute
- **Central authority:** Federal Office of Justice (Bundesamt für Justiz, BJ)
- Swiss banking secrecy is *no longer* a blanket bar to MLA in criminal matters

## Treaty Memberships

| Framework | Status | Date | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| [[budapest-convention]] | Party | 2011-09-21 | |
| [[second-additional-protocol]] | Signatory | 2022-05-12 | |
| UNTOC | Party | | |
| UNCAC | Party | | |

## Key Agencies

### [[switzerland-fedpol|fedpol — Federal Office of Police]]
- Responsible for combating cybercrime, detecting online offences, and coordinating cross-border investigations
- Hosts Switzerland's 24/7 contact point
- NEDIK (Netzwerk Ermittlungsunterstützung Digitale Kriminalität) — Digital Crime Investigation Support Network coordinates cantonal and federal efforts

### [[switzerland-ncsc|National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC)]]
- National CERT and cybersecurity coordination

### Cantonal Police Forces
- Switzerland's federal structure means operational cybercrime investigation is often conducted at cantonal level, with fedpol providing coordination

## Cooperation Track Record

Switzerland is *almost certainly* a frequently-requested MLA partner, particularly for:
- Seizure of assets and cryptocurrency
- Records from Swiss financial institutions
- Servers hosted in Swiss data centres

## Capacity Assessment

- **Rating:** high
- **Digital forensics:** high
- **24/7 availability:** yes
- **English proficiency:** high (among federal officials)

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the average MLA response time for Swiss requests under IMAC?
- How does NEDIK coordinate with individual cantonal cybercrime units?

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|------|-----|
| 1 | Switzerland — Octopus Cybercrime Community | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/switzerland |
| 2 | Cybercrime policies/strategies Switzerland | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/country-wiki-ap/-/asset_publisher/CmDb7M4RGb4Z/content/switzerland/pop_up |
| 3 | Treaty 185 signatures chart | Council of Europe | — | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |

title: United Republic of Tanzania
text:
## Summary

Tanzania is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]. Its primary cybercrime legislation is the **Cybercrimes Act, 2015** [1], which has been controversial for including content-related offences that civil society organizations have characterized as restricting free expression.

Tanzania cooperates internationally through **Interpol** and **Commonwealth** channels. Capacity for cross-border cybercrime cooperation is *likely* low due to limited specialized institutional infrastructure.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Cybercrimes Act No. 14 of 2015** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access and interception
- System and data interference
- Computer fraud and forgery
- Publication of false information (speech-related offences drawing civil society concern)
- Racist and xenophobic content

### Procedural Powers

The Act grants police powers to search and seize, compel production of data, and access computer systems. Real-time interception requires judicial authorization.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority for mutual legal assistance is the **Ministry of Constitutional and Legal Affairs**. Tanzania has MLA arrangements through the Commonwealth Harare Scheme and bilateral agreements.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [2]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — non-party (as of last check)

## Key Agencies

- **Tanzania Police Force Cyber Crime Unit**
- **Tanzania Communications Regulatory Authority (TCRA)**

## Cooperation Track Record

Limited public record of major joint cybercrime operations. Tanzania participates in Interpol Africa initiatives including [[africa-cyber-surge-ii]] (as a participating country).

## Capacity Assessment

Low institutional cybercrime capacity. English proficiency is high (English is an official language). Limited specialized digital forensics infrastructure.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Malabo Convention status requires verification
- Extent of participation in Interpol Africa cybercrime operations

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Cybercrimes Act No. 14 of 2015 | Parliament of Tanzania | 2015-04-25 | https://www.parliament.go.tz/ |
| [2] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Kingdom of Thailand
text:
## Summary

Thailand is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] but has substantive cybercrime legislation under the **Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007)**, as amended in 2017 (B.E. 2560). The Act has been widely criticized by civil society organizations for provisions that criminalize content considered harmful to national security or public order, which are distinct from conventional cybercrime offences.

The **Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau (CCIB)** ([[thailand-royal-police]]) of the Royal Thai Police handles most cybercrime investigations.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007)** [1] criminalizes:
- Unauthorized access to computer systems
- Interception
- Data and system interference
- Distribution of obscene/false content (controversial provisions)

**B.E. 2560 (2017) amendments** [2] expanded surveillance authorities and added additional offences. Civil society organizations have expressed concerns about speech restrictions.

### Procedural Powers

Authorities have powers to access computer data, seize equipment, and compel disclosure. Speed of cross-border cooperation varies.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Office of the Attorney General** (*สำนักงานอัยการสูงสุด*). Thailand is a party to the ASEAN Treaty on Mutual Legal Assistance in Criminal Matters.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [3]
- **ASEAN MLAT** — party
- **UN Convention against Cybercrime (2024)** — status to be verified

## Key Agencies

- **[[thailand-royal-police|Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau (CCIB)]]** — Royal Thai Police cyber unit
- **Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (MDES)** — policy and infrastructure

## Cooperation Track Record

Thailand cooperates via Interpol, ASEAN, and bilateral channels. Notable cooperation in takedown of domestic call-center scam operations and pig-butchering fraud networks based in Thailand and bordering Special Economic Zones.

## Capacity Assessment

Medium institutional capacity. Thailand has established dedicated cybercrime units. MLAT response times are variable. English proficiency in specialized units is adequate for international engagement.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Thailand is not currently a Budapest Convention party — whether accession is under consideration unclear
- Human rights concerns about Computer Crime Act provisions affect how some Western states handle MLAT requests from Thailand

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007) — English translation | Thailand Law Online | 2007 | https://www.samuiforsale.com/law-texts/computer-crime-act.html |
| [2] | 2017 amendments to Computer Crime Act (English summary) | Baker McKenzie | 2017 | https://www.bakermckenzie.com/en/ |
| [3] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Togolese Republic
text:
## Summary

Togo is a **non-party** to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] but is an early party to the **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection (ratified 18 November 2016). Togo has enacted comprehensive cybersecurity legislation and was announced in 2019 as the host of the **African Union Cybersecurity Centre** in Lomé.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

**Law 2018-026 on Cybersecurity and Combating Cybercrime** [1] defines cybercrime offences including unauthorized access, data interference, and system interference. Additional provisions cover electronic fraud and child protection.

### Procedural Powers

Togolese criminal procedure provides for search and seizure of electronic evidence and production orders, with judicial authorization for interception.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Ministry of Justice**. Togo participates in ECOWAS regional cooperation and bilateral MLATs.

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — non-party [2]
- **[[malabo-convention|African Union Malabo Convention]]** — party (2016-11-18) [3]
- **ECOWAS Directive on Cybercrime** — participating member state

## Key Agencies

- **Agence Nationale de la Cybersécurité (ANCy)** — National Cybersecurity Agency
- **Togolese Police Cybercrime Unit**

## Cooperation Track Record

Limited public record. Togo has participated in Interpol Africa operations including [[africa-cyber-surge-ii]].

## Capacity Assessment

Low institutional capacity but improving through regional initiatives. The hosting of the AU Cybersecurity Centre is expected to drive capacity development.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Implementation status of AU Cybersecurity Centre at Lomé
- Togo's current engagement with GLACY+ and C-PROC capacity building

## References

| [1] | Loi n° 2018-026 relative à la cybersécurité et à la lutte contre la cybercriminalité | République Togolaise | 2018-12-07 | https://ancy.gouv.tg/ |
| [2] | Parties/Observers to the Budapest Convention | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/parties-observers |

title: Ukraine
text:
## Summary

Ukraine is a **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party** since 2005 and has been one of the most operationally active eastern European cybercrime cooperation partners. The **Cyberpolice of Ukraine** ([[ukraine-police]]) — Department of Cyber Police within the National Police of Ukraine — leads cybercrime investigations.

Despite the ongoing Russian invasion (since February 2022), Ukraine has continued to participate in multinational cybercrime operations including those targeting ransomware infrastructure. Ukrainian law enforcement has made notable arrests of suspected ransomware affiliates.

> [!caution] Operational sensitivity
> Details of active cooperation during wartime may involve operational security considerations. Information in this profile is drawn from public sources only.

## Legal Framework for Cybercrime

### Substantive Law

Ukraine's **Criminal Code** Arts. 361-363-1 criminalize unauthorized access, data interference, and distribution of malicious software [1]. Additional provisions address computer fraud and forgery.

The **Law on Cybersecurity of Ukraine** (2017) [2] established a national cybersecurity framework including responsibilities for State Service of Special Communications and Information Protection (SSSCIP).

### Procedural Powers

Ukrainian criminal procedure provides for search and seizure of electronic evidence, data preservation, production orders, and interception.

### Mutual Legal Assistance

Central authority is the **Office of the Prosecutor General** (*Офіс Генерального прокурора*).

## Treaty Memberships

- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — party (ratified 2005-09-10) [3]
- **[[second-additional-protocol|Second Additional Protocol]]** — signatory (2022-05-12) [3]

## Key Agencies

- **[[ukraine-police|Cyberpolice of Ukraine]]** — Department of Cyber Police, National Police of Ukraine
- **Security Service of Ukraine (SBU)** — counter-intelligence and national security cyber matters

## Cooperation Track Record

Ukraine has participated in multiple major international cybercrime operations including actions against REvil/Sodinokibi and other ransomware groups. It is *highly likely* to have strong bilateral cooperation with US and EU partners.

## Capacity Assessment

Despite wartime conditions, Ukraine maintains substantial cybercrime investigative capacity. English proficiency in specialized cyber units is high. Digital forensics capacity is well-developed.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Operational impact of the war on specific MLAT timelines unclear
- Extent of cooperation with Russia (formally hostile state) ceased after 2014 / 2022
- Cross-border cooperation logistics may involve alternative channels during wartime

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Criminal Code of Ukraine — Section XVI Cyber Offences | Verkhovna Rada | 2001 | https://zakon.rada.gov.ua/laws/show/2341-14 |
| [2] | Law on Basic Principles of Cybersecurity of Ukraine (2017) | Verkhovna Rada | 2017-10-05 | https://zakon.rada.gov.ua/laws/show/2163-19 |
| [3] | Chart of Signatures and Ratifications of Treaty 185 | Council of Europe | 2025 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| [4] | [[2025-12-16_policie-cz_operation-octopus-investment-fraud-takedown-kyiv\|Operation OCTOPUS investment-fraud takedown in Kyiv (CZ+UA bilateral JIT, 17 arrests, CZK 33M seized)]] | Policie ČR / NCTEKK | 2025-12-16 | https://policie.gov.cz/clanek/mezinarodni-operace-octopus-a-connect.aspx |
| [5] | [[2026-03-18_prokuratura-lv_telephone-fraud-2-million-euros-170-money-mules\|Latvia €2M vishing money-mule case — 4-jurisdiction (LV+EE+DE+UA), Eurojust-coordinated; 2 fugitives arrested in Ivano-Frankivsk on 2026-03-12]] | Latvijas Republikas prokuratūra | 2026-03-18 | https://www.prokuratura.lv/lv/aktualitates/2026/aktualitates/izkrapti-2-miljoni-eiro-valsts-policija-telefonkrapsanas-lieta-noskaid
