| Bulgaria | Sofia City Prosecutor's Office; General Directorate Combating Organized Crime (Ministry of Interior); Sofia Directorate of Police local departments (12 officials in BG action) |
| Finland | National Bureau of Investigation; Prosecution District of Southern Finland |
| Georgia | Office of the Prosecutor General of Georgia; Investigation Service of the Ministry of Finance; Ministry of Internal Affairs of Georgia; State Security Service (250 officers in GE action) |
| Germany | Office of the Public Prosecutor General in Bamberg — Bavarian Central Office for the Prosecution of Cybercrime (ZCB Bamberg); Criminal Police Department with Central Tasks Upper Frankonia; Federal Criminal Police Office ([[germany-bka\|BKA]]); Prosecutor General's Office Dresden; Public Prosecutor's Office Leipzig; State Office of Criminal Investigations Saxony; Police Department Chemnitz; Police Department Leipzig (~60 German officials in GE action) |
| Latvia | Rīga Judicial Region Prosecution Office; Cybercrime Enforcement Department of the Central Criminal Police Department of the State Police of Latvia; Asset Recovery Office of Latvia |
| North Macedonia | Basic Public Prosecution Office for Prosecuting Organized Crime and Corruption; Ministry of Interior — police (1 prosecutor + 28 police officers in NM action) |
| Spain | Investigative Court no. 2 La Seu d'Urgell; Public Prosecution Office Lleida; [[spain-national-police\|Criminal Investigation Division of Mossos d'Esquadra]]; Guardia Civil (33 Spanish officials in AL action) |
| Sweden | Swedish Prosecution Authority — National Unit against Organised Crime; [[sweden-police\|Swedish Police, Fraud Unit Stockholm]] |
| Ukraine | Prosecutor General's Office; Main Investigative Department; Cyber Department of National Police of Ukraine (85 Ukrainian officials in UA action) |
| Coordinating | [[eurojust\|Eurojust]] (JIT setup + funding; coordination centre; 13 coordination meetings; IAW + EIO + Letter-of-Request execution); [[europol-ec3\|Europol]] (analytical

title: Fraudulent Shopping Sites Takedown
text:
## Summary

Europol and German police from Hannover, with the Verden prosecution office and European partner countries, took down Manson Market and a network of fraudulent online shopping sites tied to phishing-driven stolen data. The investigation began in autumn 2022 and culminated in the seizure of 50+ servers, the arrest of 2 key suspects, and the recovery of over 200 TB of digital evidence. The operation involved cooperation from Austria, Czechia, Finland, the Netherlands, and Poland.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agencies:**
- German Hannover Police (Polizeidirektion Hannover)
- Verden Public Prosecution Office (Staatsanwaltschaft Verden)

**Coordinating Body:**
- Europol

**Participating Countries (6):**
Germany, Austria, Czechia, Finland, Netherlands, Poland

## Results

- **2 key suspects arrested**
- **50+ servers seized**
- **200+ TB** of digital evidence secured
- Fraudulent online shopping site network dismantled
- Connection to cybercrime marketplace exposed
- Prior Al Jazeera source linkage was removed because that article covers the Bali villa cybercrime raid, not this Manson Market action.

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Europol, 2024-12-05: Europol: Fraudulent Shopping Sites Tied to Cybercrime Marketplace Taken Offline.
- BleepingComputer, 2024-12-05: Police shuts down Manson cybercrime market, arrests key suspects.
- The Hacker News, 2024-12-05: Europol Shuts Down Manson Market Fraud Marketplace, Seizes 50 Servers.
- Help Net Security, 2024-12-05: Law enforcement shuts down Manson Market cybercrime marketplace.
- TechTarget, 2024-12-05: Police bust cybercrime marketplace, phishing network.

## Operational Timeline

- 2022: activity or investigation start.
- 2024-12-04: public announcement.
- 2024-12-04: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2024-06-28: public source coverage from Al Jazeera.
- 2024-12-05: public source coverage from BleepingComputer, Europol, Help Net Security, TechTarget.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: online fraud.
- The record is categorized as takedown with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- On 5 December 2024 Europol announced a coordinated takedown of a specialized cybercrime marketplace selling regionally-sorted stolen data with account-balance filtering, plus the phishing-driven fake-shopping-site infrastructure that fed it
- Cooperation involved six EU member states: Austria, Czechia, Finland, Germany, Netherlands, and Poland, coordinated through Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)
- Two suspects (aged 27 and 37) were arrested in Germany and Austria under European Arrest Warrants and placed in pretrial detention; infrastructure was dismantled across multiple jurisdictions
- Digital evidence seized: over 50 servers, more than 200 terabytes of data — one of the largest data-volume seizures in a 2024 cybercrime operation per the Europol release
- The investigation began in autumn 2022 after reports of scammers impersonating bank employees to extract customer credentials, illustrating ~2-year investigative cycles typical of Europol-led multi-jurisdiction cases
- The marketplace served 'thousands of users' and operated as a specialized downstream outlet for phishing-harvested data — filling the supply chain role between phishing infrastructure and stolen-data resellers
- Indonesian immigration officials raided a villa in Tabanan district, Bali on 26 June 2024, detaining 103 individuals (91 men, 12 women) suspected of cybercrime and visa/residency permit misuse
- Hundreds of mobile phones and electronic devices were seized during the raid; authorities indicated the group was running cybercrime ope

title: FGP East Flanders / Europol — Crime-as-a-Service Phishing-Panel Supplier Arrest (NL→BE Surrender, 2026)
title_ko: 벨기에 동플랑드르 연방사법경찰·Europol — Crime-as-a-Service 피싱 패널 공급자 검거 (네덜란드→벨기에 인도, 2026)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / sparse-source page
> This page is built from a single tier-1 primary source: the 22 April 2026 Police.be press release. Many operational details (precise charges, full list of victim-states, executing Dutch agency, specific surrender instrument) are not stated in the source and are flagged below as such. The page should be re-evaluated as additional primary sources (Belgian prosecutor releases, Europol press release if any, court documents, or follow-up Dutch [[netherlands-politie|Politie]] communications) become available.

## Summary

On **22 April 2026** the Belgian Federal Judicial Police of East Flanders (Federale Gerechtelijke Politie Oost-Vlaanderen, FGP Oost-Vlaanderen — a regional judicial division of [[belgium-federal-police|the Belgian Federal Police]]) announced the arrest of a 20-year-old suspect for supplying *phishingpanelen* (phishing panels) — Crime-as-a-Service infrastructure used by fraud gangs to run convincing fake-bank, fake-telecom and fake-identity-app campaigns. The Belgian investigation was directed by the Dendermonde investigating judge with the East Flanders Public Prosecutor's Office (parket van Oost-Vlaanderen) and conducted "in nauwe samenwerking met Europol" (in close cooperation with [[europol-ec3|Europol]]). The suspect was arrested in the Netherlands following a **26 January 2026** house search, and **surrendered to Belgium on 16 April 2026** where he was remanded in custody.

The press release reports panels appearing in "multiple ongoing investigations across several European countries" and confirms documented use in fraud against victims in Belgium, the Netherlands and "many other European countries"; no further countries are enumerated by the source.

## Background

The supplier-level disruption fits a 2025-2026 pattern of European law enforcement targeting the upstream Crime-as-a-Service (CaaS) layer of phishing rather than only the downstream fraud crews — see [[tycoon-2fa-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2026]], [[w3ll-phishing-kit-takedown-2026]] and [[gxc-team-googlexcoder-phishing-kits-takedown-2025]]. A *phishingpaneel* in this context is a back-end management interface that lets a buyer operate fake websites and craft convincing email/SMS lures impersonating banks, telecom operators, payment platforms or identity-verification apps; it intercepts victim credentials, codes and bank data for downstream fraud. The Belgian press release describes the suspect's distribution channel as "various online channels, including Telegram" and the proceeds as "primarily collected through virtual-currency payments."

## Participating Parties

**Countries (named in the source):**
- [[belgium|Belgium]] — investigation jurisdiction; suspect remanded by the Dendermonde investigating judge.
- [[netherlands|Netherlands]] — locus of the suspect's residence and the 26 January 2026 house search; the country from which the suspect was surrendered.

**Agencies (named in the source):**
- Federale Gerechtelijke Politie Oost-Vlaanderen (FGP Oost-Vlaanderen) — Federal Judicial Police, East Flanders division, the lead investigating police service. It is a regional judicial unit of [[belgium-federal-police|the Belgian Federal Police]]; no standalone wiki page exists for the East Flanders division so it is recorded here in prose.
- Parket van Oost-Vlaanderen (East Flanders Public Prosecutor's Office) — judicial direction of the investigation. Not a current wiki entity.
- [[europol-ec3|Europol]] — explicitly named as a cooperating partner ("in samenwerking met Europol", "in nauwe samenwerking met Europol").
- The Dutch executing agency for the 26 January 2026 house search and arrest is **not explicitly named** in the source. It is *likely* [[netherlands-politie|Politie Nederland]], but this is not stated.

## Legal Framework

- The Belgian investigation was conducted under Belgian criminal procedure, directed by the **onderzoeksrechter te Dendermonde** (investigating judge at Dendermonde) in co

title: FIN7 Cybercrime Group Takedown
title_ko: FIN7 Cybercrime Group Takedown (공식 작전명 미확인; alias: FIN7 Arrest)
text:
## Summary

The FBI and U.S. Department of Justice investigated and prosecuted the FIN7 cybercrime group, which since 2015 had used malware and phishing attacks to steal over 15 million credit card numbers from more than 100 U.S. companies in the restaurant, gaming, and hotel sectors. Key members of the group were arrested in 2018, and stolen data had been sold on dark web marketplaces. The investigation involved cooperation with law enforcement in the United Kingdom, Australia, and France.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agencies:**
- FBI (Seattle Field Office)
- U.S. Department of Justice
- U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington

**Participating Countries (4):**
United States, United Kingdom, Australia, France

## Results

- **3 key members arrested** in 2018
- **15 million+ credit card numbers** stolen from 100+ companies
- FIN7 cybercrime operations significantly disrupted
- Attacks on restaurants, gaming companies, and hotels addressed

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- FBI (Seattle Field Office), 2018-08-01: FBI: How Cyber Crime Group FIN7 Attacked and Stole Data from Hundreds of U.S. Companies.
- U.S. Attorney's Office, Western District of Washington, 2018-08-01: Three Members of Notorious International Cybercrime Group "Fin7" in Custody for Role in Attacking Over 100 U.S. Companies.
- U.S. Attorney's Office, Western District of Washington, 2021-04-16: High-level organizer of notorious hacking group FIN7 sentenced to ten years in prison for scheme that compromised tens of millions of debit and..
- U.S. Department of Justice, 2021-06-24: High-Level Member of Hacking Group Sentenced to Prison for Scheme that Compromised Tens of Millions of Debit and Credit Cards.
- CBS News, 2018-08-01: Feds arrest 3 alleged FIN7 hackers accused of stealing credit card data.

## Operational Timeline

- 2015: activity or investigation start.
- 2018: public announcement.
- 2018: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2018-08-01: public source coverage from CBS News, FBI (Seattle Field Office), U.S. Attorney's Office, Western District of Washington.
- 2021-04-16: public source coverage from U.S. Attorney's Office, Western District of Washington.
- 2021-06-24: public source coverage from U.S. Department of Justice.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: hacking, malware, dark web.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest and Indictment.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status completed.
- Related legal or operational records: Us V Hladyr Fin7.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- FBI Seattle Field Office published the story on or around 2018-08-01 describing the FIN7 takedown in which three Ukrainian nationals — Dmytro Fedorov, Fedir Hladyr, and Andrii Kopakov — were arrested and charged as high-ranking members of the FIN7 cybercrime group
- FIN7 had targeted more than 100 US companies since at least 2015, predominantly in the restaurant, gaming, and hospitality sectors
- Scale of data theft: >15 million customer payment card records stolen from >6,500 point-of-sale terminals at >3,600 business locations across 47 US states and DC
- Named victim chains publicly disclosed at time of FBI reporting: Chipotle Mexican Grill, Chili's, Arby's, Jason's Deli
- Methodology: spear-phishing emails with malicious Microsoft Word attachments (embedded malware); attackers often posed as customers or legitimate business contacts and made phone calls to legitimize the phishing request — social-engineering layered on top of technical intrusion
- International cooperation: Fedorov arrested in Poland (January 2018), Hladyr in Dresden/Germany (January 2018), Kopakov in Spain (June 2018); all three extradited to the US for prosecution in the Western District of Washington (Seattle)
- The Western District of Washington announced that three senior FIN7 members were in custody on charges tied to attacks on more than 100 U.S. companies.
- The release described arrests in Poland, Ger

title: Fiscalía Colombia Cryptoactivos Fraud Network Takedown — Medellín-Hub Network Defrauding Colombia/Chile/Peru (2021–2025)
title_ko: 콜롬비아 검찰 가짜 가상자산 투자 사기 네트워크 일제 단속 (2021–2025, 메데인 허브)
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> This page is published below the preferred normal-page source threshold (`source_count >= 5`) and is marked provisional. It is anchored on a single Tier-1 primary source — Fiscalía General de la Nación (Colombia) Boletín 57752 dated 2025-11-18. It will be promoted to a fully sourced standalone page if and when additional Tier-1 corroboration (Chile Fiscalía, Peru Fiscalía, AMERIPOL/INTERPOL, or Colombian Policía Nacional press releases naming the same operation) is ingested.

## Summary

On **18 November 2025**, the **Fiscalía General de la Nación de Colombia** announced (Boletín 57752) the dismantling of an organised criminal network that had operated **fraudulent cryptocurrency- and securities-investment platforms** from **2021 through October 2025**, defrauding victims in **[[colombia|Colombia]], [[chile|Chile]], and [[peru|Peru]]** of an aggregate **COP 71.000 millones (~USD 17 million)**.

The investigation was led by the **Dirección Especializada contra los Delitos Informáticos (DECDI)** of the Fiscalía. Fourteen alleged members were captured and presented to a *juez de control de garantías*. Three were ordered to detention in a *centro carcelario*; the remaining eleven were ordered to house arrest. None of the fourteen accepted the charges, which include **estafa por medios informáticos**, **hurtos por medios informáticos**, **violación de datos personales** (all three in *modalidad delito masa*), **concierto para delinquir**, **lavado de activos**, and **enriquecimiento ilícito**.

The alleged ringleader named in the bulletin is a **Mexican national, Alejandro Maximiliano Ronquillo Treviño (alias Daniel)**.

> [!warning] Primary-source scope (L19-strict)
> The Fiscalía bulletin names the **countries of victims** (Colombia, Chile, Peru) and the **foreign nationality of one suspect** (Mexico), but it does **not** name any foreign law-enforcement agency, MLAT request, AMERIPOL/INTERPOL channel, or extradition mechanism. Secondary press accounts (El Tiempo, Infobae, El Colombiano, La Veintitrés) report that the operation was named **"Operación Dominó"** and that an additional **Israeli national, Boaz Iaakok Derra (alias Jim)**, was the principal architect operating from luxury offices in El Poblado (Medellín). These claims are **NOT** in the Fiscalía primary source and are listed in `missing_fields` for separate Tier-1 verification before any wiki claim is built on them.

## Background

According to the Fiscalía bulletin:

- The criminal organisation captured the attention of victims through **attractive social-media advertising**.
- Targets were then **contacted by telephone** and convinced to transfer **USD 200–250** to enroll in the scheme and obtain supposed short-term gains.
- The deposited funds **were never invested**. The aparentes asesores ("apparent advisors") of the scheme maintained communication with victims, **showed fictitious dividend graphs**, told them to **increase their contributions**, and warned them that **they could not withdraw**.
- Victims were instructed to install on their devices remote-access tools known as **'Anydesk'** or **'Imperios'**, ostensibly so they could verify the progress of their investments. In reality, these tools allowed the criminal network to **access and take remote control** of victims' phones and computers, **seize personal credentials**, and **take over banking products**.
- The aggregate proceeds, between 2021 and October 2025, are estimated at **COP 71.000 millones** (≈ USD 17 million at end-2025 rates) drawn from victims in Colombia, Chile, and Peru.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party (per primary source) |
|---|---|
| Lead investigative authority | Fiscalía General de la Nación (Colombia) — Dirección Especializada contra los Delitos Informáticos (DECDI) |
| Country of investigation and prosecution | [[colombia\|Colombia]] |
| Countries of disclosed victim losses | [[colombia\|Colombia]], [[chile\|Chile]], [[peru\|Peru]] |
| Country

title: FluBot Android-malware takedown — Europol EC3 'day of action' (May–June 2022)
text:
# FluBot Android-malware takedown — Europol EC3 "day of action" (May–June 2022)

> [!info] Provisional record
> This page rests on two tier-1 publishers (fedpol/OAG primary 2022-06-07 + Europol parallel 2022-06-01). Both name eleven cooperating LE jurisdictions and the underlying technical action. It is below the preferred 5-source threshold (`source_count = 2`) and is filed as a provisional structural record. It will be promoted when additional national-LE primary releases (Politie, AFP, US Secret Service, Polisen, etc.) are individually ingested.

## Summary

In May 2022, the **[[netherlands-politie|Dutch Police (Politie)]]** destroyed the **FluBot** Android banking-malware infrastructure and deactivated the malware strain, on the final phase of an international "day of action" coordinated by **[[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]]**. **Eleven law-enforcement jurisdictions** participated, with named agencies for each. **[[switzerland-fedpol|fedpol]]** participated under the leadership of the **Office of the Attorney General of Switzerland (OAG / Bundesanwaltschaft)**, in close cooperation with the Swiss [[switzerland-ncsc|NCSC]], cantonal police forces, telecommunications providers and the digitalisation service provider Switch. The fedpol/OAG announcement was issued on **7 June 2022**; the parallel Europol announcement on **1 June 2022**.

FluBot was an aggressive Android-targeting banking trojan first spotted in December 2020, propagated via SMS messages disguised as parcel-tracking or voice-mail notifications. Once installed, it stole banking-app credentials, cryptocurrency-account credentials and SMS one-time codes, and disabled built-in security mechanisms. It self-propagated through each infected phone's contact list, "spreading like wildfire."

## Background

Per the **fedpol** release of 7 June 2022:

> "At the end of May, a large-scale operation led by Europol and the Dutch police and involving other law enforcement authorities succeeded in stopping the rapid spread of a type of malware known as «FluBot». The malware, which infects mobile phones with the Android operating system via text messages, such as those sent by SMS, has also caused considerable damage in Switzerland."

Per the parallel **Europol** release of 1 June 2022:

> "This technical achievement follows a complex investigation involving law enforcement authorities of Australia, Belgium, Finland, Hungary, Ireland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands and the United States, with the coordination of international activity carried out by Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)."

(Romania is named in the Europol release's per-country agency table — Romanian Police / Poliția Română — as a cooperating LE jurisdiction in addition to the ten countries enumerated in the body lead paragraph; the eleventh participating country is therefore Romania.)

## Participating Parties

| Country | Named LE agency (per Europol tier-1 release) | Wikilink |
|---|---|---|
| Australia | Australian Federal Police | [[australia-afp]] |
| Belgium | Federal Police (Federale Politie / Police Fédérale) | [[belgium-federal-police]] |
| Finland | National Bureau of Investigation (Poliisi) | [[finland-nbi]] |
| Hungary | National Bureau of Investigation (Nemzeti Nyomozó Iroda) | (no canonical wiki page; prose only) |
| Ireland | An Garda Síochána | [[ireland-garda]] |
| Netherlands | National Police (Politie) | [[netherlands-politie]] |
| Romania | Romanian Police (Poliția Română) | [[romania-police]] |
| Spain | National Police (Policia Nacional) | [[spanish-national-police]] |
| Sweden | Swedish Police Authority (Polisen) | [[sweden-police]] |
| Switzerland | Federal Office of Police (fedpol) | [[switzerland-fedpol]] |
| United States | United States Secret Service | [[us-secret-service]] |
| Coordination | European Cybercrime Centre (EC3); J-CAT hosted at Europol | [[europol-ec3]] |
| Swiss judicial leadership | Office of the Attorney General of Switzer

title: Franco-Israeli CEO Fraud Investigation
text:
## Summary

The Franco-Israeli CEO Fraud Investigation targeted a business email compromise and CEO-fraud network that hit France-based companies and laundered proceeds through a cross-border banking structure. Public reporting ties the operation to two key French victim events in late 2021 and to follow-on asset tracing across Portugal, Hungary, Croatia, Spain, China, and Israel.

## Background

The scheme relied on executive impersonation and fake-lawyer pretexts to pressure finance staff into making urgent confidential transfers. One French real-estate developer lost approximately EUR 38 million within days, making the case one of the largest publicly reported BEC losses in Europe.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead:** France (Police Nationale, Gendarmerie Nationale)
- **Coordination:** [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- **Israel:** Israel Police
- **Croatia:** Hrvatska Policija and the Croatian anti-money-laundering office
- **Hungary:** Budapesti Rendor-fokapitanysag
- **Portugal:** Policia Judiciaria
- **Spain:** Policia Nacional

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2021-12 | Two major French victim incidents begin to expose the network |
| 2022-01 | Investigators connect the French cases and expand the probe internationally |
| 2022-01 to 2023-01 | Five action days carried out in France and Israel |
| 2023-02-08 | Europol publicly announces the dismantling results |
| 2023-02-17 | Spain and Portugal publish participating-country operational details |

## Results and Impact

- **8 arrests**: 6 in France and 2 in Israel
- **8 searches**: conducted in France and Israel
- **Approx. EUR 5.5 million** seized or frozen across participating jurisdictions
- **Country breakdown preserved in public reporting**:
  - Portugal: about EUR 3 million
  - Hungary: about EUR 1.1 million
  - Croatia: about EUR 600,000
  - Spain: about EUR 400,000
  - Cryptocurrency: about EUR 350,000

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The public sources show a classic multijurisdictional financial-investigation model:

- Europol analytical support and coordination
- Parallel bank-account tracing across multiple EU jurisdictions
- National police action in France and Israel
- Participating-country asset-freeze and shell-company investigation in Portugal, Spain, Hungary, and Croatia

Portugal's official release is especially useful because it records four companies, 17 bank accounts, and three false identities used in the laundering infrastructure.

## Money Laundering Route

Public reporting describes the route broadly as:

- France-based victim transfers

## Korean Involvement

No direct Korean participation was identified in the available public record.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Public sources vary between "seized," "frozen," and "recovered" when describing the financial figures.
- The exact post-arrest judicial outcomes are not clearly documented in open sources.
- The public record does not fully disclose the legal process used between France and Israel.

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Open-source review through 2026-04-23 did not surface accessible court records tied to the arrests.

- Polícia Judiciária, 2023-02-17: OPERAÇÃO 'SEFRICIME 2.2'.
- Help Net Security, 2023-02-17: Authorities break up gang responsible for EUR 38 million CEO fraud.

- Recorded crime classification: BEC.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest and Asset Freeze.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status completed.

title: FRONTIER+ First Joint Cross-Border Anti-Scam Operation (2025)
text:
## Summary

Between **28 April and 28 May 2025**, the [[hong-kong-police|Hong Kong Police Force]] led the first joint enforcement operation conducted under the **Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform "FRONTIER+"**, working with police forces of [[malaysia|Malaysia]], the [[maldives|Maldives]], [[singapore|Singapore]], [[south-korea|South Korea]], [[thailand|Thailand]], and the Macao Special Administrative Region of the People's Republic of China.

The month-long operation resulted in **1,858 arrests** (ages 14–81), addressed **9,268 scam cases**, froze **32,607 bank accounts**, and intercepted approximately **US$20 million** of an aggregate victim-loss pool of **US$225 million**. Over **2,700 officers** were deployed across the seven jurisdictions. *Highly likely* (≥80%) the operation marks the first multi-jurisdictional outcome of FRONTIER+ since the platform's October 2024 formalisation.

## Background

FRONTIER+ — the **Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform** — was jointly established by various national/regional anti-scam centres in **October 2024**. Per the Hong Kong release of 2025-06-03, platform membership stands at 10 jurisdictions: Australia, Canada, Hong Kong SAR, Indonesia, Macao SAR, Malaysia, the Maldives, [[singapore|Singapore]], [[south-korea|South Korea]], and [[thailand|Thailand]].

> [!note] Platform-membership discrepancy
> The Singapore Police Force's *CAD Annual Report 2024* (publish date 2025-12; raw `2025-12-01_police-gov-sg_cad-report-2024-operation-first-light.md`) describes FRONTIER+ formalisation on **22 October 2024** and lists **11** member jurisdictions, additionally naming **Brunei**. Both figures are taken from tier-1 primary sources; the discrepancy is preserved as published.

The platform's core functions are real-time intelligence exchange, periodic joint operations, and replication of anti-scam centre models across non-member jurisdictions. The April–May 2025 operation was its **first formal joint enforcement deployment**.

## Participating Parties

**Participating jurisdictions in this operation (7):** Hong Kong SAR, Macao SAR, Malaysia, the Maldives, Singapore, South Korea, Thailand.

**Lead participating agencies:**

- [[hong-kong-police|Hong Kong Police Force — Commercial Crime Bureau (Anti-Deception Coordination Centre)]] — operation lead.
- Macao Judiciary Police — Anti-Fraud Co-ordination Centre (no dedicated wiki page yet; original-language designation: *Polícia Judiciária*).
- [[royal-malaysia-police|Royal Malaysia Police (Polis Diraja Malaysia)]].
- Maldives Police Service (no dedicated wiki page yet).
- [[singapore-police|Singapore Police Force — Anti-Scam Command (within the Commercial Affairs Department)]].
- [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] — National Counter Scam Center / equivalent counter-fraud division.
- [[thailand-royal-police|Royal Thai Police — Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau]].

## Legal Framework

The operation rests on **each jurisdiction's domestic anti-fraud, cybercrime, and money-laundering powers**, executed in parallel rather than under a single multilateral instrument. There is no indication in the primary source that mutual-legal-assistance treaties were the principal vehicle; the operation instead relied on **police-to-police real-time intelligence exchange** through FRONTIER+'s sister-centre architecture (cf. [[informal-cooperation]]). Account freezes and asset interception in each jurisdiction were executed under domestic financial-investigation powers.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 2024-10 | FRONTIER+ Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform jointly established by anti-scam centres of multiple jurisdictions (HK release: October 2024; SPF CAD: 22 October 2024). |
| 2025-04-28 | First FRONTIER+ joint operation begins. |
| 2025-05-28 | Operation concludes after one month. |
| 2025-06-03 | HKSAR Government Information Services issues press release P2025060300475 (HKT 16:04) announcing aggregate results, wit

title: GIGN–FBI Saint-Martin Cryptocurrency Hacker Arrest (France–United States, 2026)
text:
## Summary

On **4 March 2026**, around the evening hours, a joint operation conducted by the **Antenne GIGN de Guadeloupe** (about ten GIGN operators), three investigators from the **Section de recherches (S.R.) de Saint-Martin / Saint-Barthélemy**, and three agents of the **U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)** arrested a 24-year-old U.S. national in a luxury rented villa on the French portion of the island of **Saint-Martin** (Caribbean overseas collectivity of France). The suspect is described in the Gendarmerie nationale's official press release as a "hacker" suspected of having stolen the equivalent of approximately **USD 46 million in cryptocurrency** before leaving the United States.

The operation was executed on the basis of an **international arrest warrant** (mandat d'arrêt international). The full reported stolen amount — USD 46 million in cryptocurrency — was recovered at the scene, alongside high-end IT equipment, cryptocurrency-wallet credentials, multiple phones, approximately EUR 250,000 in cash, and a loaded Glock pistol. The suspect was placed in retenue judiciaire and then in pre-trial detention pending the procédure d'extradition to the United States.

The press release was published on **6 March 2026** by the Gendarmerie nationale on its institutional website, authored by commandant Céline Morin.

## Background

Approximately one month before the action day — i.e., **early February 2026** — the **Commandement de la gendarmerie de Saint-Barthélemy et Saint-Martin (COMGEND)** was contacted by the FBI. U.S. authorities reported that one of their nationals, identified as a "target principale" (principal target), was believed to have taken refuge on the island of Saint-Martin after fleeing the United States.

The Section de recherches (S.R.) de Saint-Martin opened the local investigation under the supervision of the **parquet général de Guadeloupe**. The international leg was driven by the S.R.'s dedicated "cellule internationale" in direct liaison with the FBI. Investigators rapidly confirmed the suspect's presence on the island and located him in a rented luxury villa. FBI agents were then dispatched to Saint-Martin and an international arrest warrant was issued.

The Gendarmerie press release describes the suspect's profile as that of an individual potentially armed and willing to use a weapon, with a personal bodyguard, and notes that the **COMGEND** of Saint-Barthélemy and Saint-Martin is equipped only with a peloton d'intervention de gendarmerie mobile — not a specialised intervention unit. For this reason the **Antenne GIGN de Guadeloupe** was tasked with the action, with logistical support from gendarmerie air assets in Martinique to transport specialised equipment.

## Participating Parties

**France**

- **Gendarmerie nationale** — issuing institution of the public statement.
- **Antenne GIGN de Guadeloupe** — specialised intervention unit that executed the arrest. Approximately ten military operators.
- **Section de recherches (S.R.) de Saint-Martin / Saint-Barthélemy** — judicial-police investigative unit; three investigators present on the action day; coordinator of the international cooperation through its "cellule internationale".
- **Commandement de la gendarmerie de Saint-Barthélemy et Saint-Martin (COMGEND)** — territorial gendarmerie command that received the initial FBI request.
- **Forces aériennes de la gendarmerie de la Martinique** — air-mobility support for moving specialised intervention equipment.
- **Parquet général de Guadeloupe** — supervising prosecuting authority.

**United States**

- **Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)** — originator of the cooperation request; three FBI agents physically present during the action day in Saint-Martin.

**Local Caribbean partners (named in general terms only)**

- The press release expressly names "les Britanniques et les Hollandais" — the British and the Dutch — as local partners of the COMGEND in this operational environment. No s

title: Global Airport Action Day
title_ko: Global Airport Action Day (항공권 사기 국제 공조 단속)
text:
## Summary

The Global Airport Action Day, conducted from October 10-15, 2016, was an international operation coordinated by INTERPOL, Europol, Ameripol, and Frontex across 189 airports in 43 countries. The operation targeted airline ticket fraud perpetrated using stolen or hacked payment card information, detaining 193 suspects. The investigation revealed connections between airline ticket fraud and broader criminal activities including illegal immigration, human trafficking, drug trafficking, cybercrime, and terrorism support.

## Participating Parties

**Coordinating Bodies:**
- INTERPOL
- Europol
- Ameripol
- Frontex

**Partners:**
- IATA (International Air Transport Association)
- Perseuss
- Airlines, online travel agencies, payment card issuers
- Canadian and US law enforcement agencies
- Prior Hackread linkage was removed because that source covers Carding Action 2020, not Global Airport Action Day.

**Participating Countries:**
43 countries (specific country names not disclosed, but include major nations in the US, Canada, EU, Latin America, Asia, and Middle East)

## Results

- **193 suspects** detained at 189 airports in 43 countries
- Links to illegal immigration, human trafficking, drug trafficking, cybercrime, and terrorism identified
- Airline ticket fraud networks disrupted

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- INTERPOL, 2016-10-20: Global Airport Action Day: 40 countries nab 193 suspects in cybercrime sting.

- Il Metropolitano, 2016-10-19: Global action against airline fraudsters: 193 detained.

## Operational Timeline

- 2016-10-10: activity or investigation start.
- 2016-10-15: public announcement.
- 2016-10-15: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2016-10-19: public source coverage from Help Net Security, INTERPOL, Il Metropolitano.
- 2016-10-20: public source coverage from INTERPOL.
- 2017-01-01: public source coverage from Europol.
- 2020-11-29: public source coverage from Hackread.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: online fraud and dark web.
- The record is categorized as arrest-sweep with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- The Global Airport Action Day, conducted from October 10-15, 2016, was an international operation coordinated by INTERPOL, Europol, Ameripol, and Frontex across 189 airports in 43 countries.
- The operation targeted airline ticket fraud perpetrated using stolen or hacked payment card information, detaining 193 suspects.
- The investigation revealed connections between airline ticket fraud and broader criminal activities including illegal immigration, human trafficking, drug trafficking, cybercrime, and terrorism support.
- The Hackread article, published 29 November 2020, reports on **Carding Action 2020**, a 3-month Europol-led operation against dark-web card-trading forums — NOT on the Global Airport Action Day despite the wiki's operation link
- Carding Action 2020 was led by Europol, supported by cybersecurity firm Group-IB, with operational participation from law enforcement in Hungary, the United Kingdom, and Italy
- Estimated impact: prevented losses of approximately US$48 million / EUR 40 million; ~90,000 stolen credit-card records assessed during the investigation
- Operational focus: "card shops' (specialized sites selling stolen card data) and dark-web marketplaces — not airport-based operations or travel fraud enforcement

title: GozNym Malware Network Dismantling
text:
## Summary

The GozNym action was a coordinated multinational malware case, not a one-paragraph takedown. DOJ described the network as a transnational organized cybercrime group that used GozNym malware to capture online-banking credentials, access victim bank accounts, and launder stolen funds through U.S. and foreign beneficiary accounts. The estimated intended loss was **USD 100 million**, with tens of thousands of infected computers worldwide and a victim base concentrated in the United States and Europe.

The operational significance is the prosecution model. The United States unsealed charges against ten members of the network in the Western District of Pennsylvania, while partner jurisdictions pursued parallel domestic cases or supplied evidence against defendants who could not all be brought into U.S. custody.

## Participating Parties

### Coordinating and Judicial Bodies
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- [[eurojust|Eurojust]]
- U.S. Attorney's Office for the Western District of Pennsylvania
- [[office-of-international-affairs|DOJ Office of International Affairs]]

### Law-Enforcement and Prosecutorial Participants
- [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Pittsburgh Field Office]]
- [[us-secret-service|U.S. Secret Service]]
- Georgia Prosecutor General and Ministry of Internal Affairs
- Ukraine Prosecutor General and National Police
- Moldova Prosecutor General and General Police Inspectorate
- Public Prosecutor's Office Verden and Lueneburg Police, Germany
- Bulgaria Supreme Prosecutor's Office of Cassation and General Directorate for Combatting Organized Crime
- [[ncfta|National Cyber-Forensics and Training Alliance]]
- [[shadowserver|Shadowserver Foundation]]

### Participating Countries
The core participating jurisdictions were [[united-states|United States]], [[bulgaria|Bulgaria]], [[germany|Germany]], [[georgia|Georgia]], [[moldova|Moldova]], and [[ukraine|Ukraine]]. Russia appears mainly as a defendant-residence and fugitive jurisdiction; it is not listed as a cooperating participant in the current source set.

## Cooperation Architecture

The case combined several cooperation channels. Bulgarian authorities searched and arrested Krasimir Nikolov at the request of the United States, then extradited him to Pittsburgh in December 2016. The DOJ Office of International Affairs coordinated foreign searches, arrests, extradition requests, and evidence-sharing through Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty requests.

For defendants who could not be extradited, the model shifted to evidence sharing and parallel domestic prosecution. DOJ says evidence came from coordinated searches in Georgia, Ukraine, Moldova, and Bulgaria, plus evidence shared by U.S. and German investigations. Georgia prosecuted Alexander Konovolov and Marat Kazandjian domestically; Ukraine pursued Gennady Kapkanov; Moldova pursued Eduard Malanici and associates.

## Defendant and Role Map

| Defendant / actor | Jurisdictional status | Alleged role |
|---|---|---|
| Alexander Konovolov | Prosecuted in Georgia | Organizer who controlled more than 41,000 infected victim computers |
| Marat Kazandjian | Prosecuted in Georgia | Technical administrator and primary assistant |
| Krasimir Nikolov | Extradited from Bulgaria to the United States; guilty plea entered in April 2019 | Account-takeover specialist / casher |
| Gennady Kapkanov | Prosecuted in Ukraine | Avalanche bulletproof-hosting administrator tied to GozNym infrastructure |
| Eduard Malanici | Prosecuted in Moldova | Crypting-service provider used to evade antivirus detection |
| Farkhad Manokhin | Arrested in Sri Lanka at U.S. request, then absconded to Russia | Cash-out / drop-master role |
| Five Russian nationals | Fugitives in the DOJ record | Malware development, spam distribution, cashing, and cash-out functions |

## Evidence and Infrastructure

The indictment described a cybercrime-as-a-service structure recruited from Russian-language criminal forums. Members supplied malware development, spam distribution,

title: Hellenic Police DAOE Cryptocurrency-Investment Ponzi Takedown (2026)
text:
# Hellenic Police DAOE Cryptocurrency-Investment Ponzi Takedown (2026)

> [!info] Provisional source-count
> source_count is currently 1 (the controlling Hellenic Police press release). The operation is preserved as a fully fact-bearing page rather than a stub because the single tier-1 source is comprehensive and authoritatively names the cooperating jurisdictions and mechanisms; secondary independent media confirmation is recorded in the source-summary cross-reference table for traceability without inflating the source count.

## Summary

On the morning of **2026-03-05**, the Hellenic Police **Department for the Confrontation of Organised Crime of Crete (Τ.Α.Ο.Ε.Κ. Κρήτης)**, operating within the **Directorate for the Confrontation of Organised Crime (Δ.Α.Ο.Ε.)**, dismantled a transnational multi-member criminal organisation that had operated a cryptocurrency-investment Ponzi network since at least 2019. The synchronised action covered Crete (Rethymno, Heraklion), Attica, and Igoumenitsa (Thesprotia). Twelve members of the organisation were arrested; six additional persons were named in the case file, of whom two are members of the organisation.

Charges filed include criminal organisation, aggravated fraud, money laundering, provision of crypto-asset services without certification, concealment of assets to obtain bankruptcy, and weapons-law offences. The Hellenic Police announcement of 2026-03-06 reports total illicit gain above **€14.5 million**, **73 identified victims** with at least **€760,900** in confirmed direct losses, and **digital-currency circulation above USD 2,000,000** at current value identified on seized digital evidence.

International cooperation is the operation's defining attribute. The financial investigation was extended into six foreign jurisdictions through two distinct cooperation channels: **European Investigation Orders** (Directive 2014/41/EU) sent to **France, Germany and Malta**, and **Mutual Legal Assistance** requests sent to the **United Kingdom, Ireland and Denmark**. A parallel anti-money-laundering investigation, conducted with the **Greek National Anti-Money-Laundering Authority**, established that two members of the organisation are linked to **four offshore companies in Bulgaria**.

## Background

The case began with intelligence that was forwarded to the competent prosecutorial authority, which ordered a preliminary inquiry. The multi-month investigation by Τ.Α.Ο.Ε.Κ. Κρήτης established that the organisation had built a pyramidal (Ponzi) structure in which money paid in by new "investors" was used to pay fictitious returns to earlier participants, creating a false appearance of viability and profitability. Members exploited victims' limited understanding of cryptocurrencies, presenting in-ecosystem tokens with no real exchange value or external utility as supposedly reliable investment options, and used virtual e-wallets, locked balances, and controlled crypto exchanges to conceal money flows.

The internal organisation was strictly hierarchical: a four-person leadership nucleus that set strategy and ran public events, three senior recruiters who delivered investment seminars and pitched new "investment packages", seven operational members who recruited victims through personal and professional networks, and additional supporting members earning referral and bonus commissions. Members held titles drawn from a 21-rank hierarchy including "ambassador", "leader", and "founder's council", and signed private confidentiality agreements. The organisation also ran a 90-day "academy" internal training programme. When platforms collapsed, the network rebranded under different commercial names and migrated victims to new schemes.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead investigative unit | Hellenic Police Department for the Confrontation of Organised Crime of Crete (Τ.Α.Ο.Ε.Κ. Κρήτης), within the Directorate for the Confrontation of Organised Crime (Δ.Α.Ο.Ε.) |
| Greek operat

title: GXC Team / GoogleXcoder Phishing-Kit CaaS Takedown (Spain–Brazil, 2025-10-08)
title_ko: GXC Team / GoogleXcoder 피싱 키트 CaaS 단속 (스페인-브라질, 2025-10-08)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-10-08** the **[[spain-guardia-civil|Guardia Civil (Spain)]]** announced the dismantling of the **GXC Team** Crime-as-a-Service (CaaS) phishing operation and the arrest of its main developer, a 25-year-old **Brazilian national** operating under the alias **"GoogleXcoder"**, detained in **San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria)**. The investigation was led by the **Departamento contra el Cibercrimen de la Unidad Central Operativa (UCO)** of the Guardia Civil, under the judicial authority of the **Juzgado de Instrucción número 1 de San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria)**, with international cooperation from the **Brazilian Federal Police (Policía Federal de Brasil)** and private-sector technical support from **[[group-ib|Group-IB]]**.

The action concluded with the suspect's arrest in Cantabria and **six coordinated entries-and-searches** across Valladolid, Zaragoza, Barcelona, Palma de Mallorca, San Fernando and La Línea de la Concepción, with seizure of phishing-kit code, customer chats, and recovery of victim funds from multiple digital platforms. **Six additional individuals** using the CaaS service were identified through more than one year of device forensics and cryptocurrency-transaction reconstruction.

## Background

From **2023** onwards Spain experienced a wave of phishing campaigns impersonating major Spanish banking entities and public-administration bodies, generating thousands of victim reports and millions of euros in losses. The Guardia Civil's UCO Cybercrime Department opened an investigation aimed not only at the operator crews but at the **"brain"** designing the toolkits used by multiple criminal groups.

Investigators traced the supply chain to a developer known as **"GoogleXcoder"** running a **Crime-as-a-Service (CaaS)** business model. The developer designed and sold phishing kits capable of cloning the websites of Spanish banks and government bodies, with customisation, technical support and updates included in the subscription — a professionalised criminal structure.

Operator crews ("phishers") contacted GoogleXcoder via the Telegram messaging application, paid hundreds of euros per day for access to the kits, and used them at industrial scale: **a single day's output could include dozens of impersonated entities, thousands of deceived victims and millions of euros stolen.** One of the operator-side Telegram groups was openly named **"Robarle todo a las abuelas"** ("Steal everything from grandmothers").

The individual behind the GoogleXcoder pseudonym was unknown to law enforcement at both national and international level prior to this investigation. Locating him required complex operational tracking: he moved repeatedly between residences in different Spanish provinces, used phone lines and prepaid payment cards in fraudulent identities, and lived as a **"digital nomad"** with his family.

## Participating Parties

| Country / Sector | Authority / Partner |
|---|---|
| Spain (lead) | [[spain-guardia-civil\|Guardia Civil]] — Departamento contra el Cibercrimen de la Unidad Central Operativa (UCO) |
| Spain (judicial) | Juzgado de Instrucción número 1 de San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria) |
| Brazil | [[brazil-ministry-of-justice-public-security\|Brazil Ministry of Justice and Public Security]] — Policía Federal de Brasil (Federal Police of Brazil) |
| Private — technical | [[group-ib\|Group-IB]] |

## Legal Framework

- **Spanish criminal-procedural authority** of the Juzgado de Instrucción número 1 de San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria) — judicial authorisation for the entries and searches in six Spanish localities and for the arrest in Cantabria.
- **Investigative authority** of the Departamento contra el Cibercrimen de la Unidad Central Operativa (UCO) of the Guardia Civil under Spanish national criminal procedure.
- **Bilateral police-to-police cooperation** with the Brazilian Federal Police (Policía Federal de Brasil) — informal investigative cooperation for identific

title: Hakkari-Centred Illegal Betting and Qualified Fraud Network — 18-Province Sweep + Sarajevo Red Notice Arrest (Türkiye–Bosnia and Herzegovina, 2026)
title_ko: 하카리 중심 불법 베팅·자격사기 조직 단속 — 18개 도(道) 동시 작전 + 사라예보 적색수배 검거 (튀르키예–보스니아헤르체고비나, 2026)
text:
## Summary

On 31 March 2026 the Republic of Türkiye's Ministry of Interior, via the official state news agency Anadolu Ajansı, announced the conclusion of a four-month investigation centred in Hakkari province against a transnational organised criminal group conducting illegal sports betting and qualified fraud (nitelikli dolandırıcılık) through information-technology systems. The sweep produced **52 detentions in Türkiye across 18 provinces** and **1 Red-Notice apprehension in Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina** — the latter described as the technical-infrastructure architect of the network's back-office. The Ministry estimates approximately **20 billion Turkish lira** in cumulative money traffic associated with the network and describes this as the highest transaction volume identified to date in any Turkish cyber-crime / fraud investigation.

The international structure of the organisation was publicly disclosed by the Ministry as extending to **server and management layers located in Bosnia and Herzegovina**, making BiH not only the apprehension venue but also the hosting jurisdiction of the criminal infrastructure.

## Background

The investigation was driven from a provincial axis rather than from a centralised national-level prosecutorial structure. The lead authorities were:

- **Yüksekova Cumhuriyet Başsavcılığı** (Yüksekova Chief Public Prosecutor's Office, in Hakkari province) — coordinating prosecutor.
- **Hakkari Emniyet Müdürlüğü** (Hakkari Provincial Police Directorate) — lead investigative police unit, including its Cyber Crimes Combat Branch.

Approximately four months of physical and technical surveillance preceded the action day. Cyber-patrol and technical-analysis work identified a central back-office that managed high-volume financial flows over illegal betting platforms, used a 24/7 panel system, and converted revenues obtained through third-party accounts into crypto-assets — a layering pattern that the Ministry's statement attributes specifically to this organisation.

The 20 billion TL figure refers to **money traffic / illegal financial ecosystem layering**, not to net victim loss. The Ministry's statement does not enumerate distinct victims or aggregate victim losses.

## Participating Parties

**Türkiye**

- T.C. İçişleri Bakanlığı (Ministry of Interior of the Republic of Türkiye) — issuing authority of the public statement.
- Hakkari Emniyet Müdürlüğü — provincial police directorate; lead investigative unit.
- Yüksekova Cumhuriyet Başsavcılığı (Hakkari) — coordinating prosecutor; investigation continues under this office.
- Interpol Daire Başkanlığı (Interpol Department, Turkish General Directorate of Security) — channelled the Red Notice that produced the Sarajevo apprehension.

**Bosnia and Herzegovina**

- The Ministry's statement does not name the BiH counterpart agency that executed the Sarajevo arrest. Federalna policija (FUP), MUP Republika Srpska, SIPA, and Sarajevo cantonal police are all institutionally plausible executing authorities, but the AA wire credits the apprehension only to the Red Notice and to a "simultaneous operation."

**International coordinating body**

- [[interpol]] — Red Notice issued through the Turkish national bureau (Interpol Daire Başkanlığı). Interpol channels the notice to all member states, including the BiH National Central Bureau, but the statement does not specify which BiH NCB action triggered the arrest.

## Legal Framework

The press release does not enumerate statutes by article, but the substantive offences identified in the Ministry's statement and the structural elements of the case map to:

- **Türkiye Ceza Kanunu (TCK) Article 158** — qualified fraud (nitelikli dolandırıcılık), including the subsection covering fraud committed via information-technology systems.
- **TCK Article 220** — establishing or being a member of an organisation for the purpose of committing crime (örgüt kurma / örgüte üye olma).
- **TCK Article 282** — laundering of assets de

title: Hive Ransomware Infrastructure Takedown
text:
## Summary

From July 2022 to January 2023, the FBI, with support from 13 countries and Europol, infiltrated and dismantled the Hive ransomware group's server infrastructure. The FBI had covertly accessed Hive's networks, providing over 1,300 decryption keys to victims globally and preventing more than $100 million in ransom payments. Hive had targeted over 1,500 victims across 80+ countries, including hospitals, school districts, financial firms, and critical infrastructure.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agencies:**
- FBI
- U.S. Department of Justice

**Coordinating Body:**
- Europol

**Participating Countries (13):**
United States, Germany, Netherlands, United Kingdom, Canada, France, Romania, Lithuania, Sweden, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Ireland

**Key Agencies:**
- German Federal Criminal Police (BKA)
- Dutch National Police
- UK National Crime Agency (NCA)
- U.S. Secret Service

## Results

- **1,300+ decryption keys** provided to victims
- **$100 million+** in ransom payments prevented
- **1,500+ victims** in 80+ countries addressed
- Hive ransomware infrastructure completely dismantled

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Europol, 2023-01-26: Europol: Cybercriminals Stung as Hive Infrastructure Shut Down.
- CBS News, 2025-02-11: CBS News: DOJ Charges Two Russians with Operating Phobos Ransomware Group.
- US DOJ USAO, 2024-04-11: SoCal Man Arrested on Federal Charges Alleging He Schemed to Advertise and Sell ‘Hive’ Computer Intrusion Malware.
- TechCrunch, 2023-01-26: US announces it seized Hive ransomware gang's leak sites and decryption keys.
- DW, 2023-01-26: US, German authorities block 'Hive' ransomware website.

## Operational Timeline

- 2022-07: activity or investigation start.
- 2023-01-26: public announcement.
- 2023-01-26: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2023-01-26: public source coverage from DW, Europol, TechCrunch.
- 2024-04-11: public source coverage from US DOJ USAO.
- 2025-02-11: public source coverage from CBS News.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: malware.
- The record is categorized as takedown with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- Hive ransomware infrastructure taken down on 26 January 2023 by a coordinated operation involving 13 countries and Europol, led by the FBI with the US Secret Service, German BKA, and Dutch National Police as principal partners
- Hive had targeted 1,500+ victim companies in 80+ countries since June 2021, extracting approximately EUR 100 million in ransom payments
- Hive operated on a ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) affiliate model: affiliates received 80% of ransoms, developers 20% — standard RaaS economics
- FBI covertly infiltrated Hive's infrastructure *without detection* for several months prior to the public takedown, enabling law enforcement to identify decryption keys and share them with victims in real time
- Decryption keys distributed to victims during the covert phase prevented an estimated USD 130 million (~EUR 120 million) in additional ransom payments
- Participating countries: United States, Canada, France, Germany, Ireland, Lithuania, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Romania, Spain, Sweden, United Kingdom (13 total).
- Initial takedown resulted in no physical arrests — the operation focused on infrastructure disruption and victim decryption
- Follow-on arrest: Mikhail Matveev ('Wazawaka'), indicted by DOJ in May 2023 as a Hive affiliate, remains in Russia — illustrating the persistent non-extradition challenge

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_END -->

<!-- CANONICAL_ASSESSMENT_START -->

## Canonical Operation Assessment

This page is retained as a canonical operation because it describe

title: Hungary-Romania Swatting and Doxing Discord Joint Operation (2026)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source page
> This operation page is built from a single tier-1 primary source (the Hungarian National Police press release of 2026-01-26). The Romanian counterpart agency is named only generically in the source ("Romanian partner authority" / "Romanian police") — agency-level identification is pending a Romanian-side primary source. The page is published below the preferred publication threshold (source_count >= 5) because the case provides a structurally novel HU-RO bilateral cybercrime cooperation pattern with explicit cross-border participation that is otherwise absent from the wiki.

## Summary

A six-month Hungarian-Romanian joint investigation into a Discord-based swatting and doxing campaign culminated in a coordinated four-location raid on **2026-01-20**. The Hungarian National Bureau of Investigation (Készenléti Rendőrség Nemzeti Nyomozó Iroda — KR NNI) Cybercrime Combatting Department, the Hungarian Counter Terrorism Centre (Terrorelhárítási Központ — TEK), the Nagykanizsa Police Headquarters, and Romanian police acted simultaneously at addresses in Budapest, Nyíregyháza, Kisvárda (all Hungary), and Bihor County (Romania). Four young suspects — three Hungarian (ages 16, 18, 20) and one Romanian (age 17) — were identified and questioned. The 17-year-old Romanian suspect faces the most serious charges, including terrorcselekmény (terrorist act). All four are at liberty pending proceedings; KR NNI announced 2026-01-26 that it will consolidate the proceedings.

## Background

Hungarian police authorities began receiving an unusual cluster of severe threat reports in mid-July 2025: callers threatened to bomb educational and religious institutions, residential buildings, and police units; named individuals were threatened with death. Each call required a substantial police mobilisation. Initial criminal proceedings were opened against the persons whose phone numbers had been used to place the calls. Subsequent investigation revealed that those phone subscribers were not the actual perpetrators — they were victims whose phone numbers had been spoofed or whose identities had been impersonated after their personal data had been harvested in Discord disputes. The pattern was identified as swatting (false police report against the victim) layered on doxing (public release of victim personal data).

Reconnaissance by the KR NNI Cybercrime Combatting Department and the Nagykanizsa Police Headquarters, supported by the Counter Terrorism Centre (TEK), eventually identified four perpetrators — three of them in Hungary and one in Romania. Romanian police were brought into the investigation once the Romanian element was confirmed.

## Participating Parties

**Hungary (lead):**
- Készenléti Rendőrség Nemzeti Nyomozó Iroda (KR NNI), Kiberbűnözés Elleni Főosztály — National Bureau of Investigation, Cybercrime Combatting Department, the operational lead.
- Terrorelhárítási Központ (TEK) — Counter Terrorism Centre, supplied reconnaissance and on-site support at all Hungarian sites; TEK involvement reflects classification of bomb-threat swatting as terrorism-adjacent under Hungarian law.
- Nagykanizsai Rendőrkapitányság — Nagykanizsa Police Headquarters; conducted the Kisvárda raid and supported reconnaissance.
- Kisvárdai Rendőrkapitányság — Kisvárda Police Headquarters; took custody of the 16-year-old Hungarian suspect for questioning.

**Romania (cooperating):**
- Romanian partner authority / Romanian police ([[romanian-police|Romanian Police]] — generic identification in source). Conducted the Bihor County search alongside two visiting KR NNI cyber investigators.

**Suspects:**
| # | Age | Residence | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 17 | Bihor County, Romania | Questioned by Romanian authority in the KR NNI proceeding; charged with terrorist act, threatening with public danger, multiple false accusation counts, misuse of personal data. At liberty. |
| 2 | 16 | Kisvárda, Hungary | Questioned at Kisvárda P

title: Imminent Monitor RAT Takedown
text:
## Summary

In November-December 2019, [[europol-ec3|Europol]] coordinated an international operation that dismantled the infrastructure of **Imminent Monitor**, a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) sold commercially as a legitimate tool but widely used for cybercrime. The operation involved law enforcement from 8 countries and resulted in **13 arrests** and the seizure of **430 devices**. Investigators identified **14,500 buyers** of the RAT across **124 countries**.

The operation was notable for targeting a commodity malware tool and its user base, rather than just a single criminal group, demonstrating law enforcement's expanding approach to cybercrime enforcement.

## Background

Imminent Monitor was a Remote Access Trojan marketed as a legitimate remote administration tool but widely used for malicious purposes including espionage, data theft, and surveillance. The tool was sold through a dedicated website and allowed buyers to remotely control victims' computers, access files, record keystrokes, and activate webcams. The developers' operational security failures contributed to their identification.

## Participating Parties

### Coordinating Bodies
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- [[eurojust|Eurojust]]

### Participating Countries (8)
- [[australia|Australia]]
- [[colombia|Colombia]]
- [[czech-republic|Czech Republic]]
- [[netherlands|Netherlands]]
- [[poland|Poland]]
- [[spain|Spain]]
- [[sweden|Sweden]]
- [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom]]

### Participating Agencies
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- [[eurojust|Eurojust]]
- [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police]]

## Legal Framework

Specific legal instruments have not been detailed in available public sources. The coordination through Europol and Eurojust suggests use of EU cooperation mechanisms.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Pre-2019 | Investigation into Imminent Monitor RAT |
| 2019-11-29 | First announcements of operation |
| 2019-12-02 | Europol formal announcement |

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Count |
|--------|-------|
| Arrests | 13 |
| Devices seized | 430 |
| Buyers identified | 14,500 |
| Countries with buyers | 124 |
| Countries participating | 8 |

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

Details not fully documented in public sources. Europol and Eurojust coordination suggests formal EU cooperation channels.

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

No Korean involvement identified. However, given the global user base (124 countries), Korean users may have been among the 14,500 identified buyers.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What were the specific OpSec mistakes that led to the developers' identification?
- How many of the 14,500 buyers were ultimately investigated?
- What was the legal basis for prosecuting buyers of a tool marketed as legitimate?
- Were there follow-up actions against the buyer base?

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- The Hacker News, 2019-11-29: Europol Imminent Monitor RAT.
- WeLiveSecurity, 2019-12-03: Notorious RAT spy tool global operation.
- PortSwigger, 2019-12-01: Imminent Monitor spyware operation undone by opsec mistakes.
- ASPI, 2019-12-01: National Security Wrap 186.
- Wired-Gov, 2019-12-02: Cybercrime site selling hacking tool taken down.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- In November-December 2019, Europol coordinated an international operation that dismantled the infrastructure of **Imminent Monitor**, a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) sold commercially as a legitimate tool but widely used for cybercrime.
- The operation involved law enforcement from 8 countries and resulted in **13 arrests** and the seizure of **430 devices**.
- Investigators identified **14,500 buyers**

title: India CBI Noida Tech-Support Scam Takedown (CBI-NCA-FBI-Microsoft 4-party action, 2025-07-07)
title_ko: 인도 CBI 노이다 기술지원 사기 단속 (CBI-NCA-FBI-Microsoft 4자 협력, 2025-07-07)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-07-07** India's **Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)** raided a fraud call-centre based in **Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India**, arresting two individuals. The action followed approximately **18 months of collaboration** between the CBI, the **UK National Crime Agency (NCA)**, the **US FBI**, and **Microsoft** (Digital Crimes Unit) — who jointly identified the organised crime group, analysed data, and targeted complex IT infrastructure used by the fraudsters. **More than 100 UK victims** had been contacted by the call-centre, and UK losses are estimated at over **£390,000**; US citizens were also targeted by the same call-centre.

## Background

The investigation commenced in **early 2024** after **NCA International Liaison Officers in the USA** received intelligence from **Microsoft**, which was then cross-compared with **City of London Police Action Fraud Reports**. The fraudsters posed as **Microsoft employees**, contacting victims via **browser pop-ups** falsely claiming the victim's device was infected or had been hacked and offering paid "software solutions" for an attack that had never occurred. To disguise their identity, the fraudsters used **spoofed phone numbers** and **Voice Over Internet Protocol (VoIP)** to route calls through multiple servers in several countries. The NCA's **Liaison Office in Washington DC** brokered intelligence-sharing with the FBI under what the NCA describes as the "operational arrangement reached between the UK and the US last year" (i.e. **2024**) specifically to tackle call-centre fraud.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Lead jurisdiction | India |
| Action-day lead authority | [[cbi-india\|Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)]] |
| UK partner | [[uk-nca\|National Crime Agency (NCA)]] — National Economic Crime Centre (NECC); International Liaison Officers (US, Delhi, Washington DC); supported by [[city-of-london-police\|City of London Police]] (Action Fraud Reports) |
| US partner | [[fbi\|FBI]] — Washington Field Office; FBI Attaché in Delhi |
| Private-sector partner | [[microsoft\|Microsoft]] (Digital Crimes Unit) |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| early 2024 | Microsoft → NCA-USA Liaison Office intelligence; cross-comparison with City of London Police Action Fraud Reports begins | NCA 2025-07-11 |
| 2024 | UK-US operational arrangement on call-centre fraud reached | NCA 2025-07-11 |
| earlier in 2025 | NCA International Liaison Officers' visit to India; CBI briefed on suspect file | NCA 2025-07-11 |
| 2025-07-07 | CBI raid on Noida fraud call-centre; 2 arrests | NCA 2025-07-11 |
| 2025-07-11 | NCA public announcement | NCA 2025-07-11 |

## Results and Impact

- **2 arrests** at the 2025-07-07 Noida action day.
- **100+ UK victims** contacted; **£390,000+** in estimated UK losses.
- **US citizens also targeted** by the same call-centre (specific US-victim totals not enumerated; FBI cited the national-level USD 1.9 billion call-centre-scam-loss total contextually).
- **Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit** identified as the private-sector intelligence originator that triggered the investigation.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The cited release describes a four-party intelligence-sharing-driven cooperation model with three core mechanism classes:

1. **NCA International Liaison Officer network** (US, Delhi, Washington DC) — the primary inter-jurisdictional intelligence-sharing channel for both UK-US and UK-India sides of the cooperation.
2. **UK-US operational arrangement on call-centre fraud (2024)** — the foundational bilateral instrument for cross-Atlantic intelligence-sharing on call-centre fraud, referenced by the NCA NECC Deputy Director.
3. **Public-private cooperation** with Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit as the originating intelligence party, plus City of London Police Action Fraud Reports as the UK domestic-victim cross-comparison data feed.

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)
