title: Indonesia INP-FBI MFA-Bypass Phishing Syndicate Takedown (2026)
title_ko: INP-FBI MFA 우회 피싱 신디케이트 검거 (2026)
text:
# Indonesia INP-FBI MFA-Bypass Phishing Syndicate Takedown (2026)

> [!info] Provisional record
> This page rests on a single tier-1 primary source — an English-language press release on the official Indonesian National Police newsroom (`inp.polri.go.id`) dated 23 April 2026. It is below the preferred 5-source threshold for a normal operation page and is filed as a provisional joint-investigation record. It will be promoted when (a) Antara/Kompas/Detik Indonesian-language coverage is ingested with corroborating detail, (b) any FBI press statement or US criminal complaint surfaces, or (c) Indonesian charging documents are released.

## Summary

On **Wednesday, 22 April 2026**, the **Indonesian National Police** (Kepolisian Negara Republik Indonesia, "Polri") through its **Criminal Investigation Agency** (*Badan Reserse Kriminal*, "Bareskrim") arrested an Indonesian couple in Jakarta in a cross-border joint investigation with the **U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)**. The couple is identified by initials **GWL** (the technical developer) and **FYT** (the cryptocurrency-proceeds manager). The arrest was announced on **23 April 2026** through the INP's English-language newsroom.

The syndicate's product was a **phishing-as-a-service** capability whose distinguishing feature was the ability to bypass **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. Per the INP release, GWL had been developing and selling these "malicious scripts" since **2018**, giving the operation an approximately **8-year operational lifespan** before disruption. The scripts compromised **17,000 accounts** and victimised **34,000 people globally**, including United States victims and **nine Indonesian corporate entities**. Total losses are estimated at **USD 20 million**.

This case qualifies for the IC corpus because it is conducted as a direct **Indonesia–United States** joint investigation, with INP as lead agency and FBI as the named foreign cooperating agency, and because Indonesia is **not** a party to the Budapest Convention as of 2026-05-09, illustrating that bilateral cybercrime cooperation can occur outside Council of Europe instruments.

## Background

Phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) — where a small number of skilled developers package phishing infrastructure (kits, hosting, evasion tooling, mule networks) for sale to a wider population of less-skilled fraudsters — has been a recurring pattern in international cybercrime cooperation, with prior examples including the Swiss real-time-phishing case ([[oag-fedpol-real-time-phishing-uk-conviction-2025]]) and the W3LL phishing-kit takedown ([[w3ll-phishing-kit-takedown-2026]]).

The distinguishing technical claim in the present operation is that the seller's scripts **bypass MFA**. The INP release does not specify whether this was achieved through real-time SMS interception, session-token replay, adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) proxying, or other means, but the operational consequence — the ability to defeat MFA at scale — is consistent with the senior-tier PhaaS profile documented in the [[w3ll-phishing-kit-takedown-2026|W3LL]] record.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| **Lead investigating agency** | [[indonesia-police\|Indonesian National Police (INP) - Bareskrim]] |
| **Senior official cited** | Inspector General Nunung Syaifuddin, Deputy Chief of Bareskrim (Criminal Investigation Agency) |
| **Foreign cooperating agency** | [[fbi\|U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation]] (specific field office not stated) |
| **Communicating arm** | INP Public Relations Division (Divisi Humas Polri) |

The release describes the cooperation as "INP and the FBI have uncovered" the syndicate, suggesting a **joint investigative effort** rather than a unidirectional intelligence handoff. However, the specific cooperation channel — whether through the FBI's Legal Attaché (Legat) office in Jakarta, through an INTERPOL National Central Bureau request, through a bilateral mutual legal assistance request, or via

title: Infraud Organization Takedown
text:
## Summary

The Infraud Organization takedown was a U.S. Department of Justice-led enforcement action against a transnational cybercrime forum that specialized in carding, identity theft, stolen payment data, malware and related criminal services. The District of Nevada unsealed a nine-count superseding indictment on 2018-02-07 charging 36 defendants for alleged roles in the organization.

The action is retained as a canonical operation because the public record describes an operation-level disruption of the Infraud Organization, not merely a single defendant prosecution. The canonical result is 36 defendants charged and 13 arrests. Public reporting also identifies additional foreign arrest and extradition activity, including Sergey Medvedev's arrest in Bangkok on 2018-02-02 at U.S. request.

The core evidentiary record is stronger than the previous page suggested. It is not a media-only record: the source set includes an official DOJ charging announcement and multiple independent cybercrime or mainstream news accounts that corroborate the forum scale, loss figures, arrest count and undercover-investigation timeline.

## Target and Threat Model

Infraud was founded in October 2010 by Ukrainian Svyatoslav Bondarenko and used the slogan "In Fraud We Trust." The organization presented itself as a marketplace and coordination forum for purchasing and selling stolen identity data, credit-card and debit-card data, bank-account data, malware, ATM skimming tools and other cybercrime services.

The forum's operational value came from scale and trust. Source material describes roughly 10,901 registered members at peak and a structured criminal marketplace with administrators, super-moderators, vendors and escrow functions. This made the target analytically closer to a criminal-service platform than to a loose discussion board.

## Investigation and Enforcement Model

The public sources support a multi-year investigation rather than a single-day raid. CBS/AP reporting attributes the investigation start to 2014, when Homeland Security Investigations used an undercover agent inside the forum. Wired and Krebs describe the action as the result of sustained multinational coordination and long-running identity work against aliases, administrators and vendors.

The 2018 enforcement package combined a U.S. indictment with coordinated arrests and extradition follow-through. The DOJ indictment supplied the legal frame; HSI and other investigative components supplied forum access and identity resolution; foreign law-enforcement partners carried out or supported arrests in several countries.

## Participating Parties

| Actor | Role |
|---|---|
| [[us-doj\|US DOJ]] / District of Nevada | Charging authority, indictment and public enforcement announcement |
| [[us-dhs\|DHS / HSI]] | Undercover forum access and investigative work reported by CBS/AP |
| [[fbi-cyber-division\|FBI Cyber Division]] | U.S. federal cybercrime investigative partner recorded in the operation corpus |
| [[thailand-royal-police\|Thai law enforcement]] | Arrest support for Sergey Medvedev in Bangkok at U.S. request |
| Foreign law-enforcement partners | Arrest and extradition support across Australia, the United Kingdom, France, Italy, Kosovo and Serbia |

The structured country list records countries directly tied to arrests or operational support in the source set: United States, Australia, United Kingdom, France, Italy, Kosovo, Serbia and Thailand. Sources also describe defendants and administrators from other countries, including Ukraine, Russia, Moldova and North Macedonia, but defendant nationality is not the same as participating-country status.

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Source-supported value |
|---|---:|
| Defendants charged | 36 |
| Arrests | 13 |
| Forum peak membership | 10,901 |
| Actual losses | More than USD 530 million |
| Intended losses | More than USD 2.2 billion |
| Payment records associated with forum activity | Approximately 4.3 million |
| Publicl

title: i-Soon/APT27 Indictment (Chinese Contract Hackers)
text:
## Summary

On 5 March 2025, the US Department of Justice announced charges against **12 Chinese nationals** affiliated with Anxun Information Technology Co. Ltd. (i-Soon) and Advanced Persistent Threat 27 (APT27). The defendants included two officers of China's Ministry of Public Security (MPS), eight i-Soon employees, and APT27 members. These actors conducted computer intrusions at the direction of China's MPS and Ministry of State Security (MSS) as well as on their own initiative.

i-Soon operated as a key player in China's **hacker-for-hire ecosystem**, generating tens of millions of dollars in revenue by charging **USD 10,000 to USD 75,000** per successfully exploited email inbox.

## Background

The i-Soon case reveals the blurred line between state-sponsored cyber espionage and commercial cybercrime in China. i-Soon functioned as a contractor for Chinese intelligence agencies while also pursuing independent hacking operations for profit. Victims included:

- US-based critics and dissidents of the PRC
- A large religious organization in the US
- Foreign ministries in Asia: **Taiwan, India, South Korea, Indonesia**

## Participating Parties

- **US agencies:** DOJ, [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], Naval Criminal Investigative Service (NCIS), Department of State, Department of the Treasury
- **Enforcement tools:** Criminal indictments, Rewards for Justice (up to USD 10 million), Treasury sanctions

> [!note] This is primarily a US unilateral action. Other countries listed (China, Taiwan, India, South Korea, Indonesia) are victim or defendant countries, not cooperating law enforcement partners.

## Results and Impact

- **12 individuals indicted** for computer intrusion conspiracies
- **Treasury sanctions** imposed
- **Rewards for Justice** offer of up to USD 10 million
- Attribution of i-Soon's operations publicly documented

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

South Korea is listed as a **victim country** — the South Korean foreign ministry was targeted by i-Soon/APT27 intrusion campaigns. This is relevant to Korea's cybersecurity posture but does not represent Korean law enforcement cooperation in this case.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Will any of the 12 defendants ever be apprehended?
- What is the full scope of i-Soon's operations against Asian governments?
- How does this case affect US-China cybersecurity diplomatic engagement?
- What specific data was exfiltrated from the South Korean foreign ministry?

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US Department of Justice, 2025-03-05: Justice Department Charges 12 Chinese Contract Hackers and Law Enforcement Officers in Global Computer Intrusion Campaigns.
- WIRED, 2025-03-05: US Charges 12 Alleged Spies in China's Freewheeling Hacker-for-Hire Ecosystem.
- Infosecurity Magazine, 2025-03-06: US Charges Members of Chinese Hacker-for-Hire Group i-Soon.
- BleepingComputer, 2025-03-05: US charges Chinese hackers linked to critical infrastructure breaches.
- The CyberWire, 2025-03-06: US Justice Department charges employees of Chinese IT contractor i-Soon.

## Operational Timeline

- 2025-03-05: public announcement.
- 2025-03-05: public source coverage from BleepingComputer, US Department of Justice, WIRED.
- 2025-03-06: public source coverage from Infosecurity Magazine, The CyberWire.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: hacking.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Indictment and Asset Freeze.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status ongoing.
- Related legal or operational records: Us V Wu Haibo Isoon.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- DOJ press release announcing the charging of 12 Chinese nationals affiliated with i-Soon (Anxun Information Technology) and APT27 for global computer intrusion campaigns directed by China's MPS and MSS.
- The coordinated action involved DOJ, FBI, NCIS, State Department, and Treasury.
- i-Soon charged USD 10,000-75,000 per successfully exploited email inbox.
- Victims included

title: Italy–US extradition of a PRC HAFNIUM-campaign hacker (Polizia di Stato + FBI, Malpensa arrest July 2025 → Milan extradition April 2026)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / sparse-source page
> This page is built from a **single tier-1 primary source** (Polizia di Stato / Polizia Postale press release, 2026-04-27). The corresponding US-side primary source ([[2026-04-27_justice-gov_prolific-chinese-state-sponsored-contract-hacker-extradited-italy]]) is *not* counted toward this operation page's source count because it is already exhaustively summarised in the related case page [[us-v-xu-zewei-hafnium-extradited-italy]]. This operation page exists to document the **Italian-partner-state operational angle** (the extradition execution + chain-of-Italian-agencies) which the case page (US prosecution angle) does not fully capture. Treat all numeric/temporal claims as provisional pending a second independent primary source from the Italian side (e.g., a future ANSA wire reproducing official statements, or a Ministero della Giustizia release).

## Summary

On **2026-04-27**, the [[polizia-di-stato|Polizia di Stato]] (Italian National Police), through its [[italy-polizia-postale|Polizia Postale]] cyber unit, publicly announced that it had executed the extradition of a Chinese-national hacker (referred to in the Italian release as **"X. Z."** in line with Italian presumption-of-innocence reporting conventions; identified as **Xu Zewei** in the same-day US DOJ press release) to United States authorities. The extradition was the operational endpoint of (a) a **July 2025 arrest** at **Malpensa airport** by the Polizia Postale and Polaria di Malpensa (border police), (b) an **extradition decision by the Court of Appeal of Milan (Corte d'Appello di Milano)**, (c) authorisation by the **Italian Minister of Justice (Ministro della giustizia)**, and (d) handover via the **Servizio per la Cooperazione Internazionale di Polizia** (Italian Service for International Police Cooperation).

The Italian release frames the operation as the culmination of "una lunga collaborazione operativa tra FBI e gli investigatori della polizia italiana" — a long operational collaboration between the FBI and Italian police investigators — rather than a single-shot extradition; this is *highly likely* (per US IC analytic standard, 80–95%) to refer to information-sharing predating the July 2025 airport interception, since the suspect was intercepted while purporting to begin a holiday with his wife (i.e., not during a scheduled appearance), implying real-time targeting intelligence flow from FBI to the Italian investigators.

The underlying alleged conduct is the **HAFNIUM** Microsoft Exchange Server intrusion campaign of 2020–2021 plus prior COVID-19-research intrusions targeting US universities, attributed by US authorities to PRC Ministry of State Security — Shanghai State Security Bureau via the PRC enabling-company Shanghai Powerock Network Co. Ltd. (See [[us-v-xu-zewei-hafnium-extradited-italy]] for full case-prosecution detail.)

## Background

The HAFNIUM Microsoft Exchange Server campaign was publicly disclosed by Microsoft in March 2021 and attributed to PRC MSS by the United States and foreign partners in July 2021. The HAFNIUM campaign **almost certainly** (>95%) compromised 12,700+ US organisations per the US DOJ release of the same date as this Italian release. Phase 1 of the alleged conduct (early 2020) targeted US universities, immunologists and virologists conducting COVID-19 research; Phase 2 (late 2020 – early 2021) was the broader Microsoft Exchange Server compromise.

The named Italian-side timeline for this **extradition operation** is:

- **2025-07 (early July, exact date not enumerated)**: Arrest at Malpensa airport by Polizia Postale + Polaria di Malpensa, with the suspect intercepted alongside his wife.
- **(date not enumerated)**: Corte d'Appello di Milano extradition decision.
- **(date not enumerated)**: Italian Minister of Justice extradition authorisation.
- **2026-04 (weekend of 2026-04-25/26 per US DOJ release)**: Extradition handover executed via Servizio per la Cooperazione Intern

title: Italian C.N.C.P.O. + U.S. HSI + Europol joint takedown of a 'live distant child abuse' network — Procura di Milano (announced 2 February 2026)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source page
> This operation page is built from a single tier-1 primary source (Italian Polizia di Stato press release, 2026-02-02). It is below the preferred publication threshold of `source_count >= 5` and is therefore explicitly provisional under the project's "synthesis over proliferation" rule. It will be promoted, merged or demoted as additional tier-1 confirmations (Europol/HSI/Procura di Milano) become available.

## Summary

On 2 February 2026 the Italian State Police (Polizia di Stato) announced the conclusion of a transnational undercover investigation into "live distant child abuse" — sexual abuse of minors commissioned and viewed in real time via video-call. The investigation was conducted by the Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online (C.N.C.P.O.) of [[italy-polizia-postale|Polizia Postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]] and coordinated by the Procura della Repubblica di Milano (Public Prosecutor's Office of Milan). The press release describes international cooperation with the U.S. Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) and with [[europol-ec3|Europol]] as "determinante" (decisive).

Two men were arrested in the provinces of Trento and Reggio Calabria; four other persons aged 47–57, residing in the provinces of Roma, Latina, Brescia and Milano, were reported to the prosecutor at liberty (denunciate in stato di libertà). Seized digital devices are still under analysis. Italian investigators are additionally examining whether some of the indagati travelled to Asian countries to follow up the "distance" abuse with physical abuse in person.

Italian secondary press has widely referred to the case as "Operazione Light Bridge", but that name does not appear in the official Polizia di Stato press release and is therefore treated here as an unverified secondary-press alias.

## Background

The investigation targeted a structured "live distant child abuse" market in which:

- "Buyers" (Italian residents in this case) commissioned, in real time, sexual abuses on minors;
- "Traffickers" or "vendors" — physically present alongside the victims — carried out the abuses on demand and broadcast them via webcam;
- Initial contact between buyers and vendors occurred on "piattaforme asiatiche dedicate agli adulti" (Asian adult-content platforms), where alternative shows including some involving children were openly offered;
- After initial contact, conversations moved off the public platforms into private rooms where buyers negotiated specific preferences with vendors;
- Vendors who could not directly satisfy a request put the buyer in touch with other vendors, indicating a referral network rather than a single closed group;
- Payments were made as low-value "tips" through "i principali money business services" (the principal money-transfer business services), a structure designed to keep individual transactions below the radar of financial-control mechanisms.

According to the Polizia di Stato, "le vittime, spesso bambini molto piccoli, provengono da contesti particolarmente vulnerabili allo sfruttamento" — the victims, often very young children, come from contexts particularly vulnerable to exploitation. The press release does not identify specific Asian source jurisdictions.

## Participating Parties

**Italian side**

- [[italy-polizia-postale|Polizia di Stato — Polizia Postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]] (lead): Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online (C.N.C.P.O.) — undercover online infiltration; Centro operativo per la sicurezza cibernetica di Milano (Milan COSC) — operational core; supporting structures in Roma, Trento and Reggio Calabria.
- Procura della Repubblica di Milano (Public Prosecutor's Office of Milan) — judicial coordination.
- [[italy|Italy]] — state party.

**Foreign / international partners**

- United States Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) — explicitly named as a decisive cooperation partner. HSI does not yet have a dedic

title: Italy Riccione Cyber-Extortion Arrest — Italy-FBI Cooperation (Riccione, 31 July 2025)
title_ko: 이탈리아 리치오네 사이버 갈취범 체포 — 이탈리아·FBI 공조 (2025년 7월 31일)
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> This page is published below the preferred normal-page source threshold (`source_count >= 5`) and is marked provisional. It is anchored on a single Tier-1 primary source from the Italian Polizia di Stato dated 2025-07-31. It will be promoted once additional Tier-1 corroboration (DOJ unsealed indictment, FBI press release, Italian Ministry of Justice extradition decree) is ingested.

## Summary

On **2025-07-31**, officers of the **[[italy-polizia-postale|Italian Polizia di Stato — Servizio Polizia postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]]** arrested a foreign national at a hotel in **Riccione** (Rimini province, Emilia-Romagna, Italy) who was internationally wanted in connection with serious cyber-extortion offences committed between **2023 and the first months of 2025 against U.S.-based organisations**, with ransom demands totalling **millions of U.S. dollars**. The arrest was executed pursuant to a U.S. federal arrest warrant within an established bilateral cooperation framework with the **[[fbi|U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation]]**.

The federal warrant was operationalised by the **Centro nazionale anticrimine informatico per la protezione delle infrastrutture critiche (Cnaipic)** of the Polizia Postale, in collaboration with the **Centro operativo per la sicurezza cibernetica Emilia-Romagna** and the **Sezione operativa per la Sicurezza cibernetica di Rimini**. Numerous computing devices and several thousand euros in cash were seized at the place of arrest. The press release does not state the prison of remand or the subsequent extradition stage.

> [!note] Identity not asserted from primary source
> The official Polizia di Stato communiqué does **not** name the suspect, his nationality, or his age. Italian regional outlets (Resto del Carlino, RiminiToday, Corriere Romagna) reported the same day that the arrested individual is **42-year-old Kazakh national Roman Khlynovskiy**, that the FBI attributes approximately **USD 31 million** in illicit profit to the underlying scheme, and that the suspect was remanded to **Busto Arsizio** prison pending extradition. These particulars are noted in the raw-file context block but are flagged in `missing_fields` and are not asserted in tier-1 frontmatter fields, in accordance with L19-strict.

## Background

Between **2023 and the first months of 2025**, an organised cyber-extortion campaign targeted U.S.-based organisations ("strutture statunitensi"), generating ransom demands of millions of U.S. dollars. The Italian primary source does not name the specific U.S. victim entities, the malware family used, or the exact aggregate loss figure beyond "milioni di dollari." The suspect was identified by U.S. authorities as actively involved in the campaign and was the subject of an internationally circulated wanted-person notice and a U.S. federal arrest warrant.

Italian regional media reported, in connection with the arrest, that the FBI considers the underlying actor to be among the most dangerous hackers globally and that approximately USD 31 million was recovered or attributed as illicit gain. These media particulars are not asserted from the tier-1 source.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead Italian executing authority | [[italy-polizia-postale\|Polizia Postale (Servizio Polizia postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica)]] — Cnaipic, Centro operativo Emilia-Romagna, Sezione operativa di Rimini |
| Foreign investigating partner | [[fbi\|U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation]] |
| Country of arrest | [[italy\|Italy]] |
| Country of pending extradition | [[united-states\|United States]] |

## Legal Framework

- **Provvedimento federale**: the press release describes the operative arrest authority as a U.S. federal arrest warrant ("provvedimento federale"). The release does not specify the issuing district court, the indictment caption, or the underlying U.S. statute.
- **Bilateral cooperation framework**: the release explicitly credits "

title: Kazakhstan-Armenia Yerevan Call-Centre Investment Fraud Takedown (2025)
text:
# Kazakhstan-Armenia Yerevan Call-Centre Investment Fraud Takedown (2025)

> [!info] Provisional page (single tier-1 source)
> This page is published below the preferred source threshold of 5 because the controlling tier-1 source establishes a Central Asia / South Caucasus cooperation pairing not otherwise represented in the corpus. It will be promoted to a normal page when additional tier-1 confirmations (Investigative Committee of Armenia, polisia.kz primary post, charging documents) are ingested.

## Summary

On **2025-02-18** the press service of the **Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Republic of Kazakhstan** announced, through Sputnik Kazakhstan, that officers of the **Department for Combating Cybercrime of the MIA of Kazakhstan** had carried out a joint special operation in Yerevan together with the **Main Directorate for the Investigation of Especially Important Cases of the Investigative Committee of the Republic of Armenia**. The operation closed two call centres in Yerevan, detained 41 members of a transnational criminal group, and seized cash, gold, vehicles, computers, and a cold cryptocurrency wallet. The operation is described as ongoing, with the Armenian side continuing investigative actions supported by the Kazakh MIA Cybercrime Counteraction Department.

## Background

The press release describes a transnational organized criminal group operating two call centres in Yerevan that pitched fake "large investment companies" to citizens of Kazakhstan and European countries. The fraud pattern is consistent with the broader pattern of investment / "broker" call-centre operations now documented across Europe (see [[albania-austria-call-centre-investment-fraud-2026]]) but with a different operating geography (South Caucasus host jurisdiction, Central Asia primary victim pool, European secondary victim pool).

## Participating Parties

| Side | Unit (verbatim) | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Kazakhstan | Department for Combating Cybercrime, Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Republic of Kazakhstan (Департамент по противодействию киберпреступности МВД РК) — head: Zhandos Suiynbai (Жандос Сүйінбай) | Operational lead on the Kazakh side; named on-record source for the public statement |
| Armenia | Main Directorate for the Investigation of Especially Important Cases, Investigative Committee of the Republic of Armenia (Главное управление по расследованию особо важных дел Следственного комитета Республики Армения) | Operational lead on the Armenian side; conducted searches and continuing investigative measures in Yerevan |
| Source-backed cooperating countries | [[kazakhstan|Kazakhstan]], [[armenia|Armenia]] | Enumerated in the release |

> [!note] Wikilink discipline
> Neither the Kazakh MIA Cybercrime Counteraction Department nor the Armenian Investigative Committee currently has a wiki organization page; their full names are therefore preserved in the prose and in the `participating_agencies` list rather than as wikilinks (per L19, L23). The Armenian police high-tech crime division ([[armenia-police]]) is not the lead unit identified in this source and is intentionally not listed for this operation.

## Legal Framework

The released press text does not name a specific bilateral instrument, MLAT request, or joint investigation team agreement. The work is described as "joint operational actions" by the two named units, with the Armenian side carrying the formal procedural file. Both Kazakhstan and Armenia have separately developed cybercrime cooperation channels through Council of Europe / Octopus capacity-building, but the controlling source for this operation does not invoke a specific framework.

## Operational Timeline

- **Pre-2025-02-18:** Joint preparatory work between the Kazakh MIA Cybercrime Counteraction Department and the Armenian Investigative Committee Main Directorate for the Investigation of Especially Important Cases.
- **On or before 2025-02-18:** Action day in Yerevan; two call centres closed; 41 individual

title: KNPA Operation Breaking Chains — 2nd International Cooperation Operations Meeting (2026)
title_ko: 경찰청 작전명 '브레이킹 체인스' — 제2차 국제공조 작전회의 (2026)
text:
## Summary

On 2026-02-05–06, the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] hosted in Seoul the **2nd International Cooperation Operations Meeting** (제2차 국제공조 작전회의), held under the operation codename **Breaking Chains (브레이킹 체인스)**. The release describes Breaking Chains as a Korea-led ("韓 주도") multilateral operational platform against transnational scam crime — including scam compounds (스캠 센터), human trafficking (인신매매, *insinmaemae*), and online fraud (온라인 사기). The 2nd meeting drew 22 country-level law-enforcement agencies plus five international organisations: [[interpol|INTERPOL]], [[aseanapol|ASEANAPOL]], [[afripol|AFRIPOL]], the International Organization for Migration (IOM), and [[unodc|UNODC]].

The release also reports cumulative outcomes from the 1st meeting cycle (held 2025-11) and the Phase-1 field operation conducted 2025-12-04, demonstrating the meeting-to-operation conversion of the platform.

## Background

The Breaking Chains forum was set up by KNPA as a Korea-hosted standing multilateral cooperation forum with a recurring meeting cadence — 1st meeting in 2025-11 (Seoul); 2nd meeting on 2026-02-05–06 (Seoul) — interleaved with named field operations (Phase-1, 2025-12-04, Cambodia + Thailand). The operation codename "Breaking Chains" is explicitly framed in the press release as the policy intent to *break* the criminal *chains* binding scam-compound, human-trafficking, and online-fraud victims.

The forum is institutionally connected to a wider Korean whole-of-government scam-compound architecture:
- **대통령실 「초국가범죄 특별대응 전담팀(TF)」** (Presidential Office Transnational Crime Special Response Task Force) — named in the body of the release as the policy structure that combined with the 1st-meeting intelligence-sharing to convert leads into arrests.
- **「코리아전담반」** (Korea-dedicated Squad) — Korean police field unit deployed inside Cambodia, named here for the Phase-1 Cambodia 15-arrest tranche, and previously named in the wiki as the field unit for the [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|Korea–Cambodia–Philippines 73-person extradition]].

The Breaking Chains forum overlaps with — but is not identical to — the broader **국제공조협의체 (IICA, International Cooperation Consultative Body)** that the KNPA proposed in 2025-10 as a 46-country / 3-international-organisation initiative; subsequent Korean reporting (2026-03 헤럴드경제) frames Breaking Chains as the operational arm of IICA, but the 2026-02-05 release itself does not explicitly use the IICA acronym.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Coordinating host | [[knpa\|KNPA]] (with [[knpa-cyber-bureau\|KNPA Cyber Bureau]] for cyber-enabled crime lines) |
| International organisations (5) | [[interpol\|INTERPOL]], [[aseanapol\|ASEANAPOL]], [[afripol\|AFRIPOL]], International Organization for Migration (IOM), [[unodc\|UNODC]] |
| Country agencies (22 verbatim, named subset) | [[united-states\|United States]], [[china\|China]], [[japan\|Japan]], [[cambodia\|Cambodia]] (named verbatim in 2nd-meeting roster); [[thailand\|Thailand]], [[vietnam\|Vietnam]] (named verbatim in Phase-1 outcomes) |
| Korean policy structure | 대통령실 「초국가범죄 특별대응 전담팀 (TF)」 |
| Korea-deployed field unit (Cambodia) | 「코리아전담반」 (Korea-dedicated Squad) |
| Korean intelligence inputs (Phase-1) | 충남경찰청 형사기동대 (Chungnam Provincial Police Mobile Investigation Unit) — Cambodia case; 서울경찰청 금융범죄수사대 (Seoul Metropolitan Police Financial Crime Investigation Unit) — Thailand case |

> [!note] Translation note
> The Korean operation name 「국제공조 작전회의」 is rendered "International Cooperation Operations Meeting" — *국제공조* (gukje gongjo, "international cooperation") + *작전회의* (jakjeon hoeui, "operations meeting"). The codename 「브레이킹 체인스」 is the Korean phonetic transcription of the English codename "Breaking Chains".

## Legal Framework

The Breaking Chains forum is **not** a treaty instrument; it is an operational cooperation construct led by a national police agency. The legal foundations exposed in the releas

title: KNPA–Vietnam Public Security Joint Arrest of 5 Cambodia-Bavet Romance-Scam Organisation Members in Vietnam (2025-10-28)
title_ko: 한·베 경찰 공조 캄보디아 바벳 거점 로맨스스캠 조직원 5명 베트남 검거 (2025-10-28)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-10-28**, the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] (경찰청), in joint operation with **Vietnamese Public Security** (베트남 공안청 / *Bộ Công an Việt Nam*), simultaneously arrested **five Korean-national cadre-level members** of a Cambodia-Bavet-based web **romance-scam** (로맨스스캠) organisation across three Vietnamese cities — **Da Nang, Ho Chi Minh City, and Khanh Hoa Province**. The KNPA announced the arrests on **2025-11-04** via the official Korea.kr 정책브리핑 channel (newsId 156724852).

The criminal organisation operated from **Bavet, Cambodia** (a border town adjacent to Vietnam), targeting Korean victims through dating-app and SNS-based romance lures and monetising through cryptocurrency-investment manipulation. **192 Korean victims** suffered cumulative losses of approximately **₩4.6 billion (~USD 3.5 million)**.

KNPA International Cooperation Officer **Lee Jae-young (이재영 경찰청 국제협력관)** is quoted in the primary source explicitly attributing the outcome to Korea–Vietnam bilateral cooperation (full quote in the [Cooperation Mechanisms Used](#cooperation-mechanisms-used) section below).

## Background

The romance-scam corridor in Cambodia–Vietnam is a recognised pattern in 2024-2025 Korean LE reporting: organisations recruit Korean-language-capable cadre, set up operating compounds in Cambodia (notably Bavet, Sihanoukville, Poipet, and the Taizi 태자 단지 compound), and monetise via app/SNS-mediated romance lures targeting Korean victims. When operational pressure increases on the Cambodian operating base, cadre frequently transit into Vietnam (geographic adjacency, large Korean expatriate community in Da Nang and Ho Chi Minh City, weaker visibility into immigration databases for Korean LE).

This operation is one of the discrete Vietnam-side arrest events within the broader 2025-Q4 / 2026-Q1 / 2026-Q2 KNPA campaign architecture against Southeast Asian scam compounds. It precedes the embedded [[knpa-cambodia-korea-task-force-redcorner-arrests-2026-02|Korea-dedicated Squad operations inside Cambodia (2025-12 to 2026-02)]] and the [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|73-person mass extradition from Cambodia and the Philippines (2026-04-27)]], and is part of the same KNPA strategic response to scam-compound transnationalisation that organises the [[knpa-breaking-chains-2nd-international-operation-meeting-2026|22-country/5-IO multilateral coordination meeting (2026-02-05)]].

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead agency (Korea) | [[knpa\|Korean National Police Agency]] — specifically the 국제협력관실 (International Cooperation Officer line) |
| Embassy police attaché (Korea) | 경찰주재관 in Da Nang and Ho Chi Minh City — under [[ministry-of-foreign-affairs-korea\|MOFA]] reporting line |
| Lead agency (Vietnam) | **Vietnamese Public Security Agency** (베트남 공안청 / *Bộ Công an Việt Nam*) — recorded in prose only; no dedicated wiki entity page |
| Field-level units (Vietnam) | Da Nang City Public Security (다낭시 공안), Ho Chi Minh City Public Security (호찌민시 공안청 / *Công an Thành phố Hồ Chí Minh*), Khanh Hoa Province Immigration Public Security (카인호아성 출입국관리청 공안) |

> [!note] Vietnam Public Security wikilink
> No dedicated wiki entity page exists at present for the Vietnamese Public Security Agency or its provincial subordinates. To preserve frontmatter integrity (per LESSONS L23) the operation's `participating_agencies` list contains only entities with existing wiki pages; the Vietnamese counterpart is recorded in prose with original-language naming preserved.

## Legal Framework

| Instrument / Channel | Role in this operation |
|---|---|
| Korea–Vietnam bilateral LE cooperation (operational; non-treaty) | Primary working framework — public-security-to-public-security channel for time-sensitive joint arrest events |
| Korean Embassy police attaché (경찰주재관) network | Operational liaison between KNPA HQ and Vietnamese provincial public-security units; deployed in Da Nang and Ho Chi Minh City for this operation |

title: KNPA–Cambodia National Police Korea-Dedicated Squad Red Notice & Compound Cadre Arrests (2025-12 to 2026-02)
title_ko: 한·캄 코리아 전담반 인터폴 적색수배자·관리자급 연속 검거 (2025-12 ~ 2026-02)
text:
## Summary

On 2026-02-19, the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] published a tier-1 Korean-language press release reporting a sustained joint Korea–Cambodia bilateral enforcement campaign against Korean-targeted scam organisations operating from Cambodian compounds, conducted through the embedded **Korea-dedicated Squad** (코리아 전담반) — a joint operational unit of Korean police personnel and the [[cambodian-national-police|Cambodian National Police]] (캄보디아 경찰청), opened in November 2025 inside Cambodia. The release announces:

- **6 INTERPOL Red Notice fugitives** arrested between 2025-12-22 and 2026-02-10 (manager-class scam-organisation cadre, including 2 organisation 총책 ("masterminds") and 1 head of Korean-victim money laundering at the Taizi 태자 단지 compound).
- **Three named February 2026 takedowns**:
  - **2026-02-04** — scam-organisation manager arrested after a ~500-metre foot chase on the street.
  - **2026-02-06** — scam-organisation cadre tied to ~₩8.4 billion (~USD 6M) in victim losses arrested at a hotel hideout in joint Korea-dedicated Squad / Cambodian National Police operation.
  - **2026-02-10** — investment-fraud organisation principal tied to ~₩10.6 billion (~USD 7.6M) in victim losses arrested after multi-day CCTV tracking.
- **Cumulative since Korea-dedicated Squad opening (2025-11)**: 12 operations; 4 Korean nationals rescued (구출); 140 organised-crime suspects arrested.

The release also reports the average in-country concealment duration for the 6 Red Notice subjects as ~1 year 10 months — a primary-source datapoint on transnational fugitive concealment.

## Background

Cambodia is one of the major host jurisdictions for scam compounds targeting Korean-language victims, alongside Myanmar, Laos, and the Philippines. Korean LE has historically struggled with the gap between Korean criminal jurisdiction (over Korean-national perpetrators and Korean-national victims) and Cambodian territorial sovereignty over the compound infrastructure. Korean cooperation with Cambodia has therefore evolved from request-based MLA toward embedded operational liaison.

The **Korea-dedicated Squad** (코리아 전담반) was opened in November 2025 inside Cambodia as a joint operational unit composed of Korean police personnel (per prior reporting, ~7 Korean officers) embedded with Cambodian National Police (per prior reporting, ~12 Cambodian officers). The Squad operates as the field arm of the broader Korean whole-of-government **「초국가범죄 특별대응 전담반(TF)」** (Transnational Crime Special Response Task Force, anchored at 대통령실 — the Presidential Office), bringing together the [[knpa|KNPA]], [[national-intelligence-service-korea|NIS / 국정원]], [[ministry-of-foreign-affairs-korea|MOFA]] (specifically the Korean Embassy in Cambodia and its embedded police attaché 경찰주재관), and the [[seoul-metropolitan-police|Seoul Metropolitan Police]] INTERPOL team (서울청 인터폴팀).

The 2026-02-19 release sits within a sequence of related Korean cooperation events in 2025-11 to 2026-04 against Cambodia-hosted scam crime; see [[korea-cambodia-scam-repatriation]] (earlier 2025 cohort), [[knpa-breaking-chains-2nd-international-operation-meeting-2026]] (2026-02-05 multilateral meeting and Phase-1 Cambodia 15-arrest tranche), and [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026]] (2026-04-27 73-person mass extradition). The current page captures the discrete bilateral arrest sequence reported in the 2026-02-19 release.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead agency (Korea) | [[knpa\|Korean National Police Agency]] — specifically 국제치안협력국 (International Police Cooperation Bureau) and 국제공조1과 |
| Cyber-line lead (Korea) | [[knpa-cyber-bureau\|KNPA Cyber Bureau]] |
| INTERPOL operational team (Korea) | [[seoul-metropolitan-police\|Seoul Metropolitan Police]] INTERPOL team (서울청 인터폴팀) |
| Embassy police attaché (Korea) | 경찰주재관 in 주캄보디아 한국대사관 ([[ministry-of-foreign-affairs-korea\|MOFA]] reporting line) |
| Intelligence (Korea) | [[national-intelligence-service-korea

title: KNPA KMSAuto Clipper Malware — Lithuania-Georgia-Korea Three-State Extradition (2020–2025)
title_ko: 가상자산 편취 악성프로그램(KMSAuto) 유포 외국인 해커 인터폴 수배·송환 (한국·리투아니아·조지아 공조)
text:
## Summary

On 2025-12-29 the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] National Office of Investigation (국가수사본부) announced the extradition into [[south-korea|Korea]] of a 29-year-old [[lithuania|Lithuanian]] national identified only as 'A', accused of distributing a memory-hacking clipper malware bundled inside cracked Microsoft Windows activation tooling 'KMSAuto'. The malware silently rewrote cryptocurrency receive-addresses at transmission time on infected hosts, redirecting funds to attacker-controlled wallets. Across 2020-04-08 to 2023-01-06 the campaign distributed 2.8 million copies of the bundle worldwide, drained 3,100 distinct cryptocurrency addresses across 8,400 transactions for ~₩1.7 billion (~USD 1.18M), and reached victims in 234 countries (ISO basis), including 8 confirmed Korean victims with ~₩16M aggregate losses.

The cooperation chain is named explicitly in the KNPA release as: KNPA / Korean MOJ / Korean SPO ↔ Lithuanian MOJ + [[lithuania-police|Lithuanian Police]] (joint search of A's residence, December 2024, 22 items seized) → [[interpol|INTERPOL]] Red Notice → [[georgia|Georgian]] Police (arrest April 2025 on entry from Lithuania) → Korean [[extradition|extradition]] request to Georgia → custody in Korea late 2025. Total elapsed time from initial case opening (August 2020) to extradition was 5 years 4 months.

## Background

A single bitcoin-misdirection victim report in August 2020 ('비트코인 1개를 송금했는데 엉뚱한 주소로 송금되어 잃어버렸다', i.e. one bitcoin then valued at ~₩12 million sent to an unintended address) opened the case at the [[knpa-cyber-bureau|KNPA Cyber Investigation function]]. Forensic examination of the victim's host disclosed a 'memory-hacking' clipper that automatically replaced the destination cryptocurrency address with an attacker-controlled address at the moment of transmission. The clipper had been delivered as a payload bundled with KMSAuto, a Microsoft Windows volume-licensing-bypass utility frequently downloaded from non-official channels by users seeking to avoid Windows activation. KNPA's release explicitly attributes victim exposure to the use of non-genuine software ('정품 소프트웨어를 사용하지 않았기 때문에 피해가 발생').

Through follow-the-money work across domestic Korean cryptocurrency exchanges plus 6 third-country jurisdictions and 6 foreign cryptocurrency-related companies, KNPA traced the proceeds, expanded the Korean victim count from 1 to 8 (adding 7 victims with Korean losses totalling ~₩16M), reconstructed the full distribution timeline, and identified A as a Lithuanian-resident natural person.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Lead requesting authority | [[knpa\|KNPA National Office of Investigation (국가수사본부)]] — 사이버테러대응과 |
| Korean MLA conduits | [[ministry-of-justice-korea\|Ministry of Justice Korea]], [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea\|Supreme Prosecutors' Office Korea]] |
| Host-state joint-search authorities | Lithuanian Ministry of Justice and [[lithuania-police\|Lithuanian Police]] |
| International notice channel | [[interpol\|INTERPOL]] (Red Notice) |
| Arresting authority | Georgian Police (state agency in [[georgia\|Georgia]]) |
| Source-backed participating countries | [[south-korea\|South Korea]] (requesting), [[lithuania\|Lithuania]] (host / joint search), [[georgia\|Georgia]] (arrest / surrendering state) |

## Legal Framework

The KNPA release names the cooperation backbone as a layered stack:

1. Korea–Lithuania **mutual legal assistance** (형사사법공조), routed through Korean Ministry of Justice and Supreme Prosecutors' Office, which authorised the joint search-and-seizure of A's Lithuanian residence in December 2024 and the seizure of 22 items including phone and laptop;
2. **INTERPOL Red Notice** ('인터폴 적색 수배') issued at KNPA request after Korea concluded that the evidentiary basis was sufficient to prosecute A in Korea;
3. **Korea–Georgia extradition request** ('범죄인 인도 요청') filed once A was arrested by Georgian Police on entry from Lithuania in April 2025, executed

title: Korea–Cambodia–Philippines 73-Person Online Scam Extradition (2026)
title_ko: 캄보디아·필리핀 거점 온라인 스캠 조직원 73명 송환 작전 (2026)
text:
## Summary

On 2026-04-27 the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] announced the extradition into Korea of 73 Korean-national online-scam suspects from [[cambodia|Cambodia]] (42 persons) and the [[philippines|Philippines]] (31 persons) over a roughly two-month operation period beginning 2026-02-26. The KNPA release describes the operation as the largest single-country cyber-fugitive extradition into Korea on record. 69 of the 73 suspects entered post-arrival custody on remand orders.

The action was coordinated through a Korean whole-government task force formally identified as 범정부 「초국가범죄 특별대응TF」 (Whole-Government Transnational Crime Special Response Task Force), bringing together the [[knpa|KNPA]], the [[ministry-of-foreign-affairs-korea|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]], the [[ministry-of-justice-korea|Ministry of Justice]], and the [[national-intelligence-service-korea|National Intelligence Service]] alongside cooperation with the Cambodian National Police and the Philippine National Police. In Cambodia specifically, a Korea-side operational unit known as 「코리아전담반」 (Korea-dedicated Squad) is named as the field unit that produced the 42-person Cambodian tranche.

## Background

This 2026 operation continues the broader Korea-led scam-compound enforcement frame previously documented in the wiki as [[korea-cambodia-scam-repatriation]] (the 2025 Cambodia voice-phishing repatriation line) and is announced against the larger backdrop of recurring Cambodian and Philippine scam-compound disclosures during 2025–2026, including the Cambodia kidnapping–murder incident referenced in Korean year-end reporting for 2025. The 2026 announcement materially widens scope along three axes: (a) two requested jurisdictions in the same announcement (Cambodia and Philippines, not Cambodia alone), (b) a whole-government task force frame rather than a KNPA-only frame, and (c) a multi-crime-type cohort spanning voice phishing, romance scam, illegal gambling, a 2011 data breach, auto-loan fraud, and sex exploitation.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Lead requesting authority | [[knpa\|KNPA]] (with [[knpa-cyber-bureau\|KNPA Cyber Bureau]] for cyber-enabled crime lines) |
| Whole-government task force | 「초국가범죄 특별대응TF」 — [[knpa\|KNPA]] + [[ministry-of-foreign-affairs-korea\|MOFA]] + [[ministry-of-justice-korea\|MOJ]] + [[national-intelligence-service-korea\|NIS]] |
| Korea-deployed field unit (Cambodia) | 「코리아전담반」 (Korea-dedicated Squad) |
| Cambodian counterpart | Cambodian National Police |
| Philippine counterpart | Philippine National Police |
| Source-backed participating countries | [[south-korea\|South Korea]], [[cambodia\|Cambodia]], [[philippines\|Philippines]] |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2026-02-26 | Whole-government task-force operation period begins; 코리아전담반 field operations underway in Cambodia | KNPA 2026-04-27 |
| 2026-04-27 | KNPA announces 73-person extradition cohort and operation conclusion | KNPA 2026-04-27 |

## Results and Impact

| Cohort | Persons | Victims | Damages | Method / period |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Voice phishing (보이스피싱) | 24 | 368 | ~₩51.7 B | 검찰 / 금융감독원 사칭 |
| Romance scam | 14 | 53 | ~₩2.3 B | 만남 앱 통한 암호화폐 투자 유도 |
| Illegal gambling-site operation | 3 | — | ~₩5.9 T (operating scale) | — |
| 2011 capital-firm data breach | 1 | ~1 750 000 | ~₩100 M | 송환까지 15년 경과 |
| Auto-loan fraud | 3 | — | ~₩12 B | 2022–2024 |
| Sex exploitation | 24 | — | — | overlap with voice-phishing cohort |
| **Total extradited** | **73** | — | — | 69 remanded into custody |
| **Cambodia tranche** | 42 | — | — | 41 remanded; 코리아전담반 operation |
| **Philippines tranche** | 31 | — | — | 28 remanded |

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The KNPA release does not name a specific MLAT instrument; the cooperation is described in the language of "유기적 공조" (organic cooperation) with the Cambodian and Philippine police authorities, plus the whole-government Korean task force structure. The mec

title: Korea-Cambodia Scam Centre Repatriation
text:
## Summary

In October 2025 and January 2026, South Korea repatriated **107+ nationals** from Cambodia suspected of operating in online scam centres conducting voice phishing, romance scams, and "pig butchering" cryptocurrency fraud. An additional **20+ suspects** were repatriated from Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, and the Philippines. The Korean government estimated approximately **1,000 Korean citizens** were working in Cambodian scam centres. The January 2026 batch allegedly scammed fellow Koreans out of **KRW 48.6 billion (USD 33 million)**.

## Background

Southeast Asian scam compounds have become a major center for transnational online fraud. Workers are often lured through fake job advertisements and then trafficked into forced fraud operations targeting victims worldwide. The Korea-Cambodia repatriation is part of a broader crackdown that coincided with **US and UK sanctions against the Cambodia-based Prince Group** for running scam centres across the region.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead (Korea):** [[knpa-cyber-bureau|Korea National Police Agency (경찰청)]]
- **Domestic coordination:** [[knpa|Korea National Police Agency]]
- **Cooperating:** Cambodian National Police and Cambodian authorities
- **Additional repatriations from:** Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, Philippines
- **International context:** Concurrent US/UK sanctions against Prince Group

## Operational Timeline

1. **Ongoing:** Korean citizens recruited to Cambodian scam centres through fake job offers
2. **October 2025:** 64 Korean nationals repatriated from Cambodia
3. **October 2025:** [[knpa-cyber-bureau|KNPA]] seeks arrest warrants for 58 of the 64 (1 already arrested, 5 released)
4. **January 2026:** 73 additional suspects repatriated from Cambodia; 20+ from other Southeast Asian countries
5. **January 2026:** Yonhap reported 136 scam suspects apprehended by a joint South Korea-Cambodia police team since November 2025

## Results and Impact

- **136 suspects** reported apprehended by the joint South Korea-Cambodia police team since November 2025
- **107+ suspects** repatriated from Cambodia across two batches
- **20+ additional suspects** from Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, Philippines
- **58 arrest warrants** sought (first batch)
- **46 suspects** proceeded to trial in South Korea
- **KRW 48.6 billion (USD 33 million)** in fraud losses identified (January 2026 batch)
- **~1,000 Korean citizens** estimated in Cambodian scam centres

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

This operation is entirely Korean-led and Korea-focused. Key observations:

- Korea is both a **victim country** (citizens defrauded by voice phishing) and a **source country** (Korean nationals operating scam centres abroad)
- Korean nationals in scam compounds are often **themselves victims of trafficking** — lured by fake job offers and unable to leave
- The estimated **1,000 Korean citizens** in Cambodian scam centres represents a significant national security and law enforcement concern
- This operation demonstrates the **evolution of voice phishing infrastructure** from China-based call centres (see [[korea-china-voice-phishing-qingdao]]) to Southeast Asian scam compounds

The scale of the problem underscores the need for multilateral cooperation frameworks beyond bilateral agreements, potentially through ASEANAPOL and INTERPOL channels.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- How many of the repatriated suspects were genuine volunteers vs. trafficking victims?
- What legal framework governs repatriation of Korean nationals from countries without extradition treaties?
- What is the total number of Korean nationals across all Southeast Asian scam centres?
- How many of the 58 warrant targets have been prosecuted?

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in fo

title: Korea-China Voice Phishing Qingdao Joint Operation
text:
## Summary

The Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (SMPA), in cooperation with Chinese police in Qingdao, arrested **16 suspects** (3 Chinese nationals, 13 Koreans) for voice phishing scams that extorted **KRW 2.7 billion (USD 2 million)** from **68 victims** between December 2022 and August 2023. This was reported as the **first significant joint Korea-China voice phishing roundup** under a bilateral cooperation agreement between Seoul and Beijing law enforcement.

## Background

Voice phishing (보이스피싱) targeting Korean victims from China-based call centres has been a persistent cross-border crime pattern. Criminals recruit Korean and Chinese nationals to operate call centres in Chinese cities, impersonating Korean prosecutors, police, or financial institutions to extort victims. The Qingdao operation represents a breakthrough in bilateral enforcement cooperation.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead (Korea):** Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (서울경찰청, SMPA)
- **Lead (China):** Chinese Police, Qingdao
- **Countries:** [[south-korea|South Korea]], China

## Operational Timeline

1. **December 2022:** Ringleader (38-year-old Chinese national) establishes call centre in Qingdao
2. **April 2023:** SMPA launches investigation after victim report
3. **April-August 2023:** Investigation proceeds; 3 Korean suspects detained domestically
4. **24 August 2023:** Chinese police detain 12 members and ringleader at Qingdao office following Korean cooperation request
5. **8 September 2023:** Results reported

## Results and Impact

- **16 arrests** (13 Koreans in Korea, 3 Chinese nationals including ringleader in China)
- **KRW 2.7 billion (USD 2 million)** in financial losses identified
- **68 victims** confirmed
- **Precedent established** for Korea-China bilateral voice phishing enforcement

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

This operation is entirely Korea-driven. The Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency (서울경찰청) initiated the investigation and requested cooperation from Chinese authorities. This case is *almost certainly* a direct precursor to the larger-scale voice phishing enforcement actions under [[operation-haechi-v|HAECHI V]], where Korean and Chinese authorities jointly dismantled a major voice phishing syndicate causing USD 1.1 billion in losses.

Korea's voice phishing challenge:
- Voice phishing is a top-priority cybercrime concern for Korean law enforcement
- China-based call centres have historically been the primary origin of voice phishing attacks targeting Korean victims
- The bilateral cooperation framework established by this operation was critical for subsequent joint enforcement

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What was the specific bilateral agreement between Seoul and Beijing that enabled this operation?
- Were the arrested suspects prosecuted, and if so, in which jurisdiction?
- How many similar operations have been conducted since this precedent-setting case?

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- The Korea Times, 2023-09-08: Seoul Metropolitan Police and Chinese police jointly arrest 16 voice phishing suspects in Qingdao.
- web.archive.org, 2026-04-17: articleView.html.
- The Korea Times, 2023-09-07: Police nab 16 voice phishing scam suspects based in China.
- Yonhap News Agency, 2023-09-07: Police nab 16 voice phishing scam suspects based in China.
- Asia Economy, 2023-09-07: 16 Members of Voice Phishing Organization Based in Qingdao, China Arrested.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: Voice Phishing Ic.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status completed.
- Related legal or operational records: Operatio

title: LabHost Phishing-as-a-Service Takedown (UK Met-led, 19-country joint action, 2024-04-14 to 2024-04-17)
title_ko: LabHost 피싱 서비스 플랫폼 단속 (영국 경시청 주도, 19개국 합동 작전, 2024-04-14~2024-04-17)
text:
## Summary

Between **14 and 17 April 2024**, a **UK London Metropolitan Police-led** international investigation, supported by **Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)** and the **Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT)** hosted at Europol HQ, executed **70 searches** across **19 countries** producing **37 arrests** — including **4 individuals in the United Kingdom** linked to the running of the site, **including LabHost's original developer**. The **LabHost phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform** was shut down. LabHost provided cybercriminals with a subscription-based ($249/month average) menu of phishing kits, infrastructure for hosting fake pages, **170+ fake-website templates**, and the **LabRat** integrated campaign-management tool capable of capturing **two-factor authentication (2FA) codes**. The investigation uncovered **at least 40,000 phishing domains** linked to LabHost and **~10,000 users worldwide**.

## Background

Cybercrime-as-a-service has become a rapidly growing business model in the criminal landscape whereby threat actors rent or sell tools, expertise, or services to other cybercriminals to commit their attacks. While this model is well established with ransomware groups (e.g. [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos / LockBit]]), it has also been adopted in other aspects of cybercrime, such as phishing. LabHost commoditised phishing by offering a customisable, click-deployable kit, with subscription tiers granting access to escalating target sets including financial institutions, postal delivery services, and telecommunication providers.

What made LabHost particularly destructive was its integrated campaign management tool **LabRat**, which allowed cybercriminals to monitor and control their attacks in real time. LabRat was specifically designed to capture **two-factor authentication codes and credentials**, allowing the criminals to bypass enhanced security measures.

## Participating Parties

| Country | Lead Authority |
|---|---|
| Australia | Australian Federal Police-led Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (AFP / JPC3) |
| Austria | Criminal Intelligence Service (Bundeskriminalamt) |
| Belgium | Federal Judicial Police Brussels |
| Canada | Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) |
| Czechia | Bureau of Criminal Police and Investigation Service |
| Estonia | Estonian Police and Border Guard Board |
| Finland | National Police (Poliisi) |
| Ireland | An Garda Síochána |
| Lithuania | Lithuania Police |
| Malta | Malta Police Force |
| Netherlands | Central Netherlands Police (Politie Midden-Nederland) |
| New Zealand | New Zealand Police |
| Poland | Central Office for Combating Cybercrime (Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości) |
| Portugal | Judicial Police (Polícia Judiciária) |
| Romania | Romanian Police (Poliția Română) |
| Spain | Policía Nacional |
| Sweden | Swedish Police Authority (Polisen) |
| United Kingdom | London Metropolitan Police (lead) |
| United States | US Secret Service (USSS) + Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) |
| Coordinating | [[europol-ec3\|Europol European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] + Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT) |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| September 2023 | Europol begins supporting the case | Europol 2024-04-18 |
| (date not enumerated) | Operational sprint at Europol HQ with all 19 participating countries to identify users and victims in their jurisdictions | Europol 2024-04-18 |
| 2024-04-14 to 2024-04-17 | Action days: 70 searches globally; 37 arrests; 4 UK arrests including LabHost developer; Europol specialist deployed on-site to Dutch National Police | Europol 2024-04-18 |
| 2024-04-18 | Public announcement; LabHost platform shut down | Europol 2024-04-18 |

## Results and Impact

- **37 arrests** during 2024-04-14 → 2024-04-17 action days.
- **70 addresses searched** across the world.
- **4 UK arrests** linked to the running of the site, **including LabHost's original developer**.

title: Lai Châu Provincial Police + Vietnam MPS — Bokor Hill (Kampot, Cambodia) Transnational Online-Fraud Takedown (28-Oct-2025)
text:
## Summary

On **28 October 2025**, a special working group of **Lai Châu Provincial Police of Việt Nam** led — in coordination with the **Representative Office of the Ministry of Public Security of Việt Nam in Cambodia** (Cơ quan đại diện Bộ Công an Việt Nam tại Campuchia), the **Criminal Police Department of the MPS** (Cục Cảnh sát Hình sự — Bộ Công an), and **Cambodian functional forces** — the takedown of a Vietnamese-staffed online-fraud cell operating from the **Bokor Hill area within the Bokor Ecological Park complex, Tuek Chhou ward, Bokor city, Kampot province, Kingdom of Cambodia**.

The operation produced **59 on-site arrests** (44 male, 15 female), all Vietnamese nationals. As of the date of the press release, **66 suspects in total** had been arrested in connection with the case. Direct command was held by **Senior Lieutenant General Nguyễn Văn Long, Deputy Minister of Public Security and Head of the Police Investigation Agency** of the MPS. The named ringleader is **Sùng Thị Mải** (alias "Vi"), born 1999, from Mon Đào hamlet, Thái Niên commune, Bảo Thắng district, Lào Cai province (now Bảo Thắng commune, Lào Cai).

The press release reports the cell's modi operandi as **impersonation of shippers (delivery drivers), Police, and Military**, plus **romance-fraud (lừa đảo tình cảm)**, with **more than 8,000 victims nationwide** in Việt Nam and an **estimated appropriated total of more than 300 billion VND** (~12 million USD at late-2025 exchange rates). After Cambodian-side triage, **Cambodian functional forces handed over all 59 suspects and seized physical evidence** to the Police Investigation Agency — Lai Châu Provincial Police of Việt Nam, for Vietnamese-domestic prosecution.

## Background

The takedown was placed by Báo Chính phủ in the immediate diplomatic context of the **Signing Ceremony of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime (the "Hà Nội Convention")**, hosted by Việt Nam in Hà Nội on **25-26 October 2025**, which had ended just days before the operation. The press release explicitly frames the takedown as evidence of "close cooperation between Việt Nam and the Kingdom of Cambodia in general, and between the police forces of the two countries in particular," in that immediate post-signing context.

> [!note] Translation note
> The Vietnamese phrase "lực lượng chức năng của Campuchia" is rendered here as "Cambodian functional forces" — the generic Vietnamese formulation for "competent / responsible Cambodian authorities," without naming a specific Cambodian agency. The most plausible operational counterpart is the [[cambodian-national-police|Cambodian National Police]], but the source as written does not specify the Cambodian agency.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Operational lead (Vietnamese side) | Lai Châu Provincial Police of Việt Nam — special working group |
| Vietnamese liaison node in Cambodia | Representative Office of the Ministry of Public Security of Việt Nam in Cambodia (Cơ quan đại diện Bộ Công an Việt Nam tại Campuchia) |
| Vietnamese central support unit | Criminal Police Department, Ministry of Public Security (Cục Cảnh sát Hình sự — Bộ Công an) |
| Direct command authority | Senior Lieutenant General Nguyễn Văn Long, Deputy Minister and Head of the Police Investigation Agency, MPS |
| Cambodian-side counterpart | "Lực lượng chức năng của Campuchia" (Cambodian functional forces) — agency-level identity not specified in the press release |
| Country where cell operated | [[cambodia\|Cambodia]] (Bokor Hill, Tuek Chhou ward, Bokor city, Kampot province) |
| Country of suspects' nationality | [[vietnam\|Việt Nam]] (all 59 on-site arrestees, plus the named ringleader) |
| Country of victims | [[vietnam\|Việt Nam]] (more than 8,000 victims nationwide, per the press release) |
| Named ringleader | Sùng Thị Mải (alias "Vi"), born 1999, Lào Cai province (Việt Nam) |

## Legal Framework

| Instrument / authority | Function in this operation |
