title: Operation WireWire
text:
## Summary

Operation WireWire was a coordinated international law enforcement operation led by the U.S. Department of Justice, FBI, Department of Homeland Security, and Treasury Department to disrupt Business Email Compromise (BEC) and Email Account Compromise (EAC) schemes. The operation resulted in 74 arrests across the United States, Nigeria, Canada, Mauritius, and Poland, with approximately $2.4 million seized and $14 million in fraudulent wire transfers intercepted and recovered.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agencies:**
- U.S. Department of Justice
- FBI
- U.S. Department of Homeland Security
- U.S. Department of the Treasury
- U.S. Postal Inspection Service

**Participating Countries (5):**
United States, Nigeria, Canada, Mauritius, Poland

## Results

- **74 arrests** across 5 countries
- **$2.4 million** seized
- **$14 million** in fraudulent wire transfers intercepted and recovered
- BEC/EAC criminal networks disrupted

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ (District of Connecticut), 2016-12-20: Nigerian Nationals Charged with Operating Business Compromise Scheme.
- FBI, 2018-06-11: FBI: International BEC Takedown (Operation Wire Wire).
- Security Affairs, 2018-06-12: Operation WireWire – Law enforcement arrested 74 individuals involved in BEC scams.
- WeLiveSecurity / ESET, 2018-06-12: 74 people arrested in US-led crackdown on email scams.
- Infosecurity Magazine, 2018-06-12: BEC Scammers Disrupted in Multi-Million Dollar Swoop.

## Operational Timeline

- 2017: activity or investigation start.
- 2018-06-11: public announcement.
- 2018-06: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2016-12-20: public source coverage from US DOJ (District of Connecticut).
- 2018-06-11: public source coverage from FBI.
- 2018-06-12: public source coverage from Infosecurity Magazine, Security Affairs, WeLiveSecurity / ESET.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: BEC and malware.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest, Seizure, Asset Freeze.
- The record is categorized as arrest-sweep with status completed.
- Related legal or operational records: United States v. Odufuye and Nwoke (BEC) and Us V Odufuye Nwoke Bec.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- On 22 December 2016, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Connecticut announced charges against Adeyemi Odufuye and another defendant in a business email compromise scheme targeting U.S. businesses.
- DOJ said a federal grand jury had returned a seven-count indictment on 20 December 2016 charging conspiracy, five wire-fraud counts, and aggravated identity theft.
- The subsequent DOJ record shows the case became a clear example of multinational custody and evidence work in a BEC prosecution.
- Odufuye was arrested in the United Kingdom in December 2016 and extradited to the United States.
- Stanley Nwoke was later arrested in Mauritius in May 2018, extradited to the United States on 25 May 2018, pleaded guilty in May 2019, and was sentenced in August 2019.
- DOJ also identified assistance from the Office of International Affairs, the UK National Crime Agency, the UK Metropolitan Police, and the Mauritius Police Force.
- The public case materials describe a scheme that targeted CEOs, CFOs, controllers, nonprofits, and schools across the United States through spoofed executive emails and, in some instances, malware-bearing attachments.
- The case is a useful court anchor for Operation WireWire because it shows how a broad BEC arrest wave translated into extradition-backed district-court outcomes.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_END -->

<!-- RAW_TEXT_HIGHLIGHTS_START -->

## Raw Source Highlights

- US DOJ (District of Connecticut), 2016-

title: Operazione ELICIUS — Italy-France-Romania Joint Task Force vs. "Diskstation" NAS Ransomware Gang (2024-2025)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source page
> This page is published below the preferred 5-source threshold (CLAUDE.md "entity creation threshold") because the underlying tier-1 primary source (Polizia di Stato official press release) provides extensive verifiable detail (named LE partners across three countries, named prosecution office, specific custody actor, specific charge articles, specific search location and date). Additional tier-1 sources from France's police nationale and Romania's poliție will be added as identified. Until then, treat enumerated results as preliminary and check back for tier-1 corroboration.

## Summary

Operazione ELICIUS is a joint Italy-France-Romania police operation against the "Diskstation" ransomware gang, a Romanian-nationality organised criminal group that targeted small and medium-sized Italian enterprises across the Lombardy region with double-extortion ransomware attacks exploiting Synology DiskStation NAS appliances. The operation was led by the Italian [[italy-polizia-postale|Servizio Polizia Postale e per la Sicurezza Cibernetica]] from the Centro Operativo per la Sicurezza Cibernetica (C.O.S.C.) Milano, prosecuted by the [[italy|Procura di Milano]], and coordinated at EU level by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] with [[france-national-police|French national police]] and [[romania-police|Romanian Police]] as foreign LE partners. Searches were executed in June 2024 in Bucharest; the operation became publicly known on 14 July 2025 when the Polizia di Stato published the announcement.

## Background

The investigation began following a wave of complaints filed by companies operating in the Lombardy region (Lombardia, northern Italy) that had had the data on their information systems encrypted, resulting in operational paralysis. Targeted sectors named in the primary source: graphic design, film production, event organisation, and NGOs active internationally in civil-rights protection and charity. To recover their data, victims would have had to pay heavy cryptocurrency ransoms.

The "Diskstation" moniker is the gang's self-attribution and refers to the brand of network-attached storage (NAS) appliances — Synology DiskStation — whose vulnerabilities the gang exploited as initial access vector. (Note: the primary source does not enumerate specific CVEs.)

## Participating Parties

**Italian side:**
- **Lead investigative agency:** [[italy-polizia-postale|Servizio Polizia Postale e per la Sicurezza Cibernetica]] (Postal Police and Cyber Security Service), part of the [[polizia-di-stato|Polizia di Stato]] (Italian State Police)
- **Operations centre:** Centro Operativo per la Sicurezza Cibernetica (C.O.S.C.) di Milano
- **Prosecution authority:** Procura di Milano (Milan Public Prosecutor's Office)
- **Pre-trial custody actor:** Giudice per le Indagini Preliminari (GIP) presso il Tribunale di Milano

**Foreign LE partners (named in tier-1 source):**
- **France:** "polizie nazionali di Francia" — i.e. [[france-national-police|French national police]] (Police Nationale)
- **Romania:** "polizie nazionali di Romania" — i.e. [[romania-police|Romanian Police]] (Poliția Română)

**Coordination:**
- [[europol-ec3|EUROPOL]] — formally established the joint task force.

## Legal Framework

The Italian charges against the principal suspect rest on two articles of the Italian Criminal Code:
- **Art. 615-ter c.p.** — *Accesso abusivo a un sistema informatico o telematico* (unauthorised access to a computer or telecommunications system).
- **Art. 629 c.p.** — *Estorsione* (extortion).

Both offences are within the substantive scope of the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] (Articles 2 — illegal access — and 11 / accompanying provisions for extortion-type abuse), of which Italy, France, and Romania are all parties. Cooperation across the three jurisdictions in this operation aligns with the Convention's chapter on international cooperation (Articles 23-35) and its dual-criminality framework.

title: PF Belgium Extradition Arrest — São Paulo, Belgian national wanted for IT-forgery and fraud (March 2026)
title_ko: PF 벨기에 범죄인인도 체포 — 상파울루, IT 위변조·사기 혐의 벨기에 국적자 (2026-03)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source page
> This page is published with **source_count = 1** because the only public primary record currently available is the PF/SP press release of 2026-03-09. Per the wiki's preferred-threshold rule (≥5 sources), this is a provisional record. It will be promoted on (a) STF docket publication, (b) Belgian SPF Justice / federal prosecutor confirmation, (c) any Interpol Red Notice publication, or (d) Brazilian DRCI status update.

## Summary

On **2026-03-09** the **Polícia Federal (PF)** in **São Paulo** arrested a **Belgian national** residing in Brazil on a **Brazilian Supreme Federal Court (STF) arrest warrant for extradition purposes** (*mandado de prisão para extradição*), in response to a **Belgian extradition request**. The Belgian underlying charges, as enumerated by the PF release, include **document forgery**, **criminal threat**, **fraud**, **falsificação de tecnologia da informação** (information-technology forgery — corresponding to **computer-related forgery, Budapest Convention Art. 7**), **breach of trust**, and additional unspecified offences. The defendant is in Brazilian custody pending the STF passive-extradition proceeding.

## Background

The PF release frames the case as a routine *prisão para extradição* — the standard Brazilian device for detaining a person sought by a foreign state pending a STF decision on a passive-extradition petition. The defendant was already known to Brazilian law enforcement (the release notes *outras ocorrências criminais* — additional Brazilian criminal incidents) and had been residing in Brazil before the Belgian extradition request was formalised.

The Belgian charges include a hybrid cluster — document forgery, fraud, IT-forgery, and breach of trust — that is characteristic of computer-enabled financial-fraud cases under Belgian law. *Falsification informatique / informaticavalsheid* (Belgian Code pénal / Strafwetboek Art. 210bis) corresponds to the Budapest Convention's **Art. 7 — computer-related forgery**. *Fraude informatique / informaticabedrog* (Art. 504quater) corresponds to **Art. 8 — computer-related fraud**. The PF wording *falsificação de tecnologia da informação* is a Portuguese rendering of the IT-forgery charge that is not a Brazilian statutory term but is functionally equivalent.

> [!note] Translation note
> The release uses the phrase *falsificação de tecnologia da informação* — literally "falsification of information technology". This is the PF's translation of the Belgian charge label, not a Brazilian Penal Code offence category. In Brazilian dual-criminality analysis (see [[dual-criminality]]), the closest counterparts are likely Lei 12.737/2012 (Lei Carolina Dieckmann) Penal Code Arts. **154-A** (invasão de dispositivo informático) and **154-B**, combined with Art. **298** (falsificação de documento particular) where computer data is treated as document. The release does not assert this mapping; it is offered here as background only.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Lead investigative authority (Brazil) | Polícia Federal — Superintendência Regional em São Paulo (SR/SP), via Comunicação Social SCS/SR/SP. PF reports to the Ministério da Justiça e Segurança Pública ([[brazil-ministry-of-justice-public-security]]). |
| Brazilian judicial authority | Supremo Tribunal Federal (STF) — exclusive jurisdiction over passive extradition under Brazilian Constitution Art. 102, I, "g" |
| Requesting state | [[belgium]] — *autoridades belgas* (Belgian authorities); specific Belgian counterpart agency NOT named in the PF release |
| Requested state | [[brazil]] |

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The PF release does not name the **specific Belgian counterpart authority**. Likely candidates are the *SPF Justice / Service de la Coopération internationale en matière pénale*, the federal prosecutor (*procureur fédéral / federaal procureur*), or a specific Belgian investigating judge — but the wiki must not

title: Operação Magna Fraus Phase 2 (PF-INTERPOL Brazil-Spain-Argentina-Portugal PIX bank-fraud takedown, October 2025)
title_ko: Operação Magna Fraus 2단계 (브라질 연방경찰-INTERPOL 브라질-스페인-아르헨티나-포르투갈 PIX 은행 사기 단속, 2025-10)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / Single-Source
> This page is published below the preferred 5-source threshold (single tier-1 primary source: PF press release of 2025-10-30). It is retained as a primary-source-grounded record because the IC pattern (Brazil-led, INTERPOL-mediated, four-NCB Iberian-LatAm action-day against a PIX-rail bank-fraud organisation) is novel for the wiki. To be re-evaluated when secondary corroboration (Spanish Policía Nacional, INTERPOL, Argentine Policía Federal, or Brazilian press) becomes available.

## Summary

On **2025-10-30**, the Brazilian Federal Police (Polícia Federal, **PF**) launched **Phase 2 of Operação Magna Fraus** ("Great Fraud Operation"), a coordinated multi-jurisdiction action against an organised criminal group that drained more than **R$ 813 million** (≈ USD 145M at October 2025 rates) from accounts used by Brazilian banks and payment institutions to manage customer **PIX** (Brazilian Central Bank instant-payment system) transfers. The action combined federal-police execution in 11 cities across 7 Brazilian states with simultaneous international arrests in **Spain (6) and Argentina (2)**, coordinated through INTERPOL's Command and Coordination Centre (CCC) and National Central Bureaux in Brazil, Spain, Argentina, and Portugal. As of 2025-10-31, **21 of 26 arrest warrants had been executed** (13 in Brazil + 8 abroad), 42 search-and-seizure warrants had been served, and asset-blocking orders up to R$ 640M were in force, with 15 high-end vehicles, 25 real-estate properties, R$ 1M+ in cryptocurrency, and luxury articles seized.

## Background

Operação Magna Fraus Phase 1 (date and details not specified in the cited release) preceded this action; Phase 2 expanded the warrant footprint and added the international-arrests track. The investigation was conducted by PF with the prosecutorial cooperation of **Cyber GAECO** (Grupo de Atuação Especial de Combate ao Crime Organizado), the São Paulo state Public Prosecution Service's specialised cyber-organised-crime task group of the Ministério Público do Estado de São Paulo (상파울루주 검찰청 사이버조직범죄대응팀, MP-SP).

The criminal-organisation modus operandi targeted **infrastructure accounts** — i.e. accounts that banks and payment institutions themselves use to manage their customers' PIX-rail transfers — rather than individual customer accounts. This shifted the attack vector from end-user phishing to institutional-account compromise via **invasão de dispositivo informático** (computer-device intrusion), prosecuted under article 154-A of the Brazilian Penal Code (Lei nº 12.737/2012, the "Lei Carolina Dieckmann"). Combined with **furto mediante fraude eletrônica** (theft by electronic fraud, art. 155 §4-B) and **lavagem de dinheiro** (money laundering, Lei nº 9.613/1998), the case represents a hybrid cybercrime / financial-crime prosecution typical of contemporary PF cyber-fraud doctrine.

## Participating Parties

**Brazilian side**
- **Polícia Federal (PF)** — federal investigative authority and lead agency, executing 42 search-and-seizure and 26 arrest warrants across 11 cities (Goiânia/GO, Brasília/DF, Itajaí/SC, Balneário Camboriú/SC, São Paulo/SP, Praia Grande/SP, Belo Horizonte/MG, Betim/MG, Uberlândia/MG, João Pessoa/PB, Camaçari/BA).
- **Cyber GAECO of MP-SP** — São Paulo state Public Prosecution Service's specialised organised-crime task group; prosecutorial co-operation partner.

**International side (cooperation partners explicitly named in the PF release)**
- **INTERPOL Command and Coordination Centre (CCC)** — coordination node for simultaneous international arrests.
- **INTERPOL National Central Bureau Brazil** (host NCB).
- **INTERPOL National Central Bureau Spain** (action-day partner; 6 arrests).
- **INTERPOL National Central Bureau Argentina** (action-day partner; 2 arrests).
- **INTERPOL National Central Bureau Portugal** (cooperation partner; no arrests reported in partial balance).
- **Brigada Central de Fraudes Informáticos** of the **Pol

title: Phobos/8Base Ransomware Crackdown
text:
> [!note] Source basis
> This page is anchored to Europol's official February 11, 2025 release and reinforced with DOJ material about related Phobos arrests and the South Korea to United States extradition path. It replaces a malformed draft with broken frontmatter and excessive hedging.

## Summary

**Phobos/8Base Ransomware Crackdown** was a sustained multinational investigation against the **Phobos** ransomware ecosystem and **8Base** operators. Public official material supports a six-year investigative arc, **four arrests** announced in February 2025, **27 servers taken down**, and a meaningful Asia-Europe-U.S. linkage through the **arrest in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and extradition to the [[united-states|United States]] of a Phobos administrator**.

## Background

The operation did not arise from a single action day. Europol states that it had supported the investigation since February 2019, with dozens of operational meetings and hundreds of SIENA exchanges. Prior arrests in Italy and South Korea fed intelligence into the final February 2025 action against key 8Base figures.

## Participating Parties

### Coordination
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- [[eurojust|Eurojust]]

### Publicly Named Participating Countries
- [[belgium|Belgium]]
- [[czechia|Czechia]]
- [[france|France]]
- [[germany|Germany]]
- [[japan|Japan]]
- [[poland|Poland]]
- [[romania|Romania]]
- [[singapore|Singapore]]
- [[south-korea|South Korea]]
- [[spain|Spain]]
- [[sweden|Sweden]]
- [[switzerland|Switzerland]]
- [[thailand|Thailand]]
- [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom]]
- [[united-states|United States]]

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Arrests announced in final crackdown | 4 |
| Servers taken down | 27 |
| Companies warned | 400+ |
| Operational meetings | 37 |
| SIENA messages | nearly 600 |

This is one of the cleaner examples in the corpus of a ransomware operation where cross-border coordination is visible not only in the final action day but across the full investigative lifecycle.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The sources explicitly support three mechanisms: Europol SIENA-based operational exchange, Eurojust judicial coordination, and extradition. They do not fully disclose the arrest and extradition paperwork used in every jurisdiction, so the page avoids claiming instruments not named in public reporting.

## Korean Involvement

This operation has confirmed Korean relevance. Official material states that a Phobos administrator was arrested in [[south-korea|South Korea]] in June 2024 and extradited to the [[united-states|United States]] in November 2024. That makes this a stronger international-cooperation case than many ransomware entries because the Korea-U.S. link is not inferred; it is directly stated in the official narrative.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Public sources do not identify every agency involved in the South Korea arrest and extradition process.
- The public record supports the existence of a Korea-U.S. extradition step but not the full legal filing chain.
- Later prosecution outcomes for all four February 2025 arrestees remain thin in the currently collected source set.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Europol, 2025-02-11: Key figures behind Phobos and 8Base ransomware arrested in international cybercrime crackdown.
- US DOJ (Office of Public Affairs), 2025-02-10: Phobos Ransomware Affiliates Arrested in Coordinated International Disruption.
- US DOJ USAO, 2024-11-18: Phobos Ransomware Administrator Extradited From South Korea To Face Cybercrime Charges.
- BleepingComputer, 2025-02-11: U.S. charges Phobos and 8Base ransomware operators after international crackdown.
- TechCrunch, 2025-02-11: Authorities arrest four suspected 8base ransomware operators in global takedown.

## Operational Timeline

- 2019-02-01: activity or investigation start.
- 2025-02-11: public announcement.
- 2025-02-11: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2024-11-18: public source coverag

title: Poland CBZC arrest of Phobos-linked tooling suspect (Operation Aether, February 2026)
title_ko: 폴란드 CBZC, Phobos 도구 공급 혐의 47세 남성 체포 (Operation Aether, 2026년 2월)
text:
> [!note] Provisional / single-source page
> This page is published below the standard preferred source threshold (`source_count >= 5`). It is retained as a provisional record because the Polish CBZC press release is a tier-1 primary source documenting (a) a previously unrecorded action wave of the Europol-coordinated Operation Aether campaign and (b) the first appearance of "Operation Aether" as a named umbrella in the wiki's primary-source corpus. It should be enriched as Europol/Eurojust corroborating sources are added.

## Summary

On **2026-02-17** the Polish [[poland-police|Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime (CBZC)]] announced the arrest in **Małopolskie Voivodeship** of a 47-year-old Polish national suspected of producing, acquiring and distributing computer programs used to unlawfully obtain information stored in IT systems (Polish Criminal Code Article 269b § 1; maximum 5 years imprisonment). On the suspect's computer, police seized digital data including logins, passwords, credit card numbers and server IP addresses that CBZC described as usable for various attacks, including [[ransomware-ic|ransomware]]. The 47-year-old allegedly used encrypted messengers to contact the **Phobos** ransomware group.

CBZC explicitly attributes the arrest to its participation in **Operation Aether**, coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]]. The press release identifies the prior extradition of the alleged Phobos administrator to the [[united-states|United States]] and "coordinated arrests in Europe and beyond" as elements of the same campaign, alongside technical actions against cybercriminal infrastructure.

## Background

Phobos has been the target of an evolving international law enforcement campaign tracked elsewhere in this wiki under [[phobos-8base-crackdown|the Phobos/8Base ransomware crackdown]] and [[operation-us-v-evgenii-ptitsyn|United States v. Evgenii Ptitsyn]] (the South Korea–to–USA extradition of the alleged Phobos administrator). The CBZC release describes Phobos as a Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) organized cybercriminal group with more than 1,000 victims worldwide, including hospitals, schools, non-profit organizations, public bodies and private companies. According to [[us-doj|US Department of Justice]] documents cited by CBZC, total ransom proceeds associated with Phobos activity exceeded USD 16 million.

The 2026-02-17 Polish arrest is one of the first publicly named Operation Aether enforcement events in the wiki's primary-source corpus and the first to surface the "Aether" branding in CBZC's own communication.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead apprehending authority | [[poland-police\|Polish CBZC — joint Katowice and Kielce divisions]] |
| Domestic prosecutorial supervision | District Prosecutor's Office in Gliwice (Prokuratura Okręgowa w Gliwicach) |
| Coordinating body | [[europol-ec3\|Europol]] (named on-source as coordinator of Operation Aether) |
| Cooperating jurisdiction (named in source) | [[united-states\|United States]] — referenced through the prior extradition of the alleged Phobos administrator to the USA and via [[us-doj\|US Department of Justice]] documents on Phobos ransom totals |
| Source-backed participating countries | [[poland\|Poland]], [[united-states\|United States]] |

> [!note] Audit note on participating_countries
> Per L17 / L19, only countries explicitly named in the tier-1 primary source are asserted as `participating_countries`. The CBZC release names Poland (locus of arrest) and the USA (extradition recipient and DOJ documents). Other Europe-or-beyond jurisdictions referenced as participating in Operation Aether are not named individually in this source and are left out of the country list pending corroboration.

## Legal Framework

- **Substantive charge**: Polish Criminal Code (Kodeks karny) **Article 269b § 1** — producing, acquiring or distributing computer programs designed to unlawfully obtain information stored in IT systems (hacking tools).

title: Polri INTERPOL Red Notice Arrest of LCS — abbishopee Cambodia-based Online Fraud Network (2026)
title_ko: 인도네시아 경찰, 캄보디아 거점 'abbishopee' 온라인 사기 조직 LCS INTERPOL 적색수배자 검거 (2026)
text:
# Polri INTERPOL Red Notice Arrest of LCS — abbishopee Cambodia-based Online Fraud Network (2026)

> [!info] Provisional record
> This page rests on a single tier-1 primary source — an Indonesian-language press release on Polri's official Tribrata News portal (`tribratanews.polri.go.id`) dated 2026-05-05. It is below the preferred 5-source threshold for a normal operation page and is filed as a provisional arrest-sweep record. It will be promoted when (a) Antara/Kompas/Detik or other Indonesian-language coverage with corroborating detail is ingested, (b) any Cambodian or third-country statement on the Red Notice chain surfaces, or (c) Indonesian charging documents or court records on LCS or the prior three convictions are released.

## Summary

On **Sunday, 3 May 2026**, a joint task force of the **Indonesian National Police** (Kepolisian Negara Republik Indonesia, "Polri") arrested an Indonesian national identified by the initials **LCS** at **Bandara Internasional Soekarno-Hatta** in Jakarta. LCS was on the **INTERPOL Red Notice** wanted list and on the wanted-persons list (DPO) of Polri's **Cyber Crime Directorate** (*Direktorat Tindak Pidana Siber*, "Dittipidsiber"), within the **Criminal Investigation Agency** (*Badan Reserse Kriminal*, "Bareskrim").

LCS is alleged to have served as an **operator** of an online-fraud platform branded "**abbishopee**", run from an international fraud network based in **Cambodia** (Kamboja). The case had accumulated at least **23 separate police reports** (Laporan Polisi) across regions of Indonesia, all of which had previously been consolidated under Dittipidsiber Bareskrim Polri for centralized investigation. Three other suspects in the same network had already been arrested in earlier phases and were prosecuted to judgment at the **South Jakarta District Court** (*Pengadilan Negeri Jakarta Selatan*) before LCS himself was located.

The arrest was announced by Polri's Tribrata News national portal on **2026-05-05** at 09:59 WIB. The senior official on the record is **Brig. Gen. Pol. Himawan Bayu Aji**, Director of Cyber Crime at Bareskrim Polri, speaking in Jakarta on Monday, 4 May 2026.

This case qualifies for the IC corpus because (a) the foreign operating base of the fraud network is named (Cambodia), and (b) the multilateral coordination mechanism — INTERPOL Red Notice — is explicitly invoked, presupposing alert and tracking cooperation across at least one third country in addition to Indonesia and Cambodia.

## Background

Cambodia has emerged in 2024-2026 as one of the principal Southeast Asian "scam-compound" host states, with a recurring pattern in which the operators of online-fraud platforms targeting victims in a neighbouring country (here Indonesia, in earlier wiki cases also Vietnam, Korea, China) are themselves nationals of the targeted state who run their operation from Cambodian compounds. The wiki's [[lai-chau-cambodia-bokor-transnational-online-fraud-takedown-2025|Lai Chau / Cambodia Bokor takedown (2025)]] is a Vietnam-led example of the same dynamic; the present case is the Indonesia-led equivalent for a single named operator.

The branded platform "abbishopee" is named in the source as the vehicle used by LCS to run the online-fraud activity. The release does not specify whether "abbishopee" was a phishing site impersonating a legitimate Indonesian e-commerce brand (the orthography evokes "Shopee", a major Southeast Asian e-commerce platform), a stand-alone investment / shopping-fraud platform, or another category. No corroborating brand-impersonation analysis is offered in the release.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| **Lead investigating agency** | [[indonesia-police\|Indonesian National Police (Polri) - Bareskrim - Dittipidsiber]] |
| **Senior official cited** | Brig. Gen. Pol. Himawan Bayu Aji, Director of Cyber Crime (Direktur Tindak Pidana Siber), Bareskrim Polri |
| **Multilateral mechanism** | [[interpol\|INTERPOL]] — Red Notice |
| **

title: Portugal-Spain EAW Surrender of Luso-Angolan Cyber Fraud Money-Laundering Suspect (2025)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source
> This page is below the preferred source threshold (5+) and is published as a provisional record of a single tier-1 PJ press release. Expand when corroborating Spanish or UK statements appear.

## Summary

On 22 September 2025, Portugal's Policia Judiciaria announced that a 30-year-old Portuguese-Angolan dual national had been arrested by Spanish authorities under a European Arrest Warrant issued by the DIAP da Amadora and surrendered to Portugal at the Caya/Elvas land border crossing. The suspect is charged with computer fraud (burla informatica), unauthorized computer access (acesso ilegitimo), and money laundering (branqueamento de capitais) tied to an online-gaming-platform laundering structure under investigation since 2024.

## Background

The investigation, opened in 2024 and directed by the Departamento de Investigacao e Accao Penal da Amadora (DIAP da Amadora) of the Public Ministry, focused on a structure dedicated to laundering proceeds from cyber-enabled fraud through online gaming platforms. Portugal's National Unit for Combating Cybercrime and Technological Crime (Unidade Nacional de Combate ao Cibercrime e a Criminalidade Tecnologica - UNC3T), a unit of [[portugal-policia-judiciaria|Policia Judiciaria]], compiled the underlying evidence. The PJ's International Cooperation Unit (Unidade de Cooperacao Internacional - UCI) handled cross-border judicial coordination.

## Participating Parties

- **Portugal**: Policia Judiciaria (UNC3T as investigating cybercrime unit; UCI as international cooperation unit); DIAP da Amadora of the Public Ministry; Criminal Court of Amadora (judicial authority).
- **Spain**: Spanish judicial and police authorities (executing-state authorities for the European Arrest Warrant; specific agency not named in the PJ press release).
- **United Kingdom**: Identified in the source only as the suspect's place of residence from April 2025 until relocation to Spain. No UK law enforcement participation is described in the source.
- **Angola**: Identified in the source only as the suspect's secondary nationality. No Angolan agency cooperation is described in the source.

## Legal Framework

- [[european-arrest-warrant|European Arrest Warrant]] issued by the DIAP da Amadora (Portugal as issuing state; Spain as executing state).
- Underlying Portuguese criminal charges: computer fraud, illegal computer access, and money laundering.

## Operational Timeline

- 2024: PJ / DIAP da Amadora opens the underlying cyber-fraud / money-laundering investigation.
- April 2025: Suspect is in the United Kingdom, per the PJ press release.
- September 2025: Suspect is located in Spain and arrested by Spanish authorities under the European Arrest Warrant.
- 2025-09-22: PJ publishes the press release announcing the surrender; the suspect is presented to the Criminal Court of Amadora, which orders preventive detention.

## Results and Impact

- 1 suspect arrested in Spain and surrendered to Portugal.
- Preventive detention ordered by the Criminal Court of Amadora.
- No monetary recovery, server seizure, or victim count is reported in the source.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

- Direct EU judicial cooperation via the European Arrest Warrant, without recourse to [[eurojust|Eurojust]] or [[europol-ec3|Europol]] coordination as documented in this source.
- PJ-internal liaison through UCI (International Cooperation Unit).
- Physical surrender at the Caya/Elvas land border crossing between Portugal and Spain.

## Challenges and Friction Points

- *Likely*: Tracing a dual-national suspect who relocated from the United Kingdom (post-Brexit, outside the EU EAW system) to Spain (within the EAW system) implies the suspect's earlier UK presence could not be addressed by EAW alone; the actual arrest was triggered only after the move to Spain. The source does not describe how UK presence was monitored. Confidence: roughly even chance, based on inference from the chronology in a single source.

title: Project Compass — Europol ECTC Cooperation against The Com Network (28-country, January 2025 — ongoing)
title_ko: Compass 프로젝트 — Europol ECTC 'The Com' 네트워크 대응 협력 (28개국, 2025-01~ 진행중)
text:
## Summary

**Project Compass** is an ongoing **Europol European Counter Terrorism Centre (ECTC)**-coordinated cooperation project, active since **January 2025**, uniting **28 country partners** to fight against **The Com (Community)** — a nihilistic extremist transnational virtual network targeting minors and vulnerable young people. Partner roster includes Five Eyes states (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand), 22 EU Member States, and 3 EFTA partners (Iceland, Norway, Switzerland). As of the captured Europol Operations page, results include **30 perpetrators arrested** (contributed to AP Dolphin), **4 victims safeguarded**, **62 victims identified or partially identified**, **179 perpetrators identified or partially identified**, and **9 joint awareness-raising activities**.

## Background

The Com operates openly on various online platforms — social media, communication and messaging apps, music platforms, and online games — and is structured into three subgroups:

- **Cyber Com** — Targets online commerce or infrastructure by orchestrating network intrusions and deployment of ransomware.
- **Offline Com** — Encourages damage to property, harm to others or acts of terrorism to promote a violent nihilistic worldview.
- **(S)extortion Com** — Extorts minors to commit both online and offline sex crimes, as well as the promotion of self-harm and suicide, crimes against people, animals and property.

Within The Com, group **764** has been active since **2021** and is notorious for its recruitment and grooming tactics as well as its levels of violence. The group targets young people, encouraging them to participate in violent acts and create explicit content, which is then used to blackmail victims or shared among group members. Over time, 764's activities have become intertwined with violent extremist groups and other harmful networks promoting satanic or accelerationist ideologies.

## Participating Parties

| Region | Partners (28) |
|---|---|
| Five Eyes | Australia; Canada (RCMP); New Zealand; United Kingdom (UK CT Policing, [[uk-nca\|NCA]]); United States ([[fbi\|FBI]], HSI) |
| EU Member States | Austria, Belgium, Croatia, Czechia, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, Slovenia, Spain, Sweden |
| EFTA / non-EU European | Iceland, Norway, Switzerland |
| Coordination authority | [[europol-ec3\|Europol European Counter Terrorism Centre (ECTC)]] |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2021 | The Com / 764 subgroup begins active recruitment, grooming, and violence-coercion targeting minors | Europol Compass page |
| 2025-01 | Project Compass launched at Europol ECTC | Europol Compass page |
| (date not enumerated) | First Project Compass data sprints; partner countries present ongoing cases | Europol Compass page |
| 2026 (capture date approximate) | Results snapshot: 30 arrests; 4 victims safeguarded; 62 identified victims; 179 identified perpetrators; 9 awareness-raising activities | Europol Compass page |

## Results and Impact

- **30 perpetrators arrested** (contributed to AP Dolphin).
- **4 victims safeguarded.**
- **62 identified and partially identified victims.**
- **179 identified and partially identified perpetrators.**
- **9 joint awareness-raising activities.**

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The Europol Compass page describes the following discrete IC mechanism classes:

1. **Europol ECTC project framework** — long-running coordination structure for sustained intelligence-sharing and case coordination across 28 countries.
2. **Data sprints** — Europol-organised meetings where invited countries present their ongoing cases to coordinate investigations and identify cross-border links. First wiki record of this mechanism class.
3. **Five-Eyes + EU + EFTA participation under Europol Working Arrangements** — partnership architecture spanning EU member states, EFTA non-EU European partner

title: SocksEscort / AVrecon Residential Proxy Takedown (March 2026)
text:
## Summary

On **11 March 2026**, a US-European law-enforcement action disrupted **SocksEscort**, a residential proxy service that monetized routers and IoT devices infected with **AVrecon** malware. [[eurojust|Eurojust]] announced the public takedown on **12 March 2026**; the [[us-doj|US Department of Justice]] and [[fbi|FBI]] materials identify the service and malware family that the earlier Eurojust framing did not name.

The action was materially larger than the original page reflected. Eurojust reported **24 servers taken down in seven countries**, **34 domains seized**, infected modems disconnected from the proxy service, approximately **EUR 3.5 million in cryptocurrency frozen by US authorities**, about **369,000 compromised routers and other devices across 163 countries**, and roughly **124,000 customer users**. DOJ added that SocksEscort had offered access to about **369,000 IP addresses since summer 2020** and that, as of February 2026, the service listed about **8,000 active infected routers**, including about **2,500 in the United States**.

> [!note] Reconciliation note
> This page originally treated the service as unnamed because it was based on a short Eurojust ingest. Subsequent official DOJ and FBI publications identify the service as **SocksEscort** and the malware as **AVrecon**. The "4 unnamed countries" and "servers not stated" gaps are therefore resolved.

## What Was Targeted

SocksEscort sold paid access to residential IP addresses so customers could route traffic through ordinary home and small-business networks. That made malicious activity appear to originate from legitimate consumer connections rather than from known criminal infrastructure.

The technical substrate was **AVrecon**, malware used to compromise routers and IoT devices. The FBI FLASH says the malware targeted routers and IoT devices across approximately 163 countries, primarily SOHO devices and consumer networking equipment. The proxy service then sold access to those devices as residential proxies.

## Criminal Use

The sources document several abuse patterns:

- Concealing the origin of fraud traffic through residential IP addresses
- Takeovers of bank and cryptocurrency accounts
- Fraudulent unemployment-insurance claims
- Ad fraud, password spraying, marketplace fraud, banking fraud, and romance-fraud activity observed by the FBI and partners
- Use of the proxy network to evade IP reputation, geolocation, and block-list controls

DOJ listed concrete US victim examples, including a New York cryptocurrency-exchange customer defrauded of about USD 1 million in cryptocurrency, a Pennsylvania manufacturing business defrauded of about USD 700,000, and current or former US service members whose MILITARY STAR cards were defrauded of about USD 100,000.

## Participating Parties

| Country / Body | Publicly identified participants | Role |
|---|---|---|
| [[united-states]] | [[us-doj]], US Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of California, [[fbi|FBI Sacramento]], [[us-dcis|DCIS]], IRS Criminal Investigation Oakland, DOJ OIA, DOJ National Security Cyber Section, DOJ CCIPS, ICHIP The Hague, FinCEN, California Highway Patrol | US-led court-authorized disruption, domain seizures, investigation, crypto/financial support |
| [[france]] | Paris J3 Anti-Cybercrime unit, JIRS/JUNALCO financial and cybercrime section, [[france-national-police\|OFAC]] | Server/infrastructure action and judicial coordination |
| [[austria]] | Criminal Intelligence Service Austria, Cybercrime-Competence-Center C4, Public Prosecutor's Office Vienna | Server/infrastructure action and judicial coordination |
| [[netherlands]] | Public Prosecutor's Office Limburg, Police Limburg / Dutch National Police | Server/infrastructure action and judicial coordination |
| [[bulgaria]] | District Public Prosecution Office Plovdiv, GDBOP Cybercrime Directorate | Action-day support through Eurojust-transmitted judicial request |
| [[germany]] | Dusseldorf Police Headquarters, ZAC

title: QakBot / Gallyamov Indictment
text:
## Summary

The US Department of Justice unsealed an indictment on 22 May 2025 charging **Rustam Rafailevich Gallyamov**, 48, of Moscow, Russia, with leading the Qakbot malware conspiracy since 2008. Gallyamov allegedly used Qakbot to build a botnet providing access to co-conspirators who deployed ransomware variants including **ProLock, DopplePaymer, Egregor, REvil, Conti, Name Locker, Black Basta, and Cactus**.

This action was a multinational effort involving the US, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Denmark, the United Kingdom, and Canada, with [[europol-ec3|Europol]] providing support.

## Audit Note

This page intentionally fuses the 2025 indictment with the 2023 Dutch disruption layer. The multinational substance is real, but the two phases should not be read as a single procedural event.

## Background

Qakbot, also known as QBot or Pinkslipbot, is one of the longest-running malware families, active since 2008. Originally a banking trojan, it evolved into a major initial-access-as-a-service platform for ransomware operators. In August 2023, a multinational action disrupted the botnet and seized infrastructure across several countries.

The Dutch prosecution-side record is particularly useful for this operation family. In August 2023, the [[netherlands-om|Openbaar Ministerie]] and Dutch police jointly announced the Qakbot botnet disruption and said that **22 servers** tied to the malware had been seized in Dutch datacenters. That gives this repo a direct way to connect Dutch prosecutorial involvement to the wider Qakbot enforcement chain, rather than leaving the Netherlands as a nameless supporting state.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead:** US Department of Justice / [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]]
- **Coordination support:** [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]
- **Dutch prosecutor-side partner for the earlier disruption layer:** [[netherlands-om|Openbaar Ministerie - Landelijk Parket]]
- **Dutch police-side partner for the earlier disruption layer:** [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National Police]]
- **International partners:** France, Germany, Netherlands, Denmark, United Kingdom, Canada

## Results and Impact

- **Indictment** of Gallyamov for conspiracy, wire fraud, and related charges
- **USD 24 million+** in cryptocurrency targeted through civil forfeiture

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Will Gallyamov ever be apprehended given his location in Russia?
- How effective was the August 2023 botnet takedown if criminal activity continued?
- What is the total financial impact of Qakbot-facilitated ransomware attacks?

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Openbaar Ministerie, 2023-08-29: Grootste wereldwijde botnet Qakbot onschadelijk gemaakt.
- US DOJ (OPA), 2025-05-22: Leader of Qakbot Malware Conspiracy Indicted for Involvement in Global Ransomware Scheme.
- Help Net Security, 2025-05-23: DanaBot botnet disrupted, QakBot leader indicted.
- Eurojust, 2023-08-30: Malware network that infected more than 700,000 victims and caused hundreds of millions of dollars in damage worldwide dismantled in multinational..

## Operational Timeline

- 2008: activity or investigation start.
- 2025-05-22: public announcement.
- 2023-08-29: public source coverage from Openbaar Ministerie.
- 2023-08-30: public source coverage from Eurojust.
- 2025-05-22: public source coverage from US DOJ (OPA).
- 2025-05-23: public source coverage from Help Net Security.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: ransomware and malware.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status ongoing.
- Related legal or operational records: Us V Gallyamov Qakbot.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- The OM and Dutch police jointly announced the international Qakbot disruption.
- The Dutch side reported seizure of 22 servers in different datacenters in the Netherlands.
- The source shows formal prosecution involvement in a major multinational botnet operation.
- This joint OM-police release docume

title: QQAAZZ Money Laundering Network Takedown
text:
## Summary

In October 2020, the FBI and Europol coordinated the takedown of **QQAAZZ**, a multi-national money laundering network that provided laundering services to cybercriminals worldwide. The QQAAZZ network operated as a "money mule" organization, opening hundreds of bank accounts across multiple countries to launder proceeds from cybercrimes including banking trojan theft, ransomware, and BEC schemes.

The operation involved law enforcement from multiple countries across Europe and was notable for targeting the financial infrastructure that enabled cybercrime rather than the perpetrators of the underlying crimes themselves.

## Background

QQAAZZ operated as a professional money laundering service for cybercriminals, maintaining a network of bank accounts in countries including Portugal, Spain, the UK, Latvia, Georgia, and Bulgaria. The organization received stolen funds from cybercrime victims and laundered them through these accounts, taking a percentage as commission. The network served as the financial backbone for multiple cybercrime groups.

## Participating Parties

### Lead Agency
- [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]]

### Coordinating Body
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]

### Participating Countries (7+)
- [[united-states|United States]]
- [[portugal|Portugal]]
- [[spain|Spain]]
- [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom]]
- [[latvia|Latvia]]
- [[georgia|Georgia]]
- [[bulgaria|Bulgaria]]

## Legal Framework

Specific legal instruments have not been detailed in the public source.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Pre-2020 | Multi-year investigation into QQAAZZ network |
| 2020-10-15 | QQAAZZ network dismantled, charges announced |

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Network dismantled | QQAAZZ money laundering operation |
| Countries involved | 7+ |

> [!info] Legal-basis note
> Specific arrest and indictment counts need verification from official sources.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

Not detailed in public source. The multi-country scope suggests extensive use of MLATs and Europol coordination.

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

No Korean involvement identified.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- How many individuals were arrested and charged?
- What was the total amount of funds laundered through the network?
- Which specific cybercrime groups used QQAAZZ services?
- How many bank accounts were seized?

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ (Western District of Pennsylvania), 2020-09-29: OFFICIALS ANNOUNCE INTERNATIONAL OPERATION TARGETING TRANSNATIONAL CRIMINAL ORGANIZATION QQAAZZ THAT PROVIDED MONEY LAUNDERING SERVICES TO..
- Europol, 2020-10-15: Europol: 20 Arrests in QQAAZZ Multi-Million Money Laundering Case.
- CyberScoop, 2020-10-15: FBI accuses Russian money laundering QQAAZZ.
- OCCRP, 2020-10-15: Pan-Continental Operation Busts Money Launderers for Europe's Cybercriminals.
- AML Intelligence, 2020-10-16: Notorious Central Europe crime gang that laundered tens of millions of euro for hackers targeted in raids.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- On 15 October 2020, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Western District of Pennsylvania announced the unsealing of an indictment charging 14 members of the transnational QQAAZZ organization.
- DOJ said a related 2019 indictment had charged five additional QQAAZZ members and that one more conspirator had previously been arrested by criminal complaint in the United States, bringing the total number of charged defendants to 20.
- DOJ described QQAAZZ as a professional money-laundering network that provided services to high-level cybercriminal groups, including actors connecte

title: Ramnit Botnet Takedown
text:
> [!info] Page corrected (2026-04-10)
> This page was originally filed as "Europol Botnet Takedown 2023" (`botnet-takedown-europol-2023.md`). Content verification of the Europol source URL confirmed this is actually the **February 2015 Ramnit botnet takedown**. Page renamed and all data corrected.

## Summary

On 24 February 2015, the **UK National Crime Agency (NCA)** led a multinational operation to take down the **Ramnit botnet**, which had infected approximately **3.2 million computers worldwide**. The operation was coordinated through [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] and the [[j-cat|Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT)]], with law enforcement participation from [[germany]], [[italy]], and the [[netherlands]].

The Ramnit malware was a banking trojan that enabled criminals to steal personal and banking information, including passwords, and disable antivirus protection on infected Windows systems.

## Background

Ramnit was first identified around 2010 and evolved from a simple file infector into a full-featured banking trojan. By 2015 it had become one of the most prevalent botnets globally, with an estimated 3.2 million infections. The malware spread through social engineering attacks, typically via links in spam emails or redirects to exploit kits.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agency**: [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency]]

**Law Enforcement (4 countries)**:
- [[united-kingdom]] — NCA (lead)
- [[germany]] — BKA
- [[italy]] — Italian law enforcement
- [[netherlands]] — Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU)

**Coordination**: [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]], [[eurojust]], [[j-cat|J-CAT]], CERT-EU

**Private Sector Partners**:
- Microsoft — technical analysis
- Symantec — malware research and sinkholing
- AnubisNetworks — domain intelligence

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| ~2010 | Ramnit first observed as file infector |
| 2012-2014 | Evolution into banking trojan; infections reach millions |
| 2015-02-24 | Coordinated takedown: C&C servers shut down, ~300 domains sinkholed |
| 2015-02-24 | Europol public announcement |

## Results and Impact

- **~300 internet domain addresses** seized/sinkholed — redirected from criminal C&C to law enforcement sinkholes
- **Command-and-control servers** shut down across participating countries
- **3.2 million infected computers** disrupted from receiving criminal instructions
- **No arrests** were publicly announced as part of this operation

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

- [[j-cat|Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT)]] — operational coordination hub at Europol
- [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]] — analytical and coordination support
- **Public-private partnership**: Microsoft, Symantec, and AnubisNetworks provided technical intelligence and sinkhole infrastructure

## Lessons Learned

1. **Sinkholing as primary disruption**: Without arrests, the operation relied on infrastructure seizure — effective for disruption but leaving operators free to rebuild
2. **J-CAT coordination model**: Ramnit was an early showcase of J-CAT's ability to coordinate multi-country botnet takedowns from The Hague
3. **PPP (public-private partnership)**: Microsoft and Symantec's intelligence was essential — law enforcement alone lacked the technical visibility

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

No known Korean involvement in this operation. However, Ramnit infections were reported in South Korea, and [[south-korea]] was among the 190+ countries with affected systems.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- **No arrests**: Despite the scale (3.2M infections), no operators were publicly charged. Whether subsequent investigations led to arrests is unclear.
- **Ramnit resurgence**: Security researchers reported Ramnit activity resuming within months of the takedown, raising questions about the long-term effectiveness of infrastructure-only disruption.

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publ

title: Ontario RCMP iSpoof.cc Top-50 Subscriber Arrest — Toronto Couple Charged for 570+ Canadian Victim Spoofing-as-a-Service Fraud (2025)
text:
## Summary

This operation is the Canadian-side downstream prosecution of two Toronto residents alleged to have been among the top 50 worldwide subscribers of iSpoof.cc, a caller-ID spoofing-as-a-service platform that the UK Metropolitan Police took down in November 2022 under Operation Elaborate with Europol and Eurojust coordination. On 2025-02-19 the Ontario RCMP Cybercrime Investigative Team (CIT) Toronto, part of [[canada-rcmp|Royal Canadian Mounted Police]] Central Region (Ontario), executed a search warrant on the suspects' residence and arrested **Chakib Mansouri (29)** and **Majdouline Alouah (31)**. The release was issued the next day, 2025-02-20, by RCMP – Central Region.

The investigation identified at least 570 Canadian victims of spoofing, phishing and smishing schemes in which the suspects impersonated banks, government employees and police using the iSpoof.cc service to display falsified caller IDs. The release states "millions of dollars" in Canadian losses and explicitly anticipates that "the number of identified victims will increase as a result of the execution of search warrants."

## Background

iSpoof.cc was a subscription-based caller-ID spoofing platform that, at peak, had as many as 38,000 worldwide subscribers. Subscribers paid for the ability to display a falsified caller ID — typically the genuine number of a bank, government department, or police force — to deceive victims into surrendering account credentials, one-time passcodes, or to induce wire transfers. The platform was taken offline in November 2022 in a UK Metropolitan Police-led operation conducted with Europol and Eurojust support; that takedown produced an intelligence package on the platform's most active subscribers worldwide, with prosecutions of high-volume subscribers expected to follow in their respective home jurisdictions.

The Ontario RCMP arrest of Mansouri and Alouah is one such follow-on prosecution: the Canadian release explicitly identifies them as "among the top 50 most active subscribers in the world." The intelligence chain implied by the partner list — UK Metropolitan Police → Dutch National Police → EUROPOL → EUROJUST → RCMP — is consistent with a Europol-mediated package being operationalised by a non-EU partner agency to support domestic Canadian charges.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead agency (prosecuting jurisdiction):** [[canada-rcmp|Royal Canadian Mounted Police]] – Central Region (Ontario), Cybercrime Investigative Team (CIT) Toronto. Press release statement attributed to Inspector **Lina Dabit**, Officer in Charge of CIT Toronto.
- **International partners (named in release):**
  - [[uk-metropolitan-police|London Metropolitan Police]] (United Kingdom) — original iSpoof.cc takedown agency.
  - [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National Police]] (Netherlands).
  - [[europol-ec3|Europol European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]].
  - [[eurojust|Eurojust]] (European Union Agency for Criminal Justice Cooperation).
- **Domestic Canadian partners (named in release, no current wiki page):** Toronto Police Service; Peel Regional Police; FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada); the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre (CAFC); the National Cybercrime Coordination Centre (NC3).

## Legal Framework

Charges laid against both Mansouri and Alouah under the Canadian Criminal Code:

- s.380(1) — Fraud
- s.342.1 — Unauthorized use of Computer
- s.462.31 — Laundering Proceeds of Crime
- s.430(1.1) — Unauthorized Possession of Credit Card Data
- s.354 — Possessing the Proceeds of Crime

The release does not specify whether a formal Mutual Legal Assistance request, [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] Article 26 spontaneous information channel, or only informal Europol/Eurojust-mediated intelligence sharing was used to obtain the underlying iSpoof.cc subscriber data from European partners. As of the publication date (2025-02-20), Canada is a party to the Budapest Convention (ratified 2015-07-08) but had not sign

title: Rex Mundi Hacking Group Takedown
title_ko: Rex Mundi Hacking Group Takedown (공식 작전명 미확인; alias: Rex Mundi Arrest)
text:
## Summary

UK, French, and Thai law enforcement, coordinated by Europol and the Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT), dismantled the Rex Mundi hacking group that had engaged in large-scale customer data breaches and cyber extortion targeting British companies. The operation resulted in the arrest of 8 suspects, including a key member arrested in Thailand. The investigation ran from May 2017 to May 2018.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agencies:**
- London Metropolitan Police
- French National Police High Tech Crime Unit
- Thai Royal Police

**Coordinating Body:**
- Europol / J-CAT (Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce)

**Participating Countries (3):**
United Kingdom, France, Thailand

## Results

- **8 suspects arrested**
- Rex Mundi hacking group dismantled
- Data breach and cyber extortion operations neutralized

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Europol, 2018-06-14: Europol: French coder who helped extort British company arrested in Thailand.
- WeLiveSecurity / ESET, 2018-06-19: Europol and partners dismantle prolific cyber-extortion gang.
- BleepingComputer, 2018-06-15: Europol Dismantles One of the Internet's Oldest Hacker Groups.
- SecurityWeek, 2018-06-15: French Nationals Arrested for Rex Mundi Hacks.
- Infosecurity Magazine, 2018-06-18: Europol Disrupts Rex Mundi Cybercrime Group.

## Operational Timeline

- 2017-05: activity or investigation start.
- 2018-05-18: public announcement.
- 2018-05-18: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2018-06-14: public source coverage from Europol.
- 2018-06-15: public source coverage from BleepingComputer, SecurityWeek.
- 2018-06-18: public source coverage from Infosecurity Magazine.
- 2018-06-19: public source coverage from WeLiveSecurity / ESET.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: hacking.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- On 18 May 2018, Royal Thai Police arrested a 25-year-old French national in Thailand on a French international arrest warrant — the eighth and final arrest in a year-long investigation into the Rex Mundi hacking/extortion group, coordinated by Europol's Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT)
- Rex Mundi's modus operandi: High-volume data breaches of customer databases followed by extortion demands.
- Ransoms typically demanded in bitcoin and escalating with daily penalties for non-payment
- Specific victim case: In May 2017 the group compromised an unnamed UK-based company and demanded €580,000 to prevent public disclosure OR €825,000 for breach details, with a €210,000/day penalty for non-payment
- Investigation timeline: May 2017 initial attack → June 2017 five arrests in France (including a main suspect who confessed to hiring dark-web hackers) → October 2017 two additional hackers arrested in France → May 2018 final accomplice apprehended in Thailand
- International cooperation: London Metropolitan Police (UK), French National Police High Tech Crime Unit (OCLCTIC-DCPJ), Royal Thai Police, Europol's 24/7 Operational Centre and J-CAT — demonstrating both EU internal cooperation and EU-ASEAN reach
- Historical context: Rex Mundi previously stole ~600,000 records from Domino's Pizza France and Belgium in June 2014 — one of several high-profile European breaches before the takedown
- WeLiveSecurity reported that the final Thailand arrest effectively closed the Rex Mundi investigation.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_END -->

<!-- CANONICAL_ASSESSMENT_START -->

## Canonical Operation Assessment

This page is retained as a canonical operation because it describes a joint-investigation against Re

title: Romania-Moldova Phishing-Proceeds Laundering JIT Action Day (Eurojust, 2025-06-12)
title_ko: 루마니아-몰도바 피싱 수익 세탁 JIT 합동 단속 (Eurojust, 2025-06-12)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-06-12**, the **Romanian Directorate for Investigating Organised Crime and Terrorism (DIICOT, Galati Regional Structure)** and the **Moldovan Prosecutor's Office for Combatting Organised Crime and Special Cases (PCCOCS)** executed a coordinated joint action day across **44 locations** in Romania and Moldova, dismantling an organised crime group laundering the proceeds of phishing fraud. The action was carried out within a **Joint Investigation Team** that Eurojust had assisted in setting up in March 2025 and continued to finance, with bilateral evidence transfer structured by **European Investigation Orders (EIOs)**.

## Background

The dismantled OCG had operated **since at least 2013** from professionally organised call centres in the Republic of Moldova. The network's suspects were Romanian, Moldovan, Ukrainian, and Italian nationals. Through phishing, the group harvested EU-wide victims' personal data, extracted their banking details, and redirected victim payments into bank accounts under network control. Detailed investigations into the network were initiated in **November 2022** by Moldova's PCCOCS.

The action day specifically focused on a sub-group within the OCG responsible for the laundering layer: opening bank accounts (often with **false Romanian identity documents**) to receive criminal proceeds, then moving funds through **a myriad of accounts and various financial platforms**, converting them into **cryptocurrency**, and trading between **electronic wallets** to render proceeds-tracing more difficult.

In total, **30 victims** have been identified, with aggregate financial losses of **EUR 20 million**.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| EU Member State authority | [[romania-diicot|Romanian Directorate for Investigating Organised Crime and Terrorism (DIICOT) — Galati Regional Structure]] |
| Non-EU Liaison Prosecutor country authority | Moldovan Prosecutor's Office for Combatting Organised Crime and Special Cases (PCCOCS) |
| Coordinating EU body | [[eurojust|European Union Agency for Criminal Justice Cooperation (Eurojust)]] |
| OCG suspect nationalities | Romanian, Moldovan, Ukrainian, Italian |
| Victim distribution | 30 identified victims across the European Union |

## Legal Framework

The joint action day rests on three discrete legal instruments:

1. **Joint Investigation Team (JIT)** — Set up with Eurojust assistance in **March 2025**; Eurojust financed JIT operations. Moldova's participation as a non-EU JIT party rests on its status as a **Eurojust Liaison Prosecutor country** (one of twelve, alongside Albania, Georgia, Iceland, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Norway, Serbia, Switzerland, Ukraine, the United Kingdom, and the United States).
2. **European Investigation Orders (EIOs)** — Bilateral evidence-transfer instrument used by Romanian and Moldovan authorities to execute formal evidence requests within the JIT framework. Eurojust supported EIO execution.
3. **Domestic search and seizure authority** — Romanian DIICOT and Moldovan PCCOCS each acted under their respective domestic warrant authority on the action day.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ 2013 | OCG begins operating professionally organised phishing call centres in Moldova | Eurojust 2025-06-12 |
| 2022-11 | Moldovan PCCOCS initiates detailed investigation into the network | Eurojust 2025-06-12 |
| 2025-03 | JIT set up with Eurojust assistance; Eurojust financing begins | Eurojust 2025-06-12 |
| 2025-06-12 | Joint action day: 44 search locations executed across Romania and Moldova; group of suspects identified | Eurojust 2025-06-12 |
| Days following 2025-06-12 | Identified suspects scheduled to be heard by the prosecution services | Eurojust 2025-06-12 |

## Results and Impact

- **44 search locations** executed across Romania and Moldova on the action day.
- **Group of suspects identified** and scheduled for prosecutorial hearings in the days following 2025-06-1

title: Hook, Line and Sinker Phishing Takedown
title_ko: Hook, Line and Sinker phishing takedown
text:
## Summary

**Hook, Line and Sinker** was a 2020 Eurojust-supported joint action day against a Romania-based organized crime group carrying out phishing, identity theft, and bank fraud against victims in Estonia and Lithuania. Romanian authorities executed the arrests and searches, while Eurojust coordinated the judicial cooperation and Europol provided operational and analytical support.

The operation qualifies for the public IC corpus because the official Eurojust and Europol records identify Romania, Estonia, and Lithuania as participating countries, describe a Joint Investigation Team, and document cross-border victimization and evidence coordination.

## Background

The group used SMS messages and emails that linked victims to cloned bank sites. Victims entered banking credentials or created new Smart-ID accounts, after which the suspects moved funds into accounts controlled by the OCG. The public sources report almost 600 victims, with more than 100 in Lithuania and nearly 500 in Estonia.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Judicial coordination | [[eurojust|Eurojust]] |
| Operational/analytical support | [[europol-ec3|Europol]] |
| Arrest/search execution | [[romanian-police|Romanian Police]], [[romania-diicot|DIICOT]], Bucharest Brigade for Countering Organized Crime |
| JIT / victim-state authorities | Estonian prosecutors and police; Lithuanian Prosecutor General's Office and [[lithuania-police|Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau]] |

## Participating Countries

- [[romania|Romania]]
- [[estonia|Estonia]]
- [[lithuania|Lithuania]]

## Legal Framework

Eurojust set up and financed a Joint Investigation Team among Estonia, Lithuania, and Romania. It also organized four coordination meetings and provided funding for Estonian police officers to participate in the operation in Romania. Europol supported information exchange, analytical work, and real-time cross-checking on the action day.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 2019-02 | Fraud activity began, according to Eurojust's summary of the case period. |
| 2019 | Eurojust began facilitating judicial cooperation. |
| 2020-03 | Eurojust supported creation of the JIT. |
| 2020-09-29 | Joint action day announced; Romanian officers searched four houses and arrested three suspects. |
| 2020-10-13 | INCIBE published Spanish and English summaries pointing to the Europol and Eurojust records. |

## Results and Impact

- 3 suspects arrested in Romania.
- 4 house searches conducted.
- Almost 600 victims identified in Estonia and Lithuania.
- Damages exceeded EUR 200,000.
- Estonian and Lithuanian banks stopped approximately EUR 450,000 in suspicious transfers.
- Eurojust funded the JIT with more than EUR 38,000.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

1. [[joint-investigation-team|Joint Investigation Team]] among Estonia, Lithuania, and Romania.
2. [[eurojust-coordination-meeting|Eurojust coordination meetings]] for action-day preparation.
3. Europol information exchange, analytical support, and on-site cross-checking.
4. Bank-side suspicious-transfer detection in the victim jurisdictions.

## Korean Involvement

No Korean involvement identified.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- The current corpus does not contain Romanian, Estonian, or Lithuanian court outcomes after the arrests.
- The Europol source page in this repository originally carried an incorrect 2024-derived slug, but the source text itself states a 29 September 2020 publication date and matches the 2020 Eurojust record.
- Public sources describe possible links to additional countries but do not identify them with enough specificity for structured country metadata.

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.
