title: Rydox Marketplace Takedown
text:
## Summary

The Rydox takedown was a coordinated cybercrime marketplace disruption announced by the [[us-doj|U.S. Department of Justice]] on 2024-12-12. According to the official DOJ release, the action combined a U.S. indictment, a domain seizure, cryptocurrency restraint, arrests in [[kosovo|Kosovo]] and [[albania|Albania]], and server seizure activity in [[malaysia|Malaysia]].

This page is important because it captures a full-stack marketplace disruption model: prosecution, infrastructure seizure, arrests, and cross-border operational support all occurred in the same action.

## Background

DOJ described Rydox as a cybercrime marketplace that sold stolen personal information, access devices, credentials, scam pages, and spamming tools. The announcement states that the marketplace had operated since 2016, completed at least 7,600 sales, offered more than 321,000 products, and served more than 18,000 users.

That combination made Rydox more than a single fraud storefront. It functioned as reusable criminal infrastructure for identity theft, phishing, spam enablement, and account abuse.

## Participating Parties

### Lead Agencies
- [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]]
- [[us-doj|U.S. Department of Justice]]

### Participating Jurisdictions
- [[united-states|United States]]
- [[kosovo|Kosovo]]
- [[albania|Albania]]
- [[malaysia|Malaysia]]

## Legal Framework

The public DOJ record clearly supports three mechanisms:

- [[mutual-legal-assistance|cross-border cooperation]] to synchronize arrests and infrastructure action

The announcement also states that one defendant was expected to face domestic prosecution in Albania, illustrating that not every participant in the same coordinated action is necessarily routed into the same U.S. case.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2016 | DOJ says Rydox began operating |
| 2024-12-12 | U.S. indictment unsealed, domain seized, arrests announced, Malaysian server action disclosed |

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Reported result |
|--------|-----------------|
| Arrests | 3 |
| Indictments | 1 U.S. case publicly referenced |
| Domain seizures | 1 |
| Server seizures | 1 server environment in Malaysia reported |
| Cryptocurrency restrained | About $225,000 |
| Marketplace revenue | At least $230,000 |
| Sales | 7,600+ |
| Marketplace products | 321,000+ |
| Users | 18,000+ |

The reported product volume and user count indicate that Rydox was not a marginal forum. It was a mature criminal marketplace whose disruption potentially removed a large amount of stolen-data inventory and cybercrime tooling from circulation.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

- Domain seizure
- Server seizure
- [[mutual-legal-assistance|Cross-border operational assistance]]
- Extradition request support

## Challenges and Friction Points

- The action required coordination across jurisdictions with different prosecutorial pathways.
- Some administrators were routed toward U.S. extradition exposure, while others were expected to face local proceedings.
- Marketplace takedowns require technical continuity so that seizure banners, domain control, and evidentiary preservation are all maintained.

## Follow-Up Actions

The defendant-specific case record is [[us-v-kutleshi-rydox]], with a linked follow-on enforcement page at [[operation-us-v-kutleshi-rydox]]. Additional public proceedings may emerge if extradition or local prosecutions become more visible.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- US DOJ (Western District of Pennsylvania), 2024-12-12: Rydox Cybercrime Marketplace Shut Down and Three Administrators Arrested.
- CyberScoop, 2024-12-12: Cybercriminal marketplace Rydox seized in international law enforcement operation.
- TechNadu, 2024-12-13: Kosovo Police Shuts Down Rydox Cybercrime Marketplace and Arrests Admins.
- Security Affairs, 2024-12-13: U.S. authorities seized cybercrime marketplace Rydox.
- Bitdefender, 2024-12-16: Rydox cybercrime marketplace seized by l

title: Seoul Eastern District Prosecutors' Office — Clark (Philippines) Voice-Phishing Call-Centre On-Site Arrest, Extradition, and Indictment (2025-2026)
title_ko: 서울동부지검 합수부 — 필리핀 클락 보이스피싱 콜센터 4명 현장 검거·강제송환·구속기소 (2025–2026)
text:
## Summary

On 2026-04-30 the **Seoul Eastern District Prosecutors' Office** ([[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청]] subordinate office) Voice-Phishing Joint Investigation Unit (보이스피싱범죄 합동수사부, 부장검사 이태순; "**합수부**") announced the on-site arrest in **Clark, Philippines** of 4 Korean-national voice-phishing call-centre operators, their forced repatriation to Korea, and indictment in custody on 2026-04-28. The lead defendant is named only as A (male, 37, 콜센터 관리책 / call-centre manager); 3 co-conspirators are unnamed in the public release. Charges: violation of the **통신사기피해환급에관한특별법** (Special Act on Refund of Damages from Telecommunications-Based Financial Fraud) and 사기 (fraud, KCC §347). Indicted offence period: 2025-06 to 2025-07. Indicted-offence fraud volume: approximately **₩130 million**.

The case is structurally distinct from [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|the contemporaneous KNPA-led 73-person extradition]] in that it is **prosecution-led** rather than police-led: the lead Korean actor is the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|SPO]] (via the Seoul Eastern District 합수부 and the SPO Organised Crime Division-deployed prosecutorial investigator (파견 검찰수사관) physically stationed in the Philippines), and the named foreign operational counterpart is the **Philippine Bureau of Immigration Fugitive Search Unit (BI FSU / 수배자 추적대)** rather than the [[philippine-national-police|Philippine National Police]].

## Background

The 합수부 (Voice-Phishing Joint Investigation Unit) was originally launched on 2022-07-29 as a 합동수사단 (Joint Investigation Team) and was formally embedded into the Seoul Eastern District Prosecutors' Office as a permanent 부 (department) on 2026-01-06. The press-release header lists the four Korean cooperating agencies fusioned into 합수부: 검찰 (Prosecution) / 경찰 (Police) / 관세청 (Korea Customs Service) / 국세청 (National Tax Service). Cumulative outputs of 합수부 from 2022-07-29 to 2026-03-31 are stated as **1,206 persons booked and 481 in custody** (organisational context, covering all 합수부 cases — not this Clark case alone).

Korean prosecutorial investigators are deployed overseas under the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|SPO]] Organised Crime Division (대검찰청 조직범죄과) with three named functions in the source text:
1. International cooperation on voice-phishing and narcotics — collecting and sharing crime-organisation and member intelligence;
2. Liaison work on overseas-fugitive cases — extradition/repatriation requests and execution;
3. Building working relationships with overseas counterpart agencies and Korean expatriate communities.

This case is the first publicly-described 2026 instance in which all three functions converge on a single voice-phishing cell.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Party |
|---|---|
| Lead Korean authority | [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea\|Supreme Prosecutors' Office]] — 서울동부지방검찰청 보이스피싱범죄 합동수사부 |
| Korean-side international-cooperation function | [[spo-international-cooperation\|SPO International Cooperation function]] (대검찰청 조직범죄과 파견 검찰수사관) |
| Korean-side repatriation channel | [[ministry-of-justice-korea\|MOJ]] 국제형사과 (International Criminal Affairs Division) + 필리핀 대사관 파견 영사 (Manila Embassy police attaché-Consul, ranked 총경 / Senior Superintendent) |
| Foreign operational counterpart | Philippine Bureau of Immigration — Fugitive Search Unit (BI FSU / 수배자 추적대) |
| Custody pathway | Bicutan immigration detention centre (PH) → INTERPOL Red Notice + Korean passport invalidation → forced repatriation |
| International coordination body | [[interpol\|INTERPOL]] (Red Notice issuance) |
| Source-backed participating countries | [[south-korea\|South Korea]], [[philippines\|Philippines]] |
| Korean four-agency 합수부 composition | 검찰 / 경찰 / 관세청 / 국세청 |

## Legal Framework

| Instrument / authority | Function in this operation |
|---|---|
| 통신사기피해환급에관한특별법 (Special Act on Refund of Damages from Telecommunications-Based Financial Fraud) | Korean substantive charge |
| 형법 §347 사기 (KCC Article 3

title: Singapore–Hong Kong–Malaysia Tri-lateral GSM Gateway Scam Syndicate Takedown (April–June 2025)
text:
# Singapore–Hong Kong–Malaysia Tri-lateral GSM Gateway Scam Syndicate Takedown (April–June 2025)

## Summary

A two-month joint enforcement operation by the Singapore Police Force (SPF), the Hong Kong Police Force (HKPF) and the Royal Malaysia Police (RMP) — running from April 2025 through June 2025 — dismantled a transnational criminal syndicate operating Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) GSM gateway devices used to route fraudulent scam calls across Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia. The operation produced 11 arrests of suspects aged 24–48 and seized 226 GSM gateway devices distributed across the three jurisdictions (75 SG, 141 HK, 10 MY). A 29-year-old suspect arrested in Malaysia on 3 June 2025 was handed over to Singapore and charged in a Singapore court on 5 June 2025 with criminal conspiracy to cheat under Penal Code s.420 read with s.120B. The case was coordinated through FRONTIER+, the Hong Kong–led 10-jurisdiction Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform, but is operationally distinct from the FRONTIER+ first joint cross-border anti-scam operation (28 April – 28 May 2025) which targeted a different set of multi-fraud typologies across seven Asia-Pacific jurisdictions.

## Background

Scam syndicates targeting Singapore have for several years deployed VoIP GSM gateways — telecom devices that take voice traffic from internet-borne sessions and re-emit it as a call from a local SIM card — to disguise the origin of fraudulent calls and defeat carrier-side overseas-call alerts. Investigators have *highly likely* (>80%) treated infrastructure dismantlement, rather than caller-side arrests alone, as a more durable disruption strategy because the gateway devices are physically located in jurisdictions that may be different from the scam operators themselves.

The collaborative intelligence-sharing initiative between SPF, HKPF, and RMP revealed a sophisticated criminal network with a cloud-based remote operating system, enabling the syndicate to link to GSM mobile phone networks across the three jurisdictions and operate the gateways remotely. This arrangement allowed fraudulent calls to be routed through local mobile phone networks, leading victims in Singapore to believe that they were receiving calls from within Singapore — a defining feature of modern impersonation, investment, and romance scam typologies in the region.

## Participating Parties

- **Singapore**: Singapore Police Force ([[singapore-police|SPF]]), with operational roles taken by the Commercial Affairs Department (CAD), Criminal Investigation Department (CID), and Police Intelligence Department (PID). Director CAD David Chew issued the public statement.
- **Hong Kong SAR**: Hong Kong Police Force ([[hong-kong-police|HKPF]]). Photographic evidence of the Hong Kong-side raids was supplied by HKPF.
- **Malaysia**: Royal Malaysia Police ([[royal-malaysia-police|RMP]] / Polis Diraja Malaysia). RMP arrested the 29-year-old suspect handed over to Singapore on 3 June 2025.

## Legal Framework

- **Singapore charging instrument**: Penal Code 1871, s. 420 (cheating) read with s. 120B (criminal conspiracy). Maximum penalty under s. 420 is imprisonment up to 10 years and a fine.
- **Cross-border coordination**: The case was operationally coordinated through [[frontier-plus-cross-border-anti-scam-2025|FRONTIER+]], the Hong Kong-led informal Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform comprising ten Asia-Pacific jurisdictions.
- **Suspect transfer**: The hand-over of the 29-year-old from Malaysia to Singapore on or around 3–5 June 2025 was effected as a police-to-police transfer rather than a formal extradition, *highly likely* (>80%) under existing bilateral arrangements between Singapore and Malaysia.
- **Domestic powers**: Each participating force exercised search-and-seizure and arrest powers under its national legislation; no MLAT or treaty-based formal request was cited in the SPF release.

## Operational Timeline

- **April 2025**: Joint intelligence-sha

title: Singapore-Thailand Joint Arrest of ALTDOS / DESORDEN / GHOSTR / 0mid16B Data-Breach-Extortion Threat Actor (SPF + Royal Thai Police, 26 February 2025)
title_ko: 싱가포르·태국 합동 데이터 침해·갈취 위협 행위자 검거 — ALTDOS/DESORDEN/GHOSTR/0mid16B (싱가포르 경찰청 + 태국 왕립경찰, 2025-02-26)
text:
## Summary

On **26 February 2025**, the **Royal Thai Police (RTP) Central Investigation Bureau (CIB)** arrested a **39-year-old male threat actor** in Thailand, suspected of operating under sequential aliases **ALTDOS, DESORDEN, GHOSTR, and 0mid16B** since 2020 and responsible for at least **75 international data-breach-extortion cases** (per SPF; the corresponding Group-IB release reports 90+ victims and 13TB of stolen personal data). The arrest concluded a **~5-year cross-border investigation** led by the **Singapore Police Force Cybercrime Command (SPF CCC)** in collaboration with RTP CIB, supported by the **Cyber Security Agency (CSA) of Singapore** and (per Group-IB) **Group-IB's Digital Crime Resistance Centers in Singapore and Thailand**.

Charges (Thailand): Thai Criminal Code + Computer-Related Crime Act. Assets seized: **> 10 million THB** including laptops, mobile phones, luxury vehicles, and branded bags.

> [!note] 4-alias 5-year correlation
> ALTDOS → DESORDEN → GHOSTR → 0mid16B over 5 years (2020–2025) required sustained writing-style + format-of-posts + posting-website + target-region pattern analysis to attribute to one actor. The arrest depended on this analytical tradecraft layer.

## Background

The threat actor's tradecraft (per Group-IB and corroborated by the SPF release):

- **Initial access**: SQL injection (sqlmap) + RDP exploitation against vulnerable internet-facing servers
- **Persistence/control**: Cracked CobaltStrike beacon
- **Exfiltration**: Database export to rented cloud servers (no significant lateral movement)
- **Monetisation primary**: Ransom demand to victim for non-disclosure
- **Monetisation secondary** (when victims refused): Reputational-pressure escalation — direct customer notifications via email or instant messengers; tip-offs to media; notifications to data-protection regulators
- **Monetisation tertiary**: Sale of unique data leaks on dark web forums

Victim industries: healthcare, retail, property investment, finance, e-commerce, logistics, technology, hospitality, insurance, recruitment.

Victim geography (per Group-IB release): primary Asia-Pacific (Thailand, Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, India — 65 cases) + UK, US, Canada, Middle East (later phases under GHOSTR / 0mid16B aliases).

The 4-alias trajectory: ALTDOS (Thailand-focus, 2020+) → DESORDEN (Asia-Pacific industry breadth) → GHOSTR (international expansion) → 0mid16B (latest). Group-IB attributed the aliases to one actor based on writing-style correlation, format-of-posts, posting-website preferences, target-region overlap, and timeline correlations.

## Participating Parties

| Agency | Country | Role |
|---|---|---|
| [[singapore-police\|Singapore Police Force (SPF) Cybercrime Command]] | Singapore | **Lead investigative agency**; sustained 5-year tracking from initial 11 Singapore victim reports |
| [[thailand-royal-police\|Royal Thai Police (RTP) Central Investigation Bureau]] | Thailand | **Arresting authority** (26 February 2025); evidence seizure (> 10M THB) |
| Cyber Security Agency (CSA) of Singapore | Singapore | Singapore-side leads support |
| Group-IB Digital Crime Resistance Centers (DCRCs) | Singapore + Thailand | Private-sector long-run tracking + alias correlation (2020–2025) |

## Legal Framework

- **Bilateral SPF-RTP cybercrime cooperation** under standing police-to-police channels — the cited release does not specify a single MLAR or treaty instrument.
- **Thai Criminal Code + Computer-Related Crime Act** — basis for the 26 February 2025 arrest in Thailand.
- **Singapore criminal procedure** (Computer Misuse Act etc.) for potential downstream Singapore-side charges arising from the 11 original Singapore-victim reports.

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The cited SPF release does not enumerate specific Thai Criminal Code provisions or Computer-Related Crime Act articles. Subsequent Thai prosecutorial documents may clarify.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 2020 | 11 S

title: Silk Road Marketplace Takedown
text:
## Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

Ulbricht was convicted on all counts in February 2015 and sentenced to two concurrent life terms plus 40 years on 29 May 2015. He was pardoned by President Trump on 21 January 2025 after serving approximately 11 years.

From an international cooperation perspective, the case is significant as an early example of cross-border digital evidence collection (Iceland server), follow-on extraditions (Ireland, Thailand), and large-scale cryptocurrency seizure. It is almost certainly the most historically influential dark web enforcement action, establishing precedents and operational models that shaped all subsequent dark web marketplace investigations.

## Background

### Origins of Silk Road

Silk Road was launched in approximately February 2011 as a Tor-hidden service accessible only through the Tor anonymity network. It operated as a marketplace where vendors could sell illegal drugs, counterfeit documents, and other illicit goods using Bitcoin as the exclusive payment method. The platform's design deliberately combined Tor's IP-masking with Bitcoin's pseudonymity to create what Ulbricht believed would be an enforcement-proof marketplace.

### Scale of Operations

By the time of its shutdown, Silk Road had become the most sophisticated and extensive criminal marketplace on the internet:

- **Users**: Approximately 957,079 registered accounts
- **Vendors**: Thousands of active sellers
- **Transactions**: An estimated 1.2 million transactions
- **Revenue**: Approximately USD 1.2 billion in total sales
- **Commission**: Ulbricht collected commissions totaling approximately 614,305 BTC (~$80 million at contemporary rates)
- **Listings**: Included narcotics, counterfeit identity documents, computer hacking tools, and other illicit goods

### Investigation Origins

The investigation into Silk Road began after a Gawker article in June 2011 brought the marketplace to widespread public attention and prompted Senator Chuck Schumer to call on the DEA and DOJ to shut it down. Multiple US federal agencies opened parallel investigations:

- **FBI New York**: Led the primary investigation through its Cyber Division
- **IRS Criminal Investigation**: Special Agent Gary Alford identified Ulbricht through online postings linking his real identity to the "Dread Pirate Roberts" persona
- **DEA**: Conducted undercover operations on the marketplace
- **HSI (Homeland Security Investigations)**: Investigated supply chain and international aspects
- **USPIS**: Investigated packages shipped through the postal system

## Participating Parties

### Lead Agencies

| Agency | Role |
|---|---|
| [[fbi-cyber-division]] (New York Field Office) | Lead investigative agency; server identification; Ulbricht arrest |
| [[us-doj]] (SDNY) | Prosecution; USAO Southern District of New York |
| IRS Criminal Investigation | Key identification of Ulbricht through open-source intelligence |
| DEA New York OCDETF Strike Force | Undercover operations on Silk Road |
| ICE Homeland Security Investigations | International coordination; supply chain investigation |
| US Postal Inspection Service | Package interdiction |

### International Cooperation Partners

| Country | Role | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| [[iceland]] | Hosted Silk Road server in Reykjavik data center; Icelandic police cloned server contents and

title: Simda Botnet Takedown
text:
## Summary

On April 9, 2015, INTERPOL coordinated an international operation to take down the Simda botnet, which had infected over 770,000 computers across 190+ countries. Law enforcement agencies from the Netherlands, United States, Russia, Luxembourg, and Poland, along with private sector partners Microsoft, Kaspersky Lab, Trend Micro, and Japan Cyber Defense Institute, simultaneously seized command-and-control servers to neutralize the botnet used for personal and financial data theft.

## Participating Parties

**Lead Agency:**
- INTERPOL (headquartered operation from Singapore)

**Participating Agencies:**
- Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit (NHTCU)
- FBI (United States)
- Police Grand-Ducale Section Nouvelles Technologies (Luxembourg)
- Russian Ministry of Interior Cybercrime Department

**Private Sector Partners:**
- Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit
- Kaspersky Lab
- Trend Micro
- Japan Cyber Defense Institute

**Participating Countries (7):**
Netherlands, United States, Russia, Luxembourg, Poland, Japan, Singapore

## Results

- **14 command-and-control servers** simultaneously seized
- **770,000+** infected computers identified across 190+ countries
- Simda botnet infrastructure neutralized

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- INTERPOL, 2015-04-13: INTERPOL: INTERPOL Coordinates Global Operation to Take Down Simda Botnet.
- Kaspersky, 2015-04-13: Kaspersky Lab Joins Forces with INTERPOL, Industry and Law Enforcement Partners to Disrupt Simda Botnet.
- Kaspersky, 2015-04-13: Simda botnet: a stealthy malware waiter.
- Trend Micro, 2015-04-13: Trend Micro Joins INTERPOL Botnet Takedown, Presents at INTERPOL World 2015.
- TechSpot, 2015-04-15: Interpol-led operation takes down the botnet that infected over 770K PCs.

## Operational Timeline

- 2015-04-09: activity or investigation start.
- 2015-04-09: public announcement.
- 2015-04-09: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2015-04-13: public source coverage from INTERPOL, Kaspersky, Trend Micro.
- 2015-04-15: public source coverage from TechSpot.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: malware.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Seizure and Takedown.
- The record is categorized as takedown with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- Simda botnet takedown coordinated on 9 April 2015 by INTERPOL's Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI) in Singapore; public announcement on 13 April 2015
- Simda had infected 770,000+ computers worldwide across 190+ countries; most-affected regions: United States, United Kingdom, Turkey, Canada, and Russia
- 90,000+ new infections recorded in the United States alone during the first two months of 2015, highlighting the active-campaign status at time of takedown
- 10 command-and-control servers seized in the Netherlands; additional servers taken down in the United States, Russia, Luxembourg, and Poland
- Cooperation partners: INTERPOL IGCI (coordination), Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit (forensic intelligence), Kaspersky Lab, Trend Micro, Japan's Cyber Defense Institute, Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit, FBI, Luxembourg police, Russian Ministry of Interior cybercrime division (Department 'K')
- Notable aspect: this operation is a *rare* example of Russian law enforcement (Dept.
- K) participating in a multinational cybercrime takedown — one of the last such instances before 2022 geopolitical deterioration
- Business model: Simda operators monetized via pay-per-install — charging criminal 'users' per successful malware installation for distributing other payloads

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_END -->

<!-- RAW_TEXT_HIGHLIGHTS_START -->

- INTERPOL, 2015-04-09: INTER

title: Spanish Pay-TV Streaming Network Takedown
text:
## Summary

The Spanish pay-TV streaming network takedown was a June 2020 Eurojust-supported action against a criminal organisation selling illegal access to subscription television channels, streaming services, and films. The source reports that the group operated mainly from Spain and used websites and servers in the EU and third countries.

The action produced 11 arrests in Spain, Denmark, Sweden, and Germany, and took down 50 servers in France, Germany, the Netherlands, Belgium, Denmark, Sweden, the Czech Republic, Poland, and Spain. Europol provided analytical support and participated with Eurojust in operational preparation.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Lead investigative line | [[spain-national-police|Spanish National Police]] and Spanish judicial authorities |
| Judicial coordination | [[eurojust|Eurojust]] |
| Law-enforcement support | [[europol-ec3|Europol / EC3]] |
| Source-backed participating countries | [[spain|Spain]], [[denmark|Denmark]], [[sweden|Sweden]], [[germany|Germany]], [[france|France]], [[netherlands|Netherlands]], [[belgium|Belgium]], [[czechia|Czechia]], [[poland|Poland]] |

## Results and Impact

- 11 suspects were arrested.
- 50 servers were taken down.
- The network had an estimated 2 million subscribers worldwide.
- Spanish reporting described approximately 1,000 websites and more than 40,000 channels, series, and platform feeds.
- Authorities seized assets with a reported value of about EUR 4.8 million and froze about EUR 1.1 million.

## References

| # | Title | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Eurojust supports new major crackdown on fraud with pay TV | Eurojust | 2020-06-10 | https://www.eurojust.europa.eu/news/eurojust-supports-new-major-crackdown-fraud-pay-tv |
| [2] | La Policia Nacional desarticula una red de television pirata con dos millones de clientes | La Nueva Espana / EFE | 2020-06-10 | https://www.lne.es/sucesos/2020/06/10/policia-nacional-desarticula-red-television-14528628.html |
| [3] | A group that provided 2 million people with a cheap way to connect to multiple paid streaming services such as Netflix is arrested | GIGAZINE | 2020-06-11 | https://gigazine.net/gsc_news/en/20200611-illegal-streaming-ring |

title: Operation Cryptoscam (SPF-FPB-INTERPOL Singapore-Brazil cooperation, May 2025)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-06-17** the **Singapore Police Force (SPF)** announced its pivotal role in **Operation Cryptoscam** — one of **two simultaneous operations** launched by the **Federal Police of Brazil (FPB)** in **May 2025** against criminal organisations involved in financial crimes, cyber fraud, drug trafficking, and money laundering. Across the two simultaneous operations the FPB issued **26 search-and-seizure warrants** and **11 arrest warrants**, and seized assets and valuables linked to the criminal groups.

The Singapore-side track of the operation began with a **January 2024** police report to SPF stating that a victim had suffered losses of **approximately USD 1.4 million** due to his **cryptocurrency wallet being compromised**. The **SPF Cybercrime Command (CCC)** under the **Criminal Investigation Department (CID)** conducted extensive follow-up investigations, established that the stolen funds had been traced to **Brazil**, and alerted the **FPB through INTERPOL international cooperation channels**.

## Background

The cited SPF release describes the two simultaneous May 2025 FPB operations as targeting criminal organisations involved in financial crimes, cyber fraud, drug trafficking, and money laundering. **Operation Cryptoscam was the SPF-originated track**; the second simultaneous operation is referenced but not described.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Originating jurisdiction | Singapore |
| Originating authority | [[singapore-police\|Singapore Police Force]] — Cybercrime Command (CCC) under the Criminal Investigation Department (CID); Commander AC Paul Tay |
| Action-day jurisdiction | Brazil |
| Action-day authority | Federal Police of Brazil (FPB) — Repression Electronic Bank Fraud Coordination + FPB Regional Office in Itajaí, Santa Catarina |
| Coordinating body | [[interpol-igci\|INTERPOL]] (Cybercrime Directorate; Director Neal Jetton) |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2024-01 | SPF receives police report — Singapore victim suffered approximately USD 1.4 million in losses from compromised cryptocurrency wallet | SPF 2025-06-17 |
| (date not enumerated) | SPF Cybercrime Command (CCC/CID) investigation traces stolen funds to Brazil | SPF 2025-06-17 |
| (date not enumerated) | FPB alerted via INTERPOL international cooperation channels; FPB commences investigation | SPF 2025-06-17 |
| 2025-05 | FPB executes Operation Cryptoscam alongside one other simultaneous operation: 26 search-and-seizure warrants + 11 arrest warrants total across the two operations | SPF 2025-06-17 |
| 2025-06-17 | SPF public announcement | SPF 2025-06-17 |

## Results and Impact

- **11 arrest warrants** issued by FPB across the two simultaneous May 2025 operations.
- **26 search-and-seizure warrants** issued by FPB across the two simultaneous May 2025 operations.
- **Assets and valuables seized** by FPB (specific totals not enumerated in the cited release).
- **Underlying victim loss**: approximately **USD 1.4 million** (Singapore victim, cryptocurrency-wallet compromise, January 2024).

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The cited release describes a single foundational IC mechanism class for this operation: **INTERPOL international cooperation channels** as the cross-jurisdictional alerting mechanism between SPF and FPB. The Singapore-side investigation was originated and conducted under the SPF Cybercrime Command (CCC) within the Criminal Investigation Department (CID), with the cross-border step occurring at the Singapore-to-Brazil-via-INTERPOL alerting handoff. Brazil-side action was executed under FPB authority, jointly handled by the Repression Electronic Bank Fraud Coordination unit and the FPB Regional Office in Itajaí, Santa Catarina.

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

South Korea is not named in the cited SPF release among the participating jurisdictions or the underlying victim cohort. The case is recorded in the wiki for the Singapore-Brazil-INTERPOL coopera

title: Sweden–US extradition in EUR 6 million bank-employee-impersonation fraud (Eurojust-coordinated, action day Nov 2024 → extradition 2026-04-10)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / sparse-source page
> This page is built from a **single tier-1 primary source** (Eurojust press release dated 2026-04-11). No corresponding US DOJ press release has been ingested into this wiki at the time of creation. Treat numeric and temporal claims as provisional pending a second independent primary source (e.g., a US Attorney's Office for the Central District of California release, or a Swedish Prosecution Authority statement). The ≥2-country IC requirement is met on the strength of the Eurojust release, which itself enumerates Swedish (Swedish Prosecution Authority, National Unit Against Organised Crime; Swedish Police) and US (DOJ Office of International Affairs; US Attorney's Office for the Central District of California; IRS-CI; HSI; US Marshals Service) authorities by name.

## Summary

On **2026-04-11**, [[eurojust|Eurojust]] (the European Union Agency for Criminal Justice Cooperation) publicly announced that the **alleged main perpetrator of a large-scale Swedish bank-employee-impersonation fraud scheme had been extradited from the United States to Sweden on 2026-04-10**. The transfer was executed from U.S. Marshals Service custody in the Central District of California to Swedish law enforcement, after a US extradition proceeding handled by the **U.S. Department of Justice's Office of International Affairs ([[office-of-international-affairs]])** and the **U.S. Attorney's Office for the Central District of California**. The extradition followed a coordinated **November 2024 action day** in which the main perpetrator was arrested in the United States and two accomplices were arrested in Sweden. Throughout the entire arc — from pre-action-day investigation to extradition handover — Eurojust provided the judicial-coordination spine.

The underlying alleged conduct is a telephone-based bank-employee-impersonation fraud scheme operating since approximately **2019**, in which fraudsters called victims claiming to be bank employees and induced them to execute money transfers to criminal-group-controlled accounts. **At least 25 victims** were defrauded with estimated damages totalling approximately **EUR 6 million**; some victims lost their entire life savings.

## Background

The alleged criminal group operated a vishing (voice phishing) fraud pattern that is a recurring template in EU consumer fraud: the caller poses as a bank-fraud-prevention employee, instructs the victim to "secure" their account by transferring funds to a "safe" account, and uses real-time scripted social engineering to walk the victim through the transfer. The Eurojust release does not name the bank(s) impersonated, the recruitment/operations geography of the criminal group, or the technical infrastructure used (call-routing, money-mule structure). The scheme is described as operating since "approximately 2019," giving a roughly five-to-six-year operational window before the November 2024 action day.

## Participating Parties

### Sweden (requesting state, victim-jurisdiction)

| Swedish authority | Role in this operation |
|---|---|
| **Swedish Prosecution Authority — National Unit Against Organised Crime** | Lead Swedish prosecutorial authority; will further question the extradited main suspect on Swedish soil. *No dedicated wiki page; recorded in prose only.* |
| **Swedish Police** ([[sweden-police|Swedish Police Authority]]) | Lead Swedish investigative agency; executed the November 2024 arrests in Sweden of the two accomplices. |

### United States (extradition source state, locus of main-suspect arrest)

| US authority | Role in this operation |
|---|---|
| **DOJ Office of International Affairs** ([[office-of-international-affairs]]) | US-side coordination counterpart for the extradition proceeding. |
| **US Attorney's Office for the Central District of California** | Federal prosecutorial office that handled the extradition matter in the C.D. Cal. *No dedicated wiki page; recorded in prose only.* |
| **Internal Revenue

title: Treasury–UK NCA Sanctions on Aeza Group Russian Bulletproof Hosting (2025)
text:
# Treasury–UK NCA Sanctions on Aeza Group Russian Bulletproof Hosting (2025)

## Summary

On 1 July 2025, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), acting in coordination with the United Kingdom's [[uk-nca|National Crime Agency]], designated **Aeza Group** — a St. Petersburg, Russia bulletproof hosting (BPH) services provider — together with three Aeza-affiliated companies (Aeza International Ltd. in the UK, Aeza Logistic LLC, and Cloud Solutions LLC) and four Aeza Group leaders (Arsenii Aleksandrovich Penzev, Yurii Meruzhanovich Bozoyan, Vladimir Vyacheslavovich Gast, and Igor Anatolyevich Knyazev) (Treasury press release SB-0185; reliability: high; credibility: confirmed). All designations were issued under Executive Order 13694, as further amended by E.O. 14144 and E.O. 14306.

The action is described on the record as building on [[us-treasury|OFAC]]'s February 2025 designation of the ZServers BPH provider, establishing a documented Treasury campaign against the Russian-aligned BPH ecosystem in 2025. The action is **almost certainly** the most consequential bilateral U.S.–UK cyber-sanctions designation of a Russian BPH provider in mid-2025 (high confidence, based on the on-the-record framing in the Treasury release and the subsequent November 2025 follow-on action against Aeza's rebranded successor). The cybercriminal customer portfolio identified by Treasury — Meduza and Lumma infostealers, BianLian ransomware, RedLine infostealer panels, and the BlackSprut darknet drug marketplace — places the operation squarely at the intersection of [[ransomware-ic]], [[malware-ic]], and [[cybercrime-infrastructure-ic]] cooperation.

## Background

Bulletproof hosting providers sell access to specialized servers and other computer infrastructure designed to help cybercriminals — ransomware actors, infostealer operators, and darknet marketplace operators — evade detection and resist law enforcement attempts to disrupt their malicious activities. By design, BPH providers offer high resistance to abuse-complaint takedowns, accept anonymous or laundered payment, and concentrate risk in jurisdictions where law-enforcement disruption of the underlying infrastructure is operationally difficult.

[[united-states|U.S.]] Treasury treats BPH providers as a high-priority enabler layer of the cybercrime ecosystem. The Aeza Group designation is the second of three documented 2025 OFAC sanctions actions against the Russian-aligned BPH ecosystem:

1. **February 2025**: OFAC designation of ZServers BPH (referenced on the record in the SB-0185 release; not itself in scope of this operation page).
2. **1 July 2025 (this operation)**: OFAC + UK NCA designation of Aeza Group, three affiliates, and four leaders.
3. **19 November 2025**: trilateral OFAC + Australia DFAT + UK FCDO designation of Media Land LLC and the [[treasury-us-au-uk-sanctions-media-land-russian-bulletproof-hosting-2025|Aeza sanctions-evasion network (Hypercore Ltd. / Smart Digital Ideas DOO / Datavice MCHJ)]], explicitly framed as a response to Aeza's post-July 2025 rebranding strategy.

This three-action sequence over nine months is **likely** the clearest 2025 illustration of the Treasury "maintenance-of-pressure" approach to Russian BPH (medium-high confidence).

## Participating Parties

**Lead jurisdiction**:
- [[united-states|United States]] — Department of the Treasury, [[us-treasury|Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)]], with FinCEN supplemental advisory linkage on the BlackSprut darknet-drug-trafficking dimension.

**Bilateral coordination partner**:
- [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom]] — [[uk-nca|National Crime Agency (NCA)]]. The release describes OFAC as acting "in coordination with" the NCA specifically for the designation of the Aeza UK front company (Aeza International Ltd.).

**Designated-target jurisdiction**:
- [[russia|Russia]] — host state of Aeza Group, Aeza Logistic LLC, Cloud Solutions LLC, and the four designated individuals.

title: US Treasury OFAC Doppelganger Russian Foreign Malign Influence Sanctions (JY-2559, September 2024)
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> Published below the preferred 5-source threshold (CLAUDE.md "Entity creation threshold"). One tier-1 primary source (Treasury press release JY-2559, fetched via curl_cffi TLS impersonation). The companion DOJ release is the same-day enforcement partner and is already wiki-resident as [[us-doj-doppelganger-russian-influence-op-domain-seizures-2024]]. Subsequent OFAC fact sheet, Federal Register E.O. 14024 entry, and OFAC SDN List update entries should be ingested to lift this page to standard publication state.

> [!warning] IC scope flag
> The Treasury press release names **no foreign government, foreign LE counterpart, or international organization** as a same-day cooperation partner. The release does cross-reference (a) the DOJ/FBI same-day domain seizure (US domestic) and (b) a *prior* EU sanction of ANO Dialog for War on Fakes — note that the EU action is **prior and separate**, not coordinated with this 2024-09-04 designation. The IC dimension here is therefore: foreign-national targets, foreign state-actor predicate, US-only enforcement, no reciprocal foreign assistance documented.

## Summary

On **4 September 2024**, the **U.S. Department of the Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)** issued press release **JY-2559**, designating **10 Russian individuals and 2 Russian entities** under **Executive Order 14024** (Russia harmful foreign activities; IEEPA authority) as the financial-sanctions component of a coordinated U.S.-government response to Moscow's malign influence efforts targeting the 2024 U.S. presidential election (Source: [[2024-09-04_treasury_sanctions-doppelganger-russian-malign-influence-operators|Treasury JY-2559]]).

The action is the **same-day companion** to:

1. **DOJ / FBI 32-domain seizure** under the [[us-doj-doppelganger-russian-influence-op-domain-seizures-2024|EDPA Doppelgänger seizure warrant]] and SDNY indictment of two RT employees (Kalashnikov and Afanasyeva — both also designated by this OFAC action).
2. **U.S. State Department** Foreign Mission designation of **Rossiya Segodnya** and five subsidiaries (RIA Novosti, RT, TV-Novosti, Ruptly, Sputnik), plus visa restrictions and a Rewards for Justice (RFJ) offer of up to **$10 million** for information on foreign election interference.

It is *almost certain* (>95% confidence, tier-1 Treasury primary source) that the 10+2 designation count, the entity identities (ANO Dialog and ANO Dialog Regions), and the JY-2559 / E.O. 14024 authority basis are correct as listed.

## Background

"Doppelgänger" is the public/industry name for a long-running Russian state-directed covert foreign malign influence operation — first publicly identified in 2022 — that uses **cybersquatted domains mimicking Western news outlets** combined with paid social-media amplification to push Russian government narratives. Treasury identifies **ANO Dialog** as **linked to Doppelgänger**, specifically as the operator of the **"War on Fakes"** disinformation website and a contributor to the **"Reliable Recent News" (RRN)** outlet (Source: [[2024-09-04_treasury_sanctions-doppelganger-russian-malign-influence-operators|Treasury JY-2559]]).

Treasury alleges:

- In **fall 2022**, ANO Dialog Director General Vladimir Tabak attended a meeting with senior Russian government officials where the presumed success of Doppelgänger activity was discussed and feedback/guidance for future projects was given.
- In **July 2022**, ANO Dialog and Tabak provided services to the Russian government for the creation of fake online posts on RRN and War on Fakes "to elicit an emotional response from the audiences." An ANO Dialog advisor purchased the domain **"rrn.world"** to avoid using "rrussianews.com" which would have maintained a Russia connection.
- In **late fall 2023**, ANO Dialog personnel identified U.S., UK, and other public figures as potential targets for deepfake projects. (Treasury notes the EU has **separately** sanctioned ANO Dialog for its role

title: Treasury Sanctions Facilitators of DPRK IT Worker Fraud — Amnokgang / Quangvietdnbg / Vietnam-Laos-Spain Network (March 2026)
title_ko: DPRK IT 노동자 사기 조력자 미 재무부 제재 — Amnokgang / Quangvietdnbg / 베트남-라오스-스페인 네트워크 (2026년 3월)
text:
## Summary

On 2026-03-12, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued press release SB-0416 announcing sanctions on six individuals and two entities for their roles in DPRK government-orchestrated IT worker schemes that systematically defraud U.S. businesses. The action targets facilitators based in [[north-korea|the DPRK]], [[vietnam|Vietnam]], [[laos|Laos]], and [[spain|Spain]]. Six designations were made pursuant to Executive Order (E.O.) 13810 and two pursuant to E.O. 13382 (Do Phi Khanh and Hoang Van Nguyen, both for support to U.S.-sanctioned DPRK nuclear procurement facilitator Kim Se Un). The release attributes nearly USD 800 million in DPRK IT worker revenue in 2024 to the broader scheme — the highest single-year scale figure in OFAC's public enforcement record on DPRK IT workers to date. The action introduces the **Amnokgang → Quangvietdnbg / Vietnam-based front-company** structure (Nguyen Quang Viet's company converted approximately USD 2.5 million into cryptocurrency for North Koreans between mid-2023 and mid-2025) as a discrete, IC-relevant facilitation pattern.

## Background

The DPRK government employs overseas IT worker delegations to generate revenue for its weapons of mass destruction (WMD) and ballistic missile programs, in violation of U.S. and United Nations sanctions. Per the SB-0416 release, "the DPRK government reportedly appropriates the majority of the wages earned by these overseas IT workers, generating hundreds of millions of dollars to support the regime's WMD and ballistic missile programs … including nearly $800 million in 2024." DPRK-affiliated workers commonly use fraudulent documentation, stolen identities, and fabricated personas to obtain employment with U.S. and allied-country businesses, and in certain instances "have also covertly introduced malware into company networks to extract proprietary and sensitive information."

SB-0416 builds on a chain of prior OFAC actions in this enforcement track, most directly:

- The August 27, 2025 OFAC SB-0230 action ([[treasury-sanctions-dprk-it-worker-network-andreyev-chinyong-2025|Andreyev / Chinyong network]]) — Russian-national facilitator + Russia-based DPRK consular official + Chinese front company + DPRK trading company.
- The November 4, 2025 OFAC SB-0302 action ([[treasury-sanctions-dprk-bankers-laundering-cybercrime-2025|DPRK bankers / Ryujong Credit Bank / KMCTC]]) — financial-institution layer above the IT worker monetization layer.
- The June 30, 2025 DOJ RevGen Initiative actions ([[us-doj-coordinated-nationwide-actions-dprk-it-workers-2025]]) — domestic U.S. criminal track on DPRK IT worker fraud.

The release is highly likely (>80% confidence) part of the same continuing whole-of-government posture, and is described in the body as part of "the United States' whole-of-government effort to counter the DPRK's wide-ranging revenue generation schemes."

## Designated Targets

The eight designees, with the legal authority cited for each:

| # | Designee | Type | Nexus | Authority | Key Conduct |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Amnokgang Technology Development Company** | Entity | DPRK | E.O. 13810 (operating in the IT industry in North Korea) | DPRK IT company managing overseas IT worker delegations and procuring/selling military and commercial technology |
| 2 | **Nguyen Quang Viet** | Individual | Vietnamese national; CEO of Quangvietdnbg | E.O. 13810 (material support to Amnokgang) | Converted approximately USD 2.5 million into cryptocurrency for North Koreans, mid-2023 to mid-2025, including illicit earnings from Amnokgang-associated IT workers |
| 3 | **Quangvietdnbg International Services Company Limited** | Entity | Vietnam-based front company | E.O. 13810 (owned/controlled by Nguyen) | Vehicle providing currency conversion services for North Koreans |
| 4 | **Do Phi Khanh** | Individual | Proxy for U.S.-sanctioned DPRK nuclear procurement facilitator Kim Se Un | E.O. 13382 (support to Kim

title: Treasury OFAC Designation of Korea Sobaeksu Trading Company and DPRK IT-Worker Facilitators (July 2025)
text:
# Treasury OFAC Designation of Korea Sobaeksu Trading Company and DPRK IT-Worker Facilitators (July 2025)

## Summary

On **24 July 2025**, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) designated the **Korea Sobaeksu Trading Company** (a.k.a. Sobaeksu United Corporation) and three associated DPRK individuals — **Kim Se Un**, **Jo Kyong Hun**, and **Myong Chol Min** — for evading U.S. and United Nations sanctions and generating revenue for the DPRK government, including through fraudulent IT-worker schemes that funnel earnings into the regime's nuclear and ballistic missile programs. The action was paired the same day with a Department of Justice unsealing of indictments against seven DPRK nationals for International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) violations involving counterfeit cigarette trafficking, and with State Department reward offers under the Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program of up to $15 million in total. Based on the single tier-1 Treasury press release, the operation is **almost certainly** an interagency designation package rather than an arrest-driven enforcement action: assessed outcome is **success** in the narrow sense of completing the SDN listings and rewards announcement, while operational impact (asset blocking, prosecution, deterrence) will accrue over time.

## Background

The DPRK has long dispatched teams of skilled IT workers overseas, using fraudulent documents, stolen identities, and false personas to obfuscate their nationality and infiltrate legitimate companies in the United States and other allied jurisdictions. According to the 24 July 2025 Treasury release, the regime withholds most of the wages earned by these workers, generating "hundreds of millions of dollars" annually to support weapons of mass destruction and ballistic missile programs. The release notes that DPRK IT workers have, in some cases, introduced malware into client networks to exfiltrate proprietary data, blurring the line between sanctions-evading employment fraud and cyber intrusion. Korea Sobaeksu Trading Company is described as a front company for the U.S.-designated **Munitions Industry Department**, the DPRK body that oversees the nuclear program and ballistic missile development; Sobaeksu has previously dispatched IT-worker teams overseas, including to Vietnam, and has engaged in nuclear procurement on behalf of the Munitions Industry Department. The 24 July action sits within an escalating 2024-2026 enforcement arc against DPRK IT-worker infrastructure that includes [[us-doj-coordinated-nationwide-actions-dprk-it-workers-2025]], [[treasury-sanctions-dprk-it-worker-network-andreyev-chinyong-2025]], and the bank-laundering-focused [[treasury-sanctions-dprk-bankers-laundering-cybercrime-2025]].

## Participating Parties

Lead designating authority: **[[us-treasury]]** OFAC. The press release explicitly characterizes the action as a "whole-of-government effort" coordinated with the **Departments of Justice, Homeland Security, and State**; the **Federal Bureau of Investigation**; and **Homeland Security Investigations**. Wiki-linked participants confirmed via Glob: [[us-treasury]], [[us-doj]], [[us-dhs]], [[fbi]]. The State Department and HSI play named roles in the release (TOC Rewards offers and investigative support respectively) but are not yet maintained as standalone wiki organization pages and so are not wikilinked here. Targets are based in **[[north-korea]]** with operational nodes in third countries (Vietnam is the only third country named in this specific release in connection with Sobaeksu's IT worker dispatch).

## Legal Framework

OFAC's designations rely on two Executive Orders, both pre-existing DPRK-focused authorities:

- **Executive Order 13382** ("Blocking Property of Weapons of Mass Destruction Proliferators and Their Supporters"): applied to **Sobaeksu** (for being owned or controlled by, or acting for or on behalf of, the U.S.-designated Munitions Ind

title: Tri-Lateral US-UK-AU Sanctions on Evil Corp Members and Concurrent DOJ-EDTX Ryzhenkov BitPaymer Indictment (2024)
text:
## Summary

On **2024-10-01**, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) designated **seven Russian-national individuals** (Eduard Benderskiy; Viktor Grigoryevich Yakubets; Aleksandr Viktorovich Ryzhenkov; Sergey Viktorovich Ryzhenkov; Aleksey Yevgenevich Shchetinin; Beyat Enverovich Ramazanov; Vadim Gennadievich Pogodin) and **two Russia-based entities** (Vympel-Assistance LLC; Solar-Invest LLC) as members and affiliates of the Russia-based [[ransomware-ic|Evil Corp]] cybercriminal group, under [[asset-freezing|E.O. 13694 as amended and E.O. 14024]]. On the same day, the **United Kingdom's** FCDO designated **15 additional** Evil Corp persons, **Australia's** DFAT designated **3 additional** Evil Corp persons, and the **U.S. Department of Justice** unsealed an Eastern District of Texas indictment charging **Aleksandr Ryzhenkov** with using **BitPaymer ransomware** to target numerous U.S. victims. The action coincided with day 2 of the U.S.-hosted Counter Ransomware Initiative (CRI) summit (over 50 countries).

This action is *almost certainly* the **first wiki-recorded US-UK-AU tri-lateral cyber-sanctions action** and the predicate template for the subsequent 2025 BPH-track sanctions chain ([[treasury-us-au-uk-zservers-sanctions-2025|Zservers]] Feb 2025; [[treasury-aeza-group-bulletproof-hosting-sanctions-2025|Aeza Group]] Jul 2025; [[treasury-us-au-uk-sanctions-media-land-russian-bulletproof-hosting-2025|Media Land]] Nov 2025).

## Background

Evil Corp is a Russia-based cybercriminal organization responsible for the development and distribution of the **Dridex** banking-trojan malware. Per Treasury, Dridex was used to harvest login credentials from hundreds of banks and other financial institutions in over 40 countries, resulting in more than **$100 million in theft losses** to U.S. and international financial institutions and their customers. On December 5, 2019, OFAC designated Evil Corp, its leader Maksim Viktorovich Yakubets, and over a dozen members, facilitators, and affiliated companies under [[asset-freezing|E.O. 13694, as amended by E.O. 13757]]; concurrently, DOJ indicted Maksim Yakubets and Evil Corp administrator Igor Turashev (see [[us-v-yakubets-dridex]] and [[operation-us-v-yakubets-dridex]]). The Department of State's Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program issued a $5 million reward for Maksim's capture. In October 2022, OFAC designated the **Russian National Engineering Corporation (NIK)** and its principals **Igor Yuryevich Chayka** and **Aleksei Valeryavich Troshin** under E.O. 14024 — Maksim Yakubets had used his employment at NIK as cover for ongoing Evil Corp activity.

The October 1, 2024 action — JY-2623 — is the next iteration of this 5-year sanctions chain, expanding the designation perimeter to include (i) Maksim's father-in-law and key state-nexus enabler Eduard Benderskiy and his two consulting companies, (ii) Maksim's father Viktor, and (iii) five additional operational members and affiliates including the second-in-command Aleksandr Ryzhenkov.

## Participating Parties

- **United States** — OFAC (lead designator under E.O. 13694 as amended and E.O. 14024); DOJ-EDTX (concurrent indictment unsealing against Aleksandr Ryzhenkov for BitPaymer ransomware); FBI (wanted poster for Aleksandr Ryzhenkov; investigative support).
- **United Kingdom** — Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) designated 15 Evil Corp members and affiliates on the same day. The UK [[uk-nca|National Crime Agency]] is *highly likely* to have provided the underlying investigative dossier given its lead role in UK ransomware enforcement, although JY-2623 does not name NCA directly.
- **Australia** — Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) designated 3 Evil Corp members and affiliates on the same day.
- **Russia** — host jurisdiction for all named designees and entities; not a participating party.

## Legal Framework

title: Treasury Sanctions Targeting Garantex, Grinex, and Russian Sanctions-Evasion Network (August 2025)
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> This operation page is published below the preferred 5-source threshold (current `source_count: 1`). It is retained because the action is a high-salience tier-1 government primary-source designation that closes a specific sanctions-evasion loophole opened by the March 2025 [[us-secret-service|USSS]]-led Garantex disruption already documented in the wiki. The page should be enriched as additional reporting (Chainalysis, TRM Labs, OFAC SDN list entries, follow-on indictments) becomes available.

## Summary

On 14 August 2025, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued press release SB-0225 announcing a layered sanctions package against the Garantex cryptocurrency exchange ecosystem. The action (1) **re-designated** Garantex Europe OU under E.O. 13694 (cyber authority), supplementing its prior April 2022 designation under E.O. 14024 (Russia financial services sector); (2) **designated Grinex**, the successor exchange created by Garantex employees in the days after the March 2025 law enforcement disruption; (3) designated three Garantex executives (Sergey Mendeleev, Aleksandr Mira Serda, Pavel Karavatsky); and (4) designated six associated companies operating in Russia and the Kyrgyz Republic (A7 LLC, A71 LLC, A7 Agent LLC, Old Vector, InDeFi Bank, Exved) that supported the exchange's role in laundering ransomware proceeds and Russia-linked sanctions evasion.

The action is **highly likely** the most consequential sanctions-side response to the post-disruption migration pattern observed in cryptocurrency exchange enforcement: when an exchange is taken down, customer funds and infrastructure are reconstituted in a successor brand. The August 2025 designation closes that loophole for the Garantex case by sanctioning the successor (Grinex) under the same cyber authority and adding the executives and infrastructure providers who enabled the migration.

## Background

Garantex was founded in late 2019, originally registered in Estonia, with operational center in Moscow and Saint Petersburg. In February 2022 it lost its Estonian digital asset license after Estonia's Financial Intelligence Unit identified AML/CFT deficiencies and links to wallets used for criminal activity. OFAC first designated Garantex on **5 April 2022** under E.O. 14024 for operating in the financial services sector of the Russian Federation. Despite the designation, Garantex continued to operate, developing infrastructure to obscure attribution of cryptocurrency wallet addresses back to the exchange.

According to OFAC, Garantex received millions of dollars in cryptocurrency directly from the proceeds of Russia-linked ransomware operations including **Conti, Black Basta, LockBit, NetWalker, and Phoenix Cryptolocker**, and provided account/exchange services to **Ryuk**-associated actors. OFAC states that over USD 100 million of known Garantex transactions are associated with illicit actors since 2019.

On **6 March 2025**, the U.S. Secret Service (USSS), in partnership with German and Finnish law enforcement, took disruptive measures against Garantex's computer infrastructure, seizing its web domain and freezing over USD 26 million in cryptocurrency. On **7 March 2025**, the U.S. Department of Justice unsealed indictments against Garantex executives Aleksandr Mira Serda and Aleksej Besciokov; Besciokov was subsequently arrested in India. Within days, Garantex officers transferred customer deposits to a newly created exchange, **Grinex**, and used a ruble-backed token (**A7A5**), issued by Kyrgyzstani firm Old Vector for Russian A7 LLC, to compensate customers whose Garantex funds had been frozen. The August 2025 OFAC action targets that reconstitution.

## Designated Targets

**Re-designated entity (added cyber authority):**
- **Garantex Europe OU** — designated under E.O. 13694, as further amended by E.O. 14144 and E.O. 14306 (cyber-enabled malicious activity), in addition to existing E.O. 14024

title: U.S. Treasury OFAC Sanctions Designation of Integrity Technology Group (Flax Typhoon, PRC) — January 2025
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> Published below the preferred 5-source threshold (CLAUDE.md "Entity creation threshold"). One tier-1 primary source (Treasury OFAC press release jy2769, 3 January 2025; reconstructed from three independent secondary mirrors per L11 — primary URL fetch timed out). Companion artifacts to ingest in subsequent rounds: (a) the OFAC SDN list entry for Integrity Tech, (b) any subsequent FBI/CISA advisory updates citing the designation, (c) any later UK/AU/CA/NZ parallel actions if issued.

> [!warning] IC scope flag — U.S. unilateral sanction
> This is a **U.S. unilateral targeted financial designation** by OFAC. Unlike the September 2024 DOJ Raptor Train takedown, this action is **not accompanied by parallel partner-state sanctions** in the United Kingdom, Australia, Canada, or New Zealand on the announcement date (per the source release). The "Five Eye partners" reference in the press release applies retrospectively to the **September 18, 2024 joint cybersecurity advisory** — not to this OFAC designation. The IC dimension here is therefore limited to **prior cooperative attribution** (FVEY advisory) rather than **coordinated financial measures**. Per CLAUDE.md "Entity creation threshold" and L19, only `[[united-states]]` (designating authority) and `[[china]]` (target nationality) are asserted as participating_countries.

> [!warning] Per L19 — only entities explicitly named in the primary source are asserted
> Asserted: **U.S. Treasury OFAC** (designating authority), **FBI** (named in the release as the agency whose September 2024 advisory documents Integrity Tech's role), and **Integrity Technology Group, Inc.** (target entity, [[china|People's Republic of China]]). NSA, USCYBERCOM CNMF, and FVEY partner cyber agencies are referenced only via the September 2024 joint advisory — they are not co-actors of this January 2025 OFAC action and are not asserted here.

## Summary

On **3 January 2025**, the **U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)** designated **Integrity Technology Group, Incorporated** — a Beijing-based, publicly-traded cybersecurity company assessed by the FBI as the corporate entity behind the China-based hacker activity industry tracks as **Flax Typhoon** — for its role in computer intrusion incidents targeting U.S. victims and U.S. critical infrastructure (Source: [[2025-01-03_treasury_sanctions-integrity-technology-group-flax-typhoon|Treasury OFAC press release jy2769, 3 Jan 2025]]).

The designation is issued under **Executive Order 13694** ("Blocking the Property of Certain Persons Engaging in Significant Malicious Cyber-Enabled Activities," 1 April 2015) **as amended by Executive Order 13757** (28 December 2016). Its legal effect is to block all Integrity Tech property and interests in the United States or in U.S.-person control, prohibit U.S.-person transactions absent OFAC license, and expose foreign financial institutions facilitating significant transactions to secondary-sanctions risk.

This designation is the **targeted-sanction companion** to the September 18, 2024 [[us-doj-raptor-train-flax-typhoon-prc-botnet-disruption-2024|DOJ court-authorized Raptor Train botnet disruption]]. The U.S. response to Flax Typhoon / Integrity Technology Group is therefore now a **three-track package**: (i) court-authorized infrastructure takeover (DOJ/FBI, Sept 2024), (ii) FVEY joint cybersecurity advisory (FBI + NSA + USCYBERCOM CNMF + AU/CA/NZ/UK partner cyber agencies, Sept 2024), and (iii) OFAC targeted financial designation (Treasury, Jan 2025). The three actions are spaced approximately **3.5 months** apart.

It is **almost certain** (>95% confidence, tier-1 Treasury source) that the designation occurred on the announced date with the stated authority and target. It is **likely** (55-80%) that follow-on or parallel partner-state designations could be issued by FVEY allies in subsequent rounds, but **no such parallel action is documented in this primary s

title: Treasury OFAC Sanctions and DOJ Indictments of Mikhail Matveev (2023)
text:
> [!info] Provisional / single-source
> This operation page is published below the preferred 5-source threshold because the Treasury press release is itself a tier-1 primary source for the administrative action it announces. Enrichment from the parallel DOJ-NJ and DOJ-DC indictments and from the State Department Rewards for Justice listing is anticipated.

# Treasury OFAC Sanctions and DOJ Indictments of Mikhail Matveev (2023)

## Summary

On **2023-05-16**, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control ([[us-treasury|OFAC]]) designated Russian national **Mikhail Pavlovich Matveev** to the Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons (SDN) List under [Executive Order 13694, as amended by E.O. 13757](https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy1486), for cyber-enabled activity targeting U.S. law enforcement, businesses, and critical infrastructure (Treasury press release JY-1486). On the same day, the [[us-doj|U.S. Department of Justice]] unsealed two parallel federal indictments — one in the **U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey** and one in the **U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia** — and the U.S. Department of State announced a reward of up to **USD 10 million** under its Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program for information leading to Matveev's arrest and/or conviction.

Treasury characterizes Matveev as a "central figure in the development and deployment of the Hive, LockBit, and Babuk ransomware variants, among others," with attribution to a 2021 Babuk affiliate attack on the police department of a major U.S. city. The action is highly likely an example of the **administrative-plus-criminal parallel-track pattern** that the United States deploys when the suspect is located in a jurisdiction (here, Russia) from which extradition is not realistically available.

## Background

The release situates Matveev within a broader U.S. government posture toward Russia-linked ransomware. Treasury cites Financial Crimes Enforcement Network ([FinCEN](https://www.fincen.gov/sites/default/files/2022-11/Financial%20Trend%20Analysis_Ransomware%20FTA%202_508%20FINAL.pdf)) data attributing 75% of ransomware-related incidents reported July–December 2021 to Russia, its proxies, or persons acting on its behalf, and notes that the Hive variant alone had targeted more than 1,500 victims in over 80 countries before its [[hive-ransomware-takedown|January 2023 disruption]].

Matveev is also described as having been "vocal about his illegal activities" — giving public media interviews, releasing exploit code, and stating that his activities would be tolerated by local authorities so long as he remained loyal to Russia. This open posture is itself the evidentiary basis Treasury foregrounds for the designation.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Entity |
|---|---|
| Lead designating authority | [[us-treasury\|U.S. Department of the Treasury]] (OFAC) |
| Concurrent prosecuting authority | [[us-doj\|U.S. Department of Justice]] (USAO-NJ and USAO-DC) |
| Investigative agency | [[fbi\|U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation]] (referenced indirectly via the Treasury-DOJ tri-track action) |
| Reward-issuing authority | U.S. Department of State (Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program) |
| Country whose institutions acted | [[united-states]] |
| Country of nexus to the target | [[russia]] (Matveev's location and nationality) |

## Legal Framework

- **Executive Order 13694** (April 1, 2015) — *Blocking the Property of Certain Persons Engaging in Significant Malicious Cyber-Enabled Activities* — as amended by **E.O. 13757** (December 28, 2016). OFAC invoked **section 1(a)(ii)(C)**, which addresses cyber-enabled activity reasonably likely to cause significant disruption to the availability of a computer or network of computers.
- **Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program** (TOCRP), administered by the U.S. Department of State, authorising rewards of up to USD 25 million (Matveev o

title: Treasury Sanctions DPRK Bankers and Institutions for Laundering Cybercrime and IT Worker Proceeds (November 2025)
title_ko: DPRK 사이버범죄 및 IT 노동자 자금세탁 관련 미 재무부 제재 (2025년 11월)
text:
> [!info] Provisional page
> This operation page is published from a single tier-1 source (OFAC SB-0302). Per the project's preferred publication threshold (`source_count >= 5`), it is treated as provisional pending corroborating sources (MSMT report excerpt, follow-on enforcement actions, partner-state designations).

## Summary

On 2025-11-04, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control ([[us-treasury|OFAC]]) announced press release SB-0302, designating eight individuals and two entities for laundering funds derived from DPRK cybercrime (including ransomware against U.S. victims) and DPRK IT worker fraud schemes. The action targeted (1) two North Korean bankers managing cryptocurrency for previously-designated First Credit Bank; (2) Korea Mangyongdae Computer Technology Company (KMCTC) and its president for IT worker delegation operations from China; (3) Ryujong Credit Bank for sanctions-evasion remittance between China and North Korea; and (4) five China- or Russia-based representatives of already-designated DPRK financial institutions. OFAC also updated First Credit Bank's SDN entry to surface its cryptocurrency addresses, an enforcement step that is highly likely intended to support compliance screening by virtual-asset service providers.

## Background

Per the release, the Government of the DPRK relies on cybercrime as a significant illicit-revenue stream for its weapons of mass destruction (WMD) and ballistic missile programs. Treasury cites that over the past three years, North Korea-affiliated cybercriminals have stolen over $3 billion, primarily in cryptocurrency. In parallel, DPRK IT workers earn hundreds of millions of dollars per year by obfuscating their nationality on freelance work platforms, sometimes splitting revenue with non-DPRK freelance partners.

The designations are explicitly framed against the backdrop of the October 22 (2025) Multilateral Sanctions Monitoring Team (MSMT) report titled "The DPRK's Violation and Evasion of UN Sanctions Through Cyber and Information Technology Worker Activities." Treasury describes the multilateral context as "stand[ing] alongside the other MSMT participating states in remaining committed to strengthening collective resilience against such threats."

## Designated Targets

**Bankers managing cybercrime cryptocurrency proceeds (E.O. 13694 as amended + E.O. 13810):**

- **Jang Kuk Chol** — North Korean banker, managed funds (including $5.3M in cryptocurrency) for [[north-korea|DPRK]]-designated First Credit Bank.
- **Ho Jong Son** — North Korean banker, same scheme as Jang.

**IT worker delegation network (E.O. 13810):**

- **Korea Mangyongdae Computer Technology Company (KMCTC)** — North Korea-based IT company operating worker delegations from Shenyang and Dandong, [[china]].
- **U Yong Su** — KMCTC president.

**Sanctions-evasion financial institution (E.O. 13810):**

- **Ryujong Credit Bank** — North Korea-based financial institution, facilitated sanctions-avoidance activities between [[china]] and [[north-korea]].

**Overseas representatives of DPRK financial institutions:**

- **Ho Yong Chol** (E.O. 13551) — representative of Korea Daesong Bank; facilitated $2.5M (USD/CNY) on behalf of Korea Daesong Bank, plus $85M+ on behalf of another DPRK government-affiliated group.
- **Han Hong Gil** (E.O. 13810) — Koryo Commercial Bank (KCB) employee; coordinated $630K+ (USD/CNY) on behalf of Ryugyong Commercial Bank.
- **Jong Sung Hyok** (E.O. 13722) — FTB chief representative in Vladivostok, [[russia]].
- **Choe Chun Pom** (E.O. 13722) — DPRK Central Bank representative; facilitated $200K+ (USD/CNY) and coordinated travel for Russian officials to Pyongyang.
- **Ri Jin Hyok** (E.O. 13722) — FTB representative; facilitated $350K+ (USD/CNY/EUR) for FTB front company.

## Participating Parties

- **Lead/coordinating agency:** [[us-treasury|U.S. Department of the Treasury (OFAC)]].
- **Country jurisdiction of action:** [[united-states]] (designating au

title: Treasury Sanctions DPRK IT Worker Fraud Network — Andreyev / Kim Ung Sun / Shenyang Geumpungri / Sinjin (August 2025)
title_ko: DPRK IT 노동자 사기 네트워크 미 재무부 제재 — Andreyev / Kim Ung Sun / Shenyang Geumpungri / Sinjin (2025년 8월)
text:
## Summary

On 2025-08-27, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued press release SB-0230 announcing sanctions on four targets — one Russian national (Vitaliy Sergeyevich Andreyev), one Russia-based DPRK consular official (Kim Ung Sun), one Chinese front company (Shenyang Geumpungri Network Technology Co., Ltd.), and one DPRK trading company (Korea Sinjin Trading Corporation) — for facilitating revenue generation for [[north-korea|the DPRK]] through the Chinyong Information Technology Cooperation Company IT worker network. All four designations were made pursuant to Executive Order 13687. The action documented approximately USD 600,000 in crypto-to-USD conversions since at least December 2024 and over USD 1 million in cumulative profits since 2021. The press release likely (>80% confidence) represents a coordinated diplomatic moment: it was issued the same day as a joint statement on DPRK IT worker threats by the U.S. Department of State and the foreign ministries of Japan and the Republic of Korea.

## Background

The DPRK government employs overseas IT worker delegations to generate hundreds of millions of dollars per year for its weapons of mass destruction and ballistic missile programs, in violation of U.S. and United Nations sanctions. Chinyong Information Technology Cooperation Company is the DPRK defense-ministry-affiliated entity that employs IT worker delegations deployed to Russia and Laos. SB-0230 explicitly expands the prior Chinyong designation, targeting additional entities in its network and "combatting its use of cryptocurrency for sanctions evasion." It is the third OFAC action in this enforcement chain in roughly two months — building on July 8 and July 24, 2025 OFAC sanctions — and is highly likely (>80% confidence) part of the same broad whole-of-government posture that produced the [[us-doj-coordinated-nationwide-actions-dprk-it-workers-2025|June 30, 2025 DOJ RevGen Initiative wave]] and was followed by the [[treasury-sanctions-dprk-bankers-laundering-cybercrime-2025|November 4, 2025 OFAC SB-0302 designations]] of DPRK bankers laundering cybercrime and IT worker proceeds.

## Designated Targets

The four designees, with the legal authority cited for each:

| # | Designee | Type | Nexus | Authority | Key Conduct |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vitaliy Sergeyevich Andreyev** | Individual | Russian national | E.O. 13687 (material support to Chinyong) | Facilitated crypto-to-USD conversions worth nearly USD 600,000 since at least December 2024 |
| 2 | **Kim Ung Sun** | Individual | Russia-based DPRK economic and trade consular official | E.O. 13687 (acting for or on behalf of the Government of North Korea) | Coordinated with Andreyev on the crypto-to-USD transfers |
| 3 | **Shenyang Geumpungri Network Technology Co., Ltd.** | Entity | Chinese front company hosting a delegation of DPRK IT workers | E.O. 13687 (acting for Chinyong) | DPRK IT worker delegation has earned over USD 1 million in profits for Chinyong and Sinjin since 2021 |
| 4 | **Korea Sinjin Trading Corporation** | Entity | DPRK-based trading company subordinate to the DPRK Ministry of People's Armed Forces General Political Bureau | E.O. 13687 (acting for or on behalf of the Government of North Korea) | Receives directives from DPRK government officials regarding the DPRK IT workers Chinyong deploys internationally |

## Participating Parties

- **Lead designating agency**: [[us-treasury|U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)]]
- **Coordinated diplomatic partners (named in release)**: U.S. Department of State; foreign ministries of Japan and the Republic of Korea (joint statement issued same day on DPRK IT worker threats)
- **Jurisdictional nexuses (named in release as host or operating jurisdictions)**: [[united-states]] (sanctioning state and harmed-business jurisdiction); [[north-korea]] (target government); [[russia]] (host of Kim Ung Sun's consular activity, A

title: OFAC Sanctions Cambodian Senator Kok An + Scam Center Network — 29 designations + Scam Center Strike Force coordinated actions (Treasury/OFAC + USAO-DC + DOJ-Crim + FBI + USSS, 2026-04-23)
title_ko: OFAC 캄보디아 상원의원 Kok An 및 스캠 센터 네트워크 제재 — 29건 지정 + Scam Center Strike Force 공동 행동 (재무부/OFAC + USAO-DC + DOJ-Crim + FBI + USSS, 2026-04-23)
text:
## Summary

On **23 April 2026**, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued **29 sanctions designations** targeting **Cambodian Senator Kok An and 28 individuals/entities in his scam-compound network** under E.O. 13694 as amended. Same-day, the **Scam Center Strike Force** (USAO-DC + DOJ Criminal Division + FBI + USSS) announced parallel actions: charges against **2 individuals** running a Burma scam compound and attempting to start one in Cambodia, the seizure of **a social media messaging app** used to recruit human-trafficking victims, and the seizure of **503 fraudulent web domains** used for cryptocurrency investment fraud. The action is undertaken in furtherance of **E.O. 14390 of 6 March 2026 ('Combating Cybercrime, Fraud, and Predatory Schemes Against American Citizens')** — a new presidential authority specifically directed at foreign-backed cybercrime / cyber-enabled fraud / extortion targeting Americans.

> [!note] Scam Center Strike Force
> The release introduces (in the wiki's corpus) the **Scam Center Strike Force** as a formal US multi-agency cooperation framework: USAO-DC + DOJ Criminal Division + FBI + USSS, focused on **Burma, Cambodia, and Laos**. Establishes a named US-side institution complementing OFAC sanctions with parallel criminal prosecutions.

## Background

Underlying scope: **Americans lost USD 10 billion+ in 2024 to Southeast Asia-based scam operations** (66% YoY increase). Scam pattern: digital asset investment fraud + romance/friendship lures + human-trafficking forced labor (passport confiscation + debt bondage + physical violence to coerce scam labor). The Cambodian network operates out of casinos and office parks retrofitted for fraudulent activity in Poipet, Sihanoukville, Bavet, and Phnom Penh.

This action chains to prior OFAC actions:
- **12 Nov 2025**: Burmese armed group + 7 others (Tai Chang scam compound, Karen State, Burma)
- **14 Oct 2025**: [[us-uk-prince-group-tco-huione-group-sanctions-2025|Prince Group TCO]] + 146 associates (US-UK joint)
- **8 Sep 2025**: 12 companies + 7 individuals (Cambodia/Burma)
- **12 Sep 2024**: Ly Yong Phat, L.Y.P. Group + 4 hotels

Together, these constitute a sustained ~18-month sanctions campaign against SE-Asia scam-compound infrastructure.

## Designated Targets

**Kok An tier (Kok An is chairman of all 7 Anco entities + Crown Resorts + Anco Brothers):**
- Kok An (Cambodian senator)
- Crown Resorts (flagship hospitality co; casinos in Poipet, Sihanoukville, Bavet)
