| 2019 | W3LLSTORE marketplace established |
| 2019–2023 | W3LLSTORE m

title: Warzone RAT Takedown (Operation against Warzone RAT) — Malta + Nigeria arrests, U.S. domain seizure
text:
## Summary

The Warzone RAT takedown was an international law enforcement action against the infrastructure and key personnel behind the Warzone Remote Access Trojan, a subscription-based malware-as-a-service tool sold from at least 2018 (and tied via the seller to an earlier "Pegasus RAT" via "Skynet-Corporation" since at least 2012). On 9 February 2024, the U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of `warzone.ws` and three related domains (D. Mass.) and unsealed two indictments — Daniel Meli (27, Malta) in the Northern District of Georgia and Prince Onyeoziri Odinakachi (31, Nigeria) in the District of Massachusetts — both arrested on 7 February 2024 by, respectively, the Malta Police Force and the Port Harcourt Zonal Command of Nigeria's Economic and Financial Crimes Commission.

On 13 February 2024 the [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police]] published its own media release (the primary source for this page) crediting the AFP's Cyber Command with identifying Warzone as an emerging cyber threat in 2020 and providing the intelligence that led to the Maltese arrest. The operation was "coordinated with international partners in large part through" the [[europol-ec3|Europol European Cybercrime Center]] (DOJ).

The DOJ acknowledged "valuable assistance" from the law enforcement authorities of Australia, Canada, Croatia, Finland, Germany (specifically the State Police Force of Saxony / [[germany-lka|LKA Saxony]]), Japan (Ministry of Justice), Malta, the Netherlands ([[netherlands-politie|Dutch National Police]]), Nigeria ([[nigeria-efcc|EFCC]]), Romania ([[romania-police|Romanian National Police]]) and the United States — eleven countries in total counting the U.S.

> [!note] Translation note
> Source documents are entirely in English; the term "Remote Access Trojan (RAT)" is preserved as the English term of art used uniformly by AFP, DOJ, and EC3.

## Background

According to the DOJ charging documents:

- Daniel Meli, of Zabbar, Malta, "since at least 2012" offered malware products and services for sale to cybercriminals through online computer-hacking forums. He allegedly assisted cybercriminals seeking to use RATs and offered teaching tools for sale, including an eBook. He sold the Warzone RAT and previously the "Pegasus RAT" through an online criminal organization called "Skynet-Corporation," and provided online customer support to purchasers of both RATs.
- Prince Onyeoziri Odinakachi, between June 2019 and no earlier than March 2023, "provided online customer support to individuals who purchased and used the Warzone RAT malware."
- The AFP states it identified Warzone as an emerging cyber threat in 2020, when the AFP Cyber Command "assisted in the identification of persons of interest and the coordination of intelligence related to the criminal network."

Warzone was distributed via deceptive email attachments and links to seemingly legitimate websites where users would download the software. Capabilities documented in DOJ filings and AFP release: bypass security; remote computer access; browse file systems; take screenshots; record keystrokes; steal usernames and passwords; access webcams. AFP additionally notes that Warzone "enabled other criminal activities such as ransomware attacks and harvesting of information that could lead to identity theft and phishing campaigns."

Subscription pricing as low as **AUD $25 / month** (AFP) / **USD $25 / month** (DOJ filings).

## Participating Parties

**Lead investigative agencies (U.S.):** [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]] via the [[fbi|FBI]] Boston Field Office and Atlanta Field Office; investigation prosecuted by AUSAs Bethany L. Rupert and Michael Herskowitz (N.D. Ga., Meli) and AUSA James R. Drabick (D. Mass., Odinakachi); seizure warrants obtained by AUSAs James R. Drabick and Carol E. Head (D. Mass.).

**Lead investigative agency (Australia):** [[australia-afp|AFP]] Cyber Command; AFP International Network coordinated intelligence sharing.

**Coordinating

title: xDedic Marketplace Takedown
text:
## Summary

xDedic was not a single arrest or a short-lived press event. It was a dark-web marketplace that sold access credentials for compromised servers worldwide, followed by a 2019 infrastructure seizure and a multi-year prosecution campaign. DOJ's January 2024 roundup states that xDedic offered more than **700,000 compromised servers** for sale, including at least **150,000 in the United States** and **8,000 in Florida**.

The marketplace enabled downstream crimes because buyers could purchase server logins and personally identifiable information, then use those resources for tax fraud, access-device fraud, wire fraud, ransomware deployment, and other intrusions. The victims listed in the official account included government infrastructure, hospitals, emergency services, call centers, transit authorities, accounting and law firms, pension funds, and universities.

## Operational Structure

The operation has two distinct phases:

| Phase | Period | What happened |
|---|---|---|
| Infrastructure takedown | 2019 | The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida seized xDedic domain names and dismantled the marketplace infrastructure with foreign partners |
| Prosecution pipeline | 2021-2024 | DOJ pursued administrators, sellers, buyers, and fraud actors through extraditions, convictions, sentencings, and pending extradition matters |

This distinction matters for the wiki model. The 2024 "19 Individuals" release is not a separate marketplace takedown; it is the official prosecution roundup for the same xDedic enforcement set. Defendant-specific operation pages should therefore point back here instead of repeating the same source set.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 2016 | xDedic was publicly exposed as a marketplace for compromised server access |
| 2019-01 | U.S. prosecutors seized xDedic domain names and dismantled the website infrastructure |
| 2021-09 | Ivanov-Tolpintsev was extradited from Poland; Odedeyi/Davies/Adejumo/Jinadu were charged in a related tax-fraud branch |
| 2022-11 | Habasescu was extradited from Spain as a marketplace administrator and technical lead |
| 2023-02 | Pankov was extradited from Georgia as a high-volume seller and NLBrute developer |
| 2024-01 | DOJ issued the 19-person roundup connecting the infrastructure takedown to the prosecution pipeline |
| 2024-08 | DOJ's victim-services page summarized additional buyer and downstream-fraud judgments |

## Cooperation Architecture

The 2019 infrastructure seizure was described as an international operation involving U.S. authorities, Belgian and Ukrainian law enforcement, [[europol-ec3|Europol]], the [[dutch-nhtcu|Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit]], and the [[germany-bka|German Bundeskriminalamt]]. The later prosecution phase added assistance from law enforcement in Belgium, Georgia, Germany, Poland, Spain, the United Kingdom, Romania, Switzerland, Estonia, Latvia, Bulgaria, Ukraine, Lithuania, and Moldova.

DOJ's Office of International Affairs appears as a core procedural actor because many defendants were foreign nationals or were located outside the United States. The record specifically shows extraditions from Spain, Georgia, Poland, and the United Kingdom-linked case stream, plus pending extradition matters for two U.K. defendants as of January 2024.

## Legal Basis

The source set supports several concrete legal mechanisms. The 2019 action used domain and infrastructure seizure to stop marketplace operation. Later U.S. cases used extradition for foreign defendants, mutual legal assistance or equivalent evidence-sharing channels for overseas investigative steps, and electronic evidence from compromised-server sales, marketplace accounts, financial records, and identity-theft/tax-fraud activity.

The record also explains why a single domestic docket cannot fully represent the operation. Some defendants were administrators, some were sellers, and others were buyers or fraud actors who used xDedic-derived a

title: xss.is Russian-speaking Cybercrime Forum Takedown (Kyiv arrest, French-led + Ukrainian SBU + Europol EC3, 2025-07-22)
title_ko: xss.is 러시아어 사이버범죄 포럼 단속 (키예프 검거, 프랑스 주도 + 우크라이나 SBU + Europol EC3, 2025-07-22)
text:
## Summary

On **2025-07-22** in **Kyiv, Ukraine**, the suspected administrator of **xss.is** (formerly **DaMaGeLaB**) — a 12-year-running Russian-speaking cybercrime forum with **50,000+ registered users** — was arrested in a French-led, Ukrainian SBU-partnered, Europol EC3-coordinated takedown. The forum was a central marketplace for stolen data, hacking tools, malware, compromised-system access, and ransomware-related services. The administrator, who has been active in the cybercrime ecosystem for nearly two decades (allegedly pseudonymous "toha" per third-party reporting), also ran **thesecure.biz**, a private messaging platform tailored to cybercriminals. Through xss.is + thesecure.biz, the suspect is thought to have earned **over EUR 7 million** in advertising and facilitation fees, serving as a trusted-third-party arbiter for criminal disputes and guarantor of transaction security for forum users.

## Background

The forum **xss.is** was active since **2013** (12+ years of operation as of the 2025-07-22 takedown) and operated as a central platform "used to coordinate, advertise and recruit" by some of the most active cybercriminal networks. The Europol release ties xss.is's role explicitly to Europol's **2025 Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment (IOCTA)**, which highlights the booming black market for stolen data as a critical driver of the cybercrime economy: platforms like xss.is enable the trade and monetisation of compromised data, hacking tools, and illicit services that fuel ransomware, fraud, identity theft, and extortion.

## Participating Parties

| Country | Lead Authority |
|---|---|
| France (lead) | Paris Prosecutor (Parquet de Paris — JUNALCO); [[france-national-police\|French Police — Paris Police Prefecture]] (Préfecture de Police de Paris — Brigade de lutte contre la cybercriminalité / BL2C) |
| Ukraine (partner) | General Prosecutor's Office of Ukraine; [[ukraine-sbu\|Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) — Cybercrime Department]] |
| Coordinating | [[europol-ec3\|Europol European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] (virtual command post September 2024; mobile office deployment to Kyiv during action week; analytical and operational support throughout) |

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2013 | xss.is (formerly DaMaGeLaB) founded; ~20-year cybercrime tenure of administrator begins | Europol 2025-07-23 |
| 2021-07 | French Police initiate investigation | Europol 2025-07-23 |
| 2024-09 | Operational phase moves to Ukraine; French police investigators deployed on the ground; Europol establishes a virtual command post | Europol 2025-07-23 |
| 2025-07-22 (action day) | Suspected administrator arrested in Kyiv; coordinated enforcement actions in Kyiv to gather evidence and dismantle infrastructure; Europol mobile office deployed to Kyiv | Europol 2025-07-23 |
| 2025-07-23 | Europol public announcement | Europol 2025-07-23 |

## Results and Impact

- **1 arrest** in Kyiv, Ukraine: suspected forum administrator.
- **xss.is forum disrupted** (50,000+ registered users, 12 years of operation).
- **thesecure.biz private messaging service** attributed to the same administrator (status of takedown not specified in the cited primary release).
- **EUR 7,000,000+** in suspected criminal revenue from advertising and facilitation fees.
- **Domain seizure**: xss.is clearnet domain taken over by French BL2C with SBU Cyber Department assistance (per third-party reporting; the primary release does not enumerate specific seizure-banner text).
- **Underlying disrupted ecosystem**: stolen-data marketplace, hacking-tool sales, malware sales, compromised-system access sales, ransomware-related services trade.

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The cited release describes three discrete IC mechanism classes for this operation:

1. **French-Ukrainian bilateral cybercrime cooperation** with French police investigators deployed on the ground in Ukraine from September 2024 onward.
2. **Europol virtual command po

title: Zambia Golden Top Call Center Bust
text:
## Summary

In **April 2024**, Zambian authorities raided the **Golden Top Support Services** call center in Roma, a suburb of Lusaka, resulting in **77 arrests** — including 22 Chinese nationals (who subsequently pleaded guilty in June 2024), 1 Cameroonian, and 54 Zambian youths aged 20-25 (Source: [[2024-06-05-therecord-zambia-golden-top-guilty-pleas]], [[2024-04-15-bitdefender-zambia-golden-top-arrests]]). The raid was led by Zambia's **Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC)** in coordination with Zambia Police, immigration, and anti-terrorism unit. Seizures included vehicles, two firearms, 97 desktop PCs, 13,000+ SIM cards, 11 SIM boxes, and 6 properties.

> [!warning] Date correction (2026-04-14)
> Earlier version of this page recorded the raid as "early 2025" based on a Tier-3 source. The Tier-2/3 sources verified in this enrichment pass confirm **April 2024** as the actual raid date. Frontmatter and case_id updated accordingly.

> [!note] Source confidence: medium
> No Tier A (agency press release) source was located for this operation — neither INTERPOL nor Europol published on the Golden Top raid. Facts are assessed as *highly likely* correct based on multiple corroborating Tier-2/3 sources (Bitdefender, The Record, Dark Reading, Saudi Gazette), but the operation lacks the legal-document anchor typical for Tier-1 entries.

The case illustrates the growing phenomenon of transnational criminal organizations setting up fraudulent call centers in African countries, often involving workers from Asia.

## Background

Fraudulent call centers have become a major cybercrime-enabled threat, with criminal organizations establishing operations in countries with lower labor costs and potentially less stringent enforcement. Zambia, like several other African countries, has seen an increase in such operations, often involving Chinese-organized crime networks that recruit or traffic workers from China and other Asian countries.

## Participating Parties

### Participating Countries
- [[zambia|Zambia]]
- [[china|China]] (nationality of some suspects)

### Participating Agencies
- Zambia Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC)
- Zambia Police Service
- Zambia Immigration Department
- Zambia Anti-Terrorism Unit

## Legal Framework

Specific legal instruments are not detailed in the public source set. The reported charges and later sentencing were handled in Zambia.

## Operational Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 2024-04 | Golden Top Support Services call center raided in Lusaka, Zambia |
| 2024-04 | 77 arrests announced |
| 2024-06 | 22 Chinese nationals and one Cameroonian sentenced in Zambia |

## Results and Impact

| Metric | Count |
|--------|-------|
| Arrests | 77 |
| Call center disrupted | 1 (Golden Top) |
| Later sentencing | 22 Chinese nationals and one Cameroonian sentenced |
| Seized items | 97 desktop PCs, 13,000+ SIM cards, 11 SIM boxes, vehicles, firearms, and six properties |

## Cooperation Mechanisms Used

The documented mechanism is a domestic Zambian search-and-seizure operation with multi-agency participation. The source set identifies multinational suspects and overseas victims, but it does not prove a formal Chinese law-enforcement role.

## Korean Involvement (한국의 참여)

No Korean involvement identified. However, Korean nationals have been targets and victims of similar call center fraud operations in Southeast Asia.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What law enforcement agencies led the raid?
- How many nationalities were represented among the 77 arrested?
- What types of fraud were conducted from the call center?
- Was there international cooperation with Chinese authorities?
- What was the total financial loss attributed to the operation?

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operatio

title: Zeus/SpyEye Joint Investigation Team Takedown
title_ko: Zeus/SpyEye Joint Investigation Team Takedown (공식 작전명 미확인; alias: Zeus SpyEye JIT)
text:
## Summary

In June 2015, Europol and Eurojust coordinated a Joint Investigation Team action against a Zeus/SpyEye banking-trojan ring. The action days were 18-19 June 2015 in Ukraine, where authorities arrested five suspects, searched eight locations across four Ukrainian cities, and seized computer equipment for forensic analysis.

The official Europol source identifies six JIT member states: Austria, Belgium, Finland, the Netherlands, Norway, and the United Kingdom. Additional cooperation came from Estonia, Latvia, Germany, Moldova, Poland, Ukraine, and the United States. Ukraine was the operational venue even though it is recorded as additional cooperation rather than a JIT member.

## Participating Parties

| Role | Parties |
|---|---|
| Coordination | [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]], [[eurojust|Eurojust]] |
| JIT members | [[austria|Austria]], [[belgium|Belgium]], [[finland|Finland]], [[netherlands|Netherlands]], [[norway|Norway]], [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom]] |
| Additional cooperation and operational venue | [[estonia|Estonia]], [[latvia|Latvia]], [[germany|Germany]], [[moldova|Moldova]], [[poland|Poland]], [[ukraine|Ukraine]], [[united-states|United States]] |

## Results

- **5 arrests** in Ukraine
- **8 searches** across four Ukrainian cities
- Computer equipment seized
- Zeus and SpyEye banking trojan criminal ring dismantled
- Online banking hacking and money laundering network neutralized
- Broader operation linked to 60 arrests, including 34 money-mule arrests

## Follow-Up Actions

> [!warning] No public court documents found
> Web search (2026-04-17) yielded no publicly accessible court filings
> for this operation. Possible reasons: non-US jurisdiction with no
> public court records system, sealed proceedings, or operation did
> not result in formal prosecution.

<!-- SOURCE_ENRICHMENT_START -->

## Source Coverage

- Europol, 2015-06-25: Europol: Major Cybercrime Ring Dismantled by Joint Investigation Team.
- Sophos, 2015-06-30: Zeus and SpyEye crime syndicate taken down by Europol.
- Help Net Security, 2015-06-25: The downfall of a major cybercrime ring exploiting banking Trojans.
- SC Media, 2015-06-25: Europol takes down high profile Ukraine-based cybergang.
- The Hacker News, 2015-06-27: Europol Arrests Gang Behind Zeus and SpyEye Banking Malware.
- Tech Monitor, 2015-06-29: Zeus & SpyEye malware gang members arrested in Ukraine.

## Operational Timeline

- 2015-06-18: activity or investigation start.
- 2015-06-19: public announcement.
- 2015-06-19: reported enforcement endpoint.
- 2015-06-25: public source coverage from Europol, Help Net Security, SC Media.
- 2015-06-27: public source coverage from The Hacker News.
- 2015-06-29: public source coverage from Tech Monitor.
- 2015-06-30: public source coverage from Sophos.

## Legal and Procedural Posture

- Recorded crime classification: malware.
- Recorded enforcement posture: Arrest and Seizure.
- The record is categorized as joint-investigation with status completed.

## Evidence and Attribution Notes

- Europol announced on 25 June 2015 the results of a Joint Investigation Team (JIT) action against a Zeus/SpyEye banking-Trojan network, with the coordinated action executed 18-19 June 2015 in Ukraine
- On the action days: 5 suspects arrested in Ukraine, 8 house searches conducted across 4 Ukrainian cities, and computer equipment and devices seized for forensic analysis
- The JIT comprised 6 core member states: Austria, Belgium, Finland, Netherlands, Norway, and United Kingdom — an unusually small and tight configuration for a cybercrime JIT — with Europol and Eurojust coordination support
- Additional cooperation (non-JIT) came from Estonia, Latvia, Germany, Moldova, Poland, Ukraine, and the United States; Ukraine was the primary operational venue despite not being a JIT party
- Estimated damages: at least EUR 2 million from the investigated cluster; the group engaged in malware development, infection, credential harvesting, and money-mule laundering ac

title: Zeus/SpyEye Crime Syndicate Takedown
text:
## Absorbed Alias Record

This page is retained for search, redirects, and legacy links, but it is **not counted as a separate canonical operation**. The same source set and enforcement facts are represented by [[zeus-spyeye-jit-takedown]], which is the better canonical title because the official Europol source describes the action as a Joint Investigation Team operation.

## Canonical Record

Use [[zeus-spyeye-jit-takedown]] for structured analysis of the June 2015 Zeus/SpyEye banking-trojan action, including participating countries, Europol/Eurojust coordination, arrests, searches, forensic seizures, and the broader money-mule arrest context.

## Why Absorbed

- The source list was identical to [[zeus-spyeye-jit-takedown]].
- The event, dates, target, action-day results, and participating jurisdictions describe the same operation.
- Keeping both as canonical records double-counted one international cooperation action.

## References

title: African Union
text:
## Summary

The **African Union (AU)** is the continental policy organization for African member states. In the cybercrime corpus, its main relevance is not day-to-day police command; it is the regional legal and policy frame that shapes how African states approach cyber security, data protection, electronic transactions, and criminalization of ICT-enabled offences.

The key AU instrument tracked here is the **African Union Convention on Cyber Security and Personal Data Protection**, often discussed as the Malabo Convention. The local source coverage emphasizes how national cybercrime laws can be mapped against AU and Budapest Convention concepts, including illegal access, illegal interception, data and system interference, misuse of devices, child sexual abuse material offences, and procedural rules for digital evidence.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The AU functions as a regional harmonization layer. Its convention and policy work help create common expectations for national legislation, which matters when cross-border investigations require compatible offences, preservation powers, disclosure rules, and mutual legal assistance procedures. That role is especially important in African-facing operations where law-enforcement partners may include national police, [[afripol]], [[interpol]], and UN capacity-building actors rather than an AU operational unit.

## IC Relevance

For IC analysis, the AU should be treated as a **norm-setting and capacity-building context** rather than as a takedown agency. References to African regional cooperation, cybercrime law reform, or data-protection harmonization can be anchored here. Operational pages should link to this page only when they discuss regional policy context or African institutional coordination; direct investigative participation should instead be attributed to the specific national agency or international organization named by the source.

## Source Coverage

Current coverage is based on a legal-policy source summarizing AU cyber-security and personal-data-protection convention themes. More official AU source harvesting would further improve treaty status, institutional ownership, and implementation tracking.

title: AFRIPOL (African Police Cooperation Organization)
text:
## Summary

AFRIPOL is the African Union's continental police cooperation mechanism. In the cybercrime corpus it appears most often with INTERPOL in Africa-wide operations that combine coordination, capacity building, and action against compromised infrastructure or online-fraud networks.

## International Cooperation Role

Africa Cyber Surge II is the clearest source-backed example. INTERPOL and AFRIPOL coordinated activity across 25 African countries, supported by private-sector intelligence and donor-funded capacity-building programs. The record should be read as regional coordination rather than as a single-country investigation. Use AFRIPOL when the source frames an action through the African Union or continental police-cooperation layer; use the national police page when the source identifies a specific executing country.

title: APCERT

## Summary

**APCERT** is the Asia-Pacific CERT cooperation network referenced in the repository through national cyber-security agency profiles. It should be treated as an incident-response coordination forum rather than a law-enforcement arresting body.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

APCERT matters to cybercrime IC because technical incident response often precedes or runs alongside criminal investigation. National CERTs can share indicators, malware samples, incident notifications, and mitigation guidance across borders while police and prosecutors handle legal process. The local KISA/KrCERT source set anchors APCERT through South Korea's national cyber-security ecosystem.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for CERT-to-CERT regional cooperation in the Asia-Pacific. Use [[krcert-cc]], [[kisa]], or a national police page when the source concerns a specific incident response or criminal investigation.

title: Armenian Police — Division for Combating High-Tech Crime
text:
## Summary

The **Division for Combating High-Tech Crime** of the Police of the Republic of Armenia is a centralized cybercrime investigation unit under the **General Department on Combating Organized Crime** [1]. It is Armenia's primary police unit for handling cybercrime cases at the preliminary investigation stage, before cases are transferred to the Investigative Committee for formal investigation [1].

The Division operates with **approximately 8 officers** and country-wide competency [1]. Given its small size, Armenia's cybercrime investigation capacity is *likely* modest in comparative terms, and the Division relies heavily on international cooperation and capacity building from Council of Europe, OSCE and other partners [2][3].

## Mandate and Authority

Under Armenian criminal procedure, the Division handles cybercrime cases in the **preliminary (pre-investigation) stage** — typically a 10-day window during which the police determine the facts and legal basis for investigation, then decide either to [1]:

1. Close the matter
2. Send the case to the local police where evidence or perpetrator is located
3. Transfer the matter to the **Investigative Committee** (Armenia's independent investigative body)

This structure means the Division is primarily an *intake, triage and operational support* unit rather than a full-cycle investigative body.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Parent:** General Department on Combating Organized Crime, RA Police [1]
- **Headcount:** ~8 officers at the Division [1]
- **Territorial presence:** No specialized local units; additional resources drawn from the Organized Crime Department as needed [1]

## IC Capabilities

- **Budapest Convention:** Armenia is a party; the Division *likely* serves as or supports the Armenian Art. 35 24/7 contact point [1][2]
- **Cooperation with leading international technology companies** (direct disclosure requests) [1]
- **OSCE-supported capacity building** — Armenia has received OSCE cybercrime training support [3]
- **Council of Europe capacity building** — participation in GLACY+/CyberSouth-type programs via the Octopus Cybercrime Community [2]

## Key Operations and Cases

Specific operation attributions are not available in the currently ingested sources. Given the Division's size and role, it is *unlikely* to lead major international operations; instead, it is *likely* to participate as a cooperating authority on Europol or Interpol regional initiatives involving Armenia.

Reported casework includes actions against illegal call centres operating from Armenia [source: Arminfo press coverage, not fully ingested].

## Cooperation Track Record

- **Budapest Convention** party — routine Art. 35 cooperation [1]
- **Council of Europe Octopus Cybercrime Community** participant [2]
- **OSCE partnership** for cybercrime capacity building [3]
- **Interpol NCB Yerevan** channel
- **Limited direct Europol cooperation** — Armenia is not an EU member state

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact establishment date of the Division
- Whether the Division is the designated 24/7 Budapest Convention contact point or whether this is held elsewhere in the RA Police or Prosecutor General's Office
- Current Division headcount (the 8-officer figure may be outdated)
- Named international operations with confirmed Armenian Division participation
- Division's relationship with Armenia's National Security Service (NSS) on state-security cyber cases

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | National Police AM — Cybercrime | Council of Europe (Octopus Cybercrime) | n.d. | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/national-police-am |
| [2] | Armenia — Cybercrime country profile | Council of Europe (Octopus) | n.d. | https://www.coe.int/en/web/octopus/-/armenia |
| [3] | OSCE supports Armenia's efforts to combat cybercrime | OSCE Transnational Threats Department | n.d. | https://tntd.osce.org/node/587822 |

title: ASEANAPOL
text:
## Summary

**ASEANAPOL** is the regional police cooperation body for Southeast Asian police agencies. In the cybercrime corpus, its clearest source-backed role is as a regional support layer in INTERPOL's online-scam operations, especially [[operation-first-light-2024|Operation First Light 2024]].

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Operation First Light 2024 source set describes a 61-country INTERPOL-coordinated crackdown on online scams, with 3,950 arrests, 14,643 additional suspects identified, 6,745 bank accounts frozen, and USD 257 million in assets seized. The source explicitly identifies ASEANAPOL as one of the regional policing bodies that bolstered the operation, alongside AFRIPOL, GCCPOL, and Europol.

That role matters because Southeast Asian cyber-enabled fraud often crosses multiple ASEAN jurisdictions before funds or victims appear elsewhere. ASEANAPOL should therefore be understood as a regional coordination and liaison layer rather than as a single national investigative agency.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when an operation describes ASEAN-level police coordination, Southeast Asian online-scam enforcement, or INTERPOL action supported by regional policing bodies. Link the individual national police agency when a source names a specific ASEAN state or arresting authority.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is currently strongest for Operation First Light 2024. The page still needs direct ASEANAPOL institutional material to capture headquarters, membership, and standing cybercrime committees.

title: Australian Federal Police — Cyber Command
text:
## Summary

The Australian Federal Police (AFP) is the primary federal law enforcement agency of [[australia|Australia]] and operates the country's lead cybercrime investigation capability. The AFP established a dedicated **Cyber Command** in January 2022, funded with AUD 89.9 million through Australia's Cyber Security Strategy 2020. Cyber Command leads the AFP's response to cybercrime and operates the Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (JPC3), a purpose-built facility in Sydney that brings together all Australian state and territory police jurisdictions.

As a Five Eyes partner, the AFP is *almost certainly* among the most operationally integrated national cybercrime units with US, UK, Canadian, and New Zealand counterparts. The AFP's predecessor cybercrime unit, the Australian High Tech Crime Centre (AHTCC), was established in July 2003, and in 2008 the AFP launched the High Tech Crimes Operations (HTCO) branch.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** Australian Federal Police — Cyber Command (under the Crime portfolio)
**Status:** *Active* — Cyber Command and JPC3 continue to operate under the same names [5][6]
**Current Cyber Command lead:** Assistant Commissioner Scott Lee (appointed under the 2025 restructure) [6]
**Reports to:** Deputy Commissioner, Crime (responsible for AFP crime and cyber strategy)

As of April 2026 the AFP Cyber Command and the Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (JPC3) remain the lead Australian federal entities for cybercrime. A material restructure at the senior-executive level took place in 2025:

- **2025 AFP senior-executive restructure:** The AFP adopted a new **four–deputy-commissioner structure** in 2025 under Commissioner Reece Kershaw, explicitly "to better respond to complex cybercrime and other tech-enabled criminality" [5][6]. The four deputy commissioners are: Ian McCartney (National Security), Grant Nicholls (Crime — responsible for cyber strategy), Neil Gaughan (ACT Policing), and Lesa Gale (International & Specialist Capabilities).
- **Cyber Command leadership:** Assistant Commissioner **Scott Lee**, formerly Assistant Commissioner for Counter Terrorism and Special Investigations Command, now leads Cyber Command [6].
- **JPC3 continuing operations:** JPC3 in Sydney continues to bring together all Australian state and territory police jurisdictions for high-harm, high-volume cybercrime enforcement [5].
- **Indo-Pacific role:** The AFP *almost certainly* retains its leading regional cybercrime partner role through 2025–2026, with continued cooperation on scam compound enforcement with Thai CCIB, the Philippines, and Indonesia.

The 2025 restructure did **not** rename or dissolve Cyber Command or JPC3; it elevated the cyber portfolio within the AFP senior-executive structure.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2003-07 | **Australian High Tech Crime Centre (AHTCC)** established | [4] |
| 2008 | AHTCC folded into AFP **High Tech Crimes Operations (HTCO)** branch | [4] |
| 2022-01 | **Cyber Command** established with AUD 89.9M funding under Cyber Security Strategy 2020 | [1][3] |
| 2022 | **Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (JPC3)** launched in Sydney | [1][2] |
| 2025 | AFP adopts **four-deputy-commissioner structure**; cyber strategy placed under Deputy Commissioner Crime; Assistant Commissioner Scott Lee appointed to lead Cyber Command | [5][6] |

## Mandate and Authority

The AFP has statutory authority to investigate federal offences including cybercrime under the Crimes Act 1914 (Cth), the Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth), and the Cybercrime Act 2001 (Cth). Cyber Command focuses on:

- Identifying risks to Australian networks and citizens
- Prosecuting cybercriminal perpetrators
- Domestic and international law enforcement cooperation
- Cybercrime prevention and capacity building

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Cyber Command (HQ):** Centralized cybercrime coordination, establis

title: Australian Signals Directorate
text:
## Summary

The **Australian Signals Directorate (ASD)** is Australia's signals-intelligence and cyber-security agency. In this corpus, ASD is primarily relevant as the parent institution for national cyber-security leadership through ASD's Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC), and as a national-security cyber actor that can inform, protect, and disrupt within Australian legal authorities.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

ASD's public cyber-security role is defensive and coordination-heavy. Its official cyber-security page identifies ASD's ACSC as leading the Australian Government's cyber-security efforts and describes services such as cyber threat monitoring, intelligence sharing, alerts, advisories, partnership programs, exercises, and uplift activity.

That makes ASD distinct from police agencies such as [[australia-afp]]. AFP is the operational law-enforcement node for criminal investigations and arrests; ASD is the national cyber-security and intelligence capability that supports resilience, response, and high-end technical context.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for Australian national cyber-security leadership, ACSC functions, and intelligence/cyber capability context. Use [[australia-afp]] for law-enforcement actions, arrests, and criminal investigation participation.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is now adequate for ASD's public mandate and defensive cyber role. Operation-specific ASD involvement should not be inferred unless a future source names ASD directly.

title: Federal Computer Crime Unit (FCCU)
text:
## Summary

The **Federal Computer Crime Unit (FCCU)** is Belgium's specialized national cybercrime police unit within the federal judicial police structure. It sits inside the broader serious-and-organized-crime directorate and provides Belgium's national-level cybercrime investigative capability.

## Mandate and Authority

Belgian Federal Police material places the FCCU within the **Centrale directie van de bestrijding van de zware en georganiseerde criminaliteit (DJSOC)**. In that structure, the FCCU handles computer crime, particularly attacks on critical infrastructure, while also supporting wider serious-crime investigations that require specialized ICT expertise.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- FCCU at federal level for nationally significant cybercrime matters
- placement inside the federal judicial police organized-crime directorate
- support relationship with other national and international partners, including Europol
- support relationship with regional computer-crime capability through the wider federal structure
- complement to Belgium's wider cybersecurity ecosystem, including non-police institutions

## IC Capabilities

- treaty-based cooperation under Belgium's Budapest Convention framework
- federal-level investigative support for serious cybercrime
- multilingual operating environment suited to Belgian and EU cooperation contexts
- operational interface with Europol and other international partners through the federal police structure
- public-facing contact function in ransomware or urgent cyber incidents through official Belgian police channels

## Key Operations and Cases

Public attribution in open sources is relatively sparse, but Belgium appears in multinational Europol-linked cyber operations and is institutionally well-placed to contribute through the FCCU and the federal judicial police system.

The 2017 official **WannaCry** notice is useful because it is not abstract institutional prose. It shows the FCCU acting as the named federal cybercrime unit for victim reporting and operational follow-up. A later Belgian police guidance page also directs ransomware victims to ensure the FCCU is informed and provides a direct FCCU phone contact.

## Cooperation Track Record

Belgium is *likely* a structurally strong cooperation partner because of its EU institutional geography, multilingual environment, and integration into Europol-, Eurojust-, and Budapest-Convention channels. The FCCU's placement in DJSOC suggests that Belgium treats cybercrime as part of its serious-and-organized-crime architecture rather than as a narrow technical specialty only.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- The exact present-day Article 35 directory entry for Belgium is still not public, so the FCCU's precise formal 24/7 designation still depends partly on the Council of Europe profile.
- Open-source detail on FCCU staffing and case volume remains limited.

## References

title: GDBOP Cybercrime Directorate
text:
## Summary

The **Cybercrime Directorate** of Bulgaria's **General Directorate Combating Organized Crime (GDBOP / GDCOC — Главна дирекция "Борба с организираната престъпност")** is the primary Bulgarian law enforcement unit responsible for investigating high-tech crime. It sits within the Ministry of Interior and hosts Bulgaria's **24/7 National Contact Point for high-tech crimes** under the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] [1][2].

As of 2023, the Cybercrime Unit was undergoing restructuring to become a separate directorate within the Ministry of Interior, reflecting the growing prioritization of cybercrime in Bulgarian law enforcement [2]. The unit also operates the **cybercrime.bg** public reporting and information platform [1].

## Mandate and Authority

The Cybercrime Directorate handles investigation of cyber-dependent and cyber-enabled crime, including [1][2]:

- Hacking and unauthorized access offenses
- Online fraud and financial cybercrime
- Online child sexual exploitation material (CSEM) — a dedicated specialized sector
- AI-assisted fraud, fake profiles, illegal gambling (areas of recent growth)

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- Cybercrime Directorate within GDBOP (transitioning to standalone directorate)
- Specialized sector for combating online CSEM
- 24/7 National Contact Point
- cybercrime.bg platform — public reporting and awareness

## IC Capabilities

- Europol partnership via the Bulgarian Europol National Unit
- INTERPOL cooperation via NCB Sofia
- Budapest Convention 24/7 network participation

## Key Operations and Cases

No major Europol-attributed operations are publicly linked to GDBOP at the level of the sources reviewed. Bulgaria *likely* participates in multi-state Europol operations through GDBOP, but attribution in public press releases is often generic ("with support of Bulgarian authorities").

## Cooperation Track Record

Bulgaria has been a party to the Budapest Convention since 2005 and joined the EU (and Europol) in 2007. The GDBOP cybercrime unit is *likely* the primary interlocutor for cross-border cybercrime cases, though detailed statistics on volume and turnaround are not published.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Has the cybercrime unit been formally elevated to a standalone directorate (post-2023 restructuring)?
- What is the current staffing and technical capacity?
- What specific international operations has GDBOP contributed to?

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | General Directorate Combating Organized Crime, Ministry of Interior (Bulgaria) | Netlaw.bg | n.d. | https://www.netlaw.bg/en/a/general-directorate-combating-organized-crime-ministry-of-interior-bulgaria |
| [2] | Cybercrime Unit at Directorate for Combating Organised Crime Is Restructured | BTA | 2023 | https://www.bta.bg/en/news/bulgaria/406674-cybercrime-unit-at-directorate-for-combating-organised-crime-is-restructured-hi |
| [3] | [[2025-12-12_bta-bg_sofia-call-centre-bulgaria-spain-guardia-civil-investment-fraud\|BTA Sofia call-centre BG-ES Guardia Civil online investment fraud takedown — 17 gold bars, 5 hardware crypto wallets (one >USD 230K), BGN 45K cash]] | BTA (Bulgarian News Agency, state wire) relaying Sofia City Prosecutor's Office | 2025-12-12 | https://www.bta.bg/en/news/bulgaria/990290-organized-crime-group-running-fraudulent-online-investment-platforms-busted-i |

title: Cybercrime Programme Office (C-PROC)
text:
## Summary

The **Cybercrime Programme Office (C-PROC)** is the capacity-building arm of the Council of Europe dedicated to cybercrime and electronic evidence. Based in **Bucharest, Romania**, it became operational on **7 April 2014** following a Memorandum of Understanding between the Council of Europe and the Government of Romania signed in October 2013. C-PROC marked its 10th anniversary in 2024.

C-PROC is *almost certainly* the most active dedicated capacity-building actor supporting implementation of the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]]. Since 2014, it has supported over 2,000 activities for more than 130 countries worldwide, making it the principal delivery channel for the Council of Europe's global cybercrime cooperation agenda.

## Mandate and Authority

C-PROC operates under the authority of the Council of Europe's Directorate General of Human Rights and Rule of Law (DGI). Its mandate is to **assist countries worldwide in strengthening their legal and institutional capacity** to respond to the challenges of cybercrime and electronic evidence, using the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] as the normative framework.

Key functions:
- Design and delivery of multi-year capacity building projects jointly funded by the EU and the Council of Europe
- Legislative assistance to countries seeking to align domestic law with the Budapest Convention
- Training of judges, prosecutors, investigators, and 24/7 Network points of contact
- Support to the [[budapest-convention|Cybercrime Convention Committee (T-CY)]] and its working groups
- Technical guidance on digital forensics, MLA drafting, and provider cooperation

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

C-PROC is organized around **joint projects** rather than a rigid hierarchical structure. Major programmes include:

| Programme | Scope | Funding |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| **GLACY+** (Global Action on Cybercrime Extended) | Global capacity building, ~15 priority countries | EU + CoE |
| **GLACY-e** | Successor programme, 35+ countries (2024-) | EU + CoE |
| **iPROCEEDS-2** | South-Eastern Europe and Turkey — focus on financial cybercrime | EU + CoE |
| **CyberEast+** | Eastern Partnership countries (Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Georgia, Moldova, Ukraine) | EU + CoE |
| **CyberSouth+** | Southern Neighbourhood (Algeria, Jordan, Lebanon, Morocco, Tunisia) | EU + CoE |
| **Octopus Project** | Global coordination and Octopus Conference series | CoE core |

## IC Capabilities

- **Standard-setting support:** Drafting templates, guidance notes, and model legislation
- **Peer-to-peer exchanges:** Facilitating exchanges between practitioners across jurisdictions
- **24/7 Network support:** Training and evaluation of national contact points under Art. 35 of the Budapest Convention
- **MLA streamlining:** Country-level assistance on central authority efficiency
- **Second Additional Protocol promotion:** Awareness raising and legal gap analysis for parties considering ratification of [[second-additional-protocol|CETS 224]]

## Key Operations and Cases

C-PROC is **not an operational agency** and does not conduct investigations. Its impact is measured through:
- Number of countries receiving sustained assistance (130+)
- Number of activities delivered (2,000+ since 2014)
- Legislative reforms achieved in partner countries
- Budapest Convention accessions supported (notably supporting the accession processes of states across Africa, Latin America, and Asia-Pacific)

## Cooperation Track Record

C-PROC maintains active working relationships with:
- **[[europol-ec3|Europol / EC3]]** — capacity-building synergies, especially for EU neighborhood
- **[[interpol-igci|INTERPOL / IGCI]]** — joint training activities
- **[[unodc|UNODC]]** — coordination on cybercrime capacity building (sometimes with tension over normative leadership)
- **OSCE** — confidence-building measures on cybersecurity
- **Private sector** — provider engagement in the T-CY context

title: Cambodian National Police
text:
> [!info] Stub
> This page was auto-created during ingestion of the 2025-11-10 Korea–Cambodia Joint Task Force for Korean nationals (코리아전담반) MOU signing, in which the Cambodian National Police is named as the Cambodian counterpart agency. It will be expanded as additional Cambodian-side sources are ingested.

## Summary

The Cambodian National Police is the national police force of the Kingdom of Cambodia, under the Ministry of Interior. In the public IC corpus the agency is documented as the Cambodian counterpart for the bilateral 한국-캄보디아 공동 전담반 (Korea–Cambodia Joint Task Force for Korean nationals, "코리아전담반") MOU signed in Phnom Penh on 2025-11-10, and as the operational counterpart for the joint Korean-Cambodian field unit that produced the 42-person Cambodian tranche of the 2026-04-27 Korea-Cambodia-Philippines extradition cycle.

## Cooperation Track Record (Public Sources)

- **2025-11-10**: Cambodian Police Chief Sar Thet 써 텟 signs the Korea–Cambodia Joint Task Force MOU with KNPA Acting Commissioner Yoo Jae-sung in Phnom Penh, in the presence of Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun and Cambodian Interior Minister Sar Sokha. (See [[korea-cambodia-joint-task-force-mou-2025]].)
- **2025-11 to early 2026**: Cambodian National Police participates in 코리아전담반 (Joint Task Force) staffed by 12 Cambodian + 7 Korean officers (19 total per Korean tier-2 reporting); 7 rounds of joint operations through early January 2026 with 135 suspects arrested in the field and 3 Korean victims rescued.
- **2026-02 to 2026-04**: Cooperates with KNPA on the [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|73-person Korean scam-suspect extradition cohort]], with 42 Korean nationals extradited from Cambodia (41 remanded into custody after arrival in Korea).

## Open Questions

- Specific Cambodian National Police division responsible for the cybercrime / Korean-fugitive cooperation line is not enumerated in the cited tier-1 sources beyond the Phnom Penh shared-office context.

title: RCMP — National Cybercrime Coordination Centre (NC3)
text:
## Summary

The **National Cybercrime Coordination Centre (NC3)** is the [[canada|Canadian]] federal government's cybercrime coordination hub, operated by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) under Federal Policing. NC3 reached initial operating capability on **April 1, 2020** and was funded through Budget 2018 with CAD 137.5 million over five years plus CAD 23 million ongoing, as part of Canada's National Cyber Security Strategy.

NC3 operates jointly with the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre (CAFC) to provide a unified public reporting mechanism for cybercrime and fraud. As a Five Eyes partner agency, the RCMP NC3 is *likely* deeply integrated with [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], [[uk-nca|UK NCA]], [[australia-afp|AFP]], and New Zealand Police operational channels.

## Mandate and Authority

Per the Government of Canada's National Cyber Security Strategy, the NC3 is mandated to:

1. **Coordinate** Canadian cybercrime investigations and collaborate with international partners
2. **Advise** Canadian police on digital investigative techniques
3. **Produce** actionable cybercrime intelligence for Canadian policing
4. **Report** — establish a national public reporting mechanism for Canadians and businesses

NC3 coordinates and deconflicts intelligence for cybercrime investigations across all levels of policing (federal, provincial, municipal) and enables efficient operations by national and international partners.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Multi-disciplinary team:** Police officers and civilians from various backgrounds (technical, intelligence, investigative)
- **Joint operation with CAFC:** National Cybercrime and Fraud Reporting System (NCFRS) is operated jointly
- **Federal Policing alignment:** NC3 sits within RCMP Federal Policing, which handles organized crime, national security, and international cooperation

## IC Capabilities

- **Five Eyes integration:** Direct operational channels with US, UK, Australian, and New Zealand cybercrime units
- **Europol liaison:** Cooperation with [[europol-ec3]]
- **INTERPOL coordination:** Through RCMP as Canada's NCB
- **Public reporting:** NCFRS enables intake of victim reports for triage and referral

## Key Operations and Cases

> [!info] Specific NC3-participated operations should be added as ingested from sources.

## Cooperation Track Record

NC3's cooperation effectiveness is *likely* high given the Five Eyes operational model, though as a relatively new unit (2020) its track record is still developing. The unit's dual role as coordinator (not direct investigator) creates a distinctive model compared to operationally-led cyber units like FBI Cyber Division.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- What is the division of labor between NC3 and RCMP Federal Policing cybercrime investigators?
- How effective has NCFRS public reporting been since its rollout?
- Does NC3 have Cyber Action Team-equivalent deployable capacity?

title: China MPS Cyber Police
text:
## Summary

**China MPS Cyber Police** is the corpus entry for cybercrime and cyber-policing functions associated with China's [[china-mps|Ministry of Public Security]]. It is not yet a fully sourced bureau-level institutional profile; current coverage is operation-led and should be read through the source context of each case.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Chinese MPS-linked cyber policing appears in two different patterns. First, it appears in cross-border anti-fraud enforcement, especially the Myanmar/Kokang telecom-scam crackdown and Korea-China voice-phishing cooperation. Second, adversarial cyber sources such as the i-Soon/APT27 indictment name MPS-linked officers in alleged state-directed or contractor-enabled intrusion activity. Those two patterns should not be merged analytically: one is criminal-enforcement cooperation, the other is attribution and prosecution of alleged state-linked cyber activity.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source specifically refers to Chinese cyber police or cyber functions under MPS. Use [[china-mps]] for the broader national police ministry. When the source is adversarial, preserve attribution language carefully and do not describe it as cooperative law-enforcement activity.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for operation context but weak for exact bureau hierarchy. Future enrichment should add direct MPS institutional pages if they are harvested.

title: Council of Europe
text:
## Summary

The **Council of Europe** is the institutional home of the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention on Cybercrime]] and a central non-police actor in cybercrime international cooperation. Its relevance to this corpus is treaty architecture: harmonized criminal offences, investigative powers, expedited preservation, production and search powers, and cross-border cooperation channels that make cybercrime investigations legally interoperable.

The corpus has three local anchors for this role: the Council of Europe's overview of the Budapest Convention, the treaty-signature/status chart for Convention 185, and the Council of Europe Cybercrime Programme Office ([[c-proc|C-PROC]]) page. Together they support the Council of Europe as a treaty administrator, status-record source, and capacity-building coordinator.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Council of Europe does not operate as an arresting agency. It supplies the legal and cooperation framework used by states, prosecutors, and police units when cybercrime cases cross borders. The Budapest Convention is particularly important because many non-European states participate in the convention system, making it a global cooperation baseline rather than a purely regional European instrument.

C-PROC extends that framework through practical capacity building. It supports countries implementing cybercrime legislation, electronic-evidence procedures, and institutional cooperation. In operational analysis, this means the Council of Europe often explains **why** two jurisdictions can cooperate even when the visible enforcement action is carried out by national police, prosecutors, [[europol-ec3]], [[interpol]], or [[eurojust]].

## IC Relevance

Use this organization page when a case depends on treaty status, 24/7 contact-point cooperation, capacity building, or comparisons between the Budapest Convention and other cybercrime-law frameworks. Do not attribute arrests, infrastructure seizures, or direct operational command to the Council of Europe unless a specific source says so.

## Source Coverage

The current source set is strong for treaty role and programme-office context, but less complete for committee governance, protocol development, and historical convention drafting. Future enrichment should add direct pages for the Cybercrime Convention Committee and additional protocol material where available in the local corpus.

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [[2026-04-17_coe-int_about-the-convention-budapest-convention|About the Convention - Budapest Convention]] | Council of Europe | 2026-04-17 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/the-budapest-convention |
| 2 | [[2026-04-17_coe-int_treaty-185-signatures-chart|Treaty 185 signatures chart]] | Council of Europe | 2026-04-17 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/conventions/full-list?module=signatures-by-treaty&treatynum=185 |
| 3 | [[2026-04-17_coe-int_cybercrime-programme-office-c-proc|Cybercrime Programme Office (C-PROC)]] | Council of Europe | 2026-04-17 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/cybercrime-office-c-proc- |

title: National Centre Against Terrorism, Extremism and Cybercrime (NCTEKK)
text:
## Summary

The **National Centre Against Terrorism, Extremism and Cybercrime (NCTEKK — Národní centrála proti terorismu, extremismu a kybernetické kriminalitě)** is a national-competence unit of the **Police of the Czech Republic** that consolidates Czech law enforcement's response to terrorism, extremism, and cybercrime. It was created on **January 1, 2023**, by extracting the terrorism, extremism, and cybercrime sections from the broader **National Centre Against Organized Crime (NCOZ — Národní centrála proti organizovanému zločinu)** [1][2].

Before the 2023 separation, cybercrime was handled by NCOZ, which itself was established on **August 1, 2016** through the merger of the Organized Crime Investigation Unit and the Anti-Corruption and Financial Crime Investigation Unit [1]. The Czech Republic has been a party to the Budapest Convention since 2013.

## Mandate and Authority

NCTEKK and its NCOZ predecessor operate under the Police of the Czech Republic (Policie České republiky), with nationwide jurisdiction. The cybercrime section [1][2]:

- Investigates serious cyber-dependent and cyber-enabled crime
- Handles cases with international dimensions
- Coordinates with Europol and INTERPOL on Czech cybercrime cases
- Serves as the likely host of the Budapest Convention 24/7 contact point for the Czech Republic

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- Cybercrime section (formerly within NCOZ, moved to NCTEKK in 2023)
- Terrorism and extremism sections
- Support for regional Czech Police cybercrime capacity

## IC Capabilities

- Europol partnership via the Czech Europol National Unit
- INTERPOL cooperation via NCB Prague
- Participation in EU-wide cybercrime operations

## Key Operations and Cases

No specific major operations are publicly attributed to NCTEKK at the level of sources reviewed. The Czech Republic *likely* participates in Europol-coordinated operations, with attribution usually consolidated under Europol press releases.

## Cooperation Track Record

The Czech Republic is a party to the Budapest Convention (since 2013) and a member of Europol. NCTEKK (and previously NCOZ) is *likely* the Czech primary interlocutor for cross-border cybercrime investigations.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Which unit currently hosts the Czech Budapest Convention 24/7 contact point — NCTEKK or the Czech Police central unit?
- What is the staffing of NCTEKK's cybercrime section?
- What operations has NCTEKK led or supported since its 2023 creation?

title: Drug Enforcement Administration
text:
## Summary

The **Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA)** is a recurring US federal partner in cybercrime cases where online infrastructure enables narcotics trafficking. Its role is clearest in darknet marketplace, counterfeit-pill, fentanyl, and crypto-mediated drug distribution investigations.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

DEA was a US co-lead in the AlphaBay component of [[alphabay-takedown]], where the narcotics marketplace dimension made drug-enforcement expertise central. DEA also appears through [[operation-dark-huntor]], a JCODE-linked international action that followed DarkMarket intelligence and produced arrests across multiple countries.

The Dream Market and Nemesis records show DEA's follow-on and partner roles in administrator or marketplace enforcement. [[operation-us-v-vallerius-dream-market]] is a prosecution of a senior marketplace administrator, while [[nemesis-market-takedown]] connects German infrastructure seizure with later US enforcement against the alleged marketplace administrator.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when the online crime problem is narcotics-centered and DEA is named as lead or partner. Use [[fbi-cyber-division]] for intrusion, botnet, and cyber-forensic lead roles; use [[us-postal-inspection]] where parcel logistics and mailed contraband are central.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for DEA's darknet marketplace and narcotics role. A direct current DEA cyber/darknet program overview would further improve institutional detail beyond operation examples.

title: National Cyber Crime Center (NC3) / National Special Crime Unit (NSK)
text:
## Summary

The **National Cyber Crime Center (NC3 — Nationalt Cyber Crime Center)** is Denmark's specialized cybercrime center, embedded within the **National Special Crime Unit (NSK — National enhed for Særlig Kriminalitet)**. NSK was created on **January 1, 2022** by merging several specialized Danish police units, including the previous standalone NC3 and the Nationwide Center for IT-related Economic Crime [1][2].

NC3 has the overall Danish police responsibility for supporting complex IT-related crime investigations — hacking, ransomware, DDoS attacks — and also handles a wide range of internet-enabled sexual crime cases (child sexual abuse material, grooming, sextortion). The NSK as a whole handles Denmark's most complex and serious cases of financial crime, organized crime, and cybercrime [1][2].

## Mandate and Authority

NC3 and NSK operate under the **Danish National Police (Rigspolitiet)** and the Danish Prosecution Service. Key functions [1][2]:

- Supporting all Danish police districts in complex cyber investigations
- Operating specialized investigative capacity for cyber-dependent crime
- Handling IT-related sexual crimes against children
- Coordinating international cybercrime requests

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

NSK consolidated the following units into one organization on 2022-01-01 [2]:

- Nationalt Cyber Crime Center (NC3) — now a center within NSK
- Special Investigation East and West
- Nationwide Center for IT-related Economic Crime
- National Forensic Science Center
- Parts of the Public Prosecutor for Economic and International Crime

## IC Capabilities

- Europol partnership via the Danish Europol National Unit
- INTERPOL cooperation via NCB Copenhagen
- Digital forensics laboratory capacity

## Key Operations and Cases

No specific major operations are publicly attributed to NC3 at the level of sources reviewed. Denmark *likely* participates in Europol-coordinated operations through NC3/NSK.

## Cooperation Track Record

Denmark is a party to the Budapest Convention (since 2005) and a member of Europol. NC3 within NSK is *likely* the primary Danish cybercrime contact for international cooperation.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Which specific Danish unit currently holds the Budapest Convention 24/7 contact role — NC3 within NSK, or a separate office?
- What is the current staffing of NC3 post-NSK merger?
- What bilateral relationships does NC3 prioritize?

## Summary

Dubai Police is the lead law-enforcement agency for the Emirate of Dubai under the United Arab Emirates Ministry of Interior. In the public IC corpus the agency is documented for its lead role in the 2026 multinational takedown of scam centers used for cryptocurrency-investment fraud, including the apprehension in Dubai of three defendants charged in the US Southern District of California.

According to the US DOJ, Dubai Police arrested 275 of at least 276 individuals dismantled across at least nine scam centers; cooperation included the FBI, Chinese Ministry of Public Security, and Royal Thai Police. title: Eurojust
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. Eurojust remains headquartered in **The Hague, Netherlands**, co-located with [[europol-ec3|Europol]], operating under **EU Regulation 2018/1727** (the Eurojust Regulation). No structural reorganization since the 2018 Regulation [6].

**President:** **Michael Schmid** (National Member for Austria), elected in 2025 and took office on **15 November 2025** for a four-year term. He succeeded **Ladislav Hamran**, who completed his second and final term as President (first elected 2017, re-elected 2021) [7][8].

**Structure:** The Eurojust College (27 National Members, one per EU member state) remains the decision-making body. Coordination Centres are activated for major cross-border operations. National Members continue to hold the authority to transmit MLA requests and authorize Joint Investigation Team (JIT) establishment on behalf of their national authorities.

**Recent mandate developments:** The European Commission has proposed amendments to the Eurojust Regulation to strengthen digital cooperation and the handling of core international crimes (e.g., war-crimes evidence related to Ukraine). Eurojust has also received an expanded mandate to negotiate **cooperation agreements with third countries** through the European Commission [9].

**Recent priorities (2024–2026):** Judicial coordination for the Operation Cronos (LockBit), Operation Endgame (Phase 1 & 2), and Phobos/8Base cybercrime takedowns; continued JIT support; expanded third-country cooperation (notably Ukraine on war-crimes and with potential new agreements under negotiation).

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> Eurojust's institutional structure is unchanged. The material change since this page was created is the **presidency transition from Hamran to Schmid in November 2025**. The operational relationships with [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]], national prosecution authorities, and third-country partners are unchanged.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2002-02-28 | **Eurojust established** by Council Decision 2002/187/JHA | — |
| 2018-12-14 | **EU Regulation 2018/1727** ("Eurojust Regulation") enters into force, replacing the 2002 Council Decision and significantly strengthening operational powers | [6] |
| 2017 / 2021 | **Ladislav Hamran** (SK) elected, then re-elected, as President for two consecutive 4-year terms | [7] |
| 2022+ | Eurojust receives expanded mandate and resources for core international crimes (Ukraine war-crimes evidence) | [9] |
| 2025-11-15 | **Michael Schmid** (AT) takes office as new President of Eurojust for a 4-year term, replacing Hamran who completed his second (final) term | [7][8] |

## Summary

Eurojust (유로저스트) is the European Union Agency for Criminal Justice Cooperation, headquartered in The Hague alongside [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]]. Established in 2002, Eurojust provides the **judicial coordination** dimension of cross-border criminal investigations. While [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]] provides operational and analytical support (the police side), Eurojust handles the prosecutorial and judicial side: ensuring that evidence is admissible, mutual legal assistance requests are properly channeled, Joint Investigation Teams have legal foundations, and European Arrest Warrants are coordinated across jurisdictions.

Eurojust is *almost certainly* indispensable to major EU cybercrime operations. It provided judicial coordination for all 5 operations covered by currently ingested sources, hosting coordination meetings, facilitating JITs, and ensuring that the multi-jurisdictional legal requirements were met for arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing.

## Mandate and Authority

Eurojust's current mandate is governed by **EU Regulation 2018/1727** (the Eurojust Regulation), which replaced the original 2002 Council Decision and significantly strengthened the agency's powers and operational capacity. Key aspects of the mandate include:

- **C

title: Europol European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. EC3 remains Europol's dedicated cybercrime centre, headquartered at Europol HQ in **The Hague, Netherlands**, operating under Europol's legal mandate (**EU Regulation 2016/794**). No structural reorganization has been publicly reported since the 2016 Regulation [8][9].

**Parent and structure:** EC3 remains a unit inside **Europol** (not a standalone agency). Europol's overall Executive Director is **Catherine De Bolle** (since May 2018, reappointed 2022; four-year term still running). EC3's internal three-pillar structure (operations / forensics and technical expertise / strategy and development) is unchanged [8].

**J-CAT (Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce):** Permanent team of liaison officers from EU member states, Five Eyes partners, Japan, Korea, and other non-EU partners, co-located at EC3. Continues to be the primary mechanism for real-time operational intelligence sharing on major cybercrime targets.

**Recent priorities (2024–2026):** Ransomware-as-a-Service (continued Operation Cronos / LockBit / Phobos / 8Base follow-up, Operation Endgame Phase 2 in May 2025), dropper/loader malware ecosystem disruption, cryptocurrency mixer investigations, CSAM takedowns (Operation Stream / KidFlix, April 2025), AI-enabled fraud. Annual **Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment (IOCTA)** continues to be published.

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> EC3 is *almost certainly* the most operationally active international cybercrime coordination body; no structural reorganization is reflected in Europol's public communications as of 2026-04-10 [8][9]. The page text below remains current.

## Summary

The European Cybercrime Centre (EC3) is Europol's dedicated unit for combating cybercrime across the EU. Established in January 2013, it serves as the **central coordination hub** for major EU cybercrime operations, providing analytical, forensic, and operational support to member states' investigations.

EC3 is *almost certainly* the most operationally active international coordination body for cybercrime takedowns, having supported or coordinated landmark operations including:
- **[[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos Phase 1]]** (Feb 2024): Disrupted LockBit ransomware group; 34 servers seized, 2 arrests, decryption keys released
- **[[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame Phase 1]]** (May 2024): Largest-ever botnet takedown (IcedID, Smokeloader, Pikabot, Bumblebee, Trickbot); 4 arrests, 100+ servers taken down, 2,000+ domains seized
- **[[operation-cronos-phase3|Operation Cronos Phase 3]]** (Oct 2024): 4 new LockBit arrests, financial sanctions against Evil Corp affiliates
- **[[phobos-8base-crackdown|Phobos/8Base Crackdown]]** (Feb 2025): 4 arrests of 8Base leadership, 27 servers taken down; includes South Korea arrest and extradition
- **[[operation-endgame-phase2|Operation Endgame Phase 2]]** (May 2025): 300 servers taken down, 650 domains neutralized, EUR 3.5M seized, 20 arrest warrants issued
- **[[emotet-takedown|Emotet takedown (Operation LadyBird)]]** (Jan 2021): Disrupted Emotet botnet infrastructure; ~700 C2 servers seized across 8 countries; uninstall module deployed to 1M+ infected machines

## Mandate and Authority

EC3 operates under Europol's mandate (EU Regulation 2016/794). It does not have independent investigative powers but supports and coordinates EU member state operations. Key functions:

- **Operational coordination:** Hosting operational meetings, coordination centers during action days
- **Joint Investigation Teams:** Providing logistical, analytical, and financial support for JITs
- **Analysis:** Maintaining databases, cross-matching intelligence across investigations
- **Forensics:** EC3's digital forensics lab supports member states with technical analysis
- **IOCTA:** Annual Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment

## IC Capabilities

- Operational analysis and cross-matching of data from multiple investigations
- Support for [[e

title: FBI Cyber Division
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active — no structural reorganization; leadership changed in June 2025.

**Assistant Director (Cyber Division):** **Brett Leatherman**, a 20+ year FBI career official, was appointed in June 2025 under FBI Director Kash Patel, succeeding **Bryan A. Vorndran** (Cyber Division AD 2021–June 2025). Vorndran departed federal service for a position at Microsoft. Leatherman was previously the Deputy Assistant Director for Cyber Operations [9][10].

**Deputy Assistant Director (Cyber Operations):** **Jason Bilnoski** (2025–present) [11].

**Structure (unchanged):** Cyber Division sits in the Criminal, Cyber, Response, and Services Branch of the FBI. 56 field offices with cyber squads; **63+ Legal Attache (Legat) offices** worldwide; Cyber Action Teams; Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3); J-CAT participation at Europol EC3.

**Recent priorities (2025–2026):** ransomware disruption (continued BlackSuit/Royal, Phobos, LockBit follow-up), state-sponsored intrusion attribution (China/iSoon, DPRK Lazarus), AI-enabled fraud. In late 2025 the FBI ran **Operation Winter Shield**, an AI-defense-themed campaign, reflecting the Cyber Division's growing AI focus [12].

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> The FBI Cyber Division has **not** undergone any material structural reorganization as of 2026-04-10. The only change is leadership transition from Vorndran to Leatherman in June 2025.

## Summary

The FBI Cyber Division (사이버과) is the primary investigative arm of the [[united-states|United States]] federal government for cyber threats. Established in 2002 in the wake of the September 11 attacks and growing awareness of cyber vulnerabilities, the division coordinates and conducts domestic and international investigations targeting state-sponsored intrusions, ransomware groups, major cybercriminal networks, and online fraud schemes.

The Cyber Division is *almost certainly* the single most consequential national agency in international cybercrime cooperation. Its global reach through 63+ Legal Attache (Legat) offices, its operation of the [[ic3|Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3)]], and its Cyber Action Teams provide unmatched capacity for rapid cross-border incident response. The FBI participated in all 8 major operations covered by currently ingested sources (5 Europol-coordinated + 3 DOJ-led), underscoring its central role in both Europol-coordinated and US-led multilateral cybercrime takedowns.

## Mandate and Authority

The FBI Cyber Division derives its authority from multiple federal statutes, including the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA, 18 U.S.C. 1030), wire fraud statutes, and various cybercrime-related provisions. The division's mandate encompasses:

- **State-sponsored cyber intrusions:** Investigating nation-state actors targeting US government, critical infrastructure, and private sector networks.
- **Ransomware:** Disrupting ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) ecosystems, identifying operators and affiliates, and seizing criminal infrastructure.
- **Online fraud:** Investigating business email compromise (BEC), romance scams, investment fraud, and other internet-enabled financial crimes.
- **Identity theft and cybercriminal networks:** Targeting credential theft, dark web marketplaces, and organized cybercriminal groups.

The FBI has extraterritorial jurisdiction over cyber offenses that affect US persons, entities, or infrastructure, which provides the legal basis for its extensive international cooperation activities.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

The Cyber Division sits within the FBI's National Security Branch and Criminal, Cyber, Response, and Services Branch structure. Key components relevant to international cooperation include:

- **Cyber Division HQ (Washington, D.C.):** Centralized coordination of national-level investigations, intelligence, and international partnerships.
- **56 Field Offices:** Each has a cyber squad or task force handling regional cybercrim

text:
## Summary

FBI is retained as the shorthand organization page for records that name the Federal Bureau of Investigation without specifying a subunit. The more detailed cybercrime unit page remains [[fbi-cyber-division]], but this page now carries enough source-backed metadata for menu navigation and legacy backlinks.

## International Cooperation Role

The FBI appears across DDoS, malware, darknet, botnet, and fraud records as a U.S. investigative agency with both domestic field-office capacity and international liaison reach. The source set links it to Operation Shrouded Horizon, DDoS-for-hire enforcement, and victim guidance after the 911 S5 disruption. Use this page where the record names FBI generally; use a more specific page when the source identifies a field office, Cyber Division, or task-force component.

It will be expanded as additional NBI-related sources are ingested.

## Summary

The Finnish National Bureau of Investigation (Keskusrikospoliisi, KRP; English: National Bureau of Investigation, NBI) is Finland's national criminal investigation agency under the Ministry of the Interior. In the public IC corpus the agency is documented for its 2025-12 cooperation with US DOJ EDMI and the German BKA in the E-Note crypto-exchange takedown.

## Cooperation Track Record (Public Sources)

- **2025-12**: Named cooperating partner in the [[e-note-crypto-exchange-takedown-2025|E-Note crypto-exchange takedown]] coordinated by US DOJ EDMI (FBI Detroit) with the German Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA).

## Open Questions

- Specific NBI division responsible for the cybercrime / money-laundering investigation line is not enumerated in the cited tier-1 source; further NBI-side sources are needed.
- The legal basis for Finnish-side cooperation (whether MLAT-based, EU EIO-based, or informal) is not described in the cited US source.

title: FIRST Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams
text:
## Summary

**FIRST** is the global Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams. In this repository it is used as context for CERT/CSIRT cooperation, not as a police or prosecutor organization.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

FIRST-style cooperation supports the technical side of cybercrime response: incident notification, trusted-team relationships, vulnerability and malware handling, and operational trust between national and sectoral response teams. That technical coordination can preserve evidence, contain infrastructure, and support later law-enforcement requests, but FIRST itself should not be described as an arresting or prosecuting body.

## IC Relevance

Use this page where a national cyber-security agency profile discusses global incident-response community membership. Use the national CERT, police, or prosecutor page for incident-specific or enforcement-specific action.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is indirect through KISA and Singapore CSA-related sources. Direct FIRST institutional harvesting would improve precision.
