title: France Gendarmerie Nationale
text:
## Summary

The **Gendarmerie nationale** is one of France's two major national law-enforcement institutions. In the cybercrime corpus it is the parent institution for [[france-gendarmerie|C3N]] and the broader Gendarmerie cyber architecture, including COMCyberGEND and the regional investigative network.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Official Gendarmerie source material describes a cyber structure that centralizes and coordinates the institution's cyber capabilities while keeping specialist investigators close to field cases. C3N handles high-end judicial cybercrime work, including dark-web, cryptocurrency-enabled, and attacks-on-systems investigations, while the wider Gendarmerie network supports territorial and regional case development.

The Gendarmerie appears in major multinational operations through that cyber-investigative function. The repository tracks it in ransomware, malware infrastructure, encrypted communications, and cyber-enabled fraud cases, often alongside [[france-national-police]], [[france-junalco]], [[europol-ec3]], and partner national agencies.

## IC Relevance

Use this parent page for institutional hierarchy, Gendarmerie-wide cyber posture, and cases where a source names the Gendarmerie broadly. Use [[france-gendarmerie|C3N]] when the source is specifically about the national cybercrime unit.

## Source Coverage

The source set is strong for the Gendarmerie's cyber structure and strategic framing. Operation pages supply the international-cooperation examples; this parent page should not duplicate all C3N detail.

title: Centre de lutte contre les criminalités numériques (C3N)
text:
## Summary

The **Centre de lutte contre les criminalités numériques (C3N)** is the French Gendarmerie's national judicial cybercrime unit. It sits inside the broader Gendarmerie cyber architecture and operates as one of the main state bodies handling higher-end cyber investigations with national competence.

## Mandate and Structure

Official Gendarmerie material shows that the cyber command structure created around **COMCyberGEND** centralizes and coordinates the institution's cyber capabilities. Within that structure, C3N and its regional investigative network handle darker-end judicial work linked to the dark web, cryptocurrency-enabled crime, attacks on information systems and other serious cyber-enabled offences.

The public-source record also makes clear that the French system is dual-track:

- the **Gendarmerie** side operates through COMCyberGEND and C3N
- the **Police Nationale** side operates through specialized police cyber offices

That division is important for this repo because French participation in multinational operations may appear under either police or gendarmerie channels depending on the case.

## IC Capabilities

- national-competence judicial cyber investigations
- regional investigative support through the Gendarmerie network
- darkweb and cryptocurrency-related investigative coverage
- regular interaction with Europol and Budapest-Convention cooperation structures

title: JUNALCO — National Jurisdiction for the Fight against Organized Crime (Cybercrime
text:
## Summary

**JUNALCO (Juridiction Nationale de Lutte contre la Criminalité Organisée)** is a specialized French national jurisdiction created in 2019 and installed at the Paris Judicial Tribunal in January 2020. It has concurrent nationwide competence over organized crime cases of very high complexity, including complex cybercrime matters handled by its dedicated **J3 cybercrime section** [1][2].

JUNALCO is *highly likely* the most operationally significant French prosecutorial body for international cybercrime cases, working in partnership with [[france-gendarmerie|Gendarmerie C3N]] and [[france-national-police|OFAC]].

## Mandate and Authority

JUNALCO was formalized by circular of 17 December 2019 on the concurrent national competence of the Paris tribunal for the fight against organized crime of very great complexity [2]. It handles serious cybercrime alongside other high-complexity organized crime categories (drug trafficking, financial crime, money laundering).

The **J3 section** is the cybercrime prevention and prosecution unit within JUNALCO, historically led by Vice-Prosecutor Johanna Brousse [1].

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> According to reporting, JUNALCO's caseload is expected to transfer to the new **PNACO (Parquet National Anti-Criminalité Organisée)** upon its creation on 5 January 2026 [1]. This page should be reviewed after that date.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- Part of the Paris Parquet (Public Prosecutor's Office)
- J3 section = dedicated cybercrime prosecutors
- Works with investigating judges assigned to cybercrime matters
- Coordinates with investigative services ([[france-gendarmerie|C3N]], [[france-national-police|OFAC]])

## IC Capabilities

- Judicial authority to issue European Investigation Orders (EIO) and MLAT requests
- Routine coordination with Eurojust for cross-border cases
- Europol/EC3 partnership on ransomware and dark web operations
- French representation in major multinational cybercrime takedowns (e.g. Operation Endgame)

## Key Operations and Cases

Specific operation attributions are not yet confirmed in ingested sources; JUNALCO is *likely* the judicial partner for major French participation in European cybercrime operations but case-by-case attribution requires further source ingestion.

## Cooperation Track Record

France is a party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] and a founding Europol member. JUNALCO *almost certainly* uses both Budapest Convention MLA channels and EU instruments (EIO, JITs via Eurojust) for its cross-border cybercrime work.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact date JUNALCO caseload transfers to PNACO (announced January 2026)
- Current staffing of J3 cybercrime section
- Specific multinational operations where JUNALCO has led French judicial coordination

## References

title: Office anti-cybercriminalité (OFAC) — French National Police
text:
## Summary

The **Office anti-cybercriminalité (OFAC)** is the French **Police Nationale**'s national cybercrime office, created by decree on **23 November 2023** and attached to the Direction Nationale de la Police Judiciaire [1][2]. OFAC replaced the former **Sous-Direction de Lutte Contre la Cybercriminalité (SDLC)** and the **Office Central de Lutte Contre la Criminalité liée aux Technologies de l'Information et de la Communication (OCLCTIC)** [2][3].

Based in Nanterre, OFAC had approximately **180 personnel** at its launch (police officers, gendarmes, engineers, technicians, administrative staff and contractors) and is scheduled to roughly double in size by 2027 [2][3]. It is *highly likely* the primary Police Nationale point of contact for international cybercrime cooperation, running in parallel with the [[france-gendarmerie|Gendarmerie C3N]].

## Mandate and Authority

Established by **Décret n° 2023-1083 du 23 novembre 2023** [1], OFAC holds national competence to:

- Combat all forms of delinquency emerging with information and communication technologies [2]
- Coordinate complex investigations into cyberattacks, darknet crime and online fraud [2]
- Centralize cybercrime police expertise within the Police Nationale
- Operate a territorial network of branches and detachments [2]

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

OFAC is organized into four operational "poles" structured around its four missions, plus a territorial network [2]. Leadership at launch:
- **Head:** Nicolas Guidoux, Contrôleur Général des services actifs de la police nationale [2]
- **Deputy:** Cécile Augeraud, Commissaire Divisionnaire [2]

## IC Capabilities

- National Police Nationale coordination point for cybercrime
- Europol EC3 cooperation (France is a founding member)
- Interpol National Central Bureau channels
- Darknet, ransomware and online fraud investigative expertise
- Dedicated Paris 2024 Olympics cyber threat focus at launch [3]

## Key Operations and Cases

Operation-by-operation attribution pending further source ingestion. OFAC and its SDLC/OCLCTIC predecessors *likely* participated in major European cybercrime operations during the period they were operational.

## Cooperation Track Record

- France is a party to the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]
- Founding member of Europol; OFAC *almost certainly* engages EC3 regularly
- Bilateral liaisons with FBI, BKA and other European partners
- Runs in parallel with [[france-gendarmerie|Gendarmerie C3N]] — France has dual Police/Gendarmerie cyber investigation networks

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current division of labor between OFAC (Police) and C3N (Gendarmerie) for international cybercrime coordination
- Which French unit holds the [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] Art. 35 24/7 contact point
- Specific operation attributions for OFAC since its creation in late 2023

## References

title: French High-Tech Crime Unit
text:
## Summary

The **French High-Tech Crime Unit** entry represents the French National Police cybercrime unit named by Europol as **OCLCTIC-DCPJ** in the Rex Mundi hacking/extortion investigation. The page should be used when a source names the unit directly, while broader police-institution context belongs on [[france-national-police]].

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The strongest repository anchor is [[rex-mundi-takedown]]. Europol's source describes a year-long investigation into a French cyber-extortion group that breached company databases and demanded bitcoin payments. The unit participated alongside the London Metropolitan Police, Royal Thai Police, Europol's 24/7 Operational Centre, and J-CAT, with the final arrest occurring in Thailand on a French international arrest warrant.

That case makes the unit relevant for three cooperation patterns: French domestic suspect identification, European operational coordination through Europol, and third-country arrest support when a French subject is located outside Europe.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for French specialist police participation in cyber-extortion, hacker-for-hire, and breach-based ransom cases. Do not use it as a generic synonym for all French cyber enforcement; C3N/Gendarmerie and judicial-prosecution pages have separate roles in the corpus.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is currently operation-specific. Additional French government sources would improve institutional history and current organizational naming, so older OCLCTIC-DCPJ references should be treated as source-bound rather than assumed current structure.

## References

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. title: German Police
text:
## Summary

**German Police** is an umbrella page for operation records that use a generic German police label. Germany's cybercrime enforcement structure is federal: [[germany-bka|BKA]] handles federal coordination and international cybercrime cooperation, while state criminal police offices (LKA) may lead or execute specific investigations depending on venue and jurisdiction.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The BKA cybercrime source identifies the federal criminal police as a central hub for international cybercrime cooperation, including darknet and malware investigations. The LKA source explains the parallel state-level criminal police structure. Together, they explain why operation pages sometimes say "German police" even when the exact lead may be BKA, ZIT Frankfurt, a state LKA, or another police authority.

In [[fake-shopping-sites-takedown-2024]], Europol describes a coordinated action involving Germany, Austria, Czechia, Finland, the Netherlands, and Poland against fraudulent shopping sites and a stolen-data marketplace. The operation page uses the generic German police label because the public source does not cleanly separate every German sub-agency in the summary layer.

## IC Relevance

Use this page only for generic or unresolved German police attribution. Use [[germany-bka]] for federal cybercrime capacity, [[zit-frankfurt]] for Frankfurt cyber prosecution, and state-LKA pages when a source names a specific Land authority.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is sufficient for routing and structure. It should not replace named-agency attribution when sources identify BKA, ZIT, or a state police office.

The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

title: Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)
text:
## Summary

The **Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)** is Germany's federal criminal police, headquartered in Wiesbaden. It serves as **INTERPOL NCB Germany** and as Germany's **central Europol National Unit**, making it the primary coordination point for all international law enforcement cooperation involving Germany.

In partnership with the **Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität (ZIT)** in Frankfurt, the BKA has led or co-led some of the most significant international cybercrime operations, including [[operation-avalanche|Operation Avalanche]] (2016), [[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame Phase 1]] (2024), and [[operation-endgame-phase2|Operation Endgame Phase 2]] (2025). The BKA is *almost certainly* the most operationally active law enforcement agency in continental Europe for cybercrime takedowns.

## Mandate and Authority

The BKA operates under the **Bundeskriminalamtgesetz (BKAG)** — the Federal Criminal Police Office Act. Key functions:

- **International cooperation:** Central point of contact for INTERPOL, Europol, and bilateral cooperation
- **Serious and organized crime investigation:** Including cybercrime with cross-border dimensions
- **Federal investigation mandate:** Assumes jurisdiction when crimes have international significance or cross state (Land) boundaries
- **Criminal intelligence:** Maintains national criminal databases and analytical systems
- **Protection of federal constitutional organs:** VIP protection duties

The BKA coordinates with Germany's 16 **Landeskriminalämter (LKAs)** — state criminal police offices — which handle cybercrime at the state level.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

### Cybercrime Division (Abteilung Cybercrime, CC)
- Dedicated cybercrime investigation unit
- Handles ransomware, botnet, dark web marketplace investigations
- Technical analysis and digital forensics
- Cryptocurrency tracing and financial crime analysis

### International Cooperation Division
- INTERPOL NCB operations
- Europol National Unit functions
- Bilateral cooperation coordination
- Incoming/outgoing MLA operational support

### Partnership with ZIT Frankfurt
The BKA works closely with the **Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität (ZIT)** — the Central Office for Combating Internet Crime — based at the Generalstaatsanwaltschaft Frankfurt. The BKA-ZIT partnership is the investigative-prosecutorial team behind Germany's major cybercrime operations:
- BKA conducts the investigation
- ZIT handles prosecution and judicial coordination
- Joint operational planning for takedowns

## IC Capabilities

- **Europol integration:** BKA officers co-located at Europol; direct SIENA access; frequent JIT participation
- **INTERPOL NCB:** I-24/7 access; Red Notice coordination; operational meeting participation
- **Digital forensics lab:** Advanced capabilities for malware analysis, network forensics, cryptocurrency tracing
- **Infrastructure seizure capacity:** Proven ability to seize and manage criminal infrastructure during takedowns
- **Multilingual capability:** High English proficiency across cybercrime unit

## Key Operations and Cases

| Operation | Year | BKA Role | Results |
|-----------|------|----------|---------|
| [[operation-avalanche]] | 2016 | Co-lead with ZIT | 39 servers seized; 50+ countries; 5 arrests |
| [[operation-endgame-phase1]] | 2024 | Lead with ZIT | 100+ servers, 2,000+ domains, 4 arrests |
| [[operation-endgame-phase2]] | 2025 | Lead with ZIT | 300 servers, 650 domains, EUR 3.5M seized |
| [[operation-cronos-phase1]] | 2024 | Key participant | LockBit infrastructure seized |
| [[operation-cronos-phase3]] | 2024 | Key participant | 4 additional LockBit arrests |
| [[phobos-8base-crackdown]] | 2025 | Key participant | 4 arrests of 8Base leadership |
| [[hive-ransomware-takedown]] | 2023 | Key participant | Hive infrastructure infiltrated |
| [[andromeda-botnet-takedown]] | 2017 | Co-lead | Major botnet disrupted |

The [[operation-avalanche|Avalanche]] and [

title: German Federal Ministry of the Interior
text:
## Summary

The **German Federal Ministry of the Interior** (BMI) is Germany's federal internal-security ministry. In this corpus it is most important as the parent policy context for [[germany-bka|BKA]] cybercrime enforcement and as an official ministry-level source for Germany's participation in large international operations.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The local BMI source covers [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]], describing it as a major international law-enforcement operation against botnets and the ransomware-enabling malware ecosystem. The operational execution belongs to agencies such as [[germany-bka]], [[zit-frankfurt]], Europol, Eurojust, and partner-country investigators; BMI's role in this corpus is to provide official German federal framing and confirmation.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source relies on German ministry-level statements, federal policy ownership, or the relationship between German internal-security governance and operational cybercrime agencies. For concrete investigative steps such as arrests, server seizures, or malware infrastructure takedowns, link the implementing agencies directly.

## Source Coverage

Current source coverage is operation-centered. Future enrichment should add BMI institutional pages on federal internal security, BKA oversight, and cyber-security strategy if available in the corpus.

title: Central Office for Combating Internet Crime (ZIT) — Frankfurt am Main
text:
## Summary

The **Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität (ZIT)** is a specialized central office of the **Generalstaatsanwaltschaft Frankfurt am Main** (General Public Prosecutor's Office Frankfurt, Hessen state), established on **1 January 2010** [1]. Originally headquartered in Gießen, ZIT relocated to Frankfurt am Main in July 2019 [1].

ZIT is *almost certainly* the single most important German prosecutorial body for international cybercrime cases. Together with the **[[germany-bka|Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)]]**, ZIT has led the German prosecutorial side of nearly every major German-driven international cybercrime operation of the last decade, including [[operation-avalanche|Avalanche]] (2016), [[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame]] (2024), and multiple darknet forum takedowns [2][3].

## Mandate and Authority

ZIT serves as the **first point of contact for the BKA** for internet crimes where jurisdiction within Germany is unclear or where a case involves numerous suspects nationwide (mass proceedings) [1]. This functional central-office status allows ZIT to take on cases that would otherwise get stuck in Germany's federal/state (Bund/Länder) jurisdictional split.

As an operational central office, ZIT handles particularly complex investigations in four priority areas [1]:

1. **Child pornography and online child sexual abuse**
2. **Darknet criminality** (marketplace, weapons, drugs, counterfeit trafficking)
3. **Cybercrime in the strict sense** (hacking, data theft, computer fraud, ransomware)
4. **Online hate crime** (Hasskriminalität)

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

ZIT's staffing has grown significantly since founding. As reported, it comprises a leading senior prosecutor, additional senior prosecutors, and approximately 11 public prosecutors [1]. ZIT is a founding member of the **Judicial Cybercrime Network**, a European network of judicial authorities for combating internet crime [1].

## IC Capabilities

- **BKA partnership:** Investigative-prosecutorial team for all major BKA cybercrime operations
- **European judicial network participation:** Judicial Cybercrime Network founding member
- **Eurojust coordination:** Standard partner for EU cross-border cyber prosecutions
- **MLA competent authority:** Receives and issues international judicial requests for internet crime matters

## Key Operations and Cases

| Operation | Year | ZIT Role | Key Result |
|-----------|------|----------|-----------|
| [[operation-avalanche]] | 2016 | Co-lead prosecutor with BKA | 39 servers seized, 50+ countries involved |
| [[operation-endgame-phase1]] | 2024 | Lead prosecutor with BKA | 100+ servers, 2,000+ domains |
| [[operation-endgame-phase2]] | 2025 | Lead prosecutor with BKA | ~300 servers, EUR 3.5M seized |
| [[operation-cronos-phase1]] | 2024 | Participant | LockBit infrastructure seizure |
| Largest cybercrime forum takedown | 2025 | ZIT-BKA press release announced shutdown of two largest cybercrime forums with 10M+ users [3] |

## Cooperation Track Record

- Standard partner of Europol EC3 for German cybercrime operations
- Active Eurojust coordination
- Bilateral prosecutorial cooperation with FBI/DoJ, Dutch prosecutors, UK CPS/NCA, and others
- Has hosted multiple German ministerial visits highlighting its operational significance [2]

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current total ZIT staffing (growth trajectory since 2010)
- Volume of ZIT-handled MLA incoming vs. outgoing requests
- ZIT's operational relationship with the 16 state-level [[germany-lka|Landeskriminalämter (LKAs)]] for cybercrime

## References

title: Landeskriminalamt (LKA) — German State Criminal Police Offices (Collective)
text:
> [!info] Collective reference
> This page is a **collective reference** for Germany's 16 state-level **Landeskriminalämter (LKAs)**, not a single agency. Each LKA is an independent authority subordinate to its Land's interior ministry. For specific LKAs (e.g. LKA Bayern, LKA Baden-Württemberg), dedicated pages should be created when needed.

## Summary

The **Landeskriminalamt (LKA)** — plural **Landeskriminalämter** — are the state-level criminal police offices of Germany. There is **one LKA in each of Germany's 16 federal states (Länder)**, directly subordinate to the state's interior ministry [1][2]. Each LKA is independent from the federal [[germany-bka|Bundeskriminalamt (BKA)]] but cooperates closely with it.

LKAs are *almost certainly* the workhorse of German cybercrime investigation: while the BKA leads major international operations, the 16 LKAs handle the bulk of state-level cybercrime casework and often provide the investigators, forensic capacity and victim interfaces that underpin federal and international operations [3].

## Mandate and Authority

LKAs supervise police operations aimed at preventing and investigating criminal offences within their state, and coordinate investigations of serious crime involving more than one regional headquarters [1]. They can take over investigative responsibility in cases of serious crime, including cybercrime, drug trafficking, organized crime, economic crime, and extremist or terrorist offences [1].

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

Each LKA typically includes [1][2][3]:

- A **Cybercrime Division** (Abteilung Cybercrime / Cybercrime und Digitale Spuren)
- A **Digital Forensics** unit
- A **Zentrale Ansprechstelle Cybercrime (ZAC)** — state central contact point for the economy
- A **State Protection** (Staatsschutz) department handling state-security-relevant cyber cases

### Example: LKA Baden-Württemberg

Baden-Württemberg's LKA features distinct departments for cybercrime and digital traces (Abteilung 5) and state protection (Abteilung 6) [3].

### Example: LKA Bayern (Munich)

LKA Bayern hosts a large cybercrime division and operates the Bavarian ZAC contact point [2].

## IC Capabilities

- **State-level Europol/BKA liaison:** LKAs route international requests through the BKA Europol/Interpol channels rather than direct foreign engagement
- **Joint operations participation:** LKA officers participate in JITs and major takedowns led or co-led by BKA
- **ZAC business reporting:** Each state ZAC provides a single point of contact for cybercrime victim companies and coordinates with the BKA's national ZAC
- **State-level digital forensics:** Most complex forensic work on German cybercrime cases runs through LKA labs

## Key Operations and Cases

LKAs routinely contribute personnel, digital forensics and witness interviews to BKA-led international operations including [[operation-endgame-phase1|Operation Endgame]], [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos]], and [[operation-avalanche|Operation Avalanche]]. Specific LKA attributions require reviewing individual operation press releases.

## Cooperation Track Record

International cybercrime cooperation by German LKAs is *almost certainly* channeled through the federal [[germany-bka|BKA]]:

- **Europol requests** → BKA Europol National Unit → relevant LKA
- **Interpol requests** → BKA NCB Germany → relevant LKA
- **MLA requests** → [[germany-frankfurt-prosecutor|ZIT Frankfurt]] or state prosecutor → LKA investigation

Direct bilateral engagement by LKAs with foreign police is *unlikely* to be standard practice outside specific joint initiatives.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- How is workload distributed between LKAs and the BKA for international cybercrime matters?
- Do any LKAs have direct Europol liaison beyond the BKA?
- Which LKAs have the largest cybercrime divisions by headcount?
- How effective is ZAC coordination across all 16 states for cross-state incidents?

## References

| # | Source | Publisher |

title: Hong Kong Police Force — Cyber Security and Technology Crime Bureau
text:
## Summary

The Cyber Security and Technology Crime Bureau (CSTCB) of the [[hong-kong-police|Hong Kong Police Force]] is the primary unit responsible for maintaining Hong Kong's cyber security and preventing and detecting technology crimes. CSTCB was established on **1 January 2015** when the Technology Crime Division of the Commercial Crime Bureau was upgraded to a full bureau, reflecting the growing significance of cybercrime as a law enforcement priority.

CSTCB sits within the "B" Department (Crime & Security) of the Hong Kong Police Force.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** Hong Kong Police Force — Cyber Security and Technology Crime Bureau (CSTCB, 網絡安全及科技罪案調查科)
**Status:** *Active* — CSTCB continues to operate under the same name and within "B" Department [1][4][5]
**Parent:** Hong Kong Police Force → "B" Department (Crime & Security)

As of April 2026 CSTCB remains the primary Hong Kong cybercrime and cyber security police unit. No reorganization has occurred since its 2015 establishment, but the bureau has **grown substantially in size**:

- **Staff growth:** The establishment of police officers in CSTCB grew from **180 at its inception in 2015** to **395 in financial year 2023–24**, and was expected to reach **403 in financial year 2024–25** [4]. This represents roughly a **2.2× expansion** over the first decade.
- **Caseload (2024):** CSTCB recorded **33,903 technology crime cases in 2024**, a slight 0.6% decrease compared to 2023 [3]. The bureau processed over **25 million pieces of cyber threat intelligence** and identified over **440,000 cyber threats** targeting Hong Kong in 2024 [3].
- **Inter-agency coordination:** In March 2025 the Hong Kong Digital Policy Office (DPO) launched the Cybersec One Programme and Cybersecurity Vendor Connect Programme; CSTCB participated as a cybercrime intelligence partner but did not merge with DPO [5].
- **National Security Law context:** The 2020 Hong Kong National Security Law and the 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (Article 23 legislation) continue to affect Hong Kong's international cooperation environment. CSTCB's operational cybercrime cooperation with non-Western partners (including mainland China MPS) *likely* remains routine; cooperation with some Western jurisdictions on politically sensitive cases *possibly* remains constrained, though on routine fraud and cybercrime CSTCB continues to engage through INTERPOL channels.

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (Article 23) may have changed the legal environment for CSTCB's data-sharing with foreign counterparts; the full operational impact is not yet publicly documented.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2015-01-01 | **CSTCB** established by upgrading the Technology Crime Division of the Commercial Crime Bureau to a full bureau (180 officers) | [1][4] |
| 2020-06 | Hong Kong National Security Law enacted — began affecting external cooperation posture (scope for cybercrime cooperation unclear) | — |
| 2023–24 | CSTCB establishment grows to **395 officers** (>2× the 2015 figure) | [4] |
| 2024 | Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (Article 23) enacted | — |
| 2024–25 | CSTCB establishment reaches **403 officers** (approved) | [4] |
| 2025-03 | DPO cybersecurity initiatives launched with CSTCB participation | [5] |

## Mandate and Authority

CSTCB's mandate covers:

- Handling cyber security issues affecting Hong Kong
- Technology crime investigations
- Computer forensic examinations
- Prevention of technology crime
- Liaison with local and overseas law enforcement agencies for cross-border technology crimes
- Intelligence exchange with foreign counterparts

The legal basis includes Hong Kong's Crimes Ordinance (Cap. 200), Telecommunications Ordinance (Cap. 106), and relevant common-law offences.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

CSTCB is organized into **four sub-divisions**:

title: Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3)
text:
## Summary

The Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) is the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]]'s central intake and analysis unit for public reports of internet-facilitated crime. Established in **May 2000** originally as the Internet Fraud Complaint Center, IC3 has become the single most comprehensive US national dataset on cyber-enabled fraud and cybercrime victimization.

IC3's **2024 Annual Internet Crime Report** recorded **859,532 complaints** with reported losses exceeding **USD 16.6 billion** — a **33% increase** in losses over 2023. Over the 2020–2024 period, IC3 received **4.2 million complaints** with a total reported loss of **USD 50.5 billion** (approximately 836,000 complaints per year on average).

## Mandate and Authority

IC3's mandate is not investigative; rather, it is **intake, analysis, and referral**:

- **Public reporting portal (ic3.gov):** Accepts complaints from victims of internet-facilitated crime, including business email compromise (BEC), romance scams, investment fraud, tech support fraud, ransomware, and cryptocurrency-enabled fraud.
- **Data analysis:** Aggregates complaints to identify trends, campaigns, and criminal networks.
- **Referral:** Routes actionable complaints to FBI field offices, other federal agencies ([[us-secret-service|US Secret Service]], DOJ, FTC, Treasury), and international partners.
- **Recovery Asset Team (RAT):** Works with victim banks and foreign financial institutions to freeze fraudulently transferred funds, particularly in BEC cases where wire transfers can sometimes be reversed within the "golden hour."

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Intake and Triage:** Complaint processing and initial victimology analysis.
- **Analysis Unit:** Trend analysis feeding FBI investigations and public reporting.
- **Recovery Asset Team (RAT):** Freeze/recover stolen funds; works through FBI Legat offices with foreign banks.
- **Publication:** Annual Internet Crime Report and periodic alerts/advisories.

## IC Capabilities

- **International liaison via FBI Legat network:** IC3 data shared with foreign partners through the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division's]] 63+ Legal Attache offices.
- **Cross-border fund recovery:** RAT coordinates with foreign banks (particularly in Hong Kong, UAE, UK, China, and other common BEC transit jurisdictions) to freeze fraudulent transfers.
- **Intelligence products:** Trend data informs international operations against BEC, romance scam, and investment fraud networks.

## Key Statistics (2024 IC3 Annual Report)

| Metric | 2024 Value |
|--------|-----------|
| Total complaints | 859,532 |
| Complaints with reported loss | 256,256 |
| Total reported losses | USD 16.6 billion (+33% YoY) |
| Cyber-enabled fraud complaints | 333,981 (38% of total) |
| Cyber-enabled fraud losses | USD 13.7 billion (83% of losses) |
| Complaints involving digital assets | ~150,000 |
| Losses involving digital assets | USD 9.3 billion (+66% YoY) |
| Elder fraud losses | USD 4.9 billion (+43% YoY) |

These figures represent **reported** losses from victims who filed with IC3 and are *highly likely* an undercount of total US cybercrime victimization given underreporting.

## Key Operations and Cases

IC3 data routinely supports specific FBI and multinational operations, including:

- BEC takedowns (e.g., Operation Rewired, Operation WireWire)
- Romance scam arrest sweeps
- Tech support fraud prosecutions

> [!info] Specific operations citing IC3 data should be added as sources are ingested.

## Cooperation Track Record

IC3 is *almost certainly* the most-cited US source for cybercrime victimization statistics globally and provides a unique intelligence advantage to the FBI's international cybercrime cooperation. Foreign law enforcement agencies *highly likely* rely on IC3 data when investigating US-origin or US-targeting cybercrime.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- IC3's recovery rate via the Recovery Asset Team for fraudulent wire transfers is

title: ICANN
text:
## Summary

**ICANN** appears in this wiki as an infrastructure-governance actor relevant to domain disruption, sinkholing, and abuse handling. It is not a police agency, but domain governance can matter when malware infrastructure relies on large-scale domain generation or abused registration channels.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Operation Avalanche is the key local reference. The takedown relied on coordinated action against a fast-changing botnet and malware infrastructure ecosystem where domain handling, sinkholing, and registrar/registry coordination were operationally significant.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for domain-governance and infrastructure-coordination roles. Keep arrest, seizure, and prosecution authority on law-enforcement and judicial pages.

title: Indonesia Immigration
text:
## Summary

**Indonesia Immigration** is relevant to the corpus because the Bali villa raid was handled through immigration enforcement rather than a completed Indonesian cybercrime prosecution. Public reporting describes the detention of 103 foreign nationals in Bali suspected of cybercrime activity, followed by planned deportation because the alleged victims were outside Indonesia.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

In [[bali-villa-cybercrime-raid-2024]], Indonesian immigration officials raided a villa in Tabanan regency, Bali, detained 91 men and 12 women, and seized hundreds of phones and other electronic devices. The reporting describes suspected targeting of victims abroad, including Malaysia, and identifies immigration violations as the immediate enforcement route.

This is important for IC analysis because it shows a common scam-compound problem: the host jurisdiction can disrupt an operation through immigration and deportation even when direct cybercrime prosecution is difficult because victims, evidence, and harms sit outside the territorial forum.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for Indonesia-based foreign-national scam or cybercrime operations where visa misuse, detention, deportation, or immigration raids are central. Use [[indonesia-police]] when sources identify police-led criminal investigation rather than immigration enforcement.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is media-heavy but consistent across AP/VOA, Al Jazeera, The Record, and Taipei Times. Direct Indonesian government releases would improve exact agency naming and legal authority detail.

title: INTERPOL ICSE Database
text:
## Summary

The **INTERPOL ICSE Database** is the corpus entry for INTERPOL's International Child Sexual Exploitation database. It is best understood as an investigative mechanism, not a conventional organization: it supports victim identification, duplicate-material deconfliction, and cross-border routing of child sexual exploitation leads.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Child sexual exploitation cases often involve material circulating across platforms and jurisdictions long after the original abuse. A shared international database helps investigators determine whether a victim is already known, whether a series is linked to another jurisdiction, and whether new imagery may reveal a still-unidentified child or location. The database therefore sits at the evidence-analysis layer of international cooperation.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when an operation, annual report, or child-protection source discusses ICSE, international victim identification, or image/video comparison in online child sexual exploitation investigations. Use [[interpol]] for the broader police organization and [[europol-vidtf]] for Europol's taskforce model.

## Source Coverage

The current source set is anchored in INTERPOL's Operation Orion and annual-report material. More direct INTERPOL ICSE institutional pages would improve mechanism-level detail and database governance coverage.

title: INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)
text:
> [!info] Updated 2026-04-08
> Added 6 new operations: Jackal, Jackal III, First Light 2024, HAECHI IV, HAECHI VI, Sentinel Africa.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. IGCI remains INTERPOL's primary hub for cybercrime operations, digital innovation, and research. Headquartered in **Singapore**, parent organization [[interpol|INTERPOL]] [10].

**10th anniversary (June 2025):** IGCI celebrated its 10-year anniversary in Singapore in a ceremony attended by INTERPOL President **Ahmed Naser Al Raisi**, Secretary General **Valdecy Urquiza**, and members of the INTERPOL Executive Committee. The anniversary explicitly reaffirmed Singapore as INTERPOL's primary cyber and innovation hub — *highly likely* indicating there is no plan to relocate or downsize the facility [10].

**Current role:**
- **Cyber Fusion Centre** continues to aggregate real-time threat intelligence from law enforcement, private sector, and academia.
- **Innovation Centre** — research on AI, virtual assets, and emerging tech for law enforcement.
- **Capacity Building Directorate** — training programs for member countries, particularly ASEAN, Africa, and Pacific Island states.
- **Operational coordination** for HAECHI series (Korea-led), Operation First Light, Operation Synergia (including **Synergia III** in March 2026), Jackal series, Serengeti, Sentinel Africa.

**Recent operational output (2024–2026):**
- HAECHI V (2024): 40 countries, 5,500+ arrests, USD 400M+ seized
- HAECHI VI (2025): USD 439M recovered
- Synergia II (2024): 22,800 malicious IPs taken down
- **Synergia III (March 2026)**: continued cyber-infrastructure takedown with private sector intelligence partners (Group-IB, Trend Micro, Kaspersky, Team Cymru) [11]
- Operation Sentinel Africa (Oct-Nov 2025): 574 arrests

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> IGCI's location, mandate, and structure are unchanged. Singapore remains the confirmed headquarters, as reaffirmed by the June 2025 10-year anniversary ceremony attended by INTERPOL's senior leadership.

## Summary

The INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI), located in Singapore, is INTERPOL's primary hub for cybercrime operations and innovation. Opened in 2015, it provides operational support, digital forensics capabilities, and capacity building for INTERPOL's 196 member countries.

## IC Capabilities

- **Cyber Fusion Centre:** Aggregates cyber threat intelligence from law enforcement, private sector, and academia to support investigations
- **I-24/7 Network:** Secure global police communications network connecting all 196 NCBs
- **Digital forensics:** Advanced laboratory supporting member countries
- **Operational coordination:** Coordinates multi-country cyber operations (HAECHI, Synergia)
- **Capacity building:** Training programs for developing countries

## Key Operations

### HAECHI Series (Financial Cybercrime)

The HAECHI series, led by [[south-korea|Korea]], is INTERPOL's flagship financial cybercrime operation:

| Operation | Year | Countries | Arrests | Seized |

The HAECHI IV press release references a "260% arrest increase over HAECHI III" but does not provide explicit HAECHI III totals. These figures require verification from an INTERPOL press release for HAECHI III.
| **[[operation-haechi-v|HAECHI V]]** | **2024** | **40** | **5,500+** | **$400M+** |
| [[operation-haechi-vi|HAECHI VI]] | 2025 | 40 | - | $439M |

**[[operation-haechi-v|Operation HAECHI V]]** (Jul-Nov 2024): The largest HAECHI operation to date. 40 countries, **5,500+ arrests**, **USD 400M+ seized**. Korean-Chinese joint sub-operation dismantled a voice phishing syndicate responsible for USD 1.1 billion in losses affecting 1,900+ victims. Targeted voice

title: INTERPOL (International Criminal Police Organization)
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. INTERPOL remains headquartered in **Lyon, France**, with **196 member countries** and its Constitution unchanged. No structural reorganization reported.

**Secretary General:** **Valdecy Urquiza** (Brazil) — took office in 2024 as INTERPOL's first Latin American Secretary General, succeeding **Jürgen Stock** (Germany, 2014–2024) whose 10-year maximum term expired. Urquiza previously served as INTERPOL's Executive Director of Police Services [10].

**President:** **Ahmed Naser Al Raisi** (United Arab Emirates) — elected at the 89th General Assembly in November 2021 for a four-year term (still in office as of April 2026; next presidential election expected at the 2025/2026 General Assembly cycle) [10].

**Structure:** General Secretariat (Lyon) + [[interpol-igci|INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)]] in Singapore + Regional Bureaus (Buenos Aires, Abidjan, Nairobi, Yaoundé, San Salvador, Singapore, Bangkok, Harare) + 196 National Central Bureaus. The IGCI in Singapore celebrated its **10-year anniversary in June 2025** at a ceremony attended by President Al Raisi and SG Urquiza, reaffirming Singapore as INTERPOL's primary cyber and innovation hub [11].

**Recent priorities (2024–2026):** Global financial-cybercrime operations (HAECHI V/VI led by Korea, First Light 2024, Operation Synergia II and **Synergia III** in March 2026), African regional operations (Serengeti, Sentinel Africa), BEC/Black Axe (Jackal series), CSAM (Cyber Guardian 2025), AI-enabled crime, silver notices for asset tracing.

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> INTERPOL's mandate and structure are unchanged. The material change since this page was created is the **Secretary General transition from Stock to Urquiza (2024)** and the continued centrality of the Singapore IGCI after its 10-year anniversary celebration in June 2025.

## Summary

**INTERPOL** (International Criminal Police Organization) is the world's largest international police organization, with **196 member countries**. Headquartered in **Lyon, France**, INTERPOL facilitates cross-border police cooperation through its secure global communications network (**I-24/7**), international notice system (**Red Notices**, Blue Notices, Purple Notices), criminal databases, and operational support for multinational law enforcement operations.

For cybercrime, INTERPOL's primary operational hub is the **INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI)** ([[interpol-igci]]) in Singapore, which houses the **Cyber Fusion Centre** and coordinates major operations including the **HAECHI** series (led by [[south-korea]]), **Jackal** (targeting Black Axe), **Serengeti**, **Synergia**, and **First Light**.

INTERPOL is *almost certainly* the most globally inclusive platform for cybercrime cooperation, reaching regions (Africa, Southeast Asia, Pacific Islands) where the [[budapest-convention]] has limited membership.

## Mandate and Authority

INTERPOL operates under its **Constitution** (adopted 1956, amended). Key principles:
- **Art. 2:** Ensure and promote mutual assistance between criminal police authorities
- **Art. 3:** Prohibition of intervention in matters of political, military, religious, or racial character
- **Sovereignty principle:** INTERPOL facilitates cooperation but has no arrest or investigative authority; all operational actions are taken by national police

INTERPOL does **not** have its own investigators or powers of arrest. It serves as a coordination, communication, and intelligence platform.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

### General Secretariat (Lyon)
- Executive Committee and Secretary General
- Operational coordination and policy
- I-24/7 network administration
- Notices issuance
- Criminal databases management

### INTERPOL Global Complex for Innovation (IGCI) — Singapore
See [[interpol-igci]] for full profile.
- **Cyber Fusion Centre:** Real-time cyber threat intelligence and operational coordination
- **Innovati

title: Garda Siochana
text:
## Summary

**An Garda Siochana** is Ireland's national police service. In the current cybercrime corpus, its strongest source-backed role is Ireland's domestic contribution to INTERPOL's **Operation Jackal** series, locally branded as **Operation Skein**.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Garda sources provide country-level detail that complements central [[interpol|INTERPOL]] summaries. The 2022 source records early Irish enforcement activity against Black Axe-linked fraud and money-laundering networks. The 2023 update adds searches, arrests, money-laundering charges, mule-account activity, and fund-recovery work for Irish corporate victims. The July 2024 source is the strongest Irish-side official anchor for [[operation-jackal-iii|Operation Jackal III]], adding domestic figures for arrests, charges, searches, bank-account blocking, and cryptocurrency seizures.

## IC Relevance

Garda coverage is useful when tracing how a multinational fraud operation translates into national enforcement. It provides an official non-U.S. perspective on Black Axe-linked business-email-compromise, romance-fraud, mule-account, and laundering investigations. That makes the Garda page a high-value link target for Operation Jackal, Operation Skein, and related African organized cyber-fraud narratives.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for the Operation Jackal / Operation Skein lineage from 2022 through 2024. It remains narrower for Garda's permanent cybercrime structure, so future enrichment should add official pages on the Garda National Cyber Crime Bureau or related specialist units if they are harvested.

title: Italy DCSA - Direzione Centrale per i Servizi Antidroga
text:
## Summary

Italy DCSA - Direzione Centrale per i Servizi Antidroga is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: Central coordination of anti-drug operations in Italy, including darknet or encrypted-platform investigations with a narcotics component. Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[italy]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: [[operation-kraken-ghost-platform]]. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. Where an operation page names multiple agencies, this organization page should be read together with the operation's lead agency, coordinating body, participating-country list, and source table.

## Source Coverage

The references below provide 3 source link(s) for the current organization record. The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

title: Italy Postal Police and Cyber Security Service
text:
## Summary

The **Servizio Polizia postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica** is Italy's core national cybercrime police service within the **Polizia di Stato** structure. It is the main institutional anchor for cybercrime investigation, online child-protection enforcement, and cyber-enabled fraud work in Italy's law-enforcement system, and it provides the organizational home for **CNAIPIC**.

## Mandate and Authority

Official Polizia di Stato normative material shows that, from **11 May 2024**, the service's competences are exercised within the newly created central directorate for scientific police and cyber security. The service's legal references also trace back to the earlier **Servizio Polizia postale e delle comunicazioni** framework, showing organizational continuity rather than an entirely new institution.

Italian Interior Ministry material also places **CNAIPIC** inside the telecommunications-security branch of the Ministry of the Interior. That matters because it shows that Italy's critical-infrastructure cyber mission is not separate from the Postal Police tradition; it is embedded in it.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- national service within the State Police system
- coordinates specialized cyber investigative activity
- supports cyber-security and communications-related law-enforcement missions
- provides an institutional home for nationally coordinated cybercrime response
- includes the national critical-infrastructure protection center CNAIPIC
- bridges operational policing, incident response, and expedited cross-border assistance

## IC Capabilities

- treaty-based and MLA-linked cybercrime support under Italy's Budapest Convention framework
- operational cybercrime investigation capacity through the State Police system
- experience in ransomware-linked, fraud, and exploitation investigations
- critical-infrastructure alerting and coordination through CNAIPIC
- participation in expedited cyber-assistance channels, including the High Tech Crime 24/7 environment referenced by the Interior Ministry

## Key Operations and Cases

The official 2017 **Operazione Criptolocker** release provides a concrete example of Postal Police cybercrime enforcement against ransomware-linked extortion and computer fraud. The case demonstrates that the service is not only policy-facing, but also operationally active in complex cyber investigations.

The 2022 Interior Ministry page on the **SINC3** project adds a second layer: it describes CNAIPIC as handling hundreds of attacks and alerts and receiving assistance requests through the G7 High Tech Crime 24/7 network. That makes the institution relevant not just to domestic investigation but also to expedited international cyber cooperation.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- The exact current allocation of responsibilities between the Postal Police cyber service, CNAIPIC, and Italy's non-police cyber institutions still needs a direct Italian organizational chart that is easier to cite than scattered ministry material.
- A fuller operational history of named multinational Postal Police cases still requires additional official-source collection.

## References

| 2 | Operazione Criptolocker della Polizia postale di Catania | Polizia di Stato | 2017-06-21 | https://www.poliziadistato.it/articolo/15594a96fe92d98208614649 |

title: Japan Cybercrime Control Center
text:
## Summary

The **Japan Cybercrime Control Center (JC3)** is a Japanese public-private cybercrime cooperation body. In the current corpus, its clearest role is in the [[lumma-stealer-takedown|Lumma Stealer takedown]], where Microsoft and secondary reporting identify Japan-side support as part of a wider Microsoft, DOJ, Europol, and Japanese action against LummaC2 infrastructure.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

JC3 is relevant where the operation depends on a hybrid model: private-sector telemetry, civil or criminal domain action, law-enforcement coordination, and regional infrastructure disruption. The Lumma operation is a useful example because it involved thousands of domains and required coordination across private-sector and government actors rather than a conventional arrest-first police operation.

## IC Relevance

Use JC3 when the source names Japan Cybercrime Control Center, Japan-side infrastructure disruption, or public-private Japanese cybercrime cooperation. Use [[japan-npa]] when the source names the National Police Agency or its Cyber Affairs Bureau as the official police actor.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is currently operation-specific and focused on Lumma. A direct JC3 institutional page would strengthen mandate, membership, and standing coordination mechanisms.

title: Japan National Police Agency Headquarters
text:
## Summary

**Japan National Police Agency Headquarters** is the parent institutional entry for Japan's national police organization. The more operational cybercrime detail belongs on [[japan-npa|Japan NPA - Cyber Affairs Bureau]], but this page preserves the hierarchy between the NPA headquarters and its cyber-specific bureau.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The local NPA source states that the Cyber Affairs Bureau was established on 1 April 2022 by restructuring and integrating cyber-related divisions, making it the central policing authority for cyberspace with direct jurisdiction for serious cases. That means the headquarters page should be treated as the parent authority, while the Cyber Affairs Bureau page should be used for bureau-level mandate, investigative structure, and cyber operations.

Operation Cyber Guardian provides an operation-level anchor. The official Japanese police document identifies the action as a Singapore-initiated international cooperative operation, confirms six participating jurisdictions, and records a 544-suspect total. This supports Japan NPA's role in regional child-protection and cyber-enabled exploitation enforcement.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source refers broadly to Japan's National Police Agency, national police headquarters, or parent-level international police cooperation. Use [[japan-npa]] for Cyber Affairs Bureau details and [[japan-jc3]] for public-private JC3 involvement.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is sufficient for parent-child hierarchy and one operation anchor. More official headquarters material would improve administrative structure and NCB Tokyo context.

title: Japan National Police Agency — Cyber Affairs Bureau
text:
## Summary

The Cyber Affairs Bureau (サイバー警察局, Saibā keisatsu-kyoku) of Japan's National Police Agency (警察庁, Keisatsu-chō) was established on **1 April 2022** by restructuring the Info-Communications Bureau and integrating cyber-related divisions from several other NPA bureaus. It is now the central policing authority for cyberspace in Japan, with direct jurisdiction for the first time (historically, NPA coordinated rather than directly investigated).

Alongside the Cyber Affairs Bureau, the NPA established the **National Cyber Unit** (サイバー特別捜査隊, later restructured as サイバー特別捜査部) with roughly 200 investigators to handle serious cybercrime cases, including attacks on national and local government, critical infrastructure, and significant transnational cases.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 警察庁 サイバー警察局 (NPA Cyber Affairs Bureau)
**Level:** Bureau (局), equal to other NPA bureaus
**Status:** *Active* — no reorganization of the bureau itself since its 1 April 2022 establishment [1][5]

As of April 2026 the Cyber Affairs Bureau continues to operate under the same name and structure established in 2022. However, its **statutory authority has been substantially expanded** in 2025–2026:

- **Active Cyber Defense Act (ACDA):** Enacted by the Diet on **16 May 2025**, with staged implementation through 2026–2027. The law expands the NPA's powers to include **active cyber defense measures**, including remote access to and neutralization of hostile infrastructure abroad, monitoring of cross-border IP traffic (subject to independent oversight), and counter-access against relay servers [5][6].
- **New National Cybersecurity Office (NCO):** In July 2025 the Cabinet Secretariat's NISC was restructured as the National Cybersecurity Office (NCO). The NCO coordinates but does not absorb the NPA Cyber Affairs Bureau — operational cybercrime enforcement authority remains with the NPA [5].
- **2025 Cybersecurity Strategy:** On 23 December 2025 Japan adopted a new Cybersecurity Strategy that incorporates the ACDA framework and clarifies the NPA and SDF role in active cyber defense [5].
- **Cybercrime caseload continues to rise:** The bureau is *highly likely* handling a growing caseload of North Korea–linked cryptocurrency theft, ransomware, and transnational scam investigations.

The bureau *almost certainly* retains its original mandate plus the newly granted active-defense authorities. An independent oversight body has been established to approve active-defense operations, addressing domestic privacy concerns from the Diet debate.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2022-04-01 | **Cyber Affairs Bureau (サイバー警察局)** established by restructuring Info-Communications Bureau | [1][3] |
| 2022-04-01 | **National Cyber Unit** (サイバー特別捜査隊) launched with ~200 investigators, direct national jurisdiction for serious cybercrime | [1][3] |
| 2025-05-16 | **Active Cyber Defense Act** passed by the Diet, expanding NPA authority for active cyber measures | [5][6] |
| 2025-07 | **NISC restructured as National Cybersecurity Office (NCO)** within the Cabinet Secretariat; NPA operational role preserved | [5] |
| 2025-12-23 | Japan adopts **new Cybersecurity Strategy** incorporating ACDA framework | [5] |
| 2026–2027 | Staged ACDA implementation; NPA operational use of active-defense powers begins | [5][6] |

## Mandate and Authority

The Cyber Affairs Bureau's mandate encompasses:

- **Policing in cyberspace** — the central NPA authority for cyber law enforcement
- **Combat cybercrime and cyberterrorism**
- **Central consolidation and analysis** of various types of cyber information
- **Implementation of effective countermeasures** related to cyber incidents
- **Close collaboration with overseas public security authorities**

Unlike prior NPA cyber arrangements, which relied on Prefectural Police (都道府県警察) for actual investigations, the National Cyber Unit under the Cyber Affairs Bureau has **direct nat

title: Kaspersky Lab
text:
## Summary

Kaspersky Lab appears in this wiki as a private-sector cybersecurity company that provides threat intelligence and malware-analysis support to law-enforcement operations. The page is not used to describe a state authority; it is used to capture private-sector technical participation.

## International Cooperation Role

The source corpus links Kaspersky to Simda, Africa Cyber Surge II, and Operation Secure. In these records the company contributes intelligence, malware or infrastructure analysis, and operational support to INTERPOL-led or multi-partner actions. When using this page, distinguish technical support from legal authority: Kaspersky can supply intelligence and analysis, but arrests, searches, seizures, and evidence production remain with public authorities and courts.

## References

title: Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA)
text:
> [!info] Status — Active, restructured 2026-01
> KISA remains the same legal entity (한국인터넷진흥원, established July 2009) under the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]]. It underwent an **internal organizational restructuring in January 2026** that created three new units: **AI보안산업본부 (AI Security Industry Division), 디지털위협예방본부 (Digital Threat Prevention Division), and 랜섬웨어대응팀 (Ransomware Response Team)** [5][6]. The restructuring is *highly likely* a response to the 2025 surge in large-scale personal-data breaches (KT, SKT, Coupang) and is aligned with the Lee Jae-myung administration's "AI 기본사회" national policy agenda. KrCERT/CC, FIRST/APCERT membership, and the international-cooperation function are preserved and strengthened.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 한국인터넷진흥원 (Korea Internet & Security Agency, KISA) — unchanged
**Parent:** [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|과학기술정보통신부]] (Ministry of Science and ICT) — unchanged
**Headquarters:** Naju, South Korea — unchanged
**Status:** **Active**, legally unchanged, internally restructured January 2026 [5][6]

The post-January 2026 structure adds an **AI보안산업본부** responsible for AI security policy, industry cultivation, R&D, and workforce development; a **디지털위협예방본부** focused on preventive digital-threat mitigation; and a dedicated **랜섬웨어대응팀** for ransomware response [5][6]. A parallel but separate MSIT-level restructuring in early 2026 also renamed MSIT's 네트워크정책실 to **정보보호네트워크정책실** and created a 5-person 사이버침해조사팀 inside MSIT [7]. These are MSIT-side changes and do not alter KISA's corporate status, but they change KISA's supervisory context.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2009-07 | **KISA established** by merging Korea Information Security Agency, NIDA, and KIICA under the Act on Internet Address Resources and related statutes | [3] |
| 2010-12 | **KrCERT/CC launched** as South Korea's national CSIRT | [2] |
| 2013 | Supervising ministry transferred from MIC/KCC to the newly created Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (미래창조과학부) | [3] |
| 2017 | Supervising ministry renamed to **과학기술정보통신부 (Ministry of Science and ICT)** after Moon administration reorganization | [3] |
| 2019 | HQ relocation to **Naju**, Jeollanam-do (Bitgaram City) completed | [3] |
| 2026-01-09 | **Internal restructuring**: AI보안산업본부, 디지털위협예방본부, 랜섬웨어대응팀 newly created; organization realigned around "AI 기본사회" policy goals | [5][6] |
| 2026 (ongoing) | MSIT-level reorganization creates 정보보호네트워크정책실 and 사이버침해조사팀; 정부 차원 정보보호 조직 근본 개편 논의 진행 중 | [7] |

## Summary

The Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA, 한국인터넷진흥원) is the sub-organization of the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]] (과학기술정보통신부) responsible for internet governance and cybersecurity of the internet in South Korea. KISA was created in **July 2009** by merging three predecessor agencies: the Korea Information Security Agency (KISA, 한국정보보호진흥원), the National Internet Development Agency (NIDA, 한국인터넷진흥원 — same Korean name, different English), and the Korean IT International Cooperation Agency (KIICA).

KISA is **not a law enforcement agency**; its role in international cooperation on cybercrime is through **incident response coordination**, **threat intelligence sharing**, and **capacity building**, rather than arrests or prosecutions. KISA operates **KrCERT/CC** (launched December 2010) as South Korea's national Computer Security Incident Response Team.

## Mandate and Authority

KISA's mandate is grounded in the Act on Promotion of Information and Communications Network Utilization and Information Protection (정보통신망법) and related statutes. Its cybercrime-relevant functions include:

- **Incident response:** KrCERT/CC coordinates response to major cyber incidents affecting Korean networks, including phishing, ransomware, DDoS, and malware campaigns.
- **Threat intelligence:** Collects, analyzes, and shares cyber threat intelligen

title: KNPA Cybercrime Enforcement Organization (경찰청 사이버범죄 수사조직)
text:
> [!warning] Status — Reorganized (as of 2023-12)
> The standalone **경찰청 사이버수사국** (KNPA Cyber Investigation Bureau) was dissolved in late 2023 and its functions were absorbed into **경찰청 국가수사본부 수사국**, with a new **사이버수사심의관 (Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer)** position created inside 수사국 at *deputy-director level* rather than bureau-director level. As of 2026-04-10 the **사이버수사국 no longer exists**; cybercrime investigation is conducted by the 사이버수사심의관 and 사이버수사과 within 수사국. A re-establishment of 사이버안전국 is under active consideration but has not yet been enacted (see [Current Status](#current-status-as-of-2026-04-10) below).
>
> This page preserves the full historical arc and current state. Operations completed before December 2023 were carried out under the 사이버수사국; operations from 2024 onward are carried out by the 수사국 사이버수사심의관 and its subordinate units.

## Summary

This page documents the **national cybercrime enforcement apparatus of the Korea National Police Agency (KNPA, 경찰청)** across three named incarnations: **사이버안전국 (Cyber Safety Bureau, ~2014–2021)**, **사이버수사국 (Cyber Investigation Bureau, 2021–2023)**, and **수사국 사이버수사심의관 (Investigation Bureau's Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer, 2023–present)**. Throughout all three iterations the unit has served as **INTERPOL NCB Korea for cyber matters** and led Korea's participation in major multilateral cybercrime operations, most notably the **HAECHI** series [1][2].

Because the operational mandate, personnel, and operational continuity have been substantially preserved across reorganizations, this page treats them as a single evolving organization rather than three separate entities.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** 경찰청 국가수사본부 수사국 — 사이버수사심의관 및 사이버수사과
**English:** KNPA National Investigation Headquarters, Investigation Bureau — Cyber Investigation Deliberation Officer and Cyber Investigation Division
**Level:** Deliberation-officer level (심의관, 경무관급), subordinate to 수사국장 (director-general of 수사국) [3]
**Parent:** 국가수사본부 (National Investigation Headquarters) → 수사국 (Investigation Bureau)

The functions previously exercised by the standalone 사이버수사국 (bureau-director level, 치안감 또는 경무관) are now exercised at deliberation-officer level within the broader 수사국. According to April 2026 press reporting, this reorganization correlated with a decline in cybercrime clearance rates (67.5% → 54.1%) and a 34.4% increase in cybercrime incidents, prompting a proposal to **re-establish a standalone 사이버안전국** [4].

**Pending re-establishment (planned, not yet in force):** The KNPA has proposed re-creating a standalone **사이버안전국 (Cyber Safety Bureau)** separated out of 수사국. The plan is subject to 행안부·기재부 협의 and the December 2026 National Assembly budget review, with implementation expected no earlier than **March 2027** if approved [4]. Under the proposal the **사이버범죄수사과** would be renamed **사이버안전과** and approximately **20 additional staff** would be added at the bureau level.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Level | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2014-08 | **사이버안전국** established under 경찰청 by elevating prior cybercrime units | 국장급 (bureau-director) | [2] |
| 2021-01-01 | **국가수사본부 (NIO)** created; 사이버안전국 absorbed into the new NIO and **renamed 사이버수사국** | 국장급 | [2][5] |
| 2021-2023 | 사이버수사국 operates at bureau level; leads HAECHI II/III/IV, Korea-China operations | 국장급 | [1] |
| 2023-12 | **사이버수사국 dissolved**; functions absorbed into 수사국; **사이버수사심의관** newly created inside 수사국 | 심의관급 (deputy-director) | [3][4] |
| 2023-2024 | Reorganization period; HAECHI V and other operations continue under the new 심의관 structure | 심의관급 | [1] |
| 2026-04 | KNPA announces plan to **re-establish 사이버안전국** (separated from 수사국) pending budget and approval | Pending | [4] |
| 2027-03 (planned, not yet in force) | **사이버안전국** expected re-establishment date if approved | 국장급 | [4] |

## Mandate and Authority

Across all three incarnations the unit's mandate has covered:
- Invest

title: Korea National Police Agency (경찰청)
text:
## Summary

The **Korea National Police Agency (경찰청, KNPA)** is the national police service of the Republic of Korea. Established in 1945, it is South Korea's primary law enforcement authority. Cybercrime investigation is handled within the **국가수사본부 (National Investigation Headquarters, NIO)**, established in 2021 as part of a major police reform.

This page is a parent-organization entry; for the operational cybercrime apparatus (currently 수사국 사이버수사심의관) see **[[knpa-cyber-bureau|KNPA Cybercrime Enforcement Organization]]**.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

- **Commissioner General**: (varies; check KNPA press releases for current holder)
- **Parent ministry**: 행정안전부 (Ministry of the Interior and Safety)
- **National Investigation Headquarters (국가수사본부)**: 3 bureaus — 수사국 (Investigation), 형사국 (Criminal), 안보수사국 (Security Investigation)
- Cybercrime investigation is currently conducted at 심의관 level within 수사국 (see [[knpa-cyber-bureau]])
- **Pending reorganization**: Re-establishment of standalone 사이버안전국 proposed, expected no earlier than 2027-03 if approved [2]

## Mandate and Authority

KNPA operates under the 경찰법 (Police Act) and 경찰공무원법 (Police Officer Act). Its mandate covers:
- General policing and investigation
- Traffic safety
- Public order
- Intelligence gathering (security-related, via 안보수사국)
- Counter-terrorism coordination
- Cybercrime investigation (via NIO)

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **국가수사본부 (NIO)** — serves as the investigation arm
  - **수사국** — general and cyber investigations (hosts [[knpa-cyber-bureau|사이버수사심의관]])
  - **형사국** — criminal and forensic investigations
  - **안보수사국** — security, counter-terrorism, technology leaks

## IC Capabilities

- **INTERPOL NCB Korea**: KNPA is the operational NCB
- **I-24/7 access**: Direct connection to INTERPOL's secure network
- **Liaison officers**: Posted to Europol, INTERPOL IGCI Singapore, and selected bilateral partners

## Key Operations and Cases

See [[knpa-cyber-bureau]] for detailed operation participation (HAECHI series, Phobos/8Base, etc.). KNPA as the parent agency hosts the operational units that execute these operations.

## Cooperation Track Record

- **INTERPOL**: Active NCB with substantial operational volume
- **Europol**: Strategic Agreement enables regular cooperation
- **FBI**: Bilateral cooperation on ransomware, DPRK cyber threats
- **China MPS, Japan NPA**: Regional bilateral cooperation

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 1945-10-21 | 경찰청 창설 |
| 1991-07-31 | 내무부 치안본부 → 경찰청으로 승격 |
| 2020 | 자치경찰제 도입 (부분) |
| 2021-01-01 | **국가수사본부 신설** — 수사 독립성 확보 개혁 |
| 2023-12 | 사이버수사국 → 수사국에 통합 (see [[knpa-cyber-bureau]]) |
| 2027-03 (planned) | 사이버안전국 재신설 예정 (pending budget approval) [2] |

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact current Commissioner General and assistant commissioners require verification against 경찰청 press releases
- The 2027 reorganization is dependent on 행안부·기재부 협의 and budget approval

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | 경찰청 공식 웹사이트 | KNPA | — | https://www.police.go.kr/ |
| [2] | 경찰청, 사이버안전국·여청안전국 신설 추진 | 뉴시스 | 2026-04-06 | https://www.newsis.com/view/NISX20260406_0003578778 |
| [3] | [[2025-12-29_korea-kr_knpa-kmsauto-lithuanian-hacker-georgia-extradition\|KNPA KMSAuto clipper extradition — Lithuanian hacker Red Notice → Georgia arrest → Korea (KR+LT+GE; 5y4m investigation)]] | 경찰청 국가수사본부 / Korea.kr | 2025-12-29 | https://www.korea.kr/briefing/pressReleaseView.do?newsId=156737083 |
| [4] | [[2026-02-19_korea-kr_knpa-cambodia-korea-task-force-redcorner-arrests\|KNPA Korea-dedicated Squad (코리아 전담반) — Cambodia bilateral op: 6 INTERPOL Red Notice fugitives + 3 Feb 2026 takedowns; ₩19B+ victim-loss attribution; Taizi compound Sihanoukville]] | Korean National Police Agency (KNPA) via 대한민국 정책브리핑 (korea.kr) | 2026-02-19 | https://www.korea.kr/briefing/pressReleaseView.do?newsId=156745543 |

title: KrCERT/CC
text:
## Summary

KrCERT/CC is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: National computer emergency response and coordination function for South Korea under KISA. Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[south-korea]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: No operation backlink is currently linked.. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. Where an operation page names multiple agencies, this organization page should be read together with the operation's lead agency, coordinating body, participating-country list, and source table.

## Source Coverage

The references below provide 1 source link(s) for the current organization record. The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

## Audit Scope

The organization audit treats a page as adequate when it explains why the entity exists in the corpus, how its source links should be interpreted, and which claims remain outside the verified record. This section is intentionally conservative: it improves navigation and reviewability without adding unsupported assertions about current staffing, statutory powers, or operational capacity.

## References

title: Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau — Cybercrime Unit
text:
## Summary

The **Lithuanian Criminal Police Bureau (Lietuvos kriminalinės policijos biuras — LKPB)** is the central criminal police authority of Lithuania, operating throughout the country on a non-territorial basis under the Police Department of the Ministry of the Interior [1][2]. It includes cybercrime investigative capability and is Lithuania's principal criminal police interface for international cybercrime cooperation.

LKPB is *highly likely* the primary Lithuanian contact for Europol EC3 cybercrime operations and for Budapest Convention Article 35 requests, though precise 24/7 contact point attribution requires verification.

## Mandate and Authority

LKPB operates with nationwide competence for serious and organized crime, including cybercrime [1][2]. It is not organized territorially but functionally, allowing specialized units (such as cybercrime) to investigate cases across Lithuania regardless of regional boundaries.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

Public information on LKPB's internal structure is limited. Confirmed details:

- Headquartered in Vilnius at Saltoniškių g. 19 [1]
- Operates specialized functional divisions, including cybercrime
- In September 2024, LKPB began an EU-funded project (Norway Grants) in partnership with the **National Cyber Security Centre (Nacionalinis kibernetinio saugumo centras — NKSC)** to create a laboratory for investigating criminal acts in cyberspace and training cybercrime investigators [3]

## IC Capabilities

- **Europol National Unit** channel (Lithuania is a Europol member state)
- **Interpol NCB Vilnius** channel
- **Budapest Convention:** Lithuania is a party; LKPB *likely* routes related cooperation
- **Joint cyber investigation lab** (under construction, funded 2024) with NKSC for digital forensics and training [3]

## Key Operations and Cases

Specific operation attributions are not confirmed in the currently ingested sources. Lithuania *likely* has participated in Europol-coordinated European cybercrime operations such as Endgame and Cronos, but attributions should be verified per operation.

## Cooperation Track Record

- EU member state since 2004; routine Europol and Eurojust cooperation
- NATO member; cyber defense cooperation through NATO channels
- Budapest Convention party
- Partner with Nordic and Baltic law enforcement on regional cyber issues

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Exact year the LKPB cybercrime unit was established
- Headcount of LKPB cybercrime investigators
- Whether LKPB holds the Lithuanian [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] Art. 35 24/7 contact point
- Participation record in named international cyber operations

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|

| [2] | Lietuvos kriminalinės policijos biuras | Policijos departamentas (LRV) | n.d. | https://policija.lrv.lt/lt/veiklos-sritys/nusikalstamu-veiklu-atskleidimas-ir-tyrimas/lietuvos-kriminalines-policijos-biuras/ |
| [3] | LKPB and NKSC joint cyber investigation laboratory project | LKPB | 2024-09 | https://lkpb.policija.lrv.lt/lt/naujienos/lietuvos-kriminalines-policijos-biuras-kartu-su-nacionaliniu-kibernetinio-saugumo-centru-prie-krasto-apsaugos-ministerijos-igyvendina-projekta-nusikalstamu-veiku-elektronineje-erdveje-tyrimui-ir-mokymams-skirtos-laboratorijos-sukurimas-ir-iveiklinimas/ |
