title: Thai Royal Police
text:
## Summary

**Thai Royal Police** is retained as an alternate-label page for the **Royal Thai Police** institution. The richer operational cyber bureau page is [[thailand-royal-police]], but this entry is useful because sources and older operation pages often use the "Thai Royal Police" wording.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Thailand appears repeatedly as an operational arrest and assistance jurisdiction. In [[rex-mundi-takedown]], Royal Thai Police arrested a French national in Thailand on a French international arrest warrant, completing the Rex Mundi extortion investigation. In [[alphabay-takedown]], Thai authorities arrested AlphaBay administrator Alexandre Cazes in Bangkok at US request, making Thailand central to the marketplace takedown's custody phase.

The official CCIB source establishes the dedicated cybercrime bureau inside the Royal Thai Police structure. More recent regional action, including [[operation-cyber-guardian]], shows Thailand participating in Asian cross-border enforcement against online child sexual exploitation.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source uses the "Thai Royal Police" label or when the question is broad Royal Thai Police participation. Use [[thailand-royal-police]] for CCIB-specific structure, current cybercrime bureau detail, and Thai online-fraud/scam enforcement capacity.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for selected operation examples and for the CCIB institutional baseline. Some older Thailand cooperation records should still be checked case by case because public sources vary between "Thai authorities," "Royal Thai Police," and CCIB-specific labels.

title: Royal Thai Police — Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau
text:
## Summary

The Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau (CCIB) of the [[thailand-royal-police|Royal Thai Police]] was established on **9 September 2020** and began operations on **22 December 2020**. It is Thailand's first dedicated national-level cybercrime police bureau and was created in response to the surge in online fraud, scam compounds, and cryptocurrency-enabled financial crime affecting Thai citizens and the region.

CCIB reportedly receives approximately **800 victim complaints per day**, the majority involving cryptocurrency-facilitated crime or money laundering. Since CCIB's inception, blockchain intelligence has played a critical role in tracing illicit financial flows — Thailand has engaged TRM Labs and other private-sector crypto intelligence providers to support investigations.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** กองบัญชาการตำรวจสืบสวนสอบสวนอาชญากรรมทางเทคโนโลยี (Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau, CCIB)
**Status:** *Active* — CCIB continues to operate under the same name and structure [1][6]
**Commissioner (as of 2025–2026):** **Pol Lt Gen Surapol Prembutr** (Commissioner of the Cyber Crime Investigation Bureau) [6]
**Parent:** [[thailand-royal-police|Royal Thai Police]]

As of April 2026 CCIB remains Thailand's lead national cybercrime bureau. Recent developments through 2025–2026:

- **Current leadership:** Pol Lt Gen **Surapol Prembutr** serves as CCIB Commissioner as of 2025–2026 [6].
- **Growing caseload:** Between **1 January and 30 September 2025**, **279,861 cybercrime petitions** were filed through the Thai Police Online platform, with reported damages of at least **28.01 billion baht** (~USD 780M). Service scams accounted for 123,865 complaints; investment scams caused the highest financial losses at **10.229 billion baht** [6].
- **2025 CIB prioritization:** The broader Central Investigation Bureau (CIB) announced in early 2025 that cybercrime and call-centre scams would be priority areas for 2025, with enhanced study of online scammer tactics [6][7].
- **Anti-scam infrastructure:** CCIB continues to partner with AIS on a scam-call alert service and operates cyber patrol task forces; the **Cyber Police Volunteers** community network supports citizen reporting [6].
- **Ongoing scam compound enforcement:** CCIB continues active enforcement against Thai-Myanmar and Thai-Cambodia border scam compound operations. 2025–2026 operations include Pattaya raids on Chinese-national-run online scam operations [8].
- **International cooperation:** CCIB *likely* continues active cooperation with the US FBI, Australian Federal Police, Chinese MPS, and the Korean KNPA on pig-butchering / romance scam / Korean-national repatriation cases from Cambodia and Myanmar scam compounds.

No structural reorganization of CCIB has been announced as of April 2026; the 2020 founding structure remains in place with a growing caseload and expanded anti-scam infrastructure.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2020-09-09 | **CCIB formally established** by Royal Thai Police | [3][4] |
| 2020-12-22 | CCIB begins operations | [4] |
| 2023 | **Emergency Decree on Measures for Prevention and Suppression of Technology Crime B.E. 2566** enacted — accelerates freezing of scam-related accounts | — |
| 2025 | CIB announces cybercrime and call-centre scams as 2025 priority; Pol Lt Gen Surapol Prembutr serves as CCIB Commissioner | [6][7] |
| 2025 (Jan–Sep) | 279,861 cybercrime complaints filed via Thai Police Online; ~28 billion baht in reported losses | [6] |

## Mandate and Authority

CCIB's authority derives from Thailand's:

- **Computer Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007)**, amended B.E. 2560 (2017) — Thailand's primary cybercrime statute
- **Criminal Procedure Code**
- **Anti-Money Laundering Act B.E. 2542 (1999)**
- **Emergency Decree on Measures for Prevention and Suppression of Technology Crime B.E. 2566 (2023)** — enacted to accelerate freezing of scam-related accounts

title: Trend Micro
text:
## Summary

**Trend Micro** is a private cybersecurity company that appears in this corpus as a threat-intelligence and malware-telemetry partner. It is especially relevant to INTERPOL-led public-private cooperation, where private-sector intelligence is converted into law-enforcement targeting, arrests, or infrastructure disruption.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Trend Micro contributed intelligence to the 2016 Nigerian BEC case and to [[operation-killer-bee]], where INTERPOL described Agent Tesla telemetry as a seed for the investigation. Trend Micro's own Killer Bee source adds vendor-side confirmation of the malware and intelligence pipeline behind the arrests.

Trend Micro also appears in [[simda-botnet-takedown]] as one of the private partners supporting the INTERPOL-coordinated botnet disruption. Later operations such as [[operation-delilah]] and [[operation-synergia-ii]] show the same pattern at larger scale: private telemetry and research are combined with INTERPOL coordination and national enforcement action.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for public-private intelligence sharing, malware telemetry, and vendor-supported cybercrime investigations. It should not be treated as a law-enforcement agency; its role is evidence development, technical analysis, and investigative lead support.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong across multiple operation types: BEC, botnet disruption, and malicious-infrastructure takedowns. More direct Trend Micro sources for every listed operation would improve source symmetry, but the current set is enough to establish recurring role and cooperation pattern.

title: Metropolitan Police Cyber Crime Unit
text:
## Summary

The **Metropolitan Police Cyber Crime Unit** is the London Metropolitan Police cybercrime entry in the corpus. Its clearest source-backed role is historical: UK Metropolitan Police support in Europol-coordinated cases such as [[operation-pleiades|Operation Pleiades]] and the [[rex-mundi-takedown|Rex Mundi takedown]].

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

Operation Pleiades shows the unit's role in a DD4BC crypto-extortion investigation, with Europol identifying the UK Metropolitan Police as a supporting agency alongside German, Australian, French, Japanese, Romanian, Swiss, and U.S. partners. The Rex Mundi source set records a UK victimization event and a cooperation chain involving London Metropolitan Police, French police, Thai police, Europol's 24/7 Operational Centre, and J-CAT.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source specifically names the Met cyber unit or London Metropolitan Police cybercrime investigators. Use [[uk-nca]] for UK national cybercrime leadership and [[uk-metropolitan-police]] for the broader police-service parent.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is operation-led and historically weighted. More official Met Police cybercrime-unit material would improve current mandate and structure.

The Metropolitan Police Service is London's police service. In this repository it mainly acts as the parent entry for London Metropolitan Police cybercrime participation references and for [[uk-met-police-cyber|Metropolitan Police Cyber Crime Unit]].

The source corpus connects the Met to cross-border cybercrime cases through Europol-coordinated operations. title: UK National Crime Agency (NCA)
text:
## Summary

The National Crime Agency (NCA) is the [[united-kingdom|United Kingdom's]] lead law enforcement agency for tackling serious and organised crime. Established on 7 October 2013, replacing the Serious Organised Crime Agency (SOCA), the NCA has a broad mandate covering cybercrime, drug trafficking, economic crime, human trafficking, and child exploitation. Its **National Cyber Crime Unit (NCCU)** is the dedicated cybercrime arm responsible for conducting and coordinating major cyber investigations.

The NCA is *highly likely* among the top three most capable national cybercrime agencies globally, alongside the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]] and comparable agencies. Its leadership of [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos Phase 1]] — the landmark disruption of the LockBit ransomware group in February 2024 — demonstrated the NCA's ability to lead complex multinational operations involving 10+ countries and coordinate with [[europol-ec3|Europol EC3]] and the FBI.

The NCA appears in selected cybercrime operation records as a lead or supporting partner and **led** Operation Cronos from the UK side.

## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Present-day name:** National Crime Agency (NCA) — National Cyber Crime Unit (NCCU)
**Status:** *Active* — NCA and NCCU continue to operate under the same names as of April 2026 [6][8]
**Parent:** Home Office (Director General reports to the Home Secretary)

As of April 2026 the NCA and its NCCU remain the UK's lead agencies for serious cybercrime. No immediate restructuring has occurred, but a significant **multi-year overhaul is under way**:

- **Independent police review:** An independent review of UK police force structures is due to report in **summer 2026**. It is expected to reshape how regional and national specialist units — including cyber and fraud teams — integrate across the 43 territorial forces and the NCA. Any resulting reorganization is *likely* to be multi-year and is unlikely to take effect before 2027 [6].
- **NCCU Crypto Cell:** The NCA established a dedicated **Crypto Cell** within the NCCU with an initial team of approximately five officers, expanding the NCA's cryptocurrency tracing and recovery capability for ransomware and fraud investigations [7].
- **Post-Brexit cooperation:** The NCA continues to work with [[europol-ec3|Europol]] through the post-Brexit UK-EU TCA arrangement rather than as an EU member state agency. Operational participation in multi-agency Europol actions (Cronos, Endgame, Phobos/8Base) has continued through 2024–2025.
- **Operational continuity:** The NCCU continues to lead UK cybercrime enforcement and participated in multiple 2024–2025 multinational operations, including Operation Cronos Phase 3, Operation Endgame Phase 2, and the Phobos/8Base crackdown.

No material change to the NCCU's name, level, or mandate is *likely* to occur before the summer 2026 police review report is published.

## Mandate and Authority

The NCA was established under the Crime and Courts Act 2013, with a mandate to:

- **Lead** the UK's fight against serious and organised crime, including cybercrime.
- **Command and coordinate** across UK law enforcement (NCA officers have jurisdiction throughout England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, with powers in Scotland by agreement).
- **Work internationally** to disrupt criminal networks that affect the UK.
- **Build intelligence** on serious and organised crime threats to inform the National Strategic Assessment.

The NCA's Director General has authority to direct UK police forces on serious and organised crime matters, though this power is exercised sparingly. The NCA reports to the Home Secretary and publishes an annual National Strategic Assessment of Serious and Organised Crime.

Key legislation underpinning NCA cyber operations includes:
- Computer Misuse Act 1990 (as amended)
- Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
- Serious Crime Act 2015
- Investigatory Powers Act 2016

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

title: Cyber Police of Ukraine — Department of Cyber Police
text:
## Summary

The **Cyber Police Department of the National Police of Ukraine** (Департамент кіберполіції Національної поліції України) was established during the 2015 Ukrainian internal affairs reform. It was formally created under Ministry of Internal Affairs **Order No. 85 of 10 November 2015** as an interregional territorial body of the Ukraine National Police and part of its criminal police [1][2].

The Cyber Police is Ukraine's primary police cybercrime authority and is *almost certainly* Ukraine's main operational partner for Europol, Interpol and Western law enforcement (FBI, NCA) on cybercrime cooperation. Since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, the unit has significantly expanded its scope to include cyber-defense support for national resilience [2][3].

## Mandate and Authority

Under Ukrainian law, the Cyber Police Department is responsible for [1][2]:

- Implementing state policy on combating cybercrime
- Organizing and carrying out operational and investigative activities
- Preventing and combating criminal offences committed with computers, computer systems, networks and telecommunication networks
- Prosecuting cybercrime perpetrators

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- Interregional territorial body within the National Police of Ukraine [1]
- Part of the criminal police (as opposed to public order police)
- Reports to the Ministry of Internal Affairs
- Includes investigators, analysts, digital forensic specialists

## IC Capabilities

- **Interpol NCB Kyiv** channel (Ukraine is an Interpol member)
- **Europol Liaison Bureau** — Ukraine has an operational agreement with Europol and has maintained a liaison presence
- **[[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]]** — Ukraine is a party (ratified 2005); the Cyber Police *likely* supports Art. 35 24/7 cooperation
- **OSCE training partnership** — documented capacity building [2]
- **Bilateral FBI, NCA, BKA cooperation** on ransomware takedowns

## Key Operations and Cases

The Ukraine Cyber Police has historically participated in major international ransomware, carding and infrastructure takedown operations as both a requesting and requested authority. Specific operation-by-operation attribution pending further source ingestion.

Since **March 2022**, by directive of the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian Armed Forces, the Cyber Police Department has also been involved in cyber defense tasks against Russian armed aggression, including [3]:

- Countering DDoS attacks
- Countering attacks against Ukrainian media organizations
- Analyzing Russian information-space activity
- Collecting cyber incident intelligence
- Supporting army and volunteer cyber operations

## Cooperation Track Record

- **Europol cooperation** — operational agreement and liaison arrangements
- **Interpol cooperation** — Project Gateway and other Interpol cyber projects
- **Budapest Convention** party since 2005
- **Council of Europe** capacity building programs (GLACY+, CyberSouth)
- **OSCE** training support [2]
- **Bilateral cooperation** with FBI, NCA, BKA, Dutch Police, and others on international ransomware and carding takedowns

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- Current Cyber Police headcount (affected by wartime conditions and personnel losses)
- Division of labor between Cyber Police and SBU (Security Service of Ukraine) cyber units
- Specific named international operations with confirmed Cyber Police participation post-2022
- How the wartime cyber-defense role interacts with criminal investigation mandate

title: United Nations General Assembly
text:
## Summary

The **United Nations General Assembly (UNGA)** is the sponsoring and adoption body for the UN cybercrime treaty process tracked in this corpus. The source set records that UNGA Resolution 74/247 created the Ad Hoc Committee process and that the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime was adopted by the General Assembly on 2024-12-24.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

UNGA's role is normative, not operational. It does not conduct arrests or investigations. Its relevance is that it supplies the political mandate for global treaty negotiation and adoption, while [[unodc]] and the treaty process handle secretariat, implementation, and future conference-of-states work.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when discussing the institutional path from UNGA resolution to the [[un-cybercrime-convention-2024|UN Cybercrime Convention]]. Use [[unodc]] for implementation support and technical assistance, and [[council-of-europe]] for the Budapest Convention ecosystem.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is treaty-centered and sufficient for the cybercrime convention process. More UNGA resolution source pages would improve procedural detail.

title: United Nations
text:
## Summary

The **United Nations (UN)** is the institutional frame for the UN cybercrime treaty ecosystem in this repository. Its concrete cybercrime relevance is handled through [[unga]] as the adopting political body, [[unodc]] as the crime-and-drugs secretariat actor, and [[un-cybercrime-convention-2024]] as the treaty record.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The UN does not function as a police agency. Its role is to create and host multilateral processes that can produce binding instruments, technical assistance, and state cooperation mechanisms. The local source set records the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime as the first globally negotiated binding treaty on cybercrime, adopted in 2024 and opened for signature in Hanoi in 2025.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for UN-level context only. For treaty provisions, status, and implementation issues, use [[un-cybercrime-convention-2024]]. For capacity-building and secretariat work, use [[unodc]]. For adoption and General Assembly mandate, use [[unga]].

## Source Coverage

Coverage is intentionally treaty-centered. Additional UN source pages may be added later for the Ad Hoc Committee, General Assembly resolutions, and conference-of-states arrangements.

title: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)
text:
## Summary

The **United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)** is the UN's lead agency for combating illicit drugs, organized crime, terrorism, and corruption. Headquartered in **Vienna, Austria**, it was established in **1997** (originally as the Office for Drug Control and Crime Prevention) through a merger of the UN International Drug Control Programme and the Crime Prevention and Criminal Justice Division; it adopted its current name in 2002.

In the cybercrime space, UNODC is *almost certainly* now the most consequential UN-level actor following the adoption of the [[un-cybercrime-convention-2024|UN Convention against Cybercrime]] in December 2024 and its opening for signature in Hanoi, Vietnam on **25–26 October 2025**, where 72 states plus the European Union signed on the opening days.

## Mandate and Authority

UNODC's cybercrime work derives from multiple overlapping mandates:

- **UN Convention against Cybercrime (adopted 2024, opened for signature 2025):** UNODC serves as the **Secretariat** for the Convention's negotiation process (Ad Hoc Committee under UNGA Res. 74/247) and for the future Conference of State Parties. The Convention is the first globally negotiated binding treaty on cybercrime.
- **UNTOC (Palermo Convention) Art. 18 and UNCAC Art. 46:** UNODC supports MLA and cooperation mechanisms under the two flagship organized crime and anti-corruption conventions.
- **Global Programme on Cybercrime (GPoC):** Delivers technical assistance and capacity building to UN Member States on cybercrime investigation, prosecution, and international cooperation.
- **Commission on Crime Prevention and Criminal Justice (CCPCJ):** The UN policy-making body on crime, served by UNODC as Secretariat.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

Within UNODC's Vienna HQ, cybercrime work is primarily located in the **Division for Treaty Affairs** (which houses the Organized Crime and Illicit Trafficking Branch and handles the UN Cybercrime Convention process) and the **Division for Operations** (which delivers the Global Programme on Cybercrime through field offices).

Key components:
- **Cybercrime and Anti-Money Laundering Section** — HQ-level policy and programme support
- **Global Programme on Cybercrime field teams** — regional hubs delivering capacity building
- **Ad Hoc Committee Secretariat** — supports the negotiation and now the implementation phase of the UN Convention
- **Regional and country offices** — UNODC operates 115+ field offices plus liaison offices in Brussels and New York

## IC Capabilities

- **Normative leadership:** Administration of the new UN Cybercrime Convention and UNTOC/UNCAC provisions relevant to electronic evidence
- **Capacity building:** Training for investigators, prosecutors, and judges; model legislation; MLA drafting support
- **24/7 Network-equivalent future role:** The UN Convention (Chapter IV) envisions a global 24/7 network with UNODC support
- **Knowledge products:** Comprehensive Study on Cybercrime (2013); training catalogues and manuals
- **Coordination platform:** Convenes the Expert Group on Cybercrime and the future Conference of State Parties

## Key Operations and Cases

UNODC is **not an operational enforcement agency**. Its impact is measured through:
- Facilitation of the UN Cybercrime Convention negotiation (concluded 2024)
- Number of countries receiving Global Programme on Cybercrime assistance
- Training delivery under GPoC to justice sector actors

## Cooperation Track Record

UNODC maintains working relationships with:
- **[[interpol-igci|INTERPOL]]** — operational cooperation and joint capacity building
- **[[europol-ec3|Europol]]** — EU–UN cooperation on organized crime and cybercrime
- **[[c-proc|Council of Europe C-PROC]]** — dual-track capacity building; some coordination tension over normative leadership post-2024
- **Private sector** — engagement with tech companies through multistakeholder processes

> [!warning] Coexistence with Budapest Convention track

title: US Defense Criminal Investigative Service
text:
## Summary

The Defense Criminal Investigative Service (DCIS) is the criminal investigative arm of the [[dod-inspector-general|Department of Defense Office of Inspector General]], established on **20 April 1981**. DCIS is a worldwide civilian federal law enforcement agency that investigates suspected criminal activities involving DoD Components and DoD contractors.

DCIS special agents investigate cybercrime affecting the US Department of Defense through the **DCIS Cyber Field Office**, whose mission is twofold: (1) investigating criminal cyber-based offences that affect DoD, specifically threats to the DoD Global Information Grid (DoDIN) and the Defense Industrial Base (DIB); and (2) providing digital media analysis and forensic support to DCIS for all investigations.

DCIS is *not* the DoD's primary counterintelligence or military counterparty for state-sponsored intrusion investigations — those roles are handled by service-branch investigative arms ([[us-ncis|NCIS]] for Navy, AFOSI for Air Force, Army CID, etc.) and intelligence community elements. DCIS focuses on **criminal** cases involving DoD systems, contractors, and personnel.

## Mandate and Authority

DCIS investigative priorities include:

- **Cybercrime** targeting DoD networks, contractors, and personnel
- **Procurement fraud** affecting DoD programs
- **Illegal technology transfer** (export control violations of dual-use and military technology)
- **Public corruption** within the DoD
- **Product substitution** in defense contracts
- **Theft of DoD property**

Authority derives from 10 U.S.C. § 1585a (DoD IG criminal investigative authority) and the Inspector General Act of 1978.

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **DCIS HQ (Alexandria, VA):** Central coordination.
- **DCIS Cyber Field Office:** Specialized cyber investigators and digital forensic examiners.
- **Field Offices:** Distributed across CONUS and select OCONUS locations.
- **DoD Cyber Crime Center (DC3) coordination:** DCIS coordinates with DC3, which provides forensic and analytic support across DoD.

## IC Capabilities

- **Defense Industrial Base focus:** DCIS investigations into cyber intrusions against defense contractors routinely involve international cooperation, particularly with allies hosting contractor operations.
- **Joint investigations with FBI:** Where DoD-related cybercrime overlaps with broader FBI investigations (e.g., Chinese APT targeting defense contractors), DCIS coordinates closely with the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI Cyber Division]].
- **Five Eyes cooperation:** DCIS leverages broader US-UK-Canada-Australia-NZ intelligence and law enforcement sharing, particularly on DIB cyber threats.
- **MLAT and letters rogatory:** Through DOJ Office of International Affairs when foreign evidence or arrests are required.

## Key Operations and Cases

DCIS has *likely* participated in multiple indictments of foreign nationals (particularly Chinese, Russian, and Iranian actors) for cyber intrusions against defense contractors. Specific DCIS attribution in public DOJ press releases is less common than FBI attribution, as DCIS often plays a supporting role.

> [!info] Specific DCIS-attributed operations should be added as sources are ingested.

## Cooperation Track Record

DCIS's cooperation track record is *likely* strong within the US interagency community (FBI, NCIS, AFOSI, DC3) and reasonable with Five Eyes defense counterparts. Direct DCIS engagement with non-ally foreign law enforcement is *unlikely* to be frequent given DoD sensitivities.

## Contradictions & Open Questions

- DCIS's role relative to the service-branch criminal investigative organizations (NCIS, AFOSI, CID) on cyber cases is sometimes unclear.
- How does DCIS coordinate with US Cyber Command and the NSA on cases that have both criminal and intelligence dimensions?
- Public reporting on DCIS cybercrime caseload and staffing is limited.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|--------|-----------|

title: U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS)
text:
## Summary

U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: Homeland security including cybercrime investigation through Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[united-states]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: [[911-s5-botnet-takedown]], [[operation-chakra-iii]], [[operation-wirewire]], [[operation-onymous]], [[operation-rewired]], [[operation-us-v-farrell-silk-road-2]], [[infraud-organization-takedown]], [[operation-lakeland-man-pleads-guilty-to-receiving-child-sex-abuse-videos-from-the-largest-darknet-child-pornography-webs]]. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. Where an operation page names multiple agencies, this organization page should be read together with the operation's lead agency, coordinating body, participating-country list, and source table.

## Source Coverage

The references below provide 3 source link(s) for the current organization record. The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

title: US Department of Justice (DOJ)
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. OIA, CCIPS, and NSD all continue to operate; however, the Criminal Division's dedicated crypto enforcement unit (**NCET**) has undergone two material reorganizations in the last 30 months (see [History and Reorganizations](#history-and-reorganizations)).

**Head of the Criminal Division:** **Matthew R. Galeotti** (since March 2025) supervises the more than 1,100 federal prosecutors and staff in the Criminal Division, including CCIPS and OIA [1].

**CCIPS (Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section):**
- Still the principal federal cybercrime prosecution coordinator; still the **Budapest Convention Art. 35 (24/7 Network) contact point** for the United States.
- In **July 2023**, CCIPS absorbed the **National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team (NCET)**, combining cryptocurrency enforcement with computer-crime expertise and roughly doubling the crypto-focused attorney headcount [2][3].
- On **7 April 2025**, deputy attorney general **Todd Blanche** issued a memo titled **"Ending Regulation by Prosecution"** that **disbanded the NCET effective immediately**. Under the memo, CCIPS retains its role advising on policy, enforcement, and training, but the specialized NCET sub-unit no longer exists as a distinct structure; cryptocurrency cases continue to be handled by CCIPS attorneys [4].

**OIA (Office of International Affairs):** Remains the central authority for all US bilateral MLATs (30+) and for extradition processing. No structural reorganization reported.

**National Security Division (NSD):** Continues to handle state-sponsored cyber espionage prosecutions (e.g., [[isoon-apt27-indictment|i-Soon/APT27]]).

**Recent priorities (2025–2026):** Shift under the current administration away from "regulation by prosecution" of digital-asset businesses and toward focus on ransomware operators, state-sponsored actors, and fraud. Continued use of sealed indictments and asset forfeiture against foreign cybercriminals.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1991 | **Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section (CCIPS)** precursor established in Criminal Division | — |
| 2021-10 | **National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team (NCET)** established as a specialized unit in the Criminal Division | [2] |
| 2023-07 | **NCET merged into CCIPS** — crypto enforcement elevated to equal status with computer crime / IP work; attorney headcount on crypto matters roughly doubled; ransomware identified as top merged-unit priority | [2][3] |
| 2025-03 | **Matthew R. Galeotti** takes over as head of the Criminal Division (supervising CCIPS and OIA) | [1] |
| 2025-04-07 | Deputy AG **Todd Blanche** issues **"Ending Regulation by Prosecution"** memo; **NCET disbanded effective immediately**. CCIPS continues to advise on crypto enforcement policy but the dedicated NCET sub-unit no longer exists | [4] |

> [!warning] Reorganization note
> The April 2025 disbanding of NCET is a **significant change in enforcement posture** but does **not** reduce CCIPS's core international cybercrime cooperation role (Budapest 24/7 contact point, MLA coordination with OIA, ransomware prosecutions). Foreign partners' working-level contacts remain the same CCIPS attorneys.

## Summary

The **US Department of Justice (DOJ)** is the central legal authority for international cybercrime cooperation involving the United States. Through its **Office of International Affairs (OIA)**, DOJ serves as the **central authority** for all US bilateral Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (30+ MLATs). Through the **Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section (CCIPS)** in the Criminal Division, DOJ leads federal cybercrime prosecution and serves as the **Budapest Convention Art. 35 contact point**.

DOJ prosecutors have brought more international cybercrime cases than *almost certainly* any other prosecution authority globally, including landmark operations such as [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operat

title: US Federal Government
text:
## Summary

**US Federal Government** is an umbrella page for cases where source material attributes an action to the US government broadly or where multiple federal agencies share the cybercrime role. It should not replace named agency pages; it is a routing page for whole-of-government actions and multi-agency federal architecture.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The US cybercrime cooperation model is distributed. [[us-doj]] and [[office-of-international-affairs]] handle prosecution, MLAT, extradition, and central-authority functions. [[fbi-cyber-division]] and IC3 provide cyber investigation and national internet-crime reporting. [[us-secret-service]] handles cyber-enabled financial crime through Cyber Fraud Task Forces. [[dea]] and [[us-postal-inspection]] are central where darknet narcotics or mailed contraband are involved.

Operation records show the whole-of-government pattern. [[bidencash-takedown]] is explicitly framed as a US government seizure involving Secret Service, FBI, DOJ, Dutch police, and private-sector support. [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]] combines DOJ, FBI, DCIS, IRS-CI, FinCEN, Eurojust, Europol, and European partners against the SocksEscort proxy service.

## IC Relevance

Use this page when a source says "US government" or when a chart needs a single umbrella node for multi-agency US federal participation. For attribution, prefer the named agency page whenever the source identifies DOJ, FBI, Secret Service, DEA, DCIS, USPIS, or another component.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for cybercrime prosecution, reporting, cyber-fraud, and selected seizure operations. It remains an umbrella page, so source coverage should grow through agency pages rather than duplicating all federal detail here.

title: NCIS
text:
## Summary

NCIS is retained as an organization node in the international cybercrime cooperation graph. The current mandate field records: Primary law enforcement and counterintelligence agency of the US Department of the Navy; cybercrime enforcement for Navy and Marine Corps networks. Country or jurisdiction metadata is recorded as [[united-states]]. This page is used for menu navigation, operation back-links, and partner analysis rather than as a complete institutional profile.

## Cooperation Graph Role

Linked operation records: [[operation-us-v-wu-haibo-isoon]]. These links are the safest basis for interpreting this organization in the repository because they connect the organization to named enforcement actions, source records, and cooperation mechanisms already present in the corpus. Where an operation page names multiple agencies, this organization page should be read together with the operation's lead agency, coordinating body, participating-country list, and source table.

## Source Coverage

The references below provide 3 source link(s) for the current organization record. The page does not infer powers, staffing, budget, or treaty authority beyond what the linked operation and source records support. When only one source is available, the record is still useful for navigation but should be treated as limited coverage until official organizational material or additional operation sources are linked.

## Data Integrity Notes

This page has been normalized for the organization audit: it includes role metadata, source links where available, references, and a clear distinction between confirmed corpus relationships and unverified institutional detail. Future edits should prefer official agency pages, prosecutor releases, court records, Europol or INTERPOL releases, and operation-specific press releases. Trade press can be used for context, but it should not override official source records for authority, participation, or legal powers.

title: US Postal Inspection Service
text:
## Summary

The **United States Postal Inspection Service (USPIS)** is the federal law-enforcement agency that connects cyber-enabled crime to the physical mail and parcel layer. In this corpus it is especially relevant to darknet drug trafficking, counterfeit pills, fentanyl distribution, and BEC/fraud operations where money mules, mailed goods, or delivery infrastructure become investigative evidence.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

USPIS appears in [[operation-wirewire]] as part of a broad US federal response to business email compromise. The FBI source describes the sweep as a multi-agency international BEC action, with Postal Inspection participation reflecting the fraud and money-movement components beyond purely technical intrusion work.

The strongest USPIS-owned sources are darknet narcotics cases. USPIS press releases confirm Postal Inspector roles in the Ogando/Dawodu/Spencer fentanyl-pill case and in the [[operation-us-v-okparaeke-dark-web|FentMaster]] investigation. Those records show why USPIS matters in cybercrime IC: the marketplace and cryptocurrency components are online, but the contraband and delivery chain require postal investigative authority.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for US postal/logistics enforcement in darknet marketplace cases, counterfeit-drug distribution, and BEC/fraud task forces. For technical intrusion or botnet work, use [[fbi-cyber-division]] or the lead investigative agency page.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for darknet narcotics and BEC participation. A direct USPIS institutional overview source would improve mandate wording; current mandate language is inferred from the agency's recurring case role in official releases.

title: US Secret Service Headquarters
text:
## Summary

**US Secret Service Headquarters** is the parent organization entry for Secret Service cyber-financial investigations. The operational cybercrime details are maintained on [[us-secret-service|US Secret Service - Cyber Fraud Task Forces]], while this page preserves the headquarters and parent-agency context.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

The Secret Service source set describes the 2020 consolidation of Electronic Crimes Task Forces and Financial Crimes Task Forces into Cyber Fraud Task Forces, reflecting the convergence of cybercrime and traditional financial crime. The cyber investigations source reinforces that the agency handles technology-enabled financial crime through that task-force model.

The [[bidencash-takedown|BidenCash takedown]] is the strongest operation-level example currently tied to Secret Service sources. The 2025 Secret Service release attributes the marketplace-domain seizure to the Secret Service Frankfurt Resident Office and Cyber Investigative Section, working with the FBI and DOJ and with international partners.

## IC Relevance

Use this page for headquarters, parent-agency, or Secret Service-wide context. Use [[us-secret-service]] for Cyber Fraud Task Forces, field offices, and specific cyber-financial investigative capacity.

## Source Coverage

Coverage is strong for the CFTF structure and one major marketplace seizure. Additional DHS and USSS headquarters pages would improve parent-organization governance detail.

title: US Secret Service — Cyber Fraud Task Forces
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active — the Cyber Fraud Task Force (CFTF) model established in July 2020 remains the operational structure, with no further material reorganization [1][2].

**Parent:** The US Secret Service remains under the **Department of Homeland Security (DHS)** after its 2003 transfer from the Treasury; this placement has not changed. Its dual protective and financial-crime missions continue, with CFTFs handling the financial-cyber investigative dimension.

**Current CFTF network:** The USSS operates **42+ domestic CFTFs** plus **overseas CFTFs / international field offices** in cities including Rome, London, Frankfurt, The Hague (Europol liaison), Hong Kong, Bangkok, and others [3]. The **Global Investigative Operations Center (GIOC)** at HQ continues to coordinate international financial cybercrime investigations.

**Recent priorities (2024–2026):** business email compromise (BEC) wire-recovery operations; cryptocurrency financial tracing; ransomware follow-the-money investigations (parallel and joint with FBI); continued presence in Europol J-CAT.

> [!note] Organizational continuity
> No structural reorganization has occurred since the July 2020 CFTF consolidation. The legacy Electronic Crimes Task Force (ECTF) and Financial Crimes Task Force (FCTF) frameworks are fully subsumed into the CFTF model (see History section below).

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1995 | First **Electronic Crimes Task Force (ECTF)** created in New York under Secret Service leadership | [2] |
| 2001 | **USA PATRIOT Act § 105** mandates nationwide ECTF network | [4] |
| 2003 | USSS transferred from US Treasury to the newly created **Department of Homeland Security** | [3] |
| 2020-07 | ECTFs and Financial Crimes Task Forces (FCTFs) **consolidated into Cyber Fraud Task Forces (CFTFs)** reflecting convergence of cyber and financial crime | [1] |
| 2020–2026 | CFTF model operational, network expanded with additional overseas offices | [3] |

## Summary

The United States Secret Service (USSS) operates the **Cyber Fraud Task Forces (CFTFs)**, announced in **July 2020** as the unified successor to the Electronic Crimes Task Forces (ECTFs, first created in New York in 1995) and Financial Crimes Task Forces (FCTFs). The merger reflects the growing convergence of cyber and traditional financial crimes.

The Secret Service holds statutory jurisdiction over a wide range of technology-based financial crimes, making it one of the two primary US federal agencies (alongside the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]]) engaged in cybercrime international cooperation.

## Mandate and Authority

Statutory authority includes:

- **18 U.S.C. § 1028** — identity theft and identification document fraud
- **18 U.S.C. § 1029** — credit card and access device fraud
- **18 U.S.C. § 1030** — computer fraud (shared with FBI)
- **18 U.S.C. § 1343** — wire fraud
- **18 U.S.C. § 1344** — bank fraud
- **USA PATRIOT Act (Section 105)** — mandated the nationwide ECTF network

USSS jurisdiction covers:

- Credit and debit card fraud
- Identity theft and false identification fraud
- Counterfeit currency and checks
- Financial institution fraud
- Telecommunications fraud
- Business email compromise (BEC)
- Ransomware with financial dimensions (shared with FBI)
- Attacks on critical financial infrastructure and payment systems

## Structure Relevant to Cybercrime IC

- **Cyber Fraud Task Forces (CFTFs):** Domestic network in major US cities, each bringing together Secret Service, state/local police, prosecutors, private industry, and academia.
- **Overseas CFTFs / International Field Offices:** Secret Service has offices in select foreign cities, including Rome, London, Frankfurt, Hong Kong, and others, providing bilateral liaison and joint investigative capacity.
- **Global Investigative Operations Center (GIOC):** Coordinates international investigations.
- **Cybercrime Investigations Division at HQ:** Central

title: US Department of the Treasury — OFAC and FinCEN (Cyber)
text:
## Current Status (as of 2026-04-10)

**Status:** Active. OFAC and FinCEN both continue to operate under the Treasury's **Office of Terrorism and Financial Intelligence (TFI)** with no structural reorganization. The most material recent change is the **delisting of Tornado Cash** in March 2025 following the Fifth Circuit's *Van Loon v. Department of the Treasury* decision (see History section and [Key Operations and Cases](#key-operations-and-cases) below) [3][5][6][7].

**Executive Order framework:** Cyber-related OFAC authority continues to derive from **EO 13694 (April 2015)** as amended by **EO 13757 (Dec 2016)**, plus IEEPA and CAATSA. These authorities have **not** been rescinded despite the Tornado Cash litigation — only the specific designation of the Tornado Cash immutable smart contracts was vacated.

**Post-Van Loon posture:** OFAC *likely* retains authority to designate **identifiable persons** behind mixer/tumbler operations (including Tornado Cash founders Roman Storm and Roman Semenov, who remain under separate criminal indictment and sanctions), but can no longer designate **immutable smart contract addresses** as "property" of a foreign national. This is *almost certainly* the most significant legal limit imposed on OFAC cyber sanctions authority since 2015 [5][6][7].

**FinCEN:** Continues Bank Secrecy Act enforcement against virtual currency businesses; remains the US representative in the Egmont Group (160+ FIUs). No structural change reported.

**Recent priorities (2025–2026):** DPRK/Lazarus Group designations; post-Van Loon recalibration of crypto-mixer enforcement strategy (shifting focus to operators/developers rather than smart-contract addresses); continued multilateral coordination with EU/UK/Canada/Australia/Japan on joint ransomware designations.

## History and Reorganizations

| Date | Event | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 2015-04 | **EO 13694** signed, establishing cyber sanctions authority under IEEPA | [1] |
| 2016-12 | **EO 13757** expands EO 13694 to election-interference actors | — |
| 2022-05-06 | **Blender.io** — first OFAC designation of a virtual currency mixer (Lazarus Group laundering) | — |
| 2022-08-08 | **Tornado Cash** designation (second mixer) — SDN list entry | [1] |
| 2023-08 | Roman Semenov (Tornado Cash co-founder) individually designated | [2] |
| 2024-11-26 | **Fifth Circuit rules in *Van Loon v. Department of the Treasury*** — holds immutable smart contracts are not "property" of a foreign national and cannot be OFAC-designated under IEEPA; remands for judgment in favor of plaintiffs | [6][7] |
| 2025-03-21 | **OFAC delists Tornado Cash** from the SDN list and removes associated smart-contract addresses; Treasury press release SB-0057 | [5][7] |
| 2025-03-24 | Plaintiffs argue case is not moot; litigation over permanent injunction continues | [5] |

> [!warning] Legal status — Tornado Cash delisting (verified 2026-04-10)
> As of 2026-04-10 the Tornado Cash smart-contract addresses **remain delisted** from the SDN list. However, Tornado Cash founders **Roman Storm** and **Roman Semenov** remain under **separate criminal indictment** (SDNY, conspiracy to commit money laundering, sanctions violations, operating unlicensed money transmitter) and individual sanctions designations, which were not affected by *Van Loon*. The *Van Loon* ruling limits OFAC's authority with respect to **code and immutable smart contracts**, not with respect to natural persons.

## Summary

The US Department of the Treasury plays a central role in international cybercrime cooperation through two distinct tools:

1. **Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)** — imposes economic sanctions against cybercriminals, state-sponsored cyber actors, and illicit virtual currency services under Executive Order 13694 (issued April 2015, amended by EO 13757 in December 2016).
2. **Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN)** — enforces Bank Secrecy Act requirements against money services businesses (including vi

title: ZIT Frankfurt
text:
## Summary

**ZIT Frankfurt** is the cybercrime-focused prosecutorial unit associated with the General Public Prosecutor's Office Frankfurt am Main in Hessen. The official Hessen source identifies ZIT as a specialized central office for internet crime, established on **1 January 2010** and later relocated to Frankfurt am Main in July 2019.

## Cybercrime Cooperation Role

ZIT is one of the core prosecutorial partners behind Germany's major cybercrime takedowns. In the IC corpus, its most visible role is as the legal and judicial counterpart to [[germany-bka|Germany's BKA]] in cases involving darknet markets, malware infrastructure, ransomware ecosystems, and cross-border server seizures.

The [[nemesis-market-takedown|Nemesis Market takedown]] is the clearest source-backed example. The BKA/ZIT release states that German and Lithuanian server infrastructure was seized in a coordinated action on 20 March 2024, with German, U.S., and Lithuanian authorities cooperating since October 2022. That release is important because it ties ZIT directly to operational judicial action rather than only to an institutional mandate.

## IC Relevance

When German cybercrime operations involve search warrants, seizure orders, forfeiture, suspect charging, or mutual legal assistance, ZIT is often the prosecutorial node that explains how BKA technical-investigative work becomes executable legal action. This page should be linked from operation pages where the source names ZIT or the Frankfurt cybercrime prosecutor as a partner.

## Source Coverage

Coverage currently combines an institutional Hessen source for mandate and establishment history with an official BKA/ZIT operation source for Nemesis. Further enrichment should add direct ZIT references from Operation Endgame and other BKA-led infrastructure cases as those source pages are normalized.

## References

title: International Cooperation on Cyber Crime - Overview
text:
# State of International Cooperation on Cyber Crime

> [!info] This page is a high-level synthesis of the entire wiki. Updated as sources are ingested.

## Project Goal

The project goal is to build a source-grounded, SNA-ready knowledge base on public international cooperation against cybercrime. The wiki prioritizes how countries, agencies, prosecutors, courts, international organizations, and private partners cooperate across borders, not threat-actor attribution for its own sake. See [[project-goal-2026-05-03]] for the operating success criteria and quality gates.

## Executive Summary

International cooperation on cybercrime has entered an *almost certainly* unprecedented period of operational intensity. The wiki now documents **256 canonical operations** across the public corpus period. It also retains 27 absorbed follow-on records for prosecution, sentencing, forfeiture, plea, and action-day traceability; those follow-on records are not counted as separate IC operations in the statistics below.

1. **Europol-coordinated** (67 operations): The largest group by count, including ransomware takedowns (Cronos, Endgame, Phobos), botnet dismantlings (Avalanche, Andromeda), forum shutdowns (Darkode, Cracked/Nulled), and financial crime operations. Key results from the most-sourced operations include 470+ servers, thousands of domains, and major cryptocurrency seizures.
2. **INTERPOL-coordinated** (36 operations): Dominant in arrest volume and geographic reach. Operations HAECHI IV/V/VI (combined: 9,000+ arrests, $1.1B+ seized), Jackal/III (~400 arrests targeting Black Axe), First Light 2024 (3,950 arrests, $257M), Serengeti (1,006 arrests), Sentinel (574 arrests), Synergia II (22,800 IPs taken down) across up to 95 countries.
3. **Eurojust-coordinated or Eurojust-supported** (14 operations): Judicial coordination for cross-border European actions, including online fraud, proxy infrastructure, cryptocurrency investment fraud, and laundering networks.
4. **DOJ/US-led** (31 canonical operations): Targeting ransomware groups, state-sponsored hacking, criminal marketplaces, botnets, and infrastructure. QakBot indictment ($24M+ seized), BlackSuit takedown (4 servers, 9 domains), i-Soon/APT27 charges (12 indicted), Infraud Organization (36 indicted, $530M losses), and 911 S5 Botnet (19M+ IPs) remain canonical US-led records.
5. **INTERPOL-AFRIPOL joint** (3 operations): Serengeti (1,006 arrests), Sentinel (574 arrests), Red Card (306 arrests) across African nations.
6. **Other bilateral, national, or unclassified coordination** (77 operations): Includes Korea-China Qingdao voice phishing, Korea-Cambodia repatriation, German-led fraud matters, and records where the coordinator is not yet normalized.

The **Europol model** involves [[europol-ec3|Europol]] as the operational coordination hub and [[eurojust|Eurojust]] as the judicial coordination body, with the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and [[uk-nca|UK NCA]] as the most consistently active national participants.

The **INTERPOL model** demonstrates unmatched global reach: [[interpol-igci|INTERPOL IGCI]] coordinated Operation Synergia II across 95 countries, First Light 2024 across 61 countries, and HAECHI V/VI across 40 countries each. The HAECHI series (now six iterations), led by [[south-korea|South Korea]], has become INTERPOL's flagship financial cybercrime enforcement program, with recoveries from HAECHI IV/V/VI alone exceeding USD 1.1 billion. The Jackal series specifically targets Black Axe BEC networks, and Operation Sentinel extended enforcement to 19 African countries.

The **DOJ-led model** leverages US multi-agency coordination (FBI, HSI, USSS, IRS-CI) combined with international partnerships for targeted enforcement actions against specific threat actors and infrastructure.

## Top 10 Operations by Significance

| Operation | Year | Coordinating Body | Key Results |
|-----------|------|-------------------|-------------|

title: Emergency Data Preservation Request (Budapest Convention Art. 29)
text:
## Summary

Emergency data preservation is the procedure used to prevent volatile electronic evidence from being deleted, overwritten, or moved before investigators can obtain it through the proper legal channel.

## Cooperation Use

Preservation is not the same as disclosure. A preservation request or order keeps data available while investigators prepare a domestic warrant, production order, MLAT request, executive-agreement request, or other lawful access process. In cross-border cases, preservation can be decisive because logs, subscriber records, cloud data, and transaction traces may have short retention periods.

## Boundary Notes

Use this page for preservation steps. Use [[electronic-evidence]] for the evidence category, [[24-7-network]] for urgent contact routing, [[mlat-process]] for formal evidence transfer, and [[cloud-act]] or [[us-uk-cloud-act-agreement]] where the source concerns a specific provider-data access framework.

## References

|---|---|---|---|---|

| [2] | [[2022-05-12_coe-int_second-additional-protocol-cets-no-224|Second Additional Protocol (CETS No. 224)]] | Council of Europe | 2022-05-12 | https://www.coe.int/en/web/cybercrime/second-additional-protocol |
| [3] | [[2026-04-29_justice-gov_cloud-act-executive-agreements|Regarding CLOUD Act Executive Agreements]] | U.S. Department of Justice | 2023-10-01 | https://www.justice.gov/es/node/1397306 |

title: Extradition Request Procedure
text:
## Summary

An extradition request is the formal procedure by which one state asks another to arrest and surrender a person for prosecution or sentence enforcement. In cybercrime, extradition is often the step that turns a public indictment into a case that can actually proceed in the charging court.

## Cooperation Use

The request must usually identify charges, facts, identity, treaty basis, and supporting judicial material. Practical issues include provisional arrest, dual criminality, nationality restrictions, specialty, human-rights objections, and local litigation. Cybercrime cases add timing pressure because suspects may move between jurisdictions and because digital evidence can be handled separately from custody.

## Boundary Notes

Use this procedure page for the request workflow. Use [[extradition]] for the broader cooperation mechanism, [[dual-criminality]] for offense matching, and [[specialty-principle]] for limits on prosecuting a surrendered person beyond the extradited offenses.

## References

| [2] | [[2024-11-18_bleepingcomputer_us-charges-phobos-ransomware-admin-after-south-korea-extradition|US charges Phobos ransomware admin after South Korea extradition]] | BleepingComputer | 2024-11-18 | https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/us-charges-phobos-ransomware-admin-after-south-korea-extradition/ |

title: Manhattan U.S. Attorney Announces The Indictment Of Ross Ulbricht, The Creator And Owner Of The “Silk Road” Website
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2014-02-04_justice-gov_manhattan-u-s-attorney-announces-the-indictment-of-ross-ulbricht.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Operator of Silk Road 2.0 Website Charged in Manhattan Federal Court
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Onymous was a joint international law enforcement operation conducted on 5-6 November 2014 by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], [[europol-ec3|Europol]], [[eurojust|Eurojust]], and the [[us-dhs|U.S. Department of Homeland Security]], targeting darknet markets and other hidden services on the Tor network. The operation resulted in the seizure of 267 .onion addresses across 27 distinct websites — including Silk Road 2.0, Cloud 9, and Hydra — the arrest of 17 individuals, and the seizure of approximately USD 1 million in Bitcoin along with cash, drugs, weapons, and computers. Blake Benthall, the alleged operator of Silk Road 2.0 (pseudonym "Defcon"), was arrested in San Francisco. The operation involved 17 European countries plus the United States and represented the largest coordinated action against Tor hidden services at the time. However, it also generated significant controversy regarding the methods used to deanonymize Tor hidden services, including questions about a possible Carnegie Mellon University research exploit.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2014-11-06_fbi-gov_operator-of-silk-road-2-0-website-charged-in-manhattan-federal-court.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Ross Ulbricht Found Guilty on All Counts
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

## Relevance to IC

title: Dridex (Bugat v5) Botnet Takeover Operation
text:
## Source Summary

The Dridex/Evil Corp disruption and prosecution was a **multi-year international law enforcement operation** targeting the Dridex banking trojan (also known as Bugat and Cridex) and its operators within the Russia-based cybercriminal organization **Evil Corp**. The operation unfolded in two major phases: (1) the **October 2015 technical disruption and arrest** of botnet administrator Andrey Ghinkul, conducted jointly by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency (NCA)]]; and (2) the **December 2019 indictment, sanctions, and reward** targeting Evil Corp leader **Maksim Yakubets** and deputy **Igor Turashev**, coordinated between the [[us-doj|US DOJ]], [[us-treasury|US Treasury (OFAC)]], and NCA.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2015-10-01_sophos-com_dridex-bugat-v5-botnet-takeover-operation.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Global Airport Action Day: 40 countries nab 193 suspects in cybercrime sting
text:
## Source Summary

The Global Airport Action Day, conducted from October 10-15, 2016, was an international operation coordinated by INTERPOL, Europol, Ameripol, and Frontex across 189 airports in 43 countries. The operation targeted airline ticket fraud perpetrated using stolen or hacked payment card information, detaining 193 suspects. The investigation revealed connections between airline ticket fraud and broader criminal activities including illegal immigration, human trafficking, drug trafficking, cybercrime, and terrorism support.

title: 'Avalanche' network dismantled in international cyber operation
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Avalanche, launched from a German investigation in 2012 and culminating on November 30, 2016, dismantled the world's largest phishing, spam, and malware distribution network known as "Avalanche." Europol, INTERPOL, FBI, and law enforcement agencies from 30+ countries, along with private cybersecurity firms, coordinated to block over 800,000 malicious domains, seize 221 servers, and arrest 5 suspects. The network had victimized users in over 180 countries through online banking hacking, phishing, spam, and malware distribution.

title: Nigerian Nationals Charged with Operating Business Compromise Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 December 2016, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Connecticut announced charges against Adeyemi Odufuye and another defendant in a business email compromise scheme targeting U.S. businesses. DOJ said a federal grand jury had returned a seven-count indictment on 20 December 2016 charging conspiracy, five wire-fraud counts, and aggravated identity theft. The subsequent DOJ record shows the case became a clear example of multinational custody and evidence work in a BEC prosecution. Odufuye was arrested in the United Kingdom in December 2016 and extradited to the United States. Stanley Nwoke was later arrested in Mauritius in May 2018, extradited to the United States on 25 May 2018, pleaded guilty in May 2019, and was sentenced in August 2019. DOJ also identified assistance from the Office of International Affairs, the UK National Crime Agency, the UK Metropolitan Police, and the Mauritius Police Force. The public case materials describe a scheme that targeted CEOs, CFOs, controllers, nonprofits, and schools across the United States through spoofed executive emails and, in some instances, malware-bearing attachments. The case is a useful court anchor for Operation WireWire because it shows how a broad BEC arrest wave translated into extradition-backed district-court outcomes.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2016-12-20_dct_odufuye-nwoke-bec-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

The FBI, German Central Criminal Investigation Office in Luneburg, Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3), the Joint Cybercrime Action Task Force (J-CAT), and Eurojust coordinated with 17 countries and private sector partners including Microsoft to neutralize the botnet. One suspect was arrested in Belarus. The operation disrupted servers and domains used for malware distribution through the Andromeda malware family.

title: Leader of International Cyber Fraud Ring Returned to United States to Face Federal Racketeering Charges
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release announcing the return of **Romeo Vasile Chita**, a Romanian national, to face a four-count federal indictment in the Northern District of Ohio (Cleveland). The release frames Chita as the leader of an international cyber fraud ring whose operations spanned **ten countries** (United States, Romania, Canada, Croatia, Latvia, Hungary, Bosnia, China, Jordan, Malaysia) and which had been running **since 2007**, causing more than USD 4 million in losses through phishing, keylogger malware, and online auction fraud. Eight co-defendants were named; two of them (Daniel Mihai Radu, Manuel Tudor) had already been extradited from Romania.

> [!note] Date provenance
> Body header dates the original announcement Tuesday, **October 9, 2018**. The OPA archived page footer reads "Updated **February 5, 2025**." The 2025-02-05 date used in this slug reflects the archived-page state at the time of ingest. Original release date is preserved in `original_release_date` and in the case page's Proceedings Timeline.

## Relevance to IC

This source is a tier-1 official record (Source Type: press release, default `reliability: high`, `credibility: confirmed` per CLAUDE.md). It is highly relevant to international cooperation on cybercrime for three reasons:

1. **Extradition cooperation with Romania.** Two co-defendants (Radu, Tudor) were extradited from Romania prior to this announcement; Chita himself was "returned to the United States" — operationally an extradition or equivalent surrender. The DOJ release explicitly thanks the Government of Romania, indicating sustained bilateral cooperation. Highly likely (>80%) the lead Romanian counterpart agency was [[romania-diicot|DIICOT]] or [[romanian-prosecution|Romanian prosecution authorities]] working with [[romania-police|Romanian Police]], although the release does not name them.
2. **Office of International Affairs as central authority.** OIA's involvement evidences formal MLA / extradition channeling rather than purely informal police-to-police cooperation — likely (55–80%) backed by the **U.S.–Romania Extradition Treaty (2007)** and the **Council of Europe Cybercrime Convention (Budapest)**, to both of which Romania and the U.S. are parties.
3. **10-country operational footprint.** Phishing infrastructure, money-mule chains, and auction-fraud monetization spanned three continents, illustrating the multi-jurisdictional evidence-collection problem typical of cyber-enabled financial fraud.

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2018-10-09_justice-gov_leader-international-cyber-fraud-ring-romeo-chita-racketeering.md`, preserving full traceability without classifying this page as a stub.

title: Irish Man Who Helped Operate The “Silk Road” Website Sentenced In Manhattan Federal Court To Over Six Years In Prison
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

## Relevance to IC

title: Europol Imminent Monitor RAT
text:

Investigators identified **14,500 buyers** of the RAT across **124 countries**.

title: Treasury Sanctions Evil Corp, the Russia-Based Cybercriminal Group Behind Dridex Malware
text:
## Source Summary

The Dridex/Evil Corp disruption and prosecution was a **multi-year international law enforcement operation** targeting the Dridex banking trojan (also known as Bugat and Cridex) and its operators within the Russia-based cybercriminal organization **Evil Corp**. The operation unfolded in two major phases: (1) the **October 2015 technical disruption and arrest** of botnet administrator Andrey Ghinkul, conducted jointly by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency (NCA)]]; and (2) the **December 2019 indictment, sanctions, and reward** targeting Evil Corp leader **Maksim Yakubets** and deputy **Igor Turashev**, coordinated between the [[us-doj|US DOJ]], [[us-treasury|US Treasury (OFAC)]], and NCA.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2019-12-05_home-treasury-gov_treasury-sanctions-evil-corp-the-russia-based-cybercriminal-group-behind-dridex.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Cyber Command, Microsoft take action against Trickbot
text:
## Source Summary

The TrickBot disruption was a multi-pronged effort in October 2020 involving both U.S. military cyber operations and a private-sector-led legal action to disable one of the world's largest and most persistent botnets. U.S. Cyber Command conducted offensive operations to interfere with TrickBot's command-and-control (C2) infrastructure, while Microsoft, acting under a court order from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, took down 120 of 128 identified TrickBot servers in the first week. The urgency was driven by concerns that TrickBot-deployed ransomware could disrupt U.S. election infrastructure ahead of the November 2020 presidential election. While the initial disruption was temporary — TrickBot operators eventually rebuilt portions of their infrastructure — the operation was followed by a sustained law enforcement campaign that produced arrests and indictments through 2024, including the arrest of developer Vladimir Dunaev in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and the indictment of nine Russian nationals.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2020-10-12_cyberscoop-com_cyber-command-microsoft-take-action-against-trickbot.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: OFFICIALS ANNOUNCE INTERNATIONAL OPERATION TARGETING TRANSNATIONAL CRIMINAL ORGANIZATION QQAAZZ THAT PROVIDED MONEY LAUNDERING SERVICES TO HIGH-LEVEL CYBERCRIMINALS
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 October 2020, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Western District of Pennsylvania announced the unsealing of an indictment charging 14 members of the transnational QQAAZZ organization. DOJ said a related 2019 indictment had charged five additional QQAAZZ members and that one more conspirator had previously been arrested by criminal complaint in the United States, bringing the total number of charged defendants to 20. DOJ described QQAAZZ as a professional money-laundering network that provided services to high-level cybercriminal groups, including actors connected to malware families such as Dridex, Trickbot, and GozNym. According to the press release and indictment summary, QQAAZZ maintained hundreds of bank accounts and shell companies across multiple countries, laundered tens of millions of dollars in stolen funds, and advertised itself on Russian-speaking cybercrime forums as a global cash-out service. The case is notable because it combined a U.S. indictment with parallel prosecutions and searches in Europe. DOJ reported more than 40 house searches in Latvia, Bulgaria, the United Kingdom, Spain, and Italy, and said prosecutions were initiated in the United States, Portugal, Spain, and the United Kingdom. The Office of International Affairs coordinated searches, arrests, extraditions, and evidence sharing.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2020-10-15_wdpa_nazarovi-qqaazz-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: DarkMarket: world's largest illegal dark web marketplace taken down
text:
## Source Summary

DarkMarket was, at the time of its shutdown on 11 January 2021, the world's largest illegal darknet marketplace by user count. The takedown was led by the cybercrime unit of the Koblenz Public Prosecutor's Office and [[germany-bka|Germany's Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA)]], with coordination through [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]]. The operation resulted in the arrest of a 34-year-old Australian national near the German-Danish border, the seizure of more than 20 servers in [[moldova|Moldova]] and [[ukraine|Ukraine]], and the recovery of intelligence on approximately 500,000 users and 2,400 vendors. The intelligence gathered from DarkMarket's infrastructure directly enabled [[operation-dark-huntor|Operation Dark HunTOR]] nine months later, which produced 150 arrests across nine countries.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2021-01-12_europol-europa-eu_darkmarket-world-s-largest-illegal-dark-web-marketplace-taken-down.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

On 27 January 2021, the Department of Justice announced the disruption of the Emotet botnet, described by Europol as "the world's most dangerous malware," in an internationally coordinated operation known as Operation LadyBird. ### Legal Action (US Component) The FBI obtained a court-authorized operation in the Middle District of North Carolina. Foreign law enforcement, working in collaboration with the FBI, replaced Emotet malware on servers in their jurisdictions with a law enforcement-created file designed to sever communication between infected computers and the botnet's operators. The remediation ran from approximately 25 January through 25 April 2021.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2021-01-27_mdnc_emotet-disruption-order.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: FBI and Global Partners Announce Results of Operation Trojan Shield
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Trojan Shield was a multi-year undercover operation in which the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] and the [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police]] helped place AN0M encrypted devices into transnational criminal networks, then used court-authorized access to those communications to support coordinated arrests and seizures. Official FBI and Europol statements describe it as the largest law-enforcement operation ever mounted against encrypted criminal communications.

## Relevance to IC

The source supports [[operation-trojan-shield]] in the `operations` corpus.

title: FBI’s Encrypted Phone Platform Infiltrated Hundreds of Criminal Syndicates; Result is Massive Worldwide Takedown
text:
## Source Summary

On 8 June 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Southern District of California announced the unsealing of a federal indictment charging 17 foreign nationals with distributing ANOM encrypted devices and services to criminal syndicates worldwide. DOJ described the indictment as the charging core of Operation Trojan Shield, the FBI-led operation in which the Bureau secretly operated the ANOM platform while collecting more than 27 million messages. According to DOJ, the defendants were charged with racketeering conspiracy for marketing and distributing thousands of encrypted communication devices to transnational criminal organizations. The same announcement linked the indictment to more than 500 arrests during the initial takedown wave and to coordinated enforcement activity across Australia, Europe, and other regions. Subsequent DOJ releases show that the criminal case matured into guilty pleas and sentencings after extraditions from multiple countries. The case is therefore especially important for international-cooperation analysis because the underlying ANOM collection depended on an international cooperation agreement with a third country for message collection and on later extradition pipelines from countries including Colombia, Finland, and the Netherlands.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2021-06-08_sdca_anom-enterprise-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.
