title: Ukrainian Cyber Criminal Extradited For Decrypting The Credentials Of Thousands Of Computers Across The World And Selling Them On A Dark Web Website
text:
## Source Summary

On 8 September 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the extradition of Glib Oleksandr Ivanov-Tolpintsev from Poland. DOJ said he was charged with conspiracy, trafficking in unauthorized access devices, and trafficking in computer passwords in a case tied to the xDedic Marketplace. According to DOJ, Ivanov-Tolpintsev used automated tools to decrypt the credentials of thousands of compromised computers around the world and sold those credentials on a dark-web marketplace. The later sentencing announcement stated that he received four years in prison and forfeited USD 82,648. The case is useful because it shows that xDedic enforcement extended to credential suppliers who monetized access at scale and were extradited from third countries.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2021-09-08_mdfl_ivanov-tolpintsev-xdedic-extradition.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Four Men Federally Charged In Transnational Wire Fraud And Identity Theft Conspiracy
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 September 2021, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the unsealing of two indictments charging Olufemi Odedeyi, Ibrahim Davies, Adetunji Adejumo, and Ibrahim Jinadu with conspiracy to commit wire fraud, theft of public money or property, and aggravated identity theft. DOJ's later xDedic roundup linked this tax-fraud conspiracy to access and PII obtained from xDedic-compromised servers. DOJ said Odedeyi and Davies were apprehended in London by the United Kingdom's National Extradition Unit and detained pending extradition to the United States. The scheme allegedly involved unauthorized access to U.S. business servers, theft of U.S. residents' identifying information, and false tax filings seeking millions of dollars in refunds. The case is important because it shows the U.K. extradition side of the xDedic-enabled tax-fraud pipeline, complementing the later U.S.-based sentencing of Adejumo and Jinadu.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2021-09-15_mdfl_odedeyi-davies-xdedic-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Department of Justice Announces Results of Operation Dark HunTor
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Dark HunTOR was a coordinated international law enforcement action announced on 26 October 2021 that resulted in the arrest of 150 suspected dark web drug traffickers across nine countries. The operation seized approximately EUR 26.7 million (~USD 31 million) in cash and cryptocurrency, 234 kg of drugs, and 45 firearms. Coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3)]] and [[eurojust|Eurojust]], Dark HunTOR is almost certainly the largest follow-on enforcement action stemming from a single dark web marketplace seizure, building directly on the January 2021 takedown of [[darkmarket-takedown|DarkMarket]].

## Relevance to IC

title: International effort takes down VPN service, VPNLab, used for criminal activity
text:
## Source Summary

On 17 January 2022, law enforcement authorities from 10 countries, coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] and supported by [[eurojust|Eurojust]], seized or disrupted the 15 servers hosting VPNLab.net, a [[bulletproof-hosting|bulletproof VPN service]] that had been used by cybercriminals to facilitate ransomware deployment, malware distribution, and other illicit activities since 2008. The operation was led by [[germany-lka|Hannover Police Department (Polizeidirektion Hannover)]] under the EMPACT security framework priority on "Cybercrime — Attacks Against Information Systems." The service had been linked to at least 150 ransomware attacks causing financial damages of at least EUR 60 million. No arrests were made during the action day, but seized customer data was expected to support follow-on investigations.

## Relevance to IC

title: Cybercrime: Aggressive malware, which targeted Android phones, successfully eliminated thanks to international cooperation involving the Swiss law enforcement authorities and other partners
text:
## Source Summary

Official Swiss federal-government press release issued on 7 June 2022 by the **Federal Office of Police (fedpol)** in cooperation with the **Office of the Attorney General of Switzerland (OAG / Bundesanwaltschaft)**, announcing that an international "day of action" coordinated by Europol — in which eleven countries participated, with the Dutch Police destroying the FluBot infrastructure in May 2022 — had successfully eliminated the FluBot Android malware. The fedpol release is mirrored on `fedpol.admin.ch` and `admin.ch/gov/en/start/documentation/media-releases.msg-id-89145.html`.

A parallel tier-1 release by **Europol** dated 1 June 2022 (`europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/takedown-of-sms-based-flubot-spyware-infecting-android-phones`) enumerates the eleven cooperating LE agencies with their national designations: Australian Federal Police; Belgian Federal Police; Finland NBI / Poliisi; Hungary NNI (Nemzeti Nyomozó Iroda); An Garda Síochána; Romanian Police (Poliția Română); Swedish Police Authority (Polisen); fedpol; Spanish Policia Nacional; Dutch Police (Politie); and the United States Secret Service. Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3) coordinated international activity; the J-CAT (Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce) hosted at Europol also supported.

## Relevance to IC

This source is a tier-1 primary record of an **eleven-country LE cooperation** against an Android-banking-malware ecosystem. It documents:

- A "day of action" coordinated by Europol EC3, with the Dutch Police technically destroying the FluBot infrastructure in May 2022.
- Switzerland's direct operational and strategic participation under OAG leadership and fedpol coordination.
- The role of national CERTs / cybersecurity centres (Switzerland NCSC), private telecommunications providers, and the digitalisation service provider Switch in the Swiss-side preliminary investigation that fed into the international action.
- The multi-jurisdictional LE roster: AU, BE, FI, HU, IE, RO, SE, CH, ES, NL, US — eleven LE jurisdictions explicitly named across the two parallel tier-1 releases.

It supports the [[flubot-takedown-2022]] operation page.

> [!note] L24 ≥2-country IC compliance
> The fedpol primary release names Europol, the Dutch police, fedpol, the OAG, NCSC, Swiss cantonal police forces, and "eleven countries"; the parallel Europol release names all eleven national LE agencies individually. Cooperating LE jurisdictions per L24 strict: AU, BE, CH, ES, FI, HU, IE, NL, RO, SE, US.

title: Eurojust coordinates action against massive investment fraud with hundreds of thousands of victims worldwide
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release dated 2022-11-10 documenting an unprecedentedly large 10-country EU-coordinated cryptocurrency-investment-fraud takedown executed over 8-9 November 2022, originating from Spanish, German, and Finnish investigative requests and operationalised through a Sweden-led JIT involving Albania, Finland, Georgia, Germany, Latvia, Spain, and Ukraine. The release establishes a multi-year (since 2018) cross-jurisdiction investigative architecture against an OCG operating dozens of call centres in several countries and hundreds of online platforms.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes the **largest single-action EU JIT-anchored cryptocurrency-investment-fraud takedown** in the wiki's pre-2025 corpus, with notable structural features:

- **Sweden-led JIT** (a structurally similar model to the [[eurojust-100m-crypto-investment-fraud-takedown-2025|2025-09 €100M Spain-Lithuania JIT crypto-fraud takedown]] and the [[eurojust-600m-crypto-money-laundering-takedown-2025|2025-11 €600M France-Belgium JIT extended to non-JIT jurisdictions]]).
- **8-country JIT scope** is the wiki's largest enumerated JIT membership for a single cryptocurrency-investment-fraud OCG.
- **Action-day-and-investigative cooperation across 10 countries**, including non-EU partners (Georgia, North Macedonia, Ukraine, Albania).
- **Bavarian Central Office for the Prosecution of Cybercrime (ZCB Bamberg)** is named for the first time in the wiki — a structural complement to the [[archetyp-market-takedown-operation-deep-sentinel-2025|Frankfurt am Main ZIT]] documented in iteration 32.
- **EUR 50 million/quarter damage scale** is the largest damages-per-quarter rate enumerated in the wiki's investment-fraud corpus.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case complements:
- [[eurojust-100m-crypto-investment-fraud-takedown-2025|Eurojust €100M crypto investment fraud takedown (Spain-Lithuania JIT, 2025-09)]]
- [[eurojust-600m-crypto-money-laundering-takedown-2025|Eurojust €600M crypto money-laundering takedown (FR-BE-CY-DE-ES, 2025-11)]]
- [[albania-austria-call-centre-investment-fraud-2026|Albania-Austria call-centre investment fraud (2026-04)]]

Together these form a 2022-2026 EU JIT-anchored crypto-investment-fraud enforcement model with Albania appearing repeatedly as either a JIT-state or action-day-state for call-centre infrastructure dismantle.

title: Moldovan National And Technical Mastermind Of xDedic Marketplace Extradited From Spain
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 November 2022, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the arrest and extradition from Spain of Alexandru Habasescu, whom DOJ described as the lead developer and technical mastermind of the xDedic Marketplace. DOJ said Habasescu was charged in a superseding indictment with conspiracy to commit access-device fraud and substantive access-device-fraud counts. The public xDedic materials connect this case directly to the January 2019 marketplace seizure. DOJ described xDedic as a dark-web platform that sold compromised server credentials and U.S. residents' personal information and said the site offered more than 700,000 compromised credentials, including at least 150,000 in the United States. According to the Department, the investigation involved cooperation with Belgium, Ukraine, Europol, the Dutch National Police, Germany's BKA, and Spain for the extradition phase. DOJ's later victim-services page states that Habasescu was ultimately sentenced to 41 months' imprisonment. The case therefore serves as a strong judicial anchor for the xDedic takedown because it links the marketplace seizure to a named administrator, extradition, conviction, and sentence.

## Relevance to IC

title: Nigerian National Sentenced To More Than Six Years In Federal Prison For International Tax Fraud Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 5 December 2022, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the sentencing of Allen Levinson, formerly known as Allen Ameh, to 78 months in prison for wire-fraud conspiracy tied to an international tax-fraud scheme. DOJ's later xDedic summary identified Levinson as a prolific xDedic Marketplace buyer. According to DOJ, Levinson purchased access to U.S.-based Certified Public Accounting firms through xDedic and used the information obtained from those servers to file hundreds of false tax returns. The scheme attempted to obtain more than USD 63 million in fraudulent refunds, and the IRS actually paid more than USD 5.5 million. Levinson was arrested in the United Kingdom in December 2020, extradited to the United States, and pleaded guilty in February 2022. The case is a strong example of how xDedic access sales translated into downstream tax and identity fraud prosecuted in U.S. federal court.

## Relevance to IC

title: Franco-Israeli gang behind EUR 38 million CEO fraud busted
text:

Europol supported the dismantling of a Franco-Israeli criminal network that employed business email compromise (BEC) and CEO fraud attacks to divert corporate payments to accounts under the threat actors' control. In one case, the fraudsters stole approximately EUR 38 million from a single company within days. The investigation, led by France, involved five action days between January 2022 and January 2023 across France and Israel. Eight suspects were arrested (six in France, two in Israel), including the group leader based in Israel. The criminal proceeds were laundered through a pre-existing money laundering scheme involving multiple bank accounts in the EU, China, and Israel. Authorities seized approximately EUR 5.5 million in total across multiple countries. - **Operation name:** (Unnamed; Franco-Israeli CEO Fraud investigation) - **Date:** Action days between January 2022 and January 2023

title: Russian Malware Developer Arrested And Extradited To The United States
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 February 2023, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the arrest and extradition of Russian national Dariy Pankov from the Republic of Georgia. DOJ said Pankov was charged with conspiracy, access-device fraud, and computer fraud in connection with his role in the xDedic Marketplace ecosystem. According to DOJ, Pankov developed a malware tool called `NLBrute`, which decrypted login credentials and enabled access to tens of thousands of compromised computers worldwide. He then sold those credentials, including through the xDedic Marketplace, and allegedly generated more than USD 358,000 in criminal proceeds. The case is important because it links the xDedic operation not only to administrators but also to a major supplier-side actor. DOJ later reported that Pankov pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 60 months' imprisonment.

title: Treasury Sanctions Russian Ransomware Actor Complicit in Attacks on Police and U.S. Critical Infrastructure (JY-1486)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury JY-1486: OFAC sanctions on Mikhail Matveev (2023-05-16)

## Bibliographic data

- **Title**: Treasury Sanctions Russian Ransomware Actor Complicit in Attacks on Police and U.S. Critical Infrastructure
- **Press release number**: JY-1486
- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Date**: 2023-05-16
- **URL**: https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy1486
- **Language**: English

## Reliability assessment

- **Reliability**: high — official Treasury press release, primary source for OFAC designation.
- **Credibility**: confirmed — designation is a self-executing administrative act announced by the issuing authority.
- **Sensitivity**: public — released by Treasury for public dissemination.

## Key claims and findings

1. OFAC designated Mikhail Pavlovich Matveev (Russian national) pursuant to **section 1(a)(ii)(C) of Executive Order 13694, as amended by E.O. 13757**, blocking all property and interests in property within U.S. jurisdiction and prohibiting U.S. persons from dealings with him. (Almost certainly accurate as a primary-source statement.)
2. On the same day, indictments against Matveev were unsealed in the **U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey** and the **U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia**. (Confirmed by Treasury; the parallel DOJ press releases are the corroborating primary sources.)
3. The **U.S. Department of State Transnational Organized Crime Rewards Program** announced an offer of up to **USD 10 million** for information leading to Matveev's arrest and/or conviction.
4. Matveev is described as "a central figure in the development and deployment of the Hive, LockBit, and Babuk ransomware variants, among others."
5. Treasury cites a 2021 Babuk affiliate attack on the police department of a major U.S. city in which personal information of officers and witnesses was stolen and posted online; Matveev publicly claimed responsibility.
6. FinCEN analysis cited in the release: 75% of ransomware-related incidents reported July–December 2021 were linked to Russia, its proxies, or persons acting on its behalf. Hive alone had targeted more than 1,500 victims in over 80 countries.

## International cooperation relevance

- The release does **not** describe extradition or arrest — Matveev remains in Russia (highly likely, given the explicit reward-for-information posture and the release's framing of Russia as a "haven for ransomware actors").
- The action illustrates a **tri-track parallel pattern** combining (a) OFAC sanctions, (b) DOJ criminal indictments in two federal districts, and (c) a State Department reward — a U.S.-unilateral toolkit deployed when MLA-based extradition from the home jurisdiction is unavailable.
- The release attributes Matveev to three separately-tracked ransomware ecosystems (Hive, LockBit, Babuk), creating cross-references to multiple subsequent international takedowns that did secure cooperation from non-Russia jurisdictions.

## Pages updated by this source

- [[treasury-matveev-russian-ransomware-sanctions-2023]] (operation page — created)

title: Closing ranks on West African organized crime: more than EUR 2 million seized in Operation Jackal
text:
## Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal from 15 to 29 May 2023, mobilizing law enforcement across 21 countries against Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups responsible for the majority of the world's cyber-enabled financial fraud. 103 arrests, 1,110 suspects identified, 208 bank accounts blocked, EUR 2.15 million seized/frozen.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Jackal
- **Timeframe:** 15-29 May 2023
- **Countries:** 21 (Argentina, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Cote d'Ivoire, France, Germany, Indonesia, Ireland, Italy, Malaysia, Netherlands, Nigeria, Portugal, South Africa, Spain, Switzerland, UAE, UK, US)
- **Arrests:** 103
- **Suspects identified:** 1,110
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 208
- **Assets seized:** EUR 2.15 million
- **Crime types:** BEC, romance scams, money laundering, credit card fraud, advance payment scams
- **Target:** Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups

## Relevance

First dedicated INTERPOL operation targeting Black Axe. 21-country scope across five continents demonstrates global nature of BEC enforcement.

title: Closing ranks on West African organized crime: more than EUR 2 million seized in Operation Jackal
text:
## Source Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal from 15 to 29 May 2023, mobilizing police forces, financial crime units, and cybercrime agencies across 21 countries to deliver a targeted strike against Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups. The operation resulted in 103 arrests, the identification of 1,110 suspects, the blocking of 208 bank accounts, and the seizure or freezing of EUR 2.15 million. Black Axe is a violent mafia-style gang renowned for cyber-enabled financial fraud, in particular business email compromise (BEC) schemes, romance scams, inheritance scams, credit card fraud, tax fraud, advance payment scams, and money laundering. - **Operation name:** Operation Jackal - **Date:** 15-29 May 2023

## Relevance to IC

title: Myanmar Rebel Offensive Helps China's Cybercrime Crackdown
text:
## Source Summary

The Myanmar-Kokang Cyber Scam Compound Crackdown was a Chinese-led cross-border law enforcement campaign, beginning in September 2023, aimed at dismantling massive cyber fraud operations based in the Kokang Self-Administered Zone (SAZ) of northern Myanmar's Shan State. The crackdown was catalyzed by China's Ministry of Public Security (MPS) issuing arrest warrants for scam kingpins and was dramatically accelerated by **Operation 1027** — a military offensive launched on October 27, 2023 by the Three Brotherhood Alliance (a coalition of ethnic armed organizations including the MNDAA) that physically overran the scam compound territories.

title: U.S.-Based Conspirators Sentenced To Prison For International Tax Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 29 November 2023, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Middle District of Florida announced the sentencing of Adetunji Adejumo and Ibrahim Jinadu for conspiracy to commit wire fraud in an international tax-fraud scheme. DOJ's xDedic roundup later linked this case to access and PII obtained from xDedic-compromised servers. According to DOJ, international conspirators obtained unauthorized access to U.S. business servers, stole U.S. residents' identifying information, and used that information to file false tax forms seeking millions of dollars in refunds. Adejumo and Jinadu, who were based in the United States, handled fraud proceeds directed to prepaid debit cards and bank accounts they controlled. DOJ reported that Adejumo received 33 months and Jinadu 27 months. The case is significant because it shows how xDedic access was converted into an organized refund-fraud pipeline involving both foreign and U.S.-based actors.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2023-11-29_mdfl_adejumo-jinadu-xdedic-sentencing.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Treasury Sanctions Mixer Used by the DPRK to Launder Stolen Virtual Currency
text:
## Source Summary

This OFAC press release (JY-1933, 2023-11-29) announces the designation of Sinbad.io, a Bitcoin-based virtual-currency mixer, as a "key money-laundering tool" of the previously-designated [[north-korea|DPRK]] Lazarus Group. The action is highly likely the most operationally significant 2023 mixer designation by OFAC because it directly ties a single laundering vehicle to three specific DPRK cryptocurrency heists with a combined ~$820 million in stolen funds: the $620M Axie Infinity heist (March 2022), the $100M Horizon Bridge heist (June 2022), and the $100M Atomic Wallet heist (June 3, 2023).

The release establishes a clear OFAC enforcement pattern against virtual-currency mixers used by Lazarus Group: Blender.io (2022-05-06, first-ever mixer designation), Tornado Cash (2022-11-08 redesignation), two OTC traders (2023-04-24), and now Sinbad.io (2023-11-29). Industry experts believe Sinbad is highly likely a successor to Blender.io, suggesting infrastructure resilience among DPRK laundering networks despite repeated designations.

The legal authorities cited are confirmed: **E.O. 13694** as amended by **E.O. 13757** (cyber-enabled activity authority) and **E.O. 13722** (DPRK government / Workers' Party authority — used because the Lazarus Group itself is a designated DPRK government instrumentality). The release does not invoke E.O. 13687 or the DPRK-specific E.O. 13810/13551 used in later 2025 DPRK financial-network designations, indicating that the cyber-enabled and DPRK-government authorities were the prosecutor's chosen frame for a mixer infrastructure target.

The release also documents a secondary use case: Sinbad is described as serving non-state cybercriminals for "sanctions evasion, drug trafficking, the purchase of child sexual abuse materials, and additional illicit sales on darknet marketplaces." This is consistent with the multi-purpose laundering profile common to Bitcoin mixers and supports reading Sinbad as both a state-sponsored-theft laundering pathway and a general criminal-utility mixer.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — tier-1 OFAC primary release on Treasury Newsroom, fetched and verified via curl_cffi.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — official OFAC determination with named legal authorities (E.O. 13694/13757/13722), specific theft dollar figures matched to public on-chain heist values, and named DPRK actor (Lazarus Group).
- **Sensitivity: public** — Treasury Newsroom publication; SDN List entry public.

title: USD 300 million seized and 3,500 suspects arrested in international financial crime operation - Operation HAECHI IV
text:
## Summary

Operation HAECHI IV, the fourth iteration of Korea-initiated HAECHI series, ran July to December 2023 across 34 countries. ~3,500 arrests, USD 300 million seized ($199M fiat, $101M virtual assets), 82,112 bank accounts blocked. Targeted voice phishing, romance scams, investment fraud, BEC, e-commerce fraud and more. 260% arrest increase over HAECHI III.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation HAECHI IV
- **Timeframe:** July – December 2023
- **Countries:** 34
- **Arrests:** ~3,500
- **Assets seized:** USD 300 million (USD 199M fiat + USD 101M virtual)
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 82,112
- **Crime types:** Voice phishing, romance scams, online sextortion, investment fraud, BEC, e-commerce fraud, money laundering
- **Emerging threats:** AI-generated voice cloning, fraudulent NFTs

## Relevance

Fourth HAECHI iteration showing massive scale increase. Cross-validated against HAECHI V data: consistent series growth (34 countries/3,500 arrests → 40 countries/5,500+ arrests).

title: Russian TrickBot malware dev sentenced to 64 months in prison
text:
## Source Summary

The TrickBot disruption was a multi-pronged effort in October 2020 involving both U.S. military cyber operations and a private-sector-led legal action to disable one of the world's largest and most persistent botnets. U.S. Cyber Command conducted offensive operations to interfere with TrickBot's command-and-control (C2) infrastructure, while Microsoft, acting under a court order from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, took down 120 of 128 identified TrickBot servers in the first week. The urgency was driven by concerns that TrickBot-deployed ransomware could disrupt U.S. election infrastructure ahead of the November 2020 presidential election. While the initial disruption was temporary — TrickBot operators eventually rebuilt portions of their infrastructure — the operation was followed by a sustained law enforcement campaign that produced arrests and indictments through 2024, including the arrest of developer Vladimir Dunaev in [[south-korea|South Korea]] and the indictment of nine Russian nationals.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2024-01-01_bleepingcomputer-com_russian-trickbot-malware-dev-sentenced-to-64-months-in-prison.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Joint cybercrime operation targets Trojan software distributor (AFP — Warzone RAT)
text:
## Summary

Australian Federal Police media release announcing the arrest in Malta of a 27-year-old man identified as a key promoter of the Warzone Remote Access Trojan, following intelligence provided by the AFP to international partners (Europol and FBI). The operation was coordinated with the U.S. Department of Justice's parallel domain-seizure and indictment action announced four days earlier (DOJ Press Release 24-163, 9 February 2024), which charged Daniel Meli (Malta) and Prince Onyeoziri Odinakachi (Nigeria) and listed acknowledgments to law enforcement in Australia, Canada, Croatia, Finland, Germany (Saxony LKA), Japan, Malta, Netherlands, Nigeria, and Romania.

This is the first AFP-published source page in the wiki documenting this operation; the entity is being introduced as a fresh tier-1 primary source for the Warzone RAT takedown.

## Provenance and reliability

Tier-1 primary press release issued by the Australian Federal Police via its official Media Release channel (afp.gov.au). Reliability rated **high** as a national law-enforcement statement on its own operational role. Credibility rated **probably-true** pending judicial outcome — indictments are allegations until proven; the AFP statement uses "alleged" and "will be alleged" consistently.

The AFP release is corroborated by:

- DOJ press release 24-163 (2024-02-09), `justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/international-cybercrime-malware-service-dismantled-federal-authorities-key-malware-sales` — provides defendant names, indictment districts, charging dates, domain list, and the full participating-agency roster.
- Court filings: Northern District of Georgia indictment (Meli, 12 Dec 2023, four counts); District of Massachusetts indictment (Odinakachi, 30 Jan 2024, conspiracy to commit multiple computer intrusion offenses).

## Sensitivity

Public. No restricted operational details.

## Translation note

Source language is English; no translation required.

title: Law enforcement disrupt world's biggest ransomware operation - Operation Cronos (LockBit)
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing the disruption of the LockBit ransomware operation through **Operation Cronos Phase 1** on 20 February 2024. This was a multi-country enforcement action involving 10 countries and 12 agencies, led by the [[uk-nca|UK National Crime Agency]] and coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] and [[eurojust|Eurojust]].

The source provides detailed operational metrics including arrests, server seizures, indictments, cryptocurrency account freezes, and the release of free decryption tools. It describes the coordination mechanisms used (SIENA messaging, operational meetings, technical sprints) and lists all participating agencies.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Cross-validated against Phase 3 (Oct 2024); no contradictions |

## Key Metrics

- **Arrests:** 2 (Poland, Ukraine)
- **Servers seized:** 34
- **Indictments:** 5 (French and US authorities)
- **Cryptocurrency accounts frozen:** 200+
- **Participating countries:** 10 (+ 4 supporting)
- **Participating agencies:** 12

title: Law enforcement disrupt world's biggest ransomware operation - Operation Cronos (LockBit)
text:
## Source Summary

On 20 February 2024, law enforcement agencies from 10 countries disrupted the LockBit ransomware operation in a joint action known as **Operation Cronos**, led by the UK's National Crime Agency and coordinated by Europol and Eurojust. LockBit had been the most deployed ransomware variant worldwide since 2022, having emerged in late 2019. The operation resulted in the seizure of 34 servers across 8 countries, 2 arrests, 5 indictments, the freezing of over 200 cryptocurrency accounts, and the identification of 14,000 rogue accounts for removal. Free decryption tools were made available via the "No More Ransom" portal. - **Operation name:** Operation Cronos - **Date:** 20 February 2024

## Relevance to IC

title: International investigation disrupts phishing-as-a-service platform LabHost
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2024-04-18 announcing the disruption of the LabHost phishing-as-a-service platform during 14-17 April 2024 international action days. The release establishes a 19-country joint cooperation pattern led by the UK Met Police and supported by Europol EC3 + J-CAT, with action-phase Europol specialist deployment to the Dutch National Police. Names every participating jurisdiction and its lead authority.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes the most fully-articulated 19-country action-day enumeration in the wiki for a phishing-as-a-service takedown — covering EU member states, EFTA partners, Anglosphere Five Eyes, and the Pacific. Notable features:

- **UK-led + Europol EC3 + J-CAT-hosted** investigation with action-phase specialist deployment to the Netherlands.
- **Five Eyes participation outside the EU** (US, UK, Canada, Australia, NZ) co-acting with EU member states under Europol J-CAT umbrella.
- **PhaaS commoditisation** of phishing — $249/month subscription, 170+ fake-website templates, LabRat 2FA-capture campaign-management tool.
- **September 2023 → April 2024** investigation timeline (~8 months).

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case fills the 2024-Q2 PhaaS gap in the wiki and complements:
- [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos / LockBit]] (2024, ransomware-as-a-service takedown)
- [[2bagoldmule-qqaazz|2BAGoldMule / QQAAZZ]] (2020 money-mule network)
- [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] series (2024-2025 botnet / loader / RAT / infostealer takedowns)
- [[latvia-sim-box-cybercrime-as-a-service-takedown-2025|Latvia SIM-box CaaS takedown]] (2025)

The 19-country LabHost roster also provides one of the longest single-operation jurisdiction enumerations in the wiki corpus.

title: Victims also from Slovenia. Europol's international operation Pandora shuts down 12 phone fraud call centres
text:
## Source Summary

Slovenska policija (Slovenian Police, Ministry of the Interior of the Republic of Slovenia) press release dated 2024-05-03, English-edition news archive, reporting on Slovenian participation in Europol-supported **Operation PANDORA**. The release confirms (a) that Slovenia was a victim country in the operation; (b) that the Slovenian police "took an active part in the operation Pandora as well, as there were also victims from the Slovenian territory"; (c) that the case turned on **months of intensive cooperation, in particular with the German law enforcement authorities**; (d) that since July 2023, Slovenian police had detected more than 40 cases of telephone-fraud calls under the guise of bank technical assistance, mainly targeting **legal persons (companies)**, with total damage of around **EUR 3.8 million** and **stolen funds flowing into bank accounts in Germany**; and (e) that International cooperation and prompt reaction by law enforcement working with banks were crucial to preventing transfers to **money-mule accounts abroad**. The release links explicitly to the Europol primary release of 2 May 2024 announcing that Operation PANDORA shut down 12 phone-fraud call centres on the **18 April 2024 action day**, with **21 persons taken into custody** and **39 suspects** identified, in raids by **German, Albanian, Bosnian-Herzegovinian, Kosovar* and Lebanese police forces** (Kosovo asterisk per UNSCR 1244/1999). The Europol record names the lead investigating units as the **Baden-Württemberg State Criminal Police Office (Landeskriminalamt Baden-Württemberg)**, supported by the **Federal Criminal Police Office (Bundeskriminalamt)**, with prosecutorial supervision by the **Freiburg Public Prosecutor's Office (Staatsanwaltschaft Freiburg)** and later the **Karlsruhe Public Prosecutor's Office (Generalstaatsanwaltschaft Karlsruhe)**, and notes specialisation by country (Bosnia-Herzegovina – debt-collection fraud; Kosovo – online banking fraud; Albania – investment fraud; Lebanon – prepaid-card fraud).

## Relevance to IC

Tier-1 primary source from a national police authority documenting the **victim-state perspective** of an Europol-supported, German-led multi-country call-centre takedown. Adds three IC-specific facts not visible from the Europol release alone: (1) Slovenia as an additional **victim jurisdiction** beyond the five Europol "participating countries"; (2) a **bilateral Slovenia–Germany police-to-police cooperation arc** that ran for "months" before the action day, with Slovenian police preventing "hundreds of thousands of euros" in losses to Slovenian citizens; (3) the **money-laundering vector**: stolen funds from Slovenian victims flowed into bank accounts in Germany, structurally connecting the call-centre takedown to a parallel money-mule layer. The release also independently corroborates the Europol-reported call-centre typology (technical-assistance impersonation of bank employees) from the Slovenian-victim angle. South Korea is not named in this release; no Korean nexus reported.

title: U.S. Charges Russian National with Developing and Operating Lockbit Ransomware
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ District of New Jersey press release (dated 2024-05-07) unsealing a 26-count federal indictment against Dmitry Yuryevich Khoroshev — alias LockBitSupp / LockBit / putinkrab; 31, of Voronezh, Russia — as the developer and administrator of the LockBit ransomware group from approximately September 2019 through May 2024. The release describes LockBit as having attacked more than 2,500 victims in at least 120 countries (1,800 in the United States), with at least USD 500 million in ransom proceeds and billions of dollars in broader losses, and Khoroshev personally receiving at least USD 100 million through his 20% developer-share of LockBit ransom payments. The DOJ press release frames the indictment as a follow-on to the February 2024 NCA-led disruption of LockBit and is announced concurrently with (a) a US Treasury (OFAC) sanctions designation, (b) a US Department of State reward of up to USD 10 million for information leading to Khoroshev's apprehension or conviction, and (c) parallel UK (OFSI) and Australian (DFAT, AFP) sanctions actions.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for [[operation-cronos-phase2|Operation Cronos Phase 2]], the May 2024 attribution and prosecution wave of the multi-phase Operation Cronos against LockBit. It supplies the indictment count (26), the named jurisdictions cooperating on concurrent sanctions/charges (US, UK, Australia), the LockBit cohort scale (2,500+ victims / 120+ countries / 1,800 US victims / $500M+ ransom / $100M+ to Khoroshev personally), and the cooperation lineage with [[operation-cronos-phase1|Phase 1 (February 2024)]] disruption. It is the connective record between the February 2024 NCA-led infrastructure disruption (Phase 1) and the October 2024 NCA-led arrests/sanctions wave ([[operation-cronos-phase3|Phase 3]]).

## Note on text fidelity

The DOJ DNJ press release was retrieved via the project's TLS-spoofing fetch tool (`tools/web_verify_tls.py`) and the raw page preserves the body text verbatim. Quotes from named officials and the verbatim victim/proceeds narrative paragraph are captured as block quotes in the raw page.

title: Take down of Austrian-based fraud scheme offering new type of crypto coin
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release dated **08 May 2024** announcing a coordinated takedown of an Austrian-based cryptocurrency exit-scam scheme that had operated between December 2017 and February 2018. National authorities in **Austria, Cyprus, and the Czech Republic**, supported by **Eurojust** and **Europol**, executed **six arrests** of the six main Austrian suspects and **six location searches**, freezing or seizing **EUR 750,000 in assets**, a **property worth EUR 1.4 million**, and **two cars**. The scheme defrauded investors of approximately **EUR 6 million**.

Eurojust enumerates the participating authorities verbatim:

- Austria: Wirtschafts- und Korruptionsstaatsanwaltschaft (WKStA, Central Public Prosecutor's Office for Economic Crimes and Corruption); Bundeskriminalamt (Criminal Intelligence Service Austria) – Cybercrime Competence Center (C4).
- Cyprus: Cyprus Police (per the Europol parallel release, specifically Cyprus Police in Larnaca).
- Czech Republic: National Organised Crime Agency (per Europol, Národní centrála proti organizovanému zločinu – NCOZ, Police of the Czech Republic).

The cooperation mechanism set is explicit: **Eurojust coordination centre** for real-time judicial communication, **Europol Virtual Command Post** at Eurojust, **rapid execution of European Arrest Warrants and search warrants**, **five Europol operational meetings**, and **deployment of a Europol specialist with mobile office to Cyprus** plus **on-site Austrian police presence in Cyprus** during the action-day search and main-suspect interrogation.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the 2024 Operation LoopX takedown — a small but useful **multi-year exit-scam prosecution case study** for cooperation between an Austrian lead agency (BK/C4 + WKStA) and two host jurisdictions (Cyprus, Czech Republic) where suspects and assets were located. The case illustrates:

1. **EAW + EIO + Eurojust coordination** as the dominant judicial cooperation toolset for intra-EU multi-jurisdictional cryptocurrency fraud prosecutions, distinct from the more recent JIT-led operations (e.g., [[latvia-sim-box-cybercrime-as-a-service-takedown-2025]] = Operation SIMCartel JIT Austria/Estonia/Latvia/Finland 2025).
2. **Cryptocurrency seizure on host jurisdiction** — Austrian officers travelled to Cyprus to advise local police on crypto-seizure procedure, a recurring IC capacity-building motif.
3. **Cold-case multi-year cybercrime prosecution** — the underlying 2017-2018 ICO exit scam was prosecuted six years later, showing that crypto-fraud international prosecutions can succeed long after the original criminal activity.
4. **Cross-jurisdiction concentration on Austrian-based scheme** — all suspects are Austrian nationals, but the investigation footprint extends to Cyprus (Europol mobile office, Austrian police presence) and the Czech Republic (NCOZ search support).

The source pairs naturally with [[latvia-sim-box-cybercrime-as-a-service-takedown-2025]] for JIT-vs-Eurojust-coordination comparison and with other Eurojust-coordinated cryptocurrency-investment-fraud cases in the corpus for trend analysis on EU-internal crypto-fraud takedowns.

## Note on text fidelity

Eurojust article body extracted via curl_cffi (chrome120 TLS impersonation) + BeautifulSoup `<article>` extraction. Cross-referenced with Europol newsroom parallel release at https://www.europol.europa.eu/media-press/newsroom/news/austrian-scammers-escape-investors-not-law-enforcement (also dated 2024-05-08; published epoch 1715151566 → 2024-05-08T06:59:26Z) for additional facts (six house searches; six Austrian suspects; EUR 500,000 crypto + EUR 250,000 fiat seizure breakdown; main-suspect interrogation in Cyprus). Eurojust press release records "08 May 2024" as the publication date.

title: Largest ever operation against botnets hits dropper malware ecosystem - Operation Endgame
text:
## Summary

Conducted between 27 and 29 May 2024 and coordinated from [[europol-ec3|Europol]]'s headquarters, the operation targeted dropper malware infrastructure that enabled ransomware deployment.

The source describes the coordination model (command post with 20+ officers from 4 countries at Europol HQ), lists all participating and supporting countries, and details the operational results. It confirms the link between dropper malware infrastructure and at least 15 ransomware groups.

text:
## Source Summary

Ransomware is *almost certainly* the single most significant cybercrime type driving international cooperation in the 2020s. Ransomware attacks encrypt victims' data and demand payment (typically in cryptocurrency) for decryption keys. The Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) model, where developers provide tools to affiliates in exchange for a share of ransom payments, has created a complex criminal ecosystem that spans multiple jurisdictions and requires coordinated international responses.

title: USD 257 million seized in global police crackdown against online scams - Operation First Light 2024
text:
## Summary

Operation First Light 2024 was a coordinated global crackdown on online scams from March to May 2024 across 61 countries. 3,950 arrests, 14,643 additional suspects identified, USD 257 million seized, 6,745 bank accounts frozen. Bolstered by AFRIPOL, ASEANAPOL, GCCPOL, and Europol. Utilized INTERPOL's I-GRIP mechanism for cross-border fund recovery.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation First Light 2024
- **Timeframe:** March – May 2024
- **Countries:** 61
- **Arrests:** 3,950
- **Additional suspects:** 14,643
- **Assets seized:** USD 257 million ($135M fiat, $2M crypto, ~$120M physical assets)
- **Bank accounts frozen:** 6,745
- **Crime types:** Phishing, investment fraud, fake online shopping, romance scams, impersonation scams

## Relevance

Part of longstanding First Light series. 61-country scope is one of the broadest IC operations. I-GRIP mechanism demonstrated for rapid cross-border fund interception. Namibia trafficking rescue highlights intersection of online scams and forced labor.

title: Texas Man Sentenced to 9 Months in Federal Prison for Operating Website that Offered Computer Attack Services
text:
## Source Summary

On 15 July 2024, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Central District of California announced that Jeremiah Sam Evans Miller had been sentenced to nine months in federal prison for operating the DDoS-for-hire service RoyalStresser.com, previously known as Supremesecurityteam.com. Miller had pleaded guilty on 6 April 2023 to conspiracy and substantive Computer Fraud and Abuse Act charges. The case formed part of Operation PowerOFF, the multinational campaign against booter and stresser services. DOJ stated that RoyalStresser was one of the services targeted in the December 2022 enforcement wave in which prosecutors in Los Angeles and Alaska charged six defendants and authorities seized 48 related domains. Although the prosecution itself was domestic, the case is highly relevant to international-cooperation analysis because DOJ expressly placed it within Operation PowerOFF, an operation involving the FBI, UK National Crime Agency, Dutch Police, and other foreign partners. The public record therefore supports the inference that Miller's case was one prosecutorial output of a broader coordinated cross-border infrastructure-disruption effort.

## Relevance to IC

title: Two Foreign Nationals Plead Guilty to Participation in LockBit Ransomware Group
text:
## Source Summary

On 18 July 2024, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of New Jersey announced that Ruslan Magomedovich Astamirov and Mikhail Vasiliev had pleaded guilty in Newark federal court to participation in the LockBit ransomware group. DOJ stated that Astamirov pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit computer fraud and abuse and conspiracy to commit wire fraud, while Vasiliev pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit computer fraud and abuse, intentional damage to a protected computer, transmission of a threat, and conspiracy to commit wire fraud. DOJ linked the guilty pleas directly to the February 2024 international disruption of LockBit under Operation Cronos. The release stated that LockBit had attacked more than 2,500 victims in at least 120 countries and that Astamirov alone derived at least USD 1.9 million in ransom payments. Vasiliev was alleged to have deployed LockBit against victims in the United States, the United Kingdom, and Switzerland and was arrested in Canada before extradition to the United States. The case is a strong operation-to-case bridge for Operation Cronos because it turns the multinational infrastructure disruption into individualized guilty pleas, extradition history, and forfeiture admissions. It also makes the international dimension unusually explicit by naming foreign victim jurisdictions and foreign arrest cooperation.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-07-18_nj_lockbit-astamirov-vasiliev-pleas.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

DOJ stated that Omotosho and his coconspirators purchased stolen access credentials and PII on xDedic and then used that data to run multiple fraud schemes. According to DOJ, the Omotosho-led group committed at least six different fraudulent schemes totaling more than USD 7.5 million in intended losses and more than USD 2 million in actual losses. The court ordered restitution of USD 2,051,108.70. The case is one of the strongest downstream-harm examples in the xDedic ecosystem because it ties marketplace purchases to a broad bundle of real-world frauds and a substantial prison term.

title: Department Disrupts North Korean Remote IT Worker Fraud Schemes Through Charges and Arrest of Nashville Facilitator
text:
## Summary

Authoritative tier-1 primary source: 2024-08-08 USAO-MDTN press release announcing the indictment and arrest of **Matthew Isaac Knoot** (Nashville, Tennessee) for hosting a "laptop farm" enabling DPRK remote IT workers (operating from China) to fraudulently obtain employment with U.S. and U.K. companies under a stolen U.S. identity ("Andrew M.").

The press release establishes a public-record legal anchor for the DOJ-wide **DPRK RevGen: Domestic Enabler Initiative** (launched March 2024 by NSD + FBI Cyber + FBI Counterintelligence), and references the **October 2023 joint U.S.-Republic of Korea guidance** and the **May 2022 tri-seal advisory** (FBI + Treasury + State) as the multi-agency cooperation backbone against DPRK IT-worker fraud.

## Verification Note

> [!warning] Slug-content alignment note (L1)
> Per the file slug, the user expected this 2024-08-08 source to bundle Knoot + Chapman (Arizona) + Didenko (Ukrainian, extradited from Poland). The actual primary source is a single-defendant USAO-MDTN press release naming only Knoot. Chapman and Didenko are separate **May 2024** prior actions referenced via hyperlink and have not been ingested as standalone sources here. The operation page derived from this source asserts only what is verifiable in the 2024-08-08 release (per L17). Future ingest of the May 2024 Chapman and Didenko USAO-DAZ / SDNY releases should be filed under their own dated source slugs.

## Key Cooperation Partners Explicitly Named

| Partner | Role |
|---|---|
| US Department of Justice (DOJ) | Department-wide initiative coordinator |
| US Department of Justice — National Security Division (NSD) — Cyber Section | Prosecuting trial counsel (Greg Nicosia) |
| US Attorney's Office, Middle District of Tennessee (USAO-MDTN) | Lead prosecuting USAO (AUSA Josh Kurtzman) |
| FBI Memphis Field Office, Nashville Resident Agency | Investigating field office |
| FBI Cyber Division | Initiative co-author (AD Bryan Vorndran statement) |
| FBI Counterintelligence Division | Initiative co-author |
| US Department of the Treasury | Co-author of May 2022 tri-seal advisory |
| US Department of State | Co-author of May 2022 tri-seal advisory |
| Republic of Korea (South Korea) | Co-author of October 2023 updated joint guidance with the United States |

## Countries Explicitly Named

| Country | Role in source |
|---|---|
| United States | Lead jurisdiction, victim companies (media/tech/finance), facilitator residence |
| North Korea (DPRK) | Threat-source state; Ministry of Defense designated; UN-prohibited WMD programs |
| China | Operating geography for DPRK IT workers; non-US laundering accounts |
| Russia | Secondary operating geography ("primarily in China and Russia") |
| Republic of Korea (South Korea) | Cooperating state via October 2023 joint guidance |
| United Kingdom | Victim country ("American and British companies") — NOT named as cooperating LE |

title: INTERPOL operation strikes major blow against West African financial crime - Operation Jackal III
text:
## Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal III from 10 April to 3 July 2024 across 21 countries on five continents, targeting online financial fraud and the West African organized crime syndicates behind it, particularly Black Axe. Approximately 300 arrests, 400+ additional suspects identified, 720+ bank accounts blocked, USD 3 million seized.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Jackal III
- **Timeframe:** 10 April – 3 July 2024
- **Countries:** 21
- **Arrests:** ~300
- **Additional suspects:** 400+
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 720+
- **Assets seized:** USD 3 million
- **Crime types:** BEC, online financial fraud, money laundering, counterfeiting
- **Target:** Black Axe and similar West African organized crime groups

## Relevance

Significant escalation from Jackal (103 arrests to ~300). Five-continent scope demonstrates global reach of BEC enforcement. Argentina 5-year investigation shows intersection of BEC with broader financial crime.

## Source Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Jackal III from 10 April to 3 July 2024 across 21 countries on five continents, targeting online financial fraud and the West African organized crime syndicates behind it, particularly Black Axe. The operation resulted in approximately 300 arrests, the identification of over 400 additional suspects, the blocking of more than 720 bank accounts, and the seizure of assets worth USD 3 million. Multiple criminal networks were dismantled worldwide. Black Axe and similar groups are considered responsible for the majority of the world's cyber-enabled financial fraud, with business email compromise (BEC) as a primary crime type. - **Operation name:** Operation Jackal III - **Date:** 10 April - 3 July 2024

## Relevance to IC

title: CBI Operation Chakra-III Dismantles Virtual Asset Network
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Chakra-III was the third phase of India's Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) ongoing multi-phase campaign against transnational cyber-enabled financial crime. Launched in July 2024 with case registration RC2312024S0007, the operation targeted tech-support scam call centers operating from multiple Indian cities — principally Gurugram, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad, and Visakhapatnam — that defrauded victims in the United States and other countries. The CBI collaborated with the FBI, Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), and [[interpol|INTERPOL]], resulting in 43 arrests and the seizure of substantial evidence including gold bullion, cash, luxury vehicles, and over 950 electronic devices.

title: Justice Department Disrupts Covert Russian Government-Sponsored Foreign Malign Influence Operation Targeting Audiences in the United States and Elsewhere
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release, dated 2024-09-04, announcing (a) seizure of 32 internet domains used in Russia's Doppelganger covert foreign malign influence operation, and (b) the unsealing of an SDNY indictment of two RT employees for funneling ~$9.7M to Tennessee-based Tenet Media to publish covert pro-Russian content. Treasury OFAC issued parallel designations the same day. The DOJ release names no foreign cooperation partner.

## Relevance to IC

This source documents [[us-doj-doppelganger-russian-influence-op-domain-seizures-2024]]. It is a borderline IC-scope source: the underlying conduct is transnational (Russian state-directed influence affecting U.S. and "elsewhere"), and the indicted defendants are Russian nationals beyond U.S. arrest reach, but no foreign LE counterpart is credited. The release is preserved for the wiki because it illustrates the U.S. pattern of substituting **unilateral domain seizure + sanctions** for cooperation pathways that are unavailable when the host state (Russia) is uncooperative — a useful comparison for IC effectiveness analysis.

> [!note] Fetch note
> Primary justice.gov URL returned HTTP 403 (Akamai bot challenge). Content cross-validated against globalsecurity.org full mirror, Lawfare, The Record, The Hacker News, JURIST, NPR. All material facts triangulated across at least two independent sources. Per L11 / L21, 403 is not equivalent to URL-dead.

title: Treasury Takes Action as Part of a U.S. Government Response to Russia's Foreign Malign Influence Operations (JY-2559)
text:
## Source Summary

U.S. Treasury OFAC press release **JY-2559**, dated **2024-09-04**, announcing the designation of **10 Russian individuals and 2 Russian entities** under **Executive Order 14024** (Russia harmful foreign activities; IEEPA) as the financial-sanctions component of a coordinated U.S. government response to Moscow's foreign malign influence operations targeting the 2024 U.S. presidential election. The designations target two clusters: (1) **RT executives** (Simonyan and seven others) plus (2) **ANO Dialog / ANO Dialog Regions** and their Director General Tabak, identified by Treasury as **linked to the Doppelgänger influence network and operators of the "War on Fakes" website and the "RRN" (Reliable Recent News) outlet**. The release explicitly cross-references the FBI's same-day domain seizures targeting RRN/ANO Dialog and previously-designated Social Design Agency (SDA).

## Relevance to IC

This source is the financial-sanctions companion to [[us-doj-doppelganger-russian-influence-op-domain-seizures-2024|the DOJ Doppelganger 32-domain seizure + SDNY RT/Tenet indictment]]. It documents the operation page [[treasury-doppelganger-russian-malign-influence-sanctions-2024]].

International cooperation scope is **limited and unilateral**:

- The release names **no foreign LE counterpart, foreign government, or international organization** as a same-day cooperation partner. The cross-reference to a prior (separate) **EU sanction of ANO Dialog** is preserved as background context but is not coordination.
- The operation illustrates the **DOJ + Treasury + State Department same-day synchronization** pattern that the U.S. now uses against state-directed cyber-enabled foreign malign influence when the host state (Russia, post-2022) is uncooperative — a comparative reference point for IC effectiveness analysis where formal MLAT / extradition / Budapest Convention pathways are unavailable.

> [!note] Fetch note
> Primary `home.treasury.gov` URL retrieved successfully via `curl_cffi` chrome120 TLS impersonation (200 OK; 133KB body; per L11 / L20). WebFetch alone timed out (Cloudflare/CDN behavior). Material facts cross-validated against Lawfare, TRM Labs, Mayer Brown, OODAloop, and Willkie Compliance Concourse coverage. All names, role titles, entity descriptions, and the JY-2559 / E.O. 14024 / 10+2 figures are from primary source body.

title: A GRU military unit launched cyberattacks against Estonian authorities
text:
## Summary

Estonian Police and Border Guard Board (PPA) press release dated **2024-09-05** announcing the attribution and indictment phase of joint operation **Toy Soldier**, in which **14 services across 10 states** cooperated with the Estonian Internal Security Service to identify Russian GRU military unit 29155 as the actor behind cyberattacks on Estonian state authorities (2020) and on Ukraine, NATO, and EU member states from 2020 onward.

The Estonian National Criminal Police, under the direction of the Office of the Prosecutor General, brought criminal proceedings against three named GRU officers (Yuri Denisov, Nikolay Korchagin, Vitali Shevchenko); the Harju District Court issued in-absentia custody orders. Parallel US prosecution by the FBI charged Denisov and Korchagin and was paired with a $10 million State Department reward. The press release explicitly invokes hybrid-warfare doctrine.

## Source Type and Reliability

- **Source type**: official national-police press release (tier 1 primary).
- **Reliability**: high. The release is from the Estonian PPA's own newsroom and names suspects, court, and partners.
- **Credibility**: confirmed. Cross-corroborated by the parallel US (FBI / State Department reward) action referenced in-text.
- **Sensitivity**: public.

## International-Cooperation Relevance

The press release documents (i) **multilateral attribution** under joint operation Toy Soldier (14 services / 10 states), (ii) **parallel prosecution** between Estonia and the United States, and (iii) **in-absentia indictment** of state-actor suspects believed to be sheltered in Russia. The Russia-shelter pattern, combined with the FBI reward and Estonian custody-in-absentia order, illustrates how partner states absorb the limits of MLAT against non-cooperating jurisdictions.

## Key Entities Named

| Entity | Role |
|---|---|
| Estonian Internal Security Service | Lead intelligence body for attribution |
| Estonian National Criminal Police | Conducted criminal proceedings |
| Estonian Office of the Prosecutor General | Directed proceedings; applied for custody orders |
| Harju District Court | Issued custody orders in absentia |
| Federal Bureau of Investigation | Parallel US criminal charges against Denisov and Korchagin |
| US Department of State | $10 million reward offer |
| GRU military unit 29155 | Attributed actor (target) |
| Yuri Denisov, Nikolay Korchagin, Vitali Shevchenko | Named GRU officers indicted |

## URL

- https://www.politsei.ee/en/news/a-gru-military-unit-launched-cyberattacks-against-estonian-authorities-12173

title: AFP Operation Kraken charges alleged head of global organised crime app
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Kraken was a multinational law enforcement operation led by the [[australia-afp|Australian Federal Police (AFP)]] and coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] that dismantled **Ghost**, a dedicated encrypted communications platform built and used exclusively by organized crime networks worldwide. The operation culminated on September 17-18, 2024, with near-simultaneous raids across Australia, Ireland, Italy, Canada, and Sweden. The platform's alleged creator and administrator, **Jay Je Yoon Jung** (32, of New South Wales, Australia), was arrested at his home in Narwee, Sydney.

title: Court-Authorized Operation Disrupts Worldwide Botnet Used by People's Republic of China State-Sponsored Hackers
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release (Press Release Number 24-1173), dated 2024-09-18, announcing a court-authorized law enforcement operation, conducted under court orders unsealed in the **Western District of Pennsylvania**, that disrupted the **Raptor Train** botnet — more than **200,000** PRC-state-controlled IoT consumer devices worldwide. The botnet was developed and controlled by **Integrity Technology Group**, a publicly-traded Beijing company assessed by FBI as the entity behind the activity tracked by industry as **Flax Typhoon**. The disruption took control of the adversary's infrastructure and issued disabling commands. The release names **French authorities** and **Five Eyes partner cyber agencies (Australia, Canada, New Zealand, United Kingdom)** as cooperation partners (the four FVEY partners are credited as co-signers of the same-day joint cybersecurity advisory rather than as joint-investigation partners). **Lumen Technologies' Black Lotus Labs** is credited as the private-sector partner that first identified and named the botnet in July 2023.

## Relevance to IC

This source documents [[us-doj-raptor-train-flax-typhoon-prc-botnet-disruption-2024]] — a tier-1 example of:

1. **Court-authorized technical disruption** as a primary IC instrument when the host state of the threat actor (PRC) will not cooperate. The U.S. exercises jurisdiction by reaching into adversary infrastructure under U.S. court order rather than via MLAT to China.
2. **Five Eyes joint cybersecurity advisory** as a public-attribution and information-sharing mechanism running parallel to (and in support of) the U.S. takedown action. The advisory is a co-authored CSA — the FVEY mechanism as currently practiced for PRC-attributed activity.
3. **Multi-track cooperation pattern**: bilateral foreign LE assistance (France, named) + multilateral cyber-agency advisory (FVEY) + private-sector technical attribution (Lumen / Black Lotus Labs). All three tracks named in the same release.
4. **Second PRC-contractor botnet disruption of 2024**, following the 911 S5 takedown — supports a 2024 enforcement-pattern thesis re: U.S. response to PRC-contractor IoT botnets.

## Confidence

It is **almost certain** (>95%) that the operation occurred on or about 2024-09-18 with the named target entity (Integrity Technology Group) and scope (>200,000 devices), based on a tier-1 DOJ primary source plus a same-day joint cybersecurity advisory (defense.gov hosted) and a same-day Lumen technical writeup. It is **highly likely** (80-95%) that the four FVEY partner countries' cyber agencies (ASD/ACSC, CCCS, GCSB/NCSC-NZ, NCSC-UK) are the unnamed "partner agencies" referenced in the advisory paragraph, but per L19 this should be confirmed by ingesting the joint advisory PDF as a separate raw source before asserting agency-level wikilinks.

> [!note] Fetch note
> Primary URL form `...people-s-republic-china-state` (with apostrophe) returns DOJ Page Not Found. Canonical live URL drops the apostrophe: `...peoples-republic-china-state`. Per L21 the DOJ Akamai Bot Manager interstitial was traversed via `tools/doj_fetch.py` with chrome-style UA + bm-verify session cookies. Body recovered ~89KB; full article text parsed.

> [!warning] L19 / L17 application
> Per L19, only countries explicitly named in this primary source are asserted on the operation page's `participating_countries`: **United States, France, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, United Kingdom**, plus the **target nationality (China / PRC)**. Specific partner agency names beyond the U.S. side are NOT asserted from this source alone — they require the companion joint cybersecurity advisory as a tier-1 source.

title: BKA / ZIT: Cybercrime — Erfolgreicher Schlag gegen die Infrastruktur von digitalen Geldwäschern der Underground Economy (Operation Final Exchange)
text:
## Source Summary

This is the **tier-1 primary German-language press release** issued jointly by the Bundeskriminalamt (BKA) and the Generalstaatsanwaltschaft Frankfurt am Main – Zentralstelle zur Bekämpfung der Internetkriminalität (ZIT) on 19 September 2024 announcing **Operation Final Exchange**: the seizure of the infrastructure of 47 Germany-hosted, Russian-language, no-KYC cryptocurrency exchange services. The release frames the takedown as an attack on the **digital money-laundering layer** of the cybercrime underground economy, naming ransomware groups, darknet vendors, and botnet operators as the principal client base of the targeted platforms.

The BKA URL was directly readable on 2026-05-09. The Wayback Machine had no archived snapshot of the BKA URL at that time, but the BKA primary text was supplemented by tier-2 corroboration from Heise Online (English edition), TheRecord (Recorded Future News), Chainalysis, AMLP Forum, Infosecurity Magazine, and Duane Morris ESE blog. Highly likely (>90%) that the named-exchange list and the Xchange.cash transaction/user statistics reflect the BKA's own briefing material because the same figures recur across independent secondary outlets.

## Statutory Citation (verbatim)

> §§ 127, 261 Abs. 1 Satz 1 Nr. 2 und Abs. 4 StGB

This is the formal charging basis carried in the BKA press release: § 127 StGB (Betreiben krimineller Handelsplattformen im Internet — operating criminal trading platforms on the internet, the relatively new offence introduced as part of Germany's 2021 cybercrime amendments) plus § 261 StGB (Geldwäsche — money laundering), aggravated form.

## Relevance to IC

This source is the strongest publicly available anchor in the wiki for:

1. **No-KYC instant-swap exchange ecosystem as a discrete IC target.** Unlike mixer takedowns ([[treasury-sinbad-mixer-dprk-lazarus-sanctions-2023]], [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025]]) that target a single mixing service, Operation Final Exchange struck **47 platforms simultaneously** — the largest single-action seizure of cryptocurrency-laundering infrastructure on the BKA timeline.
2. **Use of § 127 StGB for IC infrastructure takedowns.** The 2021 German offence of "operating criminal trading platforms on the internet" allows seizure on the basis of platform-design intent (no-KYC + facilitation knowledge) without requiring proof of specific predicate crimes. This is a model worth comparing against U.S. Bank Secrecy Act / 18 U.S.C. § 1960 (unlicensed money transmitting) charges in cases like [[operation-russian-nationals-charged-with-hacking-one-cryptocurrency-exchange-and-illicitly-operating-another]].
3. **Unilateral German action against Russian-language infrastructure.** Russia is not a participating state. This is a single-jurisdiction infrastructure takedown enabled by the fact that the targeted servers were physically Germany-hosted, regardless of who operated them or who used them. This pattern mirrors [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025]] (DE-CH bilateral) but without the cross-border partner.
4. **BKA infrastructure-takedown chronology.** The release explicitly chains Final Exchange to ChipMixer (2023), Qakbot, Emotet, and [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] (2024), positioning this as part of an ongoing strategic shift from individual prosecutions (often blocked by the absence of an extradition treaty with Russia) to **infrastructure denial**.

## Notable Named Exchanges

| Exchange | Notes |
|----------|-------|
| Xchange.cash | Top by transaction volume; ~410,000 user accounts; ~1.28–1.3M transactions processed (per Chainalysis / The Record) |
| Bankcomat.com | Operating since 2012 (per Heise / The Record); among top three by transactions |
| 60cek.org | Among top three by transactions (per Chainalysis) |
| CoinBlinker.com | Named in BKA-aligned coverage |
| Cryptostrike | Named in The Record |
| Baksman | Named in The Record |
| Prostocash | Named in The Record |
| Multichange.net | Named in Infosecurity Magazi

title: Argentine Ministry of Security / Policía Federal Argentina press release: Desarticulamos una red internacional de ciberdelincuentes (Operación KAERB — iServer phishing-as-a-service takedown)
text:
## Source Profile

This is an institutional press release dated **24 September 2024**, published on the Government of Argentina's official portal (`argentina.gob.ar`) under the **Ministerio de Seguridad Nacional / Policía Federal Argentina** path. It announces the dismantlement of the **iServer** phishing-as-a-service platform through the multinational law-enforcement action **Operación KAERB**. The Spanish-language release names the participating cooperation architecture (EUROPOL, AMERIPOL, PAcCTO 2.0, Group-IB), enumerates the six participating countries, breaks down the global victim count by country, and details the Argentine-side enforcement footprint (provincial distribution of warrants, named operational leads).

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official Government of Argentina domain (`argentina.gob.ar`), institutionally signed press release under the Ministerio de Seguridad Nacional / Policía Federal Argentina path.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement by one of the lead enforcing jurisdictions; corroborated by Group-IB's PRNewswire release of the same week and by overlapping Europol / Eurojust newsroom coverage that confirms the headline figures (17 arrests, six countries, 483,000 victims, 921 devices).
- **Sensitivity: public** — distributed for general media use; identifies the Argentine administrator only by residency (Santa Fe Province), not by name; secondary press identifies him as Iván David Cudde — that secondary identification is not asserted from this primary record.

## Key Cooperation Facts Established

| Fact | Source line (paraphrased) |
|---|---|
| Operation name | "Operación KAERB" |
| Announcement date | "24 de septiembre de 2024" |
| Participating jurisdictions | "Intervinieron … España, Argentina, Colombia, Chile, Ecuador y Perú" |
| Coordination architecture | "apoyadas por EUROPOL …, El PACCTO 2.0 … y AMERIPOL"; "alianza estratégica entre el ámbito público y privado, … aporte colaborativo de la empresa GROUP IB" |
| Aggregate enforcement | "se detuvieron a un total de 17 personas en seis países … 28 registros domiciliarios … 921 dispositivos electrónicos" |
| Argentine footprint | "12 registros … Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires (5), Santa Fe (3), Córdoba (3) y Jujuy (1)" |
| Victims | "483.000 usuarios (Chile 77.000, Colombia 70.000, Ecuador 42.000, Perú 41.500, España 30.000, Argentina 29.000 …)" |
| Platform vintage | "opera desde hace al menos 5 años, tiene más de 2.000 usuarios registrados" |
| Apex suspect | "ciudadano argentino, el administrador de la plataforma iServer, residente en la Provincia de Santa Fe, Argentina" |

## Multi-Language Terminology Note

| English | Spanish |
|---|---|
| international cybercriminal network | red internacional de ciberdelincuentes |
| phishing-as-a-service platform | plataforma de phishing como servicio |
| home searches / domiciliary searches | registros domiciliarios |
| Cyberpatrolling Division | División Ciberpatrullaje |
| Argentine National Gendarmerie | Gendarmería Nacional Argentina |
| Secretariat of Security | Secretaría de Seguridad |
| Programme of Assistance against Transnational Organised Crime 2.0 | PAcCTO 2.0 (Programa de Asistencia Contra el Crimen Transnacional Organizado) |
| Community of Police of the Americas | AMERIPOL (Comunidad de Policías de América) |

> [!note] Translation note
> "Desbloqueador" (literally "unlocker") is the Argentine-Spanish term used in the release for the subscriber-cybercriminals who used iServer to unlock stolen mobile phones. It does not have a direct English equivalent in cybercrime literature, where this niche is typically described as "phone-unlocking phishers" or "stolen-device phishing operators".

title: Three IRGC Cyber Actors Indicted for 'Hack-and-Leak' Operation Designed to Influence the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election
text:
## Source Summary

On 27 September 2024, the U.S. DOJ Office of Public Affairs, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Columbia, the DOJ National Security Division, and the FBI jointly announced an unsealed indictment in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia charging three IRGC cyber operatives — Masoud Jalili (36), Seyyed Ali Aghamiri (34), and Yaser Balaghi (37), all Iranian nationals — with a multi-year spearphishing and social-engineering campaign that culminated in a June 2024 hack-and-leak operation against U.S. Presidential Campaign 1, with stolen material disseminated to media and to individuals associated with U.S. Presidential Campaign 2. The DOJ release was coordinated the same day with a Department of State Rewards for Justice offer of up to USD 10 million for information on the subjects or on associated IRGC election-interference activity, and with a Department of the Treasury (OFAC) sanctions designation of defendant Jalili under Executive Order 13694 (as amended) and Executive Order 13848. The charged conduct includes conspiracy, computer intrusion (CFAA), wire fraud (including a domain-name false-registration variant), aggravated identity theft (eight counts), and conspiracy to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization (the IRGC). The release places the conduct within Iran's broader campaign to erode confidence in the U.S. electoral process and to acquire information useful for IRGC retaliation following the killing of Qasem Soleimani. Investigating offices include the FBI Washington Field Office (lead), FBI Cyber Division, FBI Springfield Field Office, and FBI Minneapolis Field Office.

## Relevance to IC

The release is a tier-1 instance of a U.S. coordinated denial-and-deterrence action against state-directed election-interference cyber actors. Three IC-relevant features stand out. First, the indictment is announced jointly with State Department Rewards for Justice and Treasury OFAC sanctions, illustrating the U.S. "stack" of legal actions deployed when defendants are sheltered by an adversary state. Second, the case relies on no formal mutual legal assistance partner — Iran is not a Budapest Convention party and is *highly unlikely* to extradite IRGC personnel — so the cooperation logic is informational and deterrent rather than custodial. Third, the explicit charging of material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization (IRGC) overlays counterterrorism statutory authority on cybercrime conduct, which is *likely* to influence how analogous future state-actor election-interference cases are framed. The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2024-09-27_justice-gov_three-irgc-cyber-actors-indicted-hack-and-leak-2024-election.md`, preserving traceable review.

title: INTERPOL Leads to 144 Arrests and Rescues 20 Child Victims Across South America
text:

title: Operation CHAKRA-III: Inside CBI's Explosive Takedown
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Chakra-III was the third phase of India's Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) ongoing multi-phase campaign against transnational cyber-enabled financial crime. Launched in July 2024 with case registration RC2312024S0007, the operation targeted tech-support scam call centers operating from multiple Indian cities — principally Gurugram, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad, and Visakhapatnam — that defrauded victims in the United States and other countries. The CBI collaborated with the FBI, Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), and [[interpol|INTERPOL]], resulting in 43 arrests and the seizure of substantial evidence including gold bullion, cash, luxury vehicles, and over 950 electronic devices.

## Relevance to IC

title: Treasury Sanctions Members of the Russia-Based Cybercriminal Group Evil Corp in Tri-Lateral Action with the United Kingdom and Australia (JY-2623)
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 U.S. Treasury (OFAC) press release JY-2623 documenting the October 1, 2024 tri-lateral US-UK-AU cyber-sanctions action against the Russia-based Evil Corp ransomware/Dridex group, plus a concurrent DOJ-EDTX indictment of Aleksandr Ryzhenkov for BitPaymer ransomware. The release expressly references the December 5, 2019 OFAC Evil Corp designations as the predicate, and is timed with day 2 of the U.S.-hosted Counter Ransomware Initiative summit.

## Relevance to IC

- **Tri-lateral sanctions cooperation pattern.** First wiki-recorded US-UK-AU 3-country financial-sanctions coordination on cyber. The same template (US Treasury + UK FCDO + Australia DFAT, E.O. 13694 / 14024 basis) recurs in the 2025 BPH chain (Zservers Feb 2025; Aeza Group July 2025; Media Land November 2025) — that 9-month BPH chain is the operational sequel to the 2024 Evil Corp action recorded here.
- **Sanctions-plus-prosecution combo.** OFAC designation paired with concurrent DOJ-EDTX indictment unsealing of one of the designees (Aleksandr Ryzhenkov, BitPaymer) is the canonical "designate + charge" pattern.
- **State-nexus designation.** The dual-authority designation of Eduard Benderskiy (E.O. 13694 cyber + E.O. 14024 Russian-state) ties Evil Corp formally to the Russian state via FSB / Russian Duma adjacency.
- **5-year sanctions chain.** Documents the 2019 → 2024 evolution of OFAC pressure on Evil Corp (original Dec 5 2019 designation of Maksim Yakubets and core members → Oct 2022 designations of NIK / Chayka / Troshin under E.O. 14024 → Oct 1 2024 expansion to 7 more individuals and 2 entities).

Updates [[treasury-evil-corp-tri-lateral-us-uk-au-sanctions-2024]].
