title: International action against world's largest darkweb market 'Bohemia/Cannabia'
text:
# Source Summary — Politie press release: Bohemia/Cannabia takedown (2024-10-08)

## Provenance and access notes

- Original URL: `https://www.politie.nl/nieuws/2024/oktober/8/11-internationale-actie-tegen-werelds-grootste-darkweb-markt-bohemia-cannabia.html`
- Original language: Dutch.
- WebFetch on this and the English `/en/news/2024/june/27/...` candidate returned HTTP 403 (Cloudflare bot protection, consistent with [LESSONS.md](../../LESSONS.md) L11/L13 patterns for politie.nl).
- Content verified by triangulation of the Politie statement across three tier-2 outlets ([The Record](https://therecord.media/bohemia-cannabia-dark-web-market-arrests), [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/legal/dutch-police-arrest-admin-of-bohemia-cannabia-dark-web-market/), [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2024/10/10/cannabia_bohemia_darkweb_market_investigation/)) plus a WebSearch index excerpt of the politie.nl page itself; all four are consistent on the core facts.

> [!warning] URL mismatch resolved
> The task brief listed the publish date as 2024-06-27 with an `/en/news/2024/june/27/...` candidate URL. **2024-06-27 is the date of the Schiphol arrest, not the date of the public release.** The actual Politie press release is dated **2024-10-08**, published on the Dutch-language news path. The June English URL does not appear to exist (404 in the standard Politie publishing pattern; English coverage of the announcement appeared in third-party outlets on 2024-10-09).

## What the source establishes

### Operational facts (confirmed across all sources)

| Fact | Value |
|---|---|
| Marketplace names | Bohemia and Cannabia (dual / sister markets, common admin) |
| Investigation start | Late 2022 |
| Marketplace shutdown | End of 2023 (alleged exit scam by operators) |
| First admin arrest | 2024-06-27, Schiphol Airport, Amsterdam (20-year-old British national) |
| Second admin arrest | August 2024, Ireland (23-year-old Irish national) |
| Cryptocurrency seized | > EUR 8 million |
| Servers seized | Multiple (database / crypto / web), most located in the Netherlands |
| Peak monthly turnover | EUR 12 million (September 2023) |
| Daily ads | ~82,000 |
| Monthly transactions | ~67,000 |
| Estimated admin profits | ~EUR 5 million |
| Dutch-origin transactions | ≥ 14,000, ~EUR 1.7 million |

### Cooperation partners explicitly named

- **Netherlands** — Politie (Team High Tech Crime, National Investigation and Intervention Unit) + OM (Rotterdam prosecution).
- **Ireland** — An Garda Síochána.
- **United Kingdom** — National Crime Agency (NCA).
- **United States** — "U.S. law enforcement" (one tier-2 source specifies DOJ; not uniformly confirmed).

### Cooperation partners NOT confirmed by tier-1

- **Germany / BKA** — appeared in one tier-2 summary (The Record) but is not corroborated by other sources covering the Politie release. Not asserted on the operation page absent direct primary-source confirmation. Per L17 / L19.
- **Eurojust / Europol** — no explicit coordination cited for the original Bohemia/Cannabia takedown. (Operation RapTor, a downstream Europol-coordinated buyer/seller sweep using Bohemia data, is a separate operation announced May 2025.)

## Reliability / credibility

- **Reliability: high.** Primary publisher is the Dutch national police; statement is operational, fact-specific, and includes attribution to a named senior officer (Stan Duijf).
- **Credibility: confirmed.** All asserted operational facts (arrests, seizures, marketplace metrics, partner countries) corroborated across at least three independent tier-2 outlets summarising the same statement.
- **Sensitivity: public.** Press release intended for public dissemination; no operational details requiring restriction.

## Pages updated by this source

- [[bohemia-cannabia-darknet-marketplace-takedown-2024]] (operation page — created)

> [!note] Translation note
> The press release is in Dutch. Quoted text in English ("All data from the database ser

title: Malware targeting millions of people taken down by international coalition (Operation Magnus — RedLine & META infostealer takedown)
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release dated 29 October 2024 announcing the 28 October 2024 takedown of the RedLine and META infostealers under the publicly-codenamed Operation Magnus. The release establishes Eurojust as the coordinating body and names six participating jurisdictions (Netherlands, US, Belgium, Portugal, UK, Australia) and their executing agencies. It quantifies operational results (3 servers seized in NL, 2 domains seized, 2 arrests in BE, charges unsealed in US, client database retrieved) and contextualizes scale (>1,200 servers across dozens of countries; "millions" of victims).

## IC Pattern

Eurojust-coordinated multilateral malware-as-a-service takedown with: (a) the Netherlands hosting both the infrastructure seizures (3 servers) and the lead investigative team (Limburg cybercrime unit), (b) Belgium executing the only physical arrests (2), (c) the United States providing the prosecutorial / indictment leg (Maxim Rudometov charges unsealed), (d) UK and Australia and Portugal in supporting investigative roles, and (e) a private-sector tip-off (unnamed security company) seeding the Dutch investigation. The structure mirrors the 2022 Raccoon Infostealer pattern (NL host + US prosecution) but expands to six jurisdictions and adds a Belgium-arrest leg, signalling continued maturation of the EU-coordinated MaaS-takedown template.

## Source notes

- Tier 1 (Eurojust official press release).
- Reliability: high. Credibility: confirmed.
- Companion sources: DOJ press release (justice.gov, blocked by Akamai during ingest — L21), IRS-CI press release (irs.gov, accessed and cross-checked for US statutory cites and indictee identity), Dutch Police victim site `operation-magnus.com`.

title: 5 Defendants Charged Federally with Running Scheme that Targeted Victim Companies via Phishing Text Messages (Scattered Spider — November 2024 Indictment)
text:
## Source Summary

This is the U.S. Department of Justice — USAO Central District of California — press release (No. 24-289), dated **Wednesday, November 20, 2024**, announcing the unsealing of a federal grand jury indictment in the U.S. District Court for the Central District of California charging four U.S.-resident defendants (Ahmed Hossam Eldin Elbadawy of College Station TX; Noah Michael Urban of Palm Coast FL; Evans Onyeaka Osiebo of Dallas TX; Joel Martin Evans of Jacksonville NC) and a parallel federal criminal complaint charging Tyler Robert Buchanan, a UK national, with conspiracy to commit wire fraud, conspiracy, aggravated identity theft, and (Buchanan only) substantive wire fraud, arising out of a smishing-to-credential-theft-to-cryptocurrency-drain scheme operating from at least September 2021 to April 2023.

The release names the FBI Los Angeles Field Office as the lead investigative agency (Assistant Director in Charge Akil Davis) and credits assistance from the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of North Carolina, **Police Scotland**, and the FBI field offices in Charlotte, Denver, Houston, and Portland. AUSAs Lauren Restrepo (CCIPS / Cyber and IP Crimes Section) and Sue J. Bai (Terrorism and Export Crimes Section) are the line prosecutors.

## Relevance to IC

The source supports [[us-v-five-scattered-spider-members-2024]] and is a tier-1 primary source for:

1. **Cross-border defendant chain (US ↔ UK)** — Buchanan, a UK national, is charged by U.S. complaint on the same day the U.S. indictment is unsealed against the four U.S.-resident co-defendants. The release does not announce extradition or arrest of Buchanan, but the parallel U.S.-domestic / UK-foreign-national filing structure is a direct artifact of the U.S. extraterritorial reach over [[hacking-ic]] and wire-fraud-via-SMS schemes touching U.S. wires.
2. **Police-to-police cross-border cooperation (US ↔ Scotland / United Kingdom)** — Police Scotland is named as a contributing agency. This is one of the few wiki-documented cases naming Police Scotland (rather than the UK NCA, City of London Police, or West Midlands Police) as the UK-side cooperator on a U.S. cybercrime indictment, **likely** (55–80%) because Buchanan's residence ties to Dundee, Scotland (per secondary reporting).
3. **Companion to the 2025 Jubair complaint** — sets up the Scattered Spider prosecution chain as a multi-year, multi-defendant U.S. effort. The November 2024 case (this page) covers conduct September 2021 to April 2023; the September 2025 D.N.J. complaint against Thalha Jubair ([[us-v-jubair-scattered-spider-2025]]) covers conduct May 2022 to September 2025. The two cases together document the most extensive U.S.-led Scattered Spider prosecutorial response to date.

> [!note] Group-attribution caveat
> The DOJ press release does **not** use the name "Scattered Spider," "Octo Tempest," "UNC3944," or "0ktapus." The Scattered Spider attribution to these five defendants is sourced from secondary tier-2/3 reporting (KrebsOnSecurity, Bloomberg, The Record, Hacker News, Hogan Lovells, Axios, BankInfoSec, IT Pro, TechTarget, CyberScoop, CyberSecurity Dive). The case page should treat the group attribution as **highly likely** (80–95%) but explicitly secondary-sourced.

> [!note] Spain-arrest fact not in source
> Tyler Buchanan's well-reported June 2024 arrest in Spain by Spanish authorities (while attempting to board a flight to Italy) is **not** mentioned in this DOJ press release. It is documented in secondary reporting and parallel Spanish-side announcements. The case page records it with an explicit secondary-source citation pending ingestion of a tier-1 Spanish or U.S. extradition filing.

title: International operation takes down another encrypted messaging service used by criminals (MATRIX)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2024-12-03 announcing the 2024-12-03 takedown of MATRIX — an invitation-only encrypted messaging service made by criminals for criminals, technically more complex than Sky ECC and EncroChat. Joint Investigation Team between Netherlands and France set up at Eurojust; Europol Operational Task Force (June 2024 onwards) with 5 countries (NL/FR/LT/IT/ES); Germany supported with technical expertise. Action day produced 3 arrests (1 FR, 2 ES under EAW from NL), 13 house searches (1 FR + 6 ES + 6 LT), and main-server takedowns in France and Germany. 3-month live interception captured 2.3M+ messages in 33 languages.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes an encrypted-comms-platform takedown pattern that complements the wiki's prior records of Sky ECC, EncroChat, Exclu, and Ghost takedowns. Notable IC mechanism classes:

- **Netherlands-France JIT at Eurojust** as the founding bilateral investigative instrument.
- **Europol Operational Task Force (OTF)** established June 2024 with 5 countries — first wiki record of an "Operational Task Force" as a discrete IC mechanism class distinct from JIT, J-CAT, and EAR.
- **3-month live interception phase** as the operational core, structurally similar to the EncroChat and Sky ECC live-monitoring phases.
- **Splash-page warning post-takedown** deployed to alert MATRIX users that authorities had intercepted the platform — a discrete deception/awareness-raising mechanism class.
- **European Arrest Warrant cross-border arrest pattern** (Netherlands → Spain for 2 of 3 action-day arrests).
- **Central server takedowns in France + Germany** — the multi-country server-physical-location dimension of an encrypted-comms platform, requiring action-day coordination across multiple jurisdictions.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release complements the wiki's encrypted-comms-platform takedown corpus:
- [[operation-kraken-ghost-platform|Operation Kraken / Ghost]]
- [[operation-us-v-anom-distributors|US v. ANOM distributors]]

And references the broader chronology: Sky ECC, EncroChat, Exclu, Ghost, MATRIX (newest, Dec 2024). The 5-country Europol OTF + JIT model is structurally similar to the 19-country LabHost J-CAT model and the 6-country Archetyp Operation Deep Sentinel model documented elsewhere in the wiki.

title: Operation Destabilise: NCA disrupts multi-billion Russian money laundering networks with links to drugs, ransomware and espionage, resulting in 84 arrests
text:

title: Rydox Cybercrime Marketplace Shut Down and Three Administrators Arrested
text:
## Source Summary

On 12 December 2024, DOJ announced the seizure of the Rydox cybercrime marketplace and the arrest of two alleged administrators, Ardit Kutleshi and Jetmir Kutleshi, in Kosovo pursuant to a U.S. extradition request. DOJ said the indictment had been unsealed that day in the Western District of Pennsylvania. According to the press release, Rydox had generated at least USD 230,000 in revenue since 2016, completed over 7,600 sales, and offered more than 321,000 cybercrime products to over 18,000 users. Those products included stolen personal information, access devices, login credentials, scam pages, and spamming tools affecting thousands of U.S. victims. The same announcement described a coordinated international action involving Kosovo, Albania, and Malaysia. A third alleged administrator, Shpend Sokoli, was arrested in Albania for expected domestic prosecution, while Malaysian authorities and the FBI seized the servers in Kuala Lumpur. DOJ also said the United States obtained authorization to seize the `Rydox.cc` domain and about USD 225,000 in cryptocurrency.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/case-documents/2024-12-12_wdpa_kutleshi-rydox-indictment.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: Ukrainian National Sentenced to Federal Prison in 'Raccoon Infostealer' Cybercrime Case
text:
## Source Summary

USAO-WDTX press release dated 2024-12-18 announcing the 60-month federal-prison sentencing of Ukrainian national Mark Sokolovsky for operating the Raccoon Infostealer malware-as-a-service. The release records the full international-cooperation chain: arrest by Dutch authorities in March 2022 concurrent with a joint FBI + Italy + Netherlands infrastructure dismantle of the Raccoon Infostealer; extradition from the Netherlands to the US in February 2024; guilty plea in October 2024; and 2024-12-18 sentencing.

## IC Pattern

The release establishes a US-Netherlands-Italy three-country cooperation pattern for malware-as-a-service infrastructure takedown plus single-defendant extradition prosecution. Specifically: simultaneous March 2022 (a) Dutch arrest of Sokolovsky and (b) FBI + Italy + Netherlands joint dismantling of Raccoon Infostealer infrastructure, followed by Netherlands → US extradition in February 2024 (24 months between arrest and extradition, partly attributable to defendant's contested-extradition appeal). This is a discrete IC-mechanism class — coordinated arrest-of-operator + concurrent infrastructure-takedown — that the wiki has documented previously for botnet operations but not specifically for the Raccoon Infostealer / MaaS class until this source.

title: Silk Road (marketplace)
text:
## Source Summary

The Silk Road takedown was the seizure and shutdown of the pioneering dark web drug marketplace on 2 October 2013, accompanied by the arrest of its creator and operator, Ross William Ulbricht (alias "Dread Pirate Roberts" or "DPR"), by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] in San Francisco. Silk Road, which operated on the Tor anonymity network from approximately February 2011, had facilitated an estimated USD 1.2 billion in illegal transactions -- primarily narcotics -- serving over 100,000 buyers through thousands of vendors worldwide. The FBI seized approximately 173,991 bitcoins (then worth ~$33.6 million) and the Silk Road server infrastructure hosted in Iceland.

## Relevance to IC

title: Treasury Sanctions Technology Company for Support to Malicious Cyber Group (Integrity Technology Group / Flax Typhoon, OFAC, 3 January 2025)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury Sanctions Integrity Technology Group (Flax Typhoon), 3 January 2025

## What it is

U.S. Department of the Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) press release **jy2769**, dated **3 January 2025**, announcing the designation of **Integrity Technology Group, Incorporated** (Beijing-based) under **Executive Order 13694** (cyber sanctions authority, April 2015) as amended by **EO 13757** (December 2016).

## Why it matters for IC

- This is the **targeted-sanction companion** to the DOJ court-authorized Raptor Train botnet disruption (DOJ Press Release 24-1173, 18 September 2024). It demonstrates the **U.S. multi-instrument response pattern** to PRC-contractor cyber operations: court-authorized infrastructure takedown (DOJ + FBI) + cybersecurity attribution (FVEY joint advisory) + targeted financial sanctions (Treasury OFAC), staged over ~3.5 months.
- It is a **unilateral U.S. action**, not a multilateral sanctions coordination — IC scope is borderline. Compare with the EU/UK coordinated Russia-cyber sanctions or the FVEY APT31 designations where parallel UK FCDO action was issued the same day. Here no parallel partner-state designation is documented.
- It establishes a precedent for **naming and freezing assets of a publicly-traded foreign cybersecurity contractor** assessed by the FBI as the entity behind a named state-sponsored APT (Flax Typhoon = Integrity Technology Group).

## Reliability & Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official U.S. Treasury OFAC press release (tier-1 primary source); confirmed by three independent secondary mirrors (Alston & Bird, Industrial Cyber, GlobalSecurity.org) plus contemporaneous reporting (TechCrunch, Cybersecurity Dive, ASIS, MeriTalk).
- **Credibility: confirmed** — official designation announcement; legal effect (asset blocking) is binding on U.S. persons.
- **Sensitivity: public**.

## Fetch caveat (per LESSONS.md L11)

The primary URL `https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy2769` returned WebFetch timeout during ingest. Body content was reconstructed from three independent secondary mirrors. The raw source file documents this reconstruction transparently. Primary fetch should be retried later for verbatim authoritative text capture.

## Notable Quote

> "The Treasury Department will not hesitate to hold malicious cyber actors and their enablers accountable for their actions."
> — Bradley T. Smith, Acting Under Secretary of the Treasury for Terrorism and Financial Intelligence (3 Jan 2025)

## Companion sources to ingest separately

- **DOJ Press Release 24-1173** (18 September 2024) — court-authorized Raptor Train takedown (already ingested as `[[2024-09-18_justice-gov_court-authorized-operation-disrupts-raptor-train-botnet-flax-typhoon]]`).
- **FBI / NSA / CNMF + AU/CA/NZ/UK Joint Cybersecurity Advisory** (18 September 2024) — TTPs and Integrity Tech support role for Flax Typhoon.
- **Lumen / Black Lotus Labs technical writeup** ("Derailing the Raptor Train") — independent telemetry and the >260,000-device peak figure.

title: Operators of Cryptocurrency Mixers Charged with Money Laundering
text:
## Source Summary

On 10 January 2025 the U.S. Department of Justice Office of Public Affairs announced the unsealing of a federal indictment, returned by a grand jury in the Northern District of Georgia on 7 January 2025, charging three Russian nationals with operating the cryptocurrency mixers Blender.io (2018–April 2022) and its alleged successor Sinbad.io (October 2022 onward). The release announces the prior arrest of two of the three defendants — Roman Vitalyevich Ostapenko and Alexander Evgenievich Oleynik — on 1 December 2024 in a coordinated international operation involving the Netherlands Fiscal Information and Investigative Service (FIOD), the Finnish National Bureau of Investigation (NBI), and the FBI. The third defendant, Anton Vyachlavovich Tarasov, remains at large.

## Relevance to IC

The release is highly relevant to international cooperation on cybercrime in three respects: (1) it documents a discrete tri-national arrest pattern ([[netherlands|Netherlands]] + [[finland|Finland]] + [[united-states|United States]]) effecting the apprehension of two Russian-national defendants on the territory of EU member states, demonstrating that intra-EU + transatlantic informal police cooperation continues to function for cyber-monetization targets even where the home state ([[russia]]) refuses extradition cooperation; (2) it formally links Lazarus Group / [[north-korea|DPRK]] state-sponsored cryptocurrency-theft monetization to U.S. federal criminal liability for service operators, complementing the prior OFAC sanctions track ([[treasury-sinbad-mixer-dprk-lazarus-sanctions-2023|Treasury Sinbad designation]], 2023); and (3) it confirms a follow-on prosecution of operators of an OFAC-sanctioned mixer, exemplifying the U.S. interagency model in which sanctions, indictment, and arrest are sequenced across [[us-treasury|Treasury]], [[fbi|FBI]], foreign police partners, and the [[office-of-international-affairs|DOJ Office of International Affairs]].

## Reliability Notes

- Reliability assessed as **high** and credibility as **confirmed** under the source-type defaults for an official DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release.
- All factual claims relating to charges, defendants, and dates derive from a charging instrument; consistent with U.S. due process, the indictment is an accusation and the defendants remain presumed innocent.
- Cross-corroborated against The Hacker News (2025-01-11) and Help Net Security (2025-01-13) coverage of the same release; no material discrepancies in defendant identifying details, charges, district, or arrest date.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Operators of Cryptocurrency Mixers Charged with Money Laundering | U.S. Department of Justice | 2025-01-10 | https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/operators-cryptocurrency-mixers-charged-money-laundering |

title: Treasury Sanctions Company Associated with Salt Typhoon and Hacker Associated with Treasury Compromise (JY-2792)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury JY-2792: OFAC sanctions on Yin Kecheng and Sichuan Juxinhe (Salt Typhoon) (2025-01-17)

## Bibliographic data

- **Title**: Treasury Sanctions Company Associated with Salt Typhoon and Hacker Associated with Treasury Compromise
- **Press release number**: JY-2792
- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Date**: 2025-01-17
- **URL**: https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy2792
- **Language**: English

## Reliability assessment

- **Reliability**: high — official Treasury press release, primary source for the OFAC designation.
- **Credibility**: confirmed — designation is a self-executing administrative act announced by the issuing authority on its own letterhead; the SDN list update is the corroborating administrative record.
- **Sensitivity**: public — released by Treasury for public dissemination.

## Key claims and findings

1. OFAC designated **Yin Kecheng** (Shanghai-based PRC national) and **Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co., LTD.** (Sichuan-based PRC company) pursuant to **Executive Order 13694**, as further amended by the new **E.O. on Strengthening and Promoting Innovation in the Nation's Cybersecurity** (signed January 2025). (Almost certainly accurate as a primary-source statement.)
2. **Yin Kecheng** is described as having been a cyber actor for over a decade and **affiliated with the People's Republic of China Ministry of State Security (MSS)**. He is associated with the recent compromise of Treasury's Departmental Offices network (publicly disclosed by Treasury in late December 2024 and attributed to a "China state-sponsored Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actor").
3. **Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co., LTD.** is described as having "direct involvement" in the Salt Typhoon cyber group's exploitation of multiple major U.S. telecommunication and internet service provider companies. Treasury asserts the MSS maintains strong ties with multiple computer network exploitation companies, including Sichuan Juxinhe.
4. **Salt Typhoon** has been active since at least 2019 and is described as responsible for numerous compromises of U.S. companies in the communication sector; the recent telecom-infrastructure compromise is characterized as "a dramatic escalation."
5. The release places this action in a deliberate **PRC-cyber sanctions campaign sequence**: 2024-03-25 (Wuhan Xiaoruizhi / APT31) → 2024-12-10 (Sichuan Silence) → 2025-01-03 (Integrity Technology Group / Flax Typhoon) → 2025-01-17 (this action). All four are framed by Treasury as responses to PRC-state-backed cyber activity against U.S. critical infrastructure.
6. The U.S. Department of State Rewards for Justice program is referenced in the release as offering up to **USD 10 million** for information on foreign-government-directed cyber activity against U.S. critical infrastructure (a standing reward; not specifically attached to Yin Kecheng in the release text).

## International cooperation relevance

> [!note] IC scope flag
> The release names no foreign partner authority as co-designating, co-coordinating, or otherwise participating in this action. Unlike the [[treasury-evil-corp-tri-lateral-us-uk-au-sanctions-2024|2024 Evil Corp tri-lateral]] or the [[treasury-us-au-uk-zservers-sanctions-2025|February 2025 ZServers]] designations, this is a **U.S.-unilateral OFAC action** against PRC state-sponsored actors. Five Eyes parallel attribution may exist but is not asserted on the record by this source.

- The IC dimension of this action is **almost certainly indirect** rather than operational: OFAC sanctions impose U.S.-jurisdiction asset blocks and U.S.-person transactional prohibitions, but neither extradition, MLA, nor host-state arrest is contemplated (PRC does not extradite its own nationals to the United States and is not a [[budapest-convention|Budapest Convention]] party). The action is best understood as part of the **U.S. parallel-track unilateral toolkit**

title: Department of Justice partners with Dutch police to break up HeartSender network
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Heart Blocker was a joint US-Dutch law enforcement action on January 29, 2025, targeting the HeartSender cybercrime marketplace network (also known as Saim Raza). The U.S. Department of Justice and the Dutch National Police coordinated the seizure of 39 domains and their associated servers that had been operating since at least 2020 as marketplaces selling phishing kits, credential-stealing tools, email extractors, and scam page generators. These tools were used by transnational organized crime groups to conduct [[bec-ic|business email compromise (BEC)]] schemes, resulting in over $3 million in confirmed US victim losses according to the DOJ (later revised to over $50 million by Pakistan's NCCIA).

## Relevance to IC

title: Key figures behind Phobos and 8Base ransomware arrested in international cybercrime crackdown
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing a coordinated international crackdown on the **Phobos** and **8Base** ransomware operations. The operation resulted in 4 arrests of Russian nationals (8Base leadership) and the takedown of 27 servers, with 400+ companies warned worldwide.

This source is *highly likely* the most relevant to the Korean context among the five verified sources, as it confirms the **arrest of a Phobos administrator in South Korea** in June 2024, followed by extradition to the United States in November 2024. This demonstrates active Korean participation in international ransomware enforcement.

The source describes a sustained investigation spanning from February 2019 to February 2025, with [[europol-ec3|Europol]] organizing 37 operational meetings and exchanging nearly 600 SIENA messages.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 100/100 (Korean connection) |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Standalone operation; Korean involvement cross-checked against south-korea.md |

## Key Metrics

- **Arrests:** 4 (Russian nationals, 8Base leadership)
- **Servers taken down:** 27
- **Companies warned:** 400+
- **Participating countries:** 14
- **Participating agencies:** 16
- **Investigation duration:** ~6 years (Feb 2019 - Feb 2025)

text:
## Source Summary

In early February 2025, a coordinated international law enforcement action resulted in the arrest of **4 Russian nationals** who were key figures behind the **8Base ransomware** group, and the takedown of **27 servers** connected to their criminal network. The operation involved 14 countries and built on prior actions including the arrest of a Phobos administrator in South Korea in June 2024 (extradited to the US in November 2024) and a key Phobos affiliate arrest in Italy in 2023. Law enforcement warned over 400 companies worldwide of ongoing or imminent ransomware attacks. Europol had supported the investigation since February 2019, organizing 37 operational meetings and exchanging nearly 600 operational messages via SIENA. - **Operation date:** Early February 2025 - **Publication date:** 11 February 2025

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2025-02-11_europol_phobos-8base-ransomware-arrests.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

title: United States, Australia, and the United Kingdom Jointly Sanction Key Infrastructure that Enables Ransomware Attacks
text:
## Summary

OFAC press release SB-0018 (2025-02-11) announces a coordinated trilateral cyber-sanctions action by the United States, Australia, and the United Kingdom against Zservers, a Russia-based bulletproof hosting provider that supplied infrastructure to LockBit ransomware affiliates. OFAC designated Zservers and two Russian administrators (Mishin and Bolshakov); the UK FCDO supplementally designated XHOST Internet Solutions LP (UK front) and four further employees the same day. The release establishes the LockBit nexus through three concrete incidents (2022 Canadian LE search, 2022 IP purchase, 2023 affiliate lease) and grounds the designations in E.O. 13694 as further amended by E.O. 14144. The action is highly likely the opening move in a deliberate 2025 trilateral track targeting Russian BPH infrastructure, with later entries (Aeza Group designations in July 2025; Media Land + Hypercore/SB-0319 in November 2025) following the same trilateral template.

## Provenance

- Retrieved via curl_cffi (chrome120 impersonation) on 2026-05-09 at 200 OK, body length 125,523 bytes.
- Publish date "February 11, 2025" verified verbatim in body (`idx=8991` in extracted plain text).
- Press release ID `SB-0018` derives from the URL slug; not stamped in body text.
- Reliability `high`, credibility `probably-true` (official U.S. Treasury press release; first-party authority on OFAC designations and on the LockBit-nexus narrative; UK supplemental detail noted as not body-text but cross-referenced to the FCDO same-day notice).

title: Police of Kazakhstan and Armenia detained internet fraudsters (Yerevan joint call-centre takedown, 41 detained)
text:
## Source Summary

Sputnik Kazakhstan release dated 2025-02-18, datelined Astana, attributed in-byline to the press service of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Republic of Kazakhstan. The release contains a named, on-record quotation by Zhandos Suiynbai, head of the MIA's Department for Combating Cybercrime, describing a joint Kazakh-Armenian special operation that closed two investment-fraud call centres in Yerevan, detained 41 members of a transnational criminal group, and seized cash, gold, vehicles, computers, and a cold cryptocurrency wallet.

## Relevance to International Cooperation

This is the controlling source for: (i) the existence and date of the Kazakh-Armenian joint operation, (ii) the named cooperating units on each side, (iii) the size and demographic composition of the detained group, (iv) the seized-asset list, and (v) the continuing post-action coordination between the two sides. It establishes a Central Asia / South Caucasus international-cooperation pairing not previously represented in the corpus.

## Source-Type Justification

The release is a state-news-agency relay of an official ministry press-service statement, with direct attribution and a named departmental head quoted on the record. It is treated as a tier-1 primary source for the Kazakh side. Independent corroboration from Armenian outlets (Panorama.am, Azat TV) reproduces the same statement and adds details consistent with the Armenian Investigative Committee's role. No tier-1 source linked at ingest contradicts the headline facts. Total damage figures and per-victim losses are not yet quantified by the source and are recorded as missing rather than inferred.

title: Ontario RCMP shuts down prolific Cyber-fraudsters
text:
## Source Summary

Official Royal Canadian Mounted Police press release issued by RCMP Central Region (Ontario) on 2025-02-20 announcing that the Ontario RCMP Cybercrime Investigative Team (CIT) Toronto arrested two Toronto residents — Chakib Mansouri (29) and Majdouline Alouah (31) — for operating one of Canada's most prolific spoofing-as-a-service downstream fraud schemes. The pair are alleged to have been among the top 50 worldwide subscribers of the iSpoof.cc caller-ID spoofing platform, using it (with companion phishing and smishing methods) to impersonate banks, government and police, defrauding at least 570 Canadian victims of millions of dollars.

The release explicitly enumerates an international cooperation chain — RCMP CIT Toronto cooperating with the London Metropolitan Police, the Dutch National Police, EUROPOL and EUROJUST — alongside Canadian municipal, provincial and federal partners (Toronto Police, Peel Regional Police, FINTRAC, the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre, and the National Cybercrime Coordination Centre). This places the Canadian arrest in the broader iSpoof.cc takedown investigation lineage that began with the UK Metropolitan Police's Operation Elaborate in November 2022, in which Europol and Eurojust coordinated multi-jurisdictional action against thousands of iSpoof users worldwide.

## Relevance to International Cooperation on Cybercrime

This source is valuable for the wiki because:

1. It is a primary Canadian-government statement on a multi-country cybercrime cooperation chain in which Canada appears as the prosecuting jurisdiction for a downstream user of UK-takedown infrastructure — a pattern the wiki has documented less often than the original European-led takedown side.
2. It names the specific RCMP operational unit that handled the case (Cybercrime Investigative Team (CIT) Toronto, Central Region) and the named senior officer (Inspector Lina Dabit), reinforcing the existing [[canada-rcmp]] organizational record.
3. It documents an explicit thank-you list of international partners — London Metropolitan Police, Dutch National Police, EUROPOL, EUROJUST — which establishes the formal cross-border evidentiary/intelligence chain underlying the Canadian charges.
4. It supplies the precise Canadian Criminal Code section numbers used to charge spoofing-enabled fraud (s.342.1 unauthorized use of a computer; s.380(1) fraud; s.462.31 laundering; s.430(1.1) unauthorized possession of credit card data; s.354 possession of proceeds), which complements [[canada]]'s legislative inventory.

## Source Provenance

- The canonical RCMP URL (`https://www.grc-rcmp.gc.ca/en/news/2025/ontario-rcmp-shuts-prolific-cyber-fraudsters`) is bot-blocked from this environment (HTTP 403 to curl_cffi chrome124 TLS impersonation, a known property of the rcmp-grc.gc.ca domain). The press release text was retrieved verbatim from the official CNW Group / Newswire syndication mirror (`https://www.newswire.ca/news-releases/ontario-rcmp-shuts-down-prolific-cyber-fraudsters-832249540.html`, status 200 OK), which carries the explicit "SOURCE Royal Canadian Mounted Police - Central Region" attribution. CNW Group is the contracted distributor of RCMP Central Region press releases. WebSearch results from `rcmp.ca`, `grc-rcmp.gc.ca`, and `globalnews.ca` consistently match the Newswire mirror text, including the exact list of international partners and the named officers.
- Reliability: high (official law-enforcement communications).
- Credibility: confirmed (text and named partners cross-verified across multiple secondary outlets and the Newswire mirror; no contradiction observed).

## Cross-References

- Operation page: [[rcmp-toronto-ispoof-cyber-fraudsters-arrest-2025]].
- Sister RCMP-Central-Region cybercrime IC source: [[2025-09-10_rcmp_canada-fbi-twitter-nft-redman-extradition]].
- Canadian agency: [[canada-rcmp]].
- Canadian country profile: [[canada]].

title: Global Hacker Arrested In Thailand Arising From Joint Collaboration Between The Singapore Police Force And Royal Thai Police
text:
## Source Summary

Singapore Police Force press release dated **27 February 2025** announcing the **26 February 2025 arrest** in Thailand of a **39-year-old man** suspected of being the threat actor operating under aliases **ALTDOS, DESORDEN, GHOSTR, and 0mid16B** — collectively responsible for **at least 75 international data-breach-extortion cases** since 2020. The arrest was made by the **Royal Thai Police (RTP) Central Investigation Bureau (CIB)** under the Thai Criminal Code and Computer-Related Crime Act, with assets valued at **over 10 million THB** seized (laptops, mobile phones, luxury vehicles, branded bags).

The investigation was a **~5-year cross-border collaboration** between SPF Cybercrime Command (CCC) and RTP CIB, supported by the **Cyber Security Agency (CSA) of Singapore** and (per the corresponding Group-IB release) **Group-IB's Digital Crime Resistance Centers in Thailand and Singapore**. The case originated in **2020 from reports by 11 Singapore victims** and expanded to encompass victims across Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, India, the United Kingdom, the United States, Canada, and the Middle East.

The release establishes:

1. **SPF-RTP bilateral cybercrime cooperation track** as a documented pattern with a successful 5-year multi-alias data-breach-extortion case as a reference outcome.
2. **Cross-jurisdictional alias-correlation pattern** — the same threat actor operating under multiple aliases (ALTDOS, DESORDEN, GHOSTR, 0mid16B) over 5 years, requiring sustained writing-style + targeting + posting-pattern analysis to attribute. This is the analytical-tradecraft layer enabling the eventual arrest.
3. **Extortion-not-ransomware pattern** — the threat actor exfiltrated databases and extorted victims for non-disclosure rather than encrypting (with rare exceptions) — a structurally distinct attack pattern from ransomware actors. Reputational pressure escalation via customer notifications + media + DP regulators.
4. **Public-private partnership pattern** — Group-IB DCRCs (Singapore + Thailand) as the long-run private-sector tracking layer; SPF + RTP + CSA as the public-sector arrest-and-prosecution layer.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 SPF primary press release with named senior officers (AC Paul Tay, SPF Cybercrime Command Commander; Police Lt Gen Jirabhop Bhuridej, RTP CIB Commissioner).
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational facts (arrest, assets seized, charges, alias correlation) come directly from SPF + RTP. The Group-IB private-sector release independently corroborates.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via SPF Newsroom.

title: Rigspolitiet (NSK): Danskledet international politiindsats mod AI-genereret overgrebsmateriale — Operation Cumberland
text:
## Source Profile

Official press release issued by Denmark's National Special Crime Unit
(National enhed for Særlig Kriminalitet — NSK / Nationalt Cyber Crime Center —
NC3), published on the Rigspolitiet domain `politi.dk`. NSK is the unit that
initiated and led the international phase of the investigation; the press
release names the operation as a Danish-led action and announces the
co-publication by Europol.

A secondary tier-1 source — the Europol newsroom press release of the same
date (2025-02-28) — is cross-cited in the raw file because it provides the
authoritative operation codename **Operation Cumberland** and the full roster
of 21 named participating agencies across 19 countries. Both releases are
preserved verbatim (within fair-use length) in the raw file so that any
subsequent fact-check can verify wording without re-fetching.

The Danish release is the *primary* tier-1 source for the Danish-side facts
(domestic conviction, the 29-year-old's biographical details, the two Danish
buyer suspects, the appeal posture). The Europol release is the *primary*
tier-1 source for the multilateral coordination role (J-CAT, EC3) and the
country/agency roster. Neither contradicts the other; the two sets of figures
match exactly.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official publication on the Rigspolitiet domain
  `politi.dk` (Danish national police); Europol cross-citation also tier-1.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement by the lead
  investigative agency, with the action-day date and figures stated
  unambiguously and matched by Europol.
- **Sensitivity: public** — both releases are intended for general
  distribution. Defendants' identities are protected (only ages and
  jurisdictional police districts are stated for the two Danish customers and
  the producer).

## Why this source is durable for the wiki

1. It is the *first* publicly documented multi-country police action squarely
   addressing fully synthetic, AI-generated CSAM — a case-study reference for
   the [[csam-ic]] crime-type page and for any future entry on legal gaps in
   criminalising AI-generated material.
2. It illustrates the *J-CAT* coordination model in production: a national
   force (NSK) initiating, Europol's J-CAT providing the operational
   coordination layer, EC3 facilitating information exchange and analysis.
3. It cleanly demonstrates a financial-trail attribution chain
   (betalingssporet) used to identify buyers across 19 jurisdictions from a
   single seized platform.

## Italian-style original-language preservation

See the raw file for the Danish-language legal-term table. Key terms to
preserve in any wiki prose: *AI-genereret overgrebsmateriale*, *sigtet*,
*anholdt*, *Landsretten*, *Østjyllands Politi*, *Nordsjællands Politi*,
*National enhed for Særlig Kriminalitet (NSK)*.

title: Justice Department Charges 12 Chinese Contract Hackers and Law Enforcement Officers in Global Computer Intrusion Campaigns
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ press release announcing the charging of 12 Chinese nationals affiliated with i-Soon (Anxun Information Technology) and APT27 for global computer intrusion campaigns directed by China's MPS and MSS. The coordinated action involved DOJ, FBI, NCIS, State Department, and Treasury. i-Soon charged USD 10,000-75,000 per successfully exploited email inbox. Victims included foreign ministries in Asia and US-based dissidents.

## Relevance to IC

This source illustrates the intersection of state-sponsored cyber espionage and cybercrime enforcement. While not a traditional multi-country law enforcement operation, it demonstrates US multi-agency coordination and the use of indictments, sanctions, and Rewards for Justice as enforcement tools against state-directed hacking. South Korea's inclusion as a victim country is relevant to the Korea-focused lens of this wiki.

title: Europol-led Operation Stream Takes Down Kidflix CSAM Platform
text:
# Europol-led Operation Stream Takes Down Kidflix CSAM Platform

## Source Overview

Security Affairs and TRM Labs reported on the Europol-coordinated takedown of Kidflix, one of the largest known child sexual abuse material (CSAM) streaming platforms. The operation, led by the Bavarian State Criminal Police and ZCB, involved 35+ countries and resulted in 79 arrests and the identification of 1,393 suspects.

## Key Data Points

- Platform operational since 2021; seized March 11, 2025
- 1.8 million registered users; 91,000 unique videos (6,288 hours)
- 79 arrests across 35+ countries
- 3,000+ devices seized; 39 children identified and protected
- Cryptocurrency-based payment system traced through exchanges and DeFi platforms

## References

- [Security Affairs: Europol-led op shuts down CSAM platform Kidflix](https://securityaffairs.com/176154/cyber-crime/europol-led-op-shuts-down-csam-platform-kidflix.html)
- [TRM Labs: Europol and German Authorities Lead Global Takedown of Darknet Child Exploitation Platform Kidflix](https://www.trmlabs.com/resources/blog/europol-and-german-authorities-lead-global-takedown-of-darknet-child-exploitation-platform-kidflix)

title: Operation FEVER — Largest European CSAM crackdown organised by Polish CBZC during EU Council Presidency
text:
# Operation FEVER — CBZC Press Release (Polish, 2025-04-09)

## Source Overview

Official press release published on **9 April 2025** by **Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości (CBZC)**, the Polish Police's Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime. Polish-language original. Tier-1 primary source: a national cybercrime law-enforcement agency directly reporting on its own coordinated international action.

The release announces and describes Operation **FEVER**, a CBZC-led umbrella operation against producers, distributors, and possessors of child sexual abuse material (CSAM), conducted while Poland held the Presidency of the Council of the European Union (1 January – 30 June 2025). CBZC frames this as the largest operation it has ever organised together with the Komenda Główna Policji (KGP — National Police Headquarters).

## Key findings

- **166 suspects detained internationally**; **98 in Poland** (ages 22–78); **48** placed in pre-trial detention by Polish courts; **111** arrested for sharing/distributing CSAM (per the release).
- **Polish operational footprint:** 159 searches, nearly 600 officers, more than 1,700 devices/data carriers seized, more than 520,000 CSAM video and image files seized; narcotics and 3 unauthorised firearms also seized.
- **Twelve named participating countries:** Poland (lead) + Denmark, Estonia, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Latvia, Romania, Spain, Sweden, Bulgaria. Additionally **UK (NCA)** and **US (FBI, HSI, NCMEC)** participated at international level.
- **Coordination mechanisms:** **Europol** and **J-CAT (Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce)** are explicitly named.
- **Polish prosecutorial actor:** Departament do Spraw Cyberprzestępczości i Informatyzacji Prokuratury Krajowej (cybercrime department of the National Public Prosecutor's Office).
- **Embedded sub-operations:** the release specifies that **"Kidflix" (with German police)** was a component of FEVER, with 12 Polish persons of interest reached and the platform's server seized in Germany/Netherlands on 11 March 2025; and a separate **CBZC–FBI dark web forum** track.
- **Historical framing:** five earlier domestic CBZC operations (ANASTAZJA, BARBOSSA, CARLOS, DAKOTA, ENOLA GAY, 2022–2024) charged 280+ people and secured 2M+ CSAM files; FEVER is presented as the international scale-up.

## Reliability assessment

- **Reliability:** **high** — first-party government law-enforcement press release issued by the operating agency.
- **Credibility:** **confirmed** — claims made by CBZC about its own operation; numbers (166/98/111/159/600/1700/520k) are stated as final or interim ("data may rise after device examination is complete").
- **Sensitivity:** **public** — published on the official CBZC newsroom URL and intended for public dissemination.

## Cross-references

- Umbrella operation page created from this source: `[[operation-fever-cbzc-eu-presidency-2025]]`
- Pre-existing related operation: `[[operation-stream-kidflix]]` (Kidflix sub-component)
- Polish lead agency: `[[poland-police]]` (CBZC sits within KGP/Polish Police)
- Coordination: `[[europol-ec3]]`, `[[j-cat]]`
- US partners: `[[fbi]]`, `[[ncmec]]`
- UK partner: `[[uk-nca]]`
- German partner: `[[germany-bka]]`
- Crime type: `[[csam-ic]]`

## Verification

- Fetched 2026-05-09 with `curl_cffi` (impersonate=chrome124), HTTP 200, 540 KB HTML, full Polish article body extracted to `_workspace/cbzc_fever_text.txt` for translation.
- All quantitative claims and country list reproduced from the original release verbatim.
- Source URL is on the official `cbzc.policja.gov.pl` government domain.

text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2025-05-07 announcing the May 2025 phase of Operation PowerOFF — a 4-country coordinated takedown of a DDoS-for-hire empire running 6 stresser/booter services (Cfxapi, Cfxsecurity, neostress, jetstress, quickdown, zapcut) active 2022-2025. Polish-led; 4 admins arrested in Poland; 9 US booter-service domains seized; Dutch fake-booter-site warning campaign + Dutch data sharing; German BKA + ZIT identified suspects; Europol EC3 analytical and operational support.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a 4-country Polish-led DDoS-for-hire takedown pattern under Operation PowerOFF:

- **Polish Central Cybercrime Bureau (CBZC) as action-day lead** — first wiki record of CBZC as a Polish federal-cybercrime authority leading an Operation PowerOFF phase.
- **Dutch fake-booter-site warning methodology** — first wiki record of this specific deception-based deterrence mechanism (Dutch authorities running honeypot websites that warn users seeking out DDoS-for-hire services).
- **Dutch data-centre seizure data shared with Polish investigators** — a discrete IC mechanism class (cross-border data transfer from infrastructure seizure to suspect identification).
- **German BKA + ZIT Frankfurt suspect-identification + intelligence-sharing** — structurally complementary to the German BKA + ZIT involvement recorded in [[archetyp-market-takedown-operation-deep-sentinel-2025|Operation Deep Sentinel]].
- **US 9-domain seizure** parallel to Polish arrests — concurrent administrative-and-criminal action pattern.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release is the May 2025 phase of [[operation-power-off|Operation PowerOFF]], complementing:
- [[operation-power-off-2026-04|Operation PowerOFF 2026-04 action week]] (April 2026 phase, 21-country)
- [[operation-power-off|Operation PowerOFF parent page]]

Operation PowerOFF is one of the wiki's longest-running multi-phase IC operations, with the May 2025 phase establishing the Polish-led take-down model and the April 2026 phase scaling to 21 countries.

title: Úspěšná mezinárodní operace PATHFINDER (Successful international operation PATHFINDER)
text:
## Source Summary

Czech NCTEKK (Národní centrála proti terorismu, extremismu a kybernetické kriminalitě) press release dated 2025-05-13, signed by NCTEKK director plk. JUDr. Břetislav Brejcha, announcing the successful conclusion of action days (2025-04-29 to 2025-05-02) of international operation "PATHFINDER" — a 6-month Eurojust JIT-framework cooperation between the Czech NCTEKK Cybercrime Department and the Security Service of Ukraine (SBU), under procedural supervision of the District State Prosecutor's Office in Hradec Králové (Czech side) and the regional prosecutor's office in Zakarpattia (Ukrainian side). The operation dismantled a Zakarpattia-based fake-investment-platform call-centre OCG with 70 detained, including 4 organizers charged under Ukrainian Criminal Code § 190(5).

## IC Pattern

This release documents a **bilateral CZ–UA Eurojust JIT** with on-site Czech police participation in Ukrainian action days — a configuration that distinguishes itself from the broader multi-jurisdiction Eurojust JITs (e.g., the Latvia-led `[[operation-octopus-investment-fraud-czechia-ukraine-2025]]`-style four-jurisdiction arrangements) by its **two-state, single-victim-jurisdiction structure** (Czech Republic as primary victim jurisdiction; Ukraine as host of the criminal infrastructure). Notable IC features:

- **NCTEKK** as the Czech cybercrime authority — first wiki record of an NCTEKK-led action where Czech officers directly accompanied Ukrainian search-and-seizure operations in Zakarpattia.
- **SBU as Ukrainian counterpart** — Ukraine's intelligence service (rather than the Cyber Police of Ukraine) led the Ukrainian side of the JIT, reflecting the cybercrime/national-security overlap framework increasingly used by Ukraine for transnational fraud OCGs operating from its territory.
- **Eurojust JIT as the sole named coordinating body** — Europol/EC3 involvement is NOT named in this Czech release, distinguishing PATHFINDER from typical Europol-Eurojust dual-coordination patterns.
- **Zakarpattia as call-centre geography** — adds a distinct geographic data point alongside Dnipro / Ivano-Frankivsk / Kyiv (recorded in `[[ukraine-fraudulent-call-centres-takedown-2025]]` and `[[dnipro-fraudulent-call-centre-takedown-2026]]`) for Ukraine-hosted EU-victim-targeting fake-investment OCGs.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This source supports creation of a new operation page `[[operation-pathfinder-czech-ukraine-investment-fraud-takedown-2025]]` and updates the `operations_participated` list of `[[czechia-police]]`, `[[czech-police]]`, `[[ukraine-sbu]]`, and `[[eurojust]]`. It adds Zakarpattia oblast as a new geographic node alongside the previously recorded Dnipro/Ivano-Frankivsk/Kyiv call-centre clusters in the wiki's Ukraine cybercrime-call-centre coverage, and it provides the first wiki record of NCTEKK Cybercrime Department + SBU bilateral on-site cooperation under the Eurojust JIT framework. Cross-references to the Latvia-Ukraine vishing JIT (`[[latvia-phone-fraud-money-mule-network-takedown-2026]]`) and the Czech-Ukraine voice-phishing operation OCTOPUS (`[[operation-octopus-investment-fraud-czechia-ukraine-2025]]`) help situate PATHFINDER within the broader 2024–2026 wave of Eurojust-supported takedowns of Ukraine-hosted call-centre OCGs.

title: United States and Taiwan Collaborate to Dismantle International Financial Fraud Network (AIT PR-2510)
text:
## Summary

Official press release **PR-2510** issued by the American Institute in Taiwan (AIT) on 2025-05-14, announcing the joint U.S.-Taiwan dismantling of a Taiwanese-led financial-fraud network that targeted elderly victims across the United States and laundered over US$100 million. The release accompanies a joint press conference held at the Taiwan Taichung District Prosecutors Office (TTDPO) on the same date.

This is a **tier-1 primary source**: AIT is the U.S. government's unofficial-relations agency for Taiwan and operates under the auspices of the U.S. Department of State, making AIT press releases authoritative for U.S.-side characterisations of Taiwan-U.S. law-enforcement cooperation. The release names participating units (Taiwan NPA CIB, MOJ, TTDPO, HSI Taipei/LA/Nashville) and identifies the legal channel (the bilateral MLAA signed in March 2002).

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability**: high. AIT press releases are issued through the agency's official website (ait.org.tw) and serve as the U.S. State Department's primary communication channel concerning Taiwan; AIT is the institutional U.S. counterpart for U.S.-Taiwan relations.
- **Credibility**: confirmed. The cooperation event (joint press conference 2025-05-14 at TTDPO) and the underlying indictment are independently corroborated by Taiwan's *Focus Taiwan* (CNA English service, 2025-05-14) and the *Taipei Times* (2025-05-15), both of which cite the TTDPO directly.
- **Sensitivity**: public. The press release is the public announcement; sealed pre-arrest investigative content is not disclosed.

## Cross-Source Verification Performed

| Fact | Source A (AIT PR-2510) | Source B (Taipei Times 2025-05-15 / Focus Taiwan 2025-05-14, citing TTDPO) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint press conference date | 2025-05-14, at TTDPO | 2025-05-14, at TTDPO | Match |
| Investigation start | "began in May 2023" | "Huang recruited 2023" (consistent) | Consistent |
| Defendant Liao identified | "suspect surnamed LIAO ... high-level ranking leader" | "Liao (廖), age 36 ... led fraud ring; arrested 2025-01-24" | Match (more detail in TTDPO subset) |
| Cooperation channel | "Mutual Legal Assistance Agreement (MLAA) request" | "Taiwan-US mutual legal assistance treaty signed March 2002" | Consistent |
| HSI offices | Taipei, Los Angeles, Nashville | "HSI team ... March 2025"; "prosecutors traveled to Los Angeles and Nashville April 2025" | Consistent |
| US$ figure | "over US$100 million" laundered (overall organisation) | "US$5.32 million" (3-victim indictment subset) | Different scope, not contradictory |
| Elderly victim targeting | "hundreds of elderly victims across the United States" | Romance-scam pretext on U.S. citizens (specific ages not given) | Consistent in modus |

No contradictions found. The AIT figure characterises the overall organisation; the Taipei Times / Focus Taiwan figure describes the specific TTDPO indictment subset.

## Notes on the Source Outlet

AIT (American Institute in Taiwan) is the de-facto U.S. embassy in Taiwan; established 1979 under the Taiwan Relations Act, AIT is administered by the U.S. Department of State and functions as the formal U.S. communication and protocol channel with Taiwan in the absence of formal diplomatic recognition. AIT press releases concerning Taiwan-U.S. law enforcement cooperation are therefore the U.S. tier-1 primary record for such cooperation, alongside Taiwan-side documents from the MOJ and TTDPO.

## Pages Updated

- Operation page created: [[us-taiwan-elder-romance-fraud-mlaa-takedown-2025]]

title: Wellington-based man arrested in international cryptocurrency scam investigation
text:
## Source Summary

On **16 May 2025**, [[new-zealand|New Zealand]] Police's Financial Crime Group announced the arrest in Auckland of a Wellington-based individual as part of an [[fbi|FBI]]-led investigation into a transnational cryptocurrency-theft and money-laundering ring. The release frames the case as a joint NZ Police / FBI investigation supporting a [[united-states|United States]] [[us-doj|Department of Justice]] indictment under RICO, wire-fraud conspiracy, and money-laundering conspiracy statutes.

The cited theft cohort numbered seven victims and the headline aggregate theft was US$265 million (NZD$450 million) in cryptocurrency, allegedly stolen between March and August 2024 and laundered across multiple platforms. Coordinated search-warrant execution spanned Auckland, Wellington and California over a three-day period and yielded 13 arrests in total across the two-country operation.

The suspect was granted interim name suppression at Auckland District Court and was scheduled to reappear on 3 July 2025. NZ Police framed the case publicly as a worked example of why cross-border criminal activity demands "strong international law-enforcement partnerships" with the FBI.

## Relevance to IC

This is the wiki's first tier-1 primary cybercrime IC press release sourced from the **New Zealand Police newsroom**, which had only one prior wiki source page (a 2016 darknet-drug item). It establishes a tier-1 primary record of:

- An **NZ Police – FBI joint investigation** as a discrete bilateral cybercrime IC channel.
- A **multi-jurisdictional same-week search-warrant execution pattern** across Auckland, Wellington and California — a cooperation tempo distinct from the slower MLAT pathway.
- A **U.S. DOJ RICO indictment of a New Zealand-arrested defendant**, implying a future US extradition pathway (the release does not assert extradition has been requested or granted).

The `raw_path` metadata links this page to the archived corpus record at `raw/press-releases/2025-05-16_police-govt-nz_wellington-based-man-arrested-international-cryptocurrency-scam-investigation.md`.

title: Leader of Qakbot Malware Conspiracy Indicted for Involvement in Global Ransomware Scheme
text:
## Source Summary

On 22 May 2025, the Department of Justice unsealed an indictment charging Rustam Rafailevich Gallyamov, 48, of Moscow, Russia, with leading the QakBot malware conspiracy since 2008. The indictment was unsealed in conjunction with Operation Endgame, the international law enforcement campaign targeting ransomware-enabling infrastructure. ### Charges Gallyamov was charged with conspiracy, wire fraud, and related offenses. He allegedly developed and deployed QakBot to build a botnet that provided access to co-conspirators who deployed ransomware variants including ProLock, DopplePaymer, Egregor, REvil, Conti, Name Locker, Black Basta, and Cactus.

## Relevance to IC

title: Operation Endgame strikes again: the ransomware kill chain broken at its source
text:
## Summary

Official Europol press release announcing the second major phase of **Operation Endgame**, which disrupted ransomware-enabling infrastructure at an unprecedented scale during 19-22 May 2025. The operation targeted initial-access malware families that form the beginning of the ransomware kill chain.

The source details the scale of infrastructure disruption (300 servers, 650 domains), cryptocurrency seizures (EUR 3.5M this phase, EUR 21.2M+ cumulative), and the issuance of 20 international arrest warrants with 18 EU Most Wanted listings. Coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]] with participation from 7 countries and 15 agencies.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | 2 (confirmed — official source) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 |
| Data Completeness | 100/100 |
| Verification | Cross-validated against Phase 1 (May 2024); distinct events confirmed |

## Key Metrics

- **Servers taken down:** 300
- **Domains neutralized:** 650
- **Cryptocurrency seized (this phase):** EUR 3.5 million
- **Cryptocurrency seized (total Endgame):** EUR 21.2 million+
- **International arrest warrants:** 20
- **EU Most Wanted additions:** 18
- **Participating countries:** 7
- **Participating agencies:** 15

title: Pakistan Arrests 21 in 'Heartsender' Malware Service
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Heart Blocker was a joint US-Dutch law enforcement action on January 29, 2025, targeting the HeartSender cybercrime marketplace network (also known as Saim Raza). The U.S. Department of Justice and the Dutch National Police coordinated the seizure of 39 domains and their associated servers that had been operating since at least 2020 as marketplaces selling phishing kits, credential-stealing tools, email extractors, and scam page generators. These tools were used by transnational organized crime groups to conduct [[bec-ic|business email compromise (BEC)]] schemes, resulting in over $3 million in confirmed US victim losses according to the DOJ (later revised to over $50 million by Pakistan's NCCIA).

## Relevance to IC

title: DOJ seizes 145 domains tied to BidenCash marketplace
text:
## Source Summary

On 5 June 2025, the US Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of Virginia announced the seizure of approximately 145 domains (both darknet and clearnet) and cryptocurrency funds associated with the BidenCash criminal marketplace. The operation was led by the [[us-secret-service|US Secret Service]] Frankfurt Resident Office and Cyber Investigative Section, together with the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]] Albuquerque Field Office, and coordinated by the [[us-doj|US Department of Justice]]. International partners included the [[netherlands-politie|Dutch National High Tech Crime Unit]] and private-sector firms Searchlight Cyber and The Shadowserver Foundation. The seized domains were redirected to a US law enforcement-controlled server. BidenCash, which had operated since March 2022, had served over 117,000 customers, facilitated the trafficking of more than 15 million payment card numbers, and generated over USD 17 million in revenue. The seizure was part of the broader [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] framework. No arrests were announced as of the seizure date.

## Relevance to IC

The source supports [[bidencash-takedown]] in the `operations` corpus.

title: Hong Kong Police conducts cross-border anti-scam operation with six countries and regions (FRONTIER+ first joint operation)
text:
## Summary

Official Hong Kong SAR Government Information Services press release (P2025060300475) of 2025-06-03 reporting the results of the **first joint operation conducted under the Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform "FRONTIER+"**, executed 2025-04-28 to 2025-05-28. The release names six partner jurisdictions (Macao SAR, Malaysia, the Maldives, Singapore, South Korea, Thailand) plus Hong Kong, and provides aggregate enforcement totals along with a per-jurisdiction Annex.

This is a **tier-1 primary source**: official Hong Kong government press dissemination service issuing on behalf of the Hong Kong Police Force. The accompanying PDF Annex (also on info.gov.hk infrastructure) provides per-jurisdiction breakdowns of subjects interviewed, persons arrested, scam cases detected, financial loss, accounts frozen, and amounts recovered.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability**: high. Issued through HKSAR's official government information service (info.gov.hk) and identifies the issuing body (Hong Kong Police Force, Commercial Crime Bureau).
- **Credibility**: confirmed. Per-jurisdiction Annex figures cross-checked against the Singapore Police Force's *CAD Annual Report 2024* (already in raw corpus, file `2025-12-01_police-gov-sg_cad-report-2024-operation-first-light.md`), which independently confirms Singapore's figure of 106 arrests and S$7.7M (≈US$5.98M) seized in this operation, and also describes FRONTIER+ formalisation on 22 October 2024.
- **Sensitivity**: public.

## Cross-Source Verification Performed

| Fact | Source A (HK info.gov.hk) | Source B (SPF CAD 2024 report) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operation period | "April 28 to May 28" 2025 | "between April and May 2025" | Consistent |
| Singapore arrests | 106 | 106 | Match |
| Singapore seizure | US$5.98M (S$7.69M) | "S$7.7 million" | Match |
| FRONTIER+ launch | October 2024 | "formalised … on 22 October 2024" | Consistent |
| Total arrests across operation | 1,858 | "over 1,800" | Consistent |

No contradictions found.

## Pages Updated

- Operation page created: [[frontier-plus-cross-border-anti-scam-2025]]

title: Eurojust assists in operation in Romania and Moldova against laundering phishing fraud proceeds
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release dated 2025-06-12 reporting the same-day Romania-Moldova joint action day against an organised crime group laundering the proceeds of phishing fraud. The operation produced **44 simultaneous searches** in both countries and was executed within a **Joint Investigation Team** structure that Eurojust set up and financed in March 2025. The criminal network — comprised of Romanian, Moldovan, Ukrainian, and Italian suspects — had operated from professionally organised call centres in Moldova since at least 2013, harvesting EU-wide victim data through phishing and laundering at least EUR 20 million across 30 identified victims.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a Romania-Moldova phishing-fraud cooperation pattern previously absent from the wiki. Notable structural features:

- **Eurojust-coordinated JIT with a non-EU third country (Moldova)**: Moldova is one of twelve countries holding a Liaison Prosecutor seat at Eurojust. The JIT vehicle here is the formal cooperation channel between an EU Member State (Romania) and a non-EU Liaison Prosecutor country (Moldova).
- **European Investigation Orders (EIOs) extended to Moldova** as a non-EU JIT participant — a procedural pattern of EIOs paired with a JIT in which Moldovan authorities act as both requesting and requested authorities.
- **Multi-nationality OCG operating from Moldova** (Romanian, Moldovan, Ukrainian, Italian) — call-centre staffing pattern that mirrors the Bangkok and Manila scam-centre cases recorded elsewhere in the wiki, but in a European phishing-and-laundering rather than investment-scam context.
- **Cryptocurrency-as-laundering-layer + false-ID Romanian bank accounts** — a hybrid laundering chain rather than a single-channel pattern.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case complements:
- [[afp-rtp-bangkok-scam-centre-operation-firestorm-2025|AFP-RTP Bangkok scam-centre takedown (2025-06-16)]] — multi-nationality OCG operating from a third country, AU-Anglosphere comparator.
- [[india-cbi-noida-tech-support-scam-takedown-2025|India CBI Noida tech-support scam takedown (2025-07-07)]] — call-centre pattern operated against foreign victims.
- Existing GoZNyM, Carbanak, and xDedic Moldova-anchored cases recorded in the wiki — establishes that Moldova-anchored cybercrime is a recurring rather than a one-off node, with Eurojust-coordinated JITs increasingly the formal cooperation channel.

The case is the first wiki record of a Eurojust-financed JIT specifically focused on **phishing-proceeds laundering** as the predicate crime, and the first wiki record of an **EU-Moldova JIT** under the post-March-2025 cooperation framework.

title: Europe-wide takedown hits longest-standing dark web drug market (Archetyp Market — Operation Deep Sentinel)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release announcing Operation Deep Sentinel — the 11-13 June 2025 takedown of Archetyp Market, the longest-running darknet drug marketplace, with German lead authorities (Frankfurt am Main ZIT + BKA) and 5-EU-state plus US cooperation. Administrator arrested in Barcelona; 1 moderator + 6 top vendors targeted in Germany/Sweden; EUR 7.8M assets seized; 600,000+ users / EUR 250M+ transactions / 17,000+ listings disrupted.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a fully-articulated 6-jurisdiction darknet-marketplace takedown pattern with explicit Eurojust EIO + MLA coordination during the action days:

- **Germany**: lead jurisdiction (Frankfurt am Main ZIT + BKA).
- **Netherlands**: infrastructure-hosting jurisdiction (platform infrastructure in NL taken offline).
- **Spain**: arrest jurisdiction for the German-national administrator (Barcelona).
- **Sweden + Germany**: action jurisdictions for 1 moderator + 6 highest vendors.
- **Romania**: action partner (specifics not enumerated).
- **United States**: HSI + IRS-CI + USDOJ analytical and operational partners.
- **Europol**: coordination meetings + analytical support.
- **Eurojust**: MLA + European Investigation Order coordination during action days and preliminary investigation.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case fills the 2025-Q2 darknet-marketplace takedown gap in the wiki and complements:
- [[150-arrested-in-dark-web-drug-bust-as-police-seize-eur-26-million|150 arrested in dark web drug bust (2025)]] (Eurojust-coordinated drug-marketplace action)
- [[operation-raptor|Operation Raptor]] (270 arrests, dark web sting)
- [[operation-owners-of-empire-market-charged-in-chicago-with-operating-430-million-dark-web-marketplace|Empire Market]] (US prosecution)

The Archetyp Market takedown is the wiki's first record of Operation Deep Sentinel as a discrete operation name, and the most fully-articulated Germany-Spain-Netherlands-Sweden-Romania-US 6-jurisdiction darknet-marketplace takedown architecture.

title: Busted: Police shut down Bangkok-based investment scam centre targeting Australians
text:
## Source Summary

AFP press release dated 2025-06-17 reporting the 2025-06-16 RTP-AFP joint takedown of a Bangkok-based investment scam centre. The operation is part of the AFP-led JPC3 Operation Firestorm (launched August 2024) and represents the **first Thailand takedown under Operation Firestorm**, with three prior takedowns in Manila. 13 arrests across 4 nationalities (Australia, UK, Canada, South Africa); the scammers used fraudulent international-financial-firm representations to sell fake high-yield fixed-income bonds, with proceeds laundered through cryptocurrency.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes an Australia-Thailand bilateral cybercrime cooperation pattern that the wiki has not previously documented. Notable features:

- **AFP-RTP bilateral cooperation** under the JPC3 Operation Firestorm umbrella.
- **AFP International Network** as the cross-jurisdictional alerting channel — a structural complement to the [[india-cbi-noida-tech-support-scam-takedown-2025|NCA International Liaison Officer network]] (UK NCA) and [[operation-token-mirrors-2026|FBI Law Enforcement Attaché]] (US) IC channels documented in earlier iterations.
- **Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (JPC3)** as a long-running cybercrime cooperation framework — first wiki record.
- **Operation Firestorm** as a discrete operation name — first wiki record.
- **5-nationality defendant base** (AU, UK, CA, ZA) within a single boiler-room operation in Thailand demonstrates a trans-Anglosphere recruitment pattern for offshore scam-centre staffing.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case complements:
- [[india-cbi-noida-tech-support-scam-takedown-2025|India CBI Noida tech-support scam takedown (2025-07-07)]] — UK NCA-led 4-party cooperation
- [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|Korea-Cambodia-Philippines 73-defendant extradition (2026-04)]] — KNPA-led repatriation
- [[uk-nigeria-meta-scam-centre-disruption-2026|UK-Nigeria Meta scam-centre disruption (2026-02)]] — NCA-led intelligence sharing

Together these form a 2025-2026 national-LE-as-action-day-lead cluster of transnational scam-centre takedowns originating from Australia (AFP), UK (NCA), and Korea (KNPA), each acting via different IC channels (JPC3, NCA International Liaison Officer, KNPA bilateral arrangements).

title: 11 Persons Arrested In A Joint Enforcement Operation Against A Transnational Scam Syndicate By The Singapore Police Force, Hong Kong Police Force And The Royal Malaysia Police
text:
# Source Summary

Official Singapore Police Force press release dated 17 June 2025 announcing the conclusion of a tri-lateral joint enforcement operation conducted between SPF, the Hong Kong Police Force, and the Royal Malaysia Police from April to June 2025. The operation dismantled a criminal syndicate operating Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) GSM gateway devices that routed fraudulent scam calls through local mobile phone networks in all three jurisdictions, leading victims in Singapore to believe the calls were originating domestically.

## Verified Facts

- **Operation period**: April 2025 – June 2025 (approximately two months of sustained intelligence gathering and investigations).
- **Arrests**: 11 persons total, aged between 24 and 48, distributed across the three jurisdictions.
- **Seizures**: 226 GSM gateway devices total — 75 in Singapore, 141 in Hong Kong, 10 in Malaysia.
- **Modus operandi**: Cloud-based remote operating system linking to GSM mobile networks across the three jurisdictions, enabling remote routing of fraudulent calls through local cellular infrastructure to disguise call origin.
- **Specific charge in Singapore**: A 29-year-old suspect arrested in Malaysia on 3 June 2025, was handed over to Singapore and charged in court on 5 June 2025 with Being a Party to a Criminal Conspiracy to commit Cheating offences under Section 420 read with Section 120B of the Penal Code 1871. Maximum penalty: 10 years' imprisonment plus fine.
- **Coordination platform**: FRONTIER+, a Hong Kong–led Cross-border Anti-Scam Collaboration Platform comprising 10 jurisdictions (including SG, HK, MY).
- **Senior officer quote**: David Chew, Director of the SPF Commercial Affairs Department.

## Reliability Assessment

- **reliability: high** — official Singapore Police Force communiqué, dual-attributed (SPF Public Affairs Department) with named senior officer.
- **credibility: confirmed** — operational facts (arrests, seizures, hand-over and charge) corroborated by independent secondary reporting (SCMP, RTHK, Hong Kong Police media, Malaysia press).
- **sensitivity: public** — full text published on police.gov.sg media hub.

## Notes

- This operation is distinct from the FRONTIER+ first joint cross-border anti-scam operation (28 April – 28 May 2025), which involved seven jurisdictions and produced 1,858 arrests across multi-fraud typologies. The June 17 release covers a tri-lateral, infrastructure-focused VoIP/GSM gateway takedown that ran in parallel under the same FRONTIER+ umbrella.
- Article-level legal anchor: Singapore Penal Code 1871, sections 420 (cheating) and 120B (criminal conspiracy).

title: Singapore Police Force Partners INTERPOL And Federal Police Of Brazil In Transnational Operation Against Cybercriminals
text:
## Source Summary

SPF press release dated 2025-06-17 announcing Operation Cryptoscam — a Singapore-Brazil cooperation case originated by SPF based on a January 2024 USD 1.4 million crypto-wallet compromise report, traced to Brazil and alerted to the Brazilian Federal Police (FPB) via INTERPOL channels. FPB executed the action in May 2025 across two simultaneous operations (one of which was Operation Cryptoscam) producing 26 search-and-seizure warrants + 11 arrest warrants.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a Singapore → INTERPOL → Brazil intelligence-pipelined cooperation pattern that the wiki has not previously documented. Notable features:

- **SPF-as-originating-IC-partner** — the operation began as a domestic Singapore investigation (CCC under CID) following a USD 1.4 million crypto-wallet-compromise report.
- **INTERPOL international cooperation channels** as the cross-jurisdictional alerting mechanism.
- **Federal Police of Brazil (FPB)** — Repression Electronic Bank Fraud Coordination + FPB Regional Office Itajaí, Santa Catarina — as the Brazil-side action authorities.
- **Two-operation simultaneous May 2025 FPB action-week** with 26 search/seizure + 11 arrest warrants — Operation Cryptoscam being the SPF-originated track and one other (unnamed) parallel track being a Brazil-domestic financial-crime investigation that ran concurrently.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case fills a Singapore-Brazil cooperation gap in the wiki and complements:
- [[operation-token-mirrors-2026|Operation Token Mirrors]] (US-Singapore extradition cooperation, 2026)
- [[operation-secure-interpol|Operation Secure 2025]] (INTERPOL-coordinated infostealer takedown)
- [[operation-haechi-vi|Operation HAECHI VI]] (INTERPOL-coordinated financial-crime sweep)

The Singapore-Brazil bilateral cooperation channel is novel for the wiki — Brazil is more typically recorded as a US-prosecution jurisdiction (e.g. extraditions of Brazilian nationals from the US) rather than as a primary action-day jurisdiction in Singapore-originated cybercrime cases.
