title: Serial Hacker 'IntelBroker' Charged For Causing $25 Million In Damages To Victims
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 U.S. Attorney's Office (SDNY) press release, issued jointly with FBI New York, announcing the unsealing of a four-count criminal Indictment and Complaint against Kai West (a/k/a "IntelBroker," a/k/a "Kyle Northern"), 25, a British national, for a years-long hacking and stolen-data resale scheme operated through the "IntelBroker" online identity on a hacker marketplace identified as "Forum-1" (BreachForums). West was arrested in France in February 2025 and the United States is seeking his extradition.

## Relevance to IC

- Tri-national-plus cooperation: arrest by French authorities (February 2025) and acknowledged assistance from French, Spanish, British, and Dutch authorities, coordinated by the DOJ Office of International Affairs and FBI New York. Establishes a clean US–FR–UK extradition track plus ES/NL evidentiary cooperation.
- Cybercrime-forum nexus: West is identified as the "owner" of Forum-1 (BreachForums) from August 2024 to January 2025, anchoring the case to the BreachForums-as-marketplace storyline parallel to the LeakBase takedown ([[leakbase-takedown-2026]]).
- Cross-references: feeds [[us-v-kai-west-intelbroker-2025]]; supports IC patterns on extradition from France, OIA-coordinated multi-MLA fan-out, and cryptocurrency-anonymity friction (Monero).

## Notes

- The press release identifies the underlying forum only as "Forum-1" but the IntelBroker handle and ownership window are publicly associated with BreachForums. The case page documents this as a high-confidence external attribution rather than a quote from the release.
- "Updated June 26, 2025" appears in the page metadata; the press release publication date is Wednesday, June 25, 2025. Slugs use the publish date.

title: Crypto investment fraud ring dismantled in Spain after defrauding 5 000 victims worldwide (EUR 460 million laundered)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2025-06-30 reporting the 25 June 2025 action day in Spain (Canary Islands and Madrid) that dismantled a cryptocurrency investment fraud network. The release names a four-jurisdiction cooperation chain — Spain, Estonia, France (New Caledonia gendarmerie), and the United States — with Europol coordination since 2023 and on-day deployment of a Europol crypto specialist. Headline financial figure: EUR 460 million laundered, 5,000+ victims worldwide. Action-day outcomes: 5 arrests and 5 searches (3 Canary Islands, 2 Madrid). The release also identifies a Hong Kong-based corporate-and-banking layer used by the network for fund laundering through payment gateways and third-party exchange accounts.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 primary source for a 2025 four-jurisdiction crypto investment fraud takedown that combines (a) EU member-state cooperation (Spain + Estonia) with (b) French overseas-territory gendarmerie participation (New Caledonia) and (c) US federal involvement via DHS Homeland Security Investigations, all coordinated by [[europol-ec3|Europol]]. The HSI participation is a notable instance of US involvement in an EU-led crypto fraud action that is not framed as an extradition or sanctions case — it documents an EU-coordinated investigative-cooperation pathway involving HSI's financial-crime mandate. The Hong Kong corporate-and-banking layer is the cross-jurisdictional money-laundering pattern of interest, although Hong Kong is not named as a participating jurisdiction in the action day.

## Note on text fidelity

Europol newsroom HTML embeds the press release body inside the `window.SERVER_DATA.body` JSON field. The verbatim content above was captured from that field via the project's TLS-spoofing fetch (`curl_cffi` `chrome124` impersonation) plus a JSON-body extraction step, per LESSONS L20. Action-day date is 2025-06-25; press-release publish date (epoch from `window.SERVER_DATA.published`) is 2025-06-30.

title: Justice Department Announces Coordinated, Nationwide Actions to Combat North Korean Remote Information Technology Workers' Illicit Revenue Generation Schemes
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release (25-680) dated 2025-06-30 (updated 2025-07-03) announcing the most comprehensive single-day enforcement wave in the DPRK RevGen: Domestic Enabler Initiative to date. The release packages two federal indictments (D. Mass. and N.D. Ga.), one separate plea/information (D. Mass.), one arrest (Zhenxing Wang), 29 laptop-farm searches across 16 states, 29 financial accounts and 21 fraudulent websites seized, and approximately 200 computers seized.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes the most fully-articulated multi-jurisdiction DPRK-IT-worker enforcement architecture in the wiki to date. Notable features:

- **DPRK RevGen: Domestic Enabler Initiative** — joint National Security Division + FBI Cyber and Counterintelligence Divisions effort prioritising US-based enablers of DPRK illicit revenue.
- **Cross-jurisdictional defendant base**: 8 Chinese nationals, 4 DPRK nationals, 2 Taiwanese nationals, 2 US nationals; co-conspirator locations include US, China, UAE, Taiwan; DPRK travel-document use to enter UAE.
- **Atlanta + Serbia employer cohort**: blockchain R&D company (Atlanta GA) + virtual token company (Serbia).
- **Tornado Cash + Malaysian fraudulent ID** as the laundering chain — connecting DPRK virtual-currency theft to a previously-sanctioned mixer ([[cryptex-pm2btc-sanctions|Cryptex / Pm2BTC]] is a different mixer; Tornado Cash was earlier OFAC-designated and partially de-listed).
- **ITAR data theft from California AI defense contractor** (2024-01-19 → 2024-04-02) as a national-security-relevant export-control violation embedded in an apparently economic-fraud scheme.
- **Multi-district laptop-farm actions** by FBI Denver Field Office across 14 states in June 10–17, 2025 — distinct from the October 2024 prior action across 3 states.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case complements:
- [[us-v-knoot-prince-dprk-laptop-farms-2026|US v. Knoot/Prince]] (2026-05 sentencings of two US-national laptop farm operators)
- [[3-north-korean-military-hackers-indicted-in-wide-ranging-scheme-to-commit-cyber-attacks-and-financial-crimes-a|Three North Korean Military Hackers indictment]] (DPRK Lazarus / RGB-cyber prosecution thread)

The 2025-06-30 wave is the wiki's first full single-day enforcement-architecture documentation under the DPRK RevGen Initiative — a discrete IC enforcement framework class beyond the single-defendant-prosecution pattern recorded in the existing DPRK corpus.

title: Treasury Sanctions Global Bulletproof Hosting Service Enabling Cybercriminals and Technology Theft (Treasury SB-0185)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury SB-0185 (Aeza Group bulletproof hosting sanctions)

## Bibliographic Details

- **Title**: Treasury Sanctions Global Bulletproof Hosting Service Enabling Cybercriminals and Technology Theft
- **Press Release Number**: SB-0185
- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Date**: 2025-07-01
- **URL**: https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/sb0185
- **Language**: English
- **On-the-record speaker**: Acting Under Secretary of the Treasury for Terrorism and Financial Intelligence Bradley T. Smith

## Source Assessment

- **Reliability**: high — official Treasury/OFAC press release issued in coordination with a named foreign-government partner (UK NCA). Press releases of this type carry the Treasury's formal designation findings and the recited E.O. authorities take legal effect on the date of announcement.
- **Credibility**: confirmed — designations are administrative-record actions; specific E.O. authorities, ownership stakes, and named individuals are stated on the record.
- **Sensitivity**: public.

## Key Findings

1. **Bilateral US-UK coordination on a Russian BPH provider (high confidence)**. The action is described on the record as conducted "in coordination with" the United Kingdom's National Crime Agency (NCA), with OFAC designating an Aeza Group front company in the UK (Aeza International Ltd.). This is a notable deployment of U.S. cyber-sanctions authority against a third-country shell entity, with on-the-record UK law-enforcement coordination.
2. **Bulletproof hosting as cybercrime enabler (high confidence)**. Treasury characterizes Aeza Group as having provided BPH services to a specific named portfolio of cybercriminal customers — the Meduza and Lumma infostealer operators (targeting the U.S. defense industrial base and technology companies), the BianLian ransomware operation, RedLine infostealer panels, and the BlackSprut darknet drug marketplace.
3. **Statutory basis (confirmed)**. All designations cite E.O. 13694, as further amended by E.O. 14144 and E.O. 14306 — the U.S. cyber-sanctions authority chain. The Aeza Group designation invokes the misappropriation-for-commercial-advantage prong; the affiliates' designations invoke the owned-or-controlled-by-blocked-person prong; the four individuals' designations invoke the leader/officer prong.
4. **Continuity with February 2025 ZServers action (confirmed)**. The release explicitly states that the Aeza action "builds on OFAC's February action targeting ZServers BPH," establishing a documented Treasury campaign against the Russian-aligned BPH ecosystem in 2025.
5. **Russian domestic enforcement parallel (high confidence)**. Treasury notes on the record that two of the designated individuals (Penzev and Bozoyan) had been arrested by Russian law enforcement for placing the BlackSprut illicit-drug marketplace onto Aeza infrastructure. This is an unusual pattern in which U.S. sanctions, UK NCA coordination, and host-state criminal enforcement converge on the same individuals.
6. **Sanctions-evasion follow-on (cross-source, confirmed)**. The 19 November 2025 OFAC action (Treasury SB-0319) subsequently designated Hypercore Ltd. (UK), Smart Digital Ideas DOO (Serbia), and Datavice MCHJ (Uzbekistan), explicitly framed as a response to Aeza Group's post-July 2025 rebranding and IP-relocation strategy. The July 2025 SB-0185 action is the direct predicate for that follow-on.

## Pages Created/Updated

- Created: [[treasury-aeza-group-bulletproof-hosting-sanctions-2025]]

## Notes

- This is a press-release-tier source. It confirms the existence and statutory basis of the designations but does not by itself enumerate full SDN-list metadata (DOB, passport number, address) for each designated person. A subsequent OFAC Recent Actions / SDN List pull would be needed to capture full identifiers.
- The release does not enumerate specific individual victim companies or specific ransomware payment amounts;

title: Major scam centre targeted after international operation
text:
## Source Summary

NCA press release dated 2025-07-11 reporting on the 2025-07-07 raid by India's CBI of a fraud call-centre in Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India. The release establishes a four-party 18-month collaboration (CBI / NCA / FBI / Microsoft) and the underlying intelligence chain: Microsoft → NCA-USA International Liaison Office → comparison with City of London Police Action Fraud Reports → joint NCA + FBI + Microsoft suspect-identification → NCA International Liaison Officers in India briefing the CBI → CBI raid on action day.

## IC Pattern

This case is a fully-articulated public-private four-party transnational cybercrime cooperation pattern: India + UK + US (three named law-enforcement jurisdictions) plus Microsoft (private sector). Notable features:

- **CBI as action-day lead** in a transnational cooperation initiated and supported by Western LE (NCA + FBI), inverting the more common pattern where US/UK LE acts on India-resident suspects under MLAT.
- **NCA International Liaison Officer network** as the primary inter-jurisdictional intelligence-sharing channel (US LO, Delhi LO, Washington DC LO).
- **City of London Police Action Fraud Reports** as a UK domestic-victim data feed driving offshore enforcement.
- **UK-US operational arrangement on call-centre fraud (2024)** referenced as the foundational bilateral instrument enabling the cross-Atlantic side of the cooperation.
- **Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit** as the private-sector intelligence originator, demonstrating the public-private cooperation model for tech-support scam enforcement.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

The case complements [[uk-nigeria-meta-scam-centre-disruption-2026|the UK-Nigeria Meta scam-centre disruption]] (NCA-led, 2026-02) and the [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|Korea-Cambodia-Philippines 73-defendant extradition]] (Korean-led, 2026-04) as 2025-2026 examples of national-LE-as-action-day-lead in a transnational scam-centre takedown. The UK-US operational arrangement on call-centre fraud (2024) is a reference instrument worth recording as a separate wiki entity if more sources surface.

title: Operazione "ELICIUS" — Polizia di Stato dismantles "Diskstation" ransomware gang in joint Italy-France-Romania task force coordinated by Europol
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 primary source: official Polizia di Stato press release on the agency's national portal (commissariatodips.it). The release announces the conclusion of the investigative phase of Operazione "ELICIUS", a joint Italy-France-Romania action under Europol coordination against the "Diskstation" ransomware gang. The Italian original explicitly names — twice — the foreign LE partners ("le polizie nazionali di Francia e Romania"), satisfying the iter 124 L24 strict ≥2-country IC requirement that participating jurisdictions must be named LE/prosecutorial agencies acknowledged in the tier-1 source.

## IC Pattern

- **Crime type:** ransomware against SMEs, double-extortion model with Bitcoin ransom demand. Targets exploited vulnerabilities in Synology DiskStation NAS appliances (gang's eponymous moniker).
- **IC structure:** Italian-led parallel investigation (Procura di Milano + Polizia Postale C.O.S.C. Milano) → Europol-coordinated **task force** with French and Romanian national police as foreign LE partners. France and Romania were independently investigating the same group; Europol provided the coordination shell to merge efforts.
- **Methodology fingerprint:** dual-track investigation = (a) host-side forensic analysis of compromised victim systems + (b) blockchain analysis of cryptocurrency ransom flows. Cross-border execution = Italian C.O.S.C. operators physically deployed to Bucharest searches in June 2024.
- **Custody pathway:** charges filed in Milan, pre-trial detention applied by Italian GIP — i.e. principal suspect held in Italy under Italian charges, not extradited from Romania. (Suggests EAW or arrest in flagrante by joint team — primary source mentions "in flagrante" arrest during Bucharest searches.)

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This is the wiki's first standalone Italy-France-Romania ransomware joint task force record under Europol coordination. Closest precedents:
- [[operation-eastwood]] (NoName057(16) DDoS, July 2025) — Europol-coordinated multi-EU + US action, but distinct crime type (DDoS hacktivism) and different participating roster.
- [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos (LockBit)]] — Europol-coordinated ransomware takedown but distinct gang and broader participant roster.

Operation Elicius adds:
- **Italy-Romania bilateral evidentiary cooperation** — Italian operators participating in Bucharest searches at suspects' residences. New record of cross-border physical investigative deployment in EU ransomware context.
- **NAS-vulnerability ransomware as IC subtype** — first wiki record of a ransomware operation specifically tied to Synology DiskStation NAS exploitation (vs. RDP/VPN/phishing initial access).
- **Procura di Milano as Italian central judicial authority for ransomware IC** — augments existing wiki coverage of Italian prosecution offices in cybercrime IC operations.

title: Armenian National Extradited to the United States Faces Federal Charges for Ransomware Extortion Conspiracy
text:
## Source Summary

USAO-OR press release dated 2025-07-16 announcing the 2025-06-18 extradition of Karen Serobovich Vardanyan from Ukraine to the US for his role in Ryuk ransomware attacks (March 2019 – September 2020), with co-defendants in France (Avetisyan, extradition pending) and at large (Lyulyava and Prykhodchenko, both Ukrainian nationals). The Ryuk gang received ~1,610 bitcoins valued at USD 15M+ in ransom payments.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a US-Ukraine-France three-country cooperation pattern for the Ryuk ransomware prosecution thread:

- **United States**: lead jurisdiction (FBI investigation; USAO-Oregon prosecution; DOJ OIA international-cooperation channel).
- **Ukraine**: extradition source for Vardanyan (June 2025); explicit credit to Ukrainian authorities in the press release.
- **France**: custody jurisdiction for Avetisyan; pending US extradition request.
- **Armenia**: defendant nationality (Vardanyan, Avetisyan) but not a cooperation participant.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case is the first wiki record explicitly capturing a Ryuk ransomware US prosecution thread. Ryuk is the third major Conti-family / Conti-successor ransomware brand to appear in the wiki's prosecution corpus, alongside:

- [[us-v-tymoshchuk-nefilim-megacortex-lockergoga|Tymoshchuk Nefilim/MegaCortex/LockerGoga]] (still at large, $11M reward)
- [[us-v-stryzhak-nefilim|Stryzhak Nefilim]] (extradited from Spain, pleaded guilty 2025-12)
- [[us-v-deniss-zolotarjovs|Zolotarjovs Conti/Karakurt/Royal/TommyLeaks/SchoolBoys/Akira]] (extradited from Georgia, sentenced 2026-05 to 102 months)
- This case: Vardanyan/Avetisyan/Lyulyava/Prykhodchenko Ryuk

Together these prosecutions document the Conti-family ransomware-actor extradition pipeline through Schengen/EU partners (Spain, France) and Council-of-Europe partners (Georgia, Ukraine) into US custody — a pattern worth tracking as an explicit IC-mechanism class.

title: Key figure behind major Russian-speaking cybercrime forum targeted in Ukraine (xss.is administrator arrested in Kyiv)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2025-07-23 announcing the 2025-07-22 arrest in Kyiv, Ukraine, of the suspected administrator of xss.is — a 12-year-running Russian-speaking cybercrime forum (50,000+ registered users) and one of the central marketplaces for stolen data, hacking tools, and ransomware-related services in the global cybercrime ecosystem. Lead investigation by French Police (Brigade de lutte contre la cybercriminalité / BL2C) + Paris Prosecutor (Parquet de Paris JUNALCO), with Ukrainian SBU Cybercrime Department + General Prosecutor's Office of Ukraine partnership and Europol EC3 coordination including a virtual command post (Sept 2024) and a Kyiv mobile office during the action week.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a France-Ukraine bilateral cybercrime cooperation pattern with several distinct IC mechanism classes:

- **French Police BL2C (Brigade de lutte contre la cybercriminalité, Préfecture de Police de Paris) + Paris Prosecutor (Parquet de Paris — JUNALCO) as France-side cybercrime authorities** — first wiki record of these specific French federal-cybercrime authorities as lead actors in a transnational cybercrime forum takedown.
- **Europol virtual command post (September 2024)** — first wiki record of this discrete IC support mechanism class.
- **Europol mobile office on-site deployment to Kyiv** for on-site coordination and evidence collection — structurally similar to the Europol cryptocurrency-expert deployment to Portugal in [[eurojust-100m-crypto-investment-fraud-takedown-2025|the September 2025 €100M Eurojust crypto-fraud takedown]] and the Europol specialist deployment to the Dutch National Police in [[labhost-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2024|LabHost takedown]].
- **Trusted-third-party arbiter role** of the forum administrator — establishes a discrete cybercrime-forum-business-model class in primary IC documentation: the admin not only ran the forum but also arbitrated criminal disputes and guaranteed transaction security for forum users.
- **Multi-platform takedown**: xss.is forum + thesecure.biz private messaging service for cybercriminals — both attributed to the same administrator.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release complements:
- [[leakbase-takedown-2026|LeakBase takedown (March 2026)]] — Europol-coordinated 14-country forum takedown
- [[labhost-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2024|LabHost PhaaS takedown (April 2024)]] — Europol J-CAT 19-country PhaaS takedown
- [[archetyp-market-takedown-operation-deep-sentinel-2025|Archetyp Market / Operation Deep Sentinel (June 2025)]] — Europol-coordinated 6-country darknet drug marketplace takedown
- [[operation-stream-kidflix|Operation Stream / Kidflix (March 2025)]] — Europol-coordinated 35+-country CSAM platform takedown

The xss.is takedown adds the Russian-speaking general cybercrime-forum class to the wiki's Europol-coordinated platform-takedown corpus, alongside English-speaking (LeakBase), specialised PhaaS (LabHost), darknet drug (Archetyp), and CSAM (Kidflix) takedowns.

title: Treasury Sanctions Clandestine IT Worker Network Funding the DPRK's Weapons Programs (SB-0205)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury SB-0205 (DPRK IT Worker Sanctions, 24 July 2025)

## Bibliographic Data

- **Publisher:** U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Press Release Number:** SB-0205
- **Date:** 2025-07-24
- **URL:** https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/sb0205
- **Raw file:** `raw/press-releases/2025-07-24_treasury_dprk-it-worker-korea-sobaeksu-sanctions-sb0205.md`

## Reliability Assessment

Tier-1 official U.S. Treasury press release announcing OFAC designations. Reliability **high**, credibility **confirmed**, sensitivity **public** — this is a public sanctions notice tied to SDN List entries that are independently verifiable on the OFAC recent-actions page.

## Key Findings

1. OFAC designated **Korea Sobaeksu Trading Company** (also known as Sobaeksu United Corporation) and three DPRK individuals — **Kim Se Un**, **Jo Kyong Hun**, and **Myong Chol Min** — on 24 July 2025 for evading U.S. and UN sanctions through fraudulent IT worker schemes and front-company activity supporting the DPRK's nuclear and ballistic missile programs.
2. **Sobaeksu** is identified as a front company for the U.S.-designated **Munitions Industry Department** (the DPRK body overseeing the nuclear program and ballistic missile development). Sobaeksu has dispatched IT worker teams overseas, including to Vietnam, to generate revenue, and has been involved in nuclear procurement activities.
3. **Legal basis** for the designations is highly likely the most authoritative element of the release: Sobaeksu, Kim Se Un, and Jo Kyong Hun are designated pursuant to **E.O. 13382** (proliferation finance); Myong Chol Min is designated pursuant to **E.O. 13810** (North Korea-related). The parallel DOJ unsealed indictments of seven DPRK nationals invoke the **International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA)** for counterfeit-cigarette trafficking.
4. The release describes an explicit interagency posture: OFAC designations are coordinated with DOJ unsealings, State Department TOC Rewards offers (up to $15 million total, including up to $3 million each for Kim Se Un and Myong Chol Min), and operational support from FBI and HSI. It cross-references the FBI's 23 January 2025 Public Service Announcement on DPRK IT workers and the 16 May 2022 tri-agency IT Worker Advisory.
5. The release notes that DPRK IT workers in some cases "introduced malware into company networks to exfiltrate proprietary and sensitive data," characterizing the scheme as combining sanctions evasion, identity fraud, and potential cyber intrusion.

## Pages Updated

- Created: [[treasury-dprk-it-worker-korea-sobaeksu-sanctions-2025]]

title: Cybercrime: International investigations by the OAG and fedpol result in conviction for real-time phishing in the UK
text:
## Source Summary

Official press release issued on 29 July 2025 by the Office of the Attorney General of Switzerland (Bundesanwaltschaft / Ministère public de la Confédération / Ministero pubblico della Confederazione, "OAG"), in cooperation with the Federal Office of Police (fedpol), announcing that joint Swiss investigations had led to the UK conviction of a British national for developing and distributing a phishing kit used in a real-time-phishing scheme that defrauded Swiss bank customers of approximately CHF 2.4 million.

The release is mirrored across the Swiss federal news portal (`bag.admin.ch/en/newnsb/...` and `news.admin.ch/en/newnsb/...`) and was independently reported on the same day by Keystone-SDA / SWI swissinfo.ch ("English student sentenced for defrauding Swiss bank customers"). Background detail on the modus operandi and the October 2023 arrest of the suspect in England is provided in the fedpol Report 2024 entry "Phishing scam by an international network uncovered."

## Relevance to IC

This source is a tier-1 primary record of a Swiss-UK cybercrime cooperation case in which the Swiss federal prosecution authority transferred / discontinued its proceedings in favour of UK prosecution after the developer and distributor of the phishing kit was identified, located, and arrested in England. It documents:

- Bilateral coordination between the OAG and UK prosecution authorities, including transfer of jurisdiction.
- Operational support from fedpol, Europol, and Eurojust (per the fedpol annual-report account).
- Use of the "real-time phishing" technique against Swiss cantonal-bank e-banking customers, including bypass of two-factor authentication.

It supports the [[oag-fedpol-real-time-phishing-uk-conviction-2025]] operation/case page.

title: Arrestato a Rimini cybercriminale internazionale (Riccione, 31 July 2025)
text:
## Source Summary

Polizia di Stato (Italian State Police) press release dated 2025-07-31 announcing the arrest in Riccione (Rimini province, Emilia-Romagna) of an internationally wanted foreign national accused of having actively participated in serious cyber-extortion offences committed between 2023 and the first months of 2025 against U.S.-based organisations ("strutture statunitensi"). Ransom demands attributed to the cyber-attacks ran into millions of dollars. The investigation was conducted by the Polizia Postale within an "international cooperation device" between Italian police and the U.S. FBI; the federal arrest provision was executed by officers of the **Centro nazionale anticrimine informatico per la protezione delle infrastrutture critiche (Cnaipic)** together with agents of the **Centro operativo per la sicurezza cibernetica Emilia-Romagna** and the **Sezione operativa per la Sicurezza cibernetica di Rimini**. The suspect was located at a hotel in Riccione where he was on holiday with his family. Numerous IT devices and several thousand euros in cash were seized. The release closes by characterising the operation as the result of consolidated cooperation between Italian police and the U.S. law-enforcement community, strengthened by the deployment of liaison experts at the respective embassies.

The official Polizia di Stato communiqué does NOT name the suspect, his nationality, his age, the specific U.S. victim organisations, the specific ransom-demand totals (beyond "milioni di dollari"), the prison of remand, or the formal extradition instrument. Italian regional media on the same day reported the suspect as 42-year-old Kazakh national **Roman Khlynovskiy**, the FBI-attributed illicit profit total as approximately USD 31 million, and the place of remand as Busto Arsizio prison; these media-derived particulars are recorded as contextual notes in the raw file but are not asserted in tier-1 wiki fields.

## Relevance to IC

Tier-1 primary source from the Italian national police authority documenting:
- Italy ↔ United States operational cooperation arc on a cyber-extortion case targeting U.S. structures, with FBI as the foreign partner.
- Execution in Italy of a U.S. federal arrest warrant ("provvedimento federale") via the Cnaipic specialist unit, concretely demonstrating the IC pathway from U.S. judicial authority → Italian Polizia Postale operational arrest → pending extradition.
- Identification of the suspect via the bilateral liaison-officer/embassy-expert framework explicitly named in the release.
- Multi-year reach-back: charged conduct spans 2023 through early 2025, with the arrest occurring 2025-07-31 while the suspect was on family holiday in Riccione, illustrating the practical effectiveness of international wanted-person notices and tourist-flow detection.

South Korea is not named in the release.

title: Treasury Sanctions Cryptocurrency Exchange and Network Enabling Sanctions Evasion and Cyber Criminals
text:
## Summary

Official OFAC press release (SB-0225) announcing a coordinated sanctions package targeting the Garantex cryptocurrency exchange ecosystem and its post-disruption successor Grinex. The release is the financial-sanctions complement to the March 6-7, 2025 U.S. Secret Service-led law enforcement disruption of Garantex (domain seizure, USD 26 million crypto freeze, DOJ indictments of Mira Serda and Besciokov). Highly reliable as primary government source.

## Key Entities

- **Garantex Europe OU** — re-designated cryptocurrency exchange (Russia-operated, Estonia-registered, license revoked Feb 2022)
- **Grinex** — newly designated successor exchange
- **Aleksandr Mira Serda, Sergey Mendeleev, Pavel Karavatsky** — Garantex executives newly designated
- **A7 LLC, A71 LLC, A7 Agent LLC** — Russian cross-border settlement firms (Shor / PSB-controlled)
- **Old Vector** — Kyrgyzstani A7A5 token issuer
- **InDeFi Bank, Exved** — Mendeleev-controlled financial entities

## Legal Authority

- E.O. 13694 (as amended by E.O. 14144 and E.O. 14306) — malicious cyber-enabled activities
- E.O. 14024 — Russia financial services sector (basis of April 2022 designation, retained)

## Cooperation Footprint

- USSS Cyber Investigative Section (lead)
- FBI (assistance)
- Department of State (rewards program)
- Department of Justice (parallel indictments)
- Historical (March 2025): German and Finnish law enforcement, Indian arrest of Besciokov, prior Estonian FIU enforcement (2022)

## Reliability Assessment

- **Reliability:** high (primary government press release, official designation document)
- **Credibility:** confirmed for the fact of designation; analytical claims about ransomware nexus and transaction volumes are highly likely accurate given OFAC's evidentiary thresholds, but quantitative figures (USD 100 million+ in illicit transactions, USD 26 million frozen) are not independently verifiable from this source alone.

title: Treasury Sanctions Fraud Network Funding DPRK Weapons Programs
text:
> [!info] Source Summary
> Tier-1 official US Treasury press release (SB-0230) announcing OFAC sanctions on a four-actor cross-border DPRK IT worker fraud network. Demonstrates the Russia-based DPRK consular official + Russian national facilitator + Chinese front company + DPRK trading company quadrilateral, with cryptocurrency-to-USD conversions feeding the DPRK Ministry of People's Armed Forces General Political Bureau via Chinyong.

## Source Details

- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Document type**: Press release (Number: SB-0230)
- **Publish date**: 2025-08-27 (verified in body: "August 27, 2025")
- **Authority cited**: E.O. 13687
- **Co-issued framing**: U.S. Department of State + foreign ministries of Japan and the Republic of Korea issued a joint statement on DPRK IT worker threats the same day
- **Reliability**: high (official US Treasury / OFAC press release)
- **Credibility**: confirmed (primary US government enforcement document)

## Key Quotes

- "The North Korean regime continues to target American businesses through fraud schemes involving its overseas IT workers, who steal data and demand ransom" — Under Secretary John K. Hurley.
- "Today's action expands on the designation of Chinyong Information Technology Cooperation Company, targeting additional entities in its network and combatting its use of cryptocurrency for sanctions evasion."
- "Since at least December 2024, Andreyev has worked with Kim Ung Sun, a Russia-based DPRK economic and trade consular official, to facilitate multiple financial transfers worth a total of nearly $600,000, by converting cryptocurrency to cash in U.S. dollars."

## Related Wiki Pages

title: United Kingdom National Charged in Connection with Multiple Cyber Attacks, Including on Critical Infrastructure
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 U.S. Department of Justice press release dated 18 September 2025, jointly publicising the unsealing of a federal complaint in the U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey against Thalha Jubair, a 19-year-old United Kingdom national of London. Jubair is charged with seven counts — computer fraud conspiracy, two substantive counts of computer fraud, wire fraud conspiracy, two substantive counts of wire fraud, and money laundering conspiracy — for his alleged role in approximately 120 Scattered Spider network intrusions worldwide between May 2022 and September 2025, including at least 47 U.S.-based victim entities that paid more than USD 115 million in ransom. The release singles out two named federal-victim intrusions: a U.S.-based critical infrastructure company and the U.S. Courts (the federal judiciary), both occurring in October 2024 and January 2025. Jubair was arrested by U.K. authorities on 16 September 2025 in connection with a parallel, separate U.K. investigation into an attack on U.K. critical infrastructure (TfL).

## Relevance to IC

- **Parallel UK + US prosecution of the same defendant — first dual-jurisdiction Scattered Spider chain in the wiki**: The release is the U.S.-side counterpart to the UK NCA TfL prosecution ([[uk-jubair-flowers-tfl-cyber-attack-2025]]). Highly likely (80–95%) that the dual-track represents an explicit prosecutorial division of labor: UK takes the TfL conduct under the Computer Misuse Act 1990 with no US extradition request, while the US runs a parallel D.N.J. complaint covering the 47 U.S.-victim ransom-extortion conduct (USD 115M+) and the federal-judiciary intrusion. This is a useful comparative model alongside the [[us-v-kai-west-intelbroker-2025]] track (US-as-venue + France-as-arrest-venue with extradition).
- **U.S. Courts intrusion as IC stakes**: The release names the U.S. federal judiciary as a victim of unauthorized access in October 2024 and January 2025. Treating the federal court system as a critical infrastructure–adjacent target reframes Scattered Spider's threat profile and intensifies international cooperation pressure.
- **Cooperation footprint of named foreign agencies**: The release credits "significant assistance" from the UK NCA, City of London Police, West Midlands Police; Netherlands national police + Dutch Prosecutor's Office; Romanian DIICOT and Romanian National Police; Royal Canadian Mounted Police; and Australian Federal Police. This eight-agency / six-country cooperation footprint is consistent with a Scattered Spider transnational task-force model and is broader than the cooperation publicly identified in the parallel UK NCA release on the same date.
- **OIA + CCIPS coordination role**: Office of International Affairs is named as having "provided significant assistance" — consistent with OIA's standing role coordinating multi-jurisdictional MLA traffic and extradition packages on cybercrime indictments. CCIPS prosecuted the case in tandem with AUSA-NJ.
- **Cross-references**: feeds [[us-v-jubair-scattered-spider-2025]] (this US-side case page); cross-links to [[uk-jubair-flowers-tfl-cyber-attack-2025]] (UK-side case for the same defendant) and to [[us-v-kai-west-intelbroker-2025]] (Scattered Spider–adjacent UK national prosecution with extradition track).

## Notes

- Live page successfully fetched 2026-05-09 via curl_cffi + chrome124 impersonation (status 200, ~96KB body); no Akamai meta-refresh challenge observed on this fetch (per LESSONS L21, this can vary by IP and time).
- "Scattered Spider" / "Octo Tempest" / "UNC3944" / "0ktapus" are the four common designators in the release. The wiki treats the grouping as an attribution claim consistent with private-sector threat-intel reporting; the U.S. complaint charges conspiracies, not membership in a named criminal organisation.
- The release does not name the specific U.S.-based critical infrastructure company or specify the U.S. Courts subsystem(s) accessed.
- The rele

title: Two charged for TfL cyber attack
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 UK National Crime Agency press release dated 18 September 2025, jointly publicising the City of London Police arrest and Crown Prosecution Service charging of two UK-based defendants — Thalha Jubair (19, East London) and Owen Flowers (18, Walsall) — in connection with the 31 August 2024 cyber attack on Transport for London (TfL). NCA attributes the TfL intrusion to the online criminal collective Scattered Spider. Owen Flowers, separately, is charged with conspiring to infiltrate and damage the networks of US healthcare providers SSM Health Care Corporation and Sutter Health. Jubair faces an additional RIPA charge for refusing to disclose passwords for seized devices.

## Relevance to IC

- **UK-as-prosecution-venue for cross-border cyber attacks on US victims**: Flowers' SSM Health / Sutter Health charges demonstrate that the UK can stand up domestic cybercrime prosecutions for attacks on overseas victim organisations, reducing reliance on extradition where the defendant is a UK national resident on UK soil. Highly likely (80–95%) to require US-side evidentiary cooperation (victim incident data, server logs) which is consistent with the NCA's reference to FBI partnership.
- **Domestic-LE-as-cybercrime-channel**: The arrest and charging are structured around a UK chain (NCA + City of London Police + CPS + West Midlands ROCU + British Transport Police), with the FBI named only as an "international partner" supporting the broader Scattered Spider effort. Useful as a contrast case to extradition-track prosecutions ([[us-v-kai-west-intelbroker-2025]]).
- **Cross-references**: feeds [[uk-jubair-flowers-tfl-cyber-attack-2025]]; supports IC patterns on Computer Misuse Act / RIPA enforcement, Scattered Spider attribution, and UK critical-infrastructure cyber prosecutions.

## Notes

- "£39 million loss" figure commonly cited in press coverage of the TfL incident is **not** quoted in this NCA release. The release uses the qualitative phrase "millions in losses." The case page therefore cites only "millions in losses" until a tier-1 UK source (TfL annual report or court filing) is ingested.
- Live page returned HTTP 404 at ingest time (2026-05-09); content recovered via Wayback Machine capture dated 2025-09-26 (closest post-publish snapshot). Body text and publish date verified against capture.
- "Scattered Spider" is identified by NCA as an "online criminal collective" rather than a formal organisation; case page treats this as an attribution claim rather than a charged conspiracy with named co-conspirators.

title: Detido em Espanha homem procurado em Portugal por branqueamento e burla informatica
text:
## Source Summary

Official press release from Portugal's Policia Judiciaria reporting the September 2025 execution, by Spanish authorities, of a European Arrest Warrant issued by the DIAP da Amadora against a Portuguese-Angolan dual national wanted for computer fraud (burla informatica), unauthorized computer access, and money laundering tied to an online gaming-platform laundering structure under investigation since 2024.

## Relevance to IC

Tier-1 primary source for a four-country international cooperation event (Portugal, Spain, United Kingdom, Angola) using the European Arrest Warrant as the legal vehicle. title: Eurojust coordinates action to halt cryptocurrency fraud of over 100 million euros across Europe

## Source Summary

Spain-Lithuania JIT (Joint Investigation Team) supported by Eurojust; Europol joined the JIT and deployed a cryptocurrency expert to Portugal for action-day crypto-asset seizure. The release names participating authorities for each of 6 action-day countries verbatim, including the Spanish Audiencia Nacional Court no. 1, the Portuguese Judiciary Police, Italian Guardia di Finanza Turin, Lithuanian Financial Crime Investigation Service, and Romanian DIICOT.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the September 2025 Eurojust €100M crypto-investment-fraud joint-action-day. It supplies (a) the JIT cooperation pattern (Spain-Lithuania bilateral JIT joined by Europol), (b) the European Arrest Warrant + European Investigation Order + freezing-order instruments executed under Eurojust coordination, (c) the Lithuanian-bank-account proceeds-laundering route documented in DOJ-charging-style language, (d) the cryptocurrency-expert-deployment private/public mechanism for in-country crypto seizure, and (e) verbatim authority-by-authority cooperation roster for 6 EU member states.

## Note on text fidelity

Eurojust newsroom URL was retrieved via the project's TLS-spoofing fetch tool (`tools/web_verify_tls.py`) and the raw page preserves the body text verbatim, with all named-authorities-list elements captured.

title: UK arrest following aerospace cyber incident
text:
# Source Summary — UK arrest following aerospace cyber incident (NCA, 2025-09-24)

## Bibliographic Data

- **Publisher:** UK National Crime Agency (NCA)
- **Title:** "UK arrest following aerospace cyber incident"
- **Publish date:** 24 September 2025
- **URL:** https://www.nationalcrimeagency.gov.uk/news/uk-arrest-following-aerospace-cyber-incident
- **Type:** Official law-enforcement press release (UK)
- **Language:** English
- **Length:** Short release (~200 words of substantive content)

## Reliability and Credibility

| Field | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Reliability | high | NCA official press release; primary statement from the investigating agency. |
| Credibility | probably-true | Single-source agency attribution; no second independent jurisdiction confirmation in the release itself; details are minimal pending ongoing investigation. |
| Sensitivity | public | Published on the NCA public news site. |

## Key Facts Reported

1. A man **in his forties** was arrested **in West Sussex (UK)** on the **evening of 23 September 2025** by the **National Crime Agency**, supported by the **South East ROCU**.
2. Arrest was made on **suspicion of Computer Misuse Act offences** (specific section not stated).
3. The suspect was **released on conditional bail**.
4. The arrest is connected to a **cyber incident at Collins Aerospace** reported on **19 September 2025**, which disrupted flights at **Heathrow and other European airports** over the following weekend.
5. The investigation is led by the **NCA's National Cyber Crime Unit (NCCU)**, headed by Deputy Director **Paul Foster**.
6. Foster characterises the investigation as "in its early stages" and notes work "alongside our partners here and overseas."

## What the Source Does NOT Say

- Does not name the suspect.
- Does not identify the specific Computer Misuse Act 1990 section invoked (e.g., s.1, s.2, s.3, s.3ZA).
- Does not name the ransomware family or threat actor group.
- Does not name foreign cooperating agencies (e.g., Belgian or German police), even though Brussels Airport and Berlin Brandenburg Airport were widely reported in other coverage as affected.
- Does not confirm "ransomware" as the precise attack vector — the release uses the broader term "cyber incident."
- Does not state charging decisions or prosecution venue.

> [!note] Translation note
> The NCA release is in English only. Korean translation of "Computer Misuse Act offences" is typically rendered as "컴퓨터 부정사용법 위반 혐의."

## Pages Updated

- `[[uk-collins-aerospace-airport-ransomware-arrest-2025]]` — case page (created from this source)
- `[[united-kingdom]]` — UK as arrest jurisdiction and lead investigator
- `[[germany]]` — Berlin Brandenburg Airport reportedly impacted (per surrounding context; not named in this release)
- `[[belgium]]` — Brussels Airport reportedly impacted (per surrounding context; not named in this release)
- `[[uk-nca]]` — investigating agency
- `[[ransomware-ic]]` — likely crime category (not explicitly named "ransomware" in this single release)

## Cross-Reference Caveats

> [!warning] Single-source attribution
> This source alone does **not** establish that the underlying incident was ransomware (it uses "cyber incident"). The ransomware framing in the case page relies on broader contemporaneous reporting; this NCA release is consistent with but does not by itself confirm the ransomware classification.

title: USD 439 million recovered in global financial crime operation - Operation HAECHI VI
text:
## Summary

Operation HAECHI VI, the sixth Korea-initiated HAECHI iteration, ran April to August 2025 across 40 countries. Recovered USD 439 million total ($342M fiat, $97M physical/virtual assets). 68,000+ bank accounts blocked, ~400 crypto wallets frozen. Continued targeting seven cyber-enabled fraud types. Notable Korea-UAE BEC recovery of USD 3.91 million.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation HAECHI VI
- **Timeframe:** April – August 2025
- **Countries:** 40
- **Assets recovered:** USD 439 million
- **Crypto recovered:** ~USD 16 million
- **Bank accounts blocked:** 68,000+
- **Crypto wallets frozen:** ~400
- **Crime types:** Voice phishing, romance scams, BEC, investment fraud, e-commerce fraud, money laundering

## Relevance

Sixth HAECHI iteration setting new financial recovery records. Korea-UAE I-GRIP sub-operation demonstrates rapid international fund interception critical for BEC and voice phishing cases.

title: La Guardia Civil desmantela una red de phishing bancario y detiene al principal desarrollador de kits de robo de credenciales en España (GXC Team / GoogleXcoder)
text:
## Source Summary

Official Guardia Civil press release (Spanish national gendarmerie / cybercrime authority — tier-1 primary source) announcing the dismantling of the **GXC Team / GoogleXcoder** Crime-as-a-Service phishing-kit operation. Investigation led by the **Departamento contra el Cibercrimen de la Unidad Central Operativa (UCO)** under the **Juzgado de Instrucción número 1 de San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria)**, with international cooperation from the **Brazilian Federal Police (Policía Federal de Brasil)** and private-sector support from **Group-IB**.

Key elements:

- **Target:** "GoogleXcoder" — 25-year-old Brazilian national, principal developer of CaaS phishing kits cloning Spanish banks and public-administration websites; lived as a "digital nomad" across multiple Spanish provinces using fraudulent identities, prepaid SIM cards and prepaid payment cards.
- **Customers:** Spanish-speaking phishing crews, contacted via Telegram, paid hundreds of euros per day; one criminal Telegram channel was openly named "Robarle todo a las abuelas".
- **Impact:** millions of euros stolen; victims numbered in the thousands; dozens of impersonated banking and government entities; Spanish-language phishing market substantially driven by this single supplier from 2023 onward.
- **IC scope:** Brazil ↔ Spain, with Brazilian Federal Police cooperation underpinning identification of the Brazilian-national suspect; private-sector technical support by Group-IB.
- **Outcome:** main developer arrested in San Vicente de la Barquera (Cantabria); six entries and searches in Valladolid, Zaragoza, Barcelona, Palma de Mallorca, San Fernando and La Línea de la Concepción; six additional users of the CaaS service identified; Telegram channels deactivated; investigation remains open.

This is the canonical Spanish-government primary source for the **GXC Team / GoogleXcoder** takedown and the basis for the operation page `[[gxc-team-googlexcoder-phishing-kits-takedown-2025]]`.

title: Chairman of Prince Group Indicted for Operating Cambodian Forced Labor Scam Compounds Engaged in Cryptocurrency Fraud Schemes (Chen Zhi)
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ-OPA Press Release 25-1,031 dated 2025-10-14 announcing the unsealing of an EDNY federal grand jury indictment against Cambodian national Chen Zhi (a.k.a. Vincent, 37, founder/chairman of Prince Holding Group / Prince Group) for wire fraud conspiracy and money laundering conspiracy directing forced-labor cryptocurrency-investment-fraud scam compounds across Cambodia. Concurrently, the U.S. Attorney's Office EDNY and the National Security Division filed a civil forfeiture complaint against approximately 127,271 Bitcoin (~USD 15 billion) currently in US government custody — the largest forfeiture action in DOJ history. Defendant at large; max 40 years.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes the criminal-prosecution and asset-forfeiture track parallel to the [[us-uk-prince-group-tco-huione-group-sanctions-2025|OFAC + FinCEN + UK FCDO/OFSI sanctions track on the same Prince Group TCO]]. Notable IC mechanism classes:

- **Concurrent administrative-and-criminal action**: OFAC sanctions + EDNY criminal indictment + civil forfeiture complaint + UK FCDO/OFSI sanctions all unsealed/filed on 2025-10-14.
- **Largest-ever DOJ forfeiture (127,271 BTC, ~USD 15 billion)** — the wiki's first record of a single forfeiture exceeding USD 1 billion in any cybercrime-related case, let alone exceeding USD 10 billion.
- **DEA Trident Initiative + FBI New York Joint Asian Criminal Enterprise Task Force + FBI Virtual Asset Unit** as the multi-agency investigative architecture for transnational crypto-fraud + forced-labor cases.
- **DOJ OIA + UK NCA + Isle of Man Constabulary's Proactive International Money-Laundering Investigations Team + UK FCDO** as the international-cooperation chain for the criminal track.
- **'Spraying' and 'funneling' cryptocurrency laundering techniques** documented in primary IC documentation for the first time in the wiki.
- **Phone-farm criminal-infrastructure pattern** (thousands of phones + millions of mobile numbers) recorded in primary IC documentation as a discrete scam-compound operational class.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release should be cited alongside the [[2025-10-14_treasury_us-uk-largest-action-cybercriminal-networks-southeast-asia|US Treasury OFAC + FinCEN release of the same date]] and the existing operation page [[us-uk-prince-group-tco-huione-group-sanctions-2025]]. The case page [[us-v-chen-zhi-prince-group-edny-2025]] documents the criminal-prosecution + asset-forfeiture track of this multi-track action, distinct from the administrative sanctions track on the operation page.

title: U.S. and U.K. Take Largest Action Ever Targeting Cybercriminal Networks in Southeast Asia (Prince Group TCO + Huione Group)
text:
## Source Summary

US Treasury press release SB0278 (2025-10-14) announcing the largest joint US-UK action ever targeting Southeast Asia–based cybercriminal networks: OFAC sanctions on 146 targets within Prince Group TCO (Cambodia, Chen Zhi-led), UK FCDO concurrent sanctions on Prince Group entities and individuals, FinCEN Section 311 final rule severing Cambodia-based Huione Group from the US financial system, and an unsealed EDNY criminal indictment against Chen Zhi. Underlying scope: $16.6 billion+ in cumulative US online-investment-scam losses; $10 billion+ in 2024 alone (66% YoY increase) attributable to Southeast Asia operations.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes the most fully-articulated US-UK bilateral cyber-fraud enforcement cooperation pattern in the wiki's pre-existing Asia corpus. Notable features:

- **OFAC + FinCEN + UK FCDO + UK OFSI four-channel coordinated action** under a January 2025 OFAC-OFSI MOU (foundational instrument).
- **Concurrent administrative-and-criminal action**: OFAC/UK sanctions designations + EDNY criminal indictment unsealed against Chen Zhi the same day.
- **FinCEN Section 311 final rule** as a discrete administrative-financial mechanism that severs Huione Group from the US correspondent banking system — a structurally similar pattern to the prior Cryptex sanctions ([[cryptex-pm2btc-sanctions]]) but applied here to a Cambodia-based scam-laundering conglomerate rather than a Russia-linked crypto exchange.
- **Cross-laundering nexus**: Huione Group laundered both DPRK cyber-heist proceeds ($37M+) and Southeast Asia pig-butchering proceeds ($36M+), bridging the wiki's DPRK-cyber and Southeast-Asia-scam corpora.
- **Scam-compound architecture in Cambodia**: 10+ scam compounds, industrial-scale human-trafficking + forced-labor + torture documented as the labor base for the cyberfraud operations.
- **Multi-jurisdiction shell-company web**: 100+ shell and holding companies across Mauritius, Taiwan, Singapore, Laos, Palau.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release complements:
- [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|Korea-Cambodia-Philippines 73-defendant extradition (2026-04)]] — Korean-led repatriation of Korean-national perpetrators from Cambodian / Filipino scam compounds
- [[korea-cambodia-scam-repatriation|Korea-Cambodia scam repatriation pattern]]
- [[us-doj-coordinated-nationwide-actions-dprk-it-workers-2025|DOJ DPRK RevGen Initiative (2025-06-30)]] — DPRK cyber-heist proceeds laundered through Huione cross-link
- [[afp-rtp-bangkok-scam-centre-operation-firestorm-2025|AFP-RTP Bangkok scam centre takedown (2025-06-16)]] — Australia-Thailand bilateral on parallel SEA scam-centre infrastructure

This is the wiki's first record of a Treasury OFAC + FinCEN + UK FCDO/OFSI joint sanctions architecture, and the first record of FinCEN Section 311 used against a Southeast Asian scam-laundering conglomerate.

title: Cybercrime-as-a-service takedown: 7 arrested (Latvia SIM-box CaaS network dismantled)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release (dated 2025-10-17) describing a Latvian-led action day in October 2025 dismantling a cybercrime-as-a-service (CaaS) SIM-box network that offered telephone numbers from over 80 countries for use in smishing, phishing, fraud, extortion, migrant smuggling, child sexual abuse material distribution, daughter-son scams, investment fraud, fake shops, fake bank websites, and fake-police-officer schemes. Action-day results: 26 searches, 5 arrests, ~1,200 SIM-box devices seized (operating 40,000 SIM cards), hundreds of thousands of further SIM cards seized, 5 servers seized, 2 websites taken down (gogetsms.com, apisim.com), EUR 431,000 + USD 333,000 in assets frozen, 4 luxury vehicles seized. More than **49 million online accounts** were created via the illegal service. Joint Investigation Team partners Austria, Estonia, Latvia, plus Finland support. Europol + Eurojust coordination; Shadowserver Foundation private-sector partner.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the 2025-10 Latvian-led CaaS SIM-box network takedown. It supplies (a) named-agencies-and-prosecutorial-offices for each of 4 participating countries (Austria, Estonia, Finland, Latvia), (b) the JIT cooperation mechanism (Joint Investigation Team partners Austria/Estonia/Latvia plus Finland), (c) the Europol-Shadowserver Foundation private-sector partnership pattern, (d) detailed verbatim record of crime-types-enabled including Russian-speaking-victim fake-police schemes, and (e) the operational scale of 49+ million online accounts created via the SIM-box service across 80+ source-countries. This is one of the wider published CaaS-tier prosecutions in the wiki corpus and supplements the broader Eurojust-coordinated cybercrime infrastructure takedowns including [[operation-endgame-phase3]] and [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]].

## Note on text fidelity

Europol newsroom HTML embeds the press release body inside the `window.SERVER_DATA.body` JSON field. The verbatim content above was captured from that field via the project's TLS-spoofing fetch tool (`tools/web_verify_tls.py`) plus a JSON-body extraction step. Title-vs-body discrepancy noted: title says "7 arrested" but body says "5 individuals arrested" on the action day; the 5-figure is used for `results.arrests` in the wiki record with the 7-figure flagged in body prose.

title: South Korea repatriates 64 scam centre suspects from Cambodia

## Summary

In October 2025 and January 2026, South Korea repatriated 107+ nationals from Cambodia suspected of operating in online scam compounds including voice phishing, romance scams, and pig butchering cryptocurrency fraud. An estimated 1,000 Korean citizens were working in Cambodian scam centres. Additional repatriations from Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, and the Philippines. Coincided with US/UK sanctions against Cambodia-based Prince Group.

- **Countries:** South Korea, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, Philippines

- **Financial losses:** KRW 48.6 billion (USD 33 million) from January 2026 batch

## Relevance

Illustrates evolution of voice phishing from China-based call centres to Southeast Asian trafficking-fuelled scam compounds. Korean nationals were both perpetrators and trafficking victims.

title: Báo Chính phủ (Vietnam) — Phá thành công chuyên án lừa đảo chiếm đoạt tài sản trên không gian mạng xuyên quốc gia (Lai Châu Provincial Police + Vietnam MPS Representative in Cambodia + Cambodian functional forces, Bokor Hill takedown, 28-Oct-2025)
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 Vietnamese-language primary press article published by **Báo Chính phủ (Báo điện tử Chính phủ)** — the electronic newspaper of the Government Office of the Socialist Republic of Việt Nam — on 29 October 2025 at 16:46 (GMT+7) (modified 16:49 the same day), authored by **Linh Anh**. The article reports a Việt Nam–Cambodia joint operation on 28 October 2025 that took down a transnational online-fraud cell operating from the **Bokor Hill area, Bokor Ecological Park complex, Tuek Chhou ward, Bokor city, Kampot province, Kingdom of Cambodia**, and resulted in the arrest in Cambodia of **59 Vietnamese-national suspects** (44 male, 15 female), with **66 arrests in total** in the case to date.

Operational lead is identified as a special working group of **Lai Châu Provincial Police** of Việt Nam, in coordination with the **Representative Office of the Ministry of Public Security of Việt Nam in Cambodia**, the **Criminal Police Department of the MPS** (Cục Cảnh sát Hình sự — Bộ Công an), and **Cambodian functional forces**. Direct command authority is named as **Senior Lieutenant General Nguyễn Văn Long, Deputy Minister and Head of the Police Investigation Agency of the Ministry of Public Security of Việt Nam**.

The named ringleader is **Sùng Thị Mải** (alias "Vi"), born 1999, resident of Lào Cai province (Việt Nam). The article reports the modus operandi as impersonation of shippers, Police, and Military, plus romance-fraud (lừa đảo tình cảm), with **more than 8,000 victims nationwide** in Việt Nam and an estimated appropriated amount of **more than 300 billion VND**.

After Cambodian-side triage, **Cambodian functional forces handed over all 59 suspects** plus seized physical evidence to the Police Investigation Agency — Lai Châu Provincial Police, for Vietnamese-domestic prosecution. The article frames the operation as a demonstration of close Việt Nam–Cambodia police cooperation in the immediate diplomatic context of the **Signing Ceremony of the UN Convention against Cybercrime (Hà Nội Convention)** hosted by Việt Nam in Hà Nội on 25-26 October 2025.

## Relevance to IC

This is a primary-source attestation, in Vietnamese, of three IC-relevant features:

1. **MPS Representative Office in Cambodia as a routinised liaison node.** The article names the **Representative Office of the Ministry of Public Security of Việt Nam in Cambodia** (Cơ quan đại diện Bộ Công an Việt Nam tại Campuchia) as one of the three coordinating Vietnamese-side entities, alongside the Criminal Police Department and the lead provincial police. This evidences a permanent, institutionalised Vietnamese-MPS liaison presence in Cambodia handling criminal-investigation cooperation, distinct from ad-hoc Joint-Investigation-Team setups.
2. **Cambodian-host hand-over of suspects to the requesting state's investigative agency.** The article explicitly states that after on-the-ground triage by Cambodian functional forces, the 59 suspects (all Vietnamese nationals) and seized items were handed over to the Police Investigation Agency of Lai Châu Provincial Police of Việt Nam. This is a host-state hand-over for home-state prosecution pattern, parallel in form (though not in legal basis) to comparable Korea–Cambodia, Korea–Philippines, and China–Cambodia repatriations of co-national scam-compound suspects documented elsewhere in the wiki.
3. **Diplomatic-cooperation framing tied to the Hà Nội Convention.** Báo Chính phủ explicitly frames the takedown as a deliverable of close bilateral police cooperation, framed against the immediate aftermath of the **Signing Ceremony of the UN Convention against Cybercrime in Hà Nội (25-26 October 2025)** — itself hosted by Việt Nam. This is a useful primary attestation of the Hà Nội-signing → bilateral-takedown sequencing.

## Cross-Reference Notes

- This source provides the first Vietnamese-language tier-1 attestation in the wiki of Việt Nam–Cambodia direct police cooperation against scam compounds in late 202

title: PF deflagra segunda fase de operação contra organização criminosa especializada em fraudes bancárias digitais (Operação Magna Fraus, Phase 2)
text:
## Source Summary

Brazilian Federal Police (PF) press release dated 2025-10-30 (last updated 2025-10-31) announcing Phase 2 of **Operação Magna Fraus**, a multi-jurisdiction takedown of a criminal organisation that drained more than R$ 813 million from accounts used by Brazilian banks and payment institutions to manage customer **PIX** (Brazil's instant-payment system) transfers via computer-device intrusion (invasão de dispositivo informático). The action combined federal police execution in 11 Brazilian cities across 7 states with international arrests in Spain (6) and Argentina (2), coordinated through INTERPOL channels and the Spanish National Police's computer-fraud brigade.

## IC Pattern

This release documents a **Brazil-led, INTERPOL-mediated, Iberian-Latin American multi-NCB action-day** pattern. Notable features:

- **PF as lead agency / requesting state**, with Cyber GAECO of the São Paulo Public Prosecution Service (Ministério Público do Estado de São Paulo, 상파울루주 검찰) co-operating on the prosecutorial side.
- **INTERPOL Command and Coordination Centre (CCC)** as the cross-jurisdictional coordination node for simultaneous arrests.
- **Four-NCB axis**: INTERPOL National Central Bureaux in Brazil, Spain, Argentina, and Portugal — with Portugal listed as an NCB-cooperation partner although no Portuguese arrests were reported in the partial balance (suggesting Portugal-side investigative or asset-tracing role rather than action-day arrests; flag for verification).
- **Spain's Brigada Central de Fraudes Informáticos (Polícia Nacional)** named explicitly as the operational counterpart on the Spanish side — i.e. dedicated cyber-fraud unit, not a general policing body.
- **Crime typology — bank-fraud-as-cybercrime**: PIX-rail account drain via institutional-account compromise, treated under Brazil's penal code as organised crime + computer-device intrusion + electronic-fraud theft + money laundering, illustrating Brazilian prosecution's hybrid cybercrime / financial-crime framing.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This case fills three identifiable wiki gaps:

1. **First Brazil-Spain-Argentina cybercrime IC action-day** documented in the wiki. Existing Brazil-side coverage in the wiki (e.g. [[spf-fpb-operation-cryptoscam-2025|Operation Cryptoscam (SPF-FPB, 2025)]], various US-Brazil extraditions) treats Brazil as a partner state in Singapore- or US-led cases; Magna Fraus is the inverse — Brazil-led, with Iberian-LatAm arrests on the Spain/Argentina side.
2. **PIX-payment-system attack vector** is a new technique-class in the wiki, complementary to existing bank-trojan and BEC pages.
3. **Cyber GAECO (MP-SP)** appears for the first time as a Brazilian prosecutorial partner alongside PF; this is a state-level (not federal) public-ministry cyber unit, structurally distinct from the federal-prosecutorial bodies usually seen in cross-border cases.

## Translation Notes

> [!note] Translation note
> Original-language Portuguese terms preserved on the operation page in parentheses on first mention:
> - **Operação Magna Fraus** ("Great Fraud Operation", informal English)
> - **invasão de dispositivo informático** (computer-device intrusion / unauthorised access; Lei nº 12.737/2012, "Lei Carolina Dieckmann", art. 154-A of the Brazilian Penal Code)
> - **furto mediante fraude eletrônica** (theft by electronic fraud; art. 155 §4-B Brazilian Penal Code)
> - **organização criminosa** (criminal organisation; Lei nº 12.850/2013)
> - **lavagem de dinheiro** (money laundering; Lei nº 9.613/1998)
> - **PIX** (Brazilian Central Bank instant-payment system, launched 2020)
> - **Cyber GAECO** (Grupo de Atuação Especial de Combate ao Crime Organizado — Special Action Group for the Combat of Organised Crime, of the São Paulo State Public Ministry)
> - **Brigada Central de Fraudes Informáticos** (Spanish National Police's Central Brigade for Computer Frauds; informal English "Spanish Computer-Fraud Central Brigade")

## Open Questions for Future

title: Decisive actions against cryptocurrency scammers earning over EUR 600 million
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust newsroom press release (dated 2025-11-04) describing a synchronised 5-country operation on 27 and 29 October 2025 that arrested 9 suspects in Cyprus, Spain, and Germany for their involvement in a EUR 600 million cryptocurrency money-laundering network. The network operated dozens of fake cryptocurrency investment platforms recruiting victims through social media advertising, cold calling, fake news articles, and fake celebrity testimonials, then laundered the proceeds using blockchain technology. Eurojust set up a joint investigation team (JIT) between French and Belgian authorities and brought prosecutors and investigative judges from France, Belgium, Cyprus, Spain, and Germany together at Eurojust's premises in The Hague to plan the takedown. Action-day seizures: EUR 800,000 in bank accounts, EUR 415,000 in cryptocurrencies, EUR 300,000 in cash. The release names participating authorities for each of the 5 countries verbatim, including the Spanish Mossos d'Esquadra and Policia Nacional cybercrime units, the Cypriot MOKAS, and the German Cologne Criminal Police.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the November 2025 €600M Eurojust crypto-money-laundering takedown. It supplies (a) the JIT cooperation pattern (France-Belgium JIT extended to multi-country coordination), (b) the synchronised arrest-action methodology in 3 home-country jurisdictions (Cyprus, Spain, Germany) with searches and asset freezing, (c) the verbatim per-country authority roster for each of 5 EU member states, and (d) the Eurojust-The-Hague coordination-centre pattern for multi-jurisdictional planning. Together with the [[eurojust-100m-crypto-investment-fraud-takedown-2025|earlier September 2025 €100M crypto fraud takedown]], this case demonstrates a recurring 2025 EU-coordinated crypto-investment-fraud enforcement model.

## Note on text fidelity

Eurojust newsroom URL was retrieved via the project's TLS-spoofing fetch tool (`tools/web_verify_tls.py`) and the raw page preserves the body text verbatim with all named-authorities-list elements captured.

title: KNPA — 캄보디아 바벳 거점 로맨스스캠 조직원 5명 베트남에서 검거 (2025-11-04)
text:
## Summary

Korean-language tier-1 primary press release, issued 2025-11-04 by the [[knpa|Korean National Police Agency]] via the official Korea.kr 정책브리핑 channel (newsId 156724852), reporting the 2025-10-28 simultaneous arrest of five Korean-national suspects of a Cambodia-Bavet-based web romance-scam organisation, executed in three Vietnamese cities (Da Nang, Ho Chi Minh City, Khanh Hoa Province) through joint operation with Vietnamese Public Security (**베트남 공안청 / Bộ Công an Việt Nam**).

The release explicitly attributes the arrest outcome to bilateral Korean–Vietnamese law-enforcement cooperation, with KNPA International Cooperation Officer Lee Jae-young (이재영 경찰청 국제협력관) quoted: *"이번 검거는 동남아 스캠(사기) 범죄 확산이 우려되는 상황에서 양국 간 긴밀한 국제 공조로 거둔 구체적 성과"* and *"베트남은 해외 범죄자에 대한 법집행 역량이 높고, 한국 경찰과의 상시 공조 채널을 통해 양국 협력체계가 안정적으로 작동하고 있다."*

This satisfies the L24 ≥2-country IC requirement: South Korea (lead) + Vietnam (foreign LE counterpart, named in primary source). Cambodia is identified as the operating base / origin jurisdiction and is recorded only as prose attribution, not as a participating jurisdiction.

## Source Provenance

- **Primary publication**: korea.kr 정책브리핑 (`https://www.korea.kr/briefing/pressReleaseView.do?newsId=156724852`). The Korean government policy-briefing portal reproduces national-agency releases verbatim and is treated as an authoritative tier-1 channel for KNPA statements; the substantive body of the release is in the linked PDF/HWPX attachments (Korean naming: `251104(석간용) 경찰청, 캄보디아 '바벳' 거점 로맨스스캠 조직 피의자 5명 베트남에서 검거(국제공조).pdf`).
- **Secondary verbatim reproductions** (used to recover the body text since the HTML view exposes only the title): Herald Corp (https://biz.heraldcorp.com/article/10608628), Kyunghyang (https://www.khan.co.kr/article/202511040600061), Insidevina (https://www.insidevina.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=41398), Cosmianews, Korea Daily.
- The release is publicly distributed (sensitivity: public) and is reproducible from any of the secondary outlets listed above.

title: Treasury Sanctions DPRK Bankers and Institutions Involved in Laundering Cybercrime Proceeds and IT Worker Funds
text:
## Source Summary

This OFAC press release (SB-0302) announces the designation of eight individuals and two entities tied to laundering proceeds from DPRK cybercrime (including a ransomware actor targeting U.S. victims) and DPRK IT worker fraud schemes. The release is highly likely the most significant Treasury action against the DPRK financial-enabler network since the 2025 MSMT report, given its explicit linkage of cryptocurrency-laundering pathways through First Credit Bank, Ryujong Credit Bank, and front-company representatives operating from China and Russia.

The action targets two distinct but interconnected revenue streams. First, ransomware and cryptocurrency-heist proceeds — Jang Kuk Chol and Ho Jong Son are designated as bankers managing $5.3 million in cryptocurrency for First Credit Bank, with at least some funds traced to a DPRK ransomware actor that previously targeted U.S. victims. Second, DPRK IT worker revenue — KMCTC (operating delegations from Shenyang and Dandong, China) and its president U Yong Su are designated for orchestrating IT worker dispatch and using Chinese-national banking proxies. The sanctions evasion side targets Ryujong Credit Bank plus five China- or Russia-based representatives of already-designated DPRK financial institutions (Korea Daesong Bank, KCB, Ryugyong Commercial Bank, FTB, DPRK Central Bank), with documented transaction volumes ranging from $200,000 to $85 million per individual.

The legal authorities cited are confirmed: cyber-enabled E.O. 13694 (as amended by 13757, 14144, 14306), DPRK-specific E.O. 13810, E.O. 13551 (DPRK successor authority), and E.O. 13722. Treasury frames the action as enforcement support for UN Security Council resolutions and the Multilateral Sanctions Monitoring Team (MSMT) — likely indicating ongoing multilateral information-sharing among MSMT participating states even though the unilateral designations themselves are U.S. action.

Per Treasury's own cumulative metric, North Korea-affiliated cybercriminals have stolen over $3 billion (primarily cryptocurrency) in the past three years; DPRK IT workers earn hundreds of millions of dollars per year. The release does not enumerate specific cooperating partner agencies for the designation itself but explicitly references MSMT collective resilience.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — tier-1 OFAC primary release on Treasury Newsroom.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — official OFAC determinations, naming designees, listed E.O. authorities, and specific transaction values.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Treasury Newsroom publication, SDN List public.
