title: Authorities continue to protect citizens from cybercriminals during major malware operation
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release (dated 2025-11-13) announcing the third major phase of [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]]. The release documents the dismantling of 1,025 servers worldwide and the seizure of 20 domains tied to three named malware families — Rhadamanthys (infostealer, malware-as-a-service since 2022), VenomRAT (RAT delivered through phishing), and Elysium (associated botnet). Eleven searches were conducted across participating jurisdictions, login data from 100,000+ cryptocurrency wallets was recovered, and one main RAT-line suspect was arrested in Greece. Eight participating authorities are formally enumerated in the release: Germany, France, Netherlands, Denmark, United Kingdom, United States, Australia, and Canada — with named investigative and prosecutorial offices for each. Greece is named only in the context of the RAT-suspect arrest. Coordination "from the outset" is attributed to Eurojust and Europol. The release does not designate the operation as a Joint Investigation Team.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 Eurojust source for the third phase of [[operation-endgame|Operation Endgame]] (the umbrella established in May 2024 and continued in May 2025). It supplies (a) the participating-authorities roster verbatim with named investigative and prosecutorial offices for each of eight jurisdictions; (b) the methodology shift in this phase — initial-access infostealer plus RAT plus associated botnet, rather than dropper/loader cycle (Phase 1) or ransomware-affiliate kill chain (Phase 2); (c) infrastructure-disruption metrics (1,025 servers, 20 domains, 11 searches); and (d) the Greek arrest as the operational/criminal-justice anchor for the cohort.

title: Desarticulada red señala de diseñar un modelo de estafa a través de falsas plataformas de inversión de Criptoactivos (Fiscalía Colombia, Bolletín 57752, 18 noviembre 2025)
text:
## Summary

Tier-1 primary press release from Colombia's Fiscalía General de la Nación reporting the dismantling of an organised criminal network that ran fraudulent cryptocurrency- and securities-investment platforms from 2021 through October 2025, defrauding victims in Colombia, Chile, and Peru of an aggregate ~COP 71 billion. Fourteen alleged members were captured and charged in Colombia; three were ordered to pretrial detention in a carceral facility, and the remaining eleven to house arrest. The alleged ringleader named in the bulletin is a Mexican national, Alejandro Maximiliano Ronquillo Treviño (alias Daniel). The Dirección Especializada contra los Delitos Informáticos (DECDI) of the Fiscalía led the investigation.

## International cooperation footprint (per this primary source)

- **Cross-border victimisation**: Colombia, Chile, Peru.
- **Suspect of foreign nationality**: Mexico (one suspect, alleged ringleader).
- **No foreign agency, MLAT request, AMERIPOL/INTERPOL channel, or extradition action is named in the bulletin.**

> [!note] Primary-source scope
> Per L19-strict, this source page only asserts what the Fiscalía bulletin explicitly states. Secondary press (El Tiempo, Infobae, El Colombiano) report that the operation was named "Operación Dominó" and that an additional Israeli national (Boaz Iaakok Derra, alias Jim) is allegedly the principal architect. These claims are NOT in the Fiscalía bulletin and are flagged for separate verification before any wiki claim is built on them.

title: United States, Australia, and United Kingdom Sanction Russian Cybercrime Infrastructure Supporting Ransomware (Treasury SB-0319)
text:
# Source Summary — Treasury SB-0319 (Media Land / Aeza follow-on sanctions)

## Bibliographic Details

- **Title**: United States, Australia, and United Kingdom Sanction Russian Cybercrime Infrastructure Supporting Ransomware
- **Press Release Number**: SB-0319
- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Date**: 2025-11-19
- **URL**: https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/sb0319
- **Language**: English

## Source Assessment

- **Reliability**: high — official Treasury/OFAC press release issued in coordination with named foreign-government partners (Australia DFAT, UK FCDO) and the FBI; press releases of this type carry the Treasury's formal designation findings.
- **Credibility**: confirmed — designations are administrative-record actions that take legal effect on the date of announcement; specific E.O. authorities and SDN-list rationale are stated on the record.
- **Sensitivity**: public.

## Key Findings

1. **Trilateral coordination architecture (high confidence)**. The action is described on the record as a coordinated, simultaneous designation by OFAC, Australia's Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, and the United Kingdom's Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office, with FBI playing the lead U.S. law-enforcement coordinating role.
2. **Bulletproof hosting as ransomware enabler (high confidence)**. Treasury characterizes Media Land as a "key launching pad for ransomware," explicitly tying its infrastructure to Lockbit, BlackSuit, and Play ransomware operators and to DDoS attacks against U.S. companies and critical infrastructure.
3. **Sanctions-evasion follow-on (high confidence)**. The Hypercore Ltd. designation is framed as a direct response to a rebranding strategy initiated by Aeza Group's leadership after Aeza's 1 July 2025 OFAC designation; the action is presented as a maintenance-of-pressure operation against the same target ecosystem.
4. **Cross-jurisdictional shell layering (high confidence)**. The press release identifies Aeza front/enabler entities incorporated in three jurisdictions outside Russia: the United Kingdom (Hypercore Ltd.), Serbia (Smart Digital Ideas DOO), and Uzbekistan (Datavice MCHJ).
5. **Statutory basis (confirmed)**. All designations cite E.O. 13694, as amended by E.O. 13757, E.O. 14144, and E.O. 14306 — the U.S. cyber-sanctions authority chain. No criminal indictments are announced in this release.

## Pages Created/Updated

- Created: [[treasury-us-au-uk-sanctions-media-land-russian-bulletproof-hosting-2025]]

## Notes

- This is a press-release-tier source; it confirms the existence and statutory basis of the designations but does not by itself enumerate the full SDN-list metadata for each designated person. A subsequent OFAC Recent Actions / SDN list pull would be needed to capture full identifiers for the designated individuals and entities.
- The press release names Treasury Under Secretary for Terrorism and Financial Intelligence John K. Hurley as the on-the-record speaker.

title: Europol and partners shut down 'Cryptomixer' (Operation Olympia)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release (publication date estimate 2025-11-26) announcing Operation Olympia — the 24-28 November 2025 takedown of Cryptomixer, a hybrid clear-web + dark-web cryptocurrency mixing service active since 2016 that processed over EUR 1.3 billion in Bitcoin laundering for ransomware groups, dark web markets, drug traffickers, weapons traffickers, and payment-card fraudsters. 3 Switzerland-based servers seized; cryptomixer.io domain seized; 12+ TB data + EUR 25M+ Bitcoin confiscated. German lead via BKA + Frankfurt am Main ZIT; Swiss action via Zurich City Police + Zurich Cantonal Police + Zurich PPO. Europol J-CAT coordination + on-spot forensic support.

## IC Pattern

This release complements the [[2025-12-01-eurojust-de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown|Eurojust 2025-12-01 release]] on the same operation by adding:

- **Operation Olympia name** (not in the Eurojust release).
- **Cryptomixer service identification** (not in the Eurojust release; the Eurojust release referred to "an unnamed cryptocurrency mixing service").
- **EUR 1.3 billion lifetime laundering volume** (the Eurojust release said "EUR 1.3bn in Bitcoin" via TRM Labs / AML Intelligence reporting, but the primary Europol release confirms it).
- **Service domain (cryptomixer.io) and 12 TB data seizure**.
- **Specific Swiss authorities** (Zurich City + Cantonal Police + Zurich PPO).
- **Specific German authorities** (BKA + Frankfurt am Main ZIT).
- **Europol J-CAT** as the discrete coordination instrument.
- **Chipmixer (March 2023)** as the Europol-supported predecessor mixer takedown.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release should be cited alongside the Eurojust 2025-12-01 release on [[de-ch-crypto-mixer-takedown-2025|the operation page]], with the operation page enriched to capture: (a) Operation Olympia name; (b) Cryptomixer service identification; (c) EUR 1.3 billion lifetime laundering volume; (d) cryptomixer.io domain seizure; (e) 12+ TB data; (f) specific German and Swiss participating authorities; (g) Europol J-CAT mechanism class; (h) Chipmixer (2023) as predecessor mixer takedown chronology.

title: International takedown of cryptocurrency fraud network laundering over EUR 700 million
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated 2025-11-26 (publication date estimate based on action-day timing; the Europol newsroom JSON does not include a date field). The release supersedes and extends the Eurojust 2025-11-04 release on Phase 1 alone, by adding (a) Phase 2 details (2025-11-25 to 2025-11-26 affiliate-marketing-infrastructure action days), (b) the cumulative network-laundering scale of EUR 700 million+, and (c) three additional participating countries (Bulgaria, Israel, Malta) beyond the 5-country Phase 1 roster.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a new IC mechanism class: **two-phase EU + non-EU partner takedown of crypto-fraud + affiliate-marketing infrastructure**. Notable features:

- **Phase 1 = laundering / call-centre infrastructure** action targeted at the OCG members.
- **Phase 2 = affiliate marketing infrastructure** action targeted at the deepfake-celebrity ad operators that drive victim-targeting upstream of the call centres.
- **Israel as Phase 2 participant** — the wiki's first record of Israeli National Cybercrime Unit (Intelligence Division) operational involvement in a Europol-coordinated cryptocurrency-fraud takedown.
- **Malta as Phase 1 participant + Bulgaria as Phase 2 participant** — adds two further EU member-state partners to the Phase 1-only Eurojust release.
- **Deepfake-celebrity-impersonation advertising** as the upstream marketing channel for crypto-investment-fraud — first wiki record of this specific scam-marketing methodology recorded in primary IC documentation.
- **Europol cryptocurrency-specialist deployment** (similar pattern as in [[eurojust-100m-crypto-investment-fraud-takedown-2025|the September 2025 €100M Spain-Lithuania JIT crypto-fraud takedown]]).

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release is the second tier-1 primary source for the same operation already recorded under [[eurojust-600m-crypto-money-laundering-takedown-2025]] (created in iteration 23 from the Eurojust 2025-11-04 Phase-1-only release). The Europol release should be cited alongside the Eurojust release on that operation page, with the operation page enriched to capture: (a) Phase 2 action days; (b) cumulative EUR 700M+ network-laundering scale; (c) Bulgaria, Israel, and Malta as additional participating countries; (d) deepfake-celebrity-impersonation affiliate-marketing methodology.

title: Cryptocurrency mixing service used to launder money taken down
text:
## Summary

Official Eurojust press release announcing the takedown of an unnamed cryptocurrency mixing service used by criminals to launder proceeds from drug trafficking, weapons trafficking, online fraud and other cybercrimes. The action involved authorities from [[germany|Germany]] and [[switzerland|Switzerland]], coordinated by [[eurojust|Eurojust]] and [[europol-ec3|Europol]]. During an action week, more than **EUR 25 million** in cryptocurrency was seized and **three servers** were taken down.

The release does not name the mixing service nor specify the legal basis used by Switzerland (a non-EU state), the number of arrests, or further procedural detail.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official agency press release) |
| Credibility | probably-true (official source, sparse detail) |
| Topic Relevance | 95/100 (DE-CH cybercrime IC with seizure outcome) |
| Data Completeness | 50/100 (no operation name, no arrests count, no service name) |
| Verification | Single-source as of ingest; cross-checks pending |

## Key Metrics

- **Cryptocurrency seized:** EUR 25 million+
- **Servers seized:** 3
- **Participating countries:** 2 (Germany, Switzerland) + Eurojust + Europol
- **Arrests:** not stated

title: Police Bust Organized Crime Group Running Fraudulent Online Investment Platforms (Sofia, BG-ES bilateral, December 2025)
text:
## Summary

This source is a 2025-12-12 BTA news article (Bulgarian News Agency, Bulgaria's national wire service) reporting an operational announcement by the Sofia City Prosecutor's Office. It describes a 2025-12-11 multi-agency raid in Sofia against an organized crime group running fraudulent online investment platforms via call centres. International cooperation was bilateral with Spain's Guardia Civil, channelled through EU police-cooperation mechanisms under Bulgarian authority direction.

Reliability is rated high (BTA is the official Bulgarian state news agency, and the article is a near-verbatim relay of the Sofia City Prosecutor's Office announcement). Credibility is rated probably-true rather than confirmed because the announcement is summarized through BTA's report rather than reproduced as the original SCPO release; specific numbers (suspect counts, victim counts, total scheme value) are not stated and would need a corroborating Eurojust / Europol / Spanish-side source to upgrade.

The action does NOT appear to be the same event as the Eurojust 700M cryptocurrency-fraud network takedown (Phase 1 2025-10-27, Phase 2 2025-11-25 to 2025-11-26): the announcement does not name Eurojust, Europol, France, Belgium, Cyprus, Germany, or Israel, and the timing (2025-12-11) post-dates Phase 2 by approximately two weeks. It is treated here as a distinct bilateral Bulgaria-Spain operation pending further corroboration.

title: Rozbita międzynarodowa grupa przestępcza zajmująca się praniem pieniędzy z oszustw internetowych
text:
## Source Summary

Polish-language official CBZC press release (16 December 2025) announcing the dismantlement of an international money-laundering network operated alongside cyber-enabled investment/online-fraud monetization. The investigation runs as a Eurojust-hosted Joint Investigation Team between CBZC's Kraków Branch and law enforcement from Spain, the Czech Republic, and Hungary, with action days carried out in Poland, the Czech Republic, and Hungary.

## Relevance to IC

This is the controlling source for:

- The four-country JIT roster (Poland, Spain, Czech Republic, Hungary) operating "under Eurojust auspices."
- Five additional source-named cash-out countries (Greece, Italy, Spain, Belgium, the Netherlands) for fund withdrawals.
- Suspect nationalities (Romanian, Polish, Czech, Hungarian).
- Numerical results: 12 charged, 10 in pretrial detention, ~EUR 2.23M + PLN 717,000 laundered, >40 forged IDs seized, 1 BMW X5 seized.
- Action-day chronology (initial Romanian arrests, Czech and Hungarian arrests with direct CBZC Kraków participation, 2025-12-05 Śląskie arrests, 2025-12-07 detention orders).
- The investigation's "developmental" status — further action days planned domestically and abroad.

## Reliability Note

Source-tier 1 (national cybercrime bureau official press release). Reliability `high`, credibility `confirmed` consistent with CLAUDE.md defaults for press release (official agency). No competing tier-1 cross-source has been ingested yet; numerical claims are derived directly from CBZC and are subject to updating in any later Eurojust or partner-country release.

title: PF deflagra Operação Dark Bet contra tráfico internacional de brasileiros e coação à prática de crimes cibernéticos (Nigéria nexus)
text:
## Source Summary

Official press release issued by the **Polícia Federal (PF)** of Brazil announcing **Operação Dark Bet** on **2025-12-16**. The operation targets a Brazilian organization that lured Brazilian nationals through false online-betting employment offers, trafficked them to Nigeria, and forced them under armed surveillance to operate cybercrime/scam schemes.

A mirror announcement on the Ministério da Justiça e Segurança Pública (MJSP) site (2025-12-17) adds an attributed quotation from Marina Bernardes, MJSP coordinator for human trafficking enforcement, characterizing trafficking via false BET-sector job offers as "a concrete and extremely serious reality."

## Relevance to IC

- **Cross-border cooperation case**: Brazil ↔ Nigeria, originating from cybercrime arrests on Nigerian soil that surfaced 5 Brazilian nationals as suspects/victims of forced cybercrime labor.
- **Cybercrime + trafficking convergence**: Documents the same scam-compound model previously seen in Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Myanmar) now appearing in West Africa, with Brazilian nationals as the trafficked workforce. Strengthens [[brazil]] participation in transnational scam-compound enforcement and adds [[nigeria]] as an emerging destination jurisdiction.
- **Tier-1 primary source** for the operation page [[operation-dark-bet-pf-brazil-nigeria-2025]].
- **Less-covered region balance**: This source enriches Latin America – West Africa coverage, which has been thinner than the EU/US/East-Asia clusters.

> [!note] Translation note
> Brazilian Portuguese legal phrasing: *coação à prática de crimes cibernéticos* (coercion to commit cybercrimes), *redução à condição análoga à de escravo* (reduction to a condition analogous to slavery — Brazilian Penal Code Art. 149 in standard usage, not cited in the release), *aliciamento* (recruitment with intent to traffic).

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The release does not enumerate Brazilian Penal Code articles; downstream pages should not infer specific articles without further documentary evidence (e.g., the petition or judicial order).

title: Mezinárodní operace OCTOPUS a CONNECT (NCTEKK)
text:
## Summary

Czech Police press release dated 2025-12-16 jointly announcing two Eurojust-coordinated international operations against fraudulent call centres in Ukraine. Operation OCTOPUS (concluded 2025-11-20) targeted an investment-fraud network and produced 17 arrests in Kyiv, 19 searches, approximately CZK 33 million in seizures, and identified damage of CZK 43 million across 138 victims; the press release frames the perpetrators as a single criminal group with a 31-year-old organizer from the Donetsk region. Operation CONNECT (concluded 2025-12-09) targeted a separate police/bank impersonation network running three call centres in Kyiv, Ivano-Frankivsk, and Dnipro; that operation involved a four-country (Czech Republic, Ukraine, Lithuania, Latvia) JIT.

## Reliability Notes

This is a tier-1 primary source: the official press release from the Czech national cybercrime police unit (NCTEKK) carrying a named spokesperson. The numbers for OCTOPUS (17 arrests, 19 searches, CZK 33M, CZK 43M, 138 victims) appear only in this Czech Police release among public tier-1 sources accessed during ingest. The Eurojust press release of the same date covers the CONNECT branch but does not enumerate OCTOPUS separately.

## Key Quotes

- "Případ koordinoval Eurojust prostřednictvím společného vyšetřovacího týmu (JIT) mezi ČR a Ukrajinou." (OCTOPUS — bilateral CZ-UA JIT)
- "Kriminalisté NCTEKK se zahraničními partnery z Ukrajiny, Litvy a Lotyšska v rámci společného vyšetřovacího týmu koordinovaného Eurojustem" (CONNECT — quadrilateral JIT)

## IC Scope

- Participating countries asserted by source: Czech Republic, Ukraine (OCTOPUS); Czech Republic, Ukraine, Lithuania, Latvia (CONNECT).
- Coordinating body: [[eurojust|Eurojust]].
- Mechanism: Joint Investigation Team ([[joint-investigation-team]]).
- Crime types: investment fraud (OCTOPUS) and police/bank impersonation phone fraud (CONNECT) — both [[online-fraud-ic]] and [[voice-phishing-ic]] adjacent.

title: U.S. and International Partners Take Down E-Note Crypto Exchange and Indict Russian Operator (USAO–EDMI announcement)
text:
## Source Summary

US Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of Michigan announcement (2025-12-17 press conference) of the takedown of the E-Note cryptocurrency exchange and payment-processing service, alleged to have laundered USD 70 million+ in cybercrime proceeds since 2017 (with personal money-mule activity dating to 2010). A federal indictment was unsealed against Mykhalio Petrovich Chudnovets, 39, a Russian national alleged to have controlled and operated E-Note from approximately 2011 through 2025, on one count of money laundering conspiracy carrying a maximum 20-year prison term. Three domains (e-note.com, e-note.ws, jabb.mn) and associated servers and mobile applications were seized. The German Federal Criminal Police Office (Bundeskriminalamt / BKA) and the Finnish National Bureau of Investigation (Keskusrikospoliisi / KRP) are named as cooperating European authorities. US-side participants: USAO-EDMI (United States Attorney Jerome F. Gorgon, Jr.), FBI Detroit Division (SAC Jennifer Runyan), Michigan State Police, and the Michigan Cyber Command Center.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the December 2025 US-Germany-Finland cooperation pattern in a cybercrime money-laundering platform takedown. It supplies (a) the cooperation roster, (b) the verbatim USD 70 million+ illicit-proceeds figure with a 2017 start date for the FBI tracking window, (c) the named domain trio for the seized infrastructure, and (d) the multi-decade scope of the alleged operator (2010 personal money-mule networks → 2011 platform → 2025 takedown). The wiki record also documents healthcare and critical-infrastructure US-victim sectors as a downstream-attribution category, supporting cross-corpus reasoning on ransomware-money-laundering supply chains.

## Note on text fidelity

The original DOJ EDMI press release URL was not directly accessible via this corpus's TLS-spoofing fetch tool or via Anthropic WebFetch on 2026-05-08. Verbatim content is captured from same-day tier-2 reporting (CyberScoop, BleepingComputer, Detroit News, ClickOnDetroit, The Register) that reproduces the USAO-EDMI press conference prepared remarks and the indictment summary. Future re-fetch when the DOJ EDMI URL becomes accessible should refresh the raw archive.

title: 574 arrests and USD 3 million recovered in coordinated cybercrime operation across Africa - Operation Sentinel
text:
## Summary

INTERPOL coordinated Operation Sentinel from 27 October to 27 November 2025 across 19 African countries under the AFJOC umbrella. 574 suspects arrested, USD 3 million recovered. Over 6,000 malicious links taken down and 6 ransomware variants decrypted. Focused on BEC, digital extortion, and ransomware. Funded by UK FCDO and EU-Council of Europe GLACY-e project.

## Key Data

- **Operation:** Operation Sentinel
- **Timeframe:** 27 October – 27 November 2025
- **Countries:** 19 African countries
- **Arrests:** 574
- **Assets recovered:** USD 3 million
- **Estimated losses:** USD 21 million+
- **Malicious links taken down:** 6,000+
- **Ransomware variants decrypted:** 6
- **Crime types:** BEC, digital extortion, ransomware
- **Funding:** UK FCDO, EU-Council of Europe GLACY-e

## Relevance

Largest BEC-related enforcement action on the African continent. Demonstrates donor-funded model for building African cybercrime enforcement capacity.

title: 가상자산 편취 악성프로그램(KMSAuto) 유포 외국인 해커 인터폴 수배 후 한국 송환·구속
text:
## Source Summary

KNPA Korea.kr 정책브리핑 press release (newsId=156737083) dated 2025-12-29 (online embargo 2025-12-28 09:00) announcing the extradition into Korea of a Lithuanian national accused of operating a memory-hacking clipper malware (`KMSAuto`-bundled) campaign. Verbatim Korean text was extracted from the KNPA-issued PDF attachment fetched via `curl_cffi` chrome120 against `korea.kr/common/download.do?fileId=198312514&tblKey=GMN` (HTTP 200, 313,624 bytes).

The release identifies the cooperation chain as: KNPA → 법무부 → 검찰청 → Lithuanian Ministry of Justice + Lithuanian Police (joint search 2024-12) → INTERPOL Red Notice → Georgian Police (arrest April 2025) → Korean extradition request to Georgia → custody in Korea late 2025. The release names KNPA Cyber Investigation Senior Officer 박우현 as spokesperson and the responsible unit as 국가수사본부 수사국 사이버테러대응과.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 Korean-language source for a 2025 KNPA-led three-jurisdiction (KR–LT–GE) extradition operation that uses INTERPOL Red Notice plus formal MLA as the cooperation backbone, in a clipper-malware (memory-hacking) crime line not previously documented in this wiki. It supplies (a) Korea-as-requesting-state extradition flow from Georgia, with Lithuania as the joint-search jurisdiction; (b) explicit naming of MOJ and SPO Korea as 형사사법공조 conduits alongside KNPA; (c) victim distribution across 234 countries with 6 transit-jurisdictions and 6 foreign exchanges/companies traced for crypto follow-the-money; (d) a clipper/memory-hacking malware family bundled with cracked-software distribution as the predicate cyber-dependent crime; and (e) a publicly-reported 5-year-4-month elapsed investigation time as a benchmark for Korean cross-border crypto-fraud extradition cycles.

## Note on text fidelity

The Korea.kr HTML view shell was successfully fetched (200 OK, 140,456 bytes), but the article body is hosted exclusively in the attached PDF/HWPX files; the HTML view itself only shows metadata and download links. The full verbatim Korean body therefore comes from the PDF attachment (`fileId=198312514`), preserving all numerical claims (2.8M distributions, 3,100 addresses, 8,400 transactions, ₩1.7B, 234 countries, 14 crypto assets, 22 seized items) and the named cooperating institutions (Lithuanian MOJ, Lithuanian Police, Georgian Police, INTERPOL, Korean MOJ, Korean SPO). Independent same-day Korean-press mirrors (디지털주리스트, 누리일보, 더시그널뉴스, 서울경제, 한국경제, 국민일보, 미주중앙일보, 헤럴드경제, 아시아투데이, MBC, 연합뉴스TV) corroborate the numerical breakdown and the cooperation-chain narrative without contradiction.

title: 34 arrests in Spain during action against the 'Black Axe' criminal organisation
text:
## Source Summary

Europol Newsroom press release dated **12 January 2026** announcing the arrest of 34 alleged Black Axe members in Spain (28 Seville, 3 Madrid, 2 Málaga, 1 Barcelona), conducted by the Spanish National Police in coordination with the Bavarian State Criminal Police Office (Germany) and supported by Europol. Documented damages exceed EUR 5.93 million; EUR 119,352 frozen in bank accounts and EUR 66,403 seized in cash. Europol contributions included information analysis, a Madrid-held data sprint, and on-the-spot support; two German BLKA officers were deployed on-site during the action day.

The release establishes the **Bavaria-Andalusia bilateral track** as a recurring Europol-supported cooperation pattern against Black Axe and complements the wiki's existing Black Axe coverage ([[black-axe-bec-2021]] EU-South Africa-US 2021, [[operation-jackal]] / [[operation-jackal-iii]] INTERPOL, [[black-axe-switzerland-takedown-2026]] CH-IT-DE-AT-NL April 2026). The new Spain-Germany action precedes the Switzerland action by roughly three months.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Europol primary press release; first-hand description of operation and Europol contributions.
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational figures (arrests, seizures, damages) come directly from the authority that participated; no contradicting source identified.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release is publicly distributed via Europol Newsroom.

title: Verdachte aangehouden in onderzoek naar cybercriminaliteit (OM Schiphol arrest, AvCheck follow-up)
text:
# Source Summary — OM.nl 12 January 2026 Schiphol Arrest (AvCheck follow-up)

## What this source is

Press release ("Nieuwsbericht") issued by the Dutch Public Prosecution Service (Openbaar Ministerie, OM) — specifically the Landelijk Parket (LP, "national prosecutor's office") — on 12 January 2026, announcing the Sunday-evening (11 January) arrest at Amsterdam Schiphol Airport of a 33-year-old Dutch national suspected of operating a Counter-Antivirus (CAV) testing service used by malware developers.

The release links the arrest explicitly to a coordinated international operation of May 2025 in which Dutch THTC, together with the United States and Finland, dismantled a "key service for malware developers" (in externe verslaggeving geïdentificeerd als AvCheck.net en gerelateerde crypter-domeinen — onderdeel van [[operation-endgame]]).

## Why it matters for IC

- Tier-1 primary source from a Dutch prosecutor (OM.nl) — under-sourced publisher in the wiki.
- Documents a follow-up arrest in the Netherlands of a Dutch suspect who had relocated to the UAE; international flag (signalering) was a precondition for the Schiphol stop.
- Confirms named tri-lateral cooperation: Netherlands–United States–Finland.
- Demonstrates the typical post-takedown investigative cycle: infrastructure seizure → intelligence extraction → identification of operators → international flagging → arrest at border.

## Cross-checks

- Independent reporting (The Register 2026-01-13, Infosecurity Magazine, SC Media) corroborates that the Schiphol-arrested 33-year-old is the alleged AvCheck operator and that the underlying May 2025 takedown was the AvCheck.net / Cryptor.biz / Cryptor.live / Crypt.guru seizure announced by the U.S. DOJ on 27 May 2025 (Operation Endgame).
- The OM.nl release itself does not name "AvCheck" — that linkage is asserted in tier-2 reporting. Treat the AvCheck identification as **highly likely** (consistent timeline, agencies named, suspect profile) but not directly confirmed by tier-1 source. Flag in the operation page.

## Reliability / sensitivity

- Reliability: **high** (official Dutch prosecutor press release).
- Credibility: **probably-true** (single official source; unindicted suspect; awaits trial).
- Sensitivity: **public**.

title: Swatting és doxing miatt indult nyomozás (Investigation launched for swatting and doxing) — Hungarian Police press release on Hungary-Romania joint operation
text:
## Source Summary

Hungarian National Police press release of 2026-01-26 announcing the conclusion of a six-month investigation that began in mid-July 2025 with a wave of swatting and doxing offences in Hungary. The release confirms a 2026-01-20 coordinated joint action between three Hungarian agencies (KR NNI, TEK, Nagykanizsa Police HQ) and Romanian police across four locations in Hungary (Budapest, Nyíregyháza, Kisvárda) and Romania (Bihor County). All four perpetrators — three Hungarian and one Romanian, ages 16-20 — were identified through reconnaissance work supported by the Counter Terrorism Centre (TEK). Discord was the contact-and-escalation platform; victim data harvested in Discord disputes was used to place false emergency calls.

The piece is a tier-1 primary source: it is the official announcement by the Hungarian National Bureau of Investigation (KR NNI), the operational lead, and is publicly accessible in Hungarian on police.hu (no paywall, no log-in). Reliability is high; credibility is confirmed for the procedural facts (date, agencies, locations, charges) that it asserts directly. The Romanian counterpart agency is identified only generically as "the Romanian partner authority" / "Romanian police" — a name-level gap noted in the operation page.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a previously unrepresented cooperation pattern in the wiki:

- **Hungary-Romania bilateral cybercrime cooperation on swatting/doxing** with overlapping cross-border participation: not a parallel-investigation pattern but a shared-team pattern, with Hungarian KR NNI cyber investigators physically present at the Romanian-led Bihor County search. This is closer in structure to a joint investigation team (JIT) than to a sequential MLAT pattern, although the press release does not explicitly describe a Eurojust-supported JIT.
- **TEK (Counter Terrorism Centre) reconnaissance of swatting/doxing offences** — Hungarian classification of swatting bomb threats against schools and police units as terrorism-adjacent offences, justifying TEK involvement in the reconnaissance phase. The Romanian 17-year-old is charged with "terrorist act" (terrorcselekmény) — confirming this classification at the indictment level.
- **Discord-as-vector** is documented at the official-source level for an operational case rather than only in academic or media commentary.
- **Underage and just-of-age cohort (16-20)** suggests a youth-cybercrime pattern that is increasingly transnational at the regional (CEE) level.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

Complements:

- The substantial existing US swatting corpus (e.g. [[2025-02-13_justice-gov-mdfl_california-teenager-sentenced-48-months-nationwide-swatting-spree|California teenager nationwide swatting spree]], the Bremerton, Washington series, and [[2024-06-05_apnews-com_washington-man-sentenced-for-20-swatting-calls-of-false-threats-in-us-canada|US-Canada swatting cooperation]]). Provides the first non-US-anchored swatting record in the wiki and the first record of swatting prosecuted partly under terrorism law.
- The Romanian cybercrime cooperation corpus already in the wiki (the [[romania-moldova-phishing-laundering-jit-2025|Romania-Moldova phishing-laundering JIT]], [[romania-diicot|DIICOT]] cases). Demonstrates that Romania's cooperation on cybercrime is not limited to its EU-MLA flagship channels but also extends to direct bilateral arrangements with neighbours like Hungary on lower-volume but high-public-impact offences.
- The cyberstalking/doxing corpus ([[cyberstalking-ic]]). First wiki record of Discord-platform doxing prosecuted at multi-country level.

title: U.S. Law Enforcement Assists Bulgarian Law Enforcement in Taking Down Three of the Largest Piracy Sites in The European Union
text:
## Source Summary

US Department of Justice Office of Public Affairs press release **26-82**, dated **Friday, 30 January 2026**, announcing that the U.S. government executed seizure warrants against three U.S.-registered internet domains — **zamunda.net, arenabg.com, and zelka.org** — used by commercial websites allegedly engaged in the illegal distribution of copyrighted works belonging to U.S. companies and individuals (movies, television shows, video games, software, e-books, and other content). The three websites were allegedly operated from Bulgaria; per the affidavits, the three domains receive "tens of millions of visits a year" and one is "often ranked as one of the top 10 most visited domains in Bulgaria."

The release establishes:

1. **U.S. – Bulgaria coordinated takedown structure**: simultaneous U.S. seizure of the .net/.com/.org domains plus Bulgarian search-and-seizure operations targeting the underlying infrastructure and operators.
2. **ICHIP Bucharest as the cooperation hub**: the DOJ's International Computer Hacking and Intellectual Property advisor based in Bucharest, Romania provided significant assistance — cross-border IP/cybercrime capacity-building turning into a tier-1 enforcement assist.
3. **HSI Athens + CBP Sofia + Europol triangulated technical assistance**: the U.S. Department of Homeland Security's HSI Athens office (Greece) and CBP Sofia attaché are explicitly named alongside Europol as having "critical roles" in coordinating efforts and providing technical assistance.
4. **First-of-its-kind Bulgarian piracy-platform-operator statute**: the related DOJ ICHIP April 2026 summary records that the Bulgarian arrests and prosecutions proceeded under a "new criminal statute that criminalizes operating piracy platforms, the first law of its kind in Europe, drafted with OPDAT and CCIPS assistance" — i.e., the Bulgarian statute was itself a product of U.S.–Bulgaria legislative-drafting cooperation.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release with named senior officials (AAG Duva, U.S. Attorney Kruger, ASAC Wright) and a press-release number (26-82).
- **Credibility: confirmed** — Independently corroborated by the Bulgarian News Agency (BTA) on the same day, which adds: four individuals detained; 30 addresses searched; supervised by Sofia City and Sofia District Prosecutor's Offices; coordinated by Acting Prosecutor General Borislav Sarafov.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via DOJ OPA Newsroom.

## IC Scope

- ≥2 country test: **PASS**. United States, Bulgaria are central. Greece (HSI Athens), Romania (ICHIP Bucharest), and Europol (multilateral EU coordinator) are explicitly named as cooperation partners.

## Cross-Border Cooperation Channels Documented

| Channel | Role | Quote / Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| ICHIP program (OPDAT + CCIPS) | Bilateral capacity-building → operational assist | "providing intellectual property and cybercrime technical assistance to foreign law enforcement... including Bulgaria" |
| ICHIP Bucharest | In-region advisor | "The ICHIP based in Bucharest, Romania provided significant assistance" |
| Europol | EU coordination | "the critical role of Europol... in coordinating efforts and providing technical assistance" |
| HSI Athens | DHS in-region attaché | named as "critical role" cooperation partner |
| CBP Sofia | DHS in-region attaché | named as "critical role" cooperation partner |
| IPR Center | U.S. interagency clearing house | "one of the U.S. government's key weapons in the fight against criminal counterfeiting and piracy" |
| Domain seizure (U.S.) + 30-address search-and-seizure (Bulgaria) | Operational mechanism | DOJ release + BTA same-day report |

title: Polizia di Stato: 'Pedopornografia: abusi su minori in diretta online, 2 arresti' — Italian C.N.C.P.O. + U.S. HSI + Europol joint action against 'live distant child abuse' (announced 2 Feb 2026)
text:
## Source Profile

Official press release issued by the Italian State Police (Polizia di Stato) Press Office on `poliziadistato.it`, the central institutional domain. The release announces the conclusion of an undercover transnational investigation into "live distant child abuse" run by the Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online (C.N.C.P.O.) of [[italy-polizia-postale|Polizia Postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]], coordinated by the Procura di Milano (Public Prosecutor's Office, Milan), and conducted in international cooperation with the U.S. Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) and with [[europol-ec3|Europol]].

The release is a tier-1 primary source: it is published on the official `.poliziadistato.it` Italian Polizia di Stato domain, signed institutionally by the Press Office, and provides operational figures (arrests, denunciations, geographic distribution) directly from the lead investigative agency. The Italian-language text (preserved verbatim in the corresponding raw file) is authoritative; the working English summary in this entry is for indexing only.

The investigation has been described in Italian secondary press as "Operazione Light Bridge" but that operation name does **not** appear in the official Polizia di Stato press release; it should therefore be treated as an unverified secondary-press alias.

> [!note] Operation-name caveat
> "Operazione Light Bridge" is widely cited in Italian secondary press (LSD Magazine, La Milano, Sky TG24, Matrice Digitale) for this same investigation. The official Polizia di Stato press release does not use that name; this wiki therefore treats "Light Bridge" as an unverified alias and uses descriptive titling pending confirmation from a Polizia di Stato or Procura di Milano source that explicitly uses the operation name.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official agency publication on the central `poliziadistato.it` domain.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement by the lead investigative agency, with two foreign cooperation partners (HSI, Europol) explicitly named and the prosecuting office (Procura di Milano) identified.
- **Sensitivity: public** — intended for general distribution; defendants' identities are protected per Italian practice (only ages and provinces are stated for the four denunciati; the two arrested are identified only by gender and province).

## Italian-Language Legal and Operational Terms Preserved

| Italian | English working translation |
|---|---|
| pedopornografia online | online child sexual abuse material (CSAM) |
| live distant child abuse | live distant child abuse (English term used verbatim in the Italian original) |
| denuncia in stato di libertà | report/charge filed with the prosecutor while suspect is not in custody |
| perquisizione (domiciliare/personale/informatica) | search (domiciliary / personal / computer) |
| Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online (C.N.C.P.O.) | National Centre for Combating Online Child Pornography |
| Centro operativo per la sicurezza cibernetica (C.O.S.C.) | Cybersecurity Operations Centre |
| Procura della Repubblica (di Milano) | Public Prosecutor's Office (of Milan) |
| flussi finanziari | financial flows |
| money business services | money-transfer business services (English term used verbatim in the Italian original) |
| de-anonimizzazione | de-anonymisation |

> [!note] Translation note
> "Denuncia in stato di libertà" has no exact English equivalent and is distinct from both U.S. "indictment" and U.S. "summons" practice; it denotes that a formal criminal report has been transmitted to the prosecutor while the suspect remains at liberty. The Italian text uses the English-language industry term "live distant child abuse" verbatim, reflecting standard Europol/HSI usage in this offence stream.

title: 경찰청 제2차 국제공조 작전회의 (작전명 Breaking Chains) 개최 — 22개국 + 5개 국제기구 / 서울
text:
## Source Summary

KNPA press release (정책브리핑 newsId 156743085, dated 2026-02-05) announcing the 2nd Korea-hosted **International Cooperation Operations Meeting** under the operation codename **Breaking Chains (브레이킹 체인스)**, convened in Seoul over 2026-02-05–06 with 22 country-level law-enforcement agencies and 5 international organisations — INTERPOL, ASEANAPOL, AFRIPOL, the International Organization for Migration (IOM), and the UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC). The release describes Breaking Chains as a Korea-led ("韓 주도") multilateral operational platform against transnational scam crime — including scam compounds (스캠 센터), human trafficking (인신매매), and online fraud (온라인 사기) — and reports cumulative results from the 1st meeting cycle: 26 cases / 75 tracking leads exchanged; 31 arrests + 15 Korea-bound repatriations across Vietnam-China-based scam operations; a Cambodia-based female-matching fraud takedown (15 arrests; 27 victims; ~₩2.589 B losses); and a Cambodia–Thailand human-trafficking ring leader arrest tied to a Korean-national victim murder case in Cambodia. The 2nd meeting agenda includes 13 countries running bilateral/multilateral conferences with 45 cases and 80 priority tracking leads, plus an explicit 'balloon-effect' (풍선효과) discussion track. 박성주 (NIH Commissioner) speaks for the 2nd meeting; 이재영 (International Cooperation Officer) speaks for the Phase-1 takedown announcement.

## Relevance to IC

This is a tier-1 Korean-language primary source for a *Korea-as-coordinator* multilateral cybercrime cooperation pattern that is materially distinct from the Korea-as-requesting-state extradition pattern already documented in the wiki ([[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026]], [[korea-cambodia-scam-repatriation]]). Specifically the source establishes:

1. A **Korean-hosted standing multilateral cooperation forum** ("국제공조 작전회의") with a named operational codename ("Breaking Chains" / 브레이킹 체인스), structured as a meeting series (1st: 2025-11; 2nd: 2026-02-05) plus interleaved field operations (Phase-1: 2025-12-04 Cambodia + Thailand).
2. **Five-international-organisation participation** in a Korean-hosted forum (INTERPOL, ASEANAPOL, AFRIPOL, IOM, UNODC), notably extending Korean-coordinated cooperation into the African (AFRIPOL) and migration-focused (IOM) institutional spaces beyond the Asia-Pacific frame more typical of prior Korean cybercrime IC.
3. Three **cooperation-by-mechanism types** in a single source: (i) information sharing and tracking-lead exchange (75 → 80 leads), (ii) joint enforcement / coordinated raid (Cambodia 15 + Thailand 13 + 31 prior arrests = 59 cumulative), (iii) repatriation/extradition into Korea (15 returned in Phase-1 prior arrests; 1 trafficker total head arrested for Korean-national victim case).
4. An explicit **balloon-effect** (풍선효과) policy track — i.e., recognition that strengthened cooperation in one corridor relocates organisations to weaker-enforcement jurisdictions — which is rare in primary press releases and useful for the wiki's challenges frame.
5. **Linkage to the wider Korean whole-of-government scam-compound architecture** — the 대통령실 '초국가범죄 특별대응 TF' is named as the coordinating Korean policy structure, and the 코리아전담반 (Korea-dedicated Squad) is named as the field operational unit, providing a rare cross-source bridge to [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026]].

## Note on text fidelity

The Korea.kr policy briefing room URL renders the press-release body inside a SynapDocViewer iframe whose viewer JS does not expose the document text to a TLS-spoofed static fetch (LESSONS L11 variant). The verbatim body text was therefore captured through 뉴데일리 (Save Internet NewDaily, 2026-02-05 08:26) which carries the press release as a 전재 (republication) preserving the verbatim agency text, supplemented by 이데일리 (2025-12-05) for the Phase-1 operation outcome verbatim. When korea.kr direct extraction becomes possible (Synap viewer change or CDN refresh), the raw document s

title: 47-latek związany z grupą Phobos zatrzymany przez policjantów CBZC (47-year-old linked to Phobos group arrested by CBZC officers)
text:
## Summary

Polish-language tier-1 press release from Poland's Central Bureau for Combating Cybercrime (CBZC, [[poland-police|Centralne Biuro Zwalczania Cyberprzestępczości]]) announcing the 2026-02-17 arrest in Małopolskie Voivodeship of a 47-year-old Polish national suspected of producing, acquiring and distributing hacking tools (Polish Criminal Code Art. 269b § 1) and of contacting the [[ransomware-ic|Phobos ransomware]] group via encrypted messengers. The arrest is publicly tied to CBZC's participation in **Operation Aether**, a [[europol-ec3|Europol]]-coordinated multi-jurisdictional campaign against the Phobos RaaS ecosystem.

## International Cooperation Elements

- **Coordinating body**: [[europol-ec3|Europol]] (CBZC explicitly described as a participant in Europol-coordinated Operation Aether).
- **Named cooperation with the United States**: prior extradition of the alleged Phobos administrator to the [[united-states|USA]]; reference to [[us-doj|US Department of Justice]] documents on total Phobos ransom proceeds (>USD 16 million); US victim categories named (schools in California and Connecticut, Maryland healthcare, US DoD contractor).
- **Domestic prosecutorial supervision**: District Prosecutor's Office in Gliwice (Prokuratura Okręgowa w Gliwicach).
- The release uses the phrase "skoordynowane zatrzymania w Europie i poza nią" (coordinated arrests in Europe and beyond) without naming additional specific jurisdictions in this text.

## Verification Notes

- Fetched 2026-05-10 via plain HTTPS (curl, Mozilla UA) → HTTP 200, ~32 KB body. Source returned the full Polish-language article without bot challenge.
- Date "Data publikacji 17.02.2026" is on-page, attributable to CBZC editorial system.
- The page has the banner "Strona znajduje się w archiwum" (page is in archive) — typical Polish Police CMS treatment, not a content rollback.
- This is a fresh ingest distinct from the existing wiki record sourced from cbzc.policja.gov.pl (the 2025-12-16 CBZC Kraków money-laundering JIT, which is a different operation against a different criminal cluster).

title: 경찰청 캄보디아 스캠 조직 흔드는 합동작전… 코리아 전담반 인터폴 적색수배자 6명 등 연이어 검거 (2026-02-19)
text:
## Source Summary

KNPA press release dated 2026-02-19 (online/broadcast embargo lifted 2026-02-18 09:00) reporting a sustained joint Korea–Cambodia enforcement campaign against Korean-targeted scam organisations operating from Cambodian compounds, conducted through the Korea-dedicated Squad (코리아 전담반) jointly stood up by KNPA and Cambodian National Police inside Cambodia in November 2025. The release announces six INTERPOL Red Notice fugitives arrested between 2025-12-22 and 2026-02-10, plus three named February 2026 takedowns: a 500-metre foot-chase arrest of a scam-organisation manager (2026-02-04), a hotel-hideout arrest of a major scam cadre tied to ₩8.4 billion in victim losses (2026-02-06), and a CCTV-tracking arrest of an investment-fraud principal tied to ₩10.6 billion in victim losses (2026-02-10). The Squad has run 12 operations since opening; cumulative arrests = 140 organised-crime suspects, with 4 Korean nationals rescued. The Korean side names KNPA, NIS (국정원), the Korean Embassy in Cambodia (specifically the embedded police attaché 경찰주재관), and Seoul Metropolitan Police INTERPOL team; the Cambodian side is named only as the Cambodian National Police (캄보디아 경찰청). The release is signed by the KNPA International Police Cooperation Bureau (국제치안협력국) and quoted spokesperson is its Director-General 이재영, who flags the "balloon-effect" (풍선효과) of organisations relocating to weaker-enforcement jurisdictions.

## Relevance to IC

This is a tier-1 Korean-language primary source confirming sustained, named-individual, joint Korea–Cambodia LE field cooperation through an embedded operational unit (Korea-dedicated Squad) inside the receiving jurisdiction. It is materially distinct from the existing Korea-Cambodia operations in the wiki:

1. [[korea-cambodia-scam-repatriation]] — earlier (2025) cohort of repatriations.
2. [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026]] — April 2026 73-person mass extradition (different cohort, different timeframe).
3. [[knpa-breaking-chains-2nd-international-operation-meeting-2026]] — 2026-02-05 multilateral meeting (different event, multilateral rather than bilateral, no individual takedowns named in February 2026).

The 2026-02-19 release supplies novel datapoints not in any existing wiki page:
- 6 INTERPOL Red Notice fugitives arrested in a single 12-22 to 02-10 window (named count, not cohort total).
- Three discrete February 2026 takedowns with named operational details (foot chase distance, hotel-hideout takedown method, CCTV multi-day tracking).
- Direct quote from KNPA International Police Cooperation Bureau Director-General Lee Jae-young naming the balloon-effect (풍선효과).
- Average ~1-year-10-month in-country concealment by Red Notice subjects — primary-source transnational concealment duration datapoint.
- Whole-of-Korean-government bilateral architecture explicitly diagrammed: KNPA → NIS → Embassy → Cambodian National Police.

## L24 verification

The 2026-02-19 KNPA release explicitly names **Cambodia National Police (캄보디아 경찰청)** as the cooperating foreign LE counterpart, in multiple verbatim formulations:

- "한국 경찰청 – 국정원 - 대사관 – **캄보디아 경찰청** 간 유기적인 협력 체계를 구축"
- "**한·캄 경찰 간** 합동작전을 통해 피의자를 검거"
- "**한·캄 경찰 간** 합동작전으로 현지에서 체포"
- "앞으로도 **캄보디아 경찰**과의 공조·협력을 더욱 강화해 나가고"

Two distinct countries' LE/prosecutorial agencies confirmed: South Korea (KNPA) + Cambodia (CNP). Cooperation is explicitly acknowledged in the tier-1 primary source. Cambodia is the **co-operating** jurisdiction, not the adversary/origin/destination only — Cambodian police *participate in the joint takedowns* (한·캄 합동작전 wording explicitly attributes joint operations). L24 satisfied.

## Note on text fidelity

Unlike the 2026-02-05 Breaking Chains release (Synap viewer iframe), the 2026-02-19 release exposes its body text via downloadable PDF and HWPX attachments under the KOGL (Korea Open Government License). The PDF attachment (file 198362700, ~198 KB, 2 pages) was downloaded directly via curl_cffi chrome120 and parsed;

title: Fraudsters arrested in Nigeria following NCA intelligence sharing
text:
## Source Summary

UK NCA press release (2026-02-20) describing the 2026-01-13 arrest of seven men in Agbor, Nigeria, following NCA intelligence sharing. Seizures: 26 mobile phones, 42 SIM cards, 1 laptop. The cohort allegedly operated online investment scams, cryptocurrency fraud, and phishing attacks targeting UK residents and US persons resident in the UK. The NCA describes the operation as a partnership between the NCA, Meta, and the Nigerian Police, with the Nigerian Police National Cybercrime Centre named as the operational counterpart in the parallel Meta blog post co-published on the same date.

## Relevance to IC

This is a tier-1 source for the bilateral UK-Nigeria cooperation pattern with Meta as a private-sector partner. It supplies (a) the cooperation roster, (b) the specific platform-level methodology — fake social-media accounts impersonating cryptocurrency traders, plus fraudulent Facebook groups featuring fabricated testimonials — by which Meta's contribution operates in cooperation with bilateral law-enforcement intelligence sharing, and (c) a public-record case of British-victim cryptocurrency-investment fraud being disrupted at the Nigerian-source location through Nigerian Police arrest action and Meta account-removal. The case complements the Korean-relevant scam-compound IC corpus by providing a comparable Nigerian-source cooperation pattern (UK-side requesting state, Nigeria-side host state, Meta-side platform partner).

title: Project Compass — A project dedicated to fighting against The Com (Europol Operations page)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol Operations / Project Compass page documenting an ongoing 28-country Europol-coordinated effort, led by the European Counter Terrorism Centre (ECTC), targeting "The Com" — a nihilistic extremist transnational virtual network targeting minors. Active since January 2025; results snapshot (as of capture) include 30 arrests, 4 victims safeguarded, and 179 identified perpetrators across the partner countries.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a Europol ECTC-coordinated 28-country counter-extremism + CSAM cooperation framework that the wiki has not previously documented. Notable features:

- **Europol European Counter Terrorism Centre (ECTC)** as the coordination authority — distinct from EC3 (cybercrime) or EFECC (financial crimes).
- **Five Eyes (5) + EU Member States (22) + EFTA partners (Iceland, Norway, Switzerland)** in a single project framework — the wiki's largest enumerated single-project country roster outside of INTERPOL operations.
- **The Com / 764** as a subject — a transnational virtual network combining cyber intrusions, terrorism, and CSAM extortion of minors.
- **AP Dolphin** referenced as a connected analytical project / Europol Analysis Project that received contribution from Project Compass arrests.
- **"Data sprints"** as a discrete IC mechanism class — invited countries present their ongoing cases at Europol-organised meetings to coordinate investigations and identify cross-border links.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This project complements:
- [[operation-stream-kidflix|Operation Stream / Kidflix CSAM platform takedown (March 2025)]] — single-platform CSAM takedown
- [[operation-cronos-phase1|Operation Cronos / LockBit]] — ransomware takedown (Cyber Com would structurally correspond)
- [[labhost-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2024|LabHost PhaaS takedown (April 2024)]] — phishing-platform takedown

Project Compass adds the wiki's first record of a Europol ECTC-coordinated long-running project (vs. single-action takedown) targeting a transnational virtual network.

title: Global phishing-as-a-service platform taken down in coordinated public-private action (Tycoon 2FA disruption)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release dated **2026-03-04** announcing the coordinated public-private disruption of **Tycoon 2FA**, one of the largest phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platforms worldwide. The disruption took down 330 domains forming the platform's core infrastructure (phishing pages + control panels) and was executed by law enforcement in **six countries** — Latvia, Lithuania, Portugal, Poland, Spain, and the United Kingdom — coordinated by Europol's European Cybercrime Centre (EC3). The technical takedown was led by **Microsoft** with a coalition of private partners. Investigation triggered by intelligence-sharing from **Trend Micro** through Europol's **Cyber Intelligence Extension Programme (CIEP)**, a first-of-its-kind Europol framework that hosts private-sector experts at The Hague to work alongside EC3 analysts on specific operational projects.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes several discrete IC mechanism classes worth recording:

- **Cyber Intelligence Extension Programme (CIEP)** — first Europol-described public-private operational framework that brings industry experts physically to The Hague to work alongside EC3 analysts and investigators on specific projects. Distinct from the EC3 Advisory Groups (information-sharing forums) and from one-off mobile-office deployments. The Tycoon 2FA case is described as the first publicised CIEP-driven operational outcome.
- **Microsoft-led technical takedown + LE-led seizure split** — a recurring pattern in PhaaS/malware-as-a-service disruptions where a private vendor with platform-level visibility (Microsoft, Cloudflare) executes the technical disruption (domain takedowns, control-panel seizures, abuse-channel processing) in parallel with national LE seizing physical / hosted infrastructure and pursuing operator identification. Compare with [[labhost-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2024|LabHost (April 2024)]] (Europol J-CAT 19-country PhaaS takedown) and the [[lumma-stealer-takedown|Lumma Stealer takedown (May 2025)]] (Microsoft-led with US DOJ + Europol).
- **EC3 Advisory Groups → CIEP escalation** — the release frames Trend Micro's intelligence-sharing as flowing first through EC3 Advisory Groups (steady-state info-sharing), then escalating into CIEP (operational embedding) once the strategy required deeper public-private collaboration. This is a documented two-tier public-private mechanism class.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release complements:

- [[labhost-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2024|LabHost PhaaS takedown (April 2024)]] — Europol J-CAT 19-country PhaaS takedown. Tycoon 2FA is the next-generation PhaaS-takedown event in the wiki's PhaaS corpus.
- [[lumma-stealer-takedown|Lumma Stealer takedown (May 2025)]] — Microsoft DCU-led with US DOJ + Europol; same Microsoft-led technical-disruption + LE-led seizure split.
- [[w3ll-phishing-kit-takedown-2026|W3LL phishing-kit takedown (2026)]] — same crime-class (phishing-kit infrastructure) takedown.
- [[xss-is-cybercrime-forum-takedown-2025|xss.is cybercrime-forum takedown (2025-07)]] — Europol EC3-coordinated cybercrime-platform takedown class.
- [[leakbase-takedown-2026|LeakBase takedown (March 2026)]] — Europol-coordinated forum takedown announced in the same month.

title: United States Leads Dismantlement of One of the World's Largest Hacker Forums
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release (Number 26-205, dated 2026-03-04) announcing the seizure of LeakBase — described by DOJ as one of the world's largest online forums for cybercriminals to buy and sell stolen data and cybercrime tools, with over 142,000 members and over 215,000 messages between members. On 2026-03-03 and 2026-03-04 law enforcement in 14 countries took synchronized action coordinated by Europol in The Hague: the United States and other countries shut down LeakBase, seized data and two domains, posted seizure banners, sent prevention messages to members, and collected additional evidence. Search warrants, arrests, and interviews were executed in the United States, Australia, Belgium, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Spain, and the United Kingdom; Canada, Germany, Greece, Kosovo, Malaysia, and the Netherlands provided significant assistance.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the LeakBase 14-country takedown. It supplies (a) the cooperation roster split between active-enforcement countries (8) and assistance countries (6); (b) the coordinating venue (Europol in The Hague); (c) the US lead investigative office (FBI Salt Lake City Field Office); (d) the prosecutorial line (USAO–Utah, CCIPS Senior Counsel); (e) the named domestic US task-force participants (FBI San Diego Field Office, Utah Department of Public Safety, Provo Police Department); and (f) explicit lineage with prior DOJ-led marketplace takedowns RaidForums (2022) and BreachForums (2023, with founder convicted in 2025). The release does not enumerate the LeakBase domain names beyond "two of the domains used by the forum" or name a specific operating entity.

title: Hellenic Police DAOE: Dismantling of Cryptocurrency-Investment Fraud Organisation – 12 Arrests (2026-03-06)
text:
## Source

- **Publisher**: Hellenic Police Headquarters (Αρχηγείο Ελληνικής Αστυνομίας) — official "ΔΕΛΤΙΟ ΤΥΠΟΥ" (press release)
- **URL**: <https://www.astynomia.gr/2026/03/06/06-03-2026-d-a-o-e-exarthrosi-egklimatikis-organosis-pou-diepratte-apates-me-to-proschima-ton-ependyseon-se-kryptonomismata-12-syllipseis/>
- **Published**: 2026-03-06 (Athens). Action day: 2026-03-05.
- **Language**: Greek (el)
- **Source Tier**: 1 (national law-enforcement primary press release)
- **Reliability**: high
- **Credibility**: confirmed
- **Operations referenced**: [[greek-daoe-crypto-investment-fraud-2026]]

## Why This Source Matters

This is the **controlling tier-1 primary source** for a Greek-led international cooperation operation against a Ponzi-structured cryptocurrency-investment fraud network. The release explicitly names the foreign cooperation channels and counterpart jurisdictions, satisfying the IC scope requirement (≥2 named foreign countries) with seven jurisdictions in total: Greece (lead) plus France, Germany, Malta (EIO recipients); United Kingdom, Ireland, Denmark (MLA recipients); and Bulgaria (locus of four linked offshore companies discovered in cooperation with the Greek anti-money-laundering authority).

Distinguishing features for the IC corpus:

- **Dual-track formal cooperation in one case**: simultaneous use of EU European Investigation Order (Directive 2014/41/EU) for three EU Member States and classical MLA letters of request for the UK, Ireland and Denmark — a deliberate mechanism choice driven by the post-Brexit UK position and Denmark's opt-out from the EIO Directive.
- **Crypto-financial-investigation collaboration model**: combines a national organised-crime field unit (DAOE Crete) with the Hellenic Capital Market Commission as the crypto-asset business supervisor and the Forensic Sciences Digital Forensic Sub-Directorate for on-scene wallet analysis.
- **Asset-tracing into Bulgaria via four offshore vehicles** documented in cooperation with the Greek FIU.

## Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | Confirms |
|---|---|
| Greek City Times (2026-03-07) | Independent media confirmation of action day, 12 arrests, EIO/MLA jurisdictions |
| ΤΑ ΝΕΑ (2026-03-05, Greek-language) | Day-of action coverage in Crete and Attica |
| Athens Voice (2026-03-05) | Independent confirmation of dismantling and geography |

The Hellenic Police release remains the **anchor** because it is the only document that authoritatively lists the EIO/MLA recipient jurisdictions, the €14.5M total illicit gain figure, and the Bulgaria offshore link. Secondary news pieces uniformly defer to the police release.

## References

| # | Source | Publisher | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1] | Δ.Α.Ο.Ε. : Εξάρθρωση εγκληματικής οργάνωσης που διέπραττε απάτες με το πρόσχημα των επενδύσεων σε κρυπτονομίσματα – 12 συλλήψεις | Hellenic Police (astynomia.gr) | 2026-03-06 | https://www.astynomia.gr/2026/03/06/06-03-2026-d-a-o-e-exarthrosi-egklimatikis-organosis-pou-diepratte-apates-me-to-proschima-ton-ependyseon-se-kryptonomismata-12-syllipseis/ |

title: Coopération internationale : une « target principale » interpellée lors d'une opération conjointe du GIGN et du FBI à Saint-Martin
text:
## Source Summary

Official French Gendarmerie nationale press release dated 6 March 2026, authored by commandant Céline Morin. Reports the arrest on 4 March 2026 in Saint-Martin (Caribbean overseas collectivity of France) of a 24-year-old U.S. national identified by U.S. authorities as a "target principale" — a hacker suspected of having stolen the equivalent of USD 46 million in cryptocurrency before leaving the United States.

The arrest was executed by the Antenne GIGN de Guadeloupe (about ten GIGN operators) together with three investigators from the Section de recherches de Saint-Martin, in the presence of three FBI agents, on the basis of an international arrest warrant. The suspect is in pre-trial detention pending extradition to the United States.

## Relevance to IC

This source is operationally significant for cyber-IC mapping in three ways:

1. **Direct US–France cyber cooperation** through the FBI legal-attaché channel and a French overseas-collectivity gendarmerie unit, executed via a specialised intervention unit (GIGN) rather than ordinary judicial police — a pattern not previously documented in this wiki.
2. **Multi-jurisdiction Caribbean coordination**: the press release explicitly names the British and the Dutch as local partners, reflecting the tri-national geography of Saint-Martin / Sint Maarten and the British Overseas Territories nearby.
3. **Cryptocurrency theft → recovery**: the USD 46M is reported as recovered in the same operation as the arrest, and includes seizure of wallet credentials and IT material — a tangible cyber-asset-recovery data point on the cooperation track.

## Source Tier and Reliability

- **Tier 1**: official press release on the Gendarmerie nationale's institutional domain.
- **Reliability**: high (authoritative state law-enforcement agency).
- **Credibility**: confirmed (first-person institutional account).
- **Sensitivity**: public (published on a public-facing institutional URL).

## Korean Relevance

None reported.

title: International operation targets global organised crime networks: 15 arrests across Australia, Spain, Sweden and Thailand (Operation Candy)
text:
## Source Summary

AFP media release dated **7 March 2026** describing **Operation Candy** — a Swedish-led, ~2.5-year transnational organised crime takedown that began November 2023 with the seizure of 2 mobile phones from an alleged drug trafficker in Sweden, expanded via Europol-supported forensic analysis to expose synthetic-drug-distribution + money-laundering networks across Sweden, Thailand, Spain, Germany, and Australia, and culminated in a **4 March 2026** coordinated action day with ~20 simultaneous house searches in Spain/Sweden/Thailand. Total arrests: **15** (13 overseas action-day + 2 prior Australia-side from a 2025 German Customs referral that intercepted 1.2 tonnes of illicit drugs destined for Melbourne). Criminal asset seizures: **EUR 4 million** in cash, watches, jewellery, vehicles, and yachts.

The release establishes:

1. **Phone-forensics-first investigative model** — small-scale device seizure → Europol-supported forensic analysis → multi-country expansion. Demonstrates the value of even *two* seized phones when paired with sustained Europol analytical support.
2. **Sweden + Australia + Thailand cooperation track** — relatively rare in the wiki's existing corpus (vs. typical EU + US + UK formations).
3. **Eurojust 5-country judicial coordination** explicitly cited as essential to any future prosecutions and trials, not just to action-day arrests.
4. **Digital-physical convergence pattern** — encrypted communications + online marketplaces enabled the criminal coordination, while physical drug shipments (1.2 tonnes via Germany→Australia) carried the actual harm.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 AFP media release with named officials from Swedish Police Authority, AFP, Victoria Police, ABF, Europol, and Eurojust.
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational figures (15 arrests, EUR 4M seizures, 1.2t drug interception) corroborated across the named partner agencies; no contradicting source identified.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release is publicly distributed via AFP Newsroom.

title: PF prende, em São Paulo, cidadão belga procurado pela Bélgica por fraude e falsificação de tecnologia da informação (extradição passiva, 9 mar 2026)
text:
## Source Summary

Official press release issued on **2026-03-09** by the **Comunicação Social da Polícia Federal em São Paulo** (SCS/SR/SP) announcing the arrest of a **Belgian national** in São Paulo on a Brazilian Supreme Federal Court (STF) **arrest warrant for extradition purposes**, in response to a Belgian extradition request. The Belgian underlying charges enumerated by PF include document forgery, criminal threat, fraud, **information-technology forgery (falsificação de tecnologia da informação)**, breach of trust, and unspecified additional offences.

This places the case in the cybercrime international cooperation domain because the IT-forgery charge corresponds to what the [[budapest-convention|Council of Europe Budapest Convention]] classifies as **computer-related forgery (Article 7)** — a Convention to which both Brazil (acceded 2023) and Belgium (Party since 2012) are bound.

## Relevance to IC

- **Cross-border cooperation case (Brazil ↔ Belgium)**: Belgium as requesting state, Brazil as requested state. The case documents the **passive-extradition** workflow on the Brazilian side and is the wiki's first Brazil-Belgium cybercrime-flavoured extradition record.
- **Cybercrime nexus**: The *falsificação de tecnologia da informação* charge maps onto Budapest Convention Art. 7 (computer-related forgery). The combination with *fraude* and *abuso de confiança* is a typical computer-related-fraud + forgery cluster (Budapest Arts. 7-8).
- **Tier-1 primary source** for the operation page [[pf-belgium-extradition-arrest-sao-paulo-2026]].
- **Treaty-anchoring potential**: Both Brazil and Belgium are Parties to the Budapest Convention; the case would in principle qualify for Convention Chapter III mutual legal assistance and Art. 24 extradition framing if invoked. The release does not state which treaty basis was invoked.
- **Geographic balance**: Belgium-as-requesting-state for cybercrime in Latin America has been a thinly covered axis in the wiki; this entry strengthens the Belgium-Brazil cooperation thread that was previously visible only via [[fgp-oost-vlaanderen-belgium-phishingpanelen-caas-arrest-2026]] (Belgium-side) and [[spf-fpb-operation-cryptoscam-2025]] (Brazil-side).

> [!note] Translation note
> *Falsificação de tecnologia da informação* is not a Brazilian Penal Code charge category — it is the PF's translation of the Belgian charge, which under Belgian law most likely corresponds to **falsification informatique / informaticavalsheid** (Art. 210bis Code pénal / Strafwetboek). This in turn maps to **computer-related forgery (Art. 7)** in the Budapest Convention. The PF wording is functionally equivalent but is not a Brazilian statutory term, so the Brazilian counterpart offence in the dual-criminality assessment (see [[dual-criminality]]) would be analysed under Lei 12.737/2012 (Lei Carolina Dieckmann) and Penal Code Arts. 154-A/154-B (computer-data invasion) plus Art. 298 (document forgery).

> [!warning] Legal status check needed
> The release does not enumerate Belgian Code pénal / Strafwetboek articles, the bilateral or multilateral treaty basis, or the Brazilian Lei de Migração 13.445/2017 procedural step invoked. Downstream pages must not assert specific articles or treaty references without further documentary evidence (STF docket; DRCI extradition file; Belgian SPF Justice publication). The defendant's name is not disclosed; downstream pages must not assert an identity.

title: Servers used for cybercrime around the world taken down
text:
## Summary

It provides the strongest public metric set for [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]]: eight participating countries, 24 servers taken down, 34 domains seized, approximately EUR 3.5 million in cryptocurrency frozen by US authorities, about 124,000 customer users, and roughly 369,000 compromised devices across 163 countries.

## Evaluation

| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Reliability | A (official Eurojust press release) |
| Credibility | confirmed |
| Topic Relevance | 100/100 |
| Data Completeness | 85/100 |

## Key Metrics

- **Action day:** 2026-03-11
- **Announcement:** 2026-03-12
- **Participating countries:** Austria, Bulgaria, France, Germany, Hungary, Netherlands, Romania, United States
- **Servers taken down:** 24 in seven countries

title: AVrecon Malware-Infected Routers Exploited as Residential Proxies by SocksEscort
text:
## Source Summary

FBI TLP:CLEAR FLASH that gives the strongest technical attribution for [[proxy-service-takedown-2026-03]]. It links the SocksEscort residential proxy service to **AVrecon** malware, explains the router/IoT compromise model, and provides defensive indicators and remediation advice.

## Relevance to IC

The advisory converts the enforcement story from a generic proxy-service takedown into a malware-enabled residential proxy botnet case. It also identifies the law-enforcement partners involved in the joint takedown from the technical-notification perspective.

## Extracted Facts

- **Malware:** AVrecon
- **Service:** SocksEscort residential proxy service
- **Target devices:** routers and IoT devices, especially unsupported or vulnerable SOHO devices
- **Device footprint:** approximately 163 countries
- **Compromised access sold:** approximately 369,000 devices since 2020
- **Joint-operation partners named by FBI:** FBI, Europol, French OFAC, Dutch National Police, Austria's Bundeskriminalamt, DCIS, and IRS
- **Observed abuse:** ad fraud, vulnerability exploitation attempts, password spraying, digital marketplace fraud, banking fraud, romance fraud, and other malicious activity

title: Treasury Sanctions Facilitators of DPRK IT Worker Fraud Targeting U.S. Businesses
text:
> [!info] Source Summary
> Tier-1 official US Treasury press release (SB-0416) announcing OFAC sanctions on six individuals and two entities across DPRK, Vietnam, Laos, and Spain in the DPRK IT worker fraud enforcement chain. Quantifies the broader scheme at nearly USD 800 million in 2024 revenue, and introduces the Vietnamese-national-led, Vietnam-based front-company structure (Quangvietdnbg / Nguyen Quang Viet) as a discrete IC-relevant cash-out pattern that converted approximately USD 2.5 million for North Koreans between mid-2023 and mid-2025.

## Source Details

- **Publisher**: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)
- **Document type**: Press release (Number: SB-0416)
- **Publish date**: 2026-03-12 (verified in body and `og:article:published_time`: "2026-03-12")
- **Authority cited**: E.O. 13810 (six designations) and E.O. 13382 (Do Phi Khanh; Hoang Van Nguyen)
- **Speaker quoted**: Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessent
- **Reliability**: high (official US Treasury / OFAC press release)
- **Credibility**: confirmed (primary US government enforcement document)

## Designated Targets (8 total)

| # | Name | Type | Nexus | Authority | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amnokgang Technology Development Company | Entity | DPRK | E.O. 13810 | DPRK IT company managing overseas IT worker delegations and procuring/selling military and commercial technology |
| 2 | Nguyen Quang Viet | Individual | Vietnam (national) | E.O. 13810 | CEO of Quangvietdnbg; converted ~USD 2.5M into cryptocurrency for North Koreans, mid-2023 to mid-2025 |
| 3 | Quangvietdnbg International Services Company Limited | Entity | Vietnam | E.O. 13810 | Vietnam-based front company owned/controlled by Nguyen, providing currency conversion services to North Koreans |
| 4 | Do Phi Khanh | Individual | Vietnam (proxy for DPRK facilitator Kim Se Un) | E.O. 13382 | Acts as Kim Se Un's proxy; allows Kim to use his identity to open bank accounts and launder DPRK IT worker proceeds |
| 5 | Hoang Van Nguyen | Individual | Vietnam (associate of Kim Se Un) | E.O. 13382 | Assists Kim Se Un in opening bank accounts and enabling cryptocurrency transactions; in 2022, facilitated a counterfeit-cigarette deal exceeding USD 200,000 on Kim's behalf |
| 6 | Yun Song Guk | Individual | DPRK national operating in Boten, Laos | E.O. 13810 | Led a group of North Korean IT workers conducting freelance IT work from Laos since at least 2023 |
| 7 | Hoang Minh Quang | Individual | (associate of Yun) | E.O. 13810 | Coordinated several dozen financial transactions totaling more than USD 70,000 with Yun for IT services |
| 8 | York Louis Celestino Herrera | Individual | (named in release as one of the facilitators based in Spain) | E.O. 13810 | Worked with Yun since at least 2024 to develop freelance IT service contracts |

> [!note] Cryptocurrency address designations
> The press release narrative names eight designees but does not enumerate cryptocurrency addresses in the body text. Per OFAC practice, cryptocurrency addresses associated with these designees would be published in the simultaneous SDN List update. The total of approximately 21 cryptocurrency addresses across multiple blockchains was reported by external trackers as part of the SDN List entries; this figure is not stated in the press release body and should be cross-verified against the SDN List entries directly when used in derivative analysis.

## Key Quotes

- "Today, the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) sanctioned six individuals and two entities for their roles in Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) government-orchestrated information technology (IT) worker schemes that systematically defraud U.S. businesses and generate revenue to fund the DPRK's weapons of mass destruction (WMD) programs, including nearly $800 million in 2024."
- "The North Korean regime targets American companies through deceptive schemes carried out by its overs
