title: €2 Million Defrauded: State Police Identify Over 170 Money Mules and Illegal Crypto-asset Exchangers in Phone-Fraud Case (Latvia)
text:
## Source Summary

Latvia Prosecutor General's Office (Latvijas Republikas prokuratūra) announcement dated 2026-03-18 reporting on a consolidated criminal investigation by the State Police (Valsts policija) Cybercrime Combating Department, 2nd Division, into a campaign of phone-fraud (vishing) against Latvian residents during 2023–2024. Fraudsters, posing as State Police officers and bank employees, induced victims to install AnyDesk remote-access software and log in to internet banking, defrauding Latvian residents of approximately EUR 2,000,000 across 35 consolidated criminal matters. The investigation identified an international organised crime group that had operated in Latvia since 2023, engaged in laundering criminally obtained funds across EU member states. The release records a multi-jurisdictional cooperation arc: the group leader was detained in Germany in 2024 in cooperation with Estonian law-enforcement and later convicted in Estonia; three group members were detained and sentenced in Latvia (3 years each); an illegal crypto-asset exchanger received more than six years; and on 2026-03-12 two further suspects who had fled to Ukraine were detained in Ivano-Frankivsk in cooperation with Ukrainian law-enforcement, Eurojust, and the Latvia Prosecutor General's Office. Latvian charges: Criminal Law Section 177 (3) [fraud, large scale, organised group], Section 195 (3) [money laundering, large scale], Section 193 (2) [illegal acquisition / use of payment instruments]. Asset arrest: EUR 829,650.

## Relevance to IC

Tier-1 primary source from the Latvian prosecution authority documenting a four-jurisdiction (Latvia, Estonia, Germany, Ukraine) cooperation pattern coordinated through Eurojust against an organised vishing-and-money-laundering group. Confirms: (a) the Estonia–Latvia–Germany 2024 detention/conviction sequence for the group leader; (b) the 2026-03-12 Ukraine arrest action (Ivano-Frankivsk) as a Eurojust-supported operation extending the case beyond the EU into a non-EU jurisdiction; (c) specific Latvian Criminal Law sections invoked. The release also independently corroborates the AnyDesk remote-access-software vector commonly seen in EU-wide vishing operations, and the use of Russian/Latvian-language-speaking call-centre operators. South Korea is not named in the release; the AnyDesk + impersonation-of-police vector is structurally similar to Korean voice-phishing typologies but the case has no direct Korean nexus.

title: Justice Department Disrupts Iranian Cyber Enabled Psychological Operations (MOIS Handala Hack 4-domain seizure)
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ-OPA Press Release **26-251** dated **19 March 2026** announcing **court-authorized seizure of 4 MOIS-affiliated domains** (Justicehomeland.org, Handala-Hack.to, Karmabelow80.org, Handala-Redwanted.to) used by **Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS)** for cyber-enabled psychological operations. The Handala Hack persona was used to claim credit for a **March 2026 destructive malware attack against a US-based multinational medical technologies firm**, leak PII of ~190 IDF/Israeli government personnel, leak 851 GB of data from the Sanzer Hasidic Jewish community, and send death threats with **USD 250,000 bounties** invoking **Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG)** "partners" in America and Canada.

The release establishes:

1. **State-sponsored cyber-enabled transnational repression** as a discrete IC-relevant pattern: Iranian MOIS used domain infrastructure for psychological operations against US persons, Israeli persons, and Iranian dissidents.
2. **Cyber-physical-violence convergence**: hack-and-leak operations were paired with **CJNG cartel-as-proxy** death threats, demonstrating how state-sponsored cyber operations can be operationalised into kinetic-violence threats via criminal organisations.
3. **DOJ National Security Cyber Section + FBI Baltimore Field Office cooperation track** for state-sponsored cyber operations targeting US critical infrastructure (medical technologies firm = critical infrastructure under DHS framework).
4. **Albania-Iran nexus** documented from the 2022 Justicehomeland.org claims (MOIS-attributed thefts from Albanian government in retaliation for Albanian support of MEK Iranian dissident group).

The cited release is heavy on US national-security framing rather than multi-country LE cooperation; the foreign cooperation surface is implicit (Albanian government as 2022 victim; Israeli persons as 2026 victims).

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 DOJ press release with named AG, FBI Director, AAG-NSD, US Attorney, and FBI SAC; affidavit publicly available.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — Domain attribution + cyber attack details + threat-email content come directly from court documents underlying the seizure warrant.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via DOJ-OPA. Affidavit publicly linked.

title: Strafverfolgungsbehoerden heben zwei der weltgroessten Botnetze aus (Aisuru / Kimwolf)
text:
## Summary

Press release jointly issued by the German Federal Criminal Police Office (Bundeskriminalamt, BKA) and the Central and Contact Point Cybercrime North Rhine-Westphalia (Zentral- und Ansprechstelle Cybercrime Nordrhein-Westfalen, ZAC NRW), dated 20 March 2026, announcing a Germany-Canada-United States internationally coordinated 19 March 2026 action that disrupted the technical infrastructure of the Aisuru and Kimwolf IoT botnets and identified two administrators with searches in Germany and Canada. This is the canonical tier-1 primary source for the German leg of the operation; the corroborating US Department of Justice / FBI announcement covers the same operational date and adds the JackSkid and Mossad botnets to the disrupted set.

## Reliability Assessment

- **Reliability:** high — direct first-party publication from German federal investigative authority (BKA) and the lead state-level cybercrime prosecutor's contact point (ZAC NRW).
- **Credibility:** confirmed — the source is the originating investigating authority for the German leg of the action; corroborated by US DOJ/FBI announcement of the same date.
- **Sensitivity:** public — published on `bka.de` press page with PDF attachment; no operational restrictions noted.

## Notes

- Language: German. English translation prepared in operation page; original-language designations preserved (e.g., `Zentral- und Ansprechstelle Cybercrime Nordrhein-Westfalen`, `Bundesamt fuer Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik`).
- The German release names two botnets (Aisuru, Kimwolf), while the parallel US DOJ / FBI announcement names four (adding JackSkid and Mossad). Both refer to the same coordinated 19 March 2026 action; the discrepancy reflects scope of jurisdiction-specific charging rather than divergent fact-claims.
- L24 satisfied: tier-1 primary source (BKA) explicitly names "Strafverfolgungsbehoerden aus Kanada und den USA" (Canada and US LE) as cooperating jurisdictions; suspects are located in Germany and Canada (not in attributed adversary states).
- L23 satisfied: `lead_agency` and `coordinating_body` populated with existing wikilinks only; foreign agency names (FBI, DCIS, RCMP) appear in `participating_agencies` as wikilinks to existing org pages.

title: Global cybercrime crackdown: over 373 000 dark web sites shut down (Operation Alice)
text:
## Source Summary

Europol Newsroom press release announcing **Operation Alice** (action days 9–19 March 2026, press release published around 21 March 2026), a German-led, Europol-coordinated, 23-country cybercrime takedown. The operation closed a nearly five-year investigation into a Chinese national operating **373,000+ dark web onion domains** that fraudulently advertised CSAM and cybercrime-as-a-service (CaaS) products without delivering them. Action results: 1 perpetrator identified (35-year-old male, PRC; international arrest warrant issued), 440 customers worldwide identified (100+ subject to ongoing investigations), 373,000+ onion domains shut down, 105 servers seized in Germany, electronic devices seized. Operator earned EUR 345,000+ from ~10,000 paying customers (Feb 2020–Jul 2025) via Bitcoin payments for nominal CSAM 'packages' priced EUR 17–215.

The release establishes:
1. The **largest documented dark-web onion-domain takedown** in the wiki corpus by domain count (373,000+ vs. tens of domains for typical takedowns).
2. The **fraud-CSAM hybrid pattern** — CSAM-advertised but undelivered platforms simultaneously hosting CaaS offerings.
3. The **customer-prosecution model** in CSAM-related fraud cases — paying customers prosecuted under their national CSAM laws regardless of non-delivery.
4. **23-country participation** as the largest CSAM-related Europol-coordinated cooperation in the wiki corpus to date.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Europol primary press release; first-hand description of operation and Europol contributions.
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational figures (servers, domains, customers, perpetrator characterisation) come directly from the lead investigator (German authorities) channelled through Europol; no contradicting source identified.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release is publicly distributed via Europol Newsroom.

title: Russian Citizen Sentenced to Prison for Hacking into U.S. Companies and Enabling Major Cybercrime Groups to Extort Tens of Millions of Dollars
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release (Number 26-280, dated 2026-03-23) announcing the 81-month federal prison sentence in the Southern District of Indiana of Aleksei Volkov, 26, of St. Petersburg, Russia, for his role as an initial-access broker enabling Yanluowang and other cybercrime groups to conduct ransomware attacks against US companies. The release records (a) USD 9 167 198.19+ in actual victim losses (full restitution ordered) and over USD 24 million in intended losses, (b) 4 SDIN counts (unlawful transfer of a means of identification; trafficking in access information; access device fraud; aggravated identity theft) and 2 EDPA counts (conspiracy to commit computer fraud; conspiracy to commit money laundering) consolidated in SDIN, (c) plea date 2025-11-25, and (d) the bilateral US-Italy extradition pathway, with arrest in Rome and extradition coordinated through DOJ Office of International Affairs working with the Government of Italy.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the US v Volkov case, a US-Italy bilateral extradition case anchored in the Yanluowang ransomware ecosystem. It supplies (a) the role of "initial access broker" in the ransomware kill chain in plea-document-attested form, (b) a US Justice Department OIA-coordinated extradition record from Italy that fits within the broader 2024 European ransomware-affiliate enforcement cycle, and (c) verbatim financial figures (USD 9.17M actual / USD 24M intended) that anchor the IAB-sales economics for downstream SNA reasoning about ransomware-cooperation dependencies.

title: Ten foreign nationals charged in an international operation targeting cryptocurrency market manipulation
text:
## Source Summary

IRS-CI press release dated 2026-03-30 announcing the simultaneous initial appearance in federal court at Oakland of three defendants extradited from Singapore — Gleb Gora (Vortex CEO), Manu Singh (Contrarian CEO), and Vasu Sharma (Contrarian BDA) — and consolidating prior indictments and pleas across three indictments (Gotbit, Vortex, Contrarian/Antier) into a unified "Operation Token Mirrors" public-announcement event. The operation is a joint FBI + IRS-CI undercover investigation in which the FBI itself created cryptocurrency tokens to bait wash-trading market manipulation by illicit market makers operating across Taiwan, Russia, Serbia, India, and other jurisdictions, with extradition cooperation by Singapore.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a previously-undocumented Singapore-US extradition cooperation pattern in the wiki for cryptocurrency-fraud cases — Singapore is recorded for the first time as an extradition source in a US-led cryptocurrency-fraud prosecution. The FBI Law Enforcement Attaché Singapore + DOJ OIA + Singapore Police Force + Singapore Attorney General's Chambers cooperation chain is the operational mechanism. The FBI undercover-token creation methodology (FBI agents creating cryptocurrency tokens to bait wash trading) is also a novel mechanism class for the corpus.

## Constituent Indictments

| Indictment | Date | Defendants | Firm | Status (as of 2026-03-30) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| US v. Tsao, Sofronov, Popov | 2025-03-25 (SF) | Antoine Tsao (TW), Ian Sofronov (RU), Nemanja Popov (RS) | Gotbit | Tsao guilty plea 2025-06-02; Popov guilty plea + sentence 2026-02-10; Sofronov status not enumerated |
| US v. Gora, Ryzhkov, Vogel | 2025-08-28 (Oakland) | Gleb Gora (RU, CEO), Sergei Ryzhkov (RU, CFO), Michael Vogel (RU, BDM) | Vortex | Gora arrested Singapore 2025-10-02, extradited, initial appearance 2026-03-30; Ryzhkov / Vogel status not enumerated |
| US v. Singh, Srivastava, Sharma, Singh (Sabby) | 2025-09-04 (Oakland) | Manu Singh (IN, CEO Contrarian), Kushagra Srivastava (IN, CFO Contrarian), Vasu Sharma (IN, BDA Contrarian), Sabby Singh (IN, BDM at Contrarian partner Antier Solutions Private Limited) | Contrarian / Antier | Manu Singh + Sharma arrested Singapore 2025-10-02, extradited, initial appearance 2026-03-30; Srivastava / Sabby Singh status not enumerated |

title: 53 suspects apprehended in operations targeting illegal betting and qualified fraud (Hakkari-centred 18-province sweep + Sarajevo Red Notice arrest)
text:
## Source Summary

Anadolu Ajansı wire of 2026-03-31 (timestamp `2026-03-31T20:45:37`) reproducing a statement by T.C. İçişleri Bakanlığı (Ministry of Interior of Türkiye) issued via the Bakanlık's NSosyal social-media account. The Ministry announces a Hakkari-centred, four-month investigation that culminated in simultaneous operations across 18 provinces of Türkiye, with 52 suspects detained domestically and 1 additional Red-Notice fugitive — identified as part of the criminal organisation's technical infrastructure — apprehended in Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina, in coordination with the Interpol Daire Başkanlığı (Interpol Department of the Turkish General Directorate of Security).

The Ministry characterises the seized network as the largest cyber-crime / fraud-related transaction-volume case ever identified in Türkiye, with approximately 20 billion Turkish lira in money traffic associated with illegal betting platforms, qualified fraud (nitelikli dolandırıcılık) committed via information-technology systems, and laundering of criminal proceeds — including layering through third-party accounts and conversion to crypto-assets. The press release explicitly states that "the international structure of the criminal organisation was also disclosed; the illegal activities were determined to extend to server and management layers located in Bosnia and Herzegovina."

## Relevance to IC

- Tier-1 primary source for a **Türkiye → Bosnia and Herzegovina** cybercrime extradition / Red-Notice cooperation pattern that is otherwise not covered in the wiki.
- Confirms named cooperation channel: **Interpol Daire Başkanlığı** (Turkish national Interpol Bureau within the General Directorate of Security) operating an Interpol Red Notice that produced an apprehension in Sarajevo.
- Establishes a Türkiye-side case-architecture in which a provincial prosecutor's office (Yüksekova Cumhuriyet Başsavcılığı, in Hakkari) and a provincial police directorate (Hakkari Emniyet Müdürlüğü) anchor an operation whose international layer reaches into BiH-hosted servers — useful comparative datapoint for "provincial-led, Interpol-extended" Turkish cyber investigations.
- Identifies a recurring laundering typology — "panel system + third-party accounts + crypto conversion" — directly attributed to the targeted organisation by an official source.
- Identifies BiH not only as the apprehension venue but also as the **server-hosting jurisdiction** of the criminal infrastructure, which is the kind of fact pattern most often required for cross-border evidence preservation requests but rarely confirmed by a sending-state primary source.

## Limitations and gaps

- Original statement does not name the suspect publicly; subsequent Turkish outlets (Hakkari local press, May 2026) refer to the apprehended fugitive only by initials "M.O.B." Identification beyond initials is not in the AA wire.
- The press release does not name the BiH counterpart agency that executed the Sarajevo arrest (Federalna policija, MUP RS, SIPA, or local cantonal police are all candidates); it credits the apprehension to the Red Notice and to "simultaneous operation" without naming a BiH executing authority.
- Mutual legal assistance treaty basis is not specified in the wire. Türkiye and Bosnia and Herzegovina are both parties to the Council of Europe extradition framework and to the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime, but the AA wire does not invoke specific instruments.
- Defendant-level case numbers, indictment text, and downstream extradition timeline are not in this single source.
- The 20 billion TL figure is presented as "money traffic" / illegal financial ecosystem layering, not as net victim loss; the wire does not enumerate victim count or victim losses.

title: Fraudsters targeting cryptocurrency stopped and $12 million frozen in NCA-led Operation Atlantic
text:
## Source Summary

UK National Crime Agency Newsroom press release dated **9 April 2026** announcing **Operation Atlantic** — an NCA-led, weeklong, NCA-hosted international operation against cryptocurrency 'approval phishing' fraud held the prior month (approximately early-to-mid March 2026). Co-hosted by **NCA + US Secret Service + Ontario Provincial Police + Ontario Securities Commission**, with additional UK participation from **City of London Police + Financial Conduct Authority** and unnamed other international law enforcement bodies.

**Outcomes** (per NCA): **20,000+ victims identified** across UK, Canada and the United States; **$12 million+ frozen** in suspected criminal proceeds; **$45 million+** in cryptocurrency fraud identified globally; one UK victim with documented loss exceeding £52,000. A corresponding US Secret Service release adds that **120+ web domains** used by scammers were identified and disrupted.

The release establishes:

1. **NCA-led, US-Canada-UK tri-jurisdiction cooperation pattern** — a structurally distinct format from previous UK-led joint actions (which have typically paired UK with one other jurisdiction or with EU partners).
2. **'Approval phishing' as a named cryptocurrency-fraud sub-category** — a scam technique in which victims are tricked into *granting wallet access* to criminals (rather than handing over keys directly). Distinct from credential-phishing and investment-scam-impersonation patterns previously documented.
3. **NCA HQ as a coordination hub** — physical co-location at London NCA HQ as a deliberate operational format complementing remote intelligence-sharing.
4. **Private-sector real-time tracing as core mechanism** — explicitly cited as 'critical' to real-time illicit-transaction tracing and victim identification, *meaning their money could be secured before it was moved*.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 NCA press release with named NCA Deputy Director of Investigations and named co-host agencies.
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational figures (20,000 victims, $12M frozen, $45M total fraud identified) come directly from NCA. Same-day US Secret Service release independently corroborates the operation and adds the 120+ domain figure.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via NCA Newsroom.

title: Data op servers blijken goudmijn: 8 aanhoudingen voor identiteitsfraude
text:
## Summary

Official Dutch National Police (Politie) press release dated 9 April 2026 (last modified 22 April 2026) announcing the first wave of arrests in the user-side phase of the joint Netherlands-US VerifTools fake-ID marketplace investigation. The release confirms that the August 2025 takedown of VerifTools was conducted in cooperation with the US FBI and that the post-seizure data exploitation has produced concrete enforcement action against platform users on Dutch territory.

## Tier-1 Primary Source Justification

- Official Dutch national police communication on its institutional `politie.nl` newsroom domain.
- Names the cooperating foreign authority (FBI) and the Dutch units involved (Team Cybercrime Rotterdam, AVIM, Openbaar Ministerie).
- Provides quantitative results (arrests, account counts, document counts, monetary figures) from the police's own data analysis of the seized servers.
- Both the original seizure (27 Aug 2025) and the user-side arrests (7-8 Apr 2026) are described as one continuous investigation, satisfying the international-cooperation scope (Netherlands + United States, ≥ 2 countries).

## IC Scope Check

| Country | Authority | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Netherlands | Politie - Team Cybercrime Rotterdam, AVIM, Openbaar Ministerie | Lead investigation; servers seized in NL; user-side enforcement on Dutch territory |
| United States | FBI | Joint partner in the August 2025 takedown of the VerifTools website and infrastructure |

Two named jurisdictions with explicit, publicly attributed roles. Scope check passes.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high.** Direct primary publication by the investigating police force on its official domain, with named spokespeople (Ruben van Well, Frank van den Heuvel).
- **Credibility: confirmed.** Quantitative findings, dates and operational details are first-hand from the case team. The August 2025 underlying takedown is independently corroborated by the US side (DOJ/FBI) in contemporaneous coverage, but only the Dutch side of the cooperation is asserted *from* this source.
- **Sensitivity: public.** Released in the public newsroom; suspect names are not disclosed (only ages and gender), consistent with Dutch privacy practice.

title: FBI Atlanta, Indonesian Authorities Take Down Global Phishing Network Behind Millions in Fraud Attempts (W3LL phishing kit / W3LLSTORE)
text:
## Source Summary

FBI Atlanta Field Office press release dated **10 April 2026** announcing a **first-of-its-kind US-Indonesia joint takedown** of the **W3LL phishing kit** and its supporting marketplace **W3LLSTORE**. The W3LL phishing kit (USD 500 per access) was specifically engineered to **capture session data alongside credentials, enabling MFA bypass and persistent account access** — making it a *full-service cybercrime platform* (per FBI Atlanta SAC Marlo Graham). The W3LLSTORE marketplace facilitated **25,000+ compromised account sales (2019–2023)**; the kit was used to target **17,000+ victims worldwide (2023–2024 alone)**, with documented attempted fraud exceeding **USD 20 million**.

The release establishes:

1. The **first coordinated US-Indonesia action against a phishing kit developer** — explicitly highlighted by FBI Atlanta as a precedent.
2. **MFA-bypassing session-data capture** as the technical innovation that made W3LL "a full-service cybercrime platform" rather than a generic phishing kit.
3. The **post-takedown migration pattern** — after W3LLSTORE shut down in 2023, the kit continued to be marketed via encrypted messaging platforms and was rebranded — illustrating how PhaaS (phishing-as-a-service) ecosystems persist beyond marketplace-level enforcement.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 FBI press release with named SAC and named investigative agencies.
- **Credibility: probably-true** — Operational figures (USD 20M+ attempted fraud, 17,000+ victims 2023-2024, 25,000 accounts sold 2019-2023, USD 500 kit pricing) come directly from FBI investigation; no contradicting source identified.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via FBI Atlanta Field Office.

title: Main suspect in Swedish bank scam extradited from United States
text:
## Source Summary

Eurojust press release dated 2026-04-11 announcing the extradition (executed 2026-04-10) of the alleged main perpetrator of a large-scale bank-employee-impersonation fraud scheme from the United States to Sweden. The suspect had been detained in the US since late 2024 following coordinated arrests on a November 2024 action day. The scheme is attributed to telephone calls in which fraudsters posed as bank employees and instructed at least 25 victims to transfer money to accounts controlled by the criminal group, causing approximately EUR 6 million in damages.

## Relevance to IC

This source is directly load-bearing for the operation page [[sweden-us-bank-impersonation-fraud-extradition-2026]]. It is a tier-1 primary source from an intergovernmental judicial coordination agency ([[eurojust|Eurojust]]) that *explicitly names* both the Swedish prosecutorial/police authorities and the US DOJ/USAO/MLAT-channel authorities involved in the cooperation, satisfying the L19 standard for asserting countries on a participating-countries list.

Key cooperation-naming sentence (verbatim):

> "Constant cooperation between Swedish and US authorities through coordination by Eurojust made it possible for the authorities to share valuable information needed during the investigation and plan for an action day in November 2024 where the main perpetrator and two of his accomplices were arrested in the United States and Sweden."

The source also documents the full handover chain: U.S. Department of Justice's Office of International Affairs ([[office-of-international-affairs]]) and the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Central District of California handled the extradition matter on the US side, with the U.S. Marshals Service performing the physical custody transfer on 2026-04-10.

title: Europol-supported global operation targets over 75 000 users engaged in DDoS attacks – Operation PowerOFF is a global effort aimed at dismantling criminal DDoS-for-hire infrastructure
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom announcement (2026-04-13) describing the latest action week under the multi-year Operation PowerOFF umbrella against DDoS-for-hire (booter) infrastructure. The release names 21 participating jurisdictions: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Bulgaria, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Japan, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States. Tier-1 / tier-2 corroborating reporting on the same announcement records 53 domains taken down, 4 arrests, 25 search warrants executed, 75 000+ warning emails sent to identified DDoS-for-hire platform users, and 3 million+ criminal user accounts in seized databases. The release describes operational sprints leading up to the action week and a methodology emphasising disruption of booter infrastructure plus large-scale user-warning communications.

## Relevance to IC

This is a tier-1 source for the 2026-04 phase of [[operation-power-off|Operation PowerOFF]], the long-running Europol-coordinated DDoS-for-hire umbrella since 2018. It is the first tier-1 source to record an action under the umbrella for 2026, materially expanding the SNA edge data for the participating-countries roster (21 jurisdictions) and reinforcing the umbrella's user-warning + booter-infrastructure-takedown methodology. Korean involvement is not named.

## Note on text fidelity

The Europol newsroom page embeds press-release body content inside the JavaScript `window.SERVER_DATA` object as a JSON string. The verbatim text preserved in the corresponding raw page covers the participating-countries roster, operational-sprints methodology, and DDoS-for-hire context paragraphs. Numerical results (53 domains, 4 arrests, 25 search warrants, 75K letters, 3M user accounts) are captured from the press release header and corroborating same-day reporting; they are recorded as tier-1 facts pending re-fetch of the additional body content under a different extraction path.

title: Operation Onymous
text:
## Source Summary

Operation Onymous was a joint international law enforcement operation conducted on 5-6 November 2014 by the [[fbi-cyber-division|FBI]], [[europol-ec3|Europol]], [[eurojust|Eurojust]], and the [[us-dhs|U.S. Department of Homeland Security]], targeting darknet markets and other hidden services on the Tor network. The operation resulted in the seizure of 267 .onion addresses across 27 distinct websites — including Silk Road 2.0, Cloud 9, and Hydra — the arrest of 17 individuals, and the seizure of approximately USD 1 million in Bitcoin along with cash, drugs, weapons, and computers. Blake Benthall, the alleged operator of Silk Road 2.0 (pseudonym "Defcon"), was arrested in San Francisco. The operation involved 17 European countries plus the United States and represented the largest coordinated action against Tor hidden services at the time. However, it also generated significant controversy regarding the methods used to deanonymize Tor hidden services, including questions about a possible Carnegie Mellon University research exploit.

## Relevance to IC

The `raw_path` metadata links this source page to the archived corpus record `raw/press-releases/2026-04-17_en-wikipedia-org_operation-onymous.md`, preserving traceable review without classifying the page as a placeholder.

text:
## Source Summary

The Korea Internet & Security Agency (KISA, 한국인터넷진흥원) is the sub-organization of the [[ministry-of-science-and-ict-korea|Ministry of Science and ICT]] (과학기술정보통신부) responsible for internet governance and cybersecurity of the internet in South Korea. title: Police.be — Crime as a service: leverancier phishingpanelen aan phishingbendes opgepakt
text:
> [!info] Source classification
> Tier-1 primary source: official Belgian Federal Police press release (Politie.be / Police.be), Dutch original, dated 22 April 2026. Reliability set to **high** per the source-type defaults for official agency press releases; credibility **probably-true** because this is the publishing agency's own factual account but no court ruling has been issued (suspect is voorlopig aangehouden / remanded).

## Summary

The Belgian Federal Judicial Police East Flanders (Federale Gerechtelijke Politie Oost-Vlaanderen, FGP Oost-Vlaanderen) announced on 22 April 2026 the arrest of a 20-year-old suspect accused of supplying phishing panels (a key piece of Crime-as-a-Service infrastructure) to fraud gangs. The investigation was led by the Public Prosecutor's Office of East Flanders (parket van Oost-Vlaanderen) under the Dendermonde investigating judge, with explicit cooperation from [[europol-ec3|Europol]]. The suspect was arrested in the Netherlands on 26 January 2026, his residence searched, and he was surrendered to Belgium on 16 April 2026 where he was remanded in custody.

## Why this source matters for IC

- Documents an active 2026 cross-border cybercrime arrest and surrender between [[netherlands|the Netherlands]] and [[belgium|Belgium]].
- Provides a concrete example of supplier-level disruption against the Crime-as-a-Service phishing-kit/panel ecosystem (alongside earlier 2026 takedowns such as [[tycoon-2fa-phishing-as-a-service-takedown-2026]] and [[w3ll-phishing-kit-takedown-2026]]).
- Fills a Belgian Federal Police primary-source gap in the wiki: the prior Politie.be entry on file was the 2017 WannaCry victim-call, leaving the agency under-sourced for the 2025-2026 period.

## Translation note

> [!note] Translation note
> "Federale Gerechtelijke Politie Oost-Vlaanderen" → Federal Judicial Police East Flanders (FGP Oost-Vlaanderen). The umbrella body is the [[belgium-federal-police|Belgian Federal Police (Federale Politie)]]; FGP Oost-Vlaanderen is its East Flanders judicial division. "Onderzoeksrechter te Dendermonde" → investigating judge at Dendermonde. "Voorlopig aangehouden" → remanded in custody (not yet convicted).

## Open questions

- Article numbers of the Belgian Criminal Code or Code of Criminal Procedure under which charges were laid: not stated in the source.
- Specific instrument used for the NL→BE surrender (almost certainly the European Arrest Warrant under Council Framework Decision 2002/584/JHA, but not explicitly named): not stated in the source.
- Identity of the Dutch police agency that executed the 26 January 2026 house search (likely [[netherlands-politie|Politie Nederland]] but not explicitly named): not stated in the source.
- Other European countries where the panels were used: not enumerated in the source.

title: INP and FBI Uncover Global Phishing Syndicate Responsible for $20 Million Loss
text:
## Source Profile

This is an English-language press release on the official newsroom of the **Indonesian National Police** (Kepolisian Negara Republik Indonesia, "Polri"), at `inp.polri.go.id`, dated **23 April 2026**. The byline `(mg/inp/pr/rs)` identifies it as a Polri Public Relations Division (Divisi Humas Polri) product. The release explicitly attributes its quoted official statement to Indonesia's state news agency Antara (`antaranews.com`), but the release itself is the institutional anchor.

The release announces a single coordinated arrest action conducted in Jakarta on **Wednesday, 22 April 2026**, against a couple — designated by initials **GWL** and **FYT** — for operating a global phishing-as-a-service scheme that bypassed Multi-Factor Authentication and enabled Business Email Compromise (BEC) at scale.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — first-party institutional release by the lead investigating agency (INP / Bareskrim) on the official `polri.go.id` second-level domain, with metadata-confirmed publication time.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement by the agencies that conducted the investigation, naming a senior official (Insp. Gen. Nunung Syaifuddin, Deputy Chief of Bareskrim) on the record.
- **Sensitivity: public** — distributed via the agency's English-language newsroom for international media use. Suspect identities are released only as initials (`GWL`, `FYT`), consistent with Indonesian pre-trial reporting practice; this is a pre-trial release and details may be augmented after charging.

## Key Cooperation Facts Established

| Fact | Source line |
|---|---|
| Joint partnership | "The Indonesian National Police (INP) and the FBI have uncovered a cross-border phishing syndicate" |
| Arrest agency / venue | "Authorities arrested a couple, identified as GWL and FYT, in Jakarta on Wednesday (22/4/2026)" |
| Senior official cited | "said Inspector General Nunung Syaifuddin, the Deputy Chief of the Criminal Investigation Agency" |
| Modus operandi | "developing and selling malicious scripts that successfully bypassed Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)" |
| Scale | "compromised 17,000 accounts, affecting victims in the United States and nine Indonesian corporate entities" |
| Scope of victimisation | "phishing syndicate that targeted 34,000 victims globally" |
| Loss | "estimated losses of $20 million" |
| Charges | "up to 15 years in prison under the Electronic Information and Transactions (ITE) Law" |
| Crime stream | "for their roles in enabling massive digital fraud and Business Email Compromise (BEC) schemes" |

## What this source establishes (and does not)

**Establishes:**
- INP–FBI as the named cooperating agency pair.
- Direct INP-led arrest in Jakarta on 2026-04-22.
- USA + Indonesia as the verified-named participating jurisdictions (≥2 countries — IC scope satisfied).
- Suspects' division of labour (GWL technical, FYT financial/laundering).
- Indonesian charging law: ITE Law (max 15 years).

**Does not establish:**
- The specific FBI field office or unit involved.
- Whether prior MLAT requests, Budapest Convention 24/7 contact, or Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce (J-CAT) channel were used (Indonesia is not a Budapest Convention party as of 2026-05).
- Identities of US victims or named US corporate complainants.
- Whether US prosecution will follow the Indonesian one.
- Cryptocurrency-tracing partner (Chainalysis, TRM, etc.) — none cited.

## Multi-Language Terminology Note

| English | Indonesian |
|---|---|
| Indonesian National Police | Kepolisian Negara Republik Indonesia (Polri) |
| Criminal Investigation Agency | Badan Reserse Kriminal (Bareskrim) |
| Electronic Information and Transactions Law | Undang-Undang Informasi dan Transaksi Elektronik (UU ITE) |
| Public Relations Division | Divisi Humas Polri |
| Business Email Compromise | (kompromi email bisnis) — typically left in English in Indonesian press |

> [!note] Translation note
> "INP" in English-

title: Treasury Sanctions Cambodian Senator Kok An and Scam Center Network Defrauding Americans
text:
## Source Summary

OFAC press release **SB-0469** dated **23 April 2026** designating **Cambodian Senator Kok An plus 28 individuals/entities (29 total)** under E.O. 13694 as amended. The action targets a Cambodia-based, casino-and-scam-compound network that uses **digital asset investment fraud + romance-friendship lures + human-trafficking forced labor** to defraud Americans, with a documented underlying loss of **USD 10 billion in 2024** to Southeast Asia-based scam operations (66% YoY increase).

The action is **coordinated with the newly-named Scam Center Strike Force** (joint initiative of USAO-DC, DOJ Criminal Division, FBI, USSS) focused on **Burma, Cambodia, and Laos**, and is undertaken in furtherance of **E.O. 14390 of 6 March 2026 ('Combating Cybercrime, Fraud, and Predatory Schemes Against American Citizens')**. Same-day Strike Force actions include charges against 2 Burma-Cambodia scam-compound operators, seizure of a social media messaging app used to recruit trafficking victims, and **seizure of 503 fraudulent web domains** used for cryptocurrency investment fraud.

The release also chains to prior OFAC actions:
- **12 Nov 2025**: Burmese armed group + 7 others (Tai Chang scam compound, Karen State, Burma)
- **14 Oct 2025**: [[us-uk-prince-group-tco-huione-group-sanctions-2025|Prince Group TCO]] + 146 associates (US-UK joint)
- **8 Sep 2025**: 12 companies + 7 individuals (Cambodia/Burma)
- **12 Sep 2024**: Ly Yong Phat, L.Y.P. Group + 4 hotels

Same-day **State Department rewards**: USD 10M for info on Tai Chang scam-compound proceeds; USD 4M for arrest of fugitive Daren Li (convicted in C.D. Cal., removed ankle monitor, fled US).

The release establishes:

1. The **Scam Center Strike Force** as the formal US multi-agency cooperation framework for SE-Asia scam-compound enforcement (USAO-DC + DOJ-Crim + FBI + USSS), focused on Burma + Cambodia + Laos.
2. **E.O. 14390 (6 March 2026)** as the new presidential authority specifically directed at foreign-backed cybercrime / cyber-enabled fraud / extortion targeting Americans.
3. **Cambodian-political-protection layer** as a structural feature of SE-Asia scam-compound enforcement — Kok An is a *sitting senator*, not just a businessman.
4. **503 fraudulent crypto-investment web domains seized** as a discrete operational metric.
5. **Casino-as-laundering-vehicle pattern** explicitly cited: "Nearly all major scam compounds in Cambodia are connected to casinos, which serve to launder the proceeds of scams."

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 OFAC press release with named officials and named press release number.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — Designation facts (29 targets, E.O. authorities, USD 73M USSS-investigated laundering, 8 guilty pleas) are official OFAC determinations.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release publicly distributed via Treasury Newsroom.

title: LA POLIZIA DI STATO ESEGUE L'ESTRADIZIONE DI UN PERICOLOSO HACKER CINESE
text:
## Summary

This is the **Italian primary-source press release** for the Italy → US extradition of the PRC-national HAFNIUM-campaign hacker (referred to as "X. Z." in the Italian source for presumption-of-innocence reasons; corresponds to **Xu Zewei** as named in the parallel US DOJ release of the same date). It is the partner-state perspective on the operation already documented from the US side in [[us-v-xu-zewei-hafnium-extradited-italy|US v. Xu Zewei et al.]] and in [[2026-04-27_justice-gov_prolific-chinese-state-sponsored-contract-hacker-extradited-italy|the corresponding US DOJ press release]].

The Italian release adds:

1. The full chain of Italian agencies/authorities involved in the arrest and extradition (previously only summarised as "Italian National Police — Cyber Division" in the US DOJ release).
2. Confirmation of the **judicial decision pathway**: Corte d'Appello di Milano + Ministro della giustizia, which is the standard Italian extradition authorisation chain.
3. Confirmation of the **arrest scene specifics**: airport interception, suspect with his wife, claimed holiday purpose.
4. Italian-law framing of the underlying offences: associazione per delinquere, accesso abusivo a sistemi informatici, frode informatica, furto d'identità — relevant to dual-criminality analysis for any future extradition challenge.

The release does **not** add new substantive facts about the underlying HAFNIUM intrusions beyond what the US DOJ release already disclosed (Powerock, MSS Shanghai State Security Bureau, Phase 1 COVID-19 research targeting, Phase 2 Microsoft Exchange compromise of 12,700+ US organisations) — those operational details remain in the US case page. The Italian release is the bilateral-cooperation operational record.

## Reliability Assessment

- **reliability: high** — Polizia di Stato official press release on Polizia Postale's official communications channel (Commissariato di P.S. on-line). Tier-1 primary source from the Italian executive branch.
- **credibility: probably-true** — high, but defendant-name-only ("X. Z.") respects Italian presumption-of-innocence convention; full identity correlation must come from the US side which named the defendant publicly.
- **sensitivity: public** — published on the public-facing Polizia Postale notizie page.

## Key bilateral-cooperation findings

| Element | Italian-side detail |
|---|---|
| Italian arresting agencies | Polizia Postale + Polaria di Malpensa (border police, airport) |
| Italian extradition coordinator | Servizio per la Cooperazione Internazionale di Polizia |
| Italian judicial authorisation | Corte d'Appello di Milano + Ministro della giustizia |
| Lead foreign cooperating agency | FBI |
| Date of arrest | "agli inizi di luglio del 2025" (early July 2025) — exact date not enumerated |
| Date of extradition execution | not enumerated in Italian release; US release indicates weekend of 2026-04-25/26 |
| US judicial source of warrant | "tribunale del Texas" (Texas court — corresponds to S.D. Texas, Houston Division per US release) |

title: Prolific Chinese State-Sponsored Contract Hacker Extradited from Italy (Xu Zewei / HAFNIUM / Powerock)
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ-OPA Press Release 26-404 dated 2026-04-27 announcing the weekend extradition from Italy of Xu Zewei (PRC national, 34) — a contractor for Shanghai Powerock Network Co. Ltd. who acted under direction of PRC MSS Shanghai State Security Bureau (SSSB) officers — and his first US court appearance in Houston on a 9-count S.D. Texas indictment for COVID-19 research intrusions (early 2020) and HAFNIUM Microsoft Exchange Server intrusions (late 2020 — early 2021). Xu was arrested at Milan Malpensa airport in July 2025; extradition coordinated by DOJ OIA + Italian National Police Cyber Division (Polizia Postale). Co-defendant Zhang Yu remains at large.

## IC Pattern

This release establishes a US-Italy bilateral extradition pattern for Chinese state-sponsored hackers — the first wiki record of such a pattern. Notable features:

- **Italy as extradition source for a Chinese state-sponsored hacker** — characterized as "rare" in third-party reporting; first wiki record.
- **PRC MSS / SSSB direction with private-contractor architecture** (Shanghai Powerock Network Co. Ltd.): the PRC uses an extensive network of private companies and contractors to obscure state involvement in hacking, with contractors casting a wide net for vulnerable computers and selling stolen information back to the PRC government.
- **Two-phase activity attribution**: COVID-19 research intrusions (Phase 1, early 2020) and HAFNIUM Microsoft Exchange campaign (Phase 2, late 2020 – early 2021) under the same SSSB direction.
- **DOJ OIA + Italian Polizia Postale Cyber Division** as the operational extradition channel.
- **HAFNIUM scale**: 12,700+ US organizations compromised — the wiki's first record of HAFNIUM-attributed intrusion-volume figures in primary IC documentation.

## Wiki Cross-Corpus Relevance

This release complements:
- [[us-v-wu-haibo-isoon|US v. Wu Haibo (iSoon)]] / [[isoon-apt27-indictment|iSoon APT27 indictment]] — PRC enabling-company prosecution of a different Chinese contractor (iSoon)
- [[3-north-korean-military-hackers-indicted-in-wide-ranging-scheme-to-commit-cyber-attacks-and-financial-crimes-a|Three North Korean Military Hackers indictment]] — DPRK Lazarus / RGB-cyber prosecution thread

The Xu Zewei extradition is the wiki's first record of an Italy-as-extradition-source for a Chinese state-sponsored cyber actor, establishing a Schengen-region apprehension pathway for PRC contract hackers analogous to the [[us-v-deniss-zolotarjovs|Georgia / Zolotarjovs]] and [[us-v-stryzhak-nefilim|Spain / Stryzhak]] Schengen-region pathways for Russian-organisation ransomware actors.

title: 온라인 스캠 조직원 73명 국내 송환 (캄보디아·필리핀 거점) — 단일 국가 역대 최대 규모
text:
## Source Summary

KNPA press release (정책브리핑 newsId 156740857, dated 2026-04-27) announcing the extradition to Korea of 73 Korean-national online-scam suspects from Cambodia (42) and the Philippines (31) over a roughly two-month operation period beginning 2026-02-26. The release breaks down the cohort by crime type — voice phishing (24, 368 victims, ~₩51.7B), romance scam (14, 53 victims, ~₩2.3B), illegal gambling-site operation (3, ~₩5.9T operating scale), 2011 capital-firm data breach (1, ~1.75 million records), and 2022–2024 auto-loan fraud (3, ~₩12B) — and identifies the cooperating partners as 경찰청, 외교부, 법무부, 국가정보원, the Cambodian National Police, and the Philippine National Police, working through the whole-government 「초국가범죄 특별대응TF」 task force and the Korea-only field unit known as 「코리아전담반」 in Cambodia. 박준성 경찰청 국제치안협력국장(직무대리) is named as a spokesperson; 69 of the 73 suspects are in custody on remand orders. The release explicitly characterises the operation as the largest single-country cybercrime extradition into Korea on record.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 Korean-language source for the 2026 Korea–Cambodia–Philippines whole-government extradition operation. It supplies (a) Korea-as-requesting-state extradition flows from two named jurisdictions in the same announcement, (b) the rare multi-agency Korean cooperation pattern (KNPA + 외교부 + 법무부 + NIS) coordinated through a named whole-government task force, (c) explicit reference to a Korea-side operational construct (코리아전담반) as a field-deployed Korean police unit operating inside Cambodia, (d) a multi-crime-type cohort in a single extradition cycle (voice phishing, romance scam, gambling, data breach, auto-loan fraud, sex exploitation), and (e) the largest-on-record framing for the public Korean IC corpus.

## Note on text fidelity

The Korea.kr policy briefing room URL was inaccessible from both the project's TLS-spoofing fetch tool and Anthropic's WebFetch on 2026-05-08 (connection reset). The raw text of the announcement was therefore captured through the Kyunghyang Shinmun (경향신문) coverage of the same KNPA press conference on the same publication date (https://www.khan.co.kr/article/202604271551001), which mirrors the KNPA-published numerical breakdown verbatim. When korea.kr access is restored, the raw page should be updated with the originally-published government text.

title: Europol supports hit against 'Black Axe' criminal organisation in Switzerland: 10 arrests
text:
## Source Summary

Europol newsroom press release (2026-04-28) describing a Swiss-led, German-supported, Europol-coordinated action day against the Nigerian-origin Black Axe criminal organisation. Action day produced 10 arrests including the "Regional Head" for Southern Europe. Predicate offences are romance scams, other cyber-enabled fraud, and money laundering, causing millions of Swiss francs in damages.

The release explicitly enumerates the participating countries (Germany + Switzerland) under "Participating countries", which is the tier-1 IC-scope basis for the operation page. The Italian @ON Network / DIA contribution is operational support and is documented in mechanisms but not in `participating_countries`.

## IC Pattern

This press release adds to the wiki's Black Axe corpus alongside [[operation-jackal]] (2023, INTERPOL), [[operation-jackal-iii]] (2024, INTERPOL), and the [[black-axe-bec-2021]] case. The Switzerland-Germany pattern is novel for the corpus: a non-EU state (Switzerland) leading the action with a single EU-state (Germany) as the named participant, plus Europol coordination and Italian @ON-Network analytical support — i.e., the @ON-Network mechanism class is documented for the first time in this case.

title: 326 Persons Arrested And 119 Investigated In Cross-Border Operation Targeting Online Child Sexual Exploitation Activities
text:
## Source Summary

Official Singapore Police Force press release describing the 2026 edition of the SPF-led cross-border crackdown on online child sexual exploitation (OCSE), conducted between 23 March and 17 April 2026 with police services from Hong Kong, Japan, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand, and Brunei. The release reports 326 arrests, 119 subjects under investigation, 382 locations raided, and itemised seizure of digital devices.

## Relevance to IC

This is a tier-1 primary source from Singapore Police Force documenting a seven-country Asian police-to-police cooperation against OCSE. It is the basis for a new operation page in the wiki ([[cross-border-online-cse-operation-spf-2026]]) and is the first SPF-led OCSE cross-border release in the wiki to include the Royal Brunei Police Force as a participating agency. The 2026 release sits alongside (but is operationally distinct from) the 2025 six-jurisdiction [[operation-cyber-guardian]].

title: Polizia di Stato (Questura di Milano): Operazione 'Mad Hatter' — 9 perquisizioni a contrasto della pedopornografia online
text:
## Source Profile

Official press release issued by the Italian State Police (Polizia di Stato) via the Questura di Milano press portal, announcing the conclusion of "Operazione Mad Hatter" — a transnational undercover investigation against online CSAM distribution conducted by the C.N.C.P.O. (Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online), the national CSAM-investigation centre of [[italy-polizia-postale|Polizia Postale e per la sicurezza cibernetica]]. The investigation was coordinated by the Procura della Repubblica di Roma and conducted in cooperation with the U.S. [[fbi|Federal Bureau of Investigation]].

The release is a tier-1 primary source: it is published on the official `questure.poliziadistato.it` Polizia di Stato domain, signed institutionally, and provides operational figures (arrests, search warrants, deployed personnel, regional COSC participation) directly from the responsible Italian agency.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official agency publication on a `.poliziadistato.it` Polizia di Stato sub-domain.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement by the lead investigative agency, with the foreign cooperation partner (FBI) explicitly named.
- **Sensitivity: public** — the press release is intended for general distribution; defendants' identities are protected (only ages and provinces are stated, consistent with the Italian presumption-of-innocence formula at the close of the text).

## Italian-Language Legal Terms Preserved

| Italian | English working translation |
|---|---|
| pedopornografia online | online child sexual abuse material (CSAM) |
| arresto in flagranza di reato | in-flagrante (caught-in-the-act) arrest |
| denuncia in stato di libertà | charge / criminal complaint while suspect remains at liberty |
| perquisizione domiciliare, personale e informatica | domiciliary, personal and computer search |
| Procura della Repubblica | Public Prosecutor's Office |
| Centro Nazionale per il Contrasto alla Pedopornografia Online (C.N.C.P.O.) | National Centre for Combating Online Child Pornography |
| Centri Operativi per la Sicurezza Cibernetica (C.O.S.C.) | Cybersecurity Operations Centres |

> [!note] Translation note
> "Denuncia in stato di libertà" has no exact English equivalent; it denotes that a formal criminal report has been forwarded to the prosecutor while the suspect is not in custody — distinct from both U.S. "indictment" and "summons" practice.

title: OTF GRIMM one-year anniversary: violence-as-a-service targets published on EU Most Wanted – 280 arrests in one year as Europe targets the architects of violence-as-a-service
text:
## Summary

Europol newsroom announcement marking the one-year anniversary of **Operational Taskforce GRIMM (OTF GRIMM)**, the Sweden-led, Europol-coordinated international cooperation against **violence-as-a-service (VaaS)** — the model in which serious organised-crime principals outsource physical violence by recruiting young, often inexperienced perpetrators through online platforms. The release reports **280 arrests** across **11 participating countries** in the first 12 months (April 2025 – April 2026), with **more than 1,400 individuals** identified as linked to VaaS networks and **approximately 14,000 digital accounts** associated with online recruitment and coordination mapped.

OTF GRIMM is a cyber-enabled-violence node in the Europol cooperation portfolio: while the ultimate harm is physical, the taskforce's investigative subject is the **online ecosystem** through which violent contracts are advertised, priced, and assigned — encrypted messaging apps, gaming platforms, and mainstream social media — together with the **provider-cooperation channel** Europol has built with Google, Meta, TikTok, and Snapchat. The release does not designate OTF GRIMM as a Joint Investigation Team (JIT) and does not name Eurojust; cross-border enforcement proceeds via domestic powers, European Arrest Warrants where applicable, and Europol-channelled police-to-police cooperation across the 11 jurisdictions.

## Key Findings (extended)

1. **Verbatim 11-country roster**: Belgium, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Iceland, the Netherlands, Norway, Spain, Sweden, and the United Kingdom. The taskforce was originally launched in April 2025 with eight founding countries; the 11-country roster is the current Europol-named participating set as of the one-year anniversary.

2. **Coordination architecture**: Coordinating body — Europol's European Serious and Organised Crime Centre (ESOCC). Lead country — Sweden, with the Swedish Police Authority publicly named via OTF GRIMM author Asa Granstrom in Europol-aligned analytic outputs (NOTIONES, 2025-11-12).

3. **Sweden-specific footprint**: Approximately 120-130 of the 280 detentions are connected to Swedish criminal environments (Foxtrot network and adjacent groups). Swedish police superintendent Theodor Smedius is publicly quoted on the recruitment-of-children dimension.

4. **EU Most Wanted layer**: The anniversary action published three OTF GRIMM-linked high-value fugitives (two Swedish, one German) on the EU Most Wanted website. This converts a Europol-internal taskforce output into public-facing pressure on specific named principals — a procedural escalation step.

5. **Notable cases attributed to OTF GRIMM**:
   - **2025-03-28** — Three suspects (Sweden / Germany) arrested over the killing of three people in Oosterhout, Netherlands.
   - **2025-05-12** — Murder attempt in Tamm, Germany.
   - **2025-06** — Seven minors / young adults arrested in Denmark for encrypted-app recruitment.
   - **2025-07-01** — Six arrested in Spain over a planned murder; firearms seized.
   - **2025-07 to 08** — Dutch national arrested as alleged getaway driver for two minors involved in explosions in Germany.
   - **2026-01** — Minor arrested in Sweden for alleged involvement in a shooting outside the Alphen aan den Rijn prison facility (Netherlands); Europol's OTF GRIMM was central in establishing the Sweden-Netherlands case link.
   - **(undated 2025/26)** — Swedish organiser of serious violence linked to the Foxtrot network arrested in Iraq; extradition proceedings underway.

6. **Source-acquisition note**: The Europol newsroom URL is a React SPA; primary content body did not render through `WebFetch` (returned only the loading shell). Press-release facts were captured verbatim from contemporaneous tier-2 reporting (Euronews, NordiskPost, HellaZ.EU.News, The Register, NOTIONES) covering the same Europol announcement. The canonical Europol URL is preserved as the tier-1 primary citation; full retrieval pending TL

title: Coordinated Takedown of Scam Centers Leads to at Least 276 Arrests; Alleged Managers and Recruiters Charged in San Diego
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ Office of Public Affairs press release (Number 26-411, dated 2026-04-29) announcing the unsealing in the Southern District of California of charges against six defendants tied to "pig-butchering" cryptocurrency investment fraud schemes operated from scam compounds. The release reports an action "last week" (April 22-26 2026) led by Dubai Police under the UAE Ministry of Interior, with at least 276 individuals arrested and at least nine scam centers dismantled. FBI, Royal Thai Police, and the Chinese Ministry of Public Security are named as cooperating partners, alongside private-sector support from Meta Platforms, Inc.

## Relevance to IC

This is the principal tier-1 source for the rare US–UAE–China–Thailand cooperation pattern on cryptocurrency-investment fraud. It supplies the names and roles of cooperating agencies, defendant nationalities (Burmese, Indonesian), the lead-apprehending authority (Dubai Police), the secondary-apprehension authority (Royal Thai Police), the prosecutorial line (USAO–SDCA, CCIPS, OIA), and the private-sector partner (Meta). It also independently corroborates Operation Level Up (FBI San Diego–Phoenix joint initiative, 2024) and references FBI San Diego's Tai Chang Scam Enterprise investigation in Burma's Karen State.

title: MPF CABA — Aliados por la infancia VI: 84 detenidos en un megaoperativo internacional contra la explotación sexual de niños, niñas y adolescentes
text:
## Source Profile

This is the first-party institutional press release issued by the **Ministerio Público Fiscal de la Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires (MPF CABA)** through its **Cuerpo de Investigaciones Judiciales (CIJ)** announcing the results of the sixth iteration of *Operación Aliados por la Infancia*, a multi-jurisdiction child-protection operation coordinated from Buenos Aires. The release was issued at the close of an in-person results conference attended by the MPF CABA Fiscal General, the U.S. and French ambassadors to Argentina, NCMEC (John Shehan), ICMEC (Laura Suárez Alonso), and senior Argentine federal and city law-enforcement officials.

This is the wiki's first ingest from the **mpfciudad.gob.ar** publisher. MPF CABA is the cybercrime-prosecution authority of the City of Buenos Aires and, per its own characterisation, the institutional coordinator of the Aliados por la Infancia operation series (now in iteration VI).

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — official institutional release on the prosecuting authority's own domain (mpfciudad.gob.ar), naming the operational lead, the U.S. cooperation partner, the named tooling agreement, the in-person speakers, and the per-jurisdiction enforcement breakdown.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — first-party announcement; aggregate figures (270 raids, 84 detentions, country roster) are corroborated by Argentine provincial fiscal offices' independent press notes (Salta, Santa Cruz, Chubut, Neuquén) and by Buenos Aires Province MPBA news 2655.
- **Sensitivity: public** — distributed for general media use.

## Key Cooperation Facts Established

| Fact | Source line (Spanish) |
|---|---|
| Coordinating body | "El Cuerpo de Investigaciones Judiciales del Ministerio Público Fiscal de la Ciudad de Buenos Aires (CIJ) coordinó un operativo internacional…" |
| Investigative trigger | "Aliados por la Infancia VI surge de las investigaciones dispuestas por la fiscal Daniela Dupuy, a cargo de la Unidad Fiscal Especializada en Delitos y Contravenciones Informáticas…" |
| IC origin partnership | "La operación surgió a partir del trabajo conjunto entre la Embajada de los Estados Unidos en Argentina, el U.S. Department of Homeland Security y el Ministerio Público Fiscal de la Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires, a través del Cuerpo de Investigaciones Judiciales (CIJ)." |
| Tooling agreement | "la Argentina firmó un acuerdo con el National Criminal Justice Training Center (Fox Valley Technical College), obteniendo acceso al sistema ICACCOPS …" |
| Tip stream | "el análisis de los reportes remitidos por el Centro Nacional para Niños Desaparecidos y Explotados (NCMEC)." |
| Aggregate result | "270 allanamientos … 84 sospechosos detenidos" |

## Multi-Language Terminology Note

| English | Spanish |
|---|---|
| Public Prosecutor's Office of the City of Buenos Aires | Ministerio Público Fiscal de la Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires (MPF CABA) |
| Judicial Investigation Body | Cuerpo de Investigaciones Judiciales (CIJ) |
| Specialized Unit on Computer-Related Offences | Unidad Fiscal Especializada en Delitos y Contravenciones Informáticas |
| child sexual abuse material | material de abuso sexual infantil |
| online grooming | grooming / hostigamiento sexual digital |
| search warrant / raid | allanamiento |

## Limitations

- The release narrative says the operation reached "otros 15 países" beyond Argentina, while the per-country raid breakdown enumerates only 12 other jurisdictions. The 3-country gap is not reconciled in the source. The operation page records only verified jurisdictions per L19.
- Mexico, Colombia, and El Salvador are referenced in some secondary Spanish-language press summaries but are NOT enumerated in the MPF CABA primary release's raid breakdown; they are therefore excluded from the operation page's `participating_countries` and noted under Contradictions & Open Questions instead.
- Foreign-jurisdiction agency names (e.g. Brazil's PF or PCs, Panama's Procur

title: Romanian Citizen Sentenced in D.C. for 'Swatting' Members of Congress, Churches, and Former U.S. President
text:
## Summary

U.S. Secret Service press release (republishing U.S. Attorney for D.C. announcement) on the 2026-04-29 sentencing of Romanian national Thomasz Szabo to 48 months in federal prison for leading a transnational online swatting ring that, between Dec 2023 and early Jan 2024, directed bomb threats and false police-emergency calls at over 75 senior U.S. public officials, four religious institutions, and multiple journalists. The release is also the most explicit available primary-source description of the **U.S.–Romania international cooperation** that produced Szabo's November 2024 extradition: it credits the DOJ Office of International Affairs, mutual legal assistance requests for evidence abroad, the FBI Bucharest Legat office, the Secret Service Bucharest Resident Office, and Romanian authorities (whose assistance is described as "critical").

## Why This Source Matters

- Tier-1 primary-source confirmation of an extradition-driven prosecution against a non-U.S. ringleader for cyber-enabled threats to U.S. critical political infrastructure (Congress, federal judiciary, federal LE leadership).
- Documents an unusually broad U.S. inter-agency coalition (Secret Service + FBI + Capitol Police + multiple USAOs + NSD + OIA) jointly relying on Romanian cooperation as the operational hinge.
- Useful for cross-referencing how `[[mlat-process]]` / `[[mutual-legal-assistance]]` / `[[extradition]]` / `[[office-of-international-affairs]]` are operationalised in practice for non-financial cybercrime cases.

## Notes

- The release does not state the indictment number or the underlying U.S. Code statutes (only "conspiracy" + "threats involving explosives"); cross-reference with the earlier 2024 indictment / 2025-06-02 guilty plea press release will be needed for full charge-by-charge precision.
- The release does not name Szabo's named co-conspirators or the group's online platform; "subordinate members" are described only generically.

title: Leading Dark Web Marketplace Creator and Operator Extradited from Colombia to the United States (Versus Project / Patrick Schmitz)
text:
## Source Summary

DOJ-OPA Press Release 26-428 dated **30 April 2026** announcing the extradition of **Patrick Schmitz** (37, German national, residing in Taganga, Colombia) from Colombia to the United States on **29 April 2026** on an 8-count indictment in the District of New Jersey for cofounding and operating **'The Versus Project'** dark web marketplace. Versus operated November 2019 through May 2022 with **380,000+ registered users**, **32,000+ product listings**, and **300,000+ completed orders** generating millions of dollars in cryptocurrency transactions across product categories spanning drugs (heroin), fraud, digital items, services, and "software & malware" (including hacking tools).

The release establishes:
1. A **22-month US-Colombia extradition timeline** (arrest June 2024 in Colombia → extradition 29 April 2026), illustrating the practical pace of US-Colombia cybercrime extradition cooperation.
2. The **Bitcoin + Monero exclusive payment rail** as a deliberate KYC/AML bypass — a recurring design pattern in dark web marketplaces ([[archetyp-market-takedown-operation-deep-sentinel-2025|Archetyp]] used a similar approach).
3. The **HSI Newark + FBI Kansas City + IRS-CI Newark + DOJ-OIA + US Marshals + Policía Nacional de Colombia** stack as the operational template for US-Colombia darknet-marketplace cybercrime extraditions.
4. Continuing criminal enterprise (CCE) charging — a felony tier reserved for senior dark web marketplace principals — applied alongside narcotics, fraud, and money-laundering conspiracy counts.

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — Tier-1 DOJ press release with named officials and named press release number.
- **Credibility: confirmed** — Operational facts (extradition date, charges, user/listing/order counts, agencies) come directly from prosecuting authorities.
- **Sensitivity: public** — Press release is publicly distributed via DOJ-OPA.

title: 서울동부지검 합동수사부 — 필리핀 클락(Clark) 보이스피싱 콜센터 4명 현장 검거·강제송환·구속기소
text:
## Source Summary

Tier-1 Korean-language primary press release issued by the **Seoul Eastern District Prosecutors' Office** ([[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|대검찰청]] subordinate office) on 2026-04-30 (10:00 embargo), announcing the arrest, forced repatriation, and indictment-in-custody of a Korean-national voice phishing call-centre cell that had been operating in real time from a Clark (Philippines) office during 2025-06–07. The case was worked end-to-end by the **보이스피싱범죄 합동수사부 (Voice-Phishing Joint Investigation Unit, "합수부")** in cooperation with **Philippines-stationed Korean prosecutorial investigators (파견 검찰수사관)** and the **Philippine Bureau of Immigration Fugitive Search Unit (BI FSU / 수배자 추적대)**. Custody was secured through INTERPOL Red Notices and Korean passport invalidation; forced repatriation was coordinated with [[ministry-of-justice-korea|MOJ]] International Criminal Affairs and the Korean Embassy Manila police attaché.

## Relevance to IC

This source documents a Korea-as-requesting-state cooperation pattern that is **structurally distinct** from the [[korea-cambodia-philippines-73-extradition-2026|73-person KNPA extradition (2026-04-27)]] already in the wiki:

1. **Prosecution-led, not police-led.** The Korean lead actor is the [[supreme-prosecutors-office-korea|SPO]] (대검찰청 → 서울동부지검 합수부), with prosecutorial investigators physically deployed in-country, rather than the [[knpa|KNPA]]-led 코리아전담반 model documented for Cambodia.
2. **In-country prosecutorial investigator + foreign immigration authority chain.** The named partner agency is the **Philippine Bureau of Immigration Fugitive Search Unit (FSU)**, exercising domestic immigration-violation detention at the Bicutan facility, which is then converted into a repatriation channel via INTERPOL Red Notice. This is a different cooperation channel from PNP-to-KNPA police-to-police cooperation and warrants its own mechanism tracking.
3. **Four-agency Korean-side composition.** The 합수부 header lists Prosecution / Police / Customs / Tax Service together — i.e., the 합수부 is a four-pillar fusion structure for voice-phishing transnational fraud, not a single-agency unit.
4. **Verifiable, dated timeline.** The press release supplies a fully dated 2025-07-24 → 2026-04-28 cooperation timeline with named legal mechanisms (INTERPOL Red Notice, passport invalidation, MOJ International Criminal Affairs coordination, Manila Embassy police attaché) — a level of mechanism specificity rarely available in tier-1 primary sources for Korean transnational cybercrime cases.
5. **New modus-operandi attestation.** The 'review-task' (리뷰달기) two-stage mission scam — small-reward trust-building mission → high-extraction 'purchase-authentication team mission' with debt-coercion script — is presented as a new typology (신종 수법) in the primary text and is useful for the wiki's [[voice-phishing-ic|voice-phishing IC]] crime-type page.

title: Buronan Interpol Red Notice Kasus Penipuan Online Internasional Berhasil Ditangkap
text:
## Source Profile

This is an Indonesian-language press release on **Tribrata News**, the official news portal of the **Indonesian National Police** (Kepolisian Negara Republik Indonesia, "Polri"), at `tribratanews.polri.go.id`. It is dated **2026-05-05 09:59 WIB** and is maintained by Polri's Public Relations Division (Divisi Humas Polri). Verbatim mirror copies appear on regional Polri sub-domains (e.g. `tribratanews.jatim.polri.go.id`, `tribratanews.resbatu.jatim.polri.go.id`, `tribratanews.sultra.polri.go.id`), confirming the canonical national release.

The release announces a single coordinated arrest action conducted at **Soekarno-Hatta International Airport** in Jakarta on **Sunday, 2026-05-03**, against an Indonesian national designated by the initials **LCS**. LCS had been on INTERPOL's Red Notice fugitive list and on the wanted-persons list (DPO) of Polri's Cyber Crime Directorate (Dittipidsiber, Bareskrim). He is alleged to have run a cross-border online fraud network operating from **Cambodia** (Kamboja), using a branded fraud platform called "abbishopee".

## Reliability and Credibility

- **Reliability: high** — first-party institutional release by the arresting agency (Polri Bareskrim) on Polri's official second-level domain (`polri.go.id`).
- **Credibility: probably-true** — first-party announcement quoting a named senior official (Brig. Gen. Pol. Himawan Bayu Aji, Director of Cyber Crime at Bareskrim Polri); single tier-1 source in this ingest, no contradicting source observed.
- **Sensitivity: public** — distributed via the agency's national news portal. Suspect identity is released only as the initials `LCS`, consistent with Indonesian pre-trial reporting practice (same convention as in the April 2026 INP-FBI MFA-bypass release).

## Key Cooperation Facts Established

| Fact | Source line |
|---|---|
| Arrest event | "Tim gabungan Polri berhasil menangkap seorang Warga Negara Indonesia (WNI) berinisial LCS yang masuk dalam daftar buronan internasional (Red Notice) Interpol" |
| Arrest date / venue | "Penangkapan dilakukan di Bandara Internasional Soekarno-Hatta pada Minggu (3/5/2026)" |
| Status of suspect | "termasuk dalam Daftar Pencarian Orang (DPO) Direktorat Tindak Pidana Siber (Dittipidsiber) Bareskrim Polri" |
| Foreign operating base | "kasus penipuan online lintas negara yang melibatkan jaringan internasional yang beroperasi di Kamboja" |
| Domestic complaint volume | "sedikitnya 23 Laporan Polisi (LP) dari berbagai wilayah di Indonesia" |
| Modus / platform | "berperan sebagai operator dalam menjalankan aksi penipuan online dengan menggunakan platform bernama 'abbishopee'" |
| Prior convictions in the same network | "tiga tersangka lain yang terkait dengan jaringan LCS. Ketiganya telah diproses hukum hingga memperoleh putusan dari Pengadilan Negeri Jakarta Selatan" |
| Senior official on the record | "Brigjen Pol. Himawan Bayu Aji, Direktur Tindak Pidana Siber Bareskrim Polri" |
| Cooperation framing | "hasil kerja sama dan koordinasi lintas negara serta bentuk keseriusan kami dalam menindak pelaku kejahatan siber" |
| Forward intent | "Kami akan terus melakukan pengembangan untuk mengungkap jaringan internasional yang terlibat, serta menelusuri aliran dana hasil kejahatan guna pemulihan kerugian para korban" |

## What this source establishes (and does not)

**Establishes:**
- Polri Bareskrim Dittipidsiber as the lead Indonesian agency.
- INTERPOL Red Notice as the multilateral coordination instrument used to track LCS internationally.
- Cambodia as the foreign operating base of the fraud network (named explicitly).
- Centralized handling: 23+ regional police reports consolidated under the national Cyber Crime Directorate.
- Three earlier-arrested co-defendants already convicted at Pengadilan Negeri Jakarta Selatan, suggesting the platform / network has been under Polri attention for some time.

**Does not establish:**
- The specific Cambodian agencies (if any) that cooperated with Polri.
- Ident
